q.stem: A 7-year-old girl with known persistent asthma is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance after 24 hours of escalating wheeze and cough. Her usual preventer is fluticasone 100 microg BD via spacer, with salbutamol as reliever; she has had two previous ED presentations this year and one PICU admission at age 5 requiring high-flow oxygen. At home over the last 6 hours her mother has given hourly salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer with no benefit. In the ambulance she received back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, plus IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg. There has been minimal symptomatic or objective improvement on arrival. She has had no fevers, no foreign-body history, and no recent oral steroid course in the past 6 weeks. She has not eaten since lunch and her last menstrual period is not applicable. She has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 89% on 8 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 102/68 mmHg, T 37.2 deg C
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.28, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.1 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone 100 microg BD, salbutamol PRN, no LABA, no recent oral prednisolone
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 5, two ED visits this year, allergic rhinitis, no NSAID exposure

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 10 microg/kg into the lateral thigh, reassess and repeat in 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: SC adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis-related bronchospasm or settings where inhaled therapy cannot be delivered; it is not first-line escalation in known severe asthma per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
options.rationale: After back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid have failed in severe acute asthma, IV magnesium sulphate is the next escalation as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation and mechanical ventilation with permissive hypercapnia in the emergency department now
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is high risk for bronchospasm, hypotension and barotrauma; it is reserved for impending respiratory arrest after maximal medical therapy, per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser and observe for stridor reduction over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline targets upper airway oedema in croup, not lower airway bronchospasm; it has no role in this presentation per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Switch from IV hydrocortisone to oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: She has already received an adequate dose of IV hydrocortisone; switching to oral steroid adds no acute benefit and delays escalation, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Severe / life-threatening acute paediatric asthma escalates as oxygen + back-to-back salbutamol + ipratropium + systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulphate, then IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusion, with early PICU referral as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.
key_points: Severity is assessed by speech, accessory muscle use, mental state, SpO2 and air entry
Silent chest is a sign of life-threatening asthma
IV magnesium dose is 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
Avoid intubation if at all possible; involve PICU/anaesthetics early
explanation.pearls: Previous PICU admission is a strong predictor of fatal asthma; escalate early and involve PICU before the chest goes truly silent.

q.stem: A 24-day-old term male infant is brought to ED by his parents at 9 pm with a measured rectal temperature of 38.2 deg C two hours ago, and reduced feeding for the last 12 hours, taking around 40 percent of his usual volumes. Birth was uncomplicated at 40 weeks, group B Strep status was negative, and his mother received no intrapartum antibiotics. He has been previously well, is breastfed, has had normal wet nappies until today, and has had no rashes, vomiting or diarrhoea. He has had no cough or coryza and his older sibling with a runny nose has not been febrile (this hooks the discharge-home and urinalysis-only distractors, since neonatal age alone mandates full septic workup regardless of source). There are no sick contacts at home apart from a 4-year-old sibling with a clear runny nose. He is fully immunised for age (none yet due). He looks pale, is consolable but quiet, and his parents specifically report he is not interacting as he normally does at this time of evening.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.3 deg C rectal, HR 178 bpm, RR 56 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Quiet but rousable, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, no focal signs, soft non-distended abdomen, normal tone
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP, blood culture, urinalysis and urine culture pending; LP not yet performed

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with regular paracetamol and review by the GP in 12 hours if persistent fever
options.rationale: Any fever >= 38 deg C in an infant under 3 months mandates a full septic workup and empirical antibiotics; outpatient management is unsafe per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

options.text: Await blood culture and CRP results in ED before deciding on any empirical antibiotic therapy today
options.rationale: Antibiotics must not be delayed for results in a febrile neonate; empirical therapy is started immediately after cultures per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

options.text: Admit, perform lumbar puncture, and commence IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime empirically
options.rationale: Febrile infants under 4 weeks require admission, full septic workup including LP, and empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (or ceftriaxone after 4 weeks) per RCH CPG: Febrile child and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily pending blood culture results and arrange GP review
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for possible neonatal sepsis; IV therapy is mandatory per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Perform urinalysis only; if negative, observe in the short stay unit overnight without any empirical antibiotics
options.rationale: A negative urinalysis does not exclude bacteraemia or meningitis in this age group; full workup and empirical antibiotics are required per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

explanation.summary: Any temperature >= 38 deg C in an infant < 3 months is treated as possible serious bacterial infection. Infants < 4 weeks have a full septic workup (FBC, CRP, blood culture, urine, LP) and IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime. From 4 weeks to 3 months, ceftriaxone replaces cefotaxime; LP threshold depends on clinical picture.
key_points: Threshold for full septic workup is < 3 months with fever >= 38 deg C
Empirical antibiotics: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime under 4 weeks
LP is part of the workup unless contraindicated
Never delay antibiotics for investigation results
explanation.pearls: Listeria and group B Strep cover (benzylpenicillin) is dropped after about 4-6 weeks unless specific risk factors persist.

q.subtopic_detail: Infectious diseases

q.stem: A 4-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community is retrieved to a regional ED after 18 hours of fever, headache, vomiting and increasing drowsiness. She is partially immunised: she received 6-week and 4-month vaccines but missed the 12-month and 18-month boosters, so her Hib and MenC/MenB status is incomplete. Her older brother had a febrile URTI five days ago. The family travelled three weeks ago to visit relatives in an area with known household TB exposure (her uncle is on TB treatment); the child has had no chronic cough or weight loss. She has a documented maternal history of penicillin-induced urticaria but no history of anaphylaxis. She has a VP shunt placed at age 2 for post-haemorrhagic hydrocephalus, last revised 18 months ago. On the retrieval flight she had two episodes of focal twitching of the right arm lasting 30 seconds each, which resolved without treatment. She has not received any antibiotics or steroids prior to arrival. Her parents are present and have consented to investigations.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 154 bpm, RR 32 /min, BP 88/50 mmHg, SpO2 97% on room air, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6), neck stiffness, photophobia, no rash, soft non-tense fontanelle (closed), VP shunt site clean, no papilloedema on fundoscopy, no focal neurology between events
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 18.6), CRP 248 mg/L, lactate 4.1 mmol/L, Na 128 mmol/L, glucose 6.2 mmol/L, coagulation normal; CT brain pending; LP deferred until imaging
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; no recent antibiotics; no recent steroids
data_table.History: Partial immunisation (missed Hib/MenC/MenB boosters), VP shunt in situ, TB contact 3 weeks ago, maternal penicillin urticaria, sibling URTI 5 days ago

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management before LP?

options.text: IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg then IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg plus IV vancomycin, with neurosurgery referral
options.rationale: Empirical therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis under 9 years is IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg; vancomycin is added for possible shunt-related coagulase-negative staph and resistant pneumococcus, and dexamethasone is given just before or with the first antibiotic dose per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Meningitis. Maternal penicillin urticaria does not contraindicate cephalosporins.

options.text: IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg empirically for HSV encephalitis and defer all empirical antibacterial therapy until LP is performed
options.rationale: HSV encephalitis is a differential given seizures and altered mental state, but the dominant picture (neutrophilia, very high CRP, neck stiffness, photophobia, lactic acidosis) is bacterial; aciclovir alone is inadequate cover per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg monotherapy without vancomycin or dexamethasone, then proceed directly to LP before any imaging
options.rationale: Vancomycin is required while shunt infection (coagulase-negative staph, Staph aureus) and resistant pneumococcus remain in the differential, and dexamethasone before antibiotics reduces sensorineural deafness in Hib meningitis per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence quadruple anti-tuberculous therapy and IV ceftriaxone now, deferring corticosteroid until TB PCR result returns positive
options.rationale: TB meningitis is on the differential but the acute pyogenic picture (CRP 248 mg/L, neutrophilic leucocytosis, 18-hour onset) is not TB; empirical TB therapy is not first-line and steroid timing should not wait per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

options.text: Perform LP immediately before any imaging or antibiotic to ensure maximum microbiological yield from CSF cultures today
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated before imaging when GCS is reduced, focal seizures have occurred, or signs of raised ICP are possible; antibiotics must not be delayed for LP per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

explanation.summary: In bacterial meningitis with reduced GCS, focal seizures, or possible raised ICP, antibiotics and dexamethasone are given first and CT precedes LP. A VP shunt broadens cover to include coagulase-negative staphylococci and Staph aureus, mandating vancomycin alongside ceftriaxone. Maternal urticaria does not contraindicate a third-generation cephalosporin.
key_points: Empirical regimen: ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily plus vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV (shunt risk)
Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg just before or with first antibiotic dose reduces hearing loss in Hib
Defer LP if reduced GCS, focal signs, focal seizures, or coagulopathy
Notify Public Health for invasive meningococcal disease contacts (CDNA)
Indigenous communities have higher rates of invasive Hib and meningococcal disease - vaccine catch-up matters
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemia in meningitis is most often SIADH; fluids are restricted, not bolused, unless shocked.

q.subtopic_detail: Infectious diseases

q.stem: A 28-week gestation preterm female infant, now day 12 of life and corrected age 29+5, is being cared for in a tertiary NICU. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and reached full enteral feeds of expressed breast milk at 150 mL/kg/day on day 9. Over the past 6 hours she has had increasing apnoeas requiring stimulation, two episodes of bilious aspirate from the orogastric tube totalling 6 mL, and a distended tense abdomen with visible bowel loops. Her last stool was loose and faintly blood-streaked. She is on caffeine citrate, vitamins, and iron; she has had no recent transfusion and no umbilical lines remain. There is no family history of inflammatory bowel disease. Her mother had clear liquor and received a full antenatal steroid course; there was no chorioamnionitis. On examination she is irritable and mottled.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.2 deg C axillary, HR 178 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 88% on CPAP FiO2 0.35, BP 48/28 mmHg, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Distended tense abdomen with visible loops, reduced bowel sounds, palpable tender mass-effect in right lower quadrant, no inguinal hernia, perfusion poor
data_table.Investigations: WCC 3.1 x10^9/L (neutrophils 0.8), platelets 78 x10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.0 mmol/L, abdominal x-ray shows pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas, no free intraperitoneal air
data_table.Medications: Caffeine citrate, vitamins, iron supplement; full enteral feeds since day 9; no current antibiotics
data_table.History: 28-week preterm, antenatal steroids complete, no chorioamnionitis, no umbilical line since day 7

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cease feeds, free-draining orogastric tube, IV ampicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, 10 mL/kg saline bolus, refer paediatric surgery
options.rationale: Bell stage II NEC with systemic compromise requires gut rest with OG decompression, triple antibiotic cover (ampicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole), cautious fluid resuscitation, and surgical involvement; pneumoperitoneum or clinical deterioration triggers laparotomy per RCH NICU: NEC and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at half rate, observe abdominal girth hourly, send blood cultures and repeat AXR in 12 hours
options.rationale: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas is established NEC; continued feeds risk progression to perforation per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Proceed straight to laparotomy in NICU without antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, or further imaging while orogastric tube remains clamped
options.rationale: Without pneumoperitoneum or peritonitis the initial approach is medical with surgical standby; resuscitation and antibiotics precede any operation per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Continue current feed volume, add probiotic supplementation, send blood cultures, and recheck abdominal x-ray in 12 hours
options.rationale: Probiotics are a primary-prevention strategy in selected preterm infants and have no role in active NEC management per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Convert to donor expressed breast milk, continue current ventilatory support and feed volume, with no antibiotics indicated
options.rationale: Donor milk does not treat established NEC and antibiotics are required given systemic signs and biochemical derangement per RCH NICU: NEC.

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is staged by Bell criteria. Stage II (radiological pneumatosis with systemic signs) is managed with bowel rest, OG decompression, parenteral nutrition, triple antibiotic cover, and surgical involvement. Stage III (perforation, shock) requires laparotomy.
key_points: Risk factors: prematurity, formula vs breast milk, hypoxic-ischaemic events, sepsis
Pneumatosis intestinalis on AXR is pathognomonic
Triple cover: ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole (or piperacillin-tazobactam)
NPO 7-14 days depending on stage; TPN required
Indication for laparotomy: pneumoperitoneum, deterioration despite medical therapy
explanation.pearls: Thrombocytopenia and falling platelet trend correlate with NEC severity; track them serially.

q.stem: A 9-year-old girl (weight 28 kg) with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to ED with 2 days of vomiting, abdominal pain, polyuria, and polydipsia, and increasing drowsiness over the past 4 hours. She has had no diarrhoea, no head injury, and no preceding focal neurology. She has not received any insulin yet. Her parents report a recent viral URTI two weeks ago and weight loss of 3 kg over 6 weeks. There is a family history of coeliac disease but no thyroid disease. On arrival she is moderately dehydrated with deep sighing respirations. Her capillary blood glucose is 28 mmol/L and capillary ketones are 5.6 mmol/L. She has had no IV fluids or antibiotics yet. Her allergies are nil known.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 132 bpm, RR 34 /min Kussmaul, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 96/62 mmHg, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Examination: Kussmaul respiration, acetone breath, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, soft abdomen with mild generalised tenderness, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.08, HCO3 8 mmol/L, pCO2 18 mmHg, anion gap 24, Na 132 mmol/L (corrected 136), K 5.4 mmol/L, glucose 28 mmol/L, ketones 5.6 mmol/L, urea 9.2 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L
data_table.Medications: Nil; new diagnosis of T1DM, no insulin started
data_table.History: Recent viral URTI, weight loss 3 kg over 6 weeks, family history of coeliac disease

q.lead_in: After airway and breathing assessment, what is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: IV insulin bolus 0.1 unit/kg now then fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/hr without prior fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: An insulin bolus is not given in paediatric DKA; insulin is started as a fixed-rate infusion 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation begins, at 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline and RCH CPG: DKA.

options.text: 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus, then deficit replacement over 48 hours with insulin at 0.05 unit/kg/hr later
options.rationale: Paediatric DKA fluid resuscitation is cautious (10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 30-60 min unless shocked), with deficit replaced over 48 hours and insulin started 1-2 hours after fluid commencement at 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr to minimise cerebral oedema risk per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline and RCH CPG: DKA.

options.text: Give 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and repeat as needed, then commence insulin concurrently with the second bolus
options.rationale: Rapid large fluid boluses (>10 mL/kg) increase cerebral oedema risk in paediatric DKA and are reserved for true shock; insulin is not started immediately per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 1 hour to correct metabolic acidosis before commencing fluids or insulin therapy
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is associated with worse outcomes including cerebral oedema and hypokalaemia, and is not recommended in paediatric DKA except for life-threatening hyperkalaemia per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

options.text: Give an immediate dose of IV mannitol 0.5 g/kg as prophylaxis against cerebral oedema, then commence fluids and insulin
options.rationale: Mannitol is reserved for treatment of overt cerebral oedema (e.g. headache, GCS deterioration, hypertension, bradycardia), not prophylaxis per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management prioritises cautious fluid replacement and delayed insulin to minimise cerebral oedema. Fluid resuscitation: only 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 30-60 min unless shocked; deficit (5-7%) replaced evenly over 48 hours. Insulin 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr starts 1-2 hours after fluid begins. Bicarbonate is avoided.
key_points: Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of mortality in paediatric DKA
Warning signs: headache, GCS drop, bradycardia, hypertension, focal neurology
Insulin delayed 1-2 hours; no bolus
Add 10% dextrose to fluids when glucose < 14-17 mmol/L
Monitor potassium hourly; replace early
explanation.pearls: Corrected sodium that fails to rise (or falls) as glucose falls predicts cerebral oedema.

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology

q.stem: A 5-year-old boy (weight 19 kg) with known refractory focal epilepsy on levetiracetam and sodium valproate is brought into the resuscitation bay by ambulance with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at home 35 minutes ago. Paramedics gave intranasal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg en route at 15 minutes and again at 25 minutes with no termination. On arrival he is still actively convulsing. His mother reports a recent vomiting illness over 2 days with reduced oral intake, missed afternoon levetiracetam yesterday, and no head injury. There is no fever history and no household toxin exposure. He has had no recent travel. He weighs 19 kg, has no known drug allergies, and his recent levetiracetam level was therapeutic. He has IV access in the right antecubital fossa established by paramedics. Glucose by bedside meter is 4.2 mmol/L. A 12-lead ECG performed in the bay shows narrow complex sinus tachycardia with QTc 410 ms. Resuscitation team is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 deg C tympanic, HR 165 bpm, RR 30 /min self-ventilating, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min non-rebreather, BP 110/72 mmHg, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity, eyes deviated right, gag intact, no rash, fontanelle closed, no focal injury
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.2 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.22 lactate 5.4 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, calcium normal, paracetamol/salicylate negative, levetiracetam level pending
data_table.Medications: Levetiracetam 30 mg/kg/day, sodium valproate 20 mg/kg/day; two doses intranasal midazolam already given in ambulance
data_table.History: Refractory focal epilepsy, recent gastroenteritis, missed dose 24 hours ago, no allergies

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Give a third dose of IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg now and repeat at 5 minutes if seizure activity persists
options.rationale: After two failed benzodiazepine doses the seizure is benzodiazepine-resistant; further doses increase respiratory depression without improving termination per APLS Paediatric advanced life support and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Load with IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg over 5 minutes with cardiac monitoring, then prepare RSI with thiopentone
options.rationale: After two benzodiazepine doses, second-line therapy in paediatric convulsive status is IV levetiracetam (40-60 mg/kg) or phenytoin/fosphenytoin; thiopentone (or propofol) RSI is third-line if seizure persists, per APLS algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Load with IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg given that he is already on this medication for his epilepsy
options.rationale: Loading with the existing maintenance agent risks toxicity (especially valproate in a child with possible viral illness and hepatic stress) and is not first-choice second-line per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes as second-line anticonvulsant therapy for refractory status
options.rationale: IV magnesium is for eclamptic seizures and hypomagnesaemic seizures, not idiopathic refractory status epilepticus in a euglycaemic normokalaemic normocalcaemic child per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

options.text: Give IV 10% dextrose at 2 mL/kg empirically for possible hypoglycaemia and reassess seizure activity in 5 minutes
options.rationale: Bedside glucose is 4.2 mmol/L; the seizure is not hypoglycaemic and additional dextrose does not address ongoing seizure activity per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

explanation.summary: Paediatric convulsive status epilepticus that has failed two benzodiazepine doses (about 25-30 min) escalates to a second-line agent: levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg or phenytoin/fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg, monitoring rhythm. Refractory status (45+ minutes) is managed with anaesthetic agents (thiopentone, propofol, midazolam infusion) and intubation in PICU.
key_points: Limit benzodiazepines to two doses to avoid cumulative respiratory depression
Second-line: levetiracetam (preferred for cardiac stability) or fosphenytoin/phenytoin
Check glucose, electrolytes, toxicology, and look for reversible cause
Third-line: thiopentone or propofol with RSI
Continuous EEG once intubated to assess electrographic seizures
explanation.pearls: EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials showed levetiracetam and phenytoin equivalent for second-line termination; levetiracetam is often preferred for safety.

q.stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote community presents to a regional ED with a 3-week history of intermittent migratory polyarthritis affecting his right knee, then his left ankle, and now his right wrist. His mother reports an episode of sore throat 5 weeks ago that resolved without medical review. Over the past week he has been more breathless on exertion and his teacher noted involuntary jerking movements of his hands during class. He has no significant past medical history and is fully immunised. There is a family history of rheumatic heart disease in his maternal grandmother. He has had no recent travel outside the region and no household pets or insect bites. He has no known drug allergies. His weight and growth are on the 25th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, HR 118 bpm, RR 24 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 110/68 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild pallor, choreiform movements of the hands and tongue, soft pansystolic murmur loudest at the apex radiating to the axilla, mild left ankle swelling, no rash, no subcutaneous nodules, lungs clear
data_table.Investigations: ESR 88 mm/hr, CRP 64 mg/L, ASOT 1200 IU/mL, anti-DNase B 1800 U/mL, ECG PR interval 220 ms, echocardiogram shows mild mitral regurgitation with normal LV function, throat swab pending
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; no recent antibiotic course
data_table.History: Sore throat 5 weeks ago, family history of RHD, lives in RHD-endemic community

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate single-best diagnostic and management decision?

options.text: Diagnose definite acute rheumatic fever, commence IM benzathine penicillin, aspirin, secondary prophylaxis register, cardiology referral
options.rationale: He fulfils high-risk Jones criteria for definite ARF (2 major: chorea and carditis with mitral regurgitation; supportive evidence of preceding GAS via ASOT/anti-DNase B). Treatment is benzathine penicillin, anti-inflammatory therapy, and lifelong secondary prophylaxis enrolment per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose juvenile idiopathic arthritis and commence weekly oral methotrexate 10 mg/m^2 with folic acid and rheumatology follow-up
options.rationale: Migratory polyarthritis with carditis, chorea and high streptococcal serology is ARF, not JIA; JIA does not cause chorea or mitral regurgitation per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose viral myocarditis and admit for supportive care with telemetry, paracetamol for fever, no antibiotics or anti-inflammatory therapy
options.rationale: The constellation of migratory arthritis, chorea, carditis and elevated streptococcal titres is ARF; penicillin is required for residual GAS and secondary prophylaxis per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose infective endocarditis and start IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin empirically pending three sets of blood cultures
options.rationale: There are no peripheral stigmata, no fever pattern of endocarditis, and the picture (chorea, migratory arthritis, ASOT rise) is ARF; blood cultures should be sent but empirical endocarditis therapy is not indicated per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Diagnose probable functional movement disorder, reassure the family, and refer to community psychology for outpatient assessment review
options.rationale: Sydenham chorea is organic and pathognomonic for ARF in high-risk populations; functional diagnosis is inappropriate per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

explanation.summary: In high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, Maori and Pacific peoples), the Jones criteria are modified: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor manifestations of GAS infection establish a definite ARF diagnosis. Treatment includes benzathine penicillin G IM, anti-inflammatory therapy for arthritis/carditis (aspirin or naproxen), and lifelong-or-prolonged secondary prophylaxis enrolment.
key_points: High-risk Jones major: carditis, polyarthritis or aseptic monoarthritis or polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Evidence of preceding GAS: ASOT or anti-DNase B rise, positive throat swab/culture, recent scarlet fever
Benzathine penicillin G dose: 1.2 million units IM (> 20 kg) every 3-4 weeks
Echocardiography is now mandatory in suspected ARF
Notify the state RHD register
explanation.pearls: Chorea may appear weeks after the initial illness and can be isolated; do not exclude ARF for absence of arthritis.

q.subtopic_detail: Cardiology / ID

q.stem: A 5-month-old boy is brought to ED by his 18-year-old mother with a 3-day history of irritability and reduced feeding. The mother attends with her current partner of 3 weeks. She gives an inconsistent history: she initially says the baby has not fallen, then later that he rolled from a couch yesterday. Birth was at term with no concerns; he is not yet rolling independently. He attends the maternal and child health nurse irregularly and has missed his 4-month immunisations. The partner is unemployed and they have moved house three times in the past 6 months. There is no formal record of family violence, but the mother smells of cigarette smoke and appears withdrawn. On examination there is a 1 cm bruise on the left cheek, a torn upper labial frenulum, and several small bruises on the anterior shins. He has no documented bleeding disorder and no known medical conditions.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 100% on room air, BP 88/52 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert and irritable; left cheek bruise 1 cm, torn upper frenulum with small clot, small bruises over anterior shins, anterior fontanelle soft, no retinal haemorrhages on fundoscopy by senior, normal tone, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, coagulation normal, UEC normal, LFTs mildly raised ALT 95 U/L, skeletal survey pending, head imaging pending
data_table.History: Missed 4-month immunisations, irregular MCH visits, three house moves in 6 months, new partner 3 weeks, inconsistent injury history

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, head imaging (CT then MRI), ophthalmology review, mandatory child protection notification
options.rationale: Bruising and a torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant are sentinel injuries; an inconsistent history and social risk factors compound concern. Workup includes skeletal survey, neuroimaging, and ophthalmology, with mandatory reporting per RCH CPG: Child abuse and the relevant state child protection legislation.

options.text: Discharge home with community social work follow-up arranged in the next 2 weeks and GP review tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Discharging a non-mobile infant with multiple sentinel injuries and an inconsistent history risks further harm; admission for full workup and immediate reporting is mandatory per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Order FBC and coagulation studies only, and if normal, attribute the bruising to a presumed undiagnosed inherited bleeding disorder
options.rationale: Normal coagulation does not exclude non-accidental injury; further imaging and child protection notification are required per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Explain to the mother that you suspect non-accidental injury and ask the partner to leave the room before further history-taking
options.rationale: Direct disclosure of suspicion in this manner can escalate risk to the child and provoke removal from care; suspicion is communicated through child protection processes, not unilateral confrontation per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Arrange routine outpatient paediatrician review and community social worker phone call within the next 7 days for assessment
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral is unsafe for an infant with sentinel injuries; immediate inpatient workup and notification are required per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

explanation.summary: A bruise in a non-mobile infant, a torn frenulum, and any inconsistent history are sentinel injuries for child physical abuse. Required workup includes skeletal survey, neuroimaging (CT then MRI if positive or symptoms suggest), retinal examination, coagulation screen, LFTs, and mandatory child protection notification. The medical practitioner is a mandatory reporter in every Australian state and territory.
key_points: If a baby isn't cruising, they don't have bruising
Sentinel injuries: bruise in non-mobile child, torn frenulum, intraoral injury, ear injury, genital injury
Mandatory reporting is by suspicion, not proof
Document injuries with photographs and clear anatomical drawings
Engage social work and paediatric forensic services early
explanation.pearls: Document the history in the carer's words verbatim, with quotation marks; later inconsistencies are critical evidence.

q.subtopic_detail: Child protection

q.stem: A 36-year-old G3P1 woman (one term vaginal birth, one first-trimester miscarriage) is reviewed at 33 weeks and 4 days gestation in the maternal-foetal medicine clinic. Her booking BP was 122/76 mmHg. She has chronic hypertension diagnosed at 28 years of age, well-controlled on labetalol 200 mg twice daily through pregnancy. Booking bloods showed Hb 124, platelets 235, urate 0.28, creatinine 68, AST 22, ALT 18, normal LFTs. She had a first-trimester combined risk screen and a normal 20-week morphology scan. She received aspirin 150 mg nocte from 12 weeks based on her chronic hypertension and age. She has had no headache, no visual symptoms, no epigastric pain and reports normal foetal movements. She is a non-smoker, BMI 31, allergic to penicillin (rash), influenza-vaccinated this pregnancy and has had her pertussis booster at 28 weeks. Family history: mother had pre-eclampsia and sister had HELLP at 32 weeks. Today she reports mild ankle swelling and the midwife notes 1+ proteinuria on dipstick.

data_table.Vital signs: BP today 152/96 mmHg (repeated, confirmed). HR 84. T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Fundus 33 cm, cephalic, foetal movements palpable. Brisk reflexes, no clonus. Mild bipedal oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio 38 mg/mmol. Hb 122, platelets 188 (was 235), creatinine 78 (was 68), urate 0.36, AST 28, ALT 24, LDH 240, normal coags. Foetal growth USS today: EFW 30th centile (was 45th at 28 weeks), umbilical artery PI 95th centile, normal MCA, AFI 9. CTG reactive.

q.lead_in: Using SOMANZ 2023 criteria, what is the most accurate diagnosis?

options.text: Worsening chronic hypertension only, with no new proteinuria or end-organ involvement
options.rationale: Although BP has risen, additional features (new proteinuria with PCR >=30 mg/mmol, falling platelets, rising creatinine, rising urate, falling foetal growth centile, abnormal umbilical artery Doppler) meet SOMANZ criteria for superimposed pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Gestational hypertension, new onset after 20 weeks without proteinuria
options.rationale: She has pre-existing chronic hypertension; the term is reserved for new hypertension after 20 weeks without features of pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ).

options.text: Pre-eclampsia superimposed on chronic hypertension, with new proteinuria
options.rationale: Chronic hypertension with new-onset proteinuria (PCR 38 mg/mmol, threshold >=30) plus haematological involvement (platelets fallen by ~20%), renal involvement (creatinine risen) and uteroplacental dysfunction (falling growth centile, abnormal UA Doppler) meets SOMANZ 2023 criteria for superimposed pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: HELLP syndrome, with haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets
options.rationale: HELLP requires haemolysis (low haptoglobin, schistocytes, LDH >600), elevated transaminases (typically >70) and platelets <100; she has not met those thresholds (SOMANZ).

options.text: Isolated foetal growth restriction without maternal end-organ involvement
options.rationale: FGR is part of the picture but the maternal blood pressure, proteinuria and end-organ changes mean the syndrome is pre-eclampsia with FGR as a manifestation (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: SOMANZ 2023 defines pre-eclampsia as hypertension after 20 weeks plus any new maternal or uteroplacental organ involvement (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary oedema, or uteroplacental dysfunction with FGR/abnormal Doppler/stillbirth). In chronic hypertension, the same features superimposed make it 'superimposed pre-eclampsia'.
key_points: Proteinuria threshold: PCR >=30 mg/mmol or >=300 mg/24 h or >=2+ dipstick after exclusion of UTI
Severe features: BP >=160/110, platelets <100, creatinine >=90, AST/ALT >2x normal, pulmonary oedema, neurological signs, severe FGR with abnormal Doppler
Aspirin 100-150 mg nocte from 12-16 weeks reduces pre-eclampsia in high-risk women
Birth is curative; balance maternal risk against gestation and foetal maturity
Magnesium for severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia
explanation.pearls: Proteinuria is not required to diagnose pre-eclampsia under SOMANZ if other maternal or uteroplacental organ dysfunction is present.

q.subtopic_detail: Pre-eclampsia - SOMANZ diagnostic threshold

q.stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman presents to a tertiary maternity service at 30 weeks and 3 days gestation with 4 hours of regular painful contractions every 4 minutes. Her previous pregnancy ended in a spontaneous vaginal birth at 35 weeks (this hooks the immediate-Caesarean distractor, since prior preterm vaginal birth is not in itself an indication for operative delivery now). This pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date: booking bloods normal, OGTT normal, 20-week scan normal, GBS swab not yet done. She is on no regular medications, has no allergies, smoking 5 cigarettes per day (declined to cease), and lives 90 minutes from the nearest tertiary NICU (currently in that NICU, so transfer-out is not indicated). Sterile speculum shows a long closed cervix at booking 4 weeks ago, but on TVUS today her cervical length is 18 mm and foetal fibronectin is positive. Membranes are intact. The foetal heart is reassuring on continuous CTG monitoring at the bedside.

data_table.Examination: Contractions palpable every 4 min, moderate intensity. Sterile speculum: long-looking cervix, no liquor pooling, no bleeding. No digital VE performed.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS cervical length 18 mm. Foetal fibronectin positive. Hb 120, WCC 11.8, CRP 6. CTG reactive.

q.lead_in: Which combination of interventions is most appropriate now?

options.text: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM stat and again in 24 hours, no tocolysis, neuroprotection if imminent
options.rationale: Tocolysis is indicated to gain 48 hours for steroid effect at this gestation with high-risk features (cervical length <25 mm, positive fFN, regular contractions) (RANZCOG; ACOG).

options.text: Betamethasone, nifedipine tocolysis, magnesium sulphate neuroprotection, paediatric consult, in-utero NICU transfer
options.rationale: At 30 weeks with imminent preterm birth, give antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses 24 hours apart), tocolysis with nifedipine (loading then maintenance) to gain time for steroids, and magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks per some guidelines, <32 weeks per others), with paediatric consult (RANZCOG; ANZCTG; ACOG).
source_refs: ANZ Antenatal magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection guideline

options.text: Atosiban tocolysis alone, corticosteroids only if delivery delayed beyond 24 hours, no neuroprotection
options.rationale: Corticosteroids should be administered immediately on presentation with threatened preterm labour at this gestation; delaying steroids loses their benefit if birth occurs sooner than expected (RANZCOG).

options.text: Immediate Category 1 Caesarean section for foetal protection without tocolysis or corticosteroids
options.rationale: There is no foetal indication for delivery and her contractions may be amenable to tocolysis; preterm birth should be vaginal unless other obstetric indication (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transfer ex-utero to peripheral hospital for ongoing observation, no tocolysis or steroids
options.rationale: She is already in a tertiary centre with appropriate NICU - in-utero transfer to a lower-level centre is inappropriate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Threatened preterm labour at 30 weeks with cervical length <25 mm and positive fFN: give antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses 24 h apart), tocolysis with nifedipine to gain 48 hours for steroid effect, magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection, and arrange paediatric consult.
key_points: Steroids: most benefit if birth between 24-34+6 weeks; benefits within 24 h to 7 days
Tocolysis: nifedipine first-line; atosiban alternative; contraindications include foetal compromise, chorioamnionitis, severe PE
MgSO4 neuroprotection: usually <30-32 weeks, single 4 g loading + 1 g/h or single dose
GBS prophylaxis indicated in preterm labour without GBS result
Consider in-utero transfer if NICU level inadequate
explanation.pearls: Tocolysis is to gain time for steroids and transfer - it does not improve neonatal outcomes by itself.

sources.label: ANZ Antenatal magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection guideline

q.subtopic_detail: Preterm labour - corticosteroid and tocolysis decision

q.stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman has just delivered a 4,100 g live male infant by spontaneous vaginal birth at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation. Her three previous births were uncomplicated. The third stage was actively managed with oxytocin 10 IU IM. The placenta delivered intact at 8 minutes with no obvious cotyledon defect. Within 5 minutes of placental delivery she has lost an estimated 1,200 mL of fresh red blood with ongoing trickling. On examination the uterus is soft and poorly contracted. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on inspection. She has no known coagulopathy, no anticoagulants, no prior PPH, and was Hb 116 g/L on admission. IV access is in place with 2 large-bore cannulae, fluids are running, group and hold is sent and the obstetric emergency team has been activated. Bimanual compression has been commenced.

data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, fundus at umbilicus, ongoing fresh PV bleed. Perineum: small first-degree tear, not bleeding. No clots in vagina.
data_table.Investigations: Group A Rh positive, antibody screen negative. Coags and FBC sent. Catheter inserted: 250 mL clear urine.

q.lead_in: Following first-line IV oxytocin bolus and infusion, which is the next-line uterotonic to administer per Australian PPH guidelines?

options.text: IM/IV ergometrine 250 mcg, repeatable up to 5 doses over 2 hours as uterotonic
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in this hypotensive tachycardic patient because of its vasoconstrictor effects and risk of further haemodynamic compromise; an alternative uterotonic is preferred per RANZCOG PPH.
source_refs: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007

options.text: IM carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg, repeatable up to 8 doses
options.rationale: Australian PPH algorithms (NSW Health, KEMH, RANZCOG) escalate as: oxytocin (bolus + infusion) -> ergometrine (if not hypertensive) -> carboprost 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes (max 2 mg) -> misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR. In this hypotensive, tachycardic, multiparous patient where ergometrine is relatively contraindicated by haemodynamic instability and uncertain BP trajectory, carboprost is the preferred next-line uterotonic, with tranexamic acid 1 g IV in parallel (RANZCOG PPH; NSW Health PPH).
source_refs: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007
NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007

options.text: Misoprostol 200 mcg PO single dose, no further uterotonic added at this stage
options.rationale: Misoprostol may be used as adjunctive uterotonic but the recommended dose is 800-1000 mcg PR/SL, not 200 mcg PO, and it is later in the escalation (RANZCOG).
source_refs: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV only, with no further uterotonic added at this stage
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is recommended early in PPH (WOMAN trial; given within 3 hours), but should be given in addition to ongoing uterotonics, not instead of them (RANZCOG).
source_refs: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007
WOMAN trial - tranexamic acid in PPH

options.text: Recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg IV bolus without further uterotonic at this stage
options.rationale: Recombinant FVIIa is a last-resort haemostatic agent in massive PPH refractory to standard measures; it is not a uterotonic and is not appropriate at this stage (RANZCOG).
source_refs: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007

explanation.summary: Australian PPH pharmacological escalation: oxytocin (10 IU IM or 5 IU IV bolus then 40 IU in 1 L over 4 h) -> ergometrine 250 mcg IM/IV if no hypertension -> carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes up to 2 mg -> misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR. Add tranexamic acid 1 g IV early and consider mechanical/surgical measures if not controlled.
key_points: Always work the 4Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth reduces death due to bleeding (WOMAN trial)
Massive transfusion protocol when >=1500 mL or ongoing brisk loss
Mechanical: bimanual compression, Bakri balloon, aortic compression
Surgical: B-Lynch, uterine/internal iliac artery ligation, hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: In a hypotensive woman, avoid ergometrine; choose carboprost (caution in asthma) next.

sources.label: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007
WOMAN trial - tranexamic acid in PPH

q.subtopic_detail: PPH - 4Ts pharmacological escalation

q.stem: A 38-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practice for contraceptive counselling. Her youngest child is 4 months old and exclusively breastfed. Her BMI is 33. She has migraine with aura (typical scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral headache, average twice per month) since age 19 (this hooks the COCP and patch distractors, since migraine with aura is an absolute UKMEC 4 contraindication to combined hormonal contraception). She had a left ankle ORIF 6 weeks ago for a complex fracture and remains in a removable boot, mobilising with crutches; her physiotherapist plans to discontinue the boot in 2 weeks. Her mother had a provoked DVT on the combined oral contraceptive at age 25. She is planning a long-haul flight (16 hours) in two weeks, which compounds the VTE risk already imposed by her reduced mobility post-fracture. She is normotensive (118/74) and a non-smoker. She had a Caesarean for breech with her first child and two subsequent uneventful vaginal births. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no liver disease, no breast cancer history, no current pelvic infection and no abnormal uterine bleeding. She is open to any contraceptive method that is safe in her current circumstances and prefers a long-acting reversible option if appropriate.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76. BMI 33.
data_table.Examination: Well. Left ankle in boot. No breast lumps. No pelvic tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date negative. No recent FBC.

q.lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate at this consultation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: COCP is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for migraine with aura at any age, and additionally UKMEC 3-4 with recent immobilisation post-orthopaedic surgery, BMI >=35 (here 33, but elevated baseline VTE), family history of VTE, age >=35 with multiple risk factors, and breastfeeding before 6 weeks postpartum (now 4 months so less restrictive but cumulative risk is high) (FSRH UKMEC 2016 + 2024 updates).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Combined transdermal contraceptive patch - weekly application regimen
options.rationale: Same systemic VTE/arterial risks as COCP; UKMEC 4 for migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) - clinic insertion
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1-2 in all of her stated risk categories (migraine with aura UKMEC 2, postpartum >=4 weeks UKMEC 1, VTE risk UKMEC 2, BMI 33 UKMEC 1) and provides highly effective long-term contraception suitable for breastfeeding and immobilisation; this is the most appropriate choice (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)
Family Planning Australia: Contraception in special circumstances

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) - IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 2 for migraine with aura and for breastfeeding but is UKMEC 3 in women with multiple VTE risk factors (recent surgery, immobilisation); LARC options with no VTE signal (IUDs, implant) are preferable (FSRH UKMEC).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel) - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: POP is safe (UKMEC 1-2) in her circumstances but is less effective (typical-use failure approximately 9%) than LNG-IUS and depends heavily on adherence; in a woman with this risk profile a LARC is preferable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

explanation.summary: This woman has multiple UKMEC 3/4 considerations for oestrogen-containing contraception: migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 absolute), recent orthopaedic surgery with reduced mobility (UKMEC 3), family history of VTE (UKMEC 3 if first-degree relative <45), planned long-haul flight (additional risk), age 38, BMI 33. The safest and most effective option is the LNG-IUS, which is UKMEC 1-2 across all her risk factors and offers 8 years of contraception.
key_points: Migraine with aura = UKMEC 4 for any combined hormonal contraception (any age)
Recent surgery with immobilisation = UKMEC 3-4 for CHC
BMI >=35 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
First-degree relative VTE <45 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
LNG-IUS and Cu-IUD are UKMEC 1-2 in nearly all medical contexts; insertion postpartum after 4 weeks is straightforward
explanation.pearls: Stack the UKMEC scores. Any one '4' rules out a method; combinations of '3's should push you to a different category entirely.

sources.label: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)
Family Planning Australia: Contraception in special circumstances

q.subtopic_detail: Contraception - multiple UKMEC category 3/4 considerations

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman in established labour at 40 weeks and 2 days gestation is being monitored by continuous CTG in the birth suite. Her previous birth was a normal vaginal delivery. The pregnancy was uncomplicated. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 22 hours ago with clear liquor; she has been in established labour for 9 hours. Cervical examination 30 minutes ago: 8 cm, fully effaced, station 0, position OA, intact caput. She is on no antibiotics. Over the past 90 minutes the midwife has documented a rising maternal temperature (now 38.2 degC), maternal tachycardia (HR 118), uterine tenderness between contractions, and new offensive vaginal discharge. The liquor draining now is meconium-stained (thick, particulate). Her epidural is functioning well. There are no antenatal risk factors for placental insufficiency. She is hydrated and her last paracetamol was 6 hours ago. The neonatal team and obstetric registrar have been called for review of the CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 108/68, HR 118, T 38.2 degC, RR 20, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Tender uterus between contractions. Offensive thick meconium-stained liquor. Cervix 8 cm, station 0.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116, WCC 18.2, CRP 86. CTG: baseline 175 bpm (was 145 four hours ago), variability 3-5 bpm (reduced), no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations with each of the last 6 contractions, depth 40 bpm, slow recovery; contractions 5 in 10, lasting 70 seconds.

q.lead_in: Using RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) classification, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue to observe progress, repeat VE in 2 hours, reassess CTG closely
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological (RANZCOG IFM: baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, recurrent late decelerations, plus clinical chorioamnionitis and meconium); continued observation alone is unsafe (RANZCOG IFM).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite spontaneous vaginal delivery within the hour
options.rationale: Tachysystole and uterine hyperstimulation worsen foetal compromise; augmentation is contraindicated in the presence of pathological CTG and likely chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG IFM).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

options.text: Insert foetal scalp electrode and consider foetal blood sampling for lactate assessment
options.rationale: FBS may have a role in pathological CTG with intact membranes and no other red flags, but in the presence of clinical chorioamnionitis, meconium, prolonged ROM, and recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability, the appropriate action is delivery, not further sampling (RANZCOG IFM).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

options.text: Stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, proceed directly to Category 1 Caesarean now
options.rationale: Proceeding immediately to Category 1 CS at 8 cm in a multipara without trialling intrauterine resuscitation, treating the underlying chorioamnionitis, and obtaining senior review for the most appropriate mode of birth is premature; expedited birth is required but the route depends on station, position, response to resuscitation, and senior assessment per RANZCOG IFM.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

options.text: Intrauterine resuscitation, IV antibiotics for chorioamnionitis, paracetamol for pyrexia, senior review for expedited birth
options.rationale: The combined features (pathological CTG, clinical chorioamnionitis, thick meconium, prolonged ROM 22 hours, maternal tachycardia and pyrexia) require simultaneous intrauterine resuscitation, treatment of the underlying chorioamnionitis with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, antipyretics, and senior decision-making for expedited birth (vaginal vs Cat 1 CS depending on response and progress); this is the RANZCOG IFM-aligned response (RANZCOG IFM; RANZCOG chorioamnionitis).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

explanation.summary: This is a pathological RANZCOG IFM CTG (baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, recurrent late decelerations) on a background of clinical chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, offensive discharge), thick meconium, and prolonged ROM. Required actions: intrauterine resuscitation, IV antibiotics, maternal cooling/paracetamol, and senior-led plan for expedited birth.
key_points: Pathological CTG features: baseline <100 or >160 with reduced variability, recurrent late or prolonged decelerations, sinusoidal pattern
Maternal pyrexia raises foetal baseline; treat the source and the symptom
Intrauterine resuscitation acronym (SPOIL): Stop oxytocin, Position left lateral, Oxygen (no longer routine), IV fluids, Lower BP if iatrogenic (e.g. epidural top-up), tocolysis if hyperstimulation
Chorioamnionitis: empirical IV antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole per local policy)
Mode of birth depends on station, position, progress and response to resuscitation
explanation.pearls: Maternal fever raises foetal baseline by ~10 bpm per degree; treat the fever, treat the infection, resuscitate the uterus, then make a delivery plan.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019

q.subtopic_detail: Intrapartum CTG - integrated interpretation

q.stem: An 18-month-old girl (weight 11 kg) with no prior seizure history is brought to a regional ED in active generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began 28 minutes ago at home. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 5 minutes and a second buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 15 minutes without termination. On arrival at 25 minutes she remains in generalised convulsion with eye deviation, frothing, and incontinence; IV access has just been secured on the second attempt and a bedside glucose is 4.8 mmol/L. She had a brief febrile illness with rhinorrhoea over the preceding 48 hours and a temperature of 38.6 deg C recorded today, but there is no rash, no vomiting, and no known toxic ingestion accessible in the home. She has met all developmental milestones to date, is fully immunised to the current Australian schedule, and has no family history of epilepsy or sudden infant death. The mother denies head trauma and no household members are on antiepileptic or antidepressant medications. There has been no prior antiepileptic exposure. The team has applied high-flow oxygen at 8 L/min via Hudson mask, has charcoal-impregnated suction available at the bedside, has prepared a rapid-sequence induction trolley, and the anaesthetic registrar and PICU outreach team have been paged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 28 /min (intermittent), SpO2 94% on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 96/58 mmHg, T 38.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity, GCS 3 during seizure, pupils 4 mm equal reactive, no neck stiffness assessable, no rash, anterior fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.8 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.20 pCO2 56 mmHg lactate 5.8 mmol/L, Na 137 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 mmol/L, FBC and CRP pending
data_table.Medications: Buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 5 min and 15 min, no other antiepileptics ever
data_table.History: First seizure, febrile URTI 48 h, fully immunised, no trauma, no toxin

q.lead_in: Following the APLS status epilepticus algorithm, what is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with continuous ECG monitoring
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable second-line agent in older children, but the EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials and the updated APLS Australia algorithm prefer levetiracetam, particularly in children under 2 years where phenytoin carries higher arrhythmia and infusion-site risk, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with airway support ready
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is the preferred second-line agent only in neonates; in infants and children outside the neonatal period it is third-line after levetiracetam (and after phenytoin if used), as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: A third dose of buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg and continue to observe for spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: More than two doses of benzodiazepine increases respiratory depression without improving termination rates; the algorithm advances to a second-line antiepileptic at this point, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg (max 3 g) over 5 minutes with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: After two benzodiazepine doses, the current APLS Australia and RCH algorithm recommends IV levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg as the preferred second-line agent in infants and children, supported by EcLiPSE/ConSEPT/ESETT data, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Rapid sequence induction with thiopentone 4 mg/kg and intubation as primary seizure control
options.rationale: Thiopentone induction is reserved for refractory status epilepticus after second-line agents have failed; jumping to RSI before trialling a second-line antiepileptic skips the algorithm, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

explanation.summary: Convulsive status epilepticus algorithm: oxygen, glucose check, two doses of benzodiazepine 5 minutes apart, then second-line antiepileptic at ~25 minutes. Following EcLiPSE/ConSEPT/ESETT, levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV is the preferred second-line agent in children outside the neonatal period. If still seizing, proceed to RSI with thiopentone or midazolam infusion.
key_points: Two doses of benzodiazepine max before moving on
Levetiracetam preferred over phenytoin in young children
Phenobarbitone is for neonates as second-line
Refractory status -> thiopentone RSI and PICU
Always check glucose, sodium, and consider non-convulsive causes
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine 100 mg IV should be considered in infants under 18 months with refractory seizures of unclear cause.

q.stem: A 9-year-old boy with severe persistent asthma (one PICU admission 18 months ago for life-threatening wheeze, baseline fluticasone-salmeterol BD via spacer, salbutamol PRN) is brought to a tertiary ED after 12 hours of progressive wheeze triggered by a viral URTI in the family. In ED over the past 60 minutes he has received continuous back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg with antiemetic cover, and high-flow oxygen at 10 L/min via nebuliser mask. There has been no clinical improvement: he remains exhausted, leaning forward, speaking only in single words, and looks visibly tired between breaths. He has not yet received any IV bronchodilators, IV magnesium, or aminophylline. He is normotensive but markedly tachycardic with a fine tremor and palpitations from the salbutamol. There has been no fever, no foreign body history, no chest pain, and no preceding anaphylactic features. The PICU outreach team has reviewed at the bedside and recommends escalation but has not yet started any infusions while awaiting the ED decision. He has a 22-gauge cannula in the right antecubital fossa and a second cannula being placed; his last meal was 4 hours ago. There are no known drug allergies and parents have given verbal consent for further therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 42 /min, SpO2 88% on 10 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 116/72 mmHg, T 37.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Tripoding, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, faint expiratory wheeze in lower zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted, fine tremor
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30 pCO2 50 mmHg HCO3 22 mmol/L lactate 3.8 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, glucose 9.4 mmol/L; CXR hyperinflation, no pneumothorax
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3 doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg given 35 min ago, no IV bronchodilator yet
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 7.5, fluticasone-salmeterol BD baseline, no LABA-only, no NSAID use

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes and reassess in 1 hour
options.rationale: He has already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting without escalation risks decompensation and is not the next step, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma failing oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid escalates to IV magnesium sulphate before IV salbutamol or aminophylline, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg loading over 60 minutes followed by 1 mg/kg/h infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is an option but is reserved for cases failing IV magnesium and IV salbutamol because of its narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV salbutamol 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by 1 microg/kg/min infusion
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is reasonable but typically follows IV magnesium in the RCH escalation ladder; potassium is already 3.0 mmol/L and lactate 3.8 mmol/L which IV salbutamol will worsen, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Immediate rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 2 mg/kg and rocuronium 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma carries high risk of bronchospasm, hypotension, and barotrauma and is reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma escalates: oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium, systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulphate, then IV salbutamol, then IV aminophylline, with intubation as last resort.
key_points: IV magnesium dose: 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 min
Silent chest indicates life-threatening asthma
Watch for hypokalaemia and lactic acidosis from salbutamol
Aminophylline is last-line because of arrhythmia risk
Involve PICU early when failing nebs + steroid
explanation.pearls: Ketamine is the induction agent of choice if RSI cannot be avoided because of its bronchodilator effect.

q.stem: An 11-year-old girl (weight 38 kg) is admitted to PICU with new-onset diabetic ketoacidosis (initial venous pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, corrected sodium 141 mmol/L at admission). She received an initial 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride for shock and was then commenced on a deficit-plus-maintenance regimen calculated for 7% dehydration over 48 hours, with an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 1 hour after the fluids and added potassium 40 mmol/L once she had urinated. She had no preceding head injury, no toxic ingestion, and no prior diabetes diagnosis; her family history is notable only for type 2 diabetes in a paternal uncle. Six hours into therapy the bedside nurse notes a 15-minute history of increasing headache, restlessness, and one episode of vomiting; over the past 20 minutes she has become more drowsy and confused, no longer engaging with her parents. Pre-decline GCS was 15; current GCS is 12 (E3 V3 M6). The most recent blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L (was 32, falling at 3 mmol/L/h), sodium has fallen from 138 to 132 mmol/L despite the glucose correction (corrected sodium therefore falling), and she now has bradycardia and a rising blood pressure on the trend chart. No focal neurology has yet been demonstrated; pupils are equal at 3 mm and reactive. The on-call PICU consultant has been called and the bed is being prepared for hyperosmolar therapy and possible intubation.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 62 bpm (was 110), RR 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 138/82 mmHg (was 102/64), T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6), drowsy and irritable, no focal weakness, pupils 3 mm equal reactive, no papilloedema, Kussmaul breathing resolving
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 14.2 mmol/L, ketones 3.8 mmol/L, Na 132 mmol/L (corrected was 141 at admission, now ~134), K 3.6 mmol/L, pH 7.22, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L; head CT not yet done
data_table.Fluids/insulin: 0.9% saline + KCl 40 mmol/L at deficit + maintenance for 7% over 48 h, insulin 0.05 units/kg/h, no dextrose yet added
data_table.Trajectory: Glucose falling 3 mmol/L/h, corrected Na falling, GCS dropping over 20 minutes

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion to 0.025 units/kg/h and add 5% dextrose to current fluid bag, continue current rate
options.rationale: Adding dextrose and reducing insulin is appropriate when glucose falls below ~14-17 mmol/L but does not address suspected cerebral oedema with bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS, which requires immediate hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to replace deficit over 24 hours instead of 48 hours to improve cerebral perfusion
options.rationale: Increasing fluid rate worsens cerebral oedema; current evidence (PECARN FLUID trial) supports cautious rehydration and immediate hyperosmolar therapy when oedema is suspected, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: IV hypertonic 3% sodium chloride 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, reduce fluid rate, urgent CT
options.rationale: Bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS, headache and vomiting are the Cushing-like triad of DKA-related cerebral oedema; immediate hyperosmolar therapy with 3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg) plus fluid rate reduction and head CT once stable are first-line, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for cerebral oedema and continue current fluid and insulin regimens
options.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces vasogenic oedema (e.g. tumour, meningitis) but has no role in DKA-related cytotoxic/osmotic cerebral oedema, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-28 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure today
options.rationale: Aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation below pCO2 30 mmHg in DKA cerebral oedema is associated with worse outcomes; intubation may be needed but is not the first step before hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

explanation.summary: DKA-related cerebral oedema presents 4-12 hours into treatment with headache, vomiting, falling GCS, bradycardia and hypertension. Immediate management is hyperosmolar therapy (3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduction of fluid rate by one-third, head-up positioning, and urgent imaging only once stabilised. Avoid aggressive hyperventilation.
key_points: Risk factors: new diagnosis, age <5, severe acidosis, high urea, low pCO2, bicarbonate use, rapid fluid
Treat clinically; do not wait for imaging
3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg
Reduce IV fluid rate; keep glucose falling no faster than ~5 mmol/L/h
Avoid pCO2 below 30 mmHg
explanation.pearls: Corrected sodium should rise as glucose falls; a falling corrected sodium is an early warning sign of cerebral oedema.

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology

q.stem: A 13-year-old girl (weight 52 kg) is brought to ED by her parents after they found an empty 100-tablet bottle of paracetamol 500 mg in her room and a written note. She admits to taking tablets in two separate episodes over the past 18 hours: she states she took about 30 tablets approximately 14 hours ago after an argument with her sister, then a further 20 tablets about 4 hours ago when she decided she wanted to die. She denies co-ingestion of other tablets, alcohol, or recreational drugs and there are no other empty packets in the home. She has no chronic medical illness, has a normal body mass index, is not on enzyme-inducing drugs (no carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampicin, or isoniazid), and there is no chronic alcohol use, eating disorder, or malnutrition that would raise glutathione-depletion risk. She is fully alert with no nausea or right upper quadrant pain. The on-call mental health team has been notified and is on the way. A paracetamol level taken on arrival (now 4 hours after the second ingestion and 18 hours after the first) is 320 micromol/L. ALT is 38 U/L and INR is 1.1; creatinine is 64 micromol/L; bicarbonate, lactate, and glucose are all normal. She has not vomited and has not yet received activated charcoal or N-acetylcysteine. The on-call clinical toxicologist has been contacted by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, RR 16 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 118/72 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: Alert and oriented, no jaundice, abdomen soft and non-tender, no asterixis, mood low but engaged
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 320 micromol/L (4 h post second ingestion / 18 h post first), ALT 38 U/L, INR 1.1, creatinine 64 micromol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, VBG normal
data_table.Ingestion: Approx 30 x 500 mg paracetamol 14 h ago, then approx 20 x 500 mg paracetamol 4 h ago; total ~25 g ingested in staggered fashion
data_table.Other: No co-ingestants, no enzyme inducers, no chronic illness

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management decision regarding N-acetylcysteine?

options.text: Plot the 320 micromol/L level on the nomogram at 4 hours, withhold N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions with a known time; in staggered ingestion or unknown time it cannot be used, and a low nomogram level does not exclude toxicity, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness and RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning.

options.text: Withhold N-acetylcysteine because ALT and INR are normal, and arrange repeat bloods in 8 hours to reassess
options.rationale: ALT and INR rise late (often after 24-36 hours); waiting for derangement in a staggered overdose of >200 mg/kg risks missing the treatment window where NAC is most effective, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours immediately
options.rationale: Staggered paracetamol ingestion >200 mg/kg (or >10 g in adults) over <24 hours mandates immediate full-course IV N-acetylcysteine regardless of the nomogram level; the nomogram does not apply, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness, RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning, and the Australian/New Zealand consensus statement on paracetamol overdose.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand consensus statement: paracetamol poisoning

options.text: Give activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally and reassess paracetamol level in 4 hours before deciding on N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion; here the earlier ingestion is 18 hours old and the more recent one is 4 hours old, so charcoal has minimal benefit, and N-acetylcysteine should not be delayed, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence oral methionine 2.5 g every 4 hours for 4 doses as an alternative to IV N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Oral methionine is not used in current Australian practice for paracetamol overdose; IV N-acetylcysteine is the standard antidote, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

explanation.summary: Staggered paracetamol overdose (more than one ingestion within a 24-hour window) renders the Rumack-Matthew nomogram invalid. If the total dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g, give full-course IV N-acetylcysteine regardless of the level. Charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion. Normal ALT and INR at presentation do not exclude developing toxicity.
key_points: Nomogram applies only to single ingestions with known time
Staggered ingestion >200 mg/kg or >10 g -> immediate NAC
NAC regimen: 200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h
Repeat paracetamol, ALT, INR at 8 h to decide on extension of NAC
Charcoal only if presentation within 1-2 h of ingestion
explanation.pearls: Massive ingestion (>30 g or paracetamol >1000 micromol/L at 4 h) may need double-dose NAC and dialysis consideration.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand consensus statement: paracetamol poisoning

q.subtopic_detail: Toxicology

q.stem: A 6-day-old male term infant (birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.2 kg) is brought to a regional ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy, and grey colour over the past 12 hours. He was born at 39+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, with Apgars 9 and 9, and discharged from the maternity unit at 36 hours after a normal newborn examination. The newborn pulse oximetry screen at the birth hospital was reportedly normal at 24 hours. There is no family history of congenital heart disease. On arrival in ED he is grey-mottled, with cool peripheries and weak femoral pulses bilaterally; the four-limb blood pressures show a right arm of 78/50 mmHg and a right leg of 52/30 mmHg. Pre-ductal SpO2 (right hand) is 92%, post-ductal SpO2 (foot) is 78%. He has a hyperdynamic precordium and a single loud second heart sound with no audible murmur. The chest x-ray shows cardiomegaly with pulmonary plethora. Bedside echo by the visiting paediatric cardiology fellow on tele-link demonstrates a critical coarctation of the aorta with a closing ductus arteriosus. The retrieval service is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178 bpm, RR 64 /min, SpO2 pre-ductal 92% / post-ductal 78%, BP RA 78/50, RL 52/30, T 36.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Grey-mottled, cool to mid-thigh, weak femoral pulses, hyperdynamic precordium, single loud S2, no murmur, hepatomegaly 3 cm
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.18 lactate 6.2 mmol/L base excess -12, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L, Hb 168 g/L; CXR cardiomegaly + pulmonary plethora; echo: critical coarctation with closing PDA
data_table.Differential considered and ruled out by: Sepsis (in parallel workup), hypoglycaemia (treated), inborn error of metabolism (less likely with structural finding)

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg IV to close the patent ductus arteriosus and improve systemic perfusion
options.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent systemic circulation (critical coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, hypoplastic left heart) will precipitate cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct open, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease
