options.text: Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia and improve cardiac output
options.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia at >220 bpm with absent P waves; the picture is duct-dependent systemic shock, not SVT, and adenosine has no benefit, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

options.text: Sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg orally to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance and improve systemic oxygenation
options.rationale: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; it does not maintain ductal patency, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion starting at 10-50 nanog/kg/min (commonly 0.05 microg/kg/min), with airway and apnoea management readiness
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation with a closing PDA is reopened with prostaglandin E1 infusion; the starting dose is 0.01-0.05 microg/kg/min titrated to effect, with airway support readiness because of apnoea risk, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence high-dose oxygen at FiO2 1.0 via head-box to maximise systemic saturations and reduce hypoxia-driven acidosis
options.rationale: High FiO2 in duct-dependent systemic circulation can promote ductal closure (oxygen is a stimulus for closure) and worsen the situation; target pre-ductal SpO2 ~85-92% rather than 100%, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

explanation.summary: Neonatal collapse between days 3-14 with shock, pre/post-ductal saturation differential and weak femoral pulses should be assumed to be duct-dependent congenital heart disease until proven otherwise. Treatment is IV prostaglandin E1 infusion to reopen the duct. Avoid high FiO2 and indomethacin. Be ready for apnoea, fever, and hypotension as PGE1 side effects.
key_points: Duct-dependent systemic: coarctation, IAA, HLHS, critical AS
PGE1 0.01-0.05 microg/kg/min titrated to effect
Apnoea risk: anticipate intubation
Target SpO2 ~85-92%; avoid 100% oxygen
Retrieve early to cardiac centre
explanation.pearls: Differential SpO2 with lower foot than hand is highly suggestive of a duct-dependent systemic or mixing lesion; check four-limb BPs.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

q.stem: A male infant born at 28+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia and absent end-diastolic flow on Doppler is now day 7 of life in the level 3 NICU of a tertiary perinatal centre. Mother received a full course of antenatal betamethasone and magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection. Birthweight was 1040 g (25th centile for gestation). He has been receiving minimal enteral feeds of expressed breast milk at 20 mL/kg/day for 4 days, advanced cautiously, and parenteral nutrition via a peripherally inserted central catheter; umbilical lines were removed on day 4. He is on nasal CPAP with FiO2 0.21 after extubation from surfactant therapy on day 2. Over the last 6 hours the nurses note new abdominal distension, two episodes of bilious aspirate, a single small bloody stool and a temperature instability with a low of 36.1 deg C. On examination he is mottled with a capillary refill of 4 seconds, the abdomen is tense and tender with visible bowel loops, and there is mild erythema of the abdominal wall. He has had one apnoea requiring stimulation in the last hour. He has no umbilical lines in situ. He has had no surgical procedures and no recent transfusions. He has not yet received probiotics. The treating team obtains a supine abdominal radiograph and a left lateral decubitus film, plus blood cultures, a venous gas and a full blood count.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.1 deg C, HR 178 bpm, RR 70 /min on CPAP FiO2 0.21, SpO2 91 percent
data_table.Examination: Mottled, tense distended tender abdomen, visible bowel loops, abdominal wall erythema, lethargic
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.2 x10^9/L, platelets 78 x10^9/L, CRP 65 mg/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L
data_table.Imaging: Supine AXR shows dilated bowel loops with intramural curvilinear lucencies; left lateral decubitus film shows no free intraperitoneal gas; no portal venous gas seen

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue current feeds, add oral probiotic, and repeat AXR in 24 hours
options.rationale: Continuing enteral feeds in confirmed NEC (Bell stage IIA with pneumatosis) is contraindicated and risks progression to perforation, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

options.text: Increase enteral feed volume to advance gut maturation and start oral gentamicin for bowel decontamination
options.rationale: Advancing feeds is contraindicated and oral aminoglycosides are not indicated for established NEC; bowel rest with parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics is standard, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bowel resection for suspected NEC-related intestinal perforation today
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is indicated for pneumoperitoneum, fixed bowel loop, palpable mass or clinical deterioration despite medical therapy; this baby has Bell IIA disease without perforation and warrants medical treatment, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

options.text: Cease feeds, large-bore OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, fluid resuscitation, surgical review
options.rationale: Bell stage IIA NEC (definite, with pneumatosis intestinalis) is managed medically with bowel rest, orogastric decompression, parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negative and anaerobic organisms (benzylpenicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole) and supportive care, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Cease feeds, give a single dose of IV cefotaxime and continue monitoring without surgical review
options.rationale: Monotherapy with cefotaxime does not adequately cover anaerobes, and surgical review is mandatory at diagnosis to allow timely intervention if deterioration occurs, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: NEC peaks in preterm infants in the second week of life. Bell staging: I suspected; IIA definite with pneumatosis intestinalis; IIB with portal venous gas or ascites; IIIA severe medical disease; IIIB perforation. IIA management is bowel rest, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (benzylpenicillin or ampicillin, gentamicin, metronidazole) for 7 to 10 days, parenteral nutrition, and serial AXR every 6 to 12 hours.
key_points: Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic for NEC
Portal venous gas suggests advanced disease (Bell IIB)
Free intraperitoneal air on lateral decubitus or cross-table lateral indicates perforation and surgery
Triple antibiotic cover for Gram-negative and anaerobic organisms
explanation.pearls: Falling platelet count and rising lactate are markers of deterioration and prompt surgical review even without radiographic perforation.

q.stem: A term female infant is born at 38+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth with a birthweight of 2360 g (below the 3rd centile), a head circumference of 31.5 cm (below the 3rd centile), and scattered petechiae on the trunk and limbs noted on first examination. APGARs were 8 and 9 and no resuscitation was required. The mother is a 24-year-old preschool teacher who had a flu-like illness with malaise, sore throat and mild posterior cervical lymphadenopathy at around 14 weeks gestation; the GP attributed it to a viral URTI and routine maternal serology was not repeated and TORCH screening was not done. There is no family history of hearing loss or congenital infection, no maternal HIV or syphilis, and rubella immunity was documented at booking. On day 2 of life, the baby has a moderately enlarged liver palpable 3 cm below the costal margin, a spleen tip palpable, mild conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and thrombocytopenia on FBC. A cranial ultrasound performed by the neonatologist shows scattered periventricular calcifications and mild ventricular dilatation, prompting paediatric infectious diseases review. Initial automated otoacoustic emission screen on day 2 is REFER bilaterally and is repeated next day with the same result. Audiology brainstem testing is pending. The treating team obtains urine and saliva PCR samples within the first week of life to confirm the suspected diagnosis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: SGA, microcephaly, petechiae on trunk, hepatomegaly 3 cm, mild jaundice
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 62 x10^9/L, total bilirubin 110 micromol/L with conjugated fraction 35 micromol/L, ALT 90 U/L, AST 120 U/L
data_table.Imaging: Cranial ultrasound: periventricular calcifications, mild ventricular dilatation
data_table.Microbiology: Saliva and urine CMV PCR collected on day 3 of life

q.lead_in: Once congenital CMV is confirmed by PCR within 21 days of life, what is the single most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg twice daily for 6 months with weekly FBC/LFT and audiology
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV with end-organ involvement (microcephaly, hepatitis, thrombocytopenia, intracranial calcifications, or sensorineural hearing loss) treated with oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BD for 6 months improves hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes; haematology and LFT monitoring is required because of neutropenia and hepatotoxicity, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV and AAP Red Book.

options.text: Observe with serial audiology, no antiviral, treat only confirmed progressive hearing loss after 12 months
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV warrants antiviral therapy promptly; delaying until 12 months misses the window when valganciclovir improves outcomes, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.

options.text: Commence IV ganciclovir 6 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks followed by no further therapy
options.rationale: Six weeks of IV ganciclovir was the older regimen and has been superseded by 6 months of oral valganciclovir which is non-inferior with better neurodevelopmental and audiological outcomes, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

options.text: CMV hyperimmune globulin infusion and arrange paediatric infectious diseases service review today
options.rationale: CMV hyperimmune globulin is not standard postnatal therapy for symptomatic congenital CMV; oral valganciclovir is the recommended treatment, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

options.text: Treat only audiological component with hearing aid fitting after ABR; provide no antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Hearing rehabilitation is part of care but does not replace antiviral therapy, which itself improves hearing trajectories in symptomatic infants, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.

explanation.summary: Congenital CMV is the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss in children. Diagnosis requires viral detection (urine or saliva PCR) within the first 21 days of life to distinguish from postnatal acquisition. Symptomatic infants (CNS, hepatic, haematological or SNHL) benefit from 6 months of oral valganciclovir, with regular FBC for neutropenia and LFTs. All affected children need longitudinal audiology because hearing loss can be late-onset or progressive.
key_points: Saliva PCR is the first-line screening test; urine PCR confirms
Diagnostic window is the first 21 days of life
Treat symptomatic disease with valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BD for 6 months
Monitor for neutropenia, anaemia and transaminitis on treatment
explanation.pearls: Asymptomatic congenital CMV with isolated SNHL is an area of active research; many AU centres treat with valganciclovir, but practice varies.

q.stem: A male infant is born at 40+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for placental abruption after a sentinel maternal collapse 25 minutes before delivery; the mother presented to a regional birthing unit with sudden severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding and was transferred urgently to theatre when the CTG showed prolonged bradycardia at 60 bpm. Estimated time of insult to delivery is 22 minutes. APGARs were 1 at 1 minute, 3 at 5 minutes and 5 at 10 minutes. Cord arterial gas shows pH 6.92, base deficit 18 mmol/L, and lactate 11 mmol/L. He required positive pressure ventilation from birth, intubation at 4 minutes for ongoing bradycardia, and three doses of intratracheal then intravenous adrenaline 10 microg/kg via emergency umbilical venous catheter before return of spontaneous circulation at 8 minutes. He is now 45 minutes old, intubated on conventional ventilation in the NICU at a tertiary centre with active servo-controlled cooling preparations underway, including a cooling mattress and oesophageal probe. He has reduced spontaneous activity with intermittent generalised tonic posturing of the limbs lasting around 20 seconds, sluggish pupils that are 3 mm and equally reactive, a weak suck and absent gag, and an exaggerated Moro reflex with prolonged opening. His axillary temperature on arrival was 36.4 deg C and the team has held active warming, allowing passive cooling, while preparing servo-controlled cooling. There is no congenital anomaly, no major birth trauma, and no clinical evidence of bleeding. He weighs 3540 g and has no surgical contraindication. Imaging is pending. The treating consultant must decide on definitive neuroprotective therapy in the next 15 minutes, well within the 6 hour therapeutic window from birth.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 deg C axillary on arrival, HR 130 bpm post-resuscitation, mean BP 38 mmHg on dobutamine 5 microg/kg/min, SpO2 95 percent on FiO2 0.4
data_table.Examination: Reduced activity, intermittent tonic posturing, weak suck, absent gag, exaggerated Moro, hypotonia in limbs, sluggish pupils, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Cord gas: Arterial pH 6.92, base deficit 18 mmol/L, lactate 11 mmol/L

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Permit passive cooling to 35.5 deg C until 24 hours of age then rewarm over 12 hours
options.rationale: Passive cooling to 35.5 deg C does not provide the target therapeutic range; servo-controlled cooling to 33 to 34 deg C is required to achieve neuroprotection, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

options.text: Maintain strict normothermia 36.5-37.5 deg C and commence prophylactic phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading dose
options.rationale: Normothermia is not the goal in confirmed moderate or severe HIE within 6 hours of birth; whole-body therapeutic hypothermia is the evidence-based neuroprotective therapy, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

options.text: Whole-body therapeutic hypothermia 33-34 deg C for 72 hours with aEEG, rewarm 12 hours, phenobarbitone for seizures
options.rationale: Term or near-term infants (at least 35 weeks gestation) within 6 hours of birth with evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia and moderate to severe encephalopathy (Sarnat 2 or 3) meet criteria for whole-body cooling to 33 to 34 deg C for 72 hours, with rewarming at 0.5 deg C per hour, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for clinical seizures, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Initiate selective head cooling with cap to scalp temperature 28 deg C for 48 hours and rapid rewarming
options.rationale: Selective head cooling is an alternative technique but the duration is 72 hours not 48, and Australian and New Zealand units predominantly use whole-body cooling because of monitoring convenience and equivalent outcomes, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

options.text: Delay cooling for 12 hours pending MRI brain, commence hypothermia only if injury confirmed
options.rationale: Cooling must start within 6 hours of birth to achieve neuroprotective effect; waiting for MRI misses the therapeutic window and is contraindicated, per Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

explanation.summary: Therapeutic hypothermia (whole-body or selective head cooling) reduces death and major neurodevelopmental disability in moderate to severe neonatal encephalopathy. Eligibility: at least 35 weeks gestation, age less than 6 hours, evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia (cord pH 7.0 or below, base deficit 16 or higher, or APGAR 5 or less at 10 minutes, or ongoing resuscitation), and clinical or aEEG evidence of moderate or severe encephalopathy (Sarnat 2 or 3). Target temperature 33 to 34 deg C for 72 hours, rewarm at 0.5 deg C per hour.
key_points: Window: less than 6 hours of birth
Sarnat 1 (mild) does not require cooling; Sarnat 2 to 3 do
Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for neonatal seizures
Side effects of cooling: sinus bradycardia, thrombocytopenia, subcutaneous fat necrosis
explanation.pearls: Apparent shivering and decreased seizure threshold during rewarming are recognised; rewarm slowly and monitor aEEG closely.

q.stem: A male infant is born at 40+3 weeks after a labour complicated by thick meconium-stained liquor and a non-reassuring CTG with late decelerations in the second stage. Birthweight is 3680 g. APGARs were 4 at 1 minute and 7 at 5 minutes. He required brief positive pressure ventilation in the delivery room and is now 6 hours old in the level 3 NICU at a tertiary perinatal centre, on conventional ventilation with FiO2 0.85, peak inspiratory pressure 24 cm H2O, PEEP 6 cm H2O, mean airway pressure 14 cm H2O, and an oxygenation index of 22 calculated from an arterial blood gas with PaO2 50 mmHg. Preductal SpO2 measured on the right hand is 92 percent and postductal SpO2 on the right foot is 78 percent, a sustained difference of 14 percent. Chest radiograph shows patchy coarse infiltrates with hyperinflation consistent with meconium aspiration. Urgent echocardiography by the neonatal cardiologist shows right-to-left shunting across the patent ductus arteriosus, tricuspid regurgitation with an estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 70 mmHg, septal flattening at end-systole, and structurally normal cardiac anatomy with normal pulmonary venous return on detailed views. Surfactant 100 mg/kg has already been given via the endotracheal tube. He has a normal capillary refill of 2 seconds on milrinone 0.5 microg/kg/min and dobutamine 5 microg/kg/min, with a mean arterial pressure of 42 mmHg. There is no pneumothorax on transillumination or repeat CXR.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, mean BP 42 mmHg on inotropes, preductal SpO2 92 percent, postductal SpO2 78 percent, T 36.9 deg C
data_table.Ventilation: Conventional ventilation, FiO2 0.85, mean airway pressure 14 cm H2O, oxygenation index 22
data_table.Echocardiography: Right-to-left ductal shunt, RVSP 70 mmHg, septal flattening, structurally normal heart, normal pulmonary veins

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: Prostaglandin E1 is used to maintain ductal patency in duct-dependent CHD; structurally normal heart with PPHN does not benefit, and PGE1 can worsen pulmonary congestion, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm via ventilator and reassess oxygenation index after 30-60 min
options.rationale: PPHN with an oxygenation index above 20 in a term infant after optimisation of ventilation and surfactant warrants inhaled nitric oxide at a starting dose of 20 ppm; if response is inadequate or OI rises despite iNO, ECMO referral should be considered, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn and BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation immediately and avoid pulmonary vasodilators today
options.rationale: HFOV is a useful adjunct to recruit atelectatic lung in MAS and may be combined with iNO, but it does not replace pulmonary vasodilator therapy in established PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Refer immediately for emergency ECMO without commencing iNO or HFOV trial
options.rationale: ECMO is reserved for refractory PPHN with OI persistently above 25 to 40 despite iNO and HFOV; bypassing iNO at OI 22 is premature, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Commence oral sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg every 6 hours as primary pulmonary vasodilator therapy
options.rationale: Oral sildenafil is used as adjunctive or weaning therapy after iNO; it is not first-line in acute severe PPHN where rapid pulmonary vasodilatation with iNO is required, per BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.

explanation.summary: PPHN presents as hypoxaemia disproportionate to lung disease with a preductal-postductal SpO2 difference of 10 percent or more and echocardiographic right-to-left shunting and elevated pulmonary pressures. Treatment ladder is optimise ventilation and lung recruitment, correct acidosis, ensure adequate systemic BP, then add iNO at 20 ppm. HFOV combines well with iNO. Sildenafil and milrinone are adjuncts. ECMO is reserved for refractory cases with OI persistently above 25 to 40.
key_points: Differential cyanosis (preductal greater than postductal SpO2) suggests PPHN or coarctation
Echo must confirm shunt physiology and exclude structural heart disease before iNO
iNO 20 ppm is the standard starting dose
ECMO criteria: OI greater than 25 to 40 despite iNO and HFOV, plus reversible cause
explanation.pearls: Always exclude infradiaphragmatic obstructed total anomalous pulmonary venous return on echo before attributing cyanosis to PPHN.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

q.stem: A term male infant born at 39+6 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth to a 27-year-old G1P0 woman is now 36 hours old on the postnatal ward of a tertiary maternity hospital. APGARs were 9 and 9 and the perinatal course was uncomplicated with no maternal infection, no chorioamnionitis, no prolonged ROM, no instrumental delivery, no resuscitation and a normal cord gas (arterial pH 7.32, base deficit 4 mmol/L). Birthweight was 3380 g. He has been breastfeeding well with audible swallows, with normal heelprick BSLs of 3.0 to 4.5 mmol/L throughout, and has had three wet nappies and meconium passed at 8 hours. There is no family history of consanguinity, neonatal seizures or sudden infant death. At 36 hours he develops sudden onset rhythmic jerking of the right arm and right leg lasting around 90 seconds, with lip smacking and a brief apnoea with desaturation to 88 percent, followed by a similar focal motor seizure 20 minutes later. He is now post-ictal with reduced tone in the right limbs but rouseable to handling. Examination reveals no dysmorphic features, no rash, no neurocutaneous stigmata (no cafe-au-lait or hypopigmented macules under Wood's lamp), and a normal anterior fontanelle. He has not been cooled and has no risk factors for HIE. Sodium is 138 mmol/L, ionised calcium 1.25 mmol/L, magnesium 0.8 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L, ammonia 60 micromol/L, blood gas pH 7.36 with normal lactate, and a sepsis screen including blood culture, FBC, CRP and lumbar puncture is in progress. EEG and cranial ultrasound are being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 158 bpm, RR 50 /min, SpO2 98 percent on room air, BP 70/42 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, reduced tone right side, rouseable, no dysmorphism, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Na 138 mmol/L, ionised Ca 1.25 mmol/L, Mg 0.8 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L, ammonia 60 micromol/L, pH 7.36, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, FBC and CRP normal pending culture

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg and observe for cessation of seizure activity
options.rationale: Diazepam is not the first-line agent in neonatal seizures and rectal administration is unreliable; phenobarbitone IV is preferred for sustained levels and proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Give intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg empirically for presumed hypocalcaemic seizure
options.rationale: The ionised calcium of 1.25 mmol/L is within the normal range; empirical calcium is not indicated and can cause arrhythmia if extravasated, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Commence pyridoxine 100 mg IV and withhold antiepileptic therapy until response is assessed
options.rationale: Pyridoxine is reserved for refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to standard antiepileptics; it should not delay first-line phenobarbitone, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg, blood/urine for metabolic and infection screen, LP, urgent MRI
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for confirmed neonatal seizures in Australian protocols; concurrently the cause must be sought (sepsis, metabolic, intracranial bleed or infarct, structural). MRI is preferred for parenchymal injury and stroke detection, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures and Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology.

options.text: Initiate therapeutic hypothermia at 33.5 deg C for 72 hours as initial seizure therapy
options.rationale: This baby does not meet cooling criteria; he had no perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, normal cord gas and normal APGARs, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

explanation.summary: Causes of neonatal seizures, in rough order of frequency: HIE, ischaemic stroke and intracranial haemorrhage, infection (meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis), metabolic (hypoglycaemia, hypocalcaemia, hyponatraemia, inborn errors), structural, and pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy. A term infant without perinatal hypoxia and with normal initial bloods should prompt urgent MRI and LP. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is the first-line antiepileptic in Australia.
key_points: Subtle seizures (eye deviation, cycling, lip smacking, apnoea) are common in neonates
First-line: phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV load, repeat 10 mg/kg up to total 40 mg/kg
Second-line: levetiracetam or phenytoin; pyridoxine for refractory
Always seek the cause: imaging, LP, metabolic screen, glucose, electrolytes, ammonia
explanation.pearls: Perinatal arterial ischaemic stroke commonly presents as focal seizures in the first 72 hours of life in a previously well term baby; MRI with DWI is diagnostic.

q.stem: A male infant is born at 32+0 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 28-year-old G1P0 woman with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes 5 days before delivery. She was admitted for monitoring, received prophylactic erythromycin and a course of antenatal corticosteroids; specifically, two doses of antenatal betamethasone with the second dose 48 hours before delivery, and a single dose of magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection commenced 4 hours before delivery. There was no maternal fever, the CRP remained normal, and the white cell count was 11 x10^9/L pre-delivery. APGARs were 7 and 8 with brief CPAP via T-piece in the delivery room. Birthweight is 1640 g (50th centile for gestation). He is transferred to the level 3 NICU at 10 minutes of age. From birth he has had moderate work of breathing with intercostal recession, mild grunting, and SpO2 of 89 percent in room air. He is now on nasal CPAP 7 cm H2O with FiO2 0.35 delivered via short binasal prongs, has mild grunting that is gradually improving, and is haemodynamically stable with a mean BP of 38 mmHg. The team prepares to administer surfactant in the next 30 minutes. There is no antenatal evidence of infection. He has not yet had a chest radiograph.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 160 bpm, RR 65 /min on CPAP, SpO2 92 percent on FiO2 0.35, BP mean 38 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild grunting and intercostal recession, equal air entry, capillary refill 2 seconds, no peripheral cyanosis
data_table.Antenatal: Single course antenatal betamethasone 48 hours pre-delivery, MgSO4 for neuroprotection, no maternal fever, no chorioamnionitis

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surfactant administration strategy in this infant?

options.text: Continue CPAP without surfactant unless FiO2 rises above 0.6 or work of breathing worsens
options.rationale: Threshold for surfactant in preterm RDS in many AU units is sustained FiO2 above 0.3 (under 28 weeks) or above 0.3 to 0.4 (28 to 32 weeks); ignoring an FiO2 of 0.35 in a 32-week infant with grunting risks delayed treatment, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Intubate, give surfactant, and continue mechanical ventilation for at least the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Prolonged ventilation after surfactant increases the risk of bronchopulmonary dysplasia in a haemodynamically stable preterm infant who is breathing spontaneously; less invasive strategies are preferred, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Repeat a second course of antenatal corticosteroid postnatally as IM betamethasone to the infant to enhance lung maturation
options.rationale: Postnatal corticosteroids in this context are inappropriate; antenatal corticosteroids must be given to the mother before delivery, and postnatal dexamethasone is reserved for ventilator-dependent infants at risk of BPD, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Give nebulised surfactant via the CPAP circuit at 200 mg/kg over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised surfactant remains investigational and is not currently recommended outside trials in Australia; tracheal delivery is required for proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Continue CPAP and give surfactant via thin catheter using LISA technique with spontaneous breathing
options.rationale: In a preterm infant breathing spontaneously on CPAP, LISA (also called MIST in some AU centres) allows surfactant administration via a thin tracheal catheter while CPAP and spontaneous breathing continue, and reduces the rate of mechanical ventilation and BPD compared with INSURE, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration and Cochrane: LISA versus INSURE.

explanation.summary: Preterm RDS is managed primarily with antenatal corticosteroids, early CPAP from delivery room, and surfactant delivered to those needing it. LISA delivers surfactant via a thin catheter while the infant continues spontaneous breathing on CPAP, reducing mechanical ventilation and bronchopulmonary dysplasia compared with INSURE (intubate, surfactant, extubate). Threshold for surfactant is typically FiO2 sustained above 0.3 (less than 28 weeks) or 0.3 to 0.4 (28 to 32 weeks).
key_points: Antenatal betamethasone halves RDS, IVH and neonatal mortality
Early CPAP at 6 to 8 cm H2O is first-line respiratory support
LISA / MIST reduces BPD versus INSURE in spontaneously breathing infants
Surfactant dose: poractant alfa 200 mg/kg initial; beractant 100 mg/kg
explanation.pearls: A second course of antenatal corticosteroids is considered if more than 7 to 14 days have elapsed and delivery remains likely within 7 days at gestation under 32 weeks.

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to a paediatric cardiology outpatient clinic after two episodes of exertional syncope. The first occurred while sprinting at school athletics, with a brief loss of consciousness during which her friends say her limbs jerked for around 10 seconds before she came round confused for 30 seconds. The second was while jumping in a pool 6 weeks later. There is no history of typical post-ictal phase, tongue biting, or incontinence. A maternal uncle died suddenly in his 20s while swimming; the family was told it was drowning. She takes no regular medications and has never used a QT-prolonging drug. Examination is unremarkable with normal heart sounds, no murmur, and normal blood pressure. A resting 12-lead ECG at the clinic is shown to a paediatric electrophysiologist. Manual measurement and Bazett correction across multiple leads in the same patient give a QTc of 510 ms, with broad-based notched T-waves in the precordial leads. She has not previously had any cardiac investigations or family screening, she takes no over-the-counter or herbal medications, and her resting heart rate today is 58 with no postural drop. There is no history of hearing impairment in the family.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72 bpm, BP 112/68 mmHg, BMI 21
data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm 72 bpm, QTc 510 ms (Bazett), broad-based notched T waves V2 to V4, no delta wave
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle sudden death in 20s while swimming, no other known cardiac disease
data_table.Triggers: Both episodes occurred during exertion (sprint, swimming)

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassurance, electrolyte replacement, and follow-up ECG in 12 months
options.rationale: QTc above 480 ms with exertional syncope and a suggestive family history meets diagnostic criteria for LQTS; reassurance alone is inappropriate, per Heart Rhythm Society and AHA expert consensus.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Non-selective beta-blocker propranolol, exercise restriction, cascade ECG screening of relatives
options.rationale: LQTS with syncope is high-risk; non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol or nadolol) reduce events, exercise restriction is recommended particularly for swimming in LQT1, and first-degree relatives need cascade screening with ECG +/- genetic testing, per AHA/HRS consensus and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily for QT shortening
options.rationale: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-selective beta-blockers without QT-prolonging effect (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred, per AMH and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Immediate implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) implantation as first-line therapy
options.rationale: ICD is reserved for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification, per AHA/HRS consensus.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Commence levetiracetam after referral to a neurologist for presumed seizures
options.rationale: Although hypoxic limb jerking can mimic seizures, the exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point firmly to LQTS not epilepsy, per BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

explanation.summary: Diagnostic QTc cut-offs: a single resting QTc > 480 ms with unexplained syncope, or > 500 ms without symptoms, supports LQTS (Schwartz score also used). Management includes activity modification (especially swimming/competitive sport in LQT1), avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte derangement, non-selective beta-blockade (propranolol or nadolol), genetic testing, cascade family screening, and ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough events.
key_points: QTc > 480 ms with syncope or > 500 ms asymptomatic suggests LQTS
LQT1: exertion (swimming); LQT2: auditory/emotion; LQT3: rest/sleep
First-line: nadolol or propranolol
Avoid sotalol and other QT-prolonging drugs
Cascade screening of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: A sudden death in a young relative while swimming is classic for LQT1; warn the family about both medication and electrolyte triggers and provide a written drug-avoidance list.

sources.label: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

q.stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed at a regional hospital after 10 days of migratory polyarthritis affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists in sequence over the preceding week. Each joint has been hot, painful, and swollen for 2 to 3 days before migrating to another joint, with dramatic and rapid resolution. She also had a sore throat 4 weeks ago that resolved without antibiotic treatment, and a throat swab taken at that time was not collected. She has no prior diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever and no known rheumatic heart disease on previous school-based screening echocardiograms performed at age 7. She has had no rash and no subcutaneous nodules detected on careful palpation of extensor surfaces. She has not had a documented fever during her current presentation, though her mother reports a febrile illness during the recent pharyngitis. There is no carditis clinically and a transthoracic echocardiogram performed today shows a structurally normal heart with no valvular regurgitation or pericardial effusion. ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with a PR interval of 220 ms (above the age-related upper limit). Anti-streptolysin O titre is 540 IU/mL and anti-DNase B is 380 U/mL, both elevated above the laboratory reference range. C-reactive protein is 60 mg/L, ESR is 65 mm/h, and a full blood count is otherwise unremarkable. The treating team is applying the 2020 RHDAustralia guideline using the criteria for high-risk populations.

data_table.Clinical: Migratory polyarthritis (knees, ankles, wrists), no carditis, no chorea, no subcutaneous nodules, no erythema marginatum, afebrile
data_table.Investigations: ASOT 540 IU/mL, anti-DNase B 380 U/mL, CRP 60 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/h, ECG PR 220 ms (above age threshold), echo normal
data_table.Background: Remote NT community, high-risk population, no prior ARF or RHD, sore throat 4 weeks ago

q.lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether this presentation meets the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria for an initial episode of acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population?

options.text: Does not meet criteria because she is afebrile and fever is mandatory for diagnosis
options.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with evidence of recent GAS infection, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because echocardiogram is normal and subclinical carditis is required
options.rationale: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence of carditis does not exclude ARF when other majors and minors are present, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets definite initial ARF: polyarthritis (major in high-risk), prolonged PR and elevated APRs (minors), plus recent GAS
options.rationale: Under the 2020 RHDAustralia high-risk criteria, polyarthritis is a major; she has 1 major (polyarthritis) plus 3 minors (prolonged PR for age, raised CRP and ESR) plus serological evidence of recent GAS (raised ASOT and anti-DNase B), satisfying the 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence threshold for definite initial ARF, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets criteria only as probable ARF because monoarthritis is required in high-risk populations
options.rationale: In high-risk populations, monoarthritis or polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major and yields definite, not probable, ARF when criteria are otherwise met, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because anti-DNase B is non-specific and only ASOT counts as GAS evidence
options.rationale: Insufficient information is incorrect: the diagnostic criteria have been explicitly addressed (1 major + 2 minors with GAS evidence) which suffices for a definite initial ARF diagnosis in a high-risk population (2020 RHDAustralia guideline).

explanation.summary: The 2020 RHDAustralia guideline differs from low-risk Jones in two key ways: in high-risk populations, monoarthritis and polyarthralgia are also majors (in addition to polyarthritis), and lower thresholds apply for fever (38 deg C) and ESR (30 mm/h). All groups need evidence of preceding GAS infection (ASOT, anti-DNase B, throat swab, or rapid antigen). Initial ARF requires 2 majors or 1 major + 2 minors plus GAS evidence.
key_points: High-risk Australia: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, Maori/Pacific Islander, immigrants from endemic areas
Majors high-risk: carditis (incl. subclinical), polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Minors high-risk: fever >= 38 deg C, ESR >= 30 mm/h or CRP elevated, prolonged PR for age, monoarthralgia
Recurrence: 2 majors, OR 1 major + 2 minors, OR 3 minors + GAS evidence
Treat: benzathine penicillin G IM and secondary prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: Always check the population risk first; using low-risk criteria in a high-risk patient will under-diagnose and miss the opportunity for secondary prophylaxis.

q.stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur identified at preschool entry assessment. He was born at term by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery with a birthweight of 3.2 kg, but his height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on the 10th centile, despite a mid-parental height on the 50th centile. His developmental milestones have been mildly delayed: he sat unsupported at 9 months, walked unaided at 18 months, and now uses only 2 to 3 word phrases at age 3. He has not had any significant illnesses and has been fully immunised. On examination he has hypertelorism with downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck with low posterior hairline, and a pectus carinatum superiorly with pectus excavatum inferiorly. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge, loudest on inspiration, with a soft single S2 and no thrill; the murmur does not radiate to the carotids. There is no left ventricular heave and the apex is not displaced. Blood pressure is symmetric in both arms at 96/58 mmHg and femoral pulses are present and equal. Echocardiogram performed by the paediatric cardiology team reveals a thickened, dysplastic pulmonary valve with a peak gradient of 45 mmHg, mild post-stenotic dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, and no left ventricular hypertrophy. Genetic testing for RASopathy genes via a multi-gene panel is pending.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck, pectus carinatum superior/excavatum inferior
data_table.Growth: Height 3rd centile, weight 10th centile, mid-parental height 50th centile
data_table.Cardiac: 3/6 ESM ULSE louder on inspiration, soft single S2, no thrill, no LVH, symmetric arm BPs
data_table.Echo: Dysplastic pulmonary valve, peak gradient 45 mmHg, no LV hypertrophy
data_table.Development: Walked 18 months, 2 to 3 word phrases, mild delay

q.lead_in: Which single genetic syndrome best fits this clinical picture?

options.text: Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion)
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis with a right-upper sternal edge murmur and elfin facies; this child has pulmonary stenosis and Noonan facies, per BMJ Best Practice: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Turner syndrome (45,X)
options.rationale: Turner syndrome occurs in phenotypic females with bicuspid AV and coarctation; this patient is male with pulmonary stenosis, per BMJ Best Practice: Turner syndrome.

options.text: Noonan syndrome (PTPN11 mutation)
options.rationale: Noonan syndrome features dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis (most common cardiac lesion in approximately 50%, with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in around 20%), short stature, hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, webbed neck, pectus deformity, and developmental delay; PTPN11 mutations account for about half of cases, per BMJ Best Practice: Noonan syndrome and RCH CPG.

options.text: Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation)
options.rationale: Marfan features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation, not short stature, webbed neck or pulmonary stenosis, per the revised Ghent criteria.

options.text: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)
options.rationale: T21 features upslanting fissures, brachycephaly, single palmar crease and AVSD, not pulmonary stenosis or downslanting fissures, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

explanation.summary: Noonan syndrome is an autosomal dominant RASopathy with variable expression. About 50% have pulmonary valve stenosis (often dysplastic and resistant to balloon valvuloplasty) and 20% have hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Short stature, characteristic facies (hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck), pectus deformity, mild intellectual disability, cryptorchidism, and bleeding diathesis are typical. Half the cases are due to PTPN11 mutations.
key_points: PTPN11 in approximately 50%; other RAS-MAPK genes (SOS1, RAF1, KRAS) account for most others
Dysplastic pulmonary stenosis is the cardiac hallmark
HCM in 20%; can present as infant heart failure or sudden cardiac death
Growth hormone is approved for short stature
Echo and ECG at diagnosis and periodically
explanation.pearls: A boy with the Turner phenotype is the classic teaching cue for Noonan syndrome (formerly mis-called male Turner).

q.subtopic_detail: Genetics

q.stem: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric cardiology clinic by his GP because of a tall, thin habitus and a recently identified murmur during a sports pre-participation examination. He has a height on the 99th centile and an arm span 7 cm greater than his height, with a reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio. He has long, slender fingers with positive Steinberg (thumb) and Walker-Murdoch (wrist) signs bilaterally. There is significant pectus excavatum requiring surgical consideration, a high-arched palate with dental crowding requiring orthodontic intervention, and mild scoliosis with Cobb angle 22 degrees on standing radiograph. An ophthalmology slit-lamp examination performed last month demonstrates bilateral superior ectopia lentis with mild myopia. Echocardiogram performed today shows aortic root dilatation at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.4 cm, corresponding to a Z-score of 3.8 indexed to body surface area, with mild aortic regurgitation and no evidence of dissection. The remaining cardiac anatomy is normal. His father died suddenly aged 38 from a presumed dissection of the ascending aorta while playing tennis; no formal post-mortem genetic testing was performed at the time. He currently plays competitive rugby league at school and trains four times per week with intensive resistance work. There is no history of joint dislocation or skin hyperextensibility. He has no learning difficulties, no recent syncope, and no exertional chest pain.

data_table.Skeletal: Height 99th centile, arm span > height by 7 cm, positive thumb (Steinberg) and wrist (Walker-Murdoch) signs, pectus excavatum, scoliosis (Cobb 22 deg), high-arched palate with dental crowding
data_table.Ocular: Bilateral superior ectopia lentis on slit lamp
data_table.Cardiovascular: Aortic root 4.4 cm at sinuses of Valsalva, Z-score 3.8, mild aortic regurgitation
data_table.Family history: Father died at 38 of presumed aortic dissection

q.lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure as athletic build, continue rugby league, and arrange repeat echo in 5 years
options.rationale: He meets revised Ghent criteria for Marfan syndrome (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score >= 2 with family history is more than sufficient); reassurance and continuation of high-impact contact sport is unsafe, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily and clear him to play competitive contact sport
options.rationale: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan syndrome; beta-blocker (atenolol or propranolol) or losartan is used to slow aortic dilatation, and contact and competitive sport should be avoided, per AHA scientific statement on aortopathies.
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Refer for immediate elective ascending aortic root replacement irrespective of Z-score
options.rationale: Elective root replacement is generally indicated at root diameter >= 5.0 cm in adults or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection or rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with a Z-score of 3.8 medical therapy and ongoing surveillance are first-line, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Atenolol or losartan, restrict competitive sport, annual echo and ophthalmology, FBN1 cascade screening
options.rationale: Marfan syndrome with aortic root dilatation requires medical therapy to slow root growth (beta-blocker or losartan, with evidence supporting both), restriction from competitive contact and isometric sport, annual echo surveillance, ophthalmology review for ectopia lentis and retinal complications, and cascade screening of first-degree relatives with FBN1 testing, per BMJ Best Practice and revised Ghent nosology.
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Treat with ramipril 5 mg daily and clear him for non-contact competitive swimming
options.rationale: Losartan (angiotensin receptor blocker), not ramipril, is the agent shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is also restricted, per BMJ Best Practice.

explanation.summary: The revised (2010) Ghent nosology diagnoses Marfan syndrome via various combinations of aortic root dilatation, ectopia lentis, FBN1 mutation, family history, and systemic features (systemic score >= 7). This patient meets criteria multiple ways. Management centres on slowing aortic dilatation with beta-blocker or losartan, lifestyle modification, annual echo, ophthalmology review for lens and retinal pathology, and prophylactic root replacement at threshold diameter (commonly >= 5.0 cm, or >= 4.5 cm with adverse features).
key_points: Revised Ghent (2010): aortic root Z >= 2 plus ectopia lentis is sufficient
Beta-blocker (atenolol) or losartan slows root growth
Restrict competitive contact and isometric sport
Annual echo plus ophthalmology
Cascade FBN1 testing of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: An aortic root Z-score in childhood is more meaningful than absolute diameter because of body size variability.

sources.label: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

q.subtopic_detail: Genetics

q.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary developmental paediatrician for assessment of intellectual disability and behavioural difficulties that have escalated over the past school year. His mother reports global developmental delay first noted at 18 months of age when he had only 2 words and was unable to follow simple instructions. He now attends a mainstream school with full-time aide support but has a recent Wechsler full-scale IQ of 58, placing him in the moderate intellectual disability range. He has marked attentional difficulties consistent with comorbid ADHD, hand-flapping when excited, persistently poor eye contact, perseverative speech with echolalia, and tactile hypersensitivity to clothing labels and certain food textures. He has had no seizures and has not lost previously acquired skills. On examination today he has a long narrow face with prominent forehead, large protruding ears, a high-arched palate, hyperextensible finger joints with thumbs that easily reach the volar forearm, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, and bilateral pes planus. Testicular examination shows symmetrically enlarged testes for prepubertal age, already at a Prader orchidometer volume of 6 mL bilaterally. There is no microcephaly, no neurocutaneous stigmata, and no dysmorphic facial midline defects. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with mild intellectual disability and progressive ataxia who was diagnosed in his 60s with a late-onset intention tremor and gait disorder of unclear aetiology, and a maternal grandmother who experienced primary ovarian insufficiency with cessation of menses at age 36. Microarray comparative genomic hybridisation performed at age 4 was normal, as was a metabolic screen including very long chain fatty acids. The clinical geneticist is now considering targeted single-gene testing based on the clinical pattern.

data_table.Cognitive/behaviour: FSIQ 58, global delay, hand-flapping, poor eye contact, echolalia, tactile defensiveness, attentional difficulties
data_table.Examination: Long narrow face, prominent ears, high-arched palate, joint hypermobility, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, pes planus, prepubertal macroorchidism
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle late-onset tremor/ataxia diagnosed in 60s; maternal grandmother primary ovarian insufficiency at 36

q.lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely genetic diagnosis and the appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY); send a peripheral blood karyotype with chromosome enumeration
options.rationale: Klinefelter syndrome presents with tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism), gynaecomastia in adolescence, and milder cognitive impact, per BMJ Best Practice: Klinefelter syndrome.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

options.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for 7q11.23 elastin deletion
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features elfin facies, supravalvular AS, hypercalcaemia and an overfriendly personality; family history and macroorchidism do not fit, per RCH CPG: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; request DNA methylation analysis at the 15q11-q13 locus
options.rationale: Prader-Willi presents with neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia in childhood, obesity, hypogonadism (small testes), and short stature, with no macroorchidism or X-linked family pattern, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

options.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; request TSC1 and TSC2 gene sequencing
options.rationale: TSC presents with hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas, renal angiomyolipomas, cortical tubers and epilepsy; none of these features are present, per BMJ Best Practice: Tuberous sclerosis.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

options.text: Fragile X (FMR1 CGG expansion >200); request targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation
options.rationale: The combination of moderate intellectual disability, autistic features, long face with prominent ears, joint hypermobility, post-pubertal macroorchidism (here pre-pubertal but already noted), a maternal uncle with FXTAS (fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome from a premutation) and maternal grandmother with FXPOI (fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency) strongly indicates Fragile X (FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG); microarrays do not detect trinucleotide expansions and FMR1 targeted testing with methylation analysis is required, per RCH CPG: Fragile X and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

explanation.summary: Fragile X syndrome is the commonest inherited cause of intellectual disability and a leading single-gene cause of autism. It results from CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion (> 200) in the 5' untranslated region of FMR1 on Xq27.3, with methylation silencing the gene. Premutation carriers (55 to 200 repeats) can develop FXTAS (older men) or FXPOI (women under 40). Standard microarrays do not detect trinucleotide repeats; targeted FMR1 testing (PCR with methylation analysis or Southern blot) is required.
key_points: FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG, methylated, silenced
Long face, prominent ears, macroorchidism after puberty, joint hypermobility, autistic features
Premutation 55 to 200: FXTAS in older males, FXPOI in younger females
Microarray does not detect repeat expansions
Cascade testing across maternal line is essential
explanation.pearls: A combination of a boy with intellectual disability, an older male relative with late-onset ataxia/tremor, and a female relative with early menopause is essentially diagnostic of FMR1 pathology until proven otherwise.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

q.subtopic_detail: Genetics

q.stem: A 9-year-old boy weighing 30 kg is 6 hours into treatment of newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis in a paediatric high dependency unit at a tertiary children's hospital. His initial pH was 7.02 with HCO3 5 mmol/L, glucose 38 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L (corrected sodium 142 mmol/L), potassium 4.6 mmol/L and chloride 100 mmol/L. He received a single 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then maintenance fluid plus 48-hour deficit replacement using 0.9% saline with potassium chloride 40 mmol/L, and an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 90 minutes after fluids commenced. He has not received any sodium bicarbonate. Glucose is now 16 mmol/L, pH 7.18 and HCO3 11 mmol/L, and the nurses have just transitioned the fluid to 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose. Over the last 30 minutes the bedside nurse has noted progressively increasing irritability, a new complaint of headache, and now a single episode of non-bilious vomiting. On urgent reassessment his GCS has fallen from 15 to 11 (E3 V3 M5), with a heart rate that has slowed from 120 to 78 bpm and a rising blood pressure of 138/92 mmHg. Pupils are 4 mm and sluggish bilaterally with no focal motor deficit and no posturing. The bedside glucose is 14 mmol/L and the most recent potassium was 3.9 mmol/L. The consultant intensivist is en route from the ICU upstairs, the airway trolley with appropriately sized equipment is at the bedside, and 3% saline and mannitol are available in the resus bay.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm (down from 120), BP 138/92 mmHg (up from 108/66), RR 14 /min, SpO2 98% on RA, T 36.9 deg C, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5)
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, headache, one vomit, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally, no focal neurology, fontanelle closed, no papilloedema visible
data_table.Investigations: BSL 14 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, last VBG pH 7.18 HCO3 11, urine output 1.5 mL/kg/h

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to twice maintenance and rebolus 10 mL/kg of 0.9% saline to address suspected hypovolaemia
options.rationale: Bradycardia with hypertension and falling GCS is the Cushing response indicating raised intracranial pressure from cerebral oedema; fluid expansion worsens cerebral oedema, and the fluid rate must be reduced not increased per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion and give an IV bolus of 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose is 14 mmol/L and is not the cause of declining GCS; stopping insulin worsens ketosis, and the clinical picture points to cerebral oedema not hypoglycaemia per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg IV over 15 minutes, reduce IV fluid rate by a third, urgent CT brain
options.rationale: Suspected DKA-associated cerebral oedema (declining GCS, headache, Cushing response, vomiting) is treated immediately with hypertonic saline 2.7-3% at 2.5-5 mL/kg over 10-15 minutes (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduction of IV fluid rate by one third, head-up 30 degrees, and neuroimaging only after stabilisation per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and ISPAD 2022.

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg infused over 30 minutes to correct the residual acidosis safely
options.rationale: Bicarbonate administration is an independent risk factor for DKA-associated cerebral oedema and is not indicated; pH is already improving to 7.18 per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis as cause of the altered consciousness today
options.rationale: LP in raised intracranial pressure risks coning and would delay osmotherapy; the clinical picture (Cushing response in established DKA) is cerebral oedema per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

explanation.summary: DKA-associated cerebral oedema occurs in approximately 0.5-1% of paediatric DKA episodes, typically 4-12 hours into therapy, and has a 20-25% mortality. Treatment is immediate hypertonic saline 3% (2.5-5 mL/kg over 10-15 min) or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg over 15-20 min, head up 30 degrees, reduction of fluid rate by one third, and urgent intensivist input. CT is only after stabilisation.
key_points: Clinical diagnostic criteria (Muir): abnormal motor/verbal response, decorticate or decerebrate posturing, cranial nerve palsy, abnormal respiratory pattern
Hypertonic saline 3% 2.5-5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg
Reduce maintenance fluid rate by one third
Avoid hyperventilation below pCO2 35 mmHg unless herniating
explanation.pearls: A bradycardia in a child who was just tachycardic in DKA is cerebral oedema until proven otherwise. Treat first, image after.

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology - critical deterioration

q.stem: A 6-year-old girl is referred by her GP for breast development first noticed by her mother 8 months ago. Her mother also reports the recent appearance of fine axillary hair, mild adult-type body odour, and accelerated linear growth (she has crossed two centile lines on the height chart in the past year, from 50th to 90th centile). She has had no headaches, no visual symptoms, no seizures, no abdominal pain and no vaginal bleeding. There is no neonatal history of seizures or hypothalamic insult. There is no exposure to topical oestrogen, no parental medications she could be accessing, no oestrogen-containing supplements in the household, and no soy-heavy diet. Her past medical and developmental history is unremarkable and there is no family history of central or peripheral precocious puberty, McCune-Albright syndrome, neurofibromatosis or congenital adrenal hyperplasia. On examination she has Tanner stage 3 breast development with palpable glandular tissue, Tanner stage 2 pubic hair, fine axillary hair on inspection of the underarm, no acne, no cafe-au-lait macules, no neurofibromas and no clitoromegaly. The thyroid is not palpable, the abdomen is soft with no palpable masses and no organomegaly, and the neurological examination including fundoscopy and visual fields by confrontation is normal. Bone age performed today is 9 years. Pelvic ultrasound shows a uterine length of 4.2 cm with an endometrial echo and bilateral ovaries with multiple small follicles and no ovarian cyst or mass.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84 bpm, BP 102/62 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, height 90th centile, weight 75th centile
data_table.Examination: Tanner B3 P2, fine axillary hair, mild body odour, no acne, no clitoromegaly, no abdominal mass, no cafe-au-lait macules
data_table.Investigations: Bone age 9 (chronological 6), pelvic US: uterus 4.2 cm with endometrial echo, ovaries bilateral follicles

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation to distinguish central from peripheral precocious puberty?

options.text: Pelvic MRI to look for an ovarian sex cord-stromal tumour producing oestradiol
options.rationale: The pelvic ultrasound has already shown a pubertal pattern without an ovarian mass; pelvic MRI is reserved for ultrasound abnormality or suspected peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

options.text: GnRH (or analogue) stimulation test measuring LH and FSH at 0, 30 and 60 minutes
options.rationale: GnRH (or GnRH analogue) stimulation testing with peak LH >=5 IU/L (or LH:FSH ratio >0.66) confirms central precocious puberty, while a suppressed LH response with elevated oestradiol points to a peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty and ESPE consensus.

options.text: Serum 17-OH progesterone and DHEAS to exclude non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia
options.rationale: Non-classical CAH causes premature adrenarche (pubic and axillary hair, body odour) without breast development; this child has Tanner B3 breast development, indicating gonadarche per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Empirical commencement of a GnRH analogue (leuprolide) and monitor for breast regression
options.rationale: Treatment should not precede confirmation of the diagnosis and underlying cause (including MRI brain for central PP in a girl under 6-8 years) per APEG position statement.

options.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol to look for a hypothalamic hamartoma before any biochemical testing
options.rationale: MRI brain is performed once central precocious puberty is biochemically confirmed (or in girls under 6 with central features) but is not the first discriminating test between central and peripheral per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

explanation.summary: Precocious puberty in girls is breast development before age 8 (or pubic hair/menarche before age 8/9). Central PP is gonadotropin-dependent (HPG axis activation); peripheral PP is gonadotropin-independent (ovarian, adrenal, exogenous). GnRH stimulation distinguishes the two: peak LH >=5 IU/L indicates central. Central PP in girls <6 years mandates MRI brain.
key_points: Random LH alone has poor sensitivity; stimulation is the test
Pubertal pelvic ultrasound: uterus >3.5-4 cm, endometrial echo, multifollicular ovaries
Bone age advancement >2 years suggests significant oestrogen exposure
Treatment for confirmed central PP is GnRH analogue to preserve adult height
explanation.pearls: Isolated premature thelarche (no growth spurt, no bone age advance, prepubertal LH response) does not need treatment, just surveillance.

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology - puberty

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric endocrinology outpatient clinic by her GP with a 3-month history of progressive unintentional weight loss of 6 kg despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, fine tremor, palpitations, mild anxiety, oligomenorrhoea and poor sleep, and a recent decline in academic performance noted by her teachers. Her mother has noticed that her eyes appear more prominent and that she has a fine tremor of the outstretched hands and a tendency to fidget. There is no recent viral illness, no anterior neck pain, no exogenous thyroid hormone or iodine-containing medication exposure, and no family history of thyroid disease, although a maternal cousin has type 1 diabetes mellitus and her grandmother has vitiligo. On examination she has a smooth diffuse non-tender goitre approximately twice normal size with an audible bruit on auscultation, mild bilateral proptosis with lid lag and lid retraction, fine resting tremor, warm sweaty palms, and a resting heart rate of 124 bpm in sinus rhythm. There is no pretibial myxoedema and no thyroid acropachy. Her bloods show TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L (reference 9-19), free T3 18 pmol/L (reference 3.5-6.5), anti-TSH receptor antibody 12 IU/L (reference <1.75), and a normal full blood count including a neutrophil count of 4.2 x 10^9/L with normal LFTs.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124 bpm sinus, BP 138/72 mmHg with wide pulse pressure, T 37.4 deg C, RR 18 /min
data_table.Examination: Smooth goitre with bruit, mild bilateral proptosis with lid lag, fine tremor, warm sweaty palms, no pretibial myxoedema
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L, free T3 18 pmol/L, anti-TSHR Ab 12 IU/L, neutrophils 4.2 x 10^9/L, LFT normal

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line medical management?

options.text: Propylthiouracil 5-10 mg/kg/day orally as preferred thionamide due to peripheral T4-to-T3 block
options.rationale: PTU is contraindicated as first-line in paediatric Graves' due to a black-box warning for severe hepatotoxicity and acute liver failure; carbimazole is first-line per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Definitive radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation as initial therapy without a trial of thionamide
options.rationale: Initial therapy is medical with thionamide for 18-24 months; radioiodine is offered after relapse or in older adolescents with persistent disease per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism.

options.text: Total thyroidectomy under general anaesthesia as first-line definitive therapy now
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for thionamide failure or intolerance, very large goitre, or Graves' ophthalmopathy where radioiodine is unsuitable per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day orally in divided doses plus propranolol 1-2 mg/kg/day, monitor FBC/LFT
options.rationale: Australian and ESPE-recommended first-line therapy for paediatric Graves' disease is a thionamide (carbimazole preferred to propylthiouracil because of PTU hepatotoxicity in children) at 0.5-1 mg/kg/day with a beta-blocker for adrenergic symptoms, baseline and serial neutrophil and LFT monitoring per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism and Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: Lugol's iodine 5-10 drops three times daily as monotherapy until normalisation of free T4
options.rationale: Inorganic iodine is used pre-operatively or in thyroid storm but not as monotherapy for chronic Graves' management per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

explanation.summary: Paediatric Graves' disease accounts for approximately 95% of paediatric hyperthyroidism, with a strong female predominance. Initial therapy is carbimazole (preferred over PTU due to hepatotoxicity) for at least 18-24 months, with propranolol for adrenergic symptoms. Remission rate after a course is approximately 25-30%; relapse warrants discussion of definitive therapy (radioiodine or thyroidectomy).
key_points: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day, max 30 mg/day (titration regimen) or block-and-replace
PTU avoided in children except in thyroid storm or first trimester pregnancy
Monitor for agranulocytosis (FBC if sore throat or fever) and hepatotoxicity
Anti-TSHR Ab titre at end of treatment predicts relapse risk
explanation.pearls: Counsel families about agranulocytosis: any sore throat or fever on carbimazole = stop and check FBC.

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology - thyroid

q.stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred from his GP for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia of 4 months' duration. His parents estimate his fluid intake at approximately 4 litres per day, including overnight water bottles, and he is waking 3-4 times nightly to drink and urinate, with secondary nocturnal enuresis after being dry at night for the previous 18 months. He has had a 1 kg weight loss but is otherwise growing on the 25th centile and his pubertal stage is Tanner 1 as expected. There is no history of head injury, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no anosmia and no symptoms of diabetes mellitus such as polyphagia. His past history includes Langerhans cell histiocytosis diagnosed 18 months ago at age 3 with cutaneous and bony involvement, treated with vinblastine and prednisolone, that has been in apparent remission on surveillance imaging. On examination he is alert, well perfused, with normal vital signs and an unremarkable systemic examination, including normal fundoscopy and visual field examination by confrontation. Bloods today show Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, urea 6.2 mmol/L, creatinine 38 micromol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, calcium 2.4 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 305 mOsm/kg with a paired urine osmolality of 110 mOsm/kg measured on a morning sample. His urinary specific gravity is 1.002 and the urine dipstick is otherwise negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 104/64 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, weight 18 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert, mucous membranes moist, no visual field defect, no papilloedema, no skin lesions visible
data_table.Investigations: Na 148 mmol/L, plasma osm 305 mOsm/kg, urine osm 110 mOsm/kg, SG 1.002, glucose 4.8 mmol/L

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Formal water deprivation test with paired plasma and urine osmolality, then desmopressin challenge
options.rationale: A water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in the face of hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma; the diagnosis is already biochemically established and a desmopressin trial can proceed directly per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus and ESPE consensus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Desmopressin trial with measurement of urine osmolality response, followed by MRI brain with pituitary protocol
options.rationale: When plasma is already hyperosmolar (>295 mOsm/kg) with hypernatraemia and inappropriately dilute urine (<300 mOsm/kg), DI is biochemically confirmed and a desmopressin trial (urine concentrates >50% = central DI; no response = nephrogenic DI) with MRI brain (looking for loss of posterior pituitary bright spot, infiltrative disease) is appropriate, particularly with this child's LCH history per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Renal ultrasound and urinary tract DMSA scan to assess for medullary cystic disease
options.rationale: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no response to desmopressin, but is not the first-line discriminator per BMJ Best Practice: Diabetes Insipidus.

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test to exclude type 1 diabetes mellitus as the cause
options.rationale: Random glucose of 4.8 mmol/L and a urine specific gravity of 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis from glucose; this is hypotonic polyuria not diabetes mellitus per RCH CPG.

options.text: 24-hour urinary calcium and serum parathyroid hormone to exclude hypercalciuric nephrogenic DI
options.rationale: Calcium is normal at 2.4 mmol/L and chronic hypercalcaemia is an unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is the most likely diagnosis given the history per ESPE consensus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

explanation.summary: Diabetes insipidus is suggested by hypotonic polyuria with hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma (>295) with inappropriately dilute urine (<300). Once biochemically confirmed without need for water deprivation, a desmopressin trial distinguishes central (urine concentrates) from nephrogenic (no response). MRI brain is mandatory in confirmed central DI to exclude tumour, infiltration (LCH, germ cell tumour) or malformation.
key_points: Hypernatraemia should be corrected slowly (<10-12 mmol/L per 24 hours) to avoid cerebral oedema
Causes of central DI: idiopathic, tumour (craniopharyngioma, germinoma), LCH, trauma, infection
Nephrogenic DI: X-linked AVPR2 mutation, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, lithium, sickle cell
Treatment of central DI: oral or intranasal desmopressin titrated to symptoms
explanation.pearls: LCH commonly involves the pituitary stalk and presents as central DI before any imaging-visible lesion. Repeat MRI 6-monthly if initial scan is normal.

sources.label: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

q.subtopic_detail: Endocrinology - water balance

q.stem: A 9-year-old previously well girl presents to the tertiary paediatric ED with 3 days of fever, headache and confusion, and one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes at home that resolved spontaneously without intervention. Her mother describes intermittent inappropriate laughter and word-finding difficulty for the last 24 hours and a sense that the child has been smelling something burnt that nobody else in the household can smell. There is no rash and no neck stiffness, and she has had no foreign travel, no mosquito-exposed regional travel to northern Australia or to Murray-Darling river settings, and no animal bites or scratches. Her immunisations are up to date for the standard NIP schedule (no Japanese encephalitis vaccine because of no risk exposure recorded). She is taking no regular medications and has no known allergies. On examination she has a low-grade fever, her GCS fluctuates between 13 and 15, she has subtle right-sided pronator drift and expressive dysphasia, and the rest of the cranial nerve and peripheral neurological examinations are normal. There are no purpuric lesions on full skin survey. CT brain is unremarkable. MRI is scheduled for the morning. LP is performed: opening pressure 22 cm H2O, CSF clear, WCC 96 x 10^6/L (90% lymphocytes), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (paired serum 5.1), Gram stain negative; CSF HSV, VZV, enterovirus and meningococcal PCR are pending, and an autoimmune encephalitis panel including anti-NMDAR antibodies has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 deg C, HR 108 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 110/70 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Fluctuating GCS 13 to 15, expressive dysphasia, right pronator drift, no neck stiffness, no rash
data_table.Investigations: CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis (WCC 96, 90% lymphs), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, CT brain normal, MRI pending

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management decision?

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (~20 mg/kg) q8h empirically while awaiting CSF HSV PCR
options.rationale: Olfactory hallucinations, dysphasia, temporal-lobe-localising features and a lymphocytic CSF in a child are HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise. Empirical IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (about 20 mg/kg under 12 years) q8h must be started immediately because delay increases mortality and morbidity, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for presumed anti-NMDAR encephalitis pending antibody results today
options.rationale: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a differential but immunotherapy without first excluding and treating HSV is dangerous because steroids worsen untreated HSV. Empirical aciclovir is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg and dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, withhold antivirals until PCR results
options.rationale: The CSF is lymphocytic with normal glucose, making bacterial meningitis very unlikely; HSV must be empirically covered with aciclovir without waiting for PCR results, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Arrange urgent EEG and defer all therapy until non-convulsive status epilepticus is fully excluded
options.rationale: EEG is useful for diagnosis (temporal lobe periodic discharges in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR) but is not a reason to delay empirical aciclovir in suspected encephalitis, per RCH CPG.

options.text: Commence IV ribavirin for suspected Japanese encephalitis given the recent Murray Valley exposure risk
options.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for Japanese encephalitis and no Murray Valley exposure is described. JEV is supportive care only, with vaccination as prevention, per NCIRS Japanese encephalitis vaccine.

explanation.summary: Suspected viral encephalitis with focal features (especially temporal-lobe localisation, olfactory hallucinations, dysphasia) mandates empirical IV aciclovir while CSF HSV PCR and MRI are pursued. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) is a key differential, especially in children and young women with prominent psychiatric features, dyskinesias and autonomic instability.
key_points: Aciclovir dose: 500 mg/m^2 (or 20 mg/kg) q8h IV under 12 years; 10 mg/kg q8h adolescents and adults
Maintain hydration and monitor renal function (crystal nephropathy)
