MRI: HSV shows temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity; anti-NMDAR often normal MRI
EEG: HSV periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs); anti-NMDAR extreme delta brush
Anti-NMDAR triggers include ovarian teratoma; treat with IVIG/plasmapheresis plus steroids
explanation.pearls: Olfactory hallucinations and dysphasia point to the temporal lobe; assume HSV and start aciclovir while you investigate.

q.subtopic_detail: Encephalitis with focal neurology in a school-aged child

q.stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP after his father, a recently arrived migrant from a high-tuberculosis-incidence country in South-East Asia, was diagnosed last week with smear-positive cavitating pulmonary tuberculosis sensitive to all first-line drugs on rapid molecular testing. The boy has been well, has no cough, no fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss and has gained weight along the 50th centile on his growth chart. He has not received BCG vaccination as it is not on the routine Australian schedule. He shares a bedroom with his father and has slept in the same bed during the 8 weeks prior to his father's diagnosis. He has no other identifiable contacts of concern, including childcare contacts, who have been screened by the public health unit. On examination he is well, alert and interactive, with no peripheral or cervical lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and a clear chest on auscultation. There is no erythema nodosum and no phlyctenular conjunctivitis. The local public health unit asks the GP to coordinate his contact assessment. A chest X-ray is performed and is reported as normal. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test using 0.1 mL of 10 TU/mL purified protein derivative is placed in the left forearm and read by the public health nurse at 72 hours, showing an induration of 12 mm with surrounding erythema. HIV serology is non-reactive. The mother asks whether an interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) such as QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus would be more accurate in her son and whether he needs treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96 bpm, RR 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no lymphadenopathy, clear chest, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: CXR clear, Mantoux 12 mm induration at 72 h, HIV non-reactive, no BCG history

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the Mantoux in 8 weeks because 12 mm without BCG may be non-specific
options.rationale: An induration of 12 mm in a non-BCG vaccinated child with a household smear-positive contact is well above the 10 mm threshold and is treated as latent TB infection without repeating the test, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Reassure that the Mantoux is negative because it is under 15 mm and discharge with no further investigation
options.rationale: In Australia, a Mantoux of 10 mm or more in a non-BCG vaccinated person, or 5 mm or more in HIV or other significant immunosuppression, is considered positive, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Order IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) instead because it is more specific and avoid further skin testing
options.rationale: IGRA has reduced sensitivity in children under 5 years and is not preferred as a sole test in this age group in Australian guidance; Mantoux remains the test of choice for young children, although IGRA can supplement in BCG-vaccinated children, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Start a 6-month course of rifampicin monotherapy at 10 mg/kg daily without isoniazid today
options.rationale: Rifampicin monotherapy 4 months is an alternative regimen but the Australian default for paediatric latent TB infection is isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) for 6 to 9 months with pyridoxine, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CDNA SoNG.

options.text: Diagnose latent TB and commence isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine
options.rationale: A non-BCG child with a household smear-positive contact and Mantoux 10 mm or more, normal CXR and no clinical features of active disease has latent TB infection. The standard Australian paediatric regimen is isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine to prevent peripheral neuropathy; isoniazid plus rifampicin for 3 months is an alternative, as per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: Paediatric TB contact assessment uses Mantoux as the primary test in children under 5 years (IGRA has reduced sensitivity), with cut-offs of 10 mm or more in non-BCG, 5 mm or more in HIV/immunosuppressed, and 15 mm or more in low-risk BCG-vaccinated. Active disease is excluded clinically and with CXR before starting latent TB treatment.
key_points: Mantoux cut-offs: 5 mm (HIV/immunosuppressed/recent contact in some settings), 10 mm (non-BCG migrant/contact), 15 mm (low risk)
IGRA unreliable under 5 years; not first-line in this age group in Australia
Isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine for paediatric LTBI
Alternatives: rifampicin 4 months; isoniazid + rifampicin 3 months; weekly isoniazid + rifapentine 3 months (children 2 yr and over)
Notify the state TB control unit; arrange contact tracing of the entire household
explanation.pearls: In any child under 5 with a household TB contact, treat as if infected even with a negative Mantoux until a follow-up test at 8 to 12 weeks confirms otherwise; the window period matters.

q.subtopic_detail: Latent and contact tuberculosis investigation in a young child

q.stem: An 8-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, currently in the consolidation phase of a Berlin-Frankfurt-Munster protocol and on dexamethasone, vincristine and intrathecal methotrexate, is brought to the day oncology unit after a confirmed face-to-face household exposure to his 5-year-old sister, who developed a vesicular varicella rash on her trunk and face this morning. He has not had clinical varicella infection at any time in the past, did not complete the varicella vaccination schedule because of his oncology diagnosis at the time of his routine 18-month appointment, and was last on the day oncology ward yesterday for his scheduled intrathecal chemotherapy. His mother is also concerned about her own immunity because she is 18 weeks pregnant with the family's fourth child, has no history of chickenpox or vaccination herself, and has just had a serology test sent that morning to clarify her immune status. There is also a third sibling, a 12-year-old who has had clinically diagnosed varicella as a preschooler. The patient is currently afebrile and entirely asymptomatic, with no rash, no oral lesions and no respiratory or constitutional symptoms; his most recent full blood count 2 days ago showed a WCC of 1.8 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.7 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.9 x 10^9/L, and platelets 84 x 10^9/L. His baseline varicella IgG taken at diagnosis was negative. The exposure occurred 36 hours ago, and the local paediatric oncology consultant is available by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no rash, no oral lesions, no respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: WCC 1.8 (neutrophils 0.7) x 10^9/L, platelets 84 x 10^9/L, varicella IgG negative on baseline

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate intervention for this patient?

options.text: Live attenuated varicella vaccine 0.5 mL subcutaneously within 5 days of exposure
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live vaccine and is contraindicated in significantly immunocompromised patients such as those on chemotherapy, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and Therapeutic Guidelines.

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 20 mg/kg q8h for 7 days starting from day 7 after exposure
options.rationale: Prophylactic oral aciclovir or valaciclovir from day 7 post-exposure is an alternative when VZIG is unavailable, but VZIG (or IVIG if VZIG unavailable) is preferred in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Antivirals also do not replace VZIG in this group when VZIG is available, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly for 5 days starting immediately today
options.rationale: Immediate aciclovir is treatment of established varicella, not pre-symptomatic prophylaxis; VZIG is the preferred PEP in significantly immunocompromised contacts, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Observation only and IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h if a rash develops
options.rationale: Observation alone misses the window for passive immunisation. VZIG within 96 hours (up to 10 days per current product information) substantially attenuates disease in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Treatment with IV aciclovir is correct only if disease develops, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure (up to 10 days)
options.rationale: Significantly immunocompromised varicella-susceptible contacts (including children on chemotherapy, transplant recipients, and those on high-dose corticosteroids) should receive VZIG as soon as possible after exposure, ideally within 96 hours but up to 10 days. Other indicated groups include non-immune pregnant women and neonates of mothers with peripartum varicella (5 days before to 2 days after delivery), as per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and the Australian Immunisation Handbook.

explanation.summary: VZIG (or IVIG when VZIG unavailable) is given to susceptible high-risk contacts after significant varicella exposure: significantly immunocompromised patients, non-immune pregnant women, and neonates exposed to peripartum maternal varicella. Live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in this group. Maternal varicella before 20 weeks risks congenital varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, cicatricial scarring, microcephaly, eye defects); peripartum maternal varicella threatens neonatal varicella with high mortality.
key_points: VZIG ideally within 96 h, up to 10 days from exposure
Varicella vaccine contraindicated in significant immunosuppression
Congenital varicella syndrome risk highest with maternal infection 8 to 20 weeks gestation
Neonatal varicella: maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after birth equals VZIG to neonate plus aciclovir if symptomatic
Treat established varicella in immunocompromised with IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h
explanation.pearls: If you have to think about whether someone is significantly immunocompromised, they probably are; phone for VZIG and don't wait for the rash.

q.subtopic_detail: Varicella post-exposure prophylaxis in the immunocompromised host

q.stem: A 6-year-old girl with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by her parents on day 10 of consolidation chemotherapy with high-dose methotrexate and cytarabine. She has a tunnelled Hickman line in situ for 4 months, has been febrile for 4 hours with a single reading of 38.6 deg C documented at home by her parents, has had new rigors over the last 30 minutes, and her parents report increasingly rapid breathing and that she has been progressively more lethargic over the last hour with reduced eye contact. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is mottled with cold peripheries and a capillary refill of 5 seconds, BP 78/40 mmHg, HR 162 bpm, RR 40 /min, SpO2 94% on 4 L/min nasal prongs, T 39.4 deg C. There is no obvious focus of infection on rapid head-to-toe examination, the Hickman exit site is clean and not erythematous, her chest is clear, and there is no abdominal tenderness or perianal lesion. Her full blood count two days ago showed neutrophils 0.2 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.4 x 10^9/L, haemoglobin 92 g/L, and platelets 28 x 10^9/L. She has no penicillin or other drug allergy, no previous resistant organisms colonising her central line or stool surveillance, and her last echocardiogram performed 3 months ago for routine anthracycline monitoring showed normal LV function and a normal ejection fraction of 64%. There is no history of mucositis bleeding, no recent abdominal pain and no diarrhoea. A repeat FBC, blood cultures drawn from both Hickman lumens and a peripheral site simultaneously, venous lactate, VBG, EUC, LFTs, coagulation, group and screen, urinalysis and a portable CXR are being arranged in parallel with resuscitation. The on-call oncology consultant and the paediatric intensive care registrar have been contacted and are aware.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 162 bpm, RR 40 /min, BP 78/40 mmHg, SpO2 94% on 4 L/min, CRT 5 s
data_table.Examination: Mottled cold peripheries, lethargic, no rash, no mucositis bleeding, Hickman line site clean, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Recent neutrophils 0.2 x 10^9/L, platelets 28 x 10^9/L, lactate pending, no known resistant organisms

q.lead_in: After 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus is initiated, what is the single most appropriate empirical antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: IV cefepime 50 mg/kg q8h as monotherapy for febrile neutropenia, omitting empirical aminoglycoside
options.rationale: Cefepime monotherapy is acceptable for stable febrile neutropenia per some guidelines but the patient is in septic shock with hypotension and prolonged neutropenia. In Australian paediatric practice, piperacillin-tazobactam plus an aminoglycoside (or vancomycin if line infection suspected) is preferred for haemodynamically unstable febrile neutropenia, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily plus IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is a critical pathogen in febrile neutropenia. Antipseudomonal cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus IV gentamicin, add vancomycin if line infection suspected or shock persists
options.rationale: High-risk febrile neutropenia with septic shock and an indwelling Hickman line in a profoundly neutropenic patient requires antipseudomonal beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside for synergy and Gram-negative cover. Piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg (piperacillin component) q6h plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily is standard, with vancomycin added if suspected central line infection, mucositis, MRSA risk, or ongoing instability, as per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV meropenem 20 mg/kg eight-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy without empirical aminoglycoside
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line use when there is known ESBL colonisation, severe sepsis not responding to first-line therapy, or significant beta-lactam allergy; routine first-line use risks selecting carbapenem-resistant organisms, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Delay antibiotics until blood cultures return positive to ensure organism-directed therapy today
options.rationale: Antibiotics must be administered within 60 minutes (ideally within 30 minutes for septic shock) in suspected febrile neutropenia. Each hour of delay increases mortality, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

explanation.summary: Febrile neutropenia is fever 38.0 deg C or more (single reading 38.5 deg C or sustained 38.0 deg C) with ANC under 0.5 x 10^9/L (or expected to fall). Empirical antibiotics within 60 minutes (within 30 minutes if septic shock). High-risk or haemodynamically unstable patients: piperacillin-tazobactam plus aminoglycoside; add vancomycin if line infection, MRSA risk, mucositis, or shock. Source control includes line removal if persistent positive cultures.
key_points: ANC under 0.5 x 10^9/L defines neutropenia
Antibiotics within 60 min (30 min if shocked)
First-line high-risk: piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin (+/- vancomycin)
Add antifungal cover if persistent fever beyond 96 h
G-CSF for prolonged neutropenia; cannot replace antibiotics in acute phase
explanation.pearls: Time to antibiotics is the single most modifiable predictor of mortality in paediatric febrile neutropenia; the line must be in and antibiotics running before the imaging trolley arrives.

q.subtopic_detail: Febrile neutropenia with septic shock in the immunocompromised child

q.stem: A 38-hour-old term male born at 39+5 by NVD to a G2P1 mother with an uneventful pregnancy is brought to the special care nursery by the midwife. He has had three episodes of vomiting in the last 90 minutes, the most recent two clearly bilious (bright green) and photographed on the midwife's phone for the registrar to review. He passed meconium at 18 hours of age and has been breastfeeding poorly since the second feed, latching but suckling weakly and falling asleep. Birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 3.25 kg, representing a 4% drop. On examination he is irritable with HR 178, RR 62, CRT 3 seconds, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% in room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen without obvious mass, soft, with sluggish bowel sounds. There is no inguinal hernia, no anal stenosis, and no perianal abnormality. First-degree relatives have no history of cystic fibrosis or congenital gut malformation. Antenatal scans had not commented on intra-abdominal anatomy beyond reporting a normal anatomy scan at 20 weeks, with no polyhydramnios noted. An NG tube is placed and immediately drains 25 mL of bilious fluid with relief of distension. The neonatal registrar has IV access through a peripheral cannula and is preparing maintenance fluids and a 10 mL/kg saline bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 62, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% RA, CRT 3 s, weight 3.25 kg.
data_table.Examination: Irritable, mildly distended abdomen, soft, no mass, sluggish bowel sounds. No hernia, normal anus.
data_table.Investigations: NG aspirate 25 mL bilious. IV access x 1, fluids being prepared.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Upper GI contrast study within the next hour, paediatric surgical team aware and theatre standby
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation, showing an abnormal duodeno-jejunal junction position and a corkscrew or beak appearance if volvulus is present. This must occur with surgical standby because volvulus requires laparotomy within minutes to hours to prevent loss of the midgut (RCH CPG; ANZNN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids overnight, with reassessment in the morning
options.rationale: Watch-and-wait management of bilious vomiting in a neonate can result in complete loss of the midgut within 4-6 hours of established volvulus. This is a surgical emergency (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound looking for the whirlpool sign before considering further imaging today
options.rationale: USS whirlpool sign (SMA-SMV inversion with twisted mesentery) is supportive but not gold-standard, and absence of the sign does not exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast remains the definitive investigation (RANZCR; RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph alone to exclude duodenal atresia as the diagnosis
options.rationale: Plain AXR is useful (may show double-bubble for duodenal atresia, or paucity of distal gas in volvulus) but is insufficient to confirm or exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast is required (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Contrast enema looking for microcolon to exclude meconium ileus as diagnosis
options.rationale: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant who passed meconium at 18 hours it is less likely than malrotation. Upper GI contrast is the priority investigation for bilious vomiting (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. The midgut twists around a narrow mesenteric pedicle and can necrose within hours. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation. The corrective operation is a Ladd procedure: counter-clockwise detorsion, division of Ladd bands, broadening of the mesentery, appendicectomy, and placement of small bowel on the right.
key_points: Any green vomit in the first month is malrotation/volvulus until excluded
Upper GI contrast = gold standard (DJ flexure should be left of midline at the level of pylorus)
USS whirlpool sign is supportive but not definitive
Operation = Ladd procedure
Time from established volvulus to midgut loss may be as short as 4-6 hours
explanation.pearls: If you are ever the most senior person at the bedside of a green-vomiting neonate, your job is to phone the paediatric surgical centre before you have all the answers.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

q.subtopic_detail: Bilious vomiting in the first 48 hours of life

q.stem: A 28+5 week preterm female born at birth weight 1.05 kg is now on day 11 of life in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and trophic feeds of expressed breast milk were introduced on day 5 and advanced to 60 mL/kg/day by day 10. Over the last 12 hours she has had two large bilious gastric aspirates, abdominal distension increasing from 25 cm to 28.5 cm girth, and one episode of bright red blood in the stool. She has become apnoeic with three desaturations to less than 80% in the last hour requiring stimulation. HR has risen from 145 to 178. T is 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 mmHg with mean arterial pressure 30. CRT is 4 seconds. The abdominal wall is shiny with bluish discolouration over the right flank. WCC has fallen from 9.2 to 4.1 x10^9/L, platelets from 224 to 86, CRP risen from less than 5 to 92 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L. The supine abdominal radiograph shows multiple dilated loops of bowel with intramural gas (pneumatosis intestinalis) and a curvilinear lucency over the liver consistent with portal venous gas. There is no free intraperitoneal gas on a left lateral decubitus film. The paediatric surgical service has been consulted by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 70, T 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 (MAP 30), SpO2 88% on CPAP, CRT 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Distended shiny abdomen with bluish flank discolouration. PR bright red blood.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.1, platelets 86, CRP 92, lactate 4.8. AXR: pneumatosis + portal venous gas, no free gas.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood culture results are available to avoid promoting antibiotic resistance and unnecessary coverage
options.rationale: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must be commenced immediately on suspicion of NEC. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy (RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at current volume and add oral probiotics to reduce bacterial gut translocation
options.rationale: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM. Continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury and risks perforation. Probiotics are used prophylactically in some units but have no role in established NEC (RCH CPG NEC).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparotomy regardless of radiographic findings of pneumatosis or perforation
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, clinical deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is managed medically with surgical standby (RCH CPG NEC).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Nil by mouth, OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, TPN, serial films, paediatric surgical involvement
options.rationale: This is Bell stage IIB NEC (pneumatosis with portal venous gas, no perforation). Management is medical: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (penicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines), TPN, serial AXR every 6 hours for 48-72 hours to detect perforation. Surgery is indicated for perforation (Bell IIIB) or clinical deterioration (RCH CPG NEC; ANZNN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Place a peritoneal drain at the bedside as definitive treatment without commencing antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable extremely-low-birth-weight infants with perforation, not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. There is no demonstrated perforation here (ANZNN).

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is a multifactorial inflammatory/ischaemic injury to immature gut, peaking at 28-32 weeks corrected age in the second to third postnatal week. Bell staging: I suspected (non-specific), II definite (IIA pneumatosis, IIB pneumatosis + portal gas), III advanced (IIIA critically ill no perforation, IIIB perforation).
key_points: Peak: 28-32 weeks corrected, day 7-14 of life
Risk factors: prematurity, formula feeding, rapid feed advance, hypoxia, sepsis
Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic
Management: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics, TPN, serial AXR
Surgery for perforation, fixed dilated loop, abdominal wall erythema/discolouration, or deterioration
explanation.pearls: A shiny abdomen with bluish discolouration is a late sign of full-thickness bowel wall ischaemia and is a call to the operating room.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

q.subtopic_detail: Preterm infant with feed intolerance and abdominal distension

q.stem: A 22-month-old girl is referred by her maternal and child health nurse for faltering growth. Her birth weight was on the 75th centile after an uncomplicated term pregnancy and NVD. She was exclusively breastfed until 6 months when solids were introduced including wheat-based cereals, pasta and bread, with normal progression of feeding milestones over the next several months. She is now on the 9th centile for weight and 25th for height, having crossed two major weight centiles downward over the last 8 months. Her mother reports chronic loose, bulky, pale, foul-smelling stools 3 to 5 times daily that float in the toilet, marked abdominal distension that develops over the course of the day, irritability particularly in the late afternoon, and a recent reduction in appetite. She has had no fevers, no overseas travel, no antibiotic exposure in the last 12 months, and is up to date with all routine immunisations. The paternal grandmother has type 1 diabetes mellitus and an older sibling aged 7 has confirmed coeliac disease diagnosed two years ago on duodenal biopsy. On examination today she is pale, thin, with mild abdominal distension, no organomegaly, no clubbing, and reduced subcutaneous fat over the buttocks and proximal thighs. The GP orders anti-tTG IgA and total IgA as the initial screen. Anti-tTG IgA returns at 230 U/mL (reference less than 20, more than 10 times upper limit), total IgA is 1.6 g/L (normal for age, excluding IgA deficiency), endomysial antibody is positive, and HLA-DQ2 is positive. The mother attends to discuss the results and asks the GP what should happen next. She is keen to start a strict gluten-free diet immediately because she has already done so for the older sibling and knows the supermarket products.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, RR 26, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale, thin, distended abdomen, reduced buttock fat pads, no organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: anti-tTG IgA 230 U/mL (less than 20), total IgA 1.6 g/L (normal), EMA positive, HLA-DQ2 positive.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue gluten, refer paediatric gastroenterology for duodenal biopsy, only start gluten-free after histology; baseline nutritional studies; screen relatives
options.rationale: Australian and ESPGHAN guidelines require duodenal biopsy in symptomatic children with positive serology before commencing a gluten-free diet, unless the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway is fully met (anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit of normal AND positive EMA on a separate sample AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive) and this is delivered in a specialist paediatric gastroenterology setting with full counselling. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can invalidate the histology if the family later wants definitive diagnosis. First-degree relative screening is recommended (RACGP; Coeliac Australia; ESPGHAN).
source_refs: ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

options.text: Commence a strict gluten-free diet immediately given the strongly positive serology and family history, with no further investigation needed
options.rationale: Initiating a gluten-free diet before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway delivered by paediatric gastroenterology with full criteria met on two separate serology samples. Starting too early can render later confirmatory biopsy non-diagnostic if uncertainty arises (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Discontinue gluten for 4 weeks then perform a gluten challenge with repeat serology and biopsy
options.rationale: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes the diagnostic biopsy unreliable. Gluten challenge is reserved for cases where the original diagnosis was made without adequate evidence or there is later diagnostic doubt, and is usually deferred until age 5 or later because of impact on growth (ESPGHAN).
source_refs: ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

options.text: Repeat anti-tTG IgA in 6 months and only proceed to further investigation if titres remain elevated
options.rationale: A symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA, and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed by 6 months of repeat serology. Definitive diagnosis enables treatment that drives catch-up growth (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Order an IgG-based gliadin antibody panel and faecal fat studies as the next investigations
options.rationale: IgG anti-gliadin testing has been superseded by anti-tTG and EMA. Faecal fat is rarely used in modern paediatric coeliac diagnosis. Duodenal biopsy is the definitive next step (RACGP; ESPGHAN).
source_refs: ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

explanation.summary: Coeliac disease in children: screen with anti-tTG IgA plus total IgA (false negative if IgA deficient). Positive serology in a symptomatic child warrants duodenal biopsy via paediatric gastroenterology to confirm Marsh-Oberhuber 2 or higher histology. The ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway requires anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit on one sample, positive EMA on a separate sample, HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive, and a specialist setting. Do not start a gluten-free diet before diagnosis is secured.
key_points: Screen: anti-tTG IgA + total IgA; if IgA deficient use IgG-based serology
Positive serology + symptoms = paediatric gastroenterology referral for biopsy
ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway: anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x ULN + EMA positive on second sample + HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive
Maintain gluten in diet until diagnosis confirmed
Screen first-degree relatives; monitor growth, iron, vitamin D, calcium, thyroid function and bone density
explanation.pearls: The most common reason a coeliac diagnosis becomes uncertain is starting the gluten-free diet too early. Wait for the biopsy or for the full no-biopsy criteria.

sources.label: ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

q.subtopic_detail: Toddler with faltering growth and loose stools

q.stem: A 2-year-old girl presents at 2030 on a Friday with a 24-hour history of a barking cough and a hoarse cry, becoming progressively worse over the evening. She has had a 2-day coryzal illness. Her father reports increasing breathing difficulty over the last hour and audible noise on breathing in. In the resuscitation cubicle she is sitting upright on her father's lap, looks anxious, and is making no attempt to drink. She has continuous inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. There is no drooling, she is afebrile at 37.7 deg C and her SpO2 is 95% on room air, RR 36, HR 142. Her cry is hoarse but she can speak in short phrases. She is fully immunised including Hib vaccine, has no atopy, and there has been no witnessed or suspected foreign body inhalation. There is no other family member unwell. Air entry is symmetrical with no focal crackles or wheeze. Calculated Westley score is 6, placing her in the moderate category. She has had no previous croup admissions or any history of recurrent stridor between viral illnesses. Weight is 13 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 142, SpO2 95% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Continuous stridor at rest. Marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. No drooling. Hoarse but phonating.
data_table.Investigations: Westley score 6 (moderate). No CXR. Weight 13 kg.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of immediate management steps?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg alone, discharge after 1 hour observation if she remains stable
options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is appropriate for mild croup. With Westley 6, stridor at rest and marked recession, nebulised adrenaline is also required and at least 4 hours of observation post-adrenaline is needed because of rebound (RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, observe 4 hours
options.rationale: Moderate croup (Westley 3 to 7) with stridor at rest and significant work of breathing is treated with nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg standard, up to 0.6 mg/kg if severe), and observation for at least 4 hours after adrenaline to detect rebound. Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes but lasts only about 2 hours, while steroid onset is around 1 to 2 hours (RCH CPG: Croup; AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and urgent ENT consult for suspected epiglottitis
options.rationale: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice in an unimmunised child; this is a typical viral croup picture (Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Heliox via face mask and humidified oxygen, holding dexamethasone until the ENT review
options.rationale: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit in croup and are not standard of care; withholding dexamethasone is harmful as steroid reduces return visits and admission rates (RCH CPG: Croup; Cochrane).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg, ipratropium 250 microg and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: This is the treatment for acute asthma, not croup; croup is an upper-airway disease and beta-agonists do not address subglottic oedema (RCH CPG: Croup; National Asthma Council Handbook).

explanation.summary: Moderate croup (Westley 3 to 7; stridor at rest with recession but otherwise alert and pink) is treated with nebulised adrenaline (5 mL of 1:1000) plus oral dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg standard, up to 0.6 mg/kg) and observed for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose to detect rebound. Severe croup (Westley 8 or above, hypoxia, exhaustion, drowsiness) needs PICU and consideration of intubation.
key_points: Mild (Westley 0-2): dex 0.15 mg/kg PO, observe briefly
Moderate (3-7): nebulised adrenaline + dex, observe 4 hrs post-adrenaline
Severe (8+): adrenaline + dex, ICU, anaesthetics, consider intubation
Adrenaline acts in 10-30 min, effect lasts ~2 hrs; rebound mandates observation
Steroid onset ~1-2 hrs; covers symptom course of 24-48 hrs
explanation.pearls: Drooling, tripod posture, muffled voice and toxic appearance in an unwell unimmunised child should prompt consideration of epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis rather than croup, with airway team activation rather than examination of the throat.

q.subtopic_detail: Acute respiratory

q.stem: A 10-year-old girl with asthma has been reviewed in the GP clinic with her mother for a routine 3-monthly follow-up. She has been on fluticasone propionate 100 microg twice daily (low-dose ICS) for 8 months. Over the last 3 months she has had daytime wheeze or cough on most days, has woken at night with cough at least once a week, and has needed her salbutamol on average 4 to 5 days per week including before sport. There have been no oral steroid courses in this period and no hospital presentations. The GP has watched her use her MDI and spacer (mouthpiece) in clinic and the technique is excellent. Adherence on Webster pack review is 90% and the parents confirm she takes it morning and night. There are no environmental triggers identified - no smoking in the household, no pets, and no recent changes in housing. Spirometry today shows FEV1 78% predicted and FEV1/FVC 0.74 with significant bronchodilator reversibility of 14%. She is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile and has normal ENT and dermatological examinations. There is no rhinitis, no eczema and no GORD symptoms. She has no allergy or family history requiring specialist input.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, RR 18, HR 92, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Well, normal chest examination. Growth on 50th centile.
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry: FEV1 78% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.74, bronchodilator reversibility 14%. Adherence 90%, technique correct.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in her controller therapy?

options.text: Continue current low-dose ICS and add daily oral montelukast 5 mg as maintenance add-on
options.rationale: Montelukast is an alternative or add-on option but is less effective than adding a LABA at this step in children 6 and over; the National Asthma Council prefers ICS-LABA combination as the preferred step-up (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Step up to combination ICS plus LABA (fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily)
options.rationale: She has poor symptom control on low-dose ICS despite correct technique, good adherence and no modifiable factors. The National Asthma Council preferred step-up in children 6 and over is combination low-dose ICS-LABA. Doubling the ICS dose is an alternative but ICS-LABA generally produces better symptom and lung function gains for the same total ICS exposure (National Asthma Council Handbook; GINA 2024).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Stop the regular ICS and commence as-needed budesonide-formoterol via MDI plus spacer
options.rationale: As-needed ICS-formoterol (the MART approach) is recommended in adolescents and adults at certain steps but is not the preferred maintenance pathway for a 10-year-old who is already on regular ICS with poor control; step-up to combination ICS-LABA is preferred (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment for omalizumab or biologic therapy
options.rationale: Biologic therapy is reserved for severe, uncontrolled asthma despite high-dose ICS-LABA plus adherence optimisation, and is a tertiary-care decision; she has not yet had a step-up from low-dose ICS (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Add a daily long-acting muscarinic antagonist such as tiotropium via Respimat inhaler
options.rationale: LAMA is an add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium- to high-dose ICS-LABA; it is not the first step-up from low-dose ICS in a child (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: For a school-age child with uncontrolled asthma on low-dose ICS despite confirmed correct technique, good adherence and absence of modifiable triggers, the National Asthma Council preferred step-up is combination low-dose ICS-LABA. Doubling the ICS dose is an acceptable alternative. Always recheck adherence, technique, environmental factors and the diagnosis before stepping up.
key_points: Before stepping up: recheck technique, adherence, triggers, diagnosis (the 4 As)
Preferred step-up (age 6+): low-dose ICS-LABA
Alternative: medium-dose ICS, or ICS + LTRA
Step down once control achieved for 3 months
Refer to specialist if uncontrolled on medium-dose ICS-LABA
explanation.pearls: The most common reason for 'uncontrolled asthma' is technique or adherence failure, not severity. Watch every patient use their inhaler at every review.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

q.stem: A 7-week-old previously well boy is brought to ED by his mother after 10 days of a worsening cough. The cough started as a non-specific coryzal illness with mild rhinorrhoea but over the last 5 days has become severely paroxysmal, with episodes of coughing lasting 30 to 60 seconds followed by vomiting and a sharp inspiratory sound. Between paroxysms he is settled and appears well. He has had two witnessed brief apnoeic episodes at home, each lasting less than 15 seconds, with no colour change. He is bottle-fed and taking only about 80% of his usual volume. He is not yet immunised because his mother declined the antenatal pertussis booster and the 6-week vaccines were missed. His mother has had a 3-week non-productive cough that she has attributed to a viral illness. On examination he is afebrile at 36.9 deg C, RR 42, HR 158, SpO2 97% on room air, with mild intercostal recession and unremarkable auscultation between paroxysms. FBC shows white cell count 28 x10^9/L with a marked lymphocytosis. CXR shows perihilar peribronchial thickening with no focal consolidation. A nasopharyngeal pertussis PCR has been sent. He weighs 4.8 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, RR 42, HR 158, SpO2 97% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Settled between paroxysms. Mild recession. Auscultation clear between coughs.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 28 x10^9/L, marked lymphocytosis. CXR: perihilar peribronchial thickening. Pertussis PCR sent.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days, discharge home with safety-netting
options.rationale: Amoxicillin does not cover Bordetella pertussis; azithromycin is the macrolide of choice. Pertussis in an infant under 6 months with apnoea warrants admission for observation, not discharge, and is notifiable on clinical suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days plus salbutamol nebs, no public health action
options.rationale: Azithromycin and admission are appropriate but salbutamol is not effective for pertussis bronchospasm and contact tracing plus household post-exposure prophylaxis is mandatory under CDNA guidelines (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Admit for monitoring, oral azithromycin for 5 days, notify public health, household chemoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with pertussis and any apnoeic features should be admitted because of the risk of bradycardia, apnoea and death. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days is the first-line macrolide for infants under 6 months (lower risk of pyloric stenosis than erythromycin). Pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion, and close household contacts (especially infants and pregnant women) require post-exposure prophylaxis with the same regimen (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG; NCIRS).

options.text: Oral erythromycin 40 mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days, no admission required
options.rationale: Erythromycin is associated with pyloric stenosis in infants under 1 month and has more GI side effects than azithromycin; admission of an under-6-month-old with apnoea is recommended regardless of SpO2 (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone with oral prednisolone, defer notification until pertussis PCR confirms
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone and prednisolone do not treat pertussis; pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion to permit timely contact tracing and prophylaxis, not after laboratory confirmation (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

explanation.summary: Pertussis in unvaccinated young infants presents in three phases (catarrhal, paroxysmal, convalescent) and carries significant morbidity from apnoea, pneumonia and pertussis-related pulmonary hypertension. Infants under 6 months with apnoea or feeding difficulty are admitted. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is the first-line treatment in young infants (lower pyloric stenosis risk than erythromycin). Pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion and close contacts require post-exposure prophylaxis with the same macrolide.
key_points: Three phases: catarrhal (1-2 wk) - paroxysmal (2-6 wk) - convalescent (weeks)
Infants <6 months: admit if apnoea, feeding difficulty, cyanosis
Azithromycin first-line in young infants (10 mg/kg/day x 5 days)
Notifiable disease on clinical suspicion
Post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts including pregnant women and young infants
explanation.pearls: Lymphocytosis (often >20 x10^9/L) is a clinical clue in pertussis. In ex-premature or under-6-month infants, the first sign may be apnoea rather than cough.

q.subtopic_detail: Acute respiratory

q.stem: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in the GP clinic 2 weeks after a moderate asthma exacerbation that required oral prednisolone in ED but no admission. She is on fluticasone propionate-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily and PRN salbutamol via MDI plus spacer. The clinician has reviewed her technique (excellent) and her adherence (good). The clinician now sits down to write an asthma action plan with the family using the National Asthma Council template. Her mother asks specifically what to do at home when the girl is wheezy and is needing salbutamol more often than usual. Over the last year she has had three episodes that started with several days of increased salbutamol use and mild nocturnal cough before deteriorating - on two occasions her mother gave a single dose of salbutamol and waited overnight before seeking review, and the girl ended up needing oral prednisolone each time. The clinician wants the family to act earlier next time. There is no school attendance issue and the family lives 15 minutes from the nearest ED. The school has a copy of the previous action plan but it is now 18 months old.

data_table.Vital signs: Asymptomatic at review. T 36.6 deg C, RR 16, HR 84, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Well. Normal chest examination.
data_table.Investigations: Three exacerbations in 12 months, two required oral prednisolone. Technique and adherence good.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice to include in the yellow ('worsening symptoms / use salbutamol more often') zone of her written action plan?

options.text: Continue preventer and use salbutamol 2 puffs every 4 hours, no other action
options.rationale: Yellow zone requires intensification: increased salbutamol dosing (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours), continuing preventer, considering temporary preventer increase, and arranging GP review within 24 to 48 hours - not waiting for severe deterioration (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Single oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg at home at first sign of worsening, GP review
options.rationale: Home initiation of oral prednisolone is reserved for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist guidance and a tailored plan; it is not routine yellow-zone advice in a primary-care action plan (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Stop the preventer and rely on salbutamol alone until the worsening symptoms resolve
options.rationale: Stopping the preventer worsens control; preventer should be continued or temporarily intensified during a deterioration, not withdrawn (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Salbutamol 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours, temporarily increase preventer, GP review 24-48 hours
options.rationale: The yellow zone of a National Asthma Council action plan instructs increased salbutamol use (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours via spacer), continued or temporarily increased preventer, GP review within 24 to 48 hours, and clear red-flag features to escalate to ambulance (no relief from salbutamol, severe breathlessness, blue lips, unable to speak in sentences). Written zone-based plans reduce hospitalisations and time off school (National Asthma Council Handbook; Cochrane Gibson 2003).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Present immediately to emergency department for any increase in salbutamol use regardless of severity
options.rationale: Routine ED presentation for minor increases in salbutamol use leads to unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; clear thresholds for ED versus GP versus home intensification are the purpose of the action plan (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: A written asthma action plan uses traffic-light zones: green (well, take preventer), yellow (symptoms worsening - intensify), red (severe - emergency). Yellow zone actions include increased salbutamol (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours via spacer), continued or temporarily increased preventer, and review within 24 to 48 hours. Red zone triggers emergency response (4 puffs salbutamol, repeat every 4 minutes if no improvement, call 000).
key_points: Green: regular preventer + reliever as needed; recognise triggers
Yellow: salbutamol 4-6 puffs q4h; continue or increase preventer; GP review 24-48 hrs
Red: 4 puffs every 4 min, call 000, oral prednisolone if prescribed
Update plan every 12 months and after every significant exacerbation
Provide a copy to the school and any out-of-school carer
explanation.pearls: Action plans reduce hospitalisations and school absence. The most common reason for repeated exacerbations is delayed family escalation in the yellow zone.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

q.stem: A 12-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) is admitted with a 7-day history of worsening productive cough, increased sputum volume that has become darker green and more tenacious than her usual baseline, reduced exercise tolerance with breathlessness climbing one flight of stairs at school, anorexia and 1.5 kg of weight loss over the previous 2 weeks despite her usual enzyme dosing. Her baseline FEV1 is 78% predicted; today it has fallen to 61% predicted on bedside spirometry, the largest single-visit drop she has had since starting her current modulator. She is fully adherent to her usual regimen of nebulised hypertonic saline 6%, dornase alfa, pancreatic enzyme replacement and twice-daily airway clearance physiotherapy with a positive expiratory pressure device. She is on elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor and has been stable for the last 18 months. She is fully immunised including annual influenza and is up to date with the seasonal RSV programme. Her last sputum culture 6 weeks ago grew mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa sensitive to tobramycin, ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, and meropenem, and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin. She has no penicillin allergy and her baseline serum creatinine 6 weeks ago was 55 micromol/L. On examination she is alert, T 37.8 deg C, RR 26, HR 110, SpO2 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crackles at both upper lobes and the right middle lobe with widespread expiratory wheeze. CXR shows bilateral upper-lobe bronchiectasis with new patchy consolidation at the right middle lobe compared to her baseline. A sputum culture has been sent on arrival. Her weight is 38 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, RR 26, HR 110, SpO2 93% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert. Coarse crackles both upper lobes + RML. Widespread expiratory wheeze. Cachectic appearance.
data_table.Investigations: FEV1 61% (baseline 78%). Last sputum: mucoid Pseudomonas (sensitive to tobramycin, ceftazidime, pip-taz, meropenem); MSSA. New CXR consolidation at RML.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical IV antibiotic regimen pending fresh sputum culture results?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 20 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient antipseudomonal monotherapy with home physiotherapy
options.rationale: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy is used for early Pseudomonas eradication or mild outpatient exacerbations; an FEV1 drop of 17 percentage points with new consolidation, weight loss and hypoxia warrants IV dual therapy with admission (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily plus oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa; ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime or meropenem is required. Azithromycin has anti-inflammatory effects in CF but is not adequate antipseudomonal therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus oral rifampicin as targeted antistaphylococcal combination therapy
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets MSSA, which is reasonable additional cover but does not address the chronic Pseudomonas that is far more likely to be driving this exacerbation given her sputum history (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV meropenem 40 mg/kg every 8 hours as broad-spectrum antipseudomonal monotherapy for 14 days
options.rationale: Carbapenem monotherapy is reserved for multi-resistant Pseudomonas or beta-lactam allergy; standard practice in a child with sensitive mucoid Pseudomonas is a beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside combination, preserving carbapenems for resistant strains (Therapeutic Guidelines; CF Australia Standards of Care).
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care

options.text: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg every 8 hours plus IV tobramycin 10 mg/kg once daily, alongside intensified airway clearance physiotherapy
options.rationale: A CF pulmonary exacerbation in an adolescent with chronic mucoid Pseudomonas is treated empirically with dual anti-pseudomonal IV therapy combining an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (commonly ceftazidime) with an aminoglycoside (tobramycin once daily, with levels monitored) for 10 to 14 days. Intensified airway clearance physiotherapy and nutritional optimisation are essential adjuncts and her CFTR modulator is continued. MSSA is co-covered or treated separately if persistent (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; CF Australia Standards of Care; Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Guidelines).
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care

explanation.summary: A CF pulmonary exacerbation is defined by increased cough, sputum volume or purulence, dyspnoea, anorexia, weight loss, and a fall in FEV1 (typically 10% or more from baseline). Admission is indicated for significant FEV1 decline, hypoxia or failure to respond to oral therapy. Standard empirical IV regimen for chronic mucoid Pseudomonas: a beta-lactam (ceftazidime or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus an aminoglycoside (tobramycin once daily) for 10 to 14 days, with intensified physiotherapy and dietetic input. CFTR modulators are continued throughout.
key_points: Exacerbation triggers: fall in FEV1 >=10%, increased sputum/cough, weight loss, hypoxia
Empirical IV: anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam + aminoglycoside x 10-14 days
Once-daily tobramycin with trough monitoring (renal and auditory toxicity)
Continue CFTR modulator and adjust based on culture
Adjuncts: intensified physio, nutrition, treat any DIOS or hypoglycaemia
explanation.pearls: In a CF patient with chronic Pseudomonas, never treat an exacerbation with a single agent. The double-cover principle reduces emergence of resistance and is the Australian and international standard.

sources.label: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care

q.stem: A 10-year-old boy, weight 32 kg, presents to ED 25 minutes after a wasp sting with urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, expiratory wheeze and persistent hypotension. Paramedics gave IM adrenaline 300 microg en route. On arrival to ED 10 minutes later he remained tachypnoeic with stridor, wheeze and BP 78/40, so a second IM adrenaline 300 microg was given into the anterolateral thigh and an IV 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus was commenced. Now 5 minutes after the second IM dose he remains drowsy with BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, with audible stridor and wheeze. Both IM doses were confirmed delivered correctly. The ED consultant is at the bedside, anaesthetics has been called for airway support, and a second IV line has been sited. His past medical history is unremarkable, he takes no regular medications, he has no known allergies and his immunisations are up to date. There is no family history of allergic disease, asthma, or cardiac disorders. He had been camping with his family at a regional park and was helping clear food scraps from the picnic table when stung on the right forearm. He has no relevant drug allergies, no recent fasting, and a full set of bloods including tryptase has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L NRB.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Stridor and expiratory wheeze. Lip and tongue swelling. Mottled peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.30, lactate 3.4 mmol/L.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Bolus IV adrenaline 1 mg (1:10,000) over 1 minute
options.rationale: A 1 mg IV bolus is the cardiac arrest dose and would likely cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia in a perfusing patient; infusion is preferred (ASCIA).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV promethazine 0.25 mg/kg
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and antihistamines do not treat refractory anaphylaxis and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years and can worsen hypotension; adrenaline infusion is required (ASCIA).

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min, titrated to response
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis after two correctly delivered IM doses is an indication for IV adrenaline infusion, starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min (range 0.05-1 microg/kg/min) and titrated to BP, perfusion and airway response, while continuing fluid resuscitation. Boluses of IV adrenaline outside cardiac arrest carry significant risk of arrhythmia and hypertensive crisis and are not first-line (ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline every 5 minutes only and avoid IV adrenaline
options.rationale: After two failed IM doses, IM absorption is unreliable in shock; IV infusion is indicated (ASCIA Refractory Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as first-line, no further adrenaline
options.rationale: Glucagon is considered only in patients on beta-blockers with refractory shock; it does not replace adrenaline infusion (ASCIA).

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylaxis = ongoing features after 2 appropriate IM adrenaline doses. Move to IV adrenaline infusion (0.1 microg/kg/min, titrate), aggressive IV fluids (up to 50 mL/kg crystalloid), prepare for early intubation given airway oedema, consider nebulised adrenaline for upper airway and salbutamol for bronchospasm. Glucagon if on beta-blockers; vasopressin if catecholamine-refractory.
key_points: IV adrenaline infusion: 0.1 microg/kg/min start, titrate 0.05-1
Continue fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg, repeat to 50 mL/kg
Early airway control - intubation before complete obstruction
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg for upper airway oedema
Salbutamol for persistent bronchospasm
explanation.pearls: If two correctly placed IM doses have failed, the problem is absorption or severity - not the drug. Move to infusion and call ICU.

q.subtopic_detail: Refractory anaphylaxis after two IM doses

q.stem: A 6-year-old girl, weight 22 kg, was brought in shocked with presumed septic source (community-acquired pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion on bedside lung ultrasound). She received empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg early in the resuscitation. Over the last 40 minutes she has received three 20 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% saline (total 60 mL/kg) and remains tachycardic at 156, with central capillary refill of 4 seconds, cool mottled peripheries, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L Hudson mask. She has not voided since arrival. The hepatic edge is now palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and there are bibasal crackles. Lactate has fallen from 5.4 to 3.8 mmol/L. The retrieval service is on the phone and the paediatric registrar is preparing for central access. The child has no congenital heart disease. She has no known drug allergies and her immunisations including 13-valent pneumococcal and influenza are up to date. Her mother reports a 3-day febrile illness with cough and lethargy treated by the GP with oral amoxicillin 48 hours ago without improvement. There is no chronic medical condition, no recent travel, no immunocompromise and no contact with an unwell sibling. The bedside echocardiogram clip the registrar has just performed shows a hyperdynamic but underfilled left ventricle with no pericardial effusion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, BP 78/40, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Cool mottled peripheries, narrow pulse pressure. Hepatomegaly 1 cm. Bibasal crackles. Anuric.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.8 mmol/L (was 5.4). VBG pH 7.28. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation with small effusion.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence peripheral adrenaline infusion 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min and prepare for intubation
options.rationale: She has fluid-refractory septic shock with cold-shock physiology (narrow pulse pressure, mottled cool peripheries, prolonged CR) and now signs of fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles). Australian guidelines support starting adrenaline as first-line inotrope in paediatric cold septic shock, and peripheral infusion at low-dose concentrations (0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min) is acceptable while central access is obtained. Further large-volume fluid boluses risk worsening overload (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis; APLS; Surviving Sepsis Campaign paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg saline bolus before any vasoactive medication
options.rationale: With hepatomegaly and crackles signalling overload, further large boluses are likely to worsen oedema and hypoxaemia; inotrope is the next step (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Start noradrenaline as first-line inotrope at 0.1 microg/kg/min peripherally
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is preferred in warm shock with wide pulse pressure and vasodilation; in cold shock with poor cardiac output, adrenaline is first-line in paediatric guidelines (APLS).

options.text: Commence dopamine infusion 10 microg/kg/min peripherally as first-line
options.rationale: Dopamine has fallen out of favour as first-line in paediatric septic shock due to inferior outcomes vs adrenaline/noradrenaline (Ventura 2015 RCT; Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as next step, no inotrope
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is an adjunct in catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropic support in fluid-refractory shock (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

explanation.summary: Fluid-refractory shock after 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid requires vasoactive support. Choose adrenaline for cold shock (low cardiac output, high SVR) and noradrenaline for warm shock (high cardiac output, low SVR). Peripheral low-dose infusion is acceptable while central access is established. Watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, S3, increasing oxygen requirement) and stop boluses if present.
key_points: Fluid-refractory = persistent shock after 40-60 mL/kg
Cold shock -> adrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Warm shock -> noradrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Peripheral infusion safe at low concentrations until central access
Hydrocortisone 50 mg/m2/day or 2 mg/kg if catecholamine-resistant
explanation.pearls: If you can feel the liver, you can stop the fluid. Paediatric fluid overload is iatrogenic and worsens outcomes.

q.subtopic_detail: Fluid-refractory septic shock - inotrope choice

q.stem: A 4-month-old, ex-term, breastfed boy is brought to ED by his mother because of unsettled crying for the last 12 hours. She mentions that she noticed two bruises on his cheek and a bruise on his left ear earlier in the day, which she attributes to him knocking himself on a toy in the cot. He has not yet rolled or crawled and is not pulling himself up. There is no preceding illness and no fever. He is fully immunised and has had routine well-child checks with no concerns. Examination reveals a well-grown alert infant with a 2 cm bruise to the right cheek, a 1 cm bruise to the left pinna and a 1.5 cm bruise on the left frontal scalp, no other external injuries, a normal anterior fontanelle, no focal neurology, no retinal haemorrhages on examination by the registrar, normal tone, and a normal abdomen with no organomegaly. Coagulation screen, FBC and LFTs are normal. The mother is alone and tearful when asked about who else cares for the baby. There is no family history of bleeding disorder, no preceding viral illness, and no recent immunisation in the last 7 days.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 142, RR 36, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Three bruises (cheek, ear, frontal scalp). Otherwise unremarkable. Pre-mobile.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, coags, LFTs normal. No CT or skeletal survey yet.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send for outpatient coagulation studies only and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Coags are already normal and a bleeding disorder does not explain pre-mobile bruising in suspicious patterns; comprehensive abuse workup and notification are required (RCH CPG).

options.text: Reassure the mother and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are never normal - 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise'. Discharge without investigation and mandatory reporting is a serious safeguarding failure (RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, CT head, ophthalmology review, child protection consult and mandatory report
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant meets the TEN-4 FACESp rule (Torso, Ear, Neck, age <=4 months; Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned) and is a strong red flag for inflicted injury. This child has all four red-flag locations triggered. Mandatory next steps are full skeletal survey (and repeat at 2 weeks), non-contrast CT head, dilated ophthalmology examination for retinal haemorrhages, paediatric team and child protection consultation, and mandatory notification to the state child protection authority. Doctors are mandated reporters in every Australian state and territory (RCH CPG Child abuse; AAP TEN-4 FACES).
source_refs: Pierce et al. TEN-4 FACESp validation 2021

options.text: Order a skeletal survey only, without notifying child protection, pending the imaging result
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not by waiting for confirmatory imaging; delay risks further harm and breaches statutory duty (Children and Young Persons Acts; RCH CPG).

options.text: Confront the mother directly about possible abuse before doing any investigations
options.rationale: Confrontation is not the role of the treating clinician; the priority is the child's safety, the workup, multidisciplinary consultation and statutory notification (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is the most specific sign of physical abuse in early childhood. Apply the TEN-4 FACESp rule: bruises in any of these regions or in a child <=4 months are highly suspicious. Investigations: full skeletal survey (with 2-week repeat), CT head, dilated fundus exam, coagulation screen. Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions for doctors with reasonable suspicion.
key_points: Those who don't cruise rarely bruise
TEN-4: Torso, Ear, Neck, age 4 months and under
FACESp: Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned
Skeletal survey at presentation and repeat at 2 weeks (occult fractures)
Mandatory reporting is by suspicion, not certainty - this is statutory
explanation.pearls: Document objectively, do not investigate the social history beyond what is clinically necessary, and let the multidisciplinary team and statutory agency lead the safeguarding process.

sources.label: Pierce et al. TEN-4 FACESp validation 2021
AIFS Mandatory reporting of child abuse

q.subtopic_detail: Non-accidental injury in pre-mobile infant

q.stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, was pulled from a backyard pool by her father approximately 6 minutes ago after an unwitnessed submersion estimated at 3-5 minutes. She was apnoeic and unresponsive at poolside. Her father began chest compressions immediately and bystander CPR continued until paramedics arrived 4 minutes ago. Paramedics found her in asystole, established a supraglottic airway, ventilated with 100% oxygen and continued compressions; ROSC was achieved after 3 minutes of resuscitation. She arrives in ED still intubated (now endotracheal), with HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on 100% FiO2, T 33.4 degC tympanic, GCS 3 with no sedation, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and the CXR is in progress. The pool was uncovered and the temperature ambient. There was no chlorine exposure or chemical contamination of the water. She had no known medical conditions, no medications, no allergies, and her immunisations are up to date. There is no family history of arrhythmia, sudden cardiac death, long QT syndrome, or epilepsy that might have preceded the submersion. Her father witnessed the entire event from the kitchen window and estimates the maximum time submerged at 5 minutes. An indwelling urinary catheter has drained 20 mL of clear urine since insertion 15 minutes ago, and the PICU retrieval team has been activated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on FiO2 1.0, T 33.4 degC, GCS 3.
data_table.Examination: Intubated and ventilated. Bilateral crackles. Cool peripheries. Pupils 4 mm, sluggish.
data_table.Investigations: Lung US: bilateral B-lines. VBG pending. Bedside glucose 7.1 mmol/L.

q.lead_in: Which best reflects current ANZCOR-aligned management priorities for this child?

options.text: Withdraw care now given asystole at scene; outcome is uniformly poor
options.rationale: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion time can have favourable neurological outcomes; early prognostication is unreliable and resuscitation should continue with PICU input (ANZCOR).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg and target SpO2 100% on FiO2 1.0
options.rationale: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen post-arrest neurological outcomes through cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative injury; target normoxia and normocapnia (ANZCOR).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

options.text: Aggressive rapid rewarming to 38 degC plus prophylactic broad-spectrum IV ceftriaxone now
options.rationale: Rapid rewarming and induced hyperthermia worsen neurological outcomes; antibiotics are reserved for evidence of infection or contaminated water exposure with specific organisms (ANZCOR).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Early extubation because the lungs are clear on auscultation and CXR is unremarkable
options.rationale: Pulmonary oedema and ARDS from drowning can be delayed (up to 8 hours); the patient has bilateral B-lines and a low GCS, both contraindicating early extubation (ANZCOR).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Lung-protective ventilation with PEEP, controlled rewarming to 36 degC, post-arrest care, discuss ECMO retrieval
options.rationale: Post-drowning care follows ANZCOR resuscitation principles plus post-cardiac-arrest care. Five initial rescue breaths precede compressions in drowning because hypoxia is the proximate cause. After ROSC, target normoxia (SpO2 94-98%), normocapnia, lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, treat shock with fluids and adrenaline, and discuss VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h (do not over-rewarm; mild controlled hypothermia is no longer routinely targeted but normothermia is). Antibiotics are not routine.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

explanation.summary: Drowning is primarily an asphyxial injury - prioritise oxygenation. Bystander BLS for drowning: 5 rescue breaths first, then 30:2 CPR (lay rescuer) or 15:2 (two trained rescuers in a child). Post-ROSC: lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, normoxia, normocapnia, treat shock, rewarm to 36 degC, manage as post-cardiac-arrest syndrome. Consider VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Late ARDS can develop up to 8 hours later.
key_points: 5 rescue breaths first in drowning (not compressions first)
Target SpO2 94-98% post-ROSC; avoid hyperoxia and hypocapnia
PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS; lung-protective tidal volumes
Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h; avoid hyperthermia
Antibiotics not routine; consider if contaminated water or sepsis develops
explanation.pearls: The lungs of a drowned child can look normal at 1 hour and ARDS by 8 hours. Do not extubate to room air based on the initial CXR.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

q.subtopic_detail: Paediatric drowning resuscitation

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl, weight 60 kg, is brought to ED by her mother 5 hours after ingesting an unknown number of paracetamol 500 mg tablets in an acute single ingestion. She is now tearful but cooperative, denies co-ingestion, has no abdominal pain, no jaundice, and a soft non-tender abdomen. She has no chronic liver disease and is not on enzyme-inducing medications. Vital signs are normal. Bloods drawn 4 hours and 30 minutes post-ingestion show paracetamol concentration 165 mg/L, ALT 28 U/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 58 micromol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, and a negative beta-hCG. The empty packet retrieved at home suggests she may have taken approximately 14 g (around 230 mg/kg) but the ingestion history is uncertain. The Australian paracetamol nomogram has a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 hours, dropping to 75 mg/L at 8 hours and 37 mg/L at 12 hours, after which the line is extended to 24 hours. She has no history of self-harm, no current psychiatric medications, no chronic medical illness and no regular paracetamol or hepatic enzyme-inducing medications. She lives at home with her parents, attends Year 10 and has been struggling academically and socially over the past 2 months. Her mother brought her in immediately on finding the empty packet and a written note in her bedroom. The CAMHS on-call clinician has been contacted, the empty Panadol packet has been retained for documentation, and the toxicology registrar has reviewed the bloods and the timing of the level.

data_table.Examination: Alert, tearful. No jaundice. Soft non-tender abdomen.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 165 mg/L at 4.5 h. ALT 28. INR 1.0. Creatinine 58. Bicarbonate 24. Lactate 1.4. Glucose 5.0. beta-hCG negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 8 hours and only commence NAC if above 75 mg/L
options.rationale: If the 4-hour-equivalent level is already above the treatment line, NAC should be commenced immediately rather than waiting; delaying NAC beyond 8 hours significantly reduces its hepatoprotective efficacy (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: IV N-acetylcysteine two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) and admit
options.rationale: At 4.5 hours post-ingestion the paracetamol concentration of 165 mg/L lies above the Australian 150 mg/L treatment line on the modified Rumack-Matthew nomogram (extrapolated back to 4 h would be above 150 mg/L). The reported dose also exceeds the 200 mg/kg supratherapeutic threshold. NAC is indicated. The Australian Poisons Information Centre and AMH endorse the two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions versus the traditional three-bag regimen. Psychiatric assessment is required after any deliberate self-poisoning (AMH; Australian PIC; Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and Wilderness).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Give oral activated charcoal 1 g/kg now and reassess level at 12 hours
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion (up to 4 hours for sustained release); at 5 hours post-ingestion it is unlikely to be useful, and NAC should not be delayed (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Discharge with mental health follow-up only; level is below treatment line and dose under 200 mg/kg
options.rationale: 165 mg/L at 4.5 hours is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and the reported dose of approximately 230 mg/kg exceeds 200 mg/kg; NAC is indicated. Even if there is uncertainty, the higher of the two assessment methods (level vs dose) directs management (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Commence oral methionine and arrange urgent liver transplant assessment at tertiary centre now
options.rationale: IV NAC is the standard antidote in Australia; oral methionine is not used. Transplant assessment is reserved for established fulminant hepatic failure (King's College criteria) which is not present here (AMH).

explanation.summary: Acute single paracetamol ingestion: take a 4-hour-or-later level and plot on the Australian nomogram with a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 h.
