NAC indicated if level above line, OR dose >200 mg/kg (or >10 g) with no level available within 8 hours, OR staggered/repeated supratherapeutic ingestion, OR ALT elevated. Use the two-bag IV NAC regimen: 200 mg/kg over 4 h then 100 mg/kg over 16 h. After deliberate self-poisoning, mental health assessment is mandatory.
key_points: Australian nomogram: single 150 mg/L line at 4 h, log-linear to 24 h
Two-bag NAC: 200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h (fewer reactions vs 3-bag)
Supratherapeutic: >200 mg/kg or >10 g acute
Charcoal useful only within 1-2 h (up to 4 h for SR)
Repeat ALT, INR, paracetamol at end of NAC; continue NAC if ALT rising or INR >1.3
explanation.pearls: Never withhold NAC waiting for a perfect history. When dose, level and time are uncertain, treat - hepatotoxicity is the unrecoverable error.

sources.label: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

q.subtopic_detail: Paracetamol overdose - Rumack-Matthew interpretation

q.stem: A 3-year-old boy with longstanding moderate atopic dermatitis is brought to ED by his mother with a 36-hour history of a rapidly worsening rash on his face and neck, fever to 38.7 degC, and poor oral intake. He has been using methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment twice weekly proactively and an emollient four times daily, with good control until this week. There has been no overseas travel and his immunisations are up to date including MMR and varicella. His maternal grandfather had a cold sore on his lip when he kissed the patient three days ago. On examination he is miserable, drinking small sips, with grouped 2-3 mm punched-out monomorphic erosions and umbilicated vesicles in clusters on the left cheek, periorbital region and chin, some now coalescing. There is no honey-coloured crusting elsewhere, no ocular involvement on fluorescein staining, no neck stiffness, and no focal neurology. His pre-existing flexural eczema is otherwise stable. There are scattered satellite lesions on the right cheek and on his chest. He has had no contact with hand-foot-and-mouth disease at day care, no prior episodes of this kind, and his last wet nappy was 8 hours ago. There is no fluorescein uptake on the left cornea on bedside slit-lamp by the ED registrar, no preauricular lymphadenopathy, and his capillary refill is 2 seconds. He has been started on IV maintenance fluids and a swab from a deroofed vesicle has been couriered to the lab for HSV PCR.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% on room air, capillary refill 2 sec.
data_table.Examination: Clusters of monomorphic umbilicated 2-3 mm vesicles and punched-out erosions on left cheek, periorbital and chin. No corneal fluorescein uptake. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside HSV PCR swab from a deroofed vesicle sent. FBC, CRP, U&E pending.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Daily bleach baths and increase topical methylprednisolone aceponate to twice daily on the face
options.rationale: Potent TCS escalation on the face is not appropriate, particularly with concurrent HSV; bleach baths do not treat established HSV (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for five days and continue topical corticosteroids as usual
options.rationale: This is HSV, not impetigo; mupirocin will not treat the viral infection and continuing TCS over active HSV can worsen dissemination (TG Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg four times daily for seven days for impetiginised eczema
options.rationale: Honey-crusting and polymorphic pustules would suggest S aureus impetigo; the monomorphic umbilicated vesicle morphology and HSV contact indicate eczema herpeticum (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Admit, treat as eczema herpeticum: IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly, urgent ophthalmology
options.rationale: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, fever, an HSV contact and rapid progression in an atopic child are eczema herpeticum. Periorbital involvement risks HSV keratitis, mandating admission, IV aciclovir and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG Eczema herpeticum).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Discharge home on oral valaciclovir with planned review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Periorbital involvement, fever and poor oral intake in a 3-year-old with eczema herpeticum warrant admission for IV therapy and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

explanation.summary: Eczema herpeticum is disseminated HSV in atopic skin, presenting with monomorphic 2-3 mm umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, systemic features and an HSV contact. Periocular disease threatens the cornea and mandates IV aciclovir plus ophthalmology review.
key_points: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles = HSV (eczema herpeticum)
Polymorphic pustules with honey-crust = S aureus impetiginised eczema
Periocular HSV = admit, IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h, ophthalmology
Withhold TCS on actively infected sites until controlled
Co-infection with S aureus is common; cover if features suggest
explanation.pearls: If the morphology is monomorphic, think herpetic. If you see punched-out erosions in an atopic child, treat as HSV until proven otherwise.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

q.subtopic_detail: Eczema flare with painful clustered vesicles

q.stem: A 7-week-old boy is brought to ED by his parents because he has not opened his bowels for 6 days and has had increasing abdominal distension and two episodes of bilious vomiting overnight. He was born at 39 weeks by normal vaginal delivery, weighing 3.5 kg. The mother reports that he did not pass meconium until day 3 of life and the midwife had needed to perform a rectal stimulation. He is exclusively formula-fed at 90 mL every 3 hours but has been taking less today. His weight today is 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.5 kg). When the registrar performs a careful digital rectal examination there is an explosive release of foul gas and liquid stool. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. There is no fever. The anus is normally positioned, the sacrum is intact and lower limb neurology is normal. The plain abdominal X-ray shows dilated bowel loops with a paucity of rectal gas. There is no maternal history of opioid or recreational drug use in pregnancy. Antenatal scans were unremarkable and there was no polyhydramnios. The newborn screening heel-prick was reported as normal including immunoreactive trypsinogen. He has no associated dysmorphism, no abnormal facies and no cardiac murmur. Calorie intake by parental report is approximately 130 mL/kg/day of standard infant formula. The surgical registrar has been contacted and an IV cannula sited; nasogastric tube is on standby for decompression while imaging is reviewed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 98%, capillary refill 3 sec.
data_table.Examination: Distended abdomen, tympanic, ribbon-like rectum on PR, explosive release on PR. Anus normally sited. No sacral dimple. Weight gain inadequate (only 700 g over 7 weeks).
data_table.Investigations: AXR: dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas. FBC, U&E, VBG sent.

q.lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Functional constipation; commence oral macrogol disimpaction and discharge with GP review
options.rationale: Delayed meconium passage >48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting and explosive PR are red flags for an organic cause; discharge without further investigation is unsafe (RCH CPG Constipation).

options.text: Anal stenosis; perform serial anal dilatation in the GP clinic
options.rationale: Anal stenosis presents with a narrow caudal anus on examination; here the anus is normally sited and the obstruction is functional (aganglionic segment), not anatomical at the anus (RCH CPG).

options.text: Cow's milk protein allergy; trial hydrolysed formula and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: CMPA causes loose stool, blood-streaked stool or eczema/dermatitis rather than this picture of delayed meconium and obstruction (RCH CPG).

options.text: Hirschsprung disease; refer for surgical review and arrange suction rectal biopsy
options.rationale: Delayed meconium >48 hours, abdominal distension, faltering growth, ribbon stool with explosive release on PR and bilious vomiting are classic for Hirschsprung disease. Suction rectal biopsy demonstrating aganglionosis is the diagnostic gold standard (RCH CPG; RACS).

options.text: Hypothyroidism; check TSH and free T4 before any further workup
options.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn heel-prick (Guthrie) and typically causes prolonged jaundice and lethargy; it does not produce delayed meconium with explosive rectal release (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Red flags that mandate investigation for an organic cause of constipation in infancy include delayed meconium >48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, ribbon stool, abnormal anus/sacrum/neurology and explosive release on PR. The leading diagnosis is Hirschsprung disease; suction rectal biopsy is diagnostic.
key_points: Delayed meconium >48 hours = think Hirschsprung
Explosive release on PR is a classic clue
Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic test
Total colonic Hirschsprung can be associated with Down syndrome
Bilious vomiting in an infant is surgical until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Always ask 'when did meconium pass?' and 'did the midwife need to do anything to get it out?' in any constipated infant.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Bilious vomiting

q.subtopic_detail: Red flag features for organic cause of constipation in an infant

q.stem: An 8-year-old boy is reviewed by a paediatrician at the request of his GP and school. For the last 18 months he has had difficulty sustaining attention to schoolwork, frequently leaves his seat, interrupts conversations, loses his pencil case daily and forgets multi-step instructions. His Year 3 teacher and his swimming coach have completed Conners questionnaires which were both above clinical cut-offs. His mother reports similar behaviours at home, including weekends and school holidays, since pre-school. He has had no academic regression, has age-appropriate reading and numeracy, has friends but tends to fall out with them over impulsive comments, and has no features of low mood, anxiety, tics, autism or psychosis. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death, long QT or cardiomyopathy. On examination he is well, height 50th centile, weight 60th centile, BP 100/64, HR 86 with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. His mother has separately disclosed that she also suspects ADHD in herself and is interested in being assessed after her son's care is settled. There is no history of head injury, no perinatal hypoxia, no seizures and no language delay. He has no symptoms of restless legs, no snoring on direct questioning, and his sleep onset latency is appropriate. There is no anxiety disorder, no oppositional defiant features beyond developmental norms, and no learning disability identified on school psychometric assessment. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 100/64, HR 86, regular rhythm.
data_table.Examination: No dysmorphism. Normal cardiovascular and respiratory exam. No tics observed.
data_table.Investigations: Conners ratings above cut-off from two informants. ECG: sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms, no LVH, no pre-excitation.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of restricted elimination diet for 6 weeks before pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Restrictive diets are not recommended as a primary intervention; behavioural and environmental supports plus stimulants where indicated are first-line (RACGP; TG Psychotropic).

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 0.5 mg/kg/day as first-line pharmacotherapy without a stimulant trial
options.rationale: Stimulants (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) are first-line in children with ADHD without significant contraindications; atomoxetine is reserved for stimulant intolerance, tics, anxiety or risk of diversion (AADPA ADHD Guideline 2022).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg BD under paediatrician authorisation, behavioural strategies, monitor side effects
options.rationale: He meets DSM-5 ADHD criteria (>=6 months, two settings, functional impairment). Methylphenidate is first-line; in Australia, stimulants require state authority/TGA pathway via a paediatrician. Behavioural interventions run in parallel and monitoring includes growth, BP, HR and mental state (AADPA 2022; RACGP).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Watchful waiting for a further 12 months with a school behavioural plan only
options.rationale: Symptoms have been present >6 months with functional impairment in two settings; further delay risks academic and social harm. Behavioural strategies are necessary but not sufficient (AADPA 2022).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Refer for cardiology review and stress echocardiogram before any pharmacotherapy now
options.rationale: Routine cardiology review is not required when history, examination and resting ECG are unremarkable; only red flags (family sudden death, syncope on exertion, abnormal cardiac exam or ECG) mandate cardiology input (AADPA 2022).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

explanation.summary: ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptom counts present for >=6 months, in >=2 settings, causing functional impairment, with onset before age 12. First-line pharmacotherapy is a stimulant (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) under paediatrician supervision and state authority/TGA authorisation, alongside behavioural and educational supports.
key_points: DSM-5: >=6 inattentive and/or >=6 hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (<17 yo)
Two settings, >=6 months, onset <12 years, functional impairment
Pre-stimulant: cardiac history, family SCD history, exam, ECG
Monitor height, weight, BP, HR, mood, sleep, appetite
Stimulants are S8 in Australia and require state authority
explanation.pearls: School-only symptoms suggest a learning disorder or environment problem; home-only symptoms suggest parenting or attachment. ADHD is across settings.

sources.label: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

q.subtopic_detail: Stimulant initiation in school-aged inattention and hyperactivity

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl presents to her GP with her mother, who has found superficial linear cuts on her daughter's forearm. The patient initially declines to speak in front of her mother, so the GP sees her alone after confidentiality is explained. Over a confidential HEEADSSS interview she describes 4 months of low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, poor concentration affecting her grades, weight loss of 3 kg, and feelings of hopelessness. She has been cutting her forearm with a razor weekly for 6 weeks for emotional relief. She reports passive thoughts that she would rather not be alive, but denies a current plan, intent, access to means, or previous attempts. There is no substance use, she is sexually inexperienced, and there is no disclosure of abuse. Her older brother has bipolar disorder. She agrees that her mother can be involved in safety planning. Examination shows superficial linear forearm cuts, no active bleeding, no concerning weight loss on growth chart, BMI 19. She has had no inpatient medical or psychiatric admissions previously. She has supportive friendships at school but has withdrawn from her netball team over the past 2 months. There is no history of trauma, sexual abuse or domestic violence on confidential enquiry, and no eating disorder behaviours (no restriction, no binge-purge, no body image preoccupation). She is not on any regular medications. Her mother has a history of depression treated with sertraline some years ago. She agrees to a follow-up plan and accepts referral to local youth mental health services.

data_table.Examination: Multiple superficial linear forearm cuts. Affect flat. Speech soft but coherent. No psychotic features. No infection.
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated at the initial visit.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Maintain confidentiality from the parents in all circumstances given she is a Gillick-competent minor
options.rationale: Confidentiality has limits when there is risk of harm; involving a supportive parent in safety planning improves outcomes and should be sought with the adolescent's consent, while respecting confidentiality on non-safety matters (RACGP; headspace).

options.text: Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily today with no psychological referral, and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate adolescent depression; fluoxetine (if used) should be started at 10 mg with close monitoring for activation/suicidality in the first 4 weeks, alongside psychological care (TG Psychotropic; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Admit involuntarily to a secure adolescent inpatient mental health unit today
options.rationale: Involuntary admission is reserved for acute risk with intent and plan or inability to maintain safety. With passive ideation, no plan or intent, family support, and willingness to engage, community management with safety planning is appropriate (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Written safety plan (means restriction, supports), involve mother, GP within 1 week, refer CAMHS
options.rationale: Adolescent depression with self-harm and passive suicidal ideation requires a documented safety plan with means restriction, family involvement (with consent where possible), early review, and specialist mental health referral. headspace and CAMHS provide tiered care. Pharmacotherapy is considered when psychological therapy is insufficient or symptoms are moderate-severe and persistent (RANZCP; headspace).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Reassure that self-harm without suicidal intent is normal adolescence and review 3 months
options.rationale: Self-harm with persistent low mood and passive suicidal ideation is a marker of mental illness and elevated future suicide risk; it requires structured assessment and intervention (RANZCP; headspace).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

explanation.summary: Adolescent depression with self-harm requires a HEEADSSS-based assessment, structured suicide risk assessment, a written safety plan, family involvement with consent, and tiered referral (GP -> headspace -> CAMHS). Fluoxetine is the first-line SSRI for adolescents with moderate-severe depression, started low with early review for activation.
key_points: HEEADSSS: Home, Education/Employment, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/self-harm, Safety
Safety plan: warning signs, coping, distractions, social supports, professional contacts, means restriction
Fluoxetine is the SSRI with the best evidence in adolescent depression
Black-box: monitor closely for activation/suicidality first 4 weeks of SSRI
headspace is the national youth mental health service (12-25 yo)
explanation.pearls: Means restriction (lock medicines, remove blades) is the single highest-impact element of a safety plan.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

q.subtopic_detail: Adolescent low mood with self-harm

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to ED by her parents after collapsing on standing. Over 9 months her weight has dropped from 58 kg to 38 kg; her height is 165 cm (BMI 14.0). She restricts intake to <600 kcal/day, exercises for 2-3 hours daily and induces vomiting after any meal she perceives as 'too much'. She has not menstruated for 5 months. She insists she is fat. There has been no binge eating, no laxative use, no diuretic use, no substance use, no overt depressive symptoms beyond food preoccupation, no suicidal ideation. Examination: alert, cooperative, dry mucous membranes, lanugo on the back, BP 84/52 supine dropping to 70/40 on standing with HR 38 rising to 64, T 35.4 degC, capillary refill 3 sec, peripherally cool. ECG shows sinus bradycardia, QTc 470 ms. Bedside investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, phosphate 0.7, magnesium 0.6, glucose 3.4, urea 6.2, creatinine 64, ALT 110. She had a normal birth and early development, sat at 6 months, walked at 13 months, achieved menarche at 12, and was previously academically high-performing. She denies laxative or diuretic use. She has no chest pain, no palpitations on exertion, no syncope episodes other than this collapse. She has no haematemesis, no diarrhoea, no constipation other than infrequent stool, and no abdominal pain. Family history: maternal aunt with bulimia nervosa, no thyroid disease, no diabetes, no coeliac disease. She has not previously engaged with any mental health service. Her parents are both present at the bedside and engaged in care.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/52 supine -> 70/40 standing, HR 38 -> 64 standing, T 35.4 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 14.0. Lanugo. Cool peripheries. Capillary refill 3 sec. No focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, PO4 0.7 mmol/L, Mg 0.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, ALT 110 U/L. ECG: sinus bradycardia 38, QTc 470 ms.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient paediatric dietitian and weekly community GP review
options.rationale: She meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria (HR <45, postural BP drop >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc prolongation, hypophosphataemia, hypoglycaemia); inpatient admission is mandatory (RANZCP; ANZAED; MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nightly for body image distortion and discharge home
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive in eating disorders, not a substitute for medical stabilisation and family-based treatment, and discharge home would be unsafe (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Admit and commence high-calorie refeeding at 2000 kcal/day from day 1 to rapidly restore weight
options.rationale: Aggressive refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome (fluid overload, severe hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, Wernicke encephalopathy); refeeding should start cautiously and titrate up (MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa

options.text: Admit to psychiatric ward and defer medical management until psychiatric review tomorrow
options.rationale: She is medically unstable with electrolyte derangement and bradycardia; medical stabilisation precedes or runs alongside psychiatric admission (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Admit medical bed; cautious refeeding (~5-10 kcal/kg/day), daily electrolyte monitoring, prophylactic phosphate/thiamine, refer mental health
options.rationale: She has severe anorexia nervosa with refeeding syndrome risk (low PO4, low Mg, low K, low glucose). MARSIPAN-AU mandates medical admission with cautious refeeding, electrolyte and glucose monitoring, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia, and FBT engagement (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: MARSIPAN-AU high-risk indicators for anorexia nervosa admission include BMI <14 (or <13 in adolescents), HR <45, postural BP fall >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc >450 ms, hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypoglycaemia and acute medical instability. Refeeding starts low (5-10 kcal/kg/day) with daily electrolytes, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring.
key_points: Refeeding syndrome: low PO4, K, Mg; fluid shifts; arrhythmia; Wernicke
Start refeeding cautiously - 'start low, go slow' especially in severe AN
Prophylactic thiamine 100 mg before any glucose-containing fluids
Family-Based Treatment (FBT/Maudsley) is first-line psychotherapy for adolescent AN
Monitor QTc daily during refeeding
explanation.pearls: The first 72 hours of refeeding kill people, not the starvation. Phosphate is the canary.

sources.label: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

q.subtopic_detail: Severely underweight adolescent with food restriction

q.stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 40+3 in spontaneous labour has had an epidural sited two hours ago with a top-up given 25 minutes ago for a left-sided breakthrough. Her booking BMI was 24, she is a non-smoker, has no medical comorbidities, and the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. The 36-week growth scan showed an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with normal Dopplers. The last vaginal examination 40 minutes ago showed 8 cm dilatation, +0 station, direct OA, intact caput, no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 6 hours ago with clear liquor. Oxytocin augmentation was started 90 minutes ago for slow progress between 6 and 8 cm and is currently at 12 mU/min with contractions 4 in 10, lasting 70 seconds, palpating moderate. Maternal observations are stable: BP 118/72, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99% on room air. The CTG over the last 30 minutes shows a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability of 3 to 5 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent late decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions for the last 25 minutes. There has been no prolonged deceleration. The mother is comfortable and the partogram shows progress consistent with primary dysfunctional labour. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 8 cm, +0, direct OA, clear liquor. Uterus soft between contractions. Palpates 4 in 10 moderate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 145 bpm, variability 3-5 bpm, no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations >50% of contractions x 25 min. No tachysystole on tocodynamometer.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Stop oxytocin, reposition to left lateral, give 500 mL IV crystalloid, perform foetal scalp lactate within 15 minutes
options.rationale: This CTG is FIGO pathological (recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability) but without a sustained bradycardia, so intrauterine resuscitation plus foetal scalp blood sampling for lactate is the appropriate next step before escalating to category 1 caesarean (RANZCOG IFSM 2019; FIGO 2015).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019
FIGO consensus guidelines on intrapartum foetal monitoring 2015

options.text: Continue oxytocin and observe for a further 30 minutes to determine if the pattern resolves spontaneously
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 25 minutes is pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens foetal acidosis and is contraindicated (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Proceed immediately to a category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval
options.rationale: Category 1 CS is reserved for acute foetal compromise such as prolonged bradycardia, abruption or cord prolapse; a pathological trace without acute decompensation warrants foetal scalp sampling first when feasible (RANZCOG; NICE NG229).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for acute tocolysis and continue oxytocin at the current rate
options.rationale: Terbutaline tocolysis is indicated for tachysystole-related foetal compromise; there is no tachysystole here and continuing oxytocin is inappropriate when the trace is pathological (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of membranes and apply a foetal scalp electrode without further intrauterine resuscitation
options.rationale: Membranes are already ruptured and applying an FSE without addressing the pathological pattern is insufficient; intrauterine resuscitation and foetal scalp lactate are the indicated next steps (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

explanation.summary: A FIGO pathological CTG without acute decompensation is managed with intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin, left lateral, IV fluid, exclude hypotension, exclude tachysystole, consider tocolysis) and foetal scalp lactate sampling. Lactate >=4.8 mmol/L mandates delivery; 4.2-4.7 repeat in 30 minutes; <4.2 reassuring.
key_points: FIGO pathological = >=2 abnormal features OR sinusoidal OR prolonged bradycardia >5 min
Reduced variability + recurrent late decelerations = pathological
Intrauterine resuscitation mnemonic: SPOILT (Stop oxytocin, Position, Oxygen [no longer routinely recommended], IV fluid, Lower BP causes, Tocolysis)
Foetal scalp lactate cut-offs: <4.2 normal, 4.2-4.7 borderline, >=4.8 abnormal
Category 1 CS target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Reduced variability is the most predictive single feature of foetal acidosis. Recurrent late decelerations with a flat trace is the pattern that should provoke action even without bradycardia.

sources.label: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019
FIGO consensus guidelines on intrapartum foetal monitoring 2015

q.subtopic_detail: Intrapartum CTG - recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 39+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has an epidural in situ that was sited 90 minutes ago and has provided good analgesia until the last hour, when she began to feel a sharp left-sided sensation with contractions. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no cardiac, respiratory or neurological comorbidities, no allergies, no anaphylaxis history, and her booking bloods including FBC and group and screen were unremarkable. Five minutes ago the anaesthetic registrar gave a top-up of 10 mL of 0.1% ropivacaine with 2 microg/mL fentanyl for breakthrough pain. She is currently positioned semi-recumbent with a left lateral wedge in place. Her BP has now dropped from 122/74 to 84/46 mmHg with a maternal HR of 102. She feels light-headed but is conscious and is not nauseated, has no perioral numbness, no tinnitus and no metallic taste. The CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm that has now been ongoing for 4 minutes after previously reassuring features (baseline 140, variability 10-15, accelerations present). There has been no vaginal bleeding, the uterus is soft between contractions (no hypertonus), the liquor was clear at ARM 30 minutes earlier, and there is no concern for cord prolapse on bedside vaginal examination. The anaesthetic registrar is at the bedside and IV access is patent through a 16G cannula.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/46 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (maternal), T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Awake, light-headed. Uterus soft between contractions. No PV bleeding. Cord not palpable.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm for 4 minutes following epidural top-up.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Ephedrine 6 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Ephedrine is acceptable but ANZCA and the contemporary obstetric anaesthesia literature favour phenylephrine for spinal/epidural hypotension because ephedrine crosses the placenta and increases foetal acidaemia (ANZCA; Lee 2018 meta-analysis).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Metaraminol 0.5 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Metaraminol is a reasonable alpha-agonist alternative and is commonly used in Australia, but phenylephrine has the strongest evidence base and is the first-choice vasopressor in obstetric neuraxial hypotension (ANZCA; SOAP consensus 2018).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Phenylephrine 100 microg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus is the first-line vasopressor for neuraxial-induced maternal hypotension because it preserves uterine blood flow and reduces foetal acidaemia compared with ephedrine; combined with left uterine displacement and a fluid bolus this typically resolves the prolonged deceleration (ANZCA; SOAP consensus 2018).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia
SOAP Consensus Statement on hypotension management 2018

options.text: Adrenaline 10 microg IV bolus and prepare for category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Adrenaline boluses are reserved for cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis; this is a treatable neuraxial hypotension and the deceleration should resolve with vasopressor plus position change and fluid (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Atropine 600 microg IV bolus to correct the maternal heart rate
options.rationale: Maternal heart rate is already 102; atropine treats bradycardia, not hypotension, and would not correct uteroplacental hypoperfusion (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

explanation.summary: Prolonged deceleration following an epidural top-up is most commonly due to maternal hypotension reducing uteroplacental perfusion. Manage with full left lateral position, IV crystalloid bolus 250-500 mL, and phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus (first-line). If the deceleration does not resolve within 9 minutes, prepare for category 1 caesarean.
key_points: Phenylephrine first-line for neuraxial hypotension in obstetrics
Ephedrine causes more foetal acidaemia (crosses placenta, beta-agonist on foetal heart)
Metaraminol is an acceptable Australian alternative
Always exclude cord prolapse, abruption and uterine rupture as causes of prolonged deceleration
9-minute rule: prolonged deceleration not recovering requires immediate delivery decision
explanation.pearls: Treat the maternal BP first; the foetal heart rate almost always follows. If you are reaching for atropine for the baby, you have the wrong drug.

sources.label: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia
SOAP Consensus Statement on hypotension management 2018

q.subtopic_detail: Prolonged deceleration after epidural top-up - vasopressor choice

q.stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 presents at 32+1 weeks with a 30-minute history of moderate fresh vaginal bleeding. She had spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes before the bleeding started, with a sudden gush of clear liquor followed almost immediately by bright red blood. She has not had abdominal pain at any point and reports no abdominal trauma in the preceding week. Her two previous births were both lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 18 months ago and the first 4 years ago), both for failure to progress in the first stage; she has not been counselled about VBAC this pregnancy and the antenatal plan was for an elective repeat CS at 39 weeks. Her 20-week morphology scan showed a low-lying anterior placenta (3 cm from the os) and a velamentous cord insertion with a vessel coursing close to the internal os, but a repeat 32-week transvaginal scan to characterise the cord vessel and re-measure placental edge was scheduled for next week. She is otherwise well, BMI 28, non-smoker, no cocaine or amphetamine use, no antihypertensive use, and no history of chronic hypertension or pre-eclampsia in previous pregnancies. On arrival she is mildly tachycardic at 102 with a normal BP of 124/76. The CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with a baseline of 145 bpm and reduced variability. The uterus is soft and non-tender between contractions and there is no maternal pain on speculum examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, non-tender. Speculum: moderate fresh blood at os, cervix appears closed. SROM confirmed.
data_table.Investigations: 20-week USS: low-lying anterior placenta, velamentous cord, vessel near internal os. Two previous LSCS. CTG: sinusoidal, baseline 145 bpm, reduced variability.

q.lead_in: What is the most likely cause of the antepartum haemorrhage in this woman?

options.text: Placenta praevia with marginal sinus bleeding from the lower segment
options.rationale: Praevia is suggested by the low-lying placenta but classically causes painless bleeding without ROM as the trigger; the sinusoidal CTG immediately after SROM points strongly to vasa praevia rupture (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 27a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

options.text: Placental abruption secondary to undisclosed cocaine use or trauma
options.rationale: Abruption typically causes painful bleeding with a tender, irritable or tonic uterus; the uterus here is soft and non-tender, and there is no history of trauma or stimulant use (RANZCOG).

options.text: Vasa praevia with ruptured foetal vessel at the internal os
options.rationale: Velamentous cord insertion with a vessel near the internal os, bleeding triggered immediately by SROM, and a sinusoidal CTG (indicating foetal anaemia) is the classic triad of vasa praevia rupture with foetal exsanguination. Requires immediate category 1 CS (RCOG GTG 27a; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

options.text: Uterine rupture along the previous lower-segment caesarean scar from prior delivery
options.rationale: Uterine rupture causes severe maternal pain, scar tenderness, maternal haemodynamic compromise, and often loss of foetal station; the uterus here is soft and non-tender and she is not in labour (RANZCOG VBAC).

options.text: Heavy bloody show from established early preterm labour onset now
options.rationale: Show is typically blood-stained mucous in small volume; moderate fresh bleeding after SROM with a sinusoidal trace is not consistent (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia rupture is rare but devastating: bleeding is foetal blood, not maternal, so the foetus exsanguinates rapidly while the mother appears haemodynamically intact. Classic triad: ROM, vaginal bleeding, foetal compromise (often sinusoidal CTG). Requires immediate category 1 CS. Antenatal detection at 20-week morphology scan is the key prevention strategy.
key_points: Vasa praevia: ROM-triggered foetal bleeding, sinusoidal CTG, mother haemodynamically stable
Praevia: painless maternal bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, recurrent
Abruption: painful bleeding (or concealed), tender/tonic uterus, may have DIC
Rupture: severe pain, scar tenderness, haemodynamic collapse, loss of station
Vasa praevia detected antenatally requires elective CS at 34-36 weeks after steroids
explanation.pearls: If the mother looks well but the baby looks terrible (sinusoidal CTG) after ROM, think vasa praevia and get to theatre immediately.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

q.subtopic_detail: Antepartum haemorrhage at 32 weeks - differential

q.stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 is admitted with a 24-hour history of severe frontal headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. She booked at 14 weeks with a BP of 116/72 and no proteinuria; her booking BMI was 23 and she has no chronic hypertension, no diabetes and no autoimmune disease. The 20-week morphology scan was normal and the most recent growth scan at 26 weeks showed an appropriately grown foetus with normal Dopplers. Her BP on arrival is 178/116 mmHg confirmed on repeat with a manual cuff after 5 minutes' rest, with 3+ proteinuria on dipstick and a urine PCR of 380 mg/mmol. She is reflexively brisk at 3+ with no clonus, no focal neurology, and no visual field defect on confrontation. Bloods show platelets 92, ALT 184, urate 0.42, creatinine 88, LDH normal, no haemolysis on film, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 4.2. A magnesium sulphate loading dose of 4 g IV over 20 minutes was given 30 minutes ago and a maintenance infusion of 1 g/h is now running. Despite the magnesium load her headache and blurred vision persist and her BP remains 174/112. The CTG is currently reassuring with a baseline of 138 and normal variability. She is otherwise well, has not received antenatal corticosteroids in this pregnancy, has no asthma, no contraindications to any single antihypertensive agent, and the neonatal team has been notified for likely preterm delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/112 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Headache, blurred vision persist. Hyperreflexia 3+, no clonus. RUQ tender.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92 (x10^9/L), ALT 184 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, urine PCR 380 mg/mmol, no haemolysis. CTG reassuring.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate additional antihypertensive intervention?

options.text: IV hydralazine 5 mg bolus, then 5-10 mg every 20 minutes (max 30 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Hydralazine is an acceptable second- or third-line agent in Australian guidance and is associated with more maternal hypotension and foetal distress than labetalol or nifedipine (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg bolus, then 40 mg, then 80 mg every 10 minutes (max 220 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Labetalol IV is first-line for acute severe hypertension in pre-eclampsia per SOMANZ 2023 (Australian guidance), with nifedipine immediate-release as an alternative first-line. Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis and does not lower BP. Delivery is indicated after stabilisation given persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium (SOMANZ).

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release 10 mg, repeated every 30 minutes to a maximum of three doses for control
options.rationale: Oral nifedipine immediate-release is an acceptable first-line alternative to IV labetalol per SOMANZ; either is reasonable. However, immediate-release nifedipine carries a risk of overshoot hypotension and is generally favoured when IV access is limited; in a monitored unit with IV access, labetalol is the more controllable first-line agent (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg capsule bitten and swallowed, repeated every 20 minutes as required to a maximum of three doses
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in pregnancy because of unpredictable absorption and risk of severe overshoot hypotension causing foetal compromise; only the oral immediate-release route is acceptable (SOMANZ).

options.text: Increase the magnesium sulphate maintenance infusion from 1 g/h to 2 g/h to achieve blood pressure control in her
options.rationale: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis only and does not lower blood pressure. Increasing the infusion risks magnesium toxicity including loss of patellar reflexes and respiratory depression (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium load requires (1) acute BP control to <160/110 with IV labetalol or oral nifedipine IR (first-line per SOMANZ), with hydralazine second-line, and (2) delivery after stabilisation (antenatal steroids if not given and time permits, but delivery should not be unduly delayed). Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis, not BP control.
key_points: Severe HTN target: <160/110 within 30-60 minutes
SOMANZ first-line acute severe HTN: IV labetalol OR oral nifedipine IR
Hydralazine: second/third-line, more maternal hypotension
Magnesium: seizure prophylaxis only (4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance)
Persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium = imminent eclampsia = deliver
Antenatal steroids 11.4 mg betamethasone IM x 2 doses 24 h apart if <34+6 weeks
explanation.pearls: Stabilise mother, then deliver. The cure for pre-eclampsia is delivery, and persistent neurological symptoms after a magnesium load are an indication that delivery cannot wait for the full steroid course.

q.subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia at 28 weeks - antihypertensive selection

q.stem: A 36-year-old G4P3 at 39+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 09:42 after a 4-hour labour augmented with oxytocin from 5 cm for slow progress, ending with a second stage of 30 minutes and an intact perineum. Her three previous births were unassisted vaginal deliveries at term with no complications. Her booking BMI was 26, she has no cardiac, respiratory or thromboembolic history, no allergies, and was on no regular medications. Her booking bloods including FBC, group and screen, and antibody screen were unremarkable, and her 36-week growth scan showed a normally grown foetus. The placenta was delivered by controlled cord traction at 09:48 and active third-stage management with IM oxytocin 10 units had been given immediately after the birth. Two minutes after delivery of the placenta she suddenly develops dyspnoea, agitation and a sense of impending doom while talking to her partner. Within 60 seconds she becomes cyanotic, loses consciousness and her monitor shows BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min via Hudson mask, with the trace transitioning to a narrow-complex tachycardia. Within 90 seconds she becomes pulseless with a rhythm of PEA on the defibrillator. There is now brisk PV bleeding from the uterus despite a soft contracted fundus on bimanual palpation, and oozing from her peripheral IV site and recent venipuncture. She has no history of cardiac disease, no epidural was sited at any point, no local anaesthetic was used apart from 5 mL of 1% lignocaine for a perineal infiltration after the birth, and there were no transfusions or blood products administered during labour. CPR is commenced with manual left uterine displacement and the obstetric, anaesthetic, ICU and haematology teams are paged through the MET emergency call.

data_table.Vital signs: Pre-arrest: BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min O2. Now pulseless with PEA.
data_table.Examination: Cyanotic, unconscious. Brisk PV bleeding from soft contracted uterus. Oozing from IV site.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods drawn: anticipate prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen, low platelets. CTG no longer relevant.

q.lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism with cardiopulmonary collapse and DIC
options.rationale: Sudden peripartum collapse with hypoxia, hypotension and coagulopathy (PPH plus bleeding from puncture sites) is the classic Clark triad of AFE. PEA arrest within minutes of placental delivery in a multipara fits the AFE phenotype; management is high-quality CPR with left uterine displacement, A-OK protocol (atropine, ondansetron, ketorolac), early massive transfusion and cardiac arrest team activation (RANZCOG; AFE Foundation; Clark 2016).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium
Clark SL et al. Proposed diagnostic criteria for AFE 2016

options.text: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) from perineal lignocaine infiltration
options.rationale: Only 5 mL of 1% lignocaine (50 mg) was used, far below the toxic threshold (3 mg/kg without adrenaline); LAST typically presents with seizures and arrhythmia, not the AFE phenotype (ANZCA; LAST guideline).

options.text: Eclamptic seizure with secondary cardiopulmonary cardiac arrest
options.rationale: There is no history of hypertension, proteinuria or seizures preceding collapse, and the presentation is hypoxic collapse with coagulopathy rather than seizure activity (SOMANZ).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Acute pulmonary embolism with right heart failure
options.rationale: PE is on the differential but does not classically cause the early DIC and oozing from IV sites that this woman has; AFE is more consistent with the full clinical picture (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock and arrest
options.rationale: ACS is possible but rare in a 36-year-old without risk factors and does not explain the coagulopathy/oozing or the temporal relationship with placental delivery (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare (~2-8 per 100,000 births) but devastating cause of maternal collapse, with peripartum hypoxia, hypotension and DIC as the classic triad. Diagnosis is clinical (Clark criteria); management is high-quality CPR with manual left uterine displacement (LUD), A-OK (atropine 1 mg, ondansetron 8 mg, ketorolac 30 mg IV per the controversial AFE Foundation protocol), early massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets), and consideration of perimortem caesarean by 4 minutes if no ROSC.
key_points: Clark criteria (4 required): cardiovascular collapse OR hypotension+respiratory compromise, DIC, intrapartum or within 30 min of placental delivery, no fever
Manual left uterine displacement throughout CPR (not tilt)
Perimortem CS by 4 minutes (4-minute rule) if no ROSC and >=20 weeks
Massive transfusion protocol; expect early cryoprecipitate for low fibrinogen
Differential of obstetric collapse: AFE, PE, eclampsia, MI, anaphylaxis, LAST, intracranial bleed, hypovolaemia
explanation.pearls: If the mother collapses with the placenta still in the room and starts oozing from her IV site, do not wait for confirmation - call MET and run the AFE algorithm now.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium
Clark SL et al. Proposed diagnostic criteria for AFE 2016

q.subtopic_detail: Maternal collapse in labour ward - amniotic fluid embolism

q.stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with three days of progressively worsening right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and increasing fatigue. She has not had a seizure but has a mild bifrontal headache and no visual scotomata. Her two previous pregnancies were uncomplicated and she has no personal or family history of thrombotic microangiopathy, autoimmune disease or liver disease. Her blood pressure on three readings is 146-150 / 90-94 mmHg, she has 1+ proteinuria with a urine PCR of 42 mg/mmol, and the foetal CTG is reactive with normal growth on a scan one week ago. She has had no recent gastroenteritis, no bloody diarrhoea and no recent E. coli O157:H7 or shigatoxin-producing organism exposure. Bedside bloods show haemoglobin 96 g/L (anaemia with reticulocytosis), platelets 68 x10^9/L, ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, total bilirubin 38 micromol/L (predominantly indirect), LDH 850 U/L, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L (preserved), INR 1.1, creatinine 84 micromol/L (normal for pregnancy), glucose 5.2 mmol/L, and ammonia within the normal range. A peripheral blood film shows schistocytes, polychromasia and absent platelet clumps. ADAMTS13 activity measured urgently is 65% (well above the 10% threshold for TTP).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 148/92 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Mild scleral icterus, RUQ tenderness. Uterus appropriate for dates, soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 68 x10^9/L, schistocytes on film, LDH 850 U/L, bilirubin 38 micromol/L (mostly indirect), ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, ammonia normal, ADAMTS13 65%.

q.lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and biochemical picture?

options.text: HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets)
options.rationale: HELLP is diagnosed by Haemolysis (anaemia, schistocytes, raised LDH, raised indirect bilirubin), Elevated Liver enzymes (ALT/AST >70 U/L typically) and Low Platelets (<100 x10^9/L), usually with normal coagulation and normal glucose. This patient has all three features with preserved fibrinogen, normal INR, normal glucose and ADAMTS13 >10%, distinguishing it from AFLP, TTP and HUS (RANZCOG 2023; SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy with hepatic dysfunction
options.rationale: AFLP typically causes hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy (raised INR, low fibrinogen), hyperammonaemia and more pronounced hyperbilirubinaemia. This patient has normal glucose, normal INR and fibrinogen and normal ammonia (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with neurological features
options.rationale: TTP is defined by ADAMTS13 activity <10%, with severe thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolysis, neurological features and fever; ADAMTS13 of 65% effectively excludes TTP (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome with complement-mediated injury
options.rationale: HUS is dominated by acute kidney injury and microangiopathic haemolysis with relatively preserved transaminases; this patient has normal creatinine and very high transaminases, making HUS unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy with raised bile acids
options.rationale: ICP causes pruritus and isolated raised bile acids and modest transaminitis without thrombocytopenia or haemolysis (RANZCOG ICP statement).

explanation.summary: HELLP, AFLP, TTP and HUS can all present with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic features in late pregnancy. Discriminators: HELLP has high LFTs with normal coags and glucose; AFLP has hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy and hyperammonaemia; TTP has ADAMTS13 <10%; HUS is renal-predominant.
key_points: HELLP: Hb low, platelets <100, AST/ALT >70, LDH >600, INR normal, glucose normal
AFLP: hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, hyperammonaemia, AKI
TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, severe thrombocytopenia, neuro features
HUS: AKI dominant, often post-diarrhoeal (STEC) or complement-mediated
Mississippi classification grades HELLP severity by platelet count
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, send fibrinogen, glucose, ammonia and ADAMTS13 - they discriminate HELLP from its mimics within hours.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

q.subtopic_detail: HELLP vs AFLP vs TTP vs HUS in pregnancy

q.stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive. Amniocentesis is being arranged for foetal PCR at 18 weeks (consistent with the recommendation to wait at least six weeks after maternal seroconversion). Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications. She is referred to maternal-foetal medicine to discuss treatment options pending foetal status, and asks specifically whether the medication chosen now should change if foetal infection is later confirmed on amniotic fluid PCR. The maternal-foetal medicine consultant also outlines the typical vertical transmission risk by trimester and explains why the timing of investigations matters, before turning to the choice of initial pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Well. No lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Toxoplasma IgM positive, IgG positive low avidity, maternal blood PCR positive. Booking ultrasound: viable singleton at 11 weeks, normal anatomy for gestation.

q.lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Spiramycin 1 g orally three times daily, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
options.rationale: Spiramycin is the first-trimester treatment of choice as it concentrates in the placenta and reduces vertical transmission without causing foetal harm. If foetal infection is confirmed (positive amniotic fluid PCR after 18 weeks), therapy switches to sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid to treat established foetal infection (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; RCOG). Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission; sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is reserved for confirmed foetal infection by amniotic fluid PCR at >=18 weeks.

options.text: Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in the first trimester (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the foetus is reserved for confirmed foetal infection (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
options.rationale: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Termination of pregnancy without any treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation
options.rationale: Maternal primary infection in the first trimester carries a relatively low vertical transmission risk (10-15%) although consequences if transmitted can be severe; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not the default management (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Suspected maternal toxoplasmosis is confirmed by serology (IgG avidity, IgM) and maternal PCR. Spiramycin reduces transmission without teratogenicity. Confirmed foetal infection (amniotic PCR after 18 weeks) is treated with sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid.
key_points: Spiramycin: first-line in pregnancy to prevent transmission (does not cross placenta well)
Sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid: for confirmed foetal infection from 14-18 weeks
Folinic acid (leucovorin) protects against pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow toxicity
Diagnose primary infection with low IgG avidity within 16-20 weeks of seroconversion
Vertical transmission risk: T1 ~10-15%, T2 ~30%, T3 ~60% - but severity decreases with gestation
Sources: cat faeces, raw/undercooked meat, unwashed produce, soil
explanation.pearls: Earlier infection equals lower transmission but more severe disease if transmitted; later infection equals higher transmission but milder disease.

q.subtopic_detail: Toxoplasmosis - first trimester management

q.stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 woman with focal-onset epilepsy attends a joint obstetric-medicine/neurology pre-conception clinic three months before a planned pregnancy. She has been seizure-free for two years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily monotherapy, having previously failed levetiracetam (rash and breakthrough seizures) and carbamazepine (breakthrough seizures with intolerable somnolence) for seizure control. Her previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage while she was on lamotrigine monotherapy two years ago, and she has not used lamotrigine since. She has no family history of neural tube defects or congenital malformations and her BMI is 23. She is concerned about teratogenicity having read media coverage of valproate, and asks specifically about: relative major congenital malformation rates of the commonly used AEDs, the appropriate periconceptional folate dose for women on AEDs versus the general population, the likely need for therapeutic drug monitoring during pregnancy (particularly for lamotrigine if used), and which medication she should ideally be established on by the time of conception. Her neurologist has reviewed her recent MRI and EEG which remain stable, and confirms she has reliable epilepsy without evidence of progressive structural lesion. She does not drive a heavy vehicle for work and has well-controlled mood with no current depressive symptoms. She is keen to plan a pregnancy within the next six months and is willing to delay conception if a medication switch is recommended, but wants a clear plan that balances seizure control against teratogenic risk and includes the practical issue of how to monitor any new agent through pregnancy. She also asks whether to expect a dose adjustment in pregnancy and what to do about breastfeeding postpartum, and her partner asks whether she should stop driving during the cross-titration period as a safety precaution.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Neurologically normal. BMI 23.
data_table.Investigations: Recent EEG and MRI stable. Valproate level within therapeutic range. Renal and hepatic function normal.

q.lead_in: Which combination of recommendations is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate, commence folate 0.5 mg daily, with no need for AED drug levels during pregnancy
options.rationale: Valproate carries the highest major congenital malformation rate of all AEDs (~10%) plus a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment, and is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless every alternative has failed. Folate dose for AED users is 5 mg, not 0.5 mg (Therapeutic Goods Administration; AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch to phenytoin before pregnancy as it carries the lowest teratogenicity profile of all the AEDs
options.rationale: Phenytoin causes foetal hydantoin syndrome and has a major malformation rate around 5-6%, higher than lamotrigine or levetiracetam (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Switch (under neurology) from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam pre-conception, folate 5 mg daily pre-conception, and therapeutic AED level monitoring across pregnancy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the lowest major malformation rates (~2-3%, similar to background). Valproate (~10%) is avoided in women of childbearing potential. Folate 5 mg daily is recommended for women on AEDs. Lamotrigine clearance increases substantially in pregnancy, requiring level monitoring and dose adjustment (Therapeutic Guidelines - Neurology; AMH; RANZCOG; NICE). Switch should be 3-6 months pre-conception; lamotrigine levels fall markedly in pregnancy. Continued AED therapy is essential because uncontrolled seizures cause more foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.

options.text: Continue valproate, add lamotrigine to permit valproate dose reduction, and use folate 0.5 mg daily throughout
options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk and valproate remains highly teratogenic at any dose; the goal is monotherapy with the safest agent (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Stop all antiepileptic medications before conception entirely to eliminate teratogenic risk to the developing foetus completely
options.rationale: Stopping AEDs in someone with a clear epilepsy diagnosis risks status epilepticus, maternal injury, hypoxia and foetal loss; risk of uncontrolled seizures outweighs medication risk (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception epilepsy care: switch from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity), folate 5 mg daily, plan for AED level monitoring (lamotrigine clearance rises ~2-3 fold), continue therapy because uncontrolled seizures cause more foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.
key_points: Major malformation rates: lamotrigine ~2.3%, levetiracetam ~2.4%, carbamazepine ~5%, phenytoin ~6%, topiramate ~4-9%, valproate ~10%
Valproate also causes 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
Folate 5 mg daily for AED users (vs 0.5 mg general population)
Lamotrigine levels fall in pregnancy; monitor and dose-adjust
Vitamin K 1 mg IM to neonate at birth (standard)
Breastfeeding is safe on most AEDs (lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, valproate)
explanation.pearls: Switch off valproate at least 3-6 months before conception, not once pregnancy is confirmed - by then the teratogenic window is open.

q.subtopic_detail: Epilepsy in pregnancy - AED selection and teratogenicity

q.stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with longstanding primary autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 microg daily attends the antenatal clinic at 6 weeks gestation by certain LMP with a positive home pregnancy test which she did this morning. Her TSH four weeks ago, pre-conception, was 2.0 mIU/L on her current dose and she has been clinically euthyroid. She is asymptomatic with no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no cold intolerance, fatigue, constipation, dry skin or weight change). She takes her levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30 minutes before breakfast and does not take iron or calcium within four hours of her dose. She asks the GP about the dose adjustment to make now (if any), the optimal frequency of thyroid function testing through pregnancy, and the trimester-specific TSH target range she should be aiming for, given that the laboratory reference interval she sees on her result printout (0.4-4.0 mIU/L) is for non-pregnant adults. Her previous pregnancy two years ago was complicated by first-trimester pregnancy loss at 8 weeks when her TSH was found retrospectively to be 6.5 mIU/L early in that pregnancy because she had not increased her dose at conception. She is anti-TPO positive.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Euthyroid clinically. No goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Pre-conception TSH (4 weeks ago): 2.0 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 microg daily. Anti-TPO positive.

q.lead_in: Which combined management plan most closely matches current guidance (Endocrine Society / ATA / RANZCOG)?

options.text: Continue 100 microg daily, recheck TSH at 28 weeks gestation, target TSH <4.0 mIU/L throughout the entire pregnancy
options.rationale: Dose typically needs to increase by 25-30% in early pregnancy due to increased thyroid hormone requirement; TSH should be checked every 4 weeks until mid-pregnancy, and trimester-specific upper TSH targets are lower than 4.0 (Endocrine Society; ATA).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

options.text: Reduce levothyroxine by 25% because pregnancy is associated with a hyperthyroid physiological state secondary to beta-hCG cross-reactivity
options.rationale: Although beta-hCG can transiently suppress TSH in the first trimester, women with established hypothyroidism on levothyroxine need a DOSE INCREASE of approximately 25-30%, not a reduction, due to higher thyroid-binding globulin and placental transfer (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Cease levothyroxine completely now to assess underlying endogenous thyroid function before any further dose adjustment is made in pregnancy
options.rationale: Stopping levothyroxine risks severe hypothyroidism, miscarriage and impaired neurodevelopment; continue and increase the dose (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Increase levothyroxine 25-30% now (e.g. 125 microg daily), check TSH 4-weekly until 20 weeks then once at 26-32 weeks with trimester-specific target
options.rationale: Endocrine Society/ATA/RANZCOG guidance: known hypothyroid women should immediately increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% on pregnancy confirmation, recheck TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then at least once in T3, targeting trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5 mIU/L is the historical target; current ATA recommends using local trimester-specific reference ranges, with an upper limit typically <2.5-3.0 mIU/L). The 9-doses-per-week trick is a practical 28% increase (Endocrine Society 2007/2012; ATA 2017; RANZCOG). Trimester-specific TSH targets are typically <2.5 mIU/L T1, <3.0 mIU/L T2-T3, with lower bound ~0.1 mIU/L.
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

options.text: Increase dose by 25-30% but only check TSH if she develops overt clinical symptoms of hypothyroidism in this pregnancy
options.rationale: Symptoms are unreliable in pregnancy; routine 4-weekly TSH monitoring until mid-pregnancy is required because of dynamic dose requirements (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

explanation.summary: Established hypothyroidism in pregnancy: increase levothyroxine ~25-30% on confirmation of pregnancy, monitor TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then again in third trimester, target trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5; T2/T3 <3.0 mIU/L) within the local reference range.
key_points: Immediate ~25-30% dose increase (or 9 doses/week)
TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks, then once between 26-32 weeks
Trimester-specific upper limits: T1 ~2.5, T2-T3 ~3.0 mIU/L (use local ranges if available)
Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food and 4 hours apart from iron/calcium
Postpartum: revert to pre-pregnancy dose and recheck TSH at 6 weeks
Untreated overt hypothyroidism: miscarriage, preterm birth, neurodevelopmental delay
explanation.pearls: Tell women to add two extra tablets per week as soon as they get a positive pregnancy test (9 doses per week) - a practical way to deliver the ~28% increase without waiting for clinic review.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

q.subtopic_detail: Hypothyroidism in pregnancy - levothyroxine and TSH targets

q.stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman presents to ED with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began 90 minutes ago while she was walking briskly home. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, sharp, with intermittent stabbing exacerbations, and is associated with two episodes of bilious vomiting and one episode of diarrhoea-free loose stool. She is sexually active with her regular partner of 18 months, uses condoms consistently, and her last menstrual period began 14 days ago and was normal in flow and duration. Her cycles are regular at 28 days and she has no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. There is no fever, no rigors, no dysuria, no frequency, no haematuria, no diarrhoea, no recent travel and no recent change in bowel habit. She had a similar but milder episode three months ago that resolved spontaneously after 4 hours; at that time she did not present for medical review. She had a similar mild episode 6 weeks ago that lasted 2 hours. She has no significant past medical history, no previous abdominal surgery, no allergies, and takes no regular medications. Her appendix has not been removed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Pale and distressed. Abdomen: marked tenderness right iliac fossa with guarding but no rebound; bowel sounds present. Bimanual: marked right adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness equivocal, no cervical discharge. No palpable adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. FBC: WCC 12.1, neutrophils 8.9. CRP 8 mg/L. UEC normal. Urinalysis: trace blood, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside USS in ED: enlarged right ovary 7 cm with a 5 cm complex cyst, minimal arterial Doppler flow within the ovary.

q.lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum and shock
options.rationale: A negative urine beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic pregnancy; sensitivity of urine beta-hCG approaches 99% at the time symptoms develop (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ovarian torsion of a right ovarian cyst
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting, an enlarged ovary with a cyst and reduced/absent arterial Doppler flow on TVUS in a haemodynamically stable woman with negative beta-hCG points to ovarian torsion; the prior self-resolving episode is typical of intermittent torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
options.rationale: PID typically presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers; the sudden onset, unilateral severity, normal CRP and absence of discharge here argue against PID (CDC; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with pelvic pain
options.rationale: A ruptured cyst typically produces sudden pain that improves over hours; USS shows free fluid in the pouch of Douglas without a persistent enlarged ovary and Doppler flow is preserved (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute appendicitis with right iliac fossa peritonism
options.rationale: Appendicitis usually progresses over 12-24 hours with central-to-RIF migration, anorexia and rising inflammatory markers; the abrupt very-severe onset, prior identical self-resolving episode, and TVUS findings of an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow favour torsion (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pain, nausea/vomiting, enlarged ovary +/- cyst on USS, reduced or absent Doppler flow (though preserved flow does not exclude torsion). Prior self-resolving episodes (intermittent torsion) are a major clue. Treatment is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation.
key_points: Negative beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic
Doppler flow: reduced/absent suggests torsion but normal flow does not exclude it (dual blood supply)
Ovarian mass >5 cm increases torsion risk
Treatment: urgent laparoscopy with detorsion; oophorectomy only if necrotic/malignant
Cystectomy of underlying cyst at the time, or interval if oedematous
explanation.pearls: Pregnancy, mass >5 cm, prior similar episodes, and right > left predominance are torsion risk hooks. Do not wait for absent Doppler flow before operating.

q.subtopic_detail: Acute pelvic pain - differential diagnosis

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She had mild gestational hypertension in the third trimester that resolved within 2 weeks postpartum, and her blood pressure today is 118/74 mmHg with no proteinuria. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia, no migraines and has a BMI of 25. She does not smoke. She is open to long-acting methods including intrauterine devices and the etonogestrel subdermal implant, and is also willing to consider the progestogen-only pill. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy. Her partner is not pursuing vasectomy in the near term. She has no contraindications to any specific contraceptive method on history.

data_table.Examination: Well. BP 118/74. BMI 25. Soft well-involuted uterus, no lochia, perineum healed. Lactating breasts, no mastitis.
data_table.Investigations: None required today; routine 6-week postnatal review.

q.lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as BP has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC category 4 (avoid) in breastfeeding women less than 6 weeks postpartum and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months due to potential impact on milk supply; progestogen-only methods are preferred while breastfeeding (FSRH; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole contraceptive method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
options.rationale: LAM provides ~98% efficacy only if all three criteria are met: amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND less than 6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter and she wants a more reliable method (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted today, and etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
options.rationale: Intrauterine contraception (LNG-IUS or Cu-IUD) can be safely inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (UKMEC 1-2); progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA) are also suitable in breastfeeding women from any time postpartum (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an adverse effect on milk supply
options.rationale: Progestogen-only implants do not adversely affect milk supply or infant outcomes and can be inserted at any time postpartum, including immediately postpartum (FSRH; WHO MEC).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding and should not be used at all here
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC category 1 from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (category 2 before 6 weeks) and is safe to use (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception in breastfeeding women: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS) are all safe at 6 weeks. Cu-IUD or LNG-IUS can be inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (or immediately postpartum within 48 h of birth). CHC is avoided <6 weeks (UKMEC 4) and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months while breastfeeding. LAM is ~98% effective only when ALL three criteria are met: <6 months, amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding.
key_points: POP can start day 21 postpartum without need to wait
IUD/IUS: insert immediately postpartum (within 48 h) or from 4-6 weeks
