CHC: avoid <6 weeks if breastfeeding; from 6 weeks UKMEC 2; from 6 months UKMEC 1
DMPA: UKMEC 2 before 6 weeks if breastfeeding, then UKMEC 1
LAM strict criteria: amenorrhoea + exclusive BF + <6 months postpartum
explanation.pearls: Day 21 postpartum is the earliest fertility could return in non-breastfeeding women - that's why POP and CHC have day 21 as the earliest start if other criteria allow.

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception in a breastfeeding woman

q.stem: A 53-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her GP with 18 months of disabling vasomotor symptoms: 8-12 hot flushes per day with drenching night sweats requiring change of nightwear most nights, fragmented sleep with average 4-5 hours, persistently low mood (PHQ-9 score 11), and reduced libido. She has tried fan, cooling clothing, regular exercise, alcohol avoidance and trigger-diary approaches with limited benefit. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago and she is therefore postmenopausal by clinical criteria. She has an intact uterus and has had no episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. Her BMI is 31, BP 132/84 mmHg, fasting lipids in target range and HbA1c 5.5%. She had a single provoked DVT after a long-haul flight 8 years ago and was anticoagulated for 3 months; no thrombophilia was identified and she has had no subsequent VTE. She has no personal or family history of breast cancer, no ischaemic heart disease, and no migraine with aura. She does not smoke. Cervical screening and mammography are up to date and normal. She has trialled citalopram 20 mg daily for 4 months with minimal symptomatic benefit and is now seeking menopausal hormone therapy. She does not want a monthly bleed and would prefer a no-bleed regimen if possible.

data_table.Examination: BMI 31. BP 132/84. Breast examination normal. Pelvic examination unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting lipids: total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.1. HbA1c 5.5%. TSH 1.6 mIU/L. Mammogram normal 10 months ago.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate MHT regimen for her?

options.text: Oral oestradiol 1 mg daily plus cyclical medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 12 days each month
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogen produces a planned monthly bleed and is preferred in perimenopausal women still having periods; this woman is >12 months postmenopausal and prefers a no-bleed regimen, so continuous combined therapy is preferred. Additionally, oral oestrogen is best avoided given her prior VTE (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily plus continuous medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg orally
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk (~2-fold); transdermal oestrogen at standard doses does not increase VTE risk and is preferred in women with prior VTE, obesity, or other VTE risk (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg orally once daily as monotherapy for menopausal symptom control alone
options.rationale: Tibolone is a reasonable alternative for vasomotor and libido symptoms in postmenopausal women, but it carries an increased stroke risk (especially age >60) and is not first-line in a woman with elevated cardiometabolic risk and prior VTE (BMS).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h twice weekly plus levonorgestrel 52 mg IUD (Mirena)
options.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and does not increase VTE risk at standard doses, making it the preferred route in women with prior VTE, obesity or other VTE risk factors. The LNG-IUS provides effective endometrial protection in a no-bleed manner. Together this provides combined MHT for a woman with an intact uterus (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h applied twice weekly alone, with no progestogen at all
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia and increases endometrial cancer risk; a progestogen (oral, transdermal, vaginal, or LNG-IUS) is mandatory (BMS; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: MHT principles: oestrogen alone if hysterectomy; combined (oestrogen + progestogen) if uterus intact. Cyclical progestogen for perimenopausal women still having periods; continuous combined for women >=12 months postmenopausal who want no bleed. Transdermal oestrogen preferred when VTE risk is elevated (prior VTE, obesity, age) - no first-pass hepatic effect. LNG-IUS is an effective progestogen for endometrial protection.
key_points: Uterus intact -> add progestogen (mandatory)
Hysterectomy -> oestrogen alone
Cyclical progestogen if still cycling; continuous if >12 months amenorrhoea
Transdermal oestrogen: no increased VTE risk; preferred if VTE risk factors
Absolute contraindications: current/recent breast cancer, oestrogen-dependent malignancy, undiagnosed PV bleeding, active VTE/PE, active liver disease, untreated endometrial hyperplasia
explanation.pearls: If a woman has a uterus and you forget the progestogen, you cause endometrial cancer. LNG-IUS plus transdermal oestrogen is an elegant solution in many women.

q.subtopic_detail: MHT - regimen selection and route

q.stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to her GP with 18 months of progressive vaginal dryness, deep dyspareunia and urinary urgency without incontinence. She has had three episodes of culture-confirmed E. coli urinary tract infection in the past 12 months, each requiring antibiotics, with no upper-tract involvement. She had a left-sided early-stage T1N0 oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer treated with breast-conserving lumpectomy, sentinel node biopsy, adjuvant radiotherapy and 5 years of adjuvant tamoxifen completed 12 months ago. She did not receive an aromatase inhibitor. She is currently in remission with no evidence of disease on her most recent annual surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago. She is not on systemic MHT. She has tried over-the-counter water-based vaginal lubricants for intercourse and a non-hormonal hyaluronic acid-based vaginal moisturiser (Replens) used three times weekly consistently for 4 months with only modest symptomatic benefit. She is sexually active with her long-term partner and her quality of life and intimate relationship are significantly affected. Her treating breast oncologist is aware she is exploring further treatment options for her genitourinary symptoms and is supportive of considering local therapies after shared decision-making. She has no other oestrogen-dependent malignancy and no undiagnosed bleeding.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: pale thin atrophic vaginal mucosa, friable, loss of rugae. No discharge, no lesions. Bimanual: normal-sized uterus, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS today: no growth. Last surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago: no evidence of disease.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Avoid all oestrogen-containing therapies indefinitely and continue non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers and lubricants only
options.rationale: While historically avoided, current evidence shows low-dose vaginal oestrogen produces minimal systemic absorption and recent observational data suggest no increased breast cancer recurrence risk in most survivors; ongoing severe GSM warrants further options after shared decision-making with the breast oncologist (BMS; Jean Hailes; NAMS).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Commence systemic oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h plus an LNG-IUS for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Systemic MHT is generally avoided in breast cancer survivors and is not required for GSM; local low-dose vaginal oestrogen is more appropriate and effective (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Trial low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or estradiol pessary in consultation with breast oncologist, continuing non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants
options.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen in a breast cancer survivor not on aromatase inhibitors is appropriate after shared decision-making with the oncology team; severe GSM significantly affects quality of life and the systemic absorption is minimal. Current evidence does not show increased recurrence risk for most survivors. She is on no current endocrine therapy (completed tamoxifen 12 months ago) (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes; NAMS 2020).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Refer for fractional CO2 vaginal laser treatment as first-line alternative in this patient
options.rationale: Vaginal laser therapy lacks high-quality evidence of efficacy and safety; the TGA and international societies have cautioned against its use for GSM outside clinical trials (TGA; NAMS).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Commence ospemifene orally as the first-line therapy for her dyspareunia in this setting
options.rationale: Ospemifene is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator licensed for dyspareunia, but it is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer due to oestrogen agonist effects on breast tissue (FDA labelling; BMS).

explanation.summary: GSM (vaginal atrophy + urinary symptoms) responds well to low-dose vaginal oestrogen with minimal systemic absorption. In breast cancer survivors, local vaginal oestrogen is increasingly used after shared decision-making with the oncologist, especially in those not on aromatase inhibitors and with significant symptom burden. Non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants are first-line; vaginal oestrogen is added when symptoms persist.
key_points: First-line: non-hormonal moisturisers (Replens) + lubricants
Second-line: low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or oestradiol pessary
Local vaginal oestrogen: minimal systemic absorption
In breast cancer survivors: shared decision with oncology; tamoxifen users generally acceptable; AI users more contentious
GSM also reduces recurrent UTIs in postmenopausal women
explanation.pearls: Recurrent UTIs in a postmenopausal woman are often a manifestation of GSM and respond to vaginal oestrogen better than to recurrent antibiotic courses.

sources.label: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

q.subtopic_detail: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause - local oestrogen

q.stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman has a 6-year established diagnosis of vulval lichen sclerosus, biopsy-confirmed at the time of diagnosis. She has been managed with topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment using a structured regimen (nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance long-term) and has been reasonably symptom-controlled until recently, with annual review by her gynaecologist. Over the past 4 months she has noticed a persistent fissured area on the right labium majus that has not healed despite optimisation of her steroid technique (correct application, adequate quantity confirmed at recent review). Symptoms include localised pruritus that has worsened, occasional spotting when wiping after micturition, and increasing discomfort with intercourse despite use of personal lubricant. She has no other rashes, no oral lichen planus, no eye symptoms, no skin lesions elsewhere, and no urinary or bowel symptoms. She has no postcoital bleeding aside from the localised spotting described. She is on no immunosuppressive medications and is otherwise well, with controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Her long-term partner has no penile lesions or symptoms. There is no history of previous vulval intraepithelial neoplasia, no previous vulval surgery, and her last cervical screening 18 months ago was negative for oncogenic HPV. She does not smoke. She wonders whether she still needs ongoing specialist follow-up after 6 years of stable disease, or whether she could now safely self-manage with intermittent steroid use only as needed.

data_table.Examination: Vulva: classic white atrophic 'cigarette paper' skin extending in a figure-of-eight around vulva and perianal area, loss of labia minora architecture. New finding: 1.2 cm hyperkeratotic, fissured, indurated plaque on right labium majus that has not healed.
data_table.Investigations: None done today; review appointment for chronic LS surveillance.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase topical clobetasol propionate to nightly application for a further 8 weeks and review symptoms
options.rationale: An unhealed, indurated or hyperkeratotic lesion in a woman with longstanding lichen sclerosus must not be assumed to be a steroid-responsive feature; it requires biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN or invasive squamous cell carcinoma (BSSVD; RCOG).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Add topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as a second-line steroid-sparing agent for symptomatic control of disease
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are a recognised second-line agent in lichen sclerosus, but the priority here is to biopsy the persistent suspicious lesion before changing therapy (BSSVD).

options.text: Reassure that surveillance is not required after 5 years of stable disease and discharge to self-management with PRN steroid only
options.rationale: Lichen sclerosus carries a lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma of approximately 4-5%; long-term clinician surveillance is recommended at least annually with prompt biopsy of any persistent change (BSSVD; RCOG).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the suspicious vulval lesion without prior histology being obtained
options.rationale: Diagnostic biopsy precedes any consideration of wide local excision; excisional treatment depends on the histological diagnosis (BSSVD; RCOG).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology/vulval clinic for punch biopsy of the persistent fissured lesion, continuing topical clobetasol and at least annual surveillance
options.rationale: Persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, or non-healing lesion in established lichen sclerosus requires prompt biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN (the precursor most associated with LS-related vulval SCC, typically HPV-negative) and invasive SCC. Long-term annual surveillance is recommended due to ~4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC. Topical potent steroid (clobetasol) remains the cornerstone of medical management (BSSVD 2018; RCOG; Cancer Council).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis with a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma (via differentiated VIN, typically HPV-negative). Management: potent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05% ointment) induction then maintenance, plus long-term clinician surveillance. Any persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration or non-healing lesion warrants prompt biopsy. Self-management alone is inappropriate due to malignancy risk.
key_points: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment is the gold-standard topical therapy
Structured induction then maintenance (typically twice weekly long-term)
Lifetime vulval SCC risk ~4-5% via differentiated VIN (HPV-negative pathway)
Biopsy: persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, non-healing lesion
At least annual clinician review long-term, more frequent if uncontrolled or atypical features
Tacrolimus second-line; surgery reserved for adhesions/scarring or malignancy
explanation.pearls: Differentiated VIN looks innocuous and is easy to miss - any unhealed area in LS deserves a biopsy regardless of how unremarkable it appears.

sources.label: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

q.subtopic_detail: Lichen sclerosus - surveillance and malignancy risk

q.stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman attends a postnatal review at 5 weeks following an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4 weeks gestation. She is partially breastfeeding (approximately 60% breast milk, 40% formula because she returns to her finance role in 4 weeks and the workplace lacks lactation facilities) and has had no return of menses since delivery. Her booking BMI was 31, she is a lifelong non-smoker, her blood pressure today is 132/82 mmHg, and her booking and 36-week antenatal BP readings were both normotensive. She has no personal or family history of VTE or thrombophilia, no migraine with or without aura, no current or prior breast cancer, no hepatic disease, no diabetes, and no autoimmune disease. She received enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days postpartum because of her BMI; an emergency caesarean was not performed. She and her partner want effective contraception starting as soon as possible today and she is asking specifically about either the combined oral contraceptive pill, a copper intrauterine device, a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system, or the etonogestrel subdermal implant. She is comfortable returning at any time for a procedure if that is the most appropriate option after counselling.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 30, BP 132/82 mmHg. Uterus involuted. Breasts: lactating, no inflammation.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate contraceptive option to initiate today?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 microg/levonorgestrel 150 microg, starting today
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is generally avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (WHO MEC category 3 to 4) because of theoretical effects on lactation and elevated VTE risk; she is partially breastfeeding and only 5 weeks postpartum (WHO MEC; FSRH).
source_refs: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

options.text: Defer all contraception until 6 weeks postpartum and rely on LAM in the interim
options.rationale: She does not meet LAM criteria because she is only partially breastfeeding (formula supplementation); deferral risks ovulation which can resume by day 21 to 28 in non-exclusively breastfeeding women (FSRH).

options.text: Insertion of a copper intrauterine device at 5 weeks postpartum after speculum examination
options.rationale: Copper IUD insertion is appropriate either within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks postpartum (commonly 4 to 6 weeks), so it is feasible at 5 weeks; however an immediate option that does not require procedure scheduling is preferred when the patient wants to start today and the implant is more reliably initiated in the consultation (FSRH; Family Planning Australia). The IUD is acceptable but not the single best immediate choice today.

options.text: Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system today at 5 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: LNG-IUS insertion follows similar timing to Cu-IUD (within 48 hours or after 4 weeks postpartum); however it requires procedure scheduling and speculum work and is therefore not the best immediate same-day initiation option compared with the implant (FSRH).

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant inserted today during the consultation
options.rationale: The etonogestrel implant is WHO MEC category 1 at any time postpartum in breastfeeding women, can be inserted during the postnatal consultation, has the highest typical-use efficacy of any reversible method, and avoids the VTE and lactation concerns of combined hormonal contraception in the early postpartum window (WHO MEC; FSRH; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception timing: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant) can be started any time postpartum including day 0. Cu-IUD and LNG-IUS are inserted within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks. Combined hormonal contraception is generally avoided until 6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding (and until 3 weeks if not breastfeeding, longer if VTE risk factors).
key_points: Implant: any time, WHO MEC 1 in BF women
POP: from day 21 (any time in BF women)
Cu-IUD / LNG-IUS: <48 h or >=4 weeks postpartum
COCP: avoid <3 weeks postpartum (VTE), <6 weeks if BF
LAM only reliable with exclusive BF + <6 months + amenorrhoea
explanation.pearls: Best practice is to discuss postnatal contraception antenatally and offer the implant at hospital discharge or within the first 5 days to capture women before contact is lost.

sources.label: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - timing of method initiation in a partially breastfeeding woman

q.stem: A 30-year-old G1P1 woman attends her general practice for a 6-week postnatal review having delivered a healthy term infant by an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth. She describes 3 weeks of persistent low mood, anhedonia, tearfulness on most days, poor sleep that is markedly worse than would be expected from infant feeding alone, poor concentration, reduced appetite, and pervasive feelings of worthlessness and guilt as a mother. She denies thoughts of self-harm, denies active suicidal ideation, denies thoughts of harming the infant, and denies hallucinations or unusual beliefs that would raise concern for postpartum psychosis. She has no past psychiatric history other than mild generalised anxiety in late adolescence that resolved without treatment, no family history of bipolar affective disorder, and no current substance use. She is exclusively breastfeeding her infant, has a supportive partner who has taken parental leave, and has no domestic violence concerns. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale completed today is 16, with the self-harm item (item 10) scored zero. Thyroid function tests sent last week were normal (TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L). She has tried sleep hygiene, increased exercise, and a referral to a perinatal psychologist with two sessions completed but no improvement in symptoms over the past fortnight.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 24, BP 116/72 mmHg. Affect flat. Insight preserved. No psychotic features. No suicidal ideation.
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 16 (self-harm item 0). TSH 1.4 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L. Hb 122 g/L, ferritin 48 micrograms/L.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line antidepressant to commence?

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has the longest half-life of the SSRIs and a long-lived active metabolite (norfluoxetine), leading to higher relative infant dose in breast milk; it is not first-line in lactating women (COPE; AMH).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrated up as needed
options.rationale: Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women in Australian and international perinatal guidelines because of low relative infant dose, short half-life and the largest evidence base demonstrating safety in lactation (COPE; AMH; LactMed).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

options.text: Paroxetine 20 mg orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Paroxetine has acceptable breastfeeding data but is associated with discontinuation syndromes and is preferred when the woman was successfully treated with paroxetine antenatally; it is not the routine first-line choice in lactation (COPE).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg modified-release orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is an SNRI rather than first-line for new-onset moderate postnatal depression; it is reasonable second-line after SSRI failure (COPE).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023

options.text: Lithium carbonate 500 mg orally at night as monotherapy
options.rationale: Lithium is generally avoided in breastfeeding because of high relative infant dose and risk of infant toxicity (tremor, dehydration, thyroid effects), and is reserved for bipolar disorder or psychotic depression where benefits outweigh risks (COPE; AMH).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

explanation.summary: EPDS at or above 13 prompts further assessment; combined with a 3-week history of pervasive low mood and anhedonia, this woman meets criteria for moderate postnatal depression. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women per COPE and AMH because of low transfer into milk and the strongest safety evidence. Continue psychological therapy concurrently.
key_points: EPDS cutoff: >=13 prompts further assessment; specific item 10 screens self-harm
Differential: postpartum blues (peaks day 3 to 5, resolves by day 14), PND (any time in first year), postpartum psychosis (urgent, often early)
Sertraline first-line SSRI in lactation; paroxetine alternative
Avoid fluoxetine first-line in BF; avoid lithium unless bipolar
Always screen for postpartum psychosis: confusion, hallucinations, mania, infant harm thoughts - admit urgently
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the answer in essentially every Australian medical exam scenario asking for an SSRI in lactation. Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency requiring urgent specialist admission, not outpatient SSRI.

sources.label: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal depression - first-line SSRI in a breastfeeding woman

q.stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her general practitioner 5 months after the delivery of her second child. The pregnancy itself was uncomplicated until delivery, when she had a massive postpartum haemorrhage estimated at 3500 mL secondary to uterine atony plus a small lower segment extension during an emergency caesarean section for foetal distress; she required 6 units of packed red cells, 2 units of fresh frozen plasma, 1 unit of cryoprecipitate, intraoperative tranexamic acid, intramyometrial carboprost, a Bakri balloon, and spent 36 hours in the high-dependency unit before stabilising. She made an apparent physical recovery and was discharged on day 6 postpartum on iron supplementation. Since then she has been unable to establish breastfeeding despite repeated attempts in the first week with a lactation consultant, has had no return of menses at all over the last 5 months, has lost approximately 4 kg of muscle mass with reduced exercise tolerance, sleeps 11 hours daily and is still fatigued during the day, feels persistently cold even in warm rooms, has noticed loss of axillary and pubic hair, has had two episodes of presyncope on standing in the last month, and has reduced libido. On examination today she is thin, pale, has dry skin, fine sparse hair, and a postural blood pressure drop from 108/68 mmHg supine to 86/52 mmHg standing. Her thyroid gland is non-tender and non-enlarged on palpation, and there is no goitre.

data_table.Vital signs: BP supine 108/68 mmHg, standing 86/52 mmHg. HR 58 bpm, T 36.2 degC.
data_table.Examination: Thin, pale, dry skin, fine sparse axillary and pubic hair. Thyroid non-palpable. No goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Pending - GP is determining the most appropriate first-line endocrine workup.

q.lead_in: Which combination of investigations is most appropriate to establish the diagnosis of suspected Sheehan syndrome?

options.text: Morning 0800 cortisol, TSH plus free T4, LH, FSH, oestradiol, prolactin, IGF-1, and MRI pituitary
options.rationale: Sheehan syndrome causes panhypopituitarism after pituitary ischaemic necrosis following major PPH. The investigation strategy is to assess each pituitary axis: cortisol (ACTH axis), free T4 with TSH (TSH axis - TSH may be inappropriately normal or low), LH/FSH and oestradiol (gonadotrophin axis), prolactin (lactotroph axis - typically low or undetectable), IGF-1 (GH axis), with MRI demonstrating an empty or partially empty sella (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: TSH and free T4 only, treating empirically with levothyroxine if either is low
options.rationale: TSH and free T4 alone miss the cortisol deficiency which is potentially life-threatening; starting thyroxine before replacing glucocorticoid can precipitate adrenal crisis (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Random cortisol and random TSH on the day of consultation only as initial work-up
options.rationale: Random cortisol is unreliable because of diurnal variation; 0800 morning cortisol is the appropriate screening test, and a full pituitary axis screen plus imaging is required (Endocrine Society).

options.text: 24-hour urinary free cortisol and an overnight dexamethasone suppression test as the first step
options.rationale: These tests screen for Cushing syndrome (excess cortisol), not adrenal insufficiency or hypopituitarism; they are the wrong direction of testing for suspected Sheehan syndrome (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound and anti-Mullerian hormone level to assess her ovarian reserve as first step
options.rationale: Although she has amenorrhoea, the underlying problem in Sheehan is central (low LH/FSH and low oestradiol) rather than primary ovarian failure; AMH would assess ovarian reserve but does not establish the pituitary diagnosis (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Sheehan syndrome is ischaemic necrosis of the anterior pituitary following severe postpartum haemorrhage. Failure to lactate (loss of prolactin) is often the earliest feature; secondary amenorrhoea, cold intolerance, fatigue, hyponatraemia and postural hypotension follow. Workup screens each pituitary axis and includes MRI. Treat with glucocorticoid replacement first (hydrocortisone), then thyroxine, then sex steroid replacement; GH replacement is considered in symptomatic adults.
key_points: Failure to lactate is the most specific early clue
Treat cortisol before thyroxine to avoid adrenal crisis
Hyponatraemia is common (cortisol deficiency, hypothyroidism)
MRI may show empty or partially empty sella
Prolactin is typically low or undetectable (versus high in pituitary adenoma)
explanation.pearls: Any postpartum woman with prior major PPH who fails to lactate, fails to menstruate, and presents with non-specific fatigue, hypotension or cold intolerance should have a pituitary workup until proven otherwise. Hydrocortisone before levothyroxine - always.

q.subtopic_detail: Sheehan syndrome - investigation strategy after severe postpartum haemorrhage

q.stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman was diagnosed with preeclampsia at 36 weeks gestation and delivered at 37+0 by induction of labour with a spontaneous vaginal birth, complicated only by a small first-degree perineal tear that was sutured at the time. Antenatally she received labetalol 200 mg three times daily plus methyldopa 250 mg three times daily for blood pressure control, achieving target BP below 140/90 mmHg from 34 weeks. She is now day 6 postpartum and is breastfeeding her infant exclusively with good supply. Her blood pressure today on home monitoring has been 158/102 mmHg, 154/96 mmHg and 156/100 mmHg recorded over the last 24 hours despite continuing both of her antenatal antihypertensives at full antenatal dose. She has no headache, no visual disturbance, no epigastric pain, no nausea, no oliguria, no shortness of breath, and no peripheral oedema beyond mild ankle swelling. Her bloods this morning show a normal full blood count without thrombocytopaenia, normal renal function with creatinine 70 micromol/L, AST and ALT within normal limits, and a urine protein-creatinine ratio elevated at 60 mg/mmol (down from a peak of 180 mg/mmol antenatally). She mentions feeling low and tearful since day 3 postpartum and has not yet started any antidepressant. She specifically asks today whether she still needs her medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP today average 156/99 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: No focal neurology. No epigastric tenderness. Mild ankle oedema. Breasts soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, plts 220, creatinine 70 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L, urine PCR 60 mg/mmol.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate adjustment to her antihypertensive regimen?

options.text: Increase methyldopa to 500 mg three times daily and continue current labetalol dose
options.rationale: Methyldopa is associated with depression and sedation and is generally weaned postpartum rather than uptitrated; SOMANZ recommends switching to or escalating other agents in the puerperium (SOMANZ).

options.text: Wean and stop methyldopa, increase labetalol, add nifedipine controlled-release 30 mg daily if BP elevated
options.rationale: SOMANZ recommends weaning methyldopa postpartum (depression risk, no clear postpartum benefit), continuing or uptitrating labetalol and adding a calcium channel blocker such as nifedipine controlled-release as a second agent; all three are compatible with breastfeeding (SOMANZ; AMH).

options.text: Switch labetalol to atenolol 50 mg daily and continue current methyldopa dose
options.rationale: Atenolol concentrates in breast milk and may cause neonatal bradycardia, hypotension and hypoglycaemia; it is generally avoided in breastfeeding women (AMH).

options.text: Commence oral enalapril 5 mg daily and stop labetalol now in this patient
options.rationale: Enalapril and other ACE inhibitors (such as captopril and enalapril) are acceptable in breastfeeding but should not replace labetalol in a single switch with methyldopa wean still pending; SOMANZ prefers stepwise change and labetalol or nifedipine first-line postpartum (SOMANZ).

options.text: Stop all antihypertensives now because she is more than 5 days postpartum currently
options.rationale: Postpartum BP characteristically peaks around days 3 to 6 and preeclampsia can present or worsen up to 6 weeks postpartum; continued treatment is essential while BP is sustained at or above 150/100 mmHg (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Postpartum hypertension management after preeclampsia: continue and adjust antihypertensives, monitor BP for at least 6 weeks postpartum, wean methyldopa (depression risk), and use labetalol, nifedipine, enalapril or captopril as preferred agents in breastfeeding. Preeclampsia can present de novo up to 6 weeks postpartum.
key_points: Wean methyldopa postpartum (depression risk)
Labetalol, nifedipine CR, enalapril, captopril all compatible with BF
Avoid atenolol in BF (neonatal beta-blockade)
Treat BP >=150/100 mmHg sustained; aim BP <140/90 mmHg postpartum
Monitor BP and proteinuria up to 6 weeks; postpartum review at 6 weeks plus 6 to 12 months for CVD risk
explanation.pearls: Postnatal new-onset headache plus elevated BP within 6 weeks of delivery is preeclampsia or eclampsia until proven otherwise. Always screen mood in postpartum hypertensive women - methyldopa is a hidden contributor.

q.subtopic_detail: Postpartum hypertension - antihypertensive choice in a breastfeeding woman with prior preeclampsia

q.stem: A 36-year-old G1P1 woman attends her general practitioner at 10 days postpartum because, despite frequent on-demand feeding and continuous skin-to-skin contact since birth, she has produced very little breast milk and her infant has lost 11% of birth weight on weighed feeds today. The delivery was at 38+5 by elective lower segment caesarean section for breech presentation; the third stage was complicated by a placenta with a retained cotyledon noted on inspection that was manually removed in theatre under spinal anaesthesia, with no postpartum haemorrhage (estimated blood loss 380 mL total). She had no antenatal complications, normal booking and 36-week bloods including a normal FBC and ferritin, and was discharged on day 4 in stable condition. She noticed no antenatal breast enlargement during pregnancy, which was unusual compared with her friends. On examination her breasts are small, tubular, widely spaced and noticeably asymmetric (left larger than right), with minimal glandular fullness on palpation and no breast engorgement; there are no signs of inflammation, infection, fluctuance, or mastitis. Her uterus is involuted appropriately to size, her perineum is healed, and her observations are all stable. She is not on any medications, has no past breast surgery, no nipple piercings, and has been seen by an IBCLC lactation consultant twice with optimal latch, optimal positioning, and a normal infant suck assessment confirmed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Breasts small, asymmetric, widely spaced, minimal glandular tissue, no inflammation. Uterus involuted. Infant: well, tone good, suck normal on assessment.
data_table.Investigations: Pending - GP planning investigations.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to clarify the cause of failure to establish lactation?

options.text: Infant tongue-tie clinical assessment by paediatric ENT for surgical release if confirmed
options.rationale: Optimal latch and positioning have already been confirmed by lactation consultant assessment with a normal infant suck, so a primary infant-side cause is unlikely (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Maternal full blood count and ferritin level only as initial work-up
options.rationale: Anaemia may contribute to fatigue but rarely causes complete failure of lactogenesis II; targeted prolactin and pituitary axis testing is more informative (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound only as the initial single investigation for her presentation today
options.rationale: Pelvic USS is relevant to exclude retained products but is most useful in combination with prolactin and the wider workup; alone it misses pituitary, glandular and endocrine causes (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Serum prolactin and pituitary axis screen (cortisol, TSH and free T4, LH and FSH)
options.rationale: Failure to establish lactation despite optimal latch and positioning prompts investigation for hypoprolactinaemia (Sheehan), retained products causing persistent progesterone elevation, primary insufficient glandular tissue (suggested here by tubular, widely spaced, asymmetric breasts) and significant stress. Serum prolactin is the targeted first investigation, with concurrent screening of the pituitary axis to exclude Sheehan especially in any woman with peripartum bleeding or retained tissue (ABA; LactMed; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Empirical trial of oral domperidone 10 mg three times daily without further investigation today
options.rationale: Galactagogues such as domperidone have a role in confirmed primary low supply but should be used after the underlying cause has been characterised; empirical treatment risks missing Sheehan syndrome and retained products which require specific management (ABA; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

explanation.summary: Failure to establish lactation has several distinct causes: delayed lactogenesis II (most common, transient), retained placental fragments (progesterone continues to inhibit prolactin), Sheehan syndrome (low prolactin after major PPH), insufficient glandular tissue (tubular, asymmetric, widely spaced breasts), prior breast surgery, severe stress, and PCOS. Investigation is targeted: prolactin and pituitary axis first, plus pelvic USS for retained products if any third-stage concern.
key_points: Lactogenesis II is the copious milk production phase that normally begins at day 2 to 5 postpartum
Retained placental tissue continues progesterone production and inhibits lactogenesis II
IGT clinical features: tubular, widely spaced, asymmetric breasts; minimal antenatal growth
Sheehan presents with failure to lactate as an early sign
Galactagogues (domperidone, metoclopramide) only after diagnostic workup
explanation.pearls: If you cannot tick off latch, positioning, frequency, and infant transfer as adequate, fix those first. If they are adequate and supply remains poor, investigate the mother.

sources.label: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

q.subtopic_detail: Failure to lactate - differential and investigation

q.stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder that has worsened over the past 36 hours, with a pleuritic component that is worse on deep inspiration and on lying flat. She also reports four days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and intermenstrual bleeding. She has had two new male partners over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L today), has no significant medical history, has no history of gallstones or biliary disease, drinks 4 standard drinks per week, and is on no regular medications other than the etonogestrel implant. On examination her temperature is 38.1 degrees C, pulse 104 regular, blood pressure 116/72, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99% on room air. There is right upper quadrant tenderness without Murphy's sign, mild generalised lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism, and bilateral adnexal tenderness with cervical motion tenderness on bimanual examination. Speculum shows mucopurulent cervical discharge with contact bleeding. Liver function tests show mild transaminitis (ALT 78 U/L, AST 62 U/L) with normal bilirubin and ALP. Right upper quadrant ultrasound shows normal gallbladder and biliary tree with no stones, no wall thickening and no pericholecystic fluid. Pelvic ultrasound shows no tubo-ovarian abscess but trace pelvic free fluid. Endocervical NAAT is positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 38.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: RUQ tender, Murphy's negative. Lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism. CMT, bilateral adnexal tenderness. Mucopurulent cervical discharge.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. ALT 78, AST 62, bilirubin 14, ALP 92. RUQ US: normal GB, no stones. Pelvic US: no TOA, trace free fluid. NAAT: C. trachomatis positive.

q.lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis explaining the right upper quadrant pain?

options.text: Acute viral hepatitis A infection
options.rationale: Mild transaminitis with concurrent PID and chlamydia is far more consistent with perihepatitis; viral hepatitis would not explain CMT, mucopurulent cervicitis or RUQ pleuritic pain and should produce more marked aminotransferase elevation (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute calculous cholecystitis with cholangitis
options.rationale: RUQ ultrasound is normal with no stones, no wall thickening, no pericholecystic fluid and Murphy's sign is negative, making acute cholecystitis very unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Tubo-ovarian abscess with subdiaphragmatic spread
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound did not show a tubo-ovarian abscess; trace pelvic free fluid alone does not constitute a TOA, and a TOA would not typically present primarily with RUQ pain (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
options.rationale: Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L, excluding ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (gonococcal or chlamydial perihepatitis)
options.rationale: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis is perihepatitis from haematogenous or transperitoneal spread of chlamydia (or gonorrhoea) complicating PID. RUQ pain with mild transaminitis, normal biliary imaging and concurrent PID with positive chlamydia NAAT fits the classical picture (ASHM; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is perihepatitis complicating PID, classically due to Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae, producing RUQ pain with characteristic 'violin-string' adhesions between liver capsule and abdominal wall at laparoscopy. Mild transaminitis is common; biliary imaging is normal. Treatment is the same as for PID (ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole for 14 days).
key_points: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis = chlamydial/gonococcal perihepatitis
RUQ pain + PID + normal biliary tree
Violin-string adhesions on laparoscopy (classical)
Treat as PID (no different regimen)
Other PID complications: TOA, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, 6-10x ectopic risk
explanation.pearls: Suspect Fitz-Hugh-Curtis in any sexually active young woman with RUQ pain and pelvic findings, even when LFTs are only mildly deranged. It is a clinical and supportive imaging diagnosis - laparoscopy is rarely performed.

q.subtopic_detail: PID complication - right upper quadrant pain

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with chronic HIV-1 infection is reviewed at 36+2 weeks gestation in the antenatal HIV clinic to plan delivery. She has been on tenofovir/emtricitabine/dolutegravir throughout this pregnancy, commenced at 9 weeks gestation, and her adherence has been excellent on pharmacy refill audit. Her booking CD4 count was 540 cells/microL with a baseline HIV viral load of 8400 copies/mL prior to recommencing therapy. Her most recent HIV viral load taken at 36+0 weeks is < 50 copies/mL (suppressed) and her last three viral loads in the third trimester (at 28, 32 and 36 weeks) have all been < 50 copies/mL. She had a normal vaginal birth with her previous pregnancy four years ago when she was already on suppressive cART. She has no obstetric contraindication to vaginal birth, the foetus is cephalic with appropriate interval growth on the 50th centile, there is no placenta praevia on the 32-week scan, and she has not had any complications this pregnancy. Her hepatitis B and hepatitis C serology are negative, syphilis serology is negative, and the 36-week GBS swab is negative. She is asking about the recommended mode of delivery, whether she requires intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, whether her baby will require post-exposure prophylaxis, and whether she can breastfeed.

data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 36 cm. Cephalic presentation. No oedema.
data_table.Investigations: HIV viral load at 36+0: < 50 copies/mL (suppressed). CD4 540 cells/microL. HBV/HCV negative. GBS swab pending. Growth scan 34 weeks: 50th centile, normal Dopplers.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for mode of delivery and neonatal prophylaxis?

options.text: Planned vaginal birth, no intrapartum zidovudine required, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 2-4 weeks postnatally
options.rationale: Mode of delivery and neonatal PEP regimen are correct in spirit, but neonatal zidovudine is given for 4 weeks (in low-risk infants per Australian guidance) rather than the variable 2-4 week phrasing; the more precise duration is 4 weeks of neonatal zidovudine.

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine for 6 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: With a suppressed viral load, vaginal birth is preferred over caesarean; the maternal cART has effectively eliminated transmission risk and CS adds maternal morbidity without benefit (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 38+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal triple-drug PEP for 4 weeks, breastfeeding permitted if exclusive
options.rationale: Planned CS is reserved for viral load >= 50-1000 copies/mL near term; IV zidovudine and triple-drug neonatal PEP apply to higher-risk scenarios; breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia regardless of viral suppression (ASHM).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth at term, no intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: When maternal HIV viral load is < 50 copies/mL near term, planned vaginal birth is recommended; intravenous intrapartum zidovudine is NOT required for suppressed viral load; the neonate receives oral zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks; breastfeeding is not recommended in high-income settings where safe formula is available (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth with intrapartum intravenous zidovudine infusion regardless of viral load, neonatal nevirapine single dose only
options.rationale: IV intrapartum zidovudine is only required for unsuppressed viral load (>= 50-1000) or unknown viral load at delivery; neonatal single-dose nevirapine alone is inadequate and not the Australian standard (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Women on cART with viral load < 50 copies/mL near term can safely have a planned vaginal birth; intrapartum IV zidovudine is not required. Neonate receives 4 weeks of oral zidovudine monotherapy (low-risk). Formula feeding is recommended in Australia. Planned caesarean at 38-39 weeks is reserved for viral load >= 50-1000 (or unknown) near term, with IV zidovudine intrapartum and consideration of neonatal triple therapy.
key_points: VL < 50 at 36 weeks: vaginal birth, no IV zidovudine, neonatal AZT x 4 weeks
VL 50-1000: individualised; consider CS at 38-39 weeks
VL > 1000 or unknown: pre-labour CS at 38-39 weeks + IV zidovudine + neonatal triple PEP
Avoid invasive monitoring (FSE, FBS) if possible during labour
Formula feeding recommended in Australia (no breastfeeding)
explanation.pearls: Vertical transmission risk falls to < 1% (and approaches 0.1%) when maternal viral load is suppressed throughout pregnancy. The biggest modifiable factor is maternal adherence, not mode of delivery.

q.subtopic_detail: HIV in pregnancy - mode of delivery decision

q.stem: A 26-year-old woman attends the emergency department 28 hours after condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a male partner she has just learned is living with HIV-1. He told her his combination antiretroviral treatment was 'on and off' over the past six months because he had run out of his prescription, and she does not know his current viral load. She has no symptoms, no genital ulceration, no menstruation at the time of exposure, the partner does not inject drugs, and there was no concurrent intercourse with other partners. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no significant past medical history, is on no regular medications other than the etonogestrel implant, has no drug allergies, has normal renal function on baseline pathology (creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90), is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated as a teenager), HIV antibody/antigen negative on today's baseline 4th-generation test, hepatitis C antibody negative, and syphilis EIA negative. She is asking what medications she should take, how long she needs to take them for, when she will be re-tested for HIV, and what side effects she should monitor for. She is not breastfeeding and has no plans for pregnancy in the next month.

data_table.Examination: Well. No mucosal lesions.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab negative. HBsAb positive (vaccinated). HCV Ab negative. Syphilis EIA negative. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis regimen and follow-up plan?

options.text: Single-dose tenofovir 300 mg given today, then simply repeat HIV testing again at the 6-week timepoint after exposure
options.rationale: PEP requires a 28-day course of combination antiretrovirals, not a single dose or monotherapy, which would not prevent HIV acquisition (ASHM PEP guidelines).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg daily for 28 days, with no third agent required
options.rationale: Dual NRTI-only PEP is no longer recommended in Australia; current guidance is three-drug PEP (typically TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir or raltegravir) for high-risk exposures (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir alafenamide 25 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg plus rilpivirine 25 mg daily for 7 days only
options.rationale: PEP duration is 28 days, not 7 days; rilpivirine is not a standard PEP third agent due to interactions and resistance considerations (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks and 3 months
options.rationale: Australian PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a source person with HIV not on effective treatment, within 72 hours) is TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks, and 3 months post-exposure (ASHM Australian PEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PEP until the source partner's plasma viral load is known, then retest her at 4 weeks only
options.rationale: PEP efficacy falls rapidly with time and must be commenced within 72 hours (ideally < 24 hours); delaying for source results risks losing the treatment window (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Non-occupational HIV PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive anal/vaginal sex with a source with HIV who is not virally suppressed, within 72 hours) is TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg + dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days. Baseline tests: HIV Ag/Ab, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, renal function. Follow-up HIV tests at 6 weeks and 3 months (4th gen). Counsel on adherence, side effects, and condom use during follow-up.
key_points: Window: < 72 hours, ideally < 24 hours
Regimen: TDF/FTC + dolutegravir (or raltegravir) x 28 days
Baseline: HIV, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, U&E
Follow-up: HIV Ag/Ab at 6 weeks and 3 months
If source has confirmed undetectable VL, PEP may not be needed (U=U)
explanation.pearls: U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): a source with confirmed sustained viral load < 200 copies/mL transmits HIV through sex effectively at zero risk - in those cases, PEP is not required.

q.subtopic_detail: Non-occupational HIV post-exposure prophylaxis

q.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department at 0300 hours, brought by police, reporting that she was sexually assaulted approximately 14 hours ago at a private residence by an unknown male. The reported assault involved vaginal penetration without a condom, and she believes ejaculation occurred. She has showered once since the assault but has not changed her underwear, brushed her teeth, eaten or passed urine since the event. She has no significant medical history, is on no regular medications, has no drug allergies, has had a normal menstrual period that finished 8 days ago, and is sexually active with a regular partner from whom she uses no contraception. She has had her three-dose HPV vaccine course as a teenager but does not know her hepatitis B vaccination status and does not recall having received the catch-up adolescent dose. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative, has minor bruising on her right upper arm and a small abrasion on her left knee, and has not previously had a forensic examination. There is no immediate life-threatening injury, no head injury, no loss of consciousness, no respiratory or haemodynamic compromise, and she has not consumed alcohol or other substances in the past 24 hours. A specially trained forensic medical examiner is available on call at the local sexual assault service, with chain-of-custody documentation procedures in place, and a social worker can be paged. She is asking what will happen next, what medications she can be offered tonight to reduce pregnancy and infection risk, and whether the examination is mandatory. She has not yet decided whether to formally report to police and is uncertain about disclosing to her family.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Alert, distressed, cooperative. Bruise 3 cm on right upper arm. No external genital injury visible without colposcopic examination.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab, HBsAg/Ab, HCV Ab, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT and trichomonas NAAT sent. Pregnancy and STI follow-up planned per local sexual assault service protocol.

q.lead_in: Which set of medical and forensic actions is the most appropriate tonight?

options.text: Defer forensic examination until daylight to reduce patient distress, commence empirical STI prophylaxis and offer emergency contraception
options.rationale: Forensic evidence has a < 72-hour window with degradation over time; delaying examination risks loss of evidence and reduces forensic yield. The examination should be offered now by a trained examiner (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Proceed only with forensic examination tonight; defer STI prophylaxis and emergency contraception until after kit processed
options.rationale: Medical priorities (emergency contraception, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment, hepatitis B vaccination/HBIG, safety planning) must occur alongside forensic examination, not be deferred for kit processing (RANZCOG).

options.text: Forensic examination can only proceed if the patient agrees to formally report the assault to police today
options.rationale: Forensic examination should be offered regardless of reporting intent; many services hold kits anonymously to preserve future reporting options. Linking forensic care to reporting acts as a barrier to disclosure (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Forensic examination is contraindicated because the patient has showered; reassure and discharge with general practice follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Showering reduces but does not eliminate forensic yield; trace DNA and other evidence may still be recoverable, and discharge without addressing EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP, HBV and safety is unsafe (RANZCOG).

options.text: Offer immediate forensic examination with chain-of-custody, emergency contraception, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP if eligible, hepatitis B vaccination and safety planning
options.rationale: Acute sexual assault management within 72 hours requires concurrent attention to medical priorities (EC within 120 hours, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment within 72 hours, HBV protection, mental health support and safety planning) and forensic priorities (kit collection with chain of custody by a trained examiner), regardless of whether the patient has decided to report (RANZCOG; ASHM; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance). Full care includes: forensic examination by trained examiner with documented chain of custody; LNG or UPA or copper IUD for emergency contraception; ceftriaxone IM plus azithromycin or doxycycline plus metronidazole for empirical STI prophylaxis; HIV PEP if eligible; hepatitis B vaccination (and HBIG if indicated); safety planning, counselling and follow-up.
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

explanation.summary: Acute presentation within 72 hours of sexual assault requires concurrent medical and forensic priorities: forensic examination with chain of custody by a trained examiner regardless of reporting intent; emergency contraception (levonorgestrel up to 96 hours, ulipristal up to 120 hours, or copper IUD up to 5 days); STI baseline tests and prophylaxis (ceftriaxone 500 mg IM, azithromycin 1 g or doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days, metronidazole 2 g single dose); HIV PEP eligibility assessment for source with HIV or unknown source with high-risk features; hepatitis B vaccination and HBIG where indicated; safety planning; mental health support and follow-up at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months.
key_points: Forensic kit window: 72 hours (some up to 7 days for specific samples)
EC: levonorgestrel < 96 h, ulipristal < 120 h, Cu-IUD < 5 days
STI prophylaxis: ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline + metronidazole
HIV PEP: assess within 72 h; high-risk sources warrant PEP
HBV: vaccinate if unvaccinated; HBIG if source HBsAg-positive
Forensic exam offered regardless of reporting intent
explanation.pearls: Documentation matters - use trained examiners (FAMU/SARC), follow chain-of-custody, photograph injuries, store kit per jurisdiction protocol. Mental-health and safety planning are not afterthoughts; ongoing risk of further violence is high in the first weeks.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

q.subtopic_detail: Acute sexual assault - forensic and medical priorities

q.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left iliac fossa pain that began two hours ago while exercising, associated with one episode of vomiting. The pain is colicky on a background of constant severe pain and radiates to the left flank. Her last menstrual period was 35 days ago and she normally has a 28-day cycle, but she has had irregular cycles in the past. She has had one sexual partner over the past year, uses no contraception and has had no recent abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge. She has no fever, no dysuria, no diarrhoea, no anorexia and no migratory periumbilical pain. On examination temperature is 36.9 degrees C, pulse 104, BP 116/72. The abdomen is tender in the left iliac fossa with voluntary guarding but no rebound or percussion tenderness; right iliac fossa is non-tender. Bimanual examination reveals a tender left adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness and the right adnexa is non-tender. Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bedside ultrasound by the registrar shows a 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow and reduced arterial flow.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Left iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no rebound. Right iliac fossa non-tender. Bimanual: tender left adnexal fullness, no cervical motion tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocyte esterase negative. Bedside US: 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow, reduced arterial flow.

q.lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Left ovarian torsion with ischaemia
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pain with vomiting on a background of a 7 cm ovarian cyst, tender adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness, negative beta-hCG, and ultrasound showing absent venous and reduced arterial Doppler flow are classical for ovarian torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
options.rationale: Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L, definitively excluding ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
options.rationale: PID typically presents with bilateral pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge and fever, often with subacute onset over days. The acute unilateral picture with abnormal Doppler on a cyst fits torsion better (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Acute appendicitis with pelvic peritonism
options.rationale: Appendicitis classically produces migratory periumbilical-to-right-iliac-fossa pain with anorexia and low-grade fever; left iliac fossa pain with adnexal Doppler abnormalities makes this unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Renal colic from a left ureteric stone
options.rationale: Renal colic typically produces flank-to-groin pain with microscopic haematuria; urinalysis shows no blood and ultrasound localises the abnormality to the ovary with abnormal Doppler, making torsion far more likely (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Classical features: sudden unilateral pelvic pain, often during physical activity, with nausea and vomiting; tender adnexal mass on bimanual examination; ovarian cyst (typically > 5 cm) on imaging; reduced or absent Doppler flow (venous flow lost first, then arterial). Diagnosis often requires diagnostic laparoscopy as Doppler can be normal in intermittent torsion. Detorsion with cystectomy is preferred over oophorectomy when ovarian tissue appears viable.
key_points: Torsion: sudden unilateral pain, vomiting, cyst > 5 cm, abnormal Doppler
Ectopic: beta-hCG positive, missed period, PV bleed, hypovolaemia if ruptured
PID: bilateral pain, fever, CMT, mucopurulent discharge, days-onset
Appendicitis: migratory pain, anorexia, RIF, McBurney's, Rovsing's
Doppler: venous flow lost first; intermittent torsion may show preserved flow
explanation.pearls: Beta-hCG must be checked in every woman of reproductive age with pelvic pain; missing ectopic is a never-event. A normal Doppler does not exclude torsion - if clinical suspicion is high, proceed to diagnostic laparoscopy.

q.subtopic_detail: Acute pelvic pain - differential diagnosis
Morphology scan - soft marker (echogenic intracardiac focus)
q.stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practitioner for review of her 19 week and 4 day morphology scan, performed at an accredited Australian foetal medicine imaging service. She had a low-risk combined first-trimester screen at 12+5 weeks (adjusted T21 risk 1 in 4200, NT 1.4 mm). The morphology scan report describes normal biometry on the 50th centile, a structurally normal foetus on the standard RANZCOG/ASUM views, a normally implanted posterior placenta clear of the os, normal amniotic fluid volume, and an isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle as the only abnormal finding. The cardiac four-chamber view, outflow tracts and three-vessel view are otherwise normal. There are no other soft markers (nuchal fold normal, no echogenic bowel, no pyelectasis, no short femur or humerus, no choroid plexus cyst). She has no significant medical history, no family history of congenital heart disease and is not on any medications. She is anxious because she has read online that EIF is associated with Down syndrome and asks her general practitioner what this finding means in her context and what she should do next.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundus at the umbilicus.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS at 12+5: T21 risk 1 in 4200, NT 1.4 mm. Morphology scan 19+4: biometry 50th centile, structurally normal, isolated left ventricular EIF, no other soft markers, normal placenta and fluid.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Offer additional non-invasive prenatal testing today to further confirm low aneuploidy risk before reassuring her about the isolated EIF finding
options.rationale: NIPT can be offered if the woman wishes, but it is not indicated by an isolated EIF in a woman who already has a low-risk cFTS; the residual probability of aneuploidy is too low for routine contingent screening to be cost-effective (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Refer urgently to maternal foetal medicine and offer amniocentesis given the increased risk of trisomy 21 from the isolated EIF
options.rationale: Isolated EIF in a low-risk population carries a small likelihood ratio (about 1.4 to 1.8) that does not meaningfully change a prior risk of 1 in 4200; offering amniocentesis exposes the woman to a procedure-related miscarriage risk that exceeds the residual aneuploidy probability (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Arrange a foetal echocardiogram urgently to exclude an associated structural cardiac anomaly given the EIF finding
options.rationale: An isolated EIF in the absence of other findings on a high-quality four-chamber, outflow tract and three-vessel view is not an indication for foetal echocardiography; foetal echo is indicated for diabetes, NT >=3.5 mm, family history of CHD, or other suspicious findings (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Repeat the morphology scan in four weeks to confirm persistence of the EIF before deciding on further investigations
options.rationale: An isolated EIF is a static finding requiring no surveillance; repeat scanning is not recommended unless there are growth or other concerns (ASUM).

options.text: Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in this low-risk woman does not meaningfully alter her aneuploidy risk; reassure and continue routine antenatal care
options.rationale: An isolated EIF in a woman with a low-risk first-trimester screen and no other soft markers does not meaningfully alter the prior risk of aneuploidy and is not an indication for diagnostic testing. RANZCOG and ASUM advise reassurance in this setting; the focus is most often a benign mineralisation in a papillary muscle (RANZCOG; ASUM).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: Isolated soft markers such as an echogenic intracardiac focus, choroid plexus cyst or mild pyelectasis carry only modest likelihood ratios and should be interpreted in the context of the prior screening risk. In a woman with a low-risk cFTS and no other soft markers, isolated EIF does not change management.
key_points: Soft markers: nuchal fold >=6 mm, echogenic bowel, short humerus/femur, EIF, pyelectasis >=4 mm, choroid plexus cyst
Isolated EIF likelihood ratio for T21 ~1.4 to 1.8
Nuchal fold >=6 mm has highest likelihood ratio (LR ~17 to 20)
Foetal echo if NT >=3.5 mm, diabetes, family history CHD, or structural cardiac concern
Soft markers do not require repeat scanning if isolated
explanation.pearls: Always reframe soft markers using the woman's pre-test risk - the absolute residual aneuploidy probability matters more than the likelihood ratio in isolation.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

q.subtopic_detail: Ectopic pregnancy - methotrexate eligibility
q.stem: A 29-year-old G2P0 woman is reviewed in a tertiary early pregnancy assessment unit at 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by last menstrual period after referral from her general practitioner with mild right iliac fossa pain and intermittent light per-vaginal spotting for 48 hours. Her previous pregnancy was a first-trimester miscarriage; she has no history of pelvic infection, prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD or assisted reproduction. She is haemodynamically stable, afebrile, and has no shoulder-tip pain, dizziness or peritonism. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with no intrauterine gestational sac, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas, and an unruptured right adnexal mass measuring 28 mm containing a yolk sac but no foetal pole or cardiac activity. There is no haemoperitoneum. Her beta-hCG today is 3800 IU/L (yesterday 3500 IU/L), liver function tests, full blood count and renal function are normal, and she has no contraindications to methotrexate including no breastfeeding, no peptic ulcer disease, no significant pulmonary or hepatic disease. She lives 20 minutes from the hospital, has reliable transport, can attend for follow-up blood tests, and is keen to avoid surgery if reasonable. The obstetric registrar is considering single-dose intramuscular methotrexate as outpatient management and is checking each eligibility criterion against the RANZCOG and RCOG protocols.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Mild RIF tenderness, no peritonism. Soft abdomen. No CMT. No active bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 3800 IU/L (yesterday 3500). FBC, UEC, LFTs normal. TV USS: empty uterus, no free fluid, right adnexal mass 28 mm with yolk sac, no foetal cardiac activity.

q.lead_in: Which feature in this presentation, if changed, would most clearly contraindicate single-dose IM methotrexate as outpatient management?

options.text: An adnexal mass measuring 28 mm with a yolk sac present but no foetal cardiac activity
options.rationale: The RANZCOG/RCOG eligibility criteria include adnexal mass <35 mm and absence of foetal cardiac activity; her mass is within this size and there is no cardiac activity. This finding does not contraindicate methotrexate (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: A serum beta-hCG of 3800 IU/L with a 24-hour rise of 300 IU/L
options.rationale: Methotrexate eligibility usually requires beta-hCG <5000 IU/L (some units 1500 to 5000); 3800 is below threshold and a small rise is consistent with ectopic. This is within the eligible range (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on the transvaginal ultrasound today within the right adnexal mass
options.rationale: The presence of foetal cardiac activity within the ectopic is an explicit contraindication to single-dose methotrexate in both RANZCOG and RCOG guidance because failure rates are much higher; salpingectomy or salpingostomy is the recommended management. Other absolute contraindications are haemodynamic instability, ruptured ectopic, significant haemoperitoneum, intrauterine pregnancy, breastfeeding, immunodeficiency, active hepatic or renal disease, blood dyscrasia, and inability to comply with follow-up (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Mild right iliac fossa tenderness without peritonism, fever or haemodynamic instability
options.rationale: Mild pain in a stable patient with no peritonism is not a contraindication and is common in tubal ectopic; only acute severe pain or peritonism would mandate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Residence 20 minutes from the hospital with reliable transport and stated willingness to attend follow-up
options.rationale: Good access to care and willingness to follow up are pre-requisites for outpatient methotrexate and support, rather than contraindicate, this management option (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Single-dose IM methotrexate (typically 50 mg/m2 in Australian units) is suitable for haemodynamically stable women with an unruptured tubal ectopic of <35 mm, no cardiac activity, beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, no contraindications and reliable follow-up. Failure or rising hCG triggers a second dose or surgery.
key_points: Methotrexate dose: 50 mg/m2 IM single dose, repeat at day 7 if <15% fall day 4 to 7
Eligibility: stable, unruptured, mass <35 mm, no FCA, hCG <5000, normal LFTs/FBC/UEC, no breastfeeding
Absolute contraindications: ruptured ectopic, haemodynamic instability, FCA, IUP coexists, blood dyscrasia, hepatic/renal disease, immunodeficiency, peptic ulcer disease, breastfeeding
Avoid pregnancy for 3 months after methotrexate; folate supplementation pre-conception
Surgery (laparoscopic salpingectomy) if unstable, ruptured, large mass, FCA, or contraindications to MTX
explanation.pearls: Always confirm reliable follow-up before MTX - missed day 4 and day 7 hCG checks is the commonest reason for treatment failure leading to rupture.

q.subtopic_detail: Pregnancy of unknown location - serial beta-hCG and discriminatory zone
q.stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman presents to an early pregnancy assessment unit with a positive urine pregnancy test, light per-vaginal spotting for 3 days and intermittent mild lower abdominal cramping. By last menstrual period she is approximately 6 weeks gestation but her cycles have been irregular over the last 12 months while she has been training for a half-marathon. She is haemodynamically stable, with no peritonism, shoulder-tip pain or dizziness. A high-quality transvaginal ultrasound performed by an experienced sonographer today shows an empty uterus with a thin endometrium, no intrauterine gestational sac, no adnexal mass, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas and normal-appearing ovaries bilaterally. Her serum beta-hCG today is 1200 IU/L. She has no significant past medical or surgical history, no risk factors for ectopic pregnancy (no prior pelvic infection, IUD, tubal surgery or assisted reproductive technology), and no contraindications to methotrexate. She is well, has reliable transport and is willing to attend for repeat blood tests. The clinic registrar must decide whether to expectantly manage with serial hCG and rescanning above the discriminatory zone, image again immediately, treat empirically as ectopic with methotrexate, or reassure as presumed early viable intrauterine pregnancy below the imaging threshold.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no peritonism, no CMT. Os closed, minimal brown spotting.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today. TV USS: empty uterus, thin endometrium, no adnexal mass, no free fluid, normal ovaries.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 today as presumed ectopic pregnancy based on the empty uterus on transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: A pregnancy of unknown location with hCG below the transvaginal discriminatory zone (approximately 1500 to 2000 IU/L) and no ectopic seen on ultrasound is not yet diagnosed as ectopic; treating with methotrexate could terminate a viable early intrauterine pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Arrange diagnostic laparoscopy today for definitive diagnosis and possible salpingectomy because of the empty uterus and positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for haemodynamic instability, suspected rupture or persistent diagnostic uncertainty after failed expectant management; it is not first line for PUL in a stable woman (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the serum beta-hCG in 48 hours and rescan once it exceeds the discriminatory zone of 1500 to 2000 IU/L, with clear safety-netting
options.rationale: Pregnancy of unknown location in a stable woman with hCG below the discriminatory zone is managed with serial 48-hour hCG and rescanning once the hCG exceeds the threshold. A normal early intrauterine pregnancy doubles hCG every 48 to 72 hours; a fall >=50% suggests failing pregnancy; a plateau or sub-optimal rise increases ectopic concern. Safety-netting and ready re-presentation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound today by a second sonographer to confirm absence of an intrauterine pregnancy before any follow-up
options.rationale: A high-quality scan has already been performed; immediate repeat scanning is low-yield. Waiting for the hCG to cross the discriminatory zone is the standard approach (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure that this is a normal early intrauterine pregnancy and arrange routine antenatal booking for 8 to 10 weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance without exclusion of ectopic risks rupture; PUL must be followed up to a definitive diagnosis (IUP, failing PUL, ectopic) (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Pregnancy of unknown location is defined as a positive pregnancy test with no intrauterine or extrauterine pregnancy on high-quality TV ultrasound. Management is risk-stratified by serial beta-hCG every 48 hours and rescanning once the hCG exceeds the discriminatory zone (approximately 1500 to 2000 IU/L on TV scan).
key_points: Discriminatory zone TV scan: 1500 to 2000 IU/L (some Australian units 1500)
Normal early IUP: hCG doubles every 48 to 72 hours
Failing PUL: >=50% fall in 48 hours
Suboptimal rise (<63% in 48 h) or plateau: high risk of ectopic
Safety net: PV bleeding, severe pain, shoulder-tip pain, dizziness - immediate review
explanation.pearls: Never treat a PUL with methotrexate based on a single hCG and an empty uterus; you must exclude a viable early IUP first via serial hCG and repeat imaging.

q.subtopic_detail: Molar pregnancy - recognition and surveillance
