q.stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her general practitioner at an estimated 11 weeks gestation by uncertain last menstrual period with three weeks of persistent nausea, intermittent vaginal spotting and one episode of passing what she describes as small grape-like tissue. She has no significant medical history. Examination shows a uterus palpable above the symphysis to a size consistent with 14 weeks, soft and non-tender. Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg (previously 110/70 at GP visit at 8 weeks). She has dry mucous membranes and reports mild postural symptoms. A transvaginal ultrasound at the local imaging service today shows a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with multiple small cystic spaces (described as 'snowstorm' appearance), no foetal pole, no foetal cardiac activity, and bilateral theca lutein cysts on the ovaries measuring 4 to 5 cm. The serum beta-hCG is 285 000 IU/L. Thyroid function tests show TSH 0.05 mU/L and free T4 22 pmol/L (mildly elevated). She has not been on any thyroid medication. She has been referred to the local gynaecology team and her GP wants to confirm the recognition and what the next steps are.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus size at 14 weeks (LMP-dated 11 weeks). Dry mucosa. Mild postural light-headedness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 285 000 IU/L. TSH 0.05 mU/L, fT4 22 pmol/L. TV USS: heterogeneous 'snowstorm' mass, no foetal pole, bilateral theca lutein cysts 4 to 5 cm.

q.lead_in: Which sequence of management most accurately reflects current Australian (RANZCOG) guidance for this presentation?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as outpatient with weekly hCG until undetectable, then 6-monthly surveillance for the following 12 months
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line for persistent gestational trophoblastic neoplasia, not for primary evacuation of a hydatidiform mole. Surgical evacuation is the primary treatment (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Suction curettage in theatre with histology, weekly hCG until three negative samples then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial), with reliable contraception
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the Australian/New Zealand Gestational Trophoblastic Disease group recommend suction curettage as the primary treatment for hydatidiform mole. Histopathology confirms complete (no foetus, diploid androgenetic) vs partial (foetus or foetal parts, triploid) mole. Surveillance: weekly beta-hCG until three consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete mole) or until one month after normal hCG (partial). Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout because pregnancy obscures rising hCG that would indicate persistent GTN (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG only, given that many molar pregnancies will resolve spontaneously without surgical or medical intervention
options.rationale: Molar pregnancies do not reliably resolve spontaneously; surgical evacuation with histological confirmation is required to confirm the diagnosis and remove the trophoblastic tissue (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as primary treatment given the high reported risk of choriocarcinoma associated with complete hydatidiform mole on histology
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed their family AND have a complete mole, or for refractory disease; suction curettage is the standard first-line for women wishing to preserve fertility (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Medical evacuation of the mole with methylergometrine and misoprostol, followed by weekly serum beta-hCG until undetectable for three consecutive weekly samples
options.rationale: Medical evacuation of a mole with uterotonics or prostaglandins is associated with higher rates of incomplete evacuation, embolisation of trophoblastic tissue and persistent GTN. Suction curettage is preferred (RANZCOG GTD).

explanation.summary: Hydatidiform mole is treated by suction curettage in theatre with histopathological confirmation. Beta-hCG surveillance follows: weekly until three consecutive negative samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or one month (partial). Reliable contraception is required throughout because pregnancy obscures the rising hCG signal of persistent GTN.
key_points: Complete mole: no foetus, 'snowstorm' USS, hCG often >100 000, 15 to 20% progress to GTN
Partial mole: foetus/foetal parts, triploid, 1 to 5% progress to GTN
Primary management: suction curettage in theatre with histology
Surveillance: weekly hCG until 3 consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial)
Contraception throughout surveillance; avoid pregnancy until cleared
Refer to GTD multidisciplinary service if hCG plateaus, rises, or new metastatic features develop
explanation.pearls: Suspect mole in any first-trimester pregnancy with hyperemesis, early pre-eclampsia, hyperthyroidism, very high hCG, or uterus large for dates - get a TV scan.

q.subtopic_detail: Heterotopic pregnancy after ART - discrimination from PUL/ectopic
q.stem: A 34-year-old G3P0 woman conceived through a single fresh embryo transfer IVF cycle two weeks ago and presents to a tertiary early pregnancy assessment service at 6 weeks and 5 days gestation by transfer date. She has had two previous tubal ectopic pregnancies managed with laparoscopic salpingectomy (left partial and right partial), with severe periadhesive disease documented at the second operation, and an extensive endometriosis history with bilateral chocolate cysts treated 4 years ago. She takes only luteal-phase progesterone pessaries 200 mg twice daily as part of her IVF protocol and a pregnancy multivitamin; she has no other medications and no drug allergies. Over the last 72 hours she has developed unilateral left iliac fossa pain that is intermittent but increasing, with one episode of dizziness this morning when standing. She has had no vaginal bleeding and is haemodynamically stable on arrival. Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%, respiratory rate 16. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness in the left iliac fossa without peritonism; the cervical os is closed and there is no active bleeding on speculum. Serum beta-hCG today is 12 000 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound by an experienced foetal medicine sonographer demonstrates a single intrauterine gestational sac containing a yolk sac and a viable foetal pole with CRL 7 mm and foetal cardiac activity at 122 bpm. There is a separate left adnexal complex mass measuring 30 mm with a hyperechoic ring (sometimes called the 'tubal ring' or 'bagel sign') and a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas (approximately 50 mL). Her LFTs, FBC, renal function and group/screen are normal; she is RhD positive. The on-call gynaecology consultant has been called and is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism. Os closed. No active bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 12 000 IU/L. TV USS: viable intrauterine pregnancy at 6+5 (CRL 7 mm, FH 122 bpm) AND separate left adnexal complex mass 30 mm with echogenic ring; small free fluid POD ~50 mL.

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 to treat the left adnexal ectopic, with serial beta-hCG monitoring to track resolution while continuing the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated when a viable intrauterine pregnancy coexists with an ectopic (heterotopic pregnancy) because it would terminate the IUP; surgical management is required (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Expectant management with serial 48-hour beta-hCG and repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 1 week, given haemodynamic stability and the presence of a viable intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy with a 30 mm adnexal mass, increasing pain, dizziness and free fluid carries a high rupture risk; expectant management is not appropriate. The beta-hCG trajectory is also uninterpretable because the IUP confounds it (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia with 6-hourly observations, planning to operate only if she develops haemodynamic compromise
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a haemodynamically stable but symptomatic heterotopic pregnancy with adnexal mass and free fluid is unsafe; rupture risk is high and definitive surgery should be planned urgently rather than reactively (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ultrasound-guided potassium chloride injection into the adnexal mass with continued expectant care of the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: KCl injection is occasionally used in interstitial or cornual ectopics where surgical access is hazardous, but it is not first-line for a tubal heterotopic with rupture risk and prior salpingectomy; laparoscopic surgery remains the standard (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic salpingectomy of the left adnexal ectopic with intra-operative care to preserve the intrauterine pregnancy, plus anti-D if RhD negative
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy (coexistent IUP and ectopic, much more common after ART) requires surgical treatment of the ectopic component because methotrexate would terminate the IUP. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is preferred for a complete tubal ectopic, particularly with prior contralateral salpingectomy; care is taken to minimise uterine manipulation and continue luteal support to protect the IUP. Anti-D 250 IU IM is given if RhD negative as this is a sensitising event pre-12 weeks (RANZCOG; RCOG; NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Heterotopic pregnancy (intrauterine plus ectopic) is rare in spontaneous conception (~1 in 30 000) but much more common after assisted reproductive technology (~1 in 100 to 1 in 500). Methotrexate is contraindicated because it would terminate the IUP; laparoscopic surgery is the treatment of choice, taking care to minimise uterine manipulation and continue luteal support.
key_points: Heterotopic incidence: ~1 in 30 000 spontaneous; ~1 in 100 to 500 with ART
hCG trajectory is uninterpretable when an IUP is present
Methotrexate contraindicated when a viable IUP coexists - it would terminate the IUP
Surgical treatment: laparoscopic salpingectomy (or salpingostomy) with minimal uterine handling, continue luteal support
Anti-D 250 IU IM if RhD negative; this is a sensitising event regardless of gestation
Suspect heterotopic in any post-ART pregnancy with pain and adnexal findings even when an IUP is visible
explanation.pearls: Always image both adnexae in any pregnant ART patient with pelvic pain, even after confirming an intrauterine pregnancy - the IUP does not exclude a coexistent ectopic.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

q.stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 40+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4100 g infant with a mediolateral episiotomy following a 90-minute active second stage. The CTG had been normal throughout and Apgar scores were 9 and 9. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities, GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative, and the antenatal course was uncomplicated apart from a borderline GTT that was normal on dietary management. Active management of the third stage has been completed with delivery of a complete placenta and intact membranes, with an estimated blood loss of 350 mL. On systematic per-vaginum and per-rectum examination by the obstetric registrar, there is a tear extending through the perineal skin, all layers of the perineal muscles, and approximately 60% of the thickness of the external anal sphincter complex. The internal anal sphincter is identified as intact on PR examination, and the rectal mucosa is intact with no buttonhole tear. Bleeding is controlled with pressure, the woman is haemodynamically stable, and she has a working epidural that has been topped up. The registrar plans repair in theatre with appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis and postoperative care.

data_table.Examination: Tear through perineal skin, perineal muscles, partial external anal sphincter (~60% thickness). Internal anal sphincter intact on PR. Rectal mucosa intact, no buttonhole.
data_table.Maternal: BP 116/72, HR 84, working epidural, haemodynamically stable.

q.lead_in: Which classification and repair plan is correct?

options.text: Second-degree perineal tear, repaired on the labour ward using absorbable suture material as standard
options.rationale: Second-degree tears involve perineal muscle but not the anal sphincter; involvement of the external anal sphincter makes this a third-degree (OASI) tear (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3a, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external anal sphincter repair
options.rationale: 3a is defined as <50% thickness of the external anal sphincter torn; this tear involves ~60% thickness, making it 3b (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3c, repair in theatre with a separate internal anal sphincter repair done
options.rationale: 3c requires internal anal sphincter involvement; the internal sphincter is intact on PR in this scenario (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3b, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external sphincter repair plus antibiotics, laxatives and physio
options.rationale: Sultan classification: 3a <50% EAS thickness, 3b >50% EAS thickness, 3c EAS and internal anal sphincter torn, 4 includes rectal mucosa. With ~60% EAS thickness and intact IAS and mucosa, this is a 3b. Repair in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia with end-to-end or overlap technique for the EAS (equivalent outcomes), single-dose IV antibiotics, oral laxatives (lactulose for 10-14 days), pelvic floor physiotherapy, and 6-12 week follow-up (RANZCOG OASI 2020; RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears

options.text: Fourth-degree perineal tear, repaired in theatre with rectal mucosal closure as the first step
options.rationale: Fourth-degree tears extend through the rectal mucosa; the mucosa is intact here (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

explanation.summary: Sultan classification of perineal trauma: 1st - skin only; 2nd - perineal muscle; 3rd - anal sphincter (3a <50% EAS, 3b >50% EAS, 3c EAS+IAS); 4th - rectal mucosa. OASI repair: theatre, regional anaesthesia, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, end-to-end or overlap EAS repair (equivalent outcomes), separate IAS repair if torn, mucosal repair for 4th degree, laxatives 10-14 days, physiotherapy, follow-up 6-12 weeks.
key_points: 3a <50% EAS, 3b >50% EAS, 3c EAS + IAS torn, 4th involves rectal mucosa
Repair in theatre with good lighting and assistance
PR examination is mandatory after every vaginal delivery to exclude buttonhole tear
Single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotics at repair, then oral 5-7 days
Lactulose (not stool softener) to reduce wound dehiscence
Future deliveries: vaginal birth acceptable if asymptomatic; counsel about 5-7% recurrence
explanation.pearls: PR every birth. If you do not feel the sphincter, you cannot exclude OASI. The 50% rule separates 3a from 3b.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears

q.subtopic_detail: Perineal trauma classification and OASI repair principles

q.stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 37+3 attends day assessment with a one-week history of frontal headaches not relieved by paracetamol and new BP elevation noted by her GP. Her booking BP was 110/68 and her booking BMI was 28, with no chronic hypertension, no renal disease, no autoimmune disease, no prior gestational hypertension and no family history of pre-eclampsia. She is a non-smoker and took aspirin 100 mg from 12 weeks. Today her BP on arrival is 158/102 on repeat measurement 15 minutes apart in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff, urine protein creatinine ratio is 65 mg/mmol, platelets are 145 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, ALT 32 U/L, urate 0.39 mmol/L, and the foetus is appropriately grown on the 40th centile with normal umbilical artery Dopplers, normal AFI and a reassuring CTG. She has had labetalol 200 mg orally with a fall to 142/92 after one hour and the headache has eased. There is no eclampsia, no epigastric pain, no visual disturbance, no clonus, no pulmonary oedema and no oliguria. On vaginal examination the cervix is closed, posterior, 2 cm long, firm, station -3, giving a Bishop score of 1. The on-call consultant reviews her in the unit and discusses options regarding admission, antihypertensive management, MgSO4 use and timing and route of delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/102 then 142/92 post-labetalol, HR 88, T 36.8, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Bloods: Platelets 145 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, ALT 32 U/L, urate 0.39 mmol/L. uPCR 65 mg/mmol.
data_table.Foetal: 40th centile, normal Dopplers, normal AFI, reassuring CTG.
data_table.Cervix: Closed, posterior, 2 cm long, firm, station -3 (Bishop 1).

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home on oral labetalol with weekly clinic review until 39+0 then induce labour
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia diagnosed at or beyond 37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery in the same admission, not expectant management to 39 weeks (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Admit, continue oral antihypertensive to keep BP <140/90, induce labour this admission, MgSO4 only if severe features develop
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia at >=37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133; HYPITAT trial 2009). With a Bishop score of 1 the route is induction of labour rather than caesarean unless other obstetric indications. Antihypertensive target is <140/90 (oral labetalol, nifedipine or methyldopa). MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis is indicated for severe pre-eclampsia (severe hypertension >=160/110, neurological features, severe biochemical features) rather than routine non-severe disease (SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Proceed to a category 2 caesarean section today given the unfavourable cervix on examination
options.rationale: Bishop 1 does not mandate caesarean in non-severe PE with reassuring foetal status; ripening with Foley or PGE2 followed by induction is appropriate (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit, give MgSO4 4 g loading then 1 g/h infusion regardless of severity, and induce labour now
options.rationale: Routine MgSO4 for all PE is not recommended in non-severe disease in SOMANZ; it is reserved for severe PE or eclampsia or imminent eclampsia features (severe BP, neurological signs, severe biochemistry) (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Admit for 48 hours of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses before any induction
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at 37+3 weeks because foetal lung maturity is sufficiently advanced; steroids are used between 24+0 and 34+6 (RANZCOG corticosteroids; SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia at >=37+0 weeks: deliver. Route depends on Bishop and other obstetric factors. Antihypertensive target <140/90 (oral labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; IV hydralazine or labetalol for severe). MgSO4 for severe PE (4 g IV loading, 1 g/h infusion) for seizure prophylaxis. Continue antihypertensives postpartum and review at 6 weeks.
key_points: PE definition: new HTN >=20 wks plus one of proteinuria, AKI, hepatic, neurological, haematological, uteroplacental dysfunction
Severe features: BP >=160/110, platelets <100, creatinine >90, ALT >2x ULN, headache/visual/clonus, pulmonary oedema, FGR
Delivery: >=37+0 PE deliver; 34-37 expectant in selected cases with severe features under tertiary care; <34 expectant unless severe and uncontrolled
MgSO4: 4 g IV over 20 min then 1 g/h for 24 h post-birth or post-last seizure
Postpartum BP typically peaks day 3-5
explanation.pearls: 37+0 PE = deliver. MgSO4 is for severe, not all. Antihypertensive target is <140/90 antenatally and postnatally.

q.subtopic_detail: Pre-eclampsia at term - timing of delivery and intrapartum management

q.stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 39+4 presents at 0200 with a sudden gush of clear fluid from the vagina that occurred 30 minutes ago while watching television. On sterile speculum examination there is a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix, with a positive fern test on a dried slide and a positive IGFBP-1 swab. There is no offensive odour, no PV bleeding, no meconium and no foetal scalp visible. The foetus is cephalic, the head is engaged on abdominal palpation, and the CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 138 bpm, good variability of 10-15 bpm, accelerations and no decelerations. The cervix is closed and 2 cm long on sterile speculum visualisation; digital examination has not been performed. She is afebrile at T 36.7, HR 78, BP 116/72 and her white cell count is 9 x10^9/L with CRP <5 mg/L. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities and her GBS vaginorectal swab at 36+1 was negative. She is having occasional irregular tightenings every 10-20 minutes but is not in established labour. She would prefer to avoid medical interventions if possible and is keen on as physiological a birth as is safe. The on-call obstetric registrar discusses her options.

data_table.Examination: SROM confirmed (pool, ferning, IGFBP-1 positive), clear liquor, no meconium, no foul odour, head engaged.

q.lead_in: Which best describes the optimal management plan for this woman?

options.text: Discharge home with daily review for up to 72 hours, then induce labour if not in labour
options.rationale: Australian practice for term PROM does not support prolonged outpatient expectant management beyond 24 hours; chorioamnionitis risk rises with time and induction within 24 h reduces this risk (Hannah TermPROM; RANZCOG; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Offer immediate induction with oxytocin or expectant management to 24 hours, intrapartum GBS prophylaxis only if ROM >18 hours
options.rationale: The Hannah TermPROM trial (NEJM 1996) established that immediate IOL with oxytocin or expectant management for up to 96 hours have similar perinatal outcomes, but immediate IOL reduces chorioamnionitis and postpartum endometritis. Current Australian practice offers women either immediate IOL or expectant management with a clear plan to induce by approximately 24 hours from rupture, balancing maternal preference with infection risk. With negative GBS, intrapartum antibiotics are based on risk factors (ROM >18 h, intrapartum fever) rather than routine (Hannah ME et al NEJM 1996; RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes; NICE NG235). Per RANZCOG term PROM guidance: induction at any time after PROM is acceptable; expectant management up to 24 hours is reasonable; GBS prophylaxis triggered by ROM >18 hours or other risk factors.
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Commence empirical IV ampicillin plus gentamicin immediately and induce labour with vaginal prostaglandin gel today
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of clinical infection or risk factors (GBS unknown/positive, prolonged ROM, fever); they would be added if she developed chorioamnionitis or ROM >18 h with negative GBS (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform digital vaginal examination to assess Bishop score and plan immediate caesarean section for delivery
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination after PROM should be deferred until labour is established or induction is planned because each exam increases infection risk; PROM is not an indication for caesarean (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Commence vaginal prostaglandin gel for cervical ripening overnight regardless of her stated preference now in admission
options.rationale: Patient preference matters and should be incorporated into the plan; immediate ripening is one option but not the sole appropriate plan (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

explanation.summary: Term PROM: offer immediate IOL or expectant management up to ~24 h. The Hannah TermPROM trial showed equivalent perinatal outcomes between immediate IOL and expectant management, with lower chorioamnionitis and endometritis with immediate IOL. Avoid digital VE until necessary. Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis if GBS positive or ROM >18 h with unknown GBS, or other risk factors.
key_points: Confirm PROM: pool, ferning, IGFBP-1 or PAMG-1 if uncertain
Hannah TermPROM: immediate IOL reduces chorioamnionitis (4% vs 8.6%) and endometritis without increasing CS
Avoid digital VE until established labour or induction
GBS-negative + ROM <18 h + no fever = no antibiotics
GBS-positive = intrapartum prophylaxis as soon as labour or IOL begins
ROM >18 h with unknown GBS = treat as positive
explanation.pearls: PROM at term is a shared decision: immediate IOL versus brief expectant. Avoid digital exams; they buy infection without information.

sources.label: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

q.subtopic_detail: Term PROM - timing of induction versus expectant management

q.stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 has been pushing actively in the second stage for 110 minutes with an epidural providing adequate sensory block. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no medical comorbidities, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and her 36-week scan showed an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with an EFW of 3500 g, normal Dopplers and normal liquor. The foetal head is at +2 station, in direct OA, with no caput and no moulding on examination by the registrar. The CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 8 minutes, and the registrar wishes to expedite delivery. The bladder has been catheterised and emptied, the maternal pelvis is clinically gynaecoid, and verbal consent for instrumental delivery in the room with theatre on standby has been obtained from the woman. Adequate epidural analgesia has been confirmed with cold testing, the paediatric team has been notified and the anaesthetist is in the room. The registrar applies Neville-Barnes forceps correctly with the operator's posterior wrist supported and performs three pulls with descent of the head from +2 to +3, and then no further descent on the fourth pull despite correct application, correct axis traction, and an effective maternal push with a contraction. The foetal head has not yet crowned and the perineum is not yet stretched. The consultant is now at the bedside and reviews the scenario before deciding the next step.

data_table.Examination: Head +3 after three pulls, no further descent on fourth pull, direct OA, no caput/moulding initially.
data_table.CTG: Recurrent late decelerations, reduced variability, ongoing 8 min.
data_table.Maternal: Adequate epidural, bladder catheterised, consent obtained, theatre on standby, gynaecoid pelvis.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reapply forceps with a different cup angle and attempt two further pulls
options.rationale: Repeated forceps attempts after failure to descend with three correct pulls is associated with significant maternal and neonatal trauma and is not recommended; transition to caesarean is required (RANZCOG operative vaginal birth; RCOG Green-top 26).

options.text: Switch to a ventouse to complete delivery given the head is at +3
options.rationale: Sequential use of instruments (forceps then ventouse or vice versa) is associated with significantly increased neonatal morbidity (intracranial haemorrhage, subgaleal haemorrhage, cephalhaematoma) and should be avoided. Failed forceps mandates caesarean rather than a second instrument (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 26; ACOG).

options.text: Transfer immediately to theatre for category 1 caesarean section with senior consultant present
options.rationale: Failed forceps with pathological CTG mandates category 1 caesarean section. Sequential use of instruments is contraindicated due to combined neonatal trauma risk. The trial of instrumental should occur with theatre on standby precisely so this conversion can occur within minutes. The senior consultant should be present given the complexity (RANZCOG Instrumental Vaginal Birth 2016; RCOG Green-top 26 Operative Vaginal Birth; NICE NG235).

options.text: Ask the mother to push without the forceps for one more contraction
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological and pushing without instrument has already failed for >100 minutes; expediting delivery in theatre is required (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a symphysiotomy at the bedside to widen the pelvis for delivery
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is reserved for cases of shoulder dystocia or where caesarean is impossible (low-resource settings) and is not appropriate here (RANZCOG; WHO).

explanation.summary: Operative vaginal delivery prerequisites: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, head engaged (no abdominal palpable), station >=0 (preferably +1 or below), known position, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent, appropriate operator. Failure: no descent with three correct pulls, or non-completion after a reasonable trial. Failed forceps requires caesarean - do NOT switch instruments.
key_points: Forceps: better for OP/OT after rotation (Kielland) and where pulling is needed
Ventouse: better tolerated maternally, fewer perineal injuries, but higher failure rate, contraindicated <34 weeks and face presentation
Sequential instruments: significantly higher neonatal morbidity - avoid
Trial of instrumental in theatre when high failure risk (BMI >30, EFW >4 kg, OP/OT, mid-cavity, prolonged second stage)
Category 1 CS decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: If forceps fails, the next stop is theatre, not the ventouse. The trolley is on standby for a reason.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Operative Vaginal Birth guideline

q.subtopic_detail: Operative vaginal delivery - prerequisites and failed forceps disposition

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+5 is in spontaneous labour. Twin 1 is cephalic and growth-appropriate on the 45th centile, twin 2 has been transverse throughout the third trimester on serial scans with the back inferior and is 200 g smaller than twin 1 (35th centile, within the 20% inter-twin discordance threshold and with no growth discordance suggestive of selective FGR). She had a previous uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+1 of a singleton with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a second-degree tear. There is no previous caesarean, no uterine surgery and no medical comorbidities. Her booking BMI was 26, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and she has taken iron supplementation throughout the pregnancy with a current haemoglobin of 118 g/L. Both foetuses have reassuring CTGs on dual monitoring. The cervix is 6 cm, station of the leading twin is -1, membranes are intact for the leading twin and ARM has not been performed, and an experienced obstetric consultant with extensive twin delivery experience is available throughout the labour. The anaesthetist is in the unit and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. She has expressed a clear preference for a vaginal birth if safe. After 4 hours she is fully dilated, twin 1 head is at +3, and she pushes for 25 minutes to deliver a 2900 g cephalic infant with normal Apgars. On bedside rescan by the consultant immediately after delivery of twin 1, twin 2 remains transverse with the back inferior. The CTG of twin 2 is reassuring at this point, membranes of twin 2 are intact, and the consultant is at the bedside in the delivery suite with a theatre and a paediatric team on standby.

data_table.Antenatal: DCDA twins, twin 1 cephalic, twin 2 transverse back-inferior. EFW concordance within 20%.
data_table.After twin 1 delivery: Twin 2 remains transverse, back inferior, reassuring CTG, intact membranes.
data_table.Operator: Experienced consultant at bedside, theatre available, paediatrics on standby.
data_table.Maternal: Epidural in situ, good analgesia, haemodynamically stable.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for delivery of twin 2?

options.text: Internal podalic version followed by breech extraction of twin 2
options.rationale: With twin 2 transverse after delivery of twin 1 and an experienced operator available, internal podalic version (operator inserts a hand through the cervix, identifies a foetal foot, brings it down through the introitus) followed by breech extraction is the preferred approach in Australia and is supported by the Twin Birth Study (Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013). External cephalic version is an alternative but has lower success after delivery of the first twin and risks foetal distress and abruption. Combined vaginal-CS delivery has worse outcomes than planned CS. The reassuring CTG provides a window for intervention, and internal podalic version with breech extraction is the most reliable mode in experienced hands. CS should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or non-reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Management of Twin Pregnancy 2022; RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy).
source_refs: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)
RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy

options.text: External cephalic version under ultrasound, then expect spontaneous cephalic delivery
options.rationale: ECV after delivery of twin 1 is an option but has lower success rates than internal podalic version (~60% vs >90% in experienced hands), and any delay risks cord prolapse, abruption or cervical closure. ECV may be tried first by some operators but internal podalic version + breech extraction is more reliable when the operator is experienced (RANZCOG; Twin Birth Study).

options.text: Category 2 caesarean section for delivery of twin 2 now
options.rationale: Combined vaginal-caesarean delivery (vaginal twin 1, CS twin 2) is associated with worse outcomes for both mother and twin 2 and should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or foetal compromise; the Twin Birth Study demonstrated that planned vaginal birth in a centre with experienced operators is safe (Barrett NEJM 2013).
source_refs: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)

options.text: Await spontaneous descent of twin 2 for up to 60 minutes before any intervention
options.rationale: Twin 2 transverse will not descend spontaneously; the inter-twin interval should be as short as practical (commonly within 30 minutes) before intervention to avoid cervical closure, cord prolapse with subsequent ARM, and abruption (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform ARM of twin 2 membranes immediately to allow descent
options.rationale: ARM of twin 2 while transverse risks cord prolapse and limb presentation; membranes should be left intact until the presenting part is engaged after version or breech extraction (RANZCOG; RCOG).

explanation.summary: Twin delivery: twin 1 cephalic and twin 2 cephalic = planned vaginal birth. Twin 1 cephalic, twin 2 non-cephalic = vaginal birth is appropriate where an experienced operator is available; CS only if both non-cephalic or if specific contraindications. After delivery of twin 1 with non-cephalic twin 2: internal podalic version + breech extraction (preferred) or ECV if back-superior. ARM of twin 2 only after presenting part engaged. Inter-twin interval should be minimised.
key_points: Twin Birth Study (Barrett NEJM 2013): planned vaginal birth equivalent to planned CS for selected DCDA/MCDA at 32+0 to 38+6 with twin 1 cephalic
Twin 2 non-cephalic: internal podalic version + breech extraction is preferred
ECV alternative for transverse twin 2 with back superior
Monoamniotic twins always CS (cord entanglement risk)
Inter-twin interval typically <30 min to minimise cervical closure and abruption risk
Combined vaginal-CS delivery worse than planned CS for both
explanation.pearls: Twin 1 cephalic + experienced operator = trial of vaginal birth even if twin 2 is breech or transverse. The non-cephalic second twin is not by itself a CS indication.

sources.label: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)
RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy
NICE NG137 Twin and triplet pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Twin Pregnancy guideline

q.subtopic_detail: Twin delivery - mode of birth with second twin non-cephalic

q.stem: A 32-year-old G0 presents with 14 months of constant pelvic pain rated 5/10 at baseline and 8/10 cyclically. The pain has both cyclical features (worse in the 4 days prior to and during her period) and non-cyclical features (dull background ache present every day for the last 8 months). She reports dyspareunia on deep penetration, dyschezia during menses, and increased urinary frequency without dysuria or haematuria. She is sexually active in a stable monogamous relationship of 6 years with no STI history, condom use is inconsistent, and last STI screen was 14 months ago and negative. She has been trying to conceive for 9 months without success. There is no fever, no postmenopausal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no unexplained weight loss, no PR bleeding, and no haematuria. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy. On bimanual examination there is uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus with cervical excitation. A pelvic ultrasound 2 months ago showed a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma with ground-glass appearance and normal uterine cavity. STI screen today is pending. She works as a teacher and reports anxiety and disrupted sleep due to the pain.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC, BMI 23.
data_table.Examination: Uterosacral nodularity. Fixed retroverted uterus. Cervical excitation. No palpable adnexal mass on bimanual.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma. Normal uterine cavity. STI screen pending. Beta-hCG negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure that endometriosis is confirmed on ultrasound and commence the COCP continuously without further investigation
options.rationale: Although the clinical picture and imaging are consistent with endometriosis, fertility goals, deep endometriosis on examination (uterosacral nodularity, fixed retroversion), and pending STI screen require an integrated specialist assessment; commencing the COCP would suppress fertility in someone trying to conceive (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel IUD now and review symptoms in 6 months
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis-related pain but is contraceptive and inappropriate for a woman actively trying to conceive; fertility goals must guide therapy (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Start a GnRH agonist with add-back tibolone for 6 months and reassess symptoms
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second-line medical therapy reserved for refractory endometriosis after first-line failure; they are contraceptive and would delay fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without prior imaging or specialist multidisciplinary review at all
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is no longer required to diagnose endometriosis when imaging and clinical features are characteristic; preoperative MRI for deep disease mapping and multidisciplinary planning improves surgical outcomes and protects fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary endometriosis service for specialist assessment, MRI for deep infiltrating disease mapping, and fertility-focused management
options.rationale: Suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral nodularity, dyschezia, fixed retroversion) with an endometrioma and subfertility warrants MRI mapping and multidisciplinary specialist input combining gynaecology, fertility, pelvic pain physiotherapy and psychology; the Australian National Action Plan for Endometriosis recommends this pathway (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; National Action Plan for Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Chronic pelvic pain workup screens for red flags (postmenopausal bleeding, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, pelvic mass), addresses the bladder, bowel, musculoskeletal, neurological and psychosocial dimensions, and identifies treatable gynaecological pathology (endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID, ovarian cyst). Endometriosis often has clinical and imaging features that are diagnostic without laparoscopy. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral, bowel, bladder, ureteric involvement) requires MRI mapping and multidisciplinary surgical planning. Management is biopsychosocial: medical (NSAID, COCP, progestogen, LNG-IUS, GnRH analogues), surgical (excision/ablation, fertility-preserving), pain-focused (CBT, physiotherapy, mindfulness), and fertility-specific (IVF if needed).
key_points: CPP red flags: PMB, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, fever, mass
Endometriosis no longer requires laparoscopy for diagnosis if imaging/clinical features are characteristic
Deep endometriosis suspected: MRI mapping + multidisciplinary review
LNG-IUS, COCP, progestogens are first-line medical Rx (contraceptive)
Fertility goals dictate therapy choice; consider IVF early in deep disease
explanation.pearls: Uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus on bimanual examination are physical signs of deep infiltrating endometriosis - imaging may underestimate disease, so multidisciplinary review precedes any surgical decision.

q.subtopic_detail: Chronic pelvic pain - red flag identification and biopsychosocial workup

q.stem: A 33-year-old G3P0 presents after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. The first was a missed miscarriage at 9 weeks managed expectantly, the second was a complete miscarriage at 8 weeks managed at home, and the third was a missed miscarriage at 10+2 managed with misoprostol 4 weeks ago. She conceives easily within 1 to 2 cycles each time and has regular 28-day ovulatory cycles. She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, deep vein thrombosis, stroke, or pulmonary embolism, no symptoms of systemic lupus erythematosus (no malar rash, no arthralgia, no oral ulcers, no Raynaud), no autoimmune disease, no fertility treatment, and no live births. She has never smoked, drinks one standard drink per week, has a BMI of 27, and is otherwise well. Her partner is a 35-year-old non-smoker with no children from previous relationships. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage, balanced translocation, congenital malformation, or genetic disease, and no consanguinity. She is taking folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Examination is unremarkable. A transvaginal ultrasound shows a normal uterine cavity, both ovaries normal, and no fibroids; antral follicle count is normal. Karyotype of products of conception from the third miscarriage was 46,XX (euploid). TSH is 1.8 mIU/L and HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She asks what should be tested before her next pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, BMI 27.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: normal uterine cavity, no fibroids, both ovaries normal. POC karyotype third miscarriage 46,XX (euploid). TSH 1.8 mIU/L. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

q.lead_in: Which is the most important next investigation?

options.text: Parental peripheral blood karyotype testing as the very first single investigation
options.rationale: Parental karyotype is not routinely first-line; it is reserved for couples with a euploid first-trimester loss combined with additional features (advanced maternal age, family history) or when products show structural rearrangement. Yield of parental balanced translocation is approximately 2 to 5% (ESHRE RPL 2023; RANZCOG).

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C and S, antithrombin)
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent miscarriage outside of research settings because results do not change management of pregnancy loss; treatment with anticoagulation in inherited thrombophilia alone does not improve live birth rates (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Diagnostic hysteroscopy regardless of any prior pelvic ultrasound findings reported
options.rationale: Saline infusion sonography or hysteroscopy is reserved for cases where ultrasound is equivocal or suggests cavity abnormality; her TVUS shows a normal cavity (ESHRE).

options.text: Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibodies, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart
options.rationale: After 3 consecutive miscarriages (or 2 by RANZCOG criteria), antiphospholipid syndrome screening is the highest yield test that has direct therapeutic implications (aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH in next pregnancy if positive); diagnosis requires laboratory criteria on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart (RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage; ESHRE RPL 2023).

options.text: Peripheral blood natural killer cell assay as the first investigation
options.rationale: Peripheral or uterine NK cell testing is not validated for recurrent miscarriage and is explicitly not recommended by ESHRE and RANZCOG; it does not change management and adds unjustified expense (ESHRE RPL 2023).

explanation.summary: Recurrent pregnancy loss is defined by RANZCOG as 2 or more consecutive miscarriages and by some international bodies as 3 or more. Workup with evidence-based yield includes APS screen (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2-GP-I on two occasions 12 weeks apart), thyroid function and TPO antibodies, HbA1c, parental karyotype if products show structural abnormality, and pelvic imaging to exclude uterine anomaly. APS is the only treatable cause with clear therapeutic benefit: low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live birth rates.
key_points: RANZCOG RPL definition: >=2 consecutive miscarriages
APS labs: LA, aCL, anti-beta-2-GP-I, repeated 12 weeks apart
APS treatment: aspirin 100 mg + prophylactic LMWH in pregnancy
Thyroid (TSH, TPO) and HbA1c also worth screening
Inherited thrombophilia and NK cell tests are NOT recommended
explanation.pearls: Half of all recurrent miscarriages remain unexplained even after thorough workup; the most important counselling point is that an unexplained workup still predicts a 60 to 75% chance of subsequent live birth.

q.subtopic_detail: Recurrent pregnancy loss - antiphospholipid syndrome workup

q.stem: A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, no pelvic surgery, no PID, no STI history, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative). BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, no varicocele symptoms, no testicular trauma, no mumps orchitis, no inguinal surgery, no cryptorchidism, no chronic illness, no anabolic steroid use, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. There is no family history of infertility, premature menopause, or genetic disease on either side. On her examination there are no abnormal findings; bimanual is normal with no nodularity. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic examination.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screen HPV negative 6 months ago. Beta-hCG negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.text: Semen analysis, day-21 progesterone, TSH and transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after initial workup
options.rationale: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35 (6 months if 35 or over). First-line investigations are semen analysis (assesses 40 to 50% of contributory causes), confirmation of ovulation (mid-luteal progesterone), thyroid function, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HSG or HyCoSy) is performed after initial workup if no abnormality is found (RANZCOG Infertility; NICE CG156).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation of the couple
options.rationale: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation of the couple
options.rationale: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces her chances of timely treatment (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Hysterosalpingogram and diagnostic laparoscopy as the first-line investigations of the couple
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment is part of the workup but follows initial investigations (semen analysis, ovulation confirmation, ultrasound); laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure her she can wait given young age
options.rationale: AMH is part of the broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35, or 6 months at age 35 or older, or earlier if there is a known cause. Initial workup is parallel investigation of both partners: semen analysis (according to WHO criteria), ovulation assessment (mid-luteal progesterone, cycle history, ultrasound follicle tracking), ovarian reserve markers (AMH, antral follicle count, FSH on day 2 to 5), thyroid, prolactin, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HyCoSy or HSG) follows. Laparoscopy is reserved for suspected endometriosis or adhesive disease.
key_points: <35: investigate after 12 months; >=35: investigate after 6 months
Male factor accounts for 40 to 50% - always do semen analysis
Mid-luteal (day 21 in 28-day cycle) progesterone >25 nmol/L = ovulation
Tubal patency: HyCoSy preferred (no radiation) or HSG
AMH for ovarian reserve, not as a predictor of spontaneous conception
explanation.pearls: The most common omission in infertility workup is the semen analysis - both partners must be investigated in parallel from the outset.

q.subtopic_detail: Infertility workup in a couple under 35

q.stem: A 15-year-old presents with her mother because of worsening period pain and bleeding. Menarche was at 12; cycles are regular at 28 days but have become increasingly painful since age 14, now causing her to miss 3 to 5 school days per month. Pain begins 24 hours before bleeding, peaks on day 2, and is associated with nausea, vomiting, and inability to get out of bed. She soaks through a fully saturated super pad every 90 minutes on days 1 and 2 and reports passing 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She has tried paracetamol 1 g qid and ibuprofen 400 mg tds with only partial relief. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy at age 22 and a paternal aunt has known endometriosis. There is no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no dyschezia, no haematuria, no fever, and no postcoital bleeding. She has no chronic disease. She is no longer attending netball training and has begun to refuse social outings and sleepovers during her periods, and her mother reports she has been increasingly tearful and withdrawn in the days leading up to bleeding. On abdominal examination there is mild suprapubic tenderness; she declines an internal examination. A pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 22.
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness. Declines internal examination.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. FBC: Hb 116 g/L, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 18 microg/L.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg bd (day -1 to 3), continuous COCP and pelvic ultrasound; refer if no improvement
options.rationale: Adolescent dysmenorrhoea with severe functional impact, heavy bleeding, family history of endometriosis, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia warrants empirical scheduled NSAID plus continuous (cycle-suppressing) COCP, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal not appropriate in a non-sexually-active adolescent), and specialist referral if no improvement at 3 months; diagnosis does not require laparoscopy in adolescents (RANZCOG Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; ESHRE).

options.text: Reassure her that severe period pain in adolescence is normal and continue her current analgesia regimen long-term
options.rationale: Missing 3 to 5 school days per month, social withdrawal, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia is not normal and reflects a high probability of endometriosis or other secondary cause; dismissing severe dysmenorrhoea delays diagnosis by an average of 7 to 12 years (Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without trialling any medical management as the first step
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is not first-line in adolescents; empirical medical therapy is trialled first and laparoscopy reserved for those who fail medical management or have suspected deep disease (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence a GnRH agonist with hormone add-back therapy as the next immediate step in management
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory endometriosis after failure of first-line hormonal therapy; long-term use in adolescents carries bone density concerns and is not first-line (ESHRE).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system without trialling any prior medical therapy first
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for adolescent endometriosis and heavy menstrual bleeding but is generally a second-line option after COCP trial in a non-sexually-active adolescent; insertion may require general anaesthetic (Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Adolescent endometriosis is under-recognised; diagnostic delay averages 7 to 12 years. School avoidance, heavy menstrual bleeding, severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to first-line analgesia, and a family history are red flags. Empirical management combines scheduled NSAID, continuous (suppressive) COCP to eliminate menstruation, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound, and early specialist referral. Transvaginal ultrasound is generally not appropriate in non-sexually-active adolescents. LNG-IUS is a second-line option and may require GA for insertion. GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory disease and require add-back therapy. Laparoscopy is not required to diagnose endometriosis in adolescents when the clinical picture is consistent.
key_points: Diagnostic delay in endometriosis averages 7 to 12 years
Adolescent red flags: school absence, family hx, refractory to first-line
Empirical Rx: scheduled NSAID + continuous COCP; TA ultrasound
TVUS inappropriate in non-sexually-active adolescent
LNG-IUS effective but second-line in adolescents; consider GA for insertion
explanation.pearls: If a 15-year-old is missing school every month for period pain, do not normalise it - empirical treatment plus follow-up is the standard of care, and the family history doubles the prior probability of endometriosis.

q.subtopic_detail: Adolescent endometriosis - high index of suspicion and empirical management

q.stem: A 14-year-old presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of sudden severe left iliac fossa pain. The pain began abruptly while she was playing netball, is sharp, constant, radiates to the left flank, and has been associated with vomiting four times in the last 2 hours. She had two previous brief episodes of less severe left-sided pain in the last 6 months, each lasting 30 minutes and resolving spontaneously. Menarche was at 13; her last menstrual period was 12 days ago and was normal. She has never been sexually active. There is no fever, no urinary symptoms, no diarrhoea, no PR bleeding, no recent trauma, and no chronic illness. On examination she is in obvious distress, BP 110/68, HR 116, T 37.0, with marked left iliac fossa tenderness and guarding, and a tender palpable mass. Abdomen is otherwise soft. Beta-hCG is negative. White cell count is 13.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L. A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound with Doppler shows a 7 cm complex left adnexal mass with peripherally displaced follicles, a hyperechoic whirlpool sign at the suspected pedicle, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow within the left ovary. The right ovary is normal. There is no free fluid. She has fasted for 5 hours, has IV access in the right antecubital fossa, and morphine 5 mg IV has been given with partial relief. Her parents have given consent for emergency anaesthesia and laparoscopy. The gynaecology registrar and on-call anaesthetist have reviewed her and the operating theatre is available within 30 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 37.0 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Marked left iliac fossa tenderness with guarding. Tender left adnexal mass palpable.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. WCC 13.4 x 10^9/L. CRP 18 mg/L. TVUS Doppler: 7 cm complex left adnexal mass, peripherally displaced follicles, whirlpool sign, absent venous flow, reduced arterial flow. Right ovary normal. No free fluid.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia and repeat the ultrasound in 12 hours
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; every hour of delay increases ovarian necrosis and reduces salvage rate. Observation while imaging declares itself is harmful (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to confirm diagnosis before any surgical decision is made
options.rationale: MRI adds delay without improving on Doppler ultrasound findings that are already characteristic; the diagnosis is a clinical and ultrasound diagnosis warranting immediate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy for detorsion and ovarian preservation, with oophoropexy considered intraoperatively
options.rationale: Whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles and absent venous flow on Doppler in an adolescent with characteristic clinical features is ovarian torsion. The standard of care is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion regardless of macroscopic appearance (ovaries that look black often recover function); oophorectomy is reserved for necrotic non-viable tissue or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence (RANZCOG Ovarian Cysts; ESHRE; Royal Women's Hospital Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy with planned left salpingo-oophorectomy because the ovary appears non-viable on Doppler
options.rationale: Doppler signs do not reliably predict viability; even ovaries that appear black at surgery have a high rate of functional recovery after detorsion. Oophorectomy in an adolescent should be reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and admit for suspected pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: She has never been sexually active and the imaging is diagnostic of torsion, not tubo-ovarian abscess; antibiotics do not treat torsion and delay surgery (Australian STI Management Guidelines; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Clinical features are sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, often with vomiting, intermittent prior episodes, and a palpable adnexal mass. Doppler ultrasound findings of whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow support the diagnosis, but normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual ovarian blood supply can preserve arterial flow). Management is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian preservation; oophorectomy is reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence, particularly when no ovarian mass is found or in cases with redundant ligaments. Outcomes correlate with time to surgery.
key_points: Sudden severe unilateral pain + vomiting + adnexal mass = torsion until excluded
Doppler whirlpool sign, peripheral follicles, absent venous flow = highly suggestive
Normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual blood supply)
Detorsion + ovarian preservation is the standard, even if the ovary looks ischaemic
Oophoropexy reduces recurrence, especially when no mass is present
explanation.pearls: Black does not equal dead - detorsed ischaemic-looking ovaries regularly recover function in adolescents, and the decision to remove an ovary in a 14-year-old should be reserved for clear necrosis or proven malignancy.

q.subtopic_detail: Ovarian torsion in an adolescent - urgent surgical management

q.stem: A 29-year-old G0 woman with PCOS diagnosed 18 months ago (Rotterdam criteria: oligomenorrhoea, biochemical hyperandrogenism with elevated free androgen index, polycystic ovarian morphology with 26 follicles per ovary) has been trying to conceive for 12 months with intercourse 2 to 3 times per week. Her partner has a normal semen analysis on two occasions. She has bilateral patent tubes on HyCoSy. AMH 38 pmol/L, AFC 28, normal TSH and prolactin. She has lost 6 kg over 6 months with structured lifestyle intervention (current BMI 28, down from 31), and her cycles remain oligomenorrhoeic (45 to 70 days). HbA1c 5.3% and OGTT normal. She has no contraindications to ovulation induction agents and is keen to commence pharmacological treatment. She is taking folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Pelvic ultrasound today shows polycystic-appearing ovaries with no cysts or other pathology. She has no other comorbidities. She asks which agent is recommended as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS. She has no other endocrine or metabolic comorbidity, no contraindication to letrozole or clomiphene, and she has had a pre-treatment FSH and oestradiol confirmed within normal range.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, BMI 28 (down from 31)
data_table.Examination: Mild hirsutism, no acanthosis nigricans, no virilisation
data_table.Investigations: AMH 38 pmol/L, AFC 28, TSH 1.6, prolactin normal, HbA1c 5.3%, OGTT normal; HyCoSy bilateral patency; partner normal semen analysis

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction in this patient?

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6, titrated upward if anovulatory
options.rationale: The 2023 international PCOS guideline and ASRM both recommend letrozole as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS for anovulatory infertility; letrozole achieves higher ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than clomiphene with lower multiple pregnancy risk (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6 with serial follicular tracking
options.rationale: Clomiphene was historically first-line but the PPCOS II trial and subsequent meta-analyses show letrozole achieves higher live birth rates with lower multiple pregnancy risk; clomiphene is now considered second-line or used if letrozole is unavailable (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated to 1 g twice daily as sole agent for ovulation induction
options.rationale: Metformin as monotherapy has lower ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than letrozole; it may be added to letrozole or clomiphene as adjunct (particularly with BMI >25 or insulin resistance) but is not first-line monotherapy for ovulation induction (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Recombinant FSH gonadotrophin injection from cycle day 2 with daily follicular ultrasound monitoring
options.rationale: Injectable gonadotrophins are second-line if oral ovulation induction fails; they carry higher cost, OHSS risk and multiple-pregnancy risk, and require intensive monitoring (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling under general anaesthesia as a first-line surgical option for her
options.rationale: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is a second-line option for women with clomiphene/letrozole resistance, particularly when other indications for laparoscopy exist; it is not first-line for treatment-naive PCOS (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS, achieving higher live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy rates than clomiphene (PPCOS II trial). Metformin is an adjunct (particularly with BMI >25 or impaired glucose tolerance). Gonadotrophin injection and laparoscopic ovarian drilling are second-line (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM; RANZCOG).
key_points: Letrozole: 2.5 mg daily day 2-6; titrate to 5 mg then 7.5 mg if anovulatory
Monitor first cycle with ovulation tracking (LH urine or follicular USS)
Lifestyle intervention foundational - aim for 5 to 10% weight reduction in BMI >25
Refer to fertility specialist if anovulatory after 3 to 6 ovulatory cycles or age >=35
explanation.pearls: Letrozole is teratogenic if taken during early pregnancy; use only in early follicular phase and confirm non-pregnancy.

q.subtopic_detail: PCOS - ovulation induction in fertility-seeking woman

q.stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to endocrinology with 14 months of secondary amenorrhoea, bilateral galactorrhoea, reduced libido and a 4-month history of headaches behind her eyes worse on waking. Two months ago she noticed difficulty seeing the side of her vision when driving. On examination today she has bitemporal hemianopia confirmed on confrontation and Humphrey perimetry. Prolactin is 9800 mIU/L (confirmed on a repeat fasting morning sample), TSH and free T4 are normal, beta-hCG negative, renal function normal and she takes no dopamine-blocking medications, antipsychotics, antidepressants or opioids. MRI pituitary demonstrates a 14 mm pituitary mass with suprasellar extension contacting the optic chiasm with no cavernous sinus invasion. Other anterior pituitary hormones (ACTH/cortisol axis, IGF-1, TSH/T4, LH/FSH) are otherwise within reference range. She has no contraindications to dopamine agonist therapy, no history of impulse control disorder and no cardiac valve disease. She is keen to preserve future fertility. She is highly motivated to preserve fertility, has a partner and is keen to avoid surgery if possible. She has no symptoms of pituitary apoplexy (no thunderclap headache, no collapse, no cranial nerve palsy) and her visual loss has been gradual over months rather than days.

data_table.Examination: Bitemporal hemianopia on confrontation and Humphrey; no other focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 9800 mIU/L (repeat fasting morning sample), TSH/T4 normal, beta-hCG negative, renal function normal; pituitary MRI: 14 mm mass with suprasellar extension contacting optic chiasm; other anterior pituitary axes intact

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly initially with close ophthalmology and endocrine follow-up
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred over bromocriptine for efficacy and tolerability) are first-line for macroprolactinomas including those with visual field deficits; tumour shrinkage and visual recovery occur in the majority within weeks to months. Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or apoplexy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011; eTG: Endocrinology).

options.text: Urgent transsphenoidal surgical resection of the pituitary mass within 48 hours
options.rationale: Transsphenoidal surgery is reserved for failed medical therapy, dopamine agonist intolerance, pituitary apoplexy or rapidly progressive visual loss not responsive to dopamine agonist; first-line for prolactinoma even with chiasmal compression is medical therapy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: External beam radiotherapy to the pituitary fossa as primary management
options.rationale: Radiotherapy is third-line, used after failed surgery or for residual aggressive disease; it has slow effect and risks hypopituitarism, so it is not first-line for a treatment-naive prolactinoma (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Observation with serial MRI and visual fields, deferring treatment until prolactin >20,000 mIU/L
options.rationale: Observation is inappropriate in the presence of a macroadenoma with visual field deficit; untreated chiasmal compression risks permanent visual loss, and dopamine agonist therapy is highly effective (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Cyclical combined oral contraceptive pill alone to restore menses and protect bone density
options.rationale: COCP treats secondary amenorrhoea symptomatically but does not address the tumour or visual field compromise; it can mask prolactin response monitoring and is not first-line (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

explanation.summary: Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) are first-line for prolactinomas, including macroprolactinomas with chiasmal compression; the majority show tumour shrinkage and visual recovery within weeks to months. Transsphenoidal surgery is reserved for medical failure, intolerance, apoplexy or progressive visual loss despite treatment (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011; eTG: Endocrinology).
key_points: Cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly, titrate to prolactin response; monitor for impulse control disorder and (high cumulative dose) cardiac valve effects
Repeat MRI at 3 to 6 months to assess shrinkage; visual fields at similar interval
Pre-pregnancy planning: bromocriptine has more safety data than cabergoline in early pregnancy
Pituitary apoplexy = emergency: glucocorticoid replacement + neurosurgical referral
explanation.pearls: Don't add an SSRI or antipsychotic to a patient with new amenorrhoea/galactorrhoea without first checking prolactin and considering prolactinoma.

q.subtopic_detail: Hyperprolactinaemia - macroprolactinoma management

q.stem: A 28-year-old G0 woman with PCOS (AMH 48 pmol/L pre-stimulation) presents to the emergency department 9 days after oocyte retrieval (28 oocytes collected, fresh embryo transferred). She reports 3 days of progressive abdominal distension, breathlessness on minimal exertion and reduced urine output. On examination she is tachycardic (HR 112), BP 102/64, RR 24, SpO2 94% on room air, T 36.9. Abdomen is markedly distended with tense ascites and reduced bowel sounds; she has bilateral basal crackles on chest auscultation. Bloods: haematocrit 0.51, WCC 22 x10^9/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L (baseline 60), urea 9.5 mmol/L, albumin 26 g/L, ALT 75 U/L, AST 68 U/L. CXR shows small bilateral pleural effusions. Pelvic ultrasound shows ovaries 12 cm bilaterally with large volume ascites extending above the umbilicus. Urine output has been 18 mL/hour over the last 4 hours. She has no chest pain or calf swelling but D-dimer is markedly elevated. She has no abdominal peritonism on examination and a bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes. She has been counselled that severe OHSS is a recognised complication of high-response IVF.

data_table.Examination: Tense ascites, bilateral basal crackles, distended abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Hct 0.51, WCC 22, Na 131, K 5.0, Cr 124 (baseline 60), urea 9.5, Alb 26, ALT 75; CXR bilateral small effusions; USS ovaries 12 cm bilaterally, ascites above umbilicus; urine output 18 mL/hour; D-dimer elevated

q.lead_in: Which combination of interventions is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Admit; commence IV crystalloid with albumin, prophylactic enoxaparin, fluid balance and urgent paracentesis
options.rationale: Severe/critical OHSS with haemoconcentration (Hct >0.50), WCC >20, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise requires admission, IV crystalloid (with albumin in severe hypoalbuminaemia), VTE prophylaxis (high independent VTE risk in OHSS), strict fluid balance and paracentesis to relieve tense ascites/respiratory compromise/renal dysfunction (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Discharge home with oral fluids, simple analgesia and outpatient review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe OHSS with haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise mandates admission and IV therapy; outpatient management is inappropriate at this severity (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous frusemide infusion to address oliguria and reduce the ovarian volume
options.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in OHSS until intravascular volume is restored; they worsen haemoconcentration and renal dysfunction in a third-space sequestration state (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bilateral oophorectomy to remove the hyperstimulated ovaries definitively
options.rationale: Surgery in OHSS is reserved for complications such as ovarian torsion, rupture with haemorrhage or ectopic pregnancy; hyperstimulated ovaries are friable and surgery risks bleeding and oophorectomy (RANZCOG OHSS).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and antiviral therapy pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Elevated WCC in OHSS is part of the inflammatory state; empirical antibiotics are not indicated unless infection is clinically suspected. Antibiotic stewardship and source-directed treatment apply (RANZCOG OHSS).

explanation.summary: Severe/critical OHSS with haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise requires admission, judicious IV crystalloid (albumin if albumin <30 g/L), VTE prophylaxis (OHSS confers independent high VTE risk), strict fluid balance and paracentesis for tense ascites or respiratory/renal compromise. Diuretics are contraindicated until intravascular volume is restored (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).
key_points: Critical OHSS thresholds: Hct >0.55, WCC >25, tense ascites, ARDS, VTE, hepatorenal dysfunction, anuria
Severe OHSS thresholds: Hct >0.45, WCC >15, clinical ascites, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia <35 g/L
VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin throughout admission and at least until clinical resolution; longer if pregnancy ensues
Continued worsening if pregnancy occurs (HCG-driven) - persistent symptoms warrant beta-hCG check
explanation.pearls: OHSS is one of the few conditions where IV albumin is routinely used; diuretics are explicitly contraindicated until intravascular volume is restored.

q.subtopic_detail: Severe OHSS - inpatient management priorities

q.stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She was diagnosed 14 months ago with stage II oestrogen-receptor-positive, progesterone-receptor-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast. She had wide local excision plus axillary node sampling (1 of 3 nodes positive), adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and commenced tamoxifen 20 mg daily 6 months ago which she plans to continue for at least 5 to 10 years. She has been amenorrhoeic since chemotherapy but recent bloods show a partial return of menses and an FSH of 18 IU/L with oestradiol 280 pmol/L, suggesting recovery of ovarian function. She uses condoms intermittently with her long-term male partner. She has completed her family. She is anxious to avoid pregnancy because she does not wish to interrupt her endocrine therapy and is aware of the teratogenicity of tamoxifen. She has no other major comorbidity. BMI 24, BP 118/74. The breast oncology team has documented that she should avoid any exogenous oestrogen for the duration of tamoxifen and ideally for any further endocrine therapy. She has no history of menorrhagia, no current irregular bleeding and no contraindication to a copper intrauterine device.

data_table.Investigations: FSH 18 IU/L, E2 280 pmol/L (recovering ovarian function); on tamoxifen 20 mg daily

q.lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) inserted at the next available family planning visit
options.rationale: Current and past breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for ALL hormonal contraception (combined and progestogen-only). The copper IUD is UKMEC 1 and is the recommended highly effective reversible option in a woman with breast cancer who has completed her family; female sterilisation is also acceptable (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the LNG-IUS; past breast cancer (>5 years remission) is UKMEC 3. Despite local action, systemic progestogen exposure means the LNG-IUS is NOT recommended in current or recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at the lowest effective oestrogen dose available
options.rationale: Current or recent breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to the oestrogen-receptor-positive nature of the disease and the high recurrence risk; the COCP is absolutely contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
options.rationale: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the progestogen-only implant; all hormonal methods are contraindicated and progestogen-only methods do not avoid the UKMEC 4 rating in current or recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 4 in current breast cancer; all hormonal methods are contraindicated. The copper IUD is the recommended highly effective reversible option (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for ALL hormonal contraception (combined and progestogen-only, including LNG-IUS). The copper IUD is UKMEC 1 and is the recommended highly effective reversible option. Female sterilisation is also acceptable (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).
key_points: Current breast cancer (any subtype, any time on treatment): UKMEC 4 for all hormonal methods
Past breast cancer (>5 years remission): UKMEC 3 for hormonal methods
Copper IUD: UKMEC 1 in current and past breast cancer
Tamoxifen is teratogenic - contraception is mandatory throughout treatment and for 2 months after
