explanation.pearls: Don't be reassured by the 'local action' of the LNG-IUS in current breast cancer - it is still UKMEC 4.

q.subtopic_detail: Contraception - current breast cancer (UKMEC 4)

q.stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed 6 years ago) attends for contraceptive review. Her disease has been clinically and serologically stable for 18 months on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone (5 mg daily). She has previously had two episodes of cutaneous lupus and one episode of mild lupus nephritis treated with mycophenolate, now in remission. Her most recent immunology shows positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I positive (triple positive antiphospholipid profile). She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, stroke or ischaemic heart disease and no obstetric morbidity but is high risk for VTE. BP 120/76, BMI 25, non-smoker. She has completed her family, uses condoms inconsistently and would like a more reliable method. She is concerned about both medication interactions with hydroxychloroquine and the impact of contraception on her lupus and VTE risk. Her rheumatology team has documented that combined oestrogen-containing contraception is absolutely contraindicated in the setting of triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome. She has no contraindication to an intrauterine system and has had a normal recent cervical screen and pelvic examination.

data_table.Examination: No active rash, no active arthritis
data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant positive x2 (12 weeks apart), anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, anti-beta-2-GPI positive; complement and dsDNA stable; renal function normal

q.lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill with the lowest available oestrogen dose to minimise VTE risk
options.rationale: SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies (or unknown status) is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to substantially elevated VTE and arterial thrombosis risk; the COCP is absolutely contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: In SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies, the LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 to 2: highly effective, no systemic oestrogen, minimal interaction with hydroxychloroquine, low VTE risk, and acceptable in a woman who has completed her family. The Cu-IUD (UKMEC 1) is also acceptable but the LNG-IUS additionally reduces menstrual blood loss (relevant if anticoagulation is initiated later) (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraceptive vaginal ring as it avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism
options.rationale: All combined hormonal methods (pill, patch, ring) share the same UKMEC 4 rating in SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies; route does not modify the thrombotic risk (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks for ongoing contraception
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 3 in SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies because of its adverse lipid and small but increased VTE association; an IUD (LNG or Cu) is preferred (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Continue barrier contraception alone (condoms) given the complexity of medication interactions
options.rationale: Condoms have high typical-use failure rates (~13% per year) and are inadequate for a woman who has completed her family and seeks reliable contraception; effective LARC is available and appropriate (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

explanation.summary: SLE with positive (or unknown) antiphospholipid antibodies is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception. The LNG-IUS (UKMEC 1-2) and copper IUD (UKMEC 1) are first-line LARC options. DMPA is UKMEC 3 due to lipid and small VTE association. Progestogen-only pill and implant are UKMEC 3 (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).
key_points: Triple-positive antiphospholipid profile carries highest thrombosis risk
Stable SLE without antiphospholipid antibodies: COCP UKMEC 2
Pregnancy planning in antiphospholipid syndrome requires multidisciplinary management with aspirin +/- LMWH
Hydroxychloroquine is safe in pregnancy and reduces lupus flares and obstetric APS complications
explanation.pearls: Always check antiphospholipid antibody status before prescribing combined hormonal contraception in any woman with SLE.

q.subtopic_detail: Contraception - SLE with antiphospholipid antibodies

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends for contraceptive review 7 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. She has lost 28 kg (BMI now 32, down from 42 pre-operatively). She has not yet resumed menses since surgery and reports breast tenderness, fatigue and intermittent nausea, which she attributes to her vitamin and mineral supplementation. A urine beta-hCG today is negative. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily (BP today 128/78) and previous gestational diabetes (no current diabetes; HbA1c 5.5%). She uses condoms inconsistently with her long-term male partner. Pre-operatively she was on the combined oral contraceptive pill which was stopped 6 weeks before surgery as part of the VTE risk minimisation protocol. She is fully aware that bariatric surgery is associated with rapid return of fertility and that pregnancy is recommended to be deferred for 12 to 24 months post-operatively. She wishes to use a highly effective method until she is ready for a third pregnancy in 18 months. She has been advised that bariatric surgery, particularly Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, results in malabsorption of oral hormonal contraception and reduces the reliability of the combined oral contraceptive pill and the progestogen-only pill. The bariatric service has also recommended deferring pregnancy for 12 to 24 months to allow weight stabilisation, to minimise rapid weight regain and to permit safe titration of micronutrient supplementation including thiamine, B12 and iron prior to conception.

data_table.Examination: Surgical scars healed; otherwise unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative; HbA1c 5.5%; no anaemia

q.lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena), copper IUD or etonogestrel subdermal implant
options.rationale: After malabsorptive bariatric procedures (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion), oral hormonal contraceptive absorption is unreliable; LARCs that bypass the gut (LNG-IUS, Cu-IUD, etonogestrel implant) are preferred. The COCP also carries elevated VTE risk in the post-operative period and may be UKMEC 3 to 4 with persistent obesity and other risk factors; pregnancy should be delayed 12 to 24 months post-bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at standard dose with continued condom backup
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass impairs absorption of oral hormonal contraception, reducing efficacy; FSRH advises against COCP and POP after malabsorptive bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill taken daily with a 3-hour mistiming window
options.rationale: Like the COCP, the POP has reduced and variable absorption after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; non-oral methods are preferred (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Fertility awareness method timed to predicted ovulation each cycle
options.rationale: Fertility awareness has high typical-use failure rates (~24% per year) and is particularly unreliable in the post-bariatric period when menses are returning irregularly; it does not meet the brief for highly effective contraception (Therapeutic Guidelines: Contraception).

options.text: Recommend immediate pregnancy attempts given strong weight loss and improving metabolic profile
options.rationale: Current expert consensus (ASMBS/ACOG, RANZCOG) recommends avoiding pregnancy for 12 to 24 months after bariatric surgery to allow weight stabilisation, optimise nutrition (B12, iron, folate, vitamin D) and reduce the risk of SGA, prematurity and micronutrient deficiency in the foetus (ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: After malabsorptive bariatric procedures (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion), oral hormonal contraception has reduced and unreliable absorption. LARCs that bypass the gut (LNG-IUS, Cu-IUD, etonogestrel implant, DMPA injection) are preferred. Restrictive procedures (sleeve gastrectomy, gastric band) do not impair absorption to the same degree. Pregnancy should be delayed 12 to 24 months post-bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).
key_points: Malabsorptive procedures (RYGB, BPD): avoid oral hormonal contraception
Restrictive procedures (sleeve, band): oral methods acceptable if absorbed
Rapid return of fertility post-weight loss; high unintended pregnancy risk
Multidisciplinary pre-conception planning: micronutrient repletion, optimised glycaemia, mental health
explanation.pearls: After Roux-en-Y, treat oral hormonal contraception as you would an enzyme-inducing medication - assume unreliable absorption.

q.subtopic_detail: Contraception after bariatric surgery

q.stem: A 5-year-old previously well boy is brought to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department because of right hip pain and refusal to weight-bear for the past 18 hours. His mother reports a coryzal illness that resolved 10 days ago and no history of trauma. Immunisations are up to date and he is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. On arrival he is alert, holds his right hip in slight flexion, abduction and external rotation, and cries with passive internal rotation. He has no rash, no other joint involvement and no overlying erythema or warmth at the hip. Capillary refill is 2 seconds and peripheral pulses are normal. He was given paracetamol 2 hours ago at triage. The ED registrar performs an ultrasound which demonstrates a moderate right hip effusion. Bloods and a plain pelvic radiograph have been arranged. The orthopaedic registrar asks for risk stratification using the Kocher criteria before deciding on theatre versus observation. The team specifically asks how to apply the Kocher criteria today, whether MRI should be obtained before any intervention, whether oral antibiotics with outpatient orthopaedic review would be acceptable, and whether observation with serial CRP at 12 hours would be safer than immediate aspiration. The on-call orthopaedic registrar is in theatre and can review within 30 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 deg C, HR 128 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 102/64 mmHg, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Holds right hip in flexion / abduction / external rotation, painful log-roll, no overlying erythema, refuses to bear weight even with paracetamol on board
data_table.Investigations: WCC 13.8 x10^9/L (neutrophils 9.6), CRP 38 mg/L, ESR 52 mm/h, blood culture pending, pelvic XR no fracture or SUFE, hip US moderate effusion

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and ibuprofen for simple analgesia and arrange GP review in 48 hours for likely post-viral transient synovitis with planned reassessment
options.rationale: This child meets all four Kocher criteria (fever >38.5 deg C, refusal to bear weight, ESR >40 mm/h, WCC >12 x10^9/L), giving a predicted septic arthritis probability of approximately 99 percent; discharge without joint aspiration is unsafe per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

options.text: Admit for observation and serial examinations with maintenance IV fluids, withholding empirical antibiotics until a repeat CRP and full blood count at 12 hours have been reviewed
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics and aspiration in a 4 of 4 Kocher patient risks irreversible cartilage and femoral head damage; time to washout correlates with outcome, per RCH CPG: Acute limp and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

options.text: Commence empirical oral flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly and arrange outpatient orthopaedic clinic review the following week with safety-net advice for parents
options.rationale: Oral therapy and outpatient follow-up are inappropriate for high-probability septic arthritis of the hip; IV therapy after aspiration is mandated and oral switch is only considered once cultures and clinical course are reassuring, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

options.text: Arrange urgent ultrasound-guided hip aspiration in theatre, send synovial fluid for cell count and culture, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin after sampling
options.rationale: With four Kocher criteria positive and a sonographic effusion, the predicted probability of septic arthritis is approximately 99 percent; urgent aspiration (often combined with arthrotomy and washout) confirms the diagnosis and decompresses the joint, with empirical IV flucloxacillin started immediately after sampling to cover Staphylococcus aureus, per RCH CPG: Acute limp and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

options.text: Order an urgent contrast MRI of the hip and pelvis and defer joint aspiration and antibiotics until imaging has formally excluded coexisting osteomyelitis or pelvic abscess
options.rationale: MRI has a role where osteomyelitis is suspected after equivocal initial assessment, but it should not delay aspiration and antibiotics in a 4 of 4 Kocher patient with a confirmed effusion, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

explanation.summary: The Kocher criteria (fever >38.5, non-weight-bearing, ESR >40, WCC >12) stratify septic arthritis risk in a child with an acutely irritable hip; 4 of 4 predicts approximately 99 percent probability. Definitive management is urgent aspiration / washout plus empirical IV antistaphylococcal therapy, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.
key_points: Kocher 0/4 ~ 2 percent, 1/4 ~ 3 percent, 2/4 ~ 40 percent, 3/4 ~ 93 percent, 4/4 ~ 99 percent
Empirical flucloxacillin covers Staphylococcus aureus, the commonest pathogen
Kingella kingae cover (e.g. cefazolin) considered in under 4 year olds in some centres
Antibiotics after sampling unless the child is septic and aspiration is delayed
explanation.pearls: Transient synovitis classically follows a viral URTI by 1-2 weeks but the child is afebrile and will mobilise with analgesia; fever plus refusal to weight-bear despite analgesia is a major red flag (Source: RCH CPG: Acute limp).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

q.subtopic_detail: Paediatric orthopaedics - septic arthritis vs transient synovitis

q.stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with a 5-day history of left ear pain initially diagnosed as AOM by the GP and treated with oral amoxicillin. Over the past 24 hours her mother reports new posterior auricular swelling, displacement of the pinna and worsening fever despite the antibiotics. There is no history of recent travel and immunisations are up to date. On examination the left auricle is protruding outwards and downwards. There is a fluctuant, erythematous, tender swelling behind the left ear that obliterates the post-auricular sulcus. Otoscopy shows a bulging, erythematous left tympanic membrane with pus visible behind it; the right ear is normal. There is no facial nerve palsy, no neck stiffness, no photophobia and no neurological deficit. She is alert and interactive but in pain. The ED registrar asks whether change to oral co-amoxiclav with 48-hour review would be safe, whether IV flucloxacillin alone provides adequate cover, whether immediate lumbar puncture is required given the fever, and whether MRI brain should precede antibiotics to exclude sigmoid sinus thrombosis before any intervention is commenced. Imaging is available immediately.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 152 bpm, RR 28 /min, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 98 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Left auricle protruding outwards and downwards, fluctuant tender postauricular erythematous swelling obliterating sulcus, bulging erythematous left TM with pus, GCS 15, no neck stiffness, no facial nerve palsy
data_table.Investigations: WCC 21.2 x10^9/L (neutrophils 16.4), CRP 162 mg/L, blood culture pending

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Change current oral amoxicillin to oral amoxicillin/clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days and arrange GP review in 48 hours to assess for improvement
options.rationale: This is acute mastoiditis with a fluctuant subperiosteal abscess; oral therapy is inadequate, urgent IV antibiotics, imaging and ENT surgical input are mandatory, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

options.text: Admit, commence intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly as monotherapy and arrange routine outpatient ENT clinic review within 1 week of discharge home
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin does not adequately cover Streptococcus pneumoniae and other AOM pathogens that drive mastoiditis; broader-spectrum cover and urgent (not routine) ENT input is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Admit, commence IV ceftriaxone, request urgent CT temporal bones with contrast and urgent ENT review for consideration of cortical mastoidectomy and myringotomy
options.rationale: Acute mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess requires admission, IV broad-spectrum cover (e.g. ceftriaxone, with metronidazole added if intracranial extension), contrast CT temporal bones to define abscess and exclude intracranial complications, and urgent ENT for cortical mastoidectomy +/- myringotomy +/- grommet, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture in ED for cell count, protein, glucose and culture and commence empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial meningitis complicating mastoiditis
options.rationale: There are no signs of meningitis (no neck stiffness, photophobia or altered conscious state); LP is not required, although intracranial complications must be excluded by CT and the patient examined neurologically, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

options.text: Arrange urgent MRI brain with MR venography to exclude lateral sinus thrombosis and intracranial abscess before commencing any intravenous antibiotic therapy or surgical input
options.rationale: Imaging is required, but it must not delay IV antibiotics and ENT input; CT temporal bones with contrast is the standard first-line imaging because it is rapidly available, characterises bony destruction and identifies abscess, with MRI / MR venography reserved for suspected intracranial extension or sinus thrombosis, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

explanation.summary: Acute mastoiditis is the commonest suppurative complication of AOM and presents with postauricular swelling, protruding pinna and obliteration of the postauricular sulcus, often after failed oral therapy. Management is admission, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone), urgent contrast CT temporal bones and urgent ENT for surgical drainage, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.
key_points: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the commonest pathogen; consider PCV failure
CT temporal bones with contrast identifies subperiosteal abscess and bony erosion
Add metronidazole if intracranial extension; obtain MR venography for sinus thrombosis
ENT may perform cortical mastoidectomy plus myringotomy with grommet insertion
explanation.pearls: Always palpate and inspect behind the ear in any child with persistent fever despite AOM treatment - missing mastoiditis risks intracranial spread, sigmoid sinus thrombosis and facial nerve palsy (Source: RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis).

q.subtopic_detail: Acute otitis media complications - acute mastoiditis

q.stem: A 9-month-old girl is brought to the GP for an opportunistic check after her grandmother noticed that the left pupil appeared white in a recent flash photograph. The mother reports that the eye otherwise looks normal, the baby fixes and follows in good light and reaches for toys with both hands. She was born at term by normal vaginal birth with an unremarkable antenatal course. She passed the newborn hearing screen but the parents do not recall whether the red reflex was specifically examined on the newborn check or 6-week check. There is no family history of eye disease in particular no family history of retinoblastoma. On examination today, with an ophthalmoscope held at arms length, the right pupil shows a normal red reflex but the left pupil shows a clearly visible white reflex (leukocoria). There is no strabismus on inspection, no nystagmus and no proptosis. The cornea is clear and the anterior chamber appears normal. Visual behaviour with the right eye is normal but appears reduced with the left eye occluded. The mother asks whether MRI or ophthalmology should come first.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 122 bpm, RR 30 /min
data_table.Examination: Left leukocoria with absent red reflex on direct ophthalmoscopy; right red reflex normal; no strabismus; clear cornea; normal anterior chamber

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the parents that white reflex on flash photographs is usually benign and review the child at the next routine 12-month immunisation visit
options.rationale: Leukocoria is never normal and mandates same-day or next-day paediatric ophthalmology assessment to exclude sight-threatening or life-threatening pathology, including retinoblastoma, per RANZCO Position Statement on Red Reflex Testing.

options.text: Refer routinely to a community optometrist for paediatric vision testing in 4-6 weeks before considering any specialist paediatric ophthalmology assessment for this finding
options.rationale: Routine optometry referral risks unacceptable delay; retinoblastoma is the most serious cause of leukocoria and requires urgent paediatric ophthalmology assessment, not deferred optometry review, per RANZCO and RCH CPG.

options.text: Refer urgently (within 24-48 hours) to a paediatric ophthalmologist for examination under anaesthetic to assess for retinoblastoma and other causes of leukocoria
options.rationale: Persistent unilateral leukocoria in an infant requires urgent paediatric ophthalmology referral; the most important diagnosis to exclude is retinoblastoma, which is curable when detected early but devastating when diagnosis is delayed. Differential also includes congenital cataract, persistent foetal vasculature, Coats disease and toxocariasis, per RANZCO Position Statement and RCH CPG.

options.text: Order an outpatient MRI brain and orbits under general anaesthetic as the initial investigation to characterise any intraocular mass before specialist ophthalmology referral
options.rationale: MRI is an important part of staging once retinoblastoma is diagnosed (to assess optic nerve extension and exclude pinealoblastoma in trilateral disease), but the first step is paediatric ophthalmology assessment with examination under anaesthetic, per RANZCO.

options.text: Patch the right unaffected eye for 2 hours daily for amblyopia management of the left eye and review in 3 months for clinical progress
options.rationale: Patching is for amblyopia treatment after the underlying cause has been identified and treated; instituting occlusion without identifying the leukocoric pathology could delay diagnosis of retinoblastoma, per RANZCO.

explanation.summary: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the cardinal sign of retinoblastoma and other sight-threatening pathology in infants. Red reflex examination should be performed at every newborn, 6 week and 6 month check. Any persistent abnormality requires urgent paediatric ophthalmology referral, per RANZCO Position Statement and RCH CPG.
key_points: Retinoblastoma is the commonest intraocular malignancy of childhood (most cases under 5)
Bilateral disease suggests germline RB1 mutation and trilateral risk (pineal)
Other causes of leukocoria: congenital cataract, PFV, Coats disease, ROP, toxocariasis
Treatment includes focal therapy, systemic / intra-arterial chemotherapy and enucleation
explanation.pearls: Parents often notice leukocoria first in flash photographs; never dismiss the parent who brings a photograph of a white pupil (Source: RANZCO).

q.subtopic_detail: Red reflex abnormality - leukocoria and retinoblastoma

q.stem: A 3-day-old term male infant is brought to ED by his parents because of bilateral profuse purulent eye discharge that began 24 hours ago and is rapidly increasing. He was born by normal vaginal birth at 39+2 weeks weighing 3220 g. The pregnancy was unbooked because the mother (a 19-year-old who recently migrated and was lost to antenatal care) presented in labour at the nearest hospital. Antenatal STI screening was not performed. The baby has otherwise been feeding and behaving normally. On examination there is profuse yellow-green purulent discharge from both eyes with marked eyelid oedema (chemosis) so pronounced that the lids cannot be readily opened. The cornea on the small visible portion looks slightly hazy on the right. He is afebrile with normal observations. There is no other rash, no hepatosplenomegaly and no other focus of infection. The ED registrar specifically asks whether topical chloramphenicol could be trialled first, whether oral azithromycin for chlamydial conjunctivitis would be adequate, whether the appearance represents chemical conjunctivitis from birth, and whether topical aciclovir for neonatal HSV keratoconjunctivitis should be applied empirically. The retrieval team is on the way.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 148 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral profuse purulent eye discharge, marked chemosis preventing easy lid opening, possible right corneal haze, no rash, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Maternal history: Unbooked pregnancy, no antenatal STI screening, age 19, recently migrated

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Send eye swab for Gram stain, bacterial culture and Chlamydia / Neisseria gonorrhoeae PCR, admit, commence IV ceftriaxone or cefotaxime, perform saline eye irrigation and request urgent paediatric ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis with marked chemosis in the first week of life with maternal risk factors must be treated as Neisseria gonorrhoeae until proven otherwise; gonococcal ophthalmia can perforate the cornea within 24 hours. Management is admission, IV third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime preferred in jaundiced neonates over ceftriaxone), copious saline irrigation and urgent ophthalmology, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Prescribe topical chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops one drop to each eye six-hourly for 7 days and review at the GP clinic the following day to assess improvement
options.rationale: Topical chloramphenicol alone is inadequate for gonococcal ophthalmia, which requires systemic therapy because of penetration into the cornea and risk of disseminated infection, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg once daily for 3 days as empirical therapy for likely Chlamydia trachomatis conjunctivitis without admission or specialist input
options.rationale: Chlamydial conjunctivitis typically presents at 5-14 days of life with mucopurulent discharge and less severe chemosis; this hyperacute first-week-of-life presentation with profuse purulence is gonococcal until disproven and requires IV cephalosporin, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Reassure the parents that this is most likely chemical (silver nitrate) conjunctivitis from birth, irrigate with normal saline, and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Chemical conjunctivitis occurs in the first 24-48 hours and is mild and self-limiting; silver nitrate prophylaxis is not used in Australia, and this severe hyperacute purulent presentation cannot be attributed to a chemical cause, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis.

options.text: Apply topical aciclovir 3% eye ointment five times daily for suspected neonatal herpes simplex keratoconjunctivitis and arrange paediatric review the following day
options.rationale: Neonatal HSV typically presents with vesicular skin lesions (SEM disease), keratitis with dendritic ulcer or systemic illness, and is treated with IV high-dose aciclovir, not topical alone; the presentation here is bacterial gonococcal ophthalmia, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis.

explanation.summary: Ophthalmia neonatorum is conjunctivitis in the first 28 days of life. Timing helps differentiate aetiology: chemical 24-48 hours, gonococcal day 2-5 (hyperacute, severe chemosis, corneal risk), chlamydial day 5-14 (mucopurulent, milder), HSV day 6-14 (vesicles, dendritic keratitis, systemic). Suspected gonococcal disease mandates IV third-generation cephalosporin, irrigation and urgent ophthalmology, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis.
key_points: Notify chlamydial and gonococcal infection to public health
Treat both mother and partners; arrange contact tracing
Cefotaxime preferred over ceftriaxone in jaundiced neonates (bilirubin displacement)
Chlamydia adds risk of pneumonia (interstitial pattern at 4-12 weeks)
explanation.pearls: Gonococcal ophthalmia can perforate the cornea within 24 hours; treat empirically while awaiting microbiology (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

q.subtopic_detail: Ophthalmia neonatorum - gonococcal conjunctivitis

q.stem: A 22-month-old previously well boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by ambulance with a 24 hour history of refusing to crawl, increasing irritability and fever. The parents report that he had a viral upper respiratory tract illness 2 weeks ago with a small left buccal mucosal ulcer that healed. He has had a poor oral intake over the past 12 hours and last wet nappy was 8 hours ago. He is fully immunised including PCV13 and Hib. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. On arrival he is lethargic, lying very still with the left hip held in flexion, abduction and external rotation. Any movement of the left lower limb causes him to scream. There is a small ecchymotic area over the left knee that mother reports was from a fall last week. The right leg is normal. Bloods drawn at triage and a bedside hip ultrasound are now back. The ED consultant specifically asks whether 20 mL/kg fluid boluses are still recommended, whether vancomycin and meropenem are required, whether MRI should precede antibiotics, whether observation with paracetamol is acceptable, and whether ceftriaxone monotherapy with delayed fluid resuscitation pending arterial line insertion is reasonable. The orthopaedic on-call is in theatre but can come down in 15 minutes for hip washout. The PICU bed coordinator has been notified and a bed is being made available.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 178 bpm, RR 44 /min, BP 78/40 mmHg, SpO2 97 percent on room air, cap refill 4 sec, mottled peripheries
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, lies still, left hip flexed / abducted / externally rotated, screams on any movement, small left knee bruise (parent-witnessed), dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 18.6), CRP 198 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, lactate 3.2 mmol/L, blood culture pending, left hip US large effusion

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management sequence?

options.text: Activate the paediatric sepsis pathway with 10 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, draw blood culture and synovial fluid in theatre, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin plus cefazolin (to cover Kingella kingae) and arrange urgent orthopaedic hip washout under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: This child is in compensated septic shock with septic arthritis of the left hip (4 of 4 Kocher, large effusion). Australian paediatric sepsis pathway recommends 10 mL/kg crystalloid boluses with reassessment between, blood culture before antibiotics where possible, and empirical IV anti-staphylococcal cover (flucloxacillin); in under-4s a second-generation cephalosporin such as cefazolin is added to cover Kingella kingae. Definitive management requires urgent surgical washout, per RCH CPG: Sepsis, RCH CPG: Acute limp and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 10 minutes, commence empirical intravenous vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly and meropenem 20 mg/kg eight-hourly, and admit to PICU before any orthopaedic intervention or joint aspiration
options.rationale: 20 mL/kg boluses are now discouraged as initial paediatric resuscitation in many Australian centres in favour of 10 mL/kg with reassessment because of risk of fluid overload; vancomycin / meropenem are not first-line for septic arthritis without MRSA risk or central venous device, and surgical source control must not be delayed, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

options.text: Transfuse 10 mL/kg of packed red cells immediately for presumed haemodynamic instability and arrange urgent MRI of the left hip and pelvis with contrast before commencing any intravenous antibiotics, fluids or surgical washout for joint source control
options.rationale: There is no evidence of anaemia or blood loss; crystalloid resuscitation is first-line. MRI may complement evaluation but must not delay antibiotics and source control in confirmed septic arthritis, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

options.text: Give oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg for analgesia, observe the child closely in the resuscitation bay for 4 hours and re-examine carefully before deciding on the need for joint aspiration, intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical washout
options.rationale: This child has shock and confirmed septic arthritis on examination, bloods and ultrasound; observation delays time to antibiotics and source control and is unacceptable, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg as empirical monotherapy and defer further fluid resuscitation pending insertion of an arterial line for invasive blood pressure monitoring in PICU before any orthopaedic involvement or aspiration is considered
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation must not be deferred for line placement in a shocked child; ceftriaxone alone has gaps for severe staphylococcal infection and is not preferred monotherapy for septic arthritis in Australia, per RCH CPG: Sepsis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

explanation.summary: An unwell toddler with hip septic arthritis and shock requires simultaneous resuscitation (10 mL/kg crystalloid boluses with reassessment), early empirical IV antibiotics after blood culture (flucloxacillin plus an agent active against Kingella kingae in under-4s such as cefazolin), and urgent orthopaedic source control. Vancomycin is added when MRSA risk is high or central line associated, per RCH CPG: Sepsis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
key_points: Recent oropharyngeal lesion is a hook for Kingella kingae - it enters through breached oral mucosa
Australian paediatric sepsis pathway: 10 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, reassess, repeat as needed
Source control (washout) is as important as antibiotics in septic arthritis
Add vancomycin for MRSA risk (e.g. remote Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, line-associated, severe systemic illness)
explanation.pearls: Bacteraemia precedes joint seeding; always draw blood cultures with the first peripheral cannula before antibiotics where it does not delay therapy (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

q.subtopic_detail: Septic arthritis in unwell child - resuscitation and source control

q.stem: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy and mild persistent asthma is brought to a metropolitan paediatric ED after a school excursion exposure. Witnesses describe sudden generalised urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting, audible wheeze and progression to a brief loss of postural tone within 15 minutes of eating a granola bar. The school nurse administered intramuscular adrenaline 300 microgram via an EpiPen at 10 minutes and a second dose was given by paramedics 8 minutes later with sustained improvement. On arrival to ED 45 minutes after the initial reaction he is alert, with HR 96, BP 108/64, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 2 L oxygen, with no audible wheeze, no stridor, mild residual peri-oral oedema and clearing urticaria. He has had no further drop in BP, no recurrence of wheeze, and no further vomiting. His regular ICS is continued. The family asks whether he can be discharged after a short observation. His usual asthma is well-controlled with no recent exacerbations. His parents are keen to leave once he is comfortable because they have other children at home. The treating team specifically discusses the role of corticosteroids, the minimum versus extended observation period, and whether intensive-care admission is required given the two adrenaline doses.

data_table.Pre-hospital: 2 doses IM adrenaline (school then paramedics, 8 min apart) with sustained improvement
data_table.Vital signs on arrival: HR 96, BP 108/64, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 2 L O2
data_table.Examination: Alert; mild residual peri-oral oedema; no wheeze, no stridor; clearing urticaria
data_table.Background: Known peanut allergy; mild persistent asthma on ICS; well-controlled

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition and observation plan?

options.text: Discharge home now with oral cetirizine 10 mg daily, a written ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions, a single in-date EpiPen and follow-up review in primary care within 48 hours of discharge
options.rationale: Early discharge after severe anaphylaxis - especially with asthma comorbidity and a requirement for repeat adrenaline - underestimates the risk of biphasic reaction; ASCIA recommends prolonged observation in this scenario (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Observe in the ED short-stay unit for exactly 4 hours from the last adrenaline dose and discharge if asymptomatic with the standard discharge bundle and no extended observation despite severity features
options.rationale: Four hours is the minimum standard observation after uncomplicated anaphylaxis; severe features (repeat adrenaline, asthma, transient loss of postural tone) justify longer observation under ASCIA guidance (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Admit for at least 6-12 hours of monitored observation given severe features (two adrenaline doses, asthma, transient collapse) and discharge with updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two in-date adrenaline autoinjectors, ICS optimisation and immunology referral
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends extended observation (commonly at least 6-12 hours) after severe anaphylaxis or where any risk factor for biphasic or protracted reaction is present (need for repeat adrenaline, asthma comorbidity, hypotension/collapse, severe initial reaction); discharge should include an updated action plan, two adrenaline autoinjectors, asthma optimisation and specialist follow-up (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis; ASCIA Action Plans).

options.text: Admit to PICU for 24 hours of intubated airway monitoring with a continuous intravenous adrenaline infusion titrated to MAP and prophylactic salbutamol nebuliser regardless of current clinical stability
options.rationale: He is currently stable without airway compromise; intubation and adrenaline infusion are reserved for refractory anaphylaxis with airway/circulatory failure (ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7; ASCIA).

options.text: Discharge after 1 hour of observation with a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as routine pharmacological prevention of a biphasic reaction without extended ED observation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions and one-hour observation is inadequate; ASCIA emphasises observation duration over routine steroid use (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

explanation.summary: Severe anaphylaxis (need for repeat adrenaline, asthma comorbidity, transient collapse) warrants extended monitored observation - commonly 6-12 hours - with discharge planning that includes an updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two adrenaline autoinjectors and immunology referral (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).
key_points: Risk factors for protracted/biphasic reaction extend observation beyond 4 hours (ASCIA).
Steroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions (ASCIA).
Discharge bundle: action plan, two devices, trigger education, asthma optimisation, specialist referral (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: PBS allows two adrenaline autoinjectors per prescription - always prescribe two so one stays at school.

q.subtopic_detail: Anaphylaxis with biphasic risk - disposition decision

q.stem: A 10-year-old girl presents to ED with a third episode in six months of recurrent, non-itchy, non-erythematous swelling of the face, hands and tongue lasting 36-72 hours each time, often preceded by colicky abdominal pain. There is never any urticaria, no hypotension, no respiratory distress and no clear food, medication or insect trigger. Two previous ED visits resulted in administration of intramuscular adrenaline, intravenous hydrocortisone and chlorphenamine with no apparent response and slow spontaneous resolution. Her father has had similar episodes since childhood and her paternal uncle died from a 'throat swelling' aged 28. On examination today she is comfortable with non-pitting, non-erythematous swelling of the lips and left hand, no airway compromise, soft non-tender abdomen and normal vital signs. She has no known food, drug or environmental allergy and no asthma. C4 sent earlier is reported as low. Previous attempts at empirical anti-allergic therapy with antihistamines and prednisolone produced no benefit. The treating team specifically asks about the role of tryptase, of IgE testing, of imaging-led gastroenterology workup for the colicky abdominal pain, and whether tranexamic acid is appropriate as empirical long-term prophylaxis without further testing.

data_table.Examination: Non-pitting, non-erythematous lip and left hand swelling; no airway compromise; no urticaria; soft abdomen
data_table.Family history: Father with recurrent episodes since childhood; paternal uncle died from throat swelling age 28
data_table.Investigations: C4 low

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation and acute management?

options.text: Send serum tryptase during the current attack and commence intravenous adrenaline infusion with hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as for refractory anaphylaxis unresponsive to intramuscular adrenaline
options.rationale: Tryptase rises in mast-cell mediated reactions, not in bradykinin-driven HAE; mast-cell-targeted therapy (adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines) is ineffective in HAE attacks (ASCIA: Angioedema; UK HAEi/Australasian HAE consensus).

options.text: Send C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function (and consider SERPING1 testing); treat acute attack with C1 inhibitor concentrate (or icatibant) and arrange immunology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning
options.rationale: Low C4 between attacks plus dominantly inherited recurrent non-urticarial angioedema unresponsive to anti-allergic therapy is classical for HAE; diagnosis is confirmed by C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function, with SERPING1 testing in select cases, and acute attacks are treated with C1 inhibitor concentrate or icatibant (a bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema; Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy HAE Position).

options.text: Send total IgE and a paediatric food-specific IgE panel and commence daily non-sedating antihistamine prophylaxis with up-titration as needed for symptom control
options.rationale: IgE testing is irrelevant - HAE is bradykinin-mediated and antihistamines do not prevent attacks; this is a common diagnostic delay scenario (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

options.text: Refer for contrast-enhanced abdominal CT and outpatient gastroenterology workup for the recurrent colicky abdominal pain in isolation without further immunology investigation
options.rationale: Bowel-wall oedema from HAE causes the colicky abdominal pain; isolated GI workup misses the unifying systemic diagnosis (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

options.text: Commence daily oral tranexamic acid as immediate definitive long-term prophylaxis without C1 inhibitor testing or specialist immunology input at this presentation
options.rationale: Long-term prophylaxis decisions in paediatric HAE require confirmed diagnosis, attack frequency assessment and specialist input; tranexamic acid is sometimes used in children but is not first-line empirical therapy and does not address the acute attack (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

explanation.summary: Recurrent non-urticarial angioedema lasting days, with abdominal pain, unresponsive to adrenaline/steroids/antihistamines, with a positive family history and low C4 is HAE; confirm with C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function and treat acute attacks with C1 inhibitor concentrate or icatibant (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).
key_points: Low C4 between attacks is a useful screening clue (ASCIA).
Bradykinin-mediated - adrenaline/antihistamines/steroids ineffective (ASCIA).
Long-term prophylaxis options: C1-INH replacement, attenuated androgens (rarely in children), tranexamic acid, lanadelumab (specialist) (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Family history of fatal 'throat swelling' is a near-pathognomonic red flag for HAE - always ask about it in recurrent angioedema.

q.subtopic_detail: Hereditary angioedema versus allergic angioedema in a paediatric patient

q.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with a 4-day history of progressive fever, malaise, facial swelling and a widespread morbilliform rash that started on the trunk and spread to the limbs and face. He was started on carbamazepine 5 weeks ago for newly diagnosed focal epilepsy. There is no preceding URTI, no new foods, no other new medications and no insect exposure. On arrival temperature is 38.9 deg C, HR 122, RR 24, SpO2 97% RA, BP 110/65. Examination shows facial oedema, a confluent erythematous morbilliform eruption covering 70 percent of the body surface area without Nikolsky sign or epidermal sloughing, no mucositis, and palpable cervical, axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Investigations: WCC 18.0 x10^9/L with 12% eosinophils, ALT 320 U/L, ALP 250 U/L, creatinine 95 micromol/L (baseline 55), CRP 90 mg/L. Blood film shows atypical lymphocytes. He has no known allergies and is otherwise developmentally well. The neurology team asks whether carbamazepine can be continued at half dose or switched within class. The dermatology registrar specifically queries Stevens-Johnson syndrome and Kawasaki disease as alternative diagnoses. The mother specifically wants to know whether the rash is contagious.

data_table.Examination: Facial oedema; confluent morbilliform rash 70% BSA; no Nikolsky, no mucositis; generalised lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.0 (12% eosinophils), ALT 320, ALP 250, creatinine 95 (baseline 55), CRP 90; atypical lymphocytes on film

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue carbamazepine at the current dose, treat as a self-limiting viral exanthem with supportive paracetamol and oral cetirizine, and review in clinic in 7 days
options.rationale: The temporal relationship to a known DRESS-trigger anticonvulsant with eosinophilia, hepatitis, lymphadenopathy and facial oedema is diagnostic of DRESS; continuing the drug worsens organ injury (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ASCIA: Drug allergy).

options.text: Stop carbamazepine immediately, admit for organ-function monitoring, commence systemic corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) and consult dermatology and immunology; document the reaction and avoid aromatic anticonvulsants
options.rationale: DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) typically presents 2-8 weeks after starting culprit drugs (aromatic anticonvulsants, sulfonamides, allopurinol, vancomycin); definitive management is immediate withdrawal of the culprit, admission for organ surveillance, systemic corticosteroid for moderate-severe disease and specialist input; cross-reactivity among aromatic anticonvulsants is well documented (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ASCIA: Drug allergy in children; DermNet NZ: DRESS).

options.text: Diagnose Stevens-Johnson syndrome based on fever and rash and arrange transfer to a tertiary burns unit for crystalloid fluid resuscitation and burns-style dressing care
options.rationale: SJS/TEN is characterised by epidermal sloughing, Nikolsky sign and mucositis - none present here; DRESS is the correct SCAR phenotype in this case (DermNet NZ: SJS).

options.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day and obtain urgent echocardiography to exclude coronary artery aneurysms
options.rationale: Kawasaki requires fever >=5 days plus specific criteria (conjunctivitis, mucosal change, peripheral changes, polymorphous rash, lymphadenopathy) and would not explain eosinophilia and a 5-week post-drug temporal link (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Switch carbamazepine to phenytoin at an equivalent maintenance dose and monitor liver function tests weekly without specific dermatology or immunology referral
options.rationale: Aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbitone) cross-react in DRESS and SJS/TEN; switching within the class risks recurrence (ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).

explanation.summary: DRESS is a delayed (2-8 week) systemic drug hypersensitivity with fever, morbilliform rash, facial oedema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia and organ involvement (commonly hepatitis); management is immediate culprit withdrawal, organ surveillance, systemic corticosteroid for moderate-severe disease, and avoidance of aromatic-anticonvulsant cross-reactors (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions).
key_points: Triggers: aromatic anticonvulsants, sulfonamides, allopurinol, vancomycin (ASCIA).
RegiSCAR criteria help score probability (DermNet NZ: DRESS).
Mortality up to 10% from hepatitis or reactivation of HHV-6 (DermNet NZ).
explanation.pearls: HLA-B*15:02 carriage (more common in some Asian ancestries) raises carbamazepine SJS/TEN risk - screen where indicated by ethnicity per Australian Medicines Handbook.

q.subtopic_detail: DRESS syndrome recognition after anticonvulsant exposure

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl with a 14-week history of daily widespread itchy wheals appearing without identifiable trigger and lasting less than 24 hours per individual lesion, with occasional lip swelling, is reviewed in a paediatric allergy clinic. She has been taking cetirizine 10 mg daily for 8 weeks with partial benefit but daily lesions continue and sleep is broken. She has no anaphylaxis, no respiratory or cardiovascular features, no joint pain, no fever and no systemic upset. Investigations show normal FBC, normal CRP, normal ESR, normal LFTs, normal TSH and negative coeliac serology. There is no preceding infection, no new medication, no food trigger and no physical trigger (heat, cold, pressure, exercise). Symptoms are interfering with her schoolwork and mood. Her mother asks whether long-term oral corticosteroids are appropriate. She has no asthma and no known allergies. She specifically asks whether long-term oral corticosteroids, sedating antihistamines at night, prophylactic adrenaline, or ciclosporin should be the next step before considering biologic therapy with omalizumab. School-attendance has become an issue and she is asking about a written sick certificate. She has not previously trialled biologic therapy and asks about funding.

data_table.History: 14 weeks daily wheals <24 hours each; occasional lip swelling; no anaphylaxis or systemic features
data_table.Trial: Cetirizine 10 mg daily for 8 weeks - partial response only
data_table.Investigations: FBC, CRP, ESR, LFTs, TSH, coeliac serology all normal

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue cetirizine 10 mg daily at the standard dose and add a 6-week course of oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily with no taper, then reassess for biologic therapy
options.rationale: Long-term oral corticosteroids are not recommended in chronic spontaneous urticaria because of adverse effects with no sustained benefit and high relapse on withdrawal (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria; eTG: Urticaria).

options.text: Up-titrate the non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose (e.g. cetirizine 40 mg/day or equivalent) and review at 4 weeks before considering omalizumab
options.rationale: ASCIA and international urticaria guidance recommend stepwise up-dosing of a non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose for chronic spontaneous urticaria failing standard-dose therapy, with omalizumab as third-line for refractory disease (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria; EAACI/GA2LEN/WAO guideline endorsed by ASCIA).

options.text: Add a sedating antihistamine such as promethazine 0.25 mg/kg at bedtime as the primary add-on therapy for chronic urticaria refractory to standard-dose cetirizine
options.rationale: Sedating antihistamines provide symptomatic relief at night but are not recommended as primary add-on in chronic urticaria due to cognitive and developmental concerns in children; up-titration of the non-sedating agent is preferred (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

options.text: Start intramuscular adrenaline 0.3 mg twice weekly as suppressive therapy until the wheals subside, with a written ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan for school staff
options.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for acute anaphylaxis; there is no role for prophylactic adrenaline in chronic urticaria (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Commence ciclosporin 3 mg/kg/day in divided doses as the preferred next-line agent before any consideration of antihistamine up-titration or biologic omalizumab therapy
options.rationale: Ciclosporin is a fourth-line option after both antihistamine up-titration and omalizumab have failed; it is not before antihistamine up-titration (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

explanation.summary: Chronic spontaneous urticaria failing standard-dose non-sedating H1 antihistamine is managed by stepwise up-titration of the non-sedating antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose; omalizumab is reserved for those who do not respond (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).
key_points: Avoid systemic corticosteroids for chronic urticaria - relapse on withdrawal (ASCIA).
Investigations beyond FBC/CRP/TFT only if specific clinical suspicion (ASCIA).
Omalizumab is PBS-listed in Australia for chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to up-titrated H1 antihistamine.
explanation.pearls: Urticaria with INDIVIDUAL lesions lasting >24 hours, purpura or systemic features should prompt evaluation for urticarial vasculitis.

q.subtopic_detail: Chronic spontaneous urticaria - second-line management

q.stem: A previously well, immunocompetent 4-year-old boy is brought to ED on day 4 of varicella. The parents report new escalating severe pain in the left thigh disproportionate to skin appearance, a fresh fever spike to 39.6 deg C after defervescence yesterday, and reduced weight-bearing. He has not been on ibuprofen. On examination he is unwell-appearing, HR 160, RR 36, SpO2 97% RA, BP 88/50, capillary refill 3 seconds and cool peripheries. Over the left antero-medial thigh there is a 6 cm area of dusky violaceous skin around two coalesced varicella lesions, exquisite tenderness extending beyond the visible edge, indurated subcutaneous tissue and no crepitus. There are typical varicella lesions at multiple stages elsewhere. WCC 22 x10^9/L with left shift, CRP 280 mg/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 80 micromol/L (baseline 45), Na 132 mmol/L. He has had no recent travel, no contact with TB or invasive meningococcal disease and is unimmunised for varicella due to a planned MMR schedule slip. The ED registrar specifically asks whether oral therapy with cefalexin and topical mupirocin would be sufficient for impetiginised varicella, and whether MRI of the thigh or intravenous immunoglobulin alone could substitute for early surgical exploration in this child.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6, HR 160, RR 36, SpO2 97% RA, BP 88/50, CRT 3 s
data_table.Examination: 6 cm dusky violaceous patch around varicella lesions on left thigh; tenderness beyond visible edge; indurated subcutaneous tissue; no crepitus
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 (left shift), CRP 280, lactate 4.2, creatinine 80 (baseline 45), Na 132

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Continue oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly, observe in the short-stay unit for 4 hours and discharge home if the pain settles with simple analgesia and reassurance
options.rationale: This is invasive bacterial soft tissue infection complicating varicella with septic shock physiology - delay risks death; discharge is unsafe (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis; eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Resuscitate with crystalloid, administer empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Group A streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus (including clindamycin for toxin suppression) and arrange urgent surgical assessment for source control
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, dusky skin, induration beyond the visible margin, fresh fever after defervescence on day 4 of varicella and septic shock physiology indicate invasive Group A streptococcal soft-tissue infection (potentially necrotising fasciitis); resuscitation, empirical broad cover including clindamycin for toxin suppression and urgent surgical exploration are mandatory (RCH CPG: Cellulitis; eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Sepsis).

options.text: Apply topical mupirocin 2% ointment three times daily to the dusky area and commence oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for presumed impetiginised varicella lesions
options.rationale: Topical and oral therapy are inadequate for invasive soft-tissue infection with septic shock physiology (eTG: Skin and soft tissue infections).

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as definitive monotherapy for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome without empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics or surgical input
options.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct used in streptococcal toxic shock syndrome and necrotising fasciitis but does not replace early empirical antibiotics and surgical source control (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Obtain urgent MRI of the left thigh with contrast to characterise fascial involvement before commencing intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical consultation
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis - imaging must not delay resuscitation, antibiotics and surgical exploration (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

explanation.summary: Worsening pain disproportionate to skin findings, fresh fever after defervescence, induration beyond visible margins and septic shock on day 4 of varicella signal invasive Group A streptococcal soft-tissue infection (possible necrotising fasciitis); management is resuscitation, empirical broad antibiotics including clindamycin for toxin suppression, and urgent surgical exploration (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).
key_points: Varicella is a strong risk factor for invasive GAS soft-tissue infection (RCH CPG).
Clindamycin added for exotoxin suppression (eTG).
Imaging must not delay source control (eTG).
explanation.pearls: NSAID exposure has been epidemiologically associated with increased severity of GAS soft-tissue infection in varicella - paracetamol is preferred for varicella fever in Australian paediatric practice.

q.subtopic_detail: Varicella complication in an immunocompetent child requiring escalation

q.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED on day 3 of a febrile illness with progressive painful rash. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 9 days ago for recurrent UTI. Over the past 24 hours he has developed conjunctival redness with photophobia, painful oral erosions, dysphagia and dusky atypical targetoid macules on the trunk and face that are becoming confluent. On examination temperature is 39.0 deg C, HR 140, RR 30, SpO2 96% RA, BP 95/55. There are haemorrhagic crusted lips, fibrinous oral mucosal erosions, conjunctival injection with serous discharge, urethral meatal erosion, and dusky atypical targetoid lesions over approximately 18 percent of body surface area with Nikolsky sign positive in two areas. Investigations show WCC 12 x10^9/L, CRP 70 mg/L, ALT 85 U/L, creatinine 90 micromol/L (baseline 50), Na 134 mmol/L, glucose 5.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. He has no immune deficiency and is otherwise well-grown. There is no Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander identification recorded. The retrieval team and burns unit are en route. The ED registrar specifically asks whether the trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole can be continued at half dose with oral prednisolone, whether the presentation could represent staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome or incomplete Kawasaki disease, and whether punch biopsy in ED should be performed before any therapy is commenced or transfer is initiated. The family asks how long the recovery period typically takes after transfer.

data_table.Examination: Haemorrhagic crusted lips, fibrinous oral erosions, conjunctivitis, urethral erosion; dusky atypical targetoid lesions ~18% BSA; Nikolsky positive
data_table.Investigations: WCC 12, CRP 70, ALT 85, creatinine 90 (baseline 50), Na 134, HCO3 18

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Cease trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole immediately, transfer to a centre with paediatric burns/intensive-care capability with ophthalmology and dermatology input, commence supportive care (fluid resuscitation, temperature control, analgesia, sterile non-adherent dressings, mucosal care), and document drug allergy and consider specialist-directed immunomodulation
options.rationale: SJS/TEN is a dermatological emergency - immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug is the single highest-impact intervention and improves mortality; supportive care equivalent to burns management, multidisciplinary input (paediatric ICU, burns, ophthalmology to prevent symblepharon, dermatology) and consideration of specialist-directed immunomodulation (e.g. ciclosporin, IVIG, TNF-alpha inhibitor) are the standard approach (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ANZBA paediatric SJS/TEN consensus; DermNet NZ: SJS).

options.text: Continue trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole at half dose, commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with topical chlorhexidine 0.05% to mucosal surfaces twice daily, and admit for 24 hours of fluid balance and pain monitoring before reassessment
options.rationale: Continuing the culprit drug is unequivocally harmful in SJS/TEN; oral steroid monotherapy in low-resource settings without culprit withdrawal does not change mortality and chlorhexidine to denuded mucosa causes additional injury (DermNet NZ: SJS).

options.text: Treat as staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome with intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly plus encourage oral hydration with maintenance fluids, paracetamol for fever and observation in the ED short-stay unit for 24 hours before discharge
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome occurs predominantly in infants/young children, has minimal mucosal involvement and Nikolsky sign with intact mucosae; the temporal drug link, mucositis and atypical targets here favour SJS/TEN (DermNet NZ: SSSS).

options.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours and high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day in four divided doses with echocardiography on day 3 of admission to exclude coronary aneurysms
options.rationale: Kawasaki disease produces polymorphous rash, conjunctivitis and mucosal changes but does not produce skin sloughing, Nikolsky sign or atypical targetoid lesions, and is not temporally linked to a culprit drug (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Perform a 4 mm punch biopsy of involved skin in ED, send for routine histology and direct immunofluorescence, and defer all therapy including culprit drug withdrawal and transfer until the histology result is available
options.rationale: Biopsy is helpful for confirmation and prognostication but must not delay culprit withdrawal, transfer to a specialist centre and initiation of supportive care in a child with active mucocutaneous emergency (DermNet NZ: SJS).

explanation.summary: SJS/TEN management priorities are: immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug, transfer to a specialist centre with burns and PICU expertise, multidisciplinary support including ophthalmology to prevent ocular sequelae, supportive care and specialist-directed immunomodulation; culprit withdrawal is the single highest-impact intervention (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions).
key_points: Sulfa antibiotics, aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs are common triggers (eTG; ASCIA).
Ophthalmology input within 24 hours reduces chronic ocular morbidity (DermNet NZ).
SCORTEN at 24 hours and 72 hours stratifies mortality risk (DermNet NZ).
explanation.pearls: Always document SJS/TEN drug allergy in the eHealth record (My Health Record) and on a medical alert bracelet - re-challenge can be lethal.

q.subtopic_detail: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis early management in a child

q.stem: A 52-year-old woman is brought to ED two hours after a colleague found her shivering, agitated and unsteady at work. She was started on sertraline 50 mg daily eight weeks ago for major depression. Three days ago her GP added tramadol 50 mg up to four times daily for an acute lumbar strain; she has taken one tablet six-hourly. There is no history of recent illicit drug use. She is alert but restless, sweating, and reports intermittent muscle twitching in her legs. Examination shows mydriasis, intermittent spontaneous clonus in the ankles, brisk patellar reflexes, and resting tremor of the upper limbs. She is afebrile at triage but on rechecking 30 minutes later her temperature has risen to 38.4 degrees. Pupils are 6 mm and reactive. She has no rigidity, no oculogyric movements, and her mental state is anxious but oriented and cooperative She has no other serotonergic medications, no recreational MDMA, no St John's wort, no triptan use, and her partner confirms no other tablets at home. The ED team is now choosing between supportive care, dantrolene, bromocriptine and benztropine.

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis 6 mm, spontaneous ankle clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor, shivering, no rigidity, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138, potassium 4.0, creatinine 84, CK 480 U/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, urine drug screen negative
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 50 mg daily (8 weeks), tramadol 50 mg QID (3 days)

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease serotonergic agents, give IV fluids, oral diazepam 5 mg, observe on monitored bed
options.rationale: Mild to moderate serotonin toxicity is treated by withdrawing serotonergic agents, supportive care including IV fluids and cooling, and benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus; cyproheptadine is reserved for moderate-to-severe cases not responding to supportive care (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin toxicity; Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria).

options.text: Oral bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily with active cooling on the ward
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is a dopamine agonist used in some protocols for NMS, not serotonin toxicity; this patient's hyperreflexia, clonus and serotonergic medication history fits serotonin syndrome rather than NMS (Source: eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Intramuscular benztropine 2 mg as treatment for the movement disorder
options.rationale: Benztropine treats acute dystonia and parkinsonism from D2 blockade; it has no role in serotonin toxicity and anticholinergic effects can worsen hyperthermia (Source: eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Intravenous dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg with intubation and neuromuscular paralysis
options.rationale: Dantrolene and neuromuscular blockade are reserved for life-threatening hyperthermia (above 41.5 degrees) not responding to supportive measures; aggressive escalation is not indicated at this severity (Source: eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Switch sertraline to venlafaxine and reduce the tramadol dose by half today
options.rationale: Both venlafaxine and tramadol are serotonergic; switching to another SNRI while continuing tramadol perpetuates toxicity and ignores the acute presentation requiring immediate cessation (Source: eTG Psychotropic; AMH 2024).

explanation.summary: Spontaneous lower-limb clonus plus a serotonergic agent meets Hunter Criteria for serotonin toxicity. Management is cessation of all serotonergic agents, supportive care, and benzodiazepines, with cyproheptadine for non-responders (Source: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria; eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Hunter Criteria require a serotonergic agent plus one of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus with agitation/diaphoresis, ocular clonus, tremor with hyperreflexia, or hypertonia with T above 38 and ocular/inducible clonus.
Tramadol, fentanyl, MAOIs, linezolid, St John's wort all contribute to serotonin load.
Benzodiazepines are first-line pharmacotherapy.
Cyproheptadine 12 mg loading then 2 mg q2h for moderate toxicity not responding.

q.subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome

q.stem: A 32-year-old woman has been on clozapine for 16 days, currently titrated to 200 mg nocte. She presents to the early-psychosis clinic feeling unwell for 36 hours with chest tightness on exertion, palpitations, a temperature of 38.2 degrees and a non-productive cough. There is no rash, no pleuritic pain, and no recent viral contact at home. She has no history of cardiac disease, no chest pain at rest, and observations show tachycardia. Examination reveals a faint S3 gallop, no murmurs, clear chest sounds, and no peripheral oedema. Bloods today show WCC 7.8, neutrophils 4.6 x10^9/L (within normal), CRP 78, troponin I 380 ng/L (laboratory upper reference 26 ng/L), eosinophils 0.9 x10^9/L. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 118, no ST elevation, no PR depression. She remains on clozapine titration with weekly FBC monitoring She has had no recent viral prodrome, no rash and no peripheral oedema. The clozapine prescriber is contactable, and a cardiology bed is available if needed. Echocardiogram and cardiac MRI can be arranged within hours through the on-call service She remains haemodynamically stable for now Myocarditis is the leading concern given timing.

data_table.Examination: Faint S3 gallop, clear lung fields, no oedema, no rash
data_table.Investigations: WCC 7.8, neutrophils 4.6, eosinophils 0.9 x10^9/L, CRP 78, troponin I 380 ng/L (URL 26), ECG sinus tachycardia 118, no ST changes

q.lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Continue clozapine, add aspirin and review in 48 hours with repeat troponin
options.rationale: Continuing clozapine in suspected clozapine-induced myocarditis is contraindicated and risks progression to fulminant heart failure; aspirin alone is inadequate (Source: AMH 2024; ClozaConnect/ClopineCentral product information).

options.text: Cease clozapine immediately, admit for cardiology review, and arrange urgent echocardiogram
options.rationale: In the first 4 to 8 weeks of clozapine titration, fever with tachycardia and rising troponin and CRP suggests clozapine-induced myocarditis; immediate cessation, admission, troponin trend, ECG, and echocardiogram are mandated (Source: AMH 2024; eTG Psychotropic: Clozapine adverse effects; TGA Clozapine PI).

options.text: Stop clozapine because the neutrophil count of 4.6 x10^9/L indicates impending agranulocytosis
options.rationale: A neutrophil count of 4.6 is normal; agranulocytosis is defined as neutrophils less than 0.5 x10^9/L, and clozapine is interrupted (Amber) at 1.0 to 1.5 and ceased (Red) below 1.0 in Australian programs (Source: AMH 2024; Clozapine product information).

options.text: Reduce clozapine to 100 mg and add atenolol 25 mg daily
options.rationale: Dose reduction without cessation does not address potential myocarditis; beta blockade can mask haemodynamic deterioration and is not the appropriate first response (Source: AMH 2024; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Treat empirically for community-acquired pneumonia with oral amoxicillin and doxycycline
options.rationale: The clinical picture (gallop, raised troponin, normal lung exam) points to myocarditis rather than pneumonia; treating empirically for CAP without addressing the cardiac findings is unsafe (Source: eTG Antibiotic; AMH 2024).

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis typically occurs in the first 4 to 8 weeks of titration. The Australian recommendation is weekly FBC, troponin and CRP monitoring during this window and immediate cessation if myocarditis is suspected (Source: AMH 2024; Clozapine TGA PI).
key_points: Myocarditis window: weeks 2 to 4 (most), up to 8 weeks.
Red flags: fever, tachycardia, troponin rise, CRP >50, dyspnoea, eosinophilia.
Agranulocytosis cut-offs (general): Green N >=2.0, Amber 1.5 to 2.0, Red <1.5 (some programs use 1.0).
Other clozapine adverse effects: hypersalivation, constipation/ileus, seizures, weight gain, hyperglycaemia.

q.subtopic_detail: Clozapine monitoring

q.stem: A 56-year-old man is admitted to the medical ward after presenting with a fractured wrist sustained in a fall. He drinks approximately 25 standard drinks per day and his last drink was 36 hours ago. He has a history of two previous withdrawal seizures and one prior episode of delirium tremens. He is currently tremulous, diaphoretic and reports tactile hallucinations of insects on his skin. He is oriented to person and place but disoriented to time. His CIWA-Ar score is 22. He has no head injury. He was given 100 mg IM thiamine in ED. He has no known liver decompensation; LFTs show AST 110, ALT 65, INR 1.2 and albumin 36. He has no respiratory disease. He takes no regular medications and weighs 70 kg He has no history of opioid use, no QT-prolonging medications, an ECG shows sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms, and an unblocked IV cannula is in situ. The medical team needs to choose a symptom-triggered regimen plus thiamine and electrolyte correction now He is requesting safer dosing than his prior alcohol detox He weighs 70 kg and is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor, diaphoresis, tactile hallucinations, oriented to person/place not time, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 5.6 mmol/L, Na 138, K 3.2, Mg 0.62 mmol/L, AST 110, ALT 65, INR 1.2, alcohol level 0
data_table.Medications: Thiamine 100 mg IM in ED, no regular meds

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing his withdrawal?

options.text: Continue daily thiamine 100 mg orally and observe with hourly CIWA-Ar reassessment overnight
options.rationale: A CIWA-Ar of 22 is severe withdrawal and observation alone risks progression to seizures or delirium tremens; oral thiamine is also insufficient prophylaxis in chronic heavy drinkers at risk of Wernicke's, who should receive parenteral high-dose B-complex (Pabrinex) (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Management Guidelines; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Symptom-triggered diazepam 10 to 20 mg PO 1- to 2-hourly titrated to CIWA-Ar plus IV Pabrinex
options.rationale: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing is the standard of care for severe alcohol withdrawal in Australian guidance; parenteral high-dose B-complex (Pabrinex) is required for Wernicke's prophylaxis in any heavy drinker with malnutrition risk, and electrolyte correction reduces seizure risk (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence oral carbamazepine 200 mg three times daily as the sole withdrawal agent
options.rationale: Anticonvulsants other than benzodiazepines are second-line and inferior for severe withdrawal; benzodiazepines are first-line in Australian guidance because they reduce seizures, delirium tremens and mortality (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Use an intravenous propofol infusion in a ward-based setting with bedside monitoring
options.rationale: Propofol infusion is reserved for refractory delirium tremens in ICU; it is not appropriate on a general ward and is not first-line in any setting (Source: eTG Addiction).

options.text: Restart small amounts of oral alcohol to taper consumption over 5 days at home
options.rationale: Alcohol tapering is not used in Australian inpatient practice; pharmacokinetic variability, hepatotoxicity and impaired titration make benzodiazepine-based withdrawal the standard of care (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: Severe alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar >15) requires symptom-triggered diazepam, parenteral B-complex for Wernicke's prophylaxis, and aggressive electrolyte correction. Prior seizures or DTs raise relapse risk and justify earlier escalation (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction).
key_points: Pabrinex (high-dose IV thiamine + B-complex) BD for 3 to 5 days for at-risk patients.
Oral thiamine alone is inadequate in alcoholic malnutrition.
Symptom-triggered diazepam outperforms fixed-dose regimens.
Correct hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia to reduce seizure risk.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

q.subtopic_detail: Alcohol withdrawal

q.stem: A 31-year-old woman with a 6-year history of injecting heroin presents to a community OST clinic seeking opioid agonist treatment. She last used heroin 12 hours ago. She has tested positive for HCV antibody (RNA pending) and her LFTs show ALT 84, AST 60. She has no other psychiatric medications and no significant medical comorbidity. Her observations are normal. COWS score on assessment is 14 (moderate withdrawal) with pupil dilation, sweating, yawning, mild rhinorrhoea, anxiety and bone aches. Urine drug screen is positive for opioids only.
