She is unable to attend a clinic for daily supervised dispensing because she works variable shifts but can attend twice weekly. She has no respiratory disease, no QT-prolonging medications, and an ECG today shows QTc 410 ms. She is keen to start treatment today She has read about buprenorphine-naloxone and methadone and asks about safer first-day dosing for community induction given she cannot attend daily supervised dispensing She has heard from a friend that 16 mg sublingual buprenorphine at the first dose is the fastest way to suppress withdrawal and asks whether this is safe.

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis, diaphoresis, restless, no signs of overdose; venepuncture marks both antecubital fossae
data_table.Investigations: COWS 14, AST 60, ALT 84, ECG QTc 410 ms, urine drug screen positive opioids only, HCV antibody positive (RNA pending)

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in induction?

options.text: Sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone 4 mg now, top-up 2 to 4 mg in 2 hours if withdrawal persists
options.rationale: Buprenorphine is preferred over methadone for community induction because of lower overdose risk, capacity for unsupervised takeaway doses earlier, and shorter induction; it must only be given once moderate withdrawal is established (COWS >=12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal (Source: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Commence methadone 60 mg orally today as the safer first-day community induction dose
options.rationale: Methadone induction starts low (typically 20 to 30 mg, no more than 40 mg on day 1) due to its long half-life and accumulation risk; 60 mg on day 1 has caused fatal overdose during induction (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Wait until she has been opioid-free for 24 hours and then commence oral naltrexone
options.rationale: Oral naltrexone has very high relapse rates in opioid dependence and is not first-line maintenance; it also cannot be commenced until 7 to 10 days opioid-free, with risk of overdose when re-using opioids due to loss of tolerance (Source: eTG Addiction; NGMA).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Give intramuscular naloxone now to confirm opioid dependence before any induction begins
options.rationale: Naloxone is used to reverse overdose, not to confirm dependence; deliberate precipitation of withdrawal is unethical and unnecessary - dependence is confirmed by history, examination and the COWS score (Source: NGMA).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Commence sublingual buprenorphine 16 mg as a single first dose to suppress withdrawal rapidly
options.rationale: Day-1 buprenorphine should usually start at 2 to 8 mg with cautious top-up; an immediate 16 mg dose risks oversedation, especially if any concomitant CNS depressant is present, and exceeds standard induction protocols (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

explanation.summary: Buprenorphine-naloxone induction in established moderate withdrawal (COWS >=12) is preferred for community OST, especially for patients with limited capacity for daily supervised dispensing. Methadone is started low and titrated slowly because of accumulation and overdose risk (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction).
key_points: Methadone day 1: 20 to 30 mg, max 40 mg; steady state at 5 days.
Buprenorphine: lower overdose risk, ceiling on respiratory depression, faster stabilisation.
Precipitated withdrawal: occurs if buprenorphine given too early.
ECG before methadone if dose >100 mg or QT-prolonging meds; review at 1 month.

sources.label: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

q.subtopic_detail: Opioid agonist therapy

q.stem: A 45-year-old man on methadone 80 mg daily for 3 years presents to his GP with new-onset palpitations. He was admitted for community-acquired pneumonia 10 days ago and discharged on azithromycin 500 mg daily (now stopped) and oral fluconazole 200 mg daily (continuing) for oropharyngeal candidiasis. He has also been taking diphenhydramine for sleep. Resting ECG today shows sinus rhythm at 78 with QTc 510 ms (previously 430 ms before admission). He has no chest pain, no syncope and no electrolyte derangement. Electrolytes are normal including potassium 4.1 and magnesium 0.85. He has not increased his methadone dose. He denies non-prescribed drug use and urine drug screen is positive only for methadone and benzodiazepines (he disclosed temazepam supplied by a friend) The local addiction medicine specialist is contactable and able to support either methadone dose adjustment or rotation to buprenorphine. He has no syncope, no chest pain and no family history of long-QT syndrome, and his prior baseline ECG was unremarkable He is asking whether his methadone dose needs to be reduced today He is committed to engaging with treatment changes today.

data_table.Examination: No oedema, no murmurs, clear chest, no syncope
data_table.Investigations: ECG QTc 510 ms (was 430), K 4.1, Mg 0.85, eGFR >90, LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Methadone 80 mg daily, fluconazole 200 mg daily, diphenhydramine PRN, temazepam (illicitly sourced)

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Stop fluconazole and diphenhydramine, counsel against benzodiazepines, consider methadone dose reduction or switch
options.rationale: Fluconazole inhibits methadone metabolism (CYP3A4/CYP2B6) and prolongs QT; diphenhydramine adds anticholinergic load and QT effect; the priority is removing the offending agents, addressing benzodiazepine use, and re-evaluating with repeat ECG, with a switch to buprenorphine if QT remains prolonged (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Continue the current regimen unchanged and review the patient in 4 weeks with a repeat ECG
options.rationale: A QTc of 510 ms carries significant torsades risk and demands prompt action; passive review is unsafe (Source: AMH 2024; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Increase methadone to 100 mg daily to improve compliance and stabilise opioid receptors
options.rationale: Increasing methadone worsens QT prolongation and overdose risk, particularly with co-ingested benzodiazepines; dose escalation is not a response to a prolonged QTc (Source: NGMA).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Cease methadone abruptly today and commence naltrexone after 24 hours of opioid abstinence
options.rationale: Abrupt methadone cessation precipitates withdrawal and relapse; naltrexone cannot be started until at least 7 to 10 days opioid-free and carries high relapse risk (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Add an oral beta-blocker (atenolol 25 mg daily) to reduce torsades de pointes risk acutely
options.rationale: Beta-blockers reduce risk in congenital long QT syndromes but are not the standard response to drug-induced QT prolongation; the correct approach is to identify and remove offending agents (Source: eTG Cardiovascular; AMH 2024).

explanation.summary: Methadone prolongs QTc and accumulates when CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, HIV PIs) are introduced; combined with benzodiazepines, overdose risk rises. Australian OST guidance recommends ECG before methadone if dose >100 mg or QT-prolonging meds, repeat at 1 month and after dose or interacting-drug changes (Source: NGMA).
key_points: QT-prolonging interactions: azoles, macrolides, ondansetron, SSRIs, antipsychotics, HIV PIs.
QTc >500 ms is a strong indication to switch from methadone to buprenorphine.
Concurrent benzodiazepines markedly increase overdose mortality.
Naloxone supply and overdose education are part of routine OST care.

sources.label: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

q.subtopic_detail: Methadone safety

q.stem: A 26-year-old man is brought to ED by police after acting bizarrely in a shopping centre, smashing a window and shouting at unseen individuals. He has used crystal methamphetamine for 18 months and admits to using last night. On arrival he is pacing, sweating, threatening staff verbally, and intermittently striking the wall. He has dilated pupils, marked tachycardia, hypertension and a low-grade fever. He has not had any seizures, no head injury, and there is no other identified medical contributor. He is on no regular medication. Initial verbal de-escalation has failed and he is now attempting to leave. Capillary glucose is 6.8 mmol/L. Bloods are pending. He has no known allergies The ED senior is choosing between intramuscular sedation, oral benzodiazepine alone, ketamine, IV haloperidol or physical restraint without medication. The patient weighs approximately 80 kg, has no airway concerns at present, and continuous cardiac monitoring can be commenced in the high-acuity area The on-call psychiatrist has been notified and is en route to assist with admission planning under the Mental Health Act if required after sedation The security team is also standing by.

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis, diaphoresis, agitated, hyper-vigilant, no focal neurology, no head injury, no obvious overdose features
data_table.Investigations: BSL 6.8 mmol/L, urine drug screen pending, ECG sinus tachycardia, QTc 430 ms

q.lead_in: What is the single best initial pharmacological management of his acute behavioural disturbance?

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg with cautious midazolam 2.5 to 5 mg titrated, in a monitored area
options.rationale: Australian ED guidance for severe stimulant-related acute behavioural disturbance recommends parenteral droperidol with cautious adjunctive midazolam in a monitored setting, reflecting NSW Health protocols; this rapidly controls agitation while minimising oversedation (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance; eTG Toxicology).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Oral diazepam 20 mg single dose, awaiting effect over the next 60 minutes
options.rationale: Oral medication is unsafe and impractical for severe acute behavioural disturbance with imminent harm; parenteral therapy in a monitored area is required (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Intramuscular ketamine 4 to 5 mg/kg as first-line for acute behavioural sedation
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for refractory cases when droperidol and benzodiazepine have failed, due to risks of laryngospasm, hypertension and need for airway support; it is not first-line (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline; eTG Toxicology).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg as a single dose without concurrent benzodiazepine
options.rationale: IV haloperidol carries higher risk of QT prolongation and torsades than IM droperidol and is not preferred in Australian ABD protocols; IM administration is also more deliverable in a non-cannulated agitated patient (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Physical restraint without any pharmacotherapy, awaiting natural resolution over hours
options.rationale: Prolonged physical restraint without sedation risks asphyxia, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis and sudden death, particularly in stimulant intoxication; sedation reduces these harms (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

explanation.summary: Severe acute behavioural disturbance from methamphetamine is best managed with parenteral droperidol and cautious midazolam in a monitored area, with planning for stimulant-associated hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis and cardiovascular complications (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline; eTG Toxicology).
key_points: Treat the patient, monitor airway/breathing/circulation continuously after sedation.
Cool, hydrate, assess for rhabdomyolysis (CK), check ECG and electrolytes.
Avoid pure beta-blockade for stimulant hypertension (unopposed alpha effect); use benzodiazepines first.
Methamphetamine-induced psychosis typically resolves within 1 week with abstinence; persistent symptoms warrant psychiatric review.

sources.label: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

q.subtopic_detail: Methamphetamine acute psychosis

q.stem: A 33-year-old woman is brought to ED by her partner. She has bipolar disorder and stopped lithium 3 weeks ago. Over the past 5 days she has slept 2 to 3 hours per night, has been spending savings on unnecessary purchases, and is now describing herself as 'chosen to save the city'. She has not slept in 36 hours and is pacing the ED. There is no current suicidal ideation or thoughts of harming others. She has no medical comorbidity, and a UDS and bloods are unremarkable. On MSE she is dressed flamboyantly, pressured in speech, with flight of ideas and grandiose delusions. She insists she is not unwell and refuses any treatment, including reinstating lithium. Her partner is present and supportive, and the community mental-health team has a current case manager Her partner reports she has previously responded to lithium and is keen to support any treatment, but he confirms she has not slept or eaten meaningfully for 48 hours. The ED team is considering involuntary detention and admission under the Mental Health Act for assessment and stabilisation The duty psychiatrist is on the way.

data_table.Examination: Elevated mood, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, no current SI/HI, no psychomotor retardation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH normal, urine drug screen negative, beta-hCG negative, lithium level <0.2 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Lithium ceased 3 weeks ago by patient; no current medications

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007 (or equivalent state Act)?

options.text: Detain under the MHA assessment schedule and admit to a declared mental health facility for review
options.rationale: She meets criteria as a mentally ill person (presence of mental illness, behaviour requires treatment to protect her from serious harm including risk to her health/safety such as exhaustion, financial harm) and is refusing voluntary admission; the MHA assessment schedule and admission to a declared facility for AMO review is the appropriate pathway (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007; RANZCP).

options.text: Discharge home with her partner and arrange outpatient review with the case manager tomorrow
options.rationale: Severe sleep deprivation, escalating manic behaviour and refusal of treatment make outpatient management unsafe in the next 24 hours; risk of harm to self (exhaustion, financial, behavioural) meets MHA criteria (Source: NSW MHA; RANZCP).

options.text: Apply common-law restraint and sedate her in the ED without using the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Common-law restraint is for emergency intervention to prevent immediate harm and is not appropriate as the primary framework for ongoing detention and treatment of mental illness; the MHA provides the lawful framework (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007).

options.text: Make a community treatment order today without proceeding to any inpatient admission
options.rationale: Community treatment orders are made by the Mental Health Review Tribunal following inpatient assessment and require specific procedural steps; they cannot be made unilaterally in ED at first presentation (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007).

options.text: Document her refusal of care and allow discharge from ED against medical advice
options.rationale: She lacks capacity to refuse treatment for her mental illness given her grandiose delusions and lack of insight, and the MHA exists precisely to address this situation; documenting AMA discharge is inappropriate (Source: NSW MHA; RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Acute mania with refusal of treatment and risk to self meets criteria for involuntary admission under state MHAs. The least-restrictive principle still applies, but inpatient assessment is required before any community treatment order can be considered (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007).
key_points: Schedules/forms vary by state but principles are similar: presence of mental illness, risk of serious harm, no less restrictive option.
Common-law restraint is for immediate, brief safety - not ongoing treatment.
Community treatment orders follow tribunal processes after inpatient assessment.
Capacity is decision-specific and is impaired by acute mania with grandiose delusions.

q.subtopic_detail: Mental Health Act framework

q.stem: A 64-year-old man admitted 3 days ago for orthopaedic surgery has not received scheduled benzodiazepine prophylaxis despite his documented daily alcohol intake of approximately 20 standard drinks. Last drink was 72 hours ago. He is now confused, agitated, picking at his bedding, and describing animals running across the ceiling. He has had two witnessed brief generalised tonic-clonic seizures in the past 4 hours. He is febrile, tachycardic, hypertensive, and diaphoretic. CIWA-Ar is now uninterpretable due to confusion. He has a wound dressing intact, no neurological focal deficits, and CT brain is unremarkable. He has received total diazepam 30 mg over the past 6 hours with limited effect. He has known cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B), albumin 28, INR 1.6, AST 130, ALT 70, platelets 95 The medical registrar is concerned about benzodiazepine accumulation given his cirrhosis, and is now considering escalation to a critical-care environment for safer titration and airway support if needed. The pharmacist confirms lorazepam is available and is preferred in significant hepatic impairment because it has no active metabolites His electrolytes are pending and a magnesium level is being processed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 140, BP 174/100, RR 28, SpO2 95% on room air
data_table.Examination: Confused, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), tremulous, diaphoretic, visual hallucinations, no focal neurology, post-op wound dressed
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 3.0, Mg 0.58, glucose 5.6, lactate 3.4, INR 1.6, albumin 28, AST 130, ALT 70, platelets 95, CT brain unremarkable
data_table.Medications: Diazepam total 30 mg PO over 6 hours, IV Pabrinex 2 pairs commenced

q.lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Transfer to ICU or HDU, escalate to IV diazepam (or lorazepam if hepatic impairment), correct electrolytes
options.rationale: Delirium tremens with refractory agitation and seizures is a medical emergency requiring high-acuity care; in significant hepatic impairment, lorazepam (less hepatic metabolism, no active metabolites) is preferred over diazepam, with aggressive electrolyte correction (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction; eTG Critical Care).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Continue oral diazepam, increase the frequency and reassess his condition in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral diazepam in a confused, seizing, vomiting patient is unreliable and inappropriate; this severity of DT requires parenteral therapy and high-acuity monitoring (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 10 mg as the primary treatment for delirium tremens
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower the seizure threshold and do not treat the underlying GABA deficit; benzodiazepines are first-line in DT, with low-dose antipsychotic only as an adjunct for severe hallucinations after benzodiazepines are optimised (Source: eTG Addiction; NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Load with intravenous phenytoin 1 g as primary anti-seizure prophylaxis tonight
options.rationale: Phenytoin is ineffective for alcohol-withdrawal seizures, which are GABA-mediated; benzodiazepines are both the treatment and the prophylaxis of choice (Source: eTG Neurology; eTG Addiction).

options.text: Administer intramuscular naloxone empirically in case of unrecognised opioid involvement
options.rationale: Naloxone has no role in alcohol withdrawal; it is an opioid antagonist for opioid overdose and is irrelevant in this scenario (Source: eTG Toxicology).

explanation.summary: Delirium tremens has a mortality of 5 to 15% untreated. Australian management is escalation to high-acuity care, parenteral benzodiazepines (lorazepam preferred in significant hepatic impairment because of pharmacokinetics), and aggressive electrolyte correction with high-dose B-complex (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines).
key_points: DT onset 48 to 96 hours after last drink; mortality without treatment up to 15%.
Lorazepam is preferred over diazepam in significant hepatic dysfunction.
Always replace thiamine before/with glucose and correct magnesium.
Antipsychotics only as adjunct for severe hallucinations after benzodiazepine optimisation.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

q.subtopic_detail: Delirium tremens

q.stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 with a confirmed MCDA twin pregnancy at 19 weeks and 5 days attends fortnightly twin surveillance. Booking was uneventful. The 16-week scan showed concordant growth, normal liquor in both sacs, and no membrane folding. Today's TAS and TVS demonstrate twin 1 (presumed donor) with EFW on the 22nd centile, deepest vertical pocket (DVP) of 1.6 cm, and the bladder not visualised on three sweeps over 30 minutes. Twin 2 (presumed recipient) has EFW on the 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, distended bladder, and a normal middle cerebral artery PSV. Both umbilical artery Dopplers are within normal limits and both cardiac four-chamber views appear structurally normal. The woman is well, has no contractions, and her cervix is 38 mm on TVS. She has had no episodes of vaginal bleeding, no abdominal pain, no rupture of membranes and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. Her booking BMI was 26 and her booking bloods including FBC, group and antibody screen and infection screen were unremarkable. The tertiary foetal medicine service is two hours away by road and offers fetoscopic laser ablation on a near-daily basis with experienced operators. She has expressed a strong preference for any intervention that maximises the chance of two healthy survivors.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus appropriate for 24 weeks size
data_table.Investigations: Twin 1: EFW 22nd centile, DVP 1.6 cm, bladder not seen. Twin 2: EFW 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, bladder distended, MCA-PSV normal. UA Dopplers normal both twins. Cervix 38 mm closed.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassurance only and routine fortnightly twin surveillance scan in two weeks
options.rationale: Discordant DVP with oligohydramnios in one sac and polyhydramnios in the other meets criteria for TTTS; routine surveillance alone misses the treatment window per RANZCOG.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Urgent referral to a foetal medicine unit for fetoscopic laser ablation assessment
options.rationale: DVP under 2 cm in one twin with DVP over 8 cm in the co-twin before 20 weeks defines Quintero stage I TTTS; fetoscopic laser is first-line for stage II or higher and stage I with progression, ideally before 26 weeks.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy
NICE NG137: Twin and triplet pregnancy

options.text: Therapeutic amnioreduction of the recipient sac in the local maternity unit
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is inferior to laser for stages II to IV before 26 weeks per the Eurofoetus trial; it is used only when laser is unavailable or contraindicated, and not in a local unit.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Admit for antenatal betamethasone and plan to deliver this current week
options.rationale: Delivery at 19+5 is non-viable at most Australian centres and would not treat the underlying placental shunting; corticosteroids are not indicated this early.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by cord occlusion
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for cases such as sIUGR with imminent demise threatening the co-twin, not for stage I TTTS where laser preserves both twins.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: TTTS complicates around 10 to 15 percent of MCDA pregnancies and is diagnosed by discordant amniotic fluid (DVP under 2 and over 8 cm) before 20 weeks. Fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses is first-line.
key_points: Fortnightly TVS and US from 16 weeks for MCDA (RANZCOG)
Quintero staging: I fluid only; II absent bladder donor; III abnormal Dopplers; IV hydrops; V demise
Laser ablation reduces perinatal death and neurological injury vs amnioreduction
explanation.pearls: DVP, not AFI, is the standard discordance measure in twins.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy
NICE NG137: Twin and triplet pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: TTTS - screening and recognition

q.stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 with an MCDA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 26 weeks. Booking, anatomy scan, and TTTS surveillance to date have been unremarkable. Today the smaller twin has an EFW on the 4th centile with a 28 percent inter-twin weight discordance. The larger twin tracks the 48th centile. Amniotic fluid is normal in both sacs. Umbilical artery Doppler of the smaller twin shows intermittently absent end-diastolic flow that alternates with normal forward flow within a single tracing; the larger twin's UA Doppler is normal. Both ductus venosus a-waves are positive. The cardiotocograph of both twins is reactive. The woman is well with a closed cervix of 34 mm. The pregnancy was spontaneously conceived; she has had no episodes of vaginal bleeding, no rupture of membranes, no contractions and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. Booking BMI was 24, booking bloods were unremarkable and she has had no infections during the pregnancy. The local maternity unit has a tertiary neonatal service on site with experienced obstetric and foetal-medicine input available daily. She has had no abdominal pain and no recent infections of any kind.

data_table.Investigations: Smaller twin EFW 4th centile, discordance 28%, UA Doppler intermittent AEDF (Type III). Larger twin EFW 48th centile, UA Doppler normal. DV a-waves positive both. CTG reactive both. Cervix 34 mm.

q.lead_in: Which classification and surveillance plan best fits this pattern?

options.text: Type I sIUGR; fortnightly growth scans, aim for planned delivery at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Type I has persistently positive UA end-diastolic flow with good prognosis; the intermittent AEDF here is Type III, not Type I, per Gratacos classification.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Type II sIUGR; weekly Doppler and CTG surveillance, plan delivery at 32 weeks
options.rationale: Type II has persistently absent or reversed UA flow with progressive deterioration; the intermittent pattern here is Type III.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Type III sIUGR; twice-weekly Doppler with corticosteroid cover and delivery at 32 to 34 weeks
options.rationale: Intermittent AEDF in the smaller MCDA twin defines Type III, which carries unpredictable risk of sudden co-twin demise from large anastomoses; close surveillance and planned preterm delivery are recommended.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Discordant growth without sIUGR; reassure the woman and re-scan in 2 weeks
options.rationale: EFW under the 10th centile in either twin with discordance over 25 percent meets the consensus definition of sIUGR, so this is not benign discordance.

options.text: Stage I twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome; refer urgently for fetoscopic laser ablation now
options.rationale: TTTS requires discordant fluid (DVP under 2 and over 8 cm); fluid is normal in both sacs here, so this is sIUGR rather than TTTS.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: sIUGR in MCDA twins is classified by UA Doppler: Type I positive EDF, Type II persistent AEDF or REDF, Type III intermittent AEDF. Type III carries a particular risk of unexpected single-twin demise.
key_points: Definition: EFW under 10th centile in one twin or inter-twin discordance over 25% (ISUOG)
MCDA Type III: large arterio-arterial anastomoses cause unpredictable shunt reversal
Plan delivery 32 to 34 weeks with corticosteroids per RANZCOG
explanation.pearls: Co-twin demise risk and neurological injury risk rise sharply in MCDA Type III sIUGR.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: Selective IUGR in monochorionic twins

q.stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 presents to the birth suite with regular painful contractions every 4 minutes for the past 2 hours. She has had no bleeding, no rupture of membranes, and no fever. Her booking and pregnancy have been uncomplicated. She has well-controlled chronic hypertension on labetalol 200 mg BD with booking BP averaging 130/82 and today 134/86. She has known mild aortic stenosis (peak gradient 22 mmHg, asymptomatic) and is followed by cardiology. Examination shows a soft tender uterus with palpable tightenings, cephalic presentation, and the cervix is 2 cm dilated and 50 percent effaced on speculum. Foetal heart is reassuring. Foetal fibronectin is not available at this hour. The plan is to give corticosteroids and MgSO4 for neuroprotection and transfer to a tertiary unit. She has had no antepartum haemorrhage and her booking infection screen was unremarkable. The neonatal team at the tertiary unit has confirmed cot availability; estimated transfer time by road is 90 minutes. Indomethacin and prostaglandin synthetase inhibitors are stocked at the local unit, as are atosiban and nifedipine. She has no contraindication to magnesium sulfate or to any tocolytic listed.

data_table.Examination: Uterus contracting q4min, cephalic, cervix 2 cm 50% effaced, membranes intact
data_table.Investigations: Foetal heart reassuring, MSU pending, FBC and UEC normal

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate tocolytic to commence while corticosteroids and transfer are organised?

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release as calcium-channel blocker tocolysis now
options.rationale: Nifedipine is a calcium channel blocker; combining with antihypertensives such as labetalol increases the risk of severe maternal hypotension and is relatively contraindicated per Therapeutic Guidelines.

options.text: Intravenous atosiban infusion as oxytocin-receptor antagonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: Atosiban is the preferred tocolytic when there are maternal cardiovascular concerns, is effective and has a favourable maternal safety profile vs nifedipine, and avoids the hypotensive interaction with antihypertensives.

options.text: Oral indomethacin 50 mg loading dose then 25 mg six-hourly
options.rationale: Indomethacin is generally avoided after 32 weeks (ductus arteriosus constriction, oligohydramnios) and is not first-line; at 28+3 it can be considered but is not preferred over atosiban here.

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion used primarily as tocolysis
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is not recommended as a tocolytic; it is used for foetal neuroprotection at gestations under 30 weeks (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for preventing preterm birth

options.text: Intravenous salbutamol infusion as beta-2 agonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: Beta-agonists are no longer recommended for tocolysis in Australia because of maternal cardiovascular and metabolic adverse effects.

explanation.summary: Tocolysis aims to delay birth for 48 hours to allow corticosteroid effect and in-utero transfer. Nifedipine and atosiban are equally effective overall, but choice depends on maternal comorbidity. Atosiban is preferred over nifedipine where there are cardiovascular concerns or concurrent antihypertensives.
key_points: First-line: nifedipine or atosiban (RANZCOG, eTG)
Atosiban preferred where antihypertensives, cardiac disease, or hypovolaemia
Indomethacin: avoid after 32 weeks (ductal constriction)
Beta-agonists no longer routinely used
explanation.pearls: Tocolysis is only justified when corticosteroids or transfer will meaningfully change neonatal outcome.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for preventing preterm birth

q.subtopic_detail: Preterm labour - tocolysis choice

q.stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 31+5 presents with threatened preterm labour. She had a complete course of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart at 26+0 for an earlier admission with threatened preterm labour that subsequently settled. Today she is contracting every 3 minutes, has a cervix 3 cm dilated and 70 percent effaced on speculum, and a foetal fibronectin from 4 weeks ago was positive. The CTG is reactive. The team plans to start tocolysis, transfer to a tertiary unit, and give magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection. She has no other comorbidities. She has no rupture of membranes, no bleeding, no fever and no offensive vaginal discharge. Her observations are normal and a recent maternal infection screen is unremarkable. The tertiary unit she is being transferred to has confirmed cot availability; estimated road transfer time is 75 minutes. The team is making a decision about further antenatal corticosteroid dosing, aware that her first course finished more than 7 days ago and that the cervix is now actively dilating. She has no other obstetric complication at present and her foetal observations remain reassuring.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm/70% effaced, cephalic, contractions q3min
data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive, FBC and UEC normal

q.lead_in: Regarding antenatal corticosteroids, which is most appropriate now?

options.text: Give a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM now
options.rationale: RANZCOG supports a single rescue dose when the initial course was given over 7 days ago, gestation is under 34+0, and birth is anticipated within the next 7 days.

options.text: Give a full repeat course of betamethasone (two doses 24 h apart)
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends a single rescue dose rather than a full repeat course; multiple repeats are associated with reduced birth weight.

options.text: Do not give further corticosteroids as one course is sufficient lifetime exposure
options.rationale: A single rescue dose is supported when birth is now imminent and the prior course was over 7 days ago; outright omission misses meaningful neonatal benefit.

options.text: Give dexamethasone 6 mg IM as a single rescue dose
options.rationale: The dose of dexamethasone in Australian practice is 12 mg IM per dose, not 6 mg; a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM is preferred per RANZCOG.

options.text: Withhold steroids until cervix is more than 5 cm dilated
options.rationale: Steroids are given before imminent birth, not delayed until advanced dilation, because the foetal benefit takes hours to develop.

explanation.summary: Rescue dosing is a single 11.4 mg betamethasone (or 12 mg dexamethasone) given before 34+0 when the original course was over 7 days previously and birth is anticipated within 7 days.
key_points: Rescue: single dose, not a repeat full course (RANZCOG)
Eligibility: prior course >7 days ago, gestation <34+0, birth likely within 7 days
Multiple repeats associated with lower birthweight
explanation.pearls: Document time of every steroid dose precisely; it drives later rescue decisions.

q.subtopic_detail: Antenatal corticosteroids - rescue dosing

q.stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 28+2 presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid 2 hours ago, followed by ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, no abdominal tenderness, and the foetal heart is reassuring. Speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix and the cervix appears closed. There is no offensive odour. Maternal observations are normal. Her GBS status from a 26-week swab is negative. She is admitted for expectant management with corticosteroids, MgSO4, and close maternal-foetal surveillance. She has no abdominal pain, no contractions, no foetal heart abnormality on ongoing CTG and no offensive vaginal discharge. The amniotic fluid pool is clear and not bloodstained. Maternal infection screen and FBC are unremarkable, and she has no allergies to penicillins or macrolides. The plan is for inpatient expectant management to at least 34 weeks if she remains stable, with corticosteroids, magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection and antibiotic prophylaxis to prolong latency in keeping with current RANZCOG and Royal Women's PPROM guidance. She has no contractions and no signs of clinical chorioamnionitis on serial review. Erythromycin and amoxicillin-clavulanate are both stocked at the local maternity unit.

data_table.Examination: Pool of clear AF on speculum, cervix closed, no contractions, abdomen non-tender, FH reassuring
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, CRP 6, MSU pending, GBS negative at 26 weeks

q.lead_in: Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate to prolong latency?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the ORACLE I evidence support oral erythromycin in PPROM to prolong latency and reduce neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased necrotising enterocolitis risk.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is associated with an increased rate of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis in the ORACLE I trial and is not recommended for PPROM.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g six-hourly for 48 hours of treatment only
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin is GBS intrapartum prophylaxis, not a latency regimen; alone it does not provide the prolongation seen with erythromycin.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g as a single stat dose for latency prolongation
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is used as adjunct prophylaxis at caesarean in some settings, not as the latency regimen of choice for PPROM.

options.text: No antibiotic prophylaxis unless overt chorioamnionitis later develops in this patient
options.rationale: Latency antibiotics are routinely given in PPROM under 34 weeks to prolong pregnancy and reduce neonatal infection independent of clinical chorioamnionitis.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: Erythromycin is the latency antibiotic of choice in PPROM under 34 weeks. Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated due to the NEC signal in ORACLE I. Antibiotics complement corticosteroids and MgSO4 in expectant PPROM management.
key_points: Erythromycin 250 mg QID x 10 days or until birth (RANZCOG, eTG)
Avoid co-amoxiclav (NEC risk)
Add GBS intrapartum prophylaxis once labour established
Monitor for chorioamnionitis: fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, FHR
explanation.pearls: Erythromycin allergy: clarithromycin is a reasonable alternative; check with senior.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: PPROM - latency antibiotics

q.stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 36+5 presents 3 hours after a gush of clear fluid. She has no contractions, the foetal heart is reassuring, and she is afebrile. Speculum confirms PROM with a pool of clear fluid; the cervix appears favourable. She is GBS positive on a 36-week swab. Her last birth was a spontaneous vaginal birth at term without complication. She is otherwise well and has no allergies. The unit's neonatal services routinely accept babies from 35 weeks. She has no contractions, no abdominal pain, no fever and the foetal heart is reassuring on continuous CTG. Maternal observations and a recent FBC are unremarkable. She has no penicillin allergy. The neonatal unit on site routinely accepts babies from 35 weeks and there is no logistic barrier to immediate induction of labour. The team is weighing immediate induction against short-term expectant management in the setting of PPROM beyond 36 weeks with confirmed group B streptococcal colonisation on her recent vaginal-rectal swab. She has been counselled about both immediate induction and short-term expectant management options. Induction of labour and intrapartum GBS prophylaxis can be commenced today.

data_table.Examination: Pool of clear AF, cervix favourable, no contractions, FH reassuring
data_table.Investigations: GBS positive at 36 weeks, FBC normal, CRP normal

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Plan induction of labour now with intrapartum benzylpenicillin for GBS
options.rationale: PPROM at or beyond 36+0 with a GBS-positive mother is managed by induction rather than expectant care to limit chorioamnionitis and neonatal early-onset GBS sepsis (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Expectant management at home for 96 hours with daily review
options.rationale: Outpatient expectant management at near-term PPROM with GBS positivity is unsafe due to early-onset neonatal sepsis risk and inability to start timely intrapartum prophylaxis.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine and a course of corticosteroids
options.rationale: At 36+5 corticosteroids are not routinely indicated and tocolysis would prolong rupture of membranes without benefit while increasing infection risk.

options.text: Caesarean section now due to PPROM beyond 36 weeks
options.rationale: PPROM at this gestation is not itself an indication for caesarean; vaginal birth after induction is preferred when there are no other contraindications.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Expectant management as an inpatient until 39+0 weeks
options.rationale: Delaying birth to 39 weeks with PPROM and GBS positivity is contrary to RANZCOG guidance and significantly raises infectious morbidity.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: At or beyond 36+0 with PPROM, prompt induction with appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (penicillin if GBS positive) is preferred to expectant management to reduce neonatal sepsis and chorioamnionitis.
key_points: PPROM 36+0 or more: plan induction rather than expectant care (RANZCOG)
Use benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV q4h intrapartum for GBS positivity
Steroids not routinely indicated 35+0 or more in Australia
explanation.pearls: Time of rupture is the clock that drives chorioamnionitis risk and antibiotic decisions.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: PPROM near term - expectant vs delivery

q.stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 attends preconception clinic having recently had a third painless mid-trimester loss at 19 weeks. The two prior losses were at 18 and 20 weeks; on each occasion she presented with bulging membranes and minimal contractions, and the cervix was widely dilated on speculum. Anatomy scans had been normal, there was no antepartum bleeding, and she has had no infections or instrumentation between pregnancies. She has no chronic medical conditions and takes only folate and iodine. Her last cervical screening is up to date and normal. She is keen to conceive again and asks how the next pregnancy will be managed. A detailed review of each loss documented painless cervical dilation as the dominant clinical feature, with rapid progression to delivery once membranes were exposed. There was no antepartum haemorrhage, no abruption, no clinical chorioamnionitis and no documented infection at the time of any of the losses. She has had no cervical surgery or LLETZ, no congenital uterine anomaly on previous imaging and no connective tissue disease. She has been counselled about the recurrence risk after three painless mid-trimester losses.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no abnormalities
data_table.Investigations: Previous losses at 18, 20, 19 weeks; anatomy scans normal; no infection or instrumentation

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for her next pregnancy?

options.text: History-indicated cervical cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Three or more prior second-trimester losses or preterm births consistent with cervical insufficiency is the classic indication for history-indicated (elective) cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks per RANZCOG.

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly from 16 weeks without cerclage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is offered for short cervix on TVS but is not the standard for women with three prior second-trimester losses; cerclage is preferred in this history.

options.text: Serial cervical length scans from 16 weeks with rescue cerclage if shortening
options.rationale: Ultrasound-indicated (and rescue) cerclage are reasonable for women with one prior preterm birth, not three losses; elective cerclage outperforms watchful waiting here.

options.text: Pre-conception transabdominal cervical cerclage placement before next pregnancy attempt
options.rationale: Trans-abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or anatomical contraindication to vaginal cerclage, not first-line.

options.text: Insertion of cervical pessary at 14 to 16 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Pessary evidence is mixed and not first-line in Australia for women with this history; transvaginal cerclage is the standard.

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency presents as painless mid-trimester loss with dilation and bulging membranes. Three or more such losses warrant history-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks in the next pregnancy, with subsequent cervical length surveillance.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: 3+ prior mid-trimester losses / early PTB
Ultrasound-indicated cerclage: 1+ prior PTB and TVS cervix <25 mm before 24 weeks
Rescue cerclage: cervix dilated with intact membranes <24 weeks
explanation.pearls: Always exclude acute infection and contractions before placing rescue cerclage.

q.subtopic_detail: Cervical insufficiency - cerclage

q.stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 at 37+1 with well-controlled gestational diabetes on diet alone is reviewed at her routine antenatal clinic. Foetal growth is on the 50th centile, amniotic fluid is normal, CTG is reactive, and self-monitored capillary glucose has stayed within target. She has no hypertension, no IUGR, and no other complication. She has booked into a private hospital and asks whether elective induction at 37 to 38 weeks would be safer than waiting. She has no history of stillbirth or previous large-for-gestational-age infant, and no obstetric or medical indication for early delivery. Her ADIPS-aligned OGTT thresholds were borderline at booking but glycaemic control has been excellent on diet alone, with all fasting glucose values below 5.1 mmol/L and post-prandial values below 7.0 mmol/L. The unit follows ADIPS and RANZCOG guidance which does not recommend routine early delivery before 39 weeks in well-controlled diet-managed GDM with normal foetal surveillance. She has been counselled about the lack of evidence supporting routine early delivery in well-controlled diet-managed GDM. She has been counselled about RANZCOG and ADIPS timing recommendations. Her partner is supportive of expectant management.

data_table.Examination: Cephalic, FH reassuring
data_table.Investigations: EFW 50th centile, AFI normal, BSL diary within target

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care with planned delivery around 40+0 to 40+6 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS and RANZCOG support birth by around 40+6 for diet-controlled GDM with no complications; routine early term induction does not improve outcomes and increases neonatal respiratory morbidity.

options.text: Elective induction of labour at 37+0 weeks gestation now
options.rationale: Routine induction at 37+0 in well-controlled diet-only GDM is not recommended; it increases iatrogenic late preterm morbidity without clear benefit.

options.text: Elective lower segment caesarean section at 38+0 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Elective caesarean is not indicated in well-controlled diet-only GDM without other obstetric reasons.

options.text: Planned induction at 39+0 with cervical ripening regardless of Bishop
options.rationale: Insulin-treated or complicated GDM may warrant earlier induction; for diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM, ADIPS supports expectant management to around 40+6.

options.text: Defer delivery until 41+5 weeks with daily CTG from 39 weeks
options.rationale: Even uncomplicated GDM is generally not allowed to progress beyond 40+6; deferring to 41+5 increases stillbirth risk above the accepted threshold.

explanation.summary: ADIPS guidance supports delivery by around 40+6 in uncomplicated diet-controlled GDM. Earlier elective birth is reserved for insulin treatment, macrosomia, poor control, or other complications. Late preterm and early term iatrogenic births carry meaningful neonatal respiratory morbidity.
key_points: Diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM: birth by ~40+6 (ADIPS)
Insulin-treated GDM: consider birth 38+0 to 39+0
Complicated GDM (poor control, IUGR, hypertension): individualise earlier
explanation.pearls: Avoid elective non-medical birth before 39+0 due to neonatal respiratory morbidity.

q.subtopic_detail: Late preterm / early term decisions

q.stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 29+0 is on day 9 of expectant PPROM management. She completed corticosteroids 7 days ago and erythromycin is ongoing. She has been afebrile with normal observations until this evening, when she reports increased uterine tenderness and chills. Observations: T 38.4, HR 118, BP 108/64, RR 18, SpO2 98 on RA. Her uterus is tender on palpation. CTG shows a baseline foetal heart rate of 175 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. WCC has risen to 18.6 x10^9/L, neutrophils 16.2 x10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L (from 18 mg/L two days ago). She has no offensive vaginal discharge but the speculum view shows turbid amniotic fluid pooling. Maternal lactate is 2.1 mmol/L. There are no other localising features of infection. She has no flank pain, no dysuria, no respiratory symptoms and no cellulitic or wound features on examination. A FAST scan of the abdomen is unremarkable and her venous blood gas shows pH 7.36, lactate 2.1 mmol/L, base excess -2 and normal electrolytes. The CTG over the past 30 minutes has been persistent foetal tachycardia at 170 to 180 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. Her tertiary unit has a paediatric retrieval team on standby. The team is balancing the risks of continued expectancy at this previable-leaning gestation against the strong clinical picture of clinical chorioamnionitis and early sepsis with foetal compromise.

data_table.Examination: Uterine tenderness, turbid AF on speculum, no other localising findings
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.6, neutrophils 16.2, CRP 92 (up from 18), lactate 2.1, MSU pending, CTG: baseline 175, reduced variability

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and expedite delivery now
options.rationale: Clinical chorioamnionitis in PPROM at 29 weeks is an indication for prompt delivery alongside IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, irrespective of gestation, per RANZCOG.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Continue expectant management with IV antibiotics, aim to reach 32 weeks
options.rationale: Expectant management is contraindicated once chorioamnionitis is established because of maternal sepsis and foetal injury risk; prolonging pregnancy is unsafe.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Repeat antenatal steroids and reassess clinical status in 12 hours
options.rationale: Delaying delivery for steroids during overt intra-amniotic infection raises sepsis risk; the foetus is already affected and steroids will not change the trajectory in time.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Add tocolysis to allow safe transfer to a tertiary unit
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in chorioamnionitis because it delays definitive treatment (delivery) and masks deterioration.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Amniocentesis for Gram stain and glucose before any decision
options.rationale: With clear clinical chorioamnionitis (fever, maternal and foetal tachycardia, tender uterus, rising CRP, turbid AF), delaying for amniocentesis is unnecessary and unsafe.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: Clinical chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, maternal tachycardia, foetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul or turbid fluid, leukocytosis) ends expectant PPROM management. Treatment is IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and prompt delivery regardless of gestation.
key_points: Triad-plus signs of chorioamnionitis = deliver (RANZCOG, eTG)
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics started immediately on suspicion
Mode of birth depends on obstetric factors; chorioamnionitis itself is not a caesarean indication
Continue antibiotics intrapartum and postpartum until afebrile
explanation.pearls: Foetal tachycardia is often the earliest objective sign of intra-amniotic infection.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

q.subtopic_detail: Chorioamnionitis in PPROM - delivery trigger

q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with an MCDA twin pregnancy at 27+4 presents with reduced foetal movements over 24 hours. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated; serial scans have shown concordant growth and no TTTS. Today's ultrasound confirms intrauterine demise of twin A with absent cardiac activity and small pericardial effusion. Twin B has a foetal heart rate of 148 bpm with reactive CTG, normal amniotic fluid, normal umbilical artery Doppler, and a peak systolic velocity in the middle cerebral artery of 1.8 multiples of the median. Maternal observations and coagulation profile are normal. She has no contractions and the cervix is 35 mm closed on TVS. She has had no vaginal bleeding and no rupture of membranes. Her platelet count, INR, APTT and fibrinogen are within normal limits and a repeat coagulation profile in 6 hours is planned. The MCA peak systolic velocity of 1.8 multiples of the median in the surviving twin exceeds the 1.5 MoM threshold widely used to suggest moderate to severe foetal anaemia, raising concern for an acute feto-foetal transfusion event at the time of co-twin demise in this monochorionic pregnancy. The tertiary foetal medicine service two hours away offers ultrasound-guided intrauterine transfusion of the surviving twin with experienced operators available on a same-day basis. She has been counselled on the risks of acute feto-foetal transfusion at co-twin demise.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, no contractions, no PV bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Twin A demised. Twin B: HR 148, reactive CTG, UA Doppler normal, MCA-PSV 1.8 MoM. Maternal coags normal. Cervix 35 mm.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Urgent transfer to foetal medicine for assessment for foetal anaemia
options.rationale: MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM in the surviving twin after MC co-twin demise suggests acute foetal anaemia from feto-foetal transfusion across shared anastomoses; intrauterine transfusion may reduce neurological injury (RANZCOG, ISUOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Immediate lower segment caesarean section of the surviving twin at this gestation
options.rationale: Iatrogenic birth at 27+4 inflicts severe prematurity morbidity and does not reverse hypotensive injury already sustained; intrauterine resuscitation is preferred first.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Expectant management with weekly outpatient Doppler surveillance of the surviving twin
options.rationale: MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM is a red flag for foetal anaemia and requires urgent tertiary assessment; weekly outpatient surveillance is too slow.

options.text: Commence maternal intravenous heparin to prevent disseminated intravascular coagulation in mother
options.rationale: Maternal coagulopathy after single twin demise is rare in the short term; this woman has normal coagulation and prophylactic heparin is not indicated.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the surviving twin by ultrasound-guided cord occlusion
options.rationale: Termination of the surviving healthy twin is not appropriate; the goal is to assess and rescue the survivor.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: After single twin demise in a monochorionic pregnancy, acute feto-foetal transfusion via placental anastomoses can cause severe anaemia in the survivor. MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM signals foetal anaemia and warrants urgent foetal medicine assessment for in-utero transfusion. Iatrogenic preterm birth at 27 weeks rarely improves outcome and adds severe prematurity morbidity.
key_points: Surviving MC twin: ~15% risk of death, ~25% risk of neurological injury
MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM = significant risk of foetal anaemia (ISUOG)
Urgent neuroimaging (MRI) 4 to 6 weeks later if survival
Birth timing individualised; not routinely immediate
explanation.pearls: MCA-PSV is the same screening tool used in red cell alloimmunisation; same threshold.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: Co-twin demise in monochorionic pregnancy

q.stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman, 4 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, was diagnosed with right-breast bacterial mastitis 5 days ago and commenced flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily with continued feeding and expression. Her fevers settled within 48 hours but the erythematous area has now developed a discrete, fluctuant tender lump over the past 24 hours despite ongoing antibiotics and effective drainage. She is afebrile today, haemodynamically stable, and the skin overlying the lump is intact, non-necrotic and not under tension. Bedside ultrasound performed by the GP demonstrates a well-defined, unilocular hypoechoic collection measuring 38 mm x 24 mm at 1.5 cm depth, with no overlying skin necrosis. She has no recurrent abscess history, no immunosuppression, no diabetes and wishes to continue breastfeeding. She is comfortable being managed in primary care if the lesion is amenable to office-based drainage and the local Breast Centre offers ultrasound-guided procedures on a same-day basis. She has no recent travel, no recent skin lesions and no flu-like systemic symptoms. The Breast Centre has confirmed availability for ultrasound-guided aspiration the same afternoon if requested. She has been counselled about office-based ultrasound-guided aspiration as the preferred first-line drainage option.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 84 bpm, BP 122/76 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Right breast, 4 cm fluctuant tender mass, overlying skin intact and non-necrotic, no axillary adenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: unilocular 38 x 24 mm hypoechoic collection, 1.5 cm depth, no skin necrosis. Hb 124 g/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, CRP 48 mg/L.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current oral flucloxacillin and review in 5 days without drainage
options.rationale: A drained collection requires drainage to resolve; antibiotics alone are inadequate and risk chronicity, fistula and lactiferous duct injury (ABM Clinical Protocol 36).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with culture; continue antibiotics, reaspirate if needed
options.rationale: For a lactational abscess under approximately 5 cm with intact non-necrotic skin, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration (often serial) with cultured aspirate and continued antibiotics is first-line; it preserves duct architecture, allows ongoing breastfeeding and avoids a surgical wound that complicates feeding (ABM; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Refer for theatre incision and drainage with packing under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Surgical I&D is reserved for very large abscesses, multiloculated collections, or where the skin is thinned/necrotic; it leaves a wound that may interfere with feeding and is unnecessary for a 4 cm unilocular collection (ABM).

options.text: Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast and continue antibiotics only
options.rationale: Cessation worsens milk stasis and slows resolution; current guidance is to continue feeding from both breasts where the infant will latch, or to express if the infant refuses (ABA).

options.text: Switch to oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily and observe without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic broadening without drainage is inadequate; an established collection needs source control (ABM).

explanation.summary: Lactational breast abscesses require source control. Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with cultured aspirate is first-line for abscesses up to around 5 cm with intact skin and is repeated as needed; surgical drainage is reserved for larger, multiloculated, or skin-compromised collections.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding throughout treatment
USS-guided aspiration preserves duct anatomy
Send aspirate for culture to guide ongoing antibiotics
Surgical I&D for large, multiloculated, or skin-necrosed abscesses
Antibiotics alone are inadequate once a collection has formed
explanation.pearls: An apparently improving mastitis that develops a new fluctuant area at day 5 is an abscess until ultrasound proves otherwise.

q.subtopic_detail: Lactational breast abscess - drainage strategy

q.stem: A 34-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP at 8 weeks postpartum with a 4-week history of low mood, anhedonia, marked early-morning waking despite the infant sleeping through, ruminative guilt about her mothering and a recently-completed EPDS score of 17 with positive endorsement of item 10 (passive death ideation, no plan, no intent, no access). Risk assessment with the GP confirms no active suicidal plan, no psychotic features and no infanticidal thoughts. She has previously had an episode of moderate depression at age 24 that responded well to sertraline, which she continued for 12 months without significant adverse effects, then ceased without recurrence. She is breastfeeding her healthy term infant exclusively, the infant is thriving on the 50th centile, and she wishes to continue breastfeeding. She has no other medications, no medical comorbidities, no QT-prolonging drugs and a normal ECG taken antenatally. She is open to medication alongside psychological therapy and is already on a perinatal CBT waitlist with a likely 4-week wait. She has no family history of bipolar disorder, no postpartum psychosis history and her partner is supportive at home. She is keen to start treatment quickly.

data_table.Mood: EPDS 17. Item 10 positive (passive ideation, no plan).
data_table.History: Prior MDE responsive to sertraline, no other psychiatric history.
data_table.Infant: Exclusively breastfed, thriving (50th centile), term, healthy.

q.lead_in: Which antidepressant is the most appropriate first-line choice?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrating to clinical response over 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: Sertraline has the strongest breastfeeding safety data with very low milk:plasma ratio and infant relative-dose typically below 2%, and she has a prior excellent response to it. RANZCP and LactMed list it as a preferred SSRI in breastfeeding (RANZCP Perinatal MH; LactMed).

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as a first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI
options.rationale: Fluoxetine and its long-half-life active metabolite norfluoxetine accumulate in the breastfed infant and have caused colic, irritability and poor feeding in case reports; it is not preferred when starting de novo (LactMed; RANZCP).

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily as the initial choice while she is breastfeeding
options.rationale: Citalopram has higher milk transfer and more adverse infant reports than sertraline and is associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation; it is an acceptable second-line but not first-line when starting fresh (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg orally at night for sleep and appetite while breastfeeding
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is reasonable when sedation/appetite are dominant features but data in lactation are more limited than sertraline; not first-line for a typical perinatal MDE presentation (RANZCP).

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy and await perinatal CBT in approximately 4 weeks
options.rationale: Moderate-to-severe symptoms with EPDS 17 and passive ideation warrant combined psychological plus pharmacological therapy; deferring medication risks deterioration during the wait (RANZCP; beyondblue Perinatal MH guidelines).

explanation.summary: Sertraline is the preferred first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women in Australian and international perinatal mental health guidance because of its very low infant relative dose. Past good response to a specific SSRI is a strong reason to re-use that agent.
key_points: Sertraline: preferred first-line in breastfeeding (low milk transfer)
Fluoxetine: long-half-life metabolite accumulates in infant
Citalopram/escitalopram: acceptable but more reports of adverse infant effects, dose-dependent QT
Past responder to a specific SSRI - re-use it
Moderate-severe perinatal depression warrants combined therapy
explanation.pearls: EPDS item 10 positivity requires same-visit suicide risk assessment regardless of total score.

q.subtopic_detail: SSRI choice in breastfeeding mother with new perinatal depression

q.stem: A 32-year-old G2P2 woman, 5 days post-vaginal birth of a healthy term infant, presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of pleuritic right-sided chest pain, mild dyspnoea on exertion and a swollen tender right calf. Bedside observations and ECG are detailed below. CTPA confirms a segmental right lower lobe pulmonary embolus and Doppler ultrasound shows acute right popliteal-femoral DVT. She has no haemodynamic instability, no right heart strain and no hypoxia at rest. She is exclusively breastfeeding her term, well infant and is highly motivated to continue. She has no other medical conditions, takes no medications, has no allergies, no history of bleeding disorder and her postnatal Hb is 118 g/L with normal platelets, INR and APTT. She has no recent surgery other than the vaginal birth and no contraindication to anticoagulation. The hospital pharmacist and team need to choose an anticoagulant compatible with breastfeeding for the initial and ongoing treatment period. The team specifically requires an agent with established safety in breastfeeding and a known reversal pathway in the puerperium, given the increased early postpartum bleeding risk.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Investigations: CTPA: segmental RLL PE. Doppler: right popliteal-femoral DVT. Hb 118 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, APTT 32 s, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anticoagulant regimen?

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin transitioning to warfarin with bridging until INR therapeutic; continue breastfeeding
options.rationale: Both LMWH (negligible oral bioavailability in the infant) and warfarin (high protein binding, undetectable in milk) are compatible with breastfeeding and are the RANZCOG-recommended options for postpartum VTE; DOACs are NOT recommended in lactation due to insufficient safety data (RANZCOG VTE; LactMed).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) are not recommended in lactation; manufacturer data and LactMed advise against use because safety in the breastfed infant is not established (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days then 20 mg daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is excreted into breast milk in animal studies and is not recommended in lactation; LMWH or warfarin are preferred (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Unfractionated heparin infusion only with discontinuation of breastfeeding for the treatment period
options.rationale: UFH is also compatible with breastfeeding but is not preferred over LMWH for routine VTE outside of renal failure or imminent procedure; cessation of breastfeeding is unnecessary (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily and graduated compression stockings as primary treatment for VTE
options.rationale: Aspirin is not adequate treatment for an acute PE or DVT; therapeutic anticoagulation is required (RANZCOG; eTG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE in a breastfeeding woman is treated with LMWH (preferred for ease and predictability) or warfarin; both are compatible with breastfeeding. DOACs are NOT recommended in lactation due to inadequate infant safety data.
key_points: LMWH: negligible oral bioavailability in infant - safe in lactation
Warfarin: high protein binding, undetectable in milk - safe in lactation
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran): avoid in lactation
Acute PE/DVT requires therapeutic, not prophylactic, anticoagulation
Duration usually at least 3 months for provoked postpartum VTE
explanation.pearls: Tell the patient explicitly that LMWH and warfarin do not require pump-and-dump - common misconception that interrupts breastfeeding unnecessarily.

sources.label: RANZCOG: VTE in pregnancy and puerperium

q.subtopic_detail: Anticoagulation choice in breastfeeding mother

q.stem: A 71-year-old G4P4 postmenopausal woman attends a urogynaecology outpatient clinic with 18 months of progressive vaginal pressure that is now associated with a visible bulge at the introitus when she stands at the end of the day, occasional difficulty completing defaecation that improves when she splints the posterior vaginal wall, and intermittent urinary hesitancy. She has had no postmenopausal bleeding, no smear abnormality (HPV negative 2 years ago) and no dyspareunia (not currently sexually active). She has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, BMI 28, never-smoker, chronic constipation managed with osmotic laxatives. Speculum and POP-Q examination on maximal Valsalva in the supine position record the following measurements: Aa -1, Ba +1, C +3, GH 5 cm, PB 3 cm, TVL 9 cm, Ap -2, Bp -2, D +1. The most dependent point of prolapse is therefore the cervix at +3 cm beyond the hymen. On bivalve examination her vaginal walls show appropriate support of the anterior and posterior compartments with no significant cystocele or rectocele beyond the hymen. PVR is 30 mL. She wishes to discuss the diagnosis before considering treatment options.

data_table.POP-Q (supine, Valsalva): Aa -1, Ba +1, C +3, GH 5, PB 3, TVL 9, Ap -2, Bp -2, D +1.
data_table.Examination: Cervix descends 3 cm beyond hymen; anterior and posterior walls supported at hymen or above; PVR 30 mL.

q.lead_in: Which is the most accurate description of this prolapse?

options.text: POP-Q stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse (cystocele)
options.rationale: The most dependent point is C (cervix) at +3, not Ba; the anterior wall is only at Ba +1, which is stage 2 in isolation, but the dominant defect is apical (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 apical (uterine) prolapse
options.rationale: POP-Q staging uses the most dependent point: C at +3 (>1 cm beyond hymen but TVL is 9 cm so not complete eversion) is stage 3, with the apex (uterus) being the dominant compartment. Stage 4 would require complete eversion (most distal point at +/- TVL - 2 cm) (IUGA/ICS).

options.text: POP-Q stage 4 complete vaginal vault eversion
options.rationale: Stage 4 requires the most distal point at TVL minus 2 cm or beyond (essentially complete eversion); cervix at +3 with TVL 9 cm does not meet this criterion, and vault prolapse implies prior hysterectomy (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 posterior compartment prolapse (rectocele)
options.rationale: Bp is -2, well above the hymen; the posterior compartment is not the dominant or staged defect (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 1 mixed compartment prolapse
options.rationale: Stage 1 requires the most distal point >1 cm above the hymen; cervix at +3 is well beyond that threshold (IUGA).

explanation.summary: POP-Q stage is determined by the most distal point: stage 0 no prolapse; stage 1 >1 cm above hymen; stage 2 within 1 cm of hymen; stage 3 >1 cm beyond hymen but not full eversion; stage 4 essentially complete eversion (within 2 cm of TVL). The most dependent point identifies the dominant compartment.
key_points: Six points: Aa, Ba (anterior); Ap, Bp (posterior); C (cervix/cuff), D (posterior fornix if uterus present)
TVL, GH and PB are static landmarks
Most distal point sets the stage
Stage 4 requires near-complete eversion (TVL - 2 cm)
Vault prolapse = apical descent in a post-hysterectomy patient
explanation.pearls: Always identify the dominant compartment before counselling on management; apical defects often co-exist with anterior wall descent and address them together.

q.subtopic_detail: POP-Q staging and compartment identification

q.stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman attends urogynaecology with 12 months of progressive vaginal vault prolapse 9 years after a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign menorrhagia. She has previously trialled a ring pessary for 6 months with recurrent expulsion despite refit, and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 4 months with no symptomatic benefit. POP-Q examination shows vaginal vault descent to +4 with anterior wall to +2 and posterior wall to 0; TVL is 8 cm. She is sexually active and wishes to preserve coital function; she has no obesity-related surgical risk (BMI 24), no significant cardiopulmonary disease, no diabetes and is otherwise healthy with no contraindication to general anaesthetic and abdominal surgery. She has been counselled on the TGA-restricted status of transvaginal mesh kits and the residual indications for synthetic mesh. She and her partner have made an informed decision to proceed with definitive surgical management and have specifically asked about the highest-durability option that preserves vaginal length and sexual function. She has been counselled that the highest-durability vault prolapse repair preserving vaginal length and coital function in a fit woman is laparoscopic or robotic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum.

data_table.History: TAH 9 years ago. Pessary failed (recurrent expulsion). PFMT 4 months no benefit. Sexually active, BMI 24, no comorbidities.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.text: Laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior sacral ligament
options.rationale: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) using type 1 macroporous polypropylene mesh is the gold-standard procedure for symptomatic vault prolapse where coital function is to be preserved; it has the lowest recurrence rate and best preserves vaginal length; abdominal (non-vaginal) mesh placement is not subject to the TGA's transvaginal mesh restriction (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Transvaginal mesh kit repair of the vaginal vault using a synthetic mesh device
options.rationale: Transvaginal mesh kits for prolapse repair were withdrawn by the TGA in Australia in 2017 due to harm signals and are not available (TGA mesh statement).

options.text: Sacrospinous ligament fixation of the vaginal vault through a vaginal surgical approach
options.rationale: Sacrospinous fixation is a reasonable native-tissue alternative, particularly for patients who are poor candidates for abdominal surgery, but has higher recurrence and tends to shorten and posteriorly deviate the vagina; sacrocolpopexy is more durable and preserves vaginal axis in fit candidates (RANZCOG; NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Le Fort colpocleisis with obliteration of the vagina for definitive vault support
options.rationale: Colpocleisis is an obliterative procedure reserved for older, frail, non-sexually-active women who accept loss of coital function; she remains sexually active (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat pessary trial with a Gellhorn pessary plus indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: She has already failed conservative management with recurrent pessary expulsion and ineffective PFMT and now elects surgery; another pessary is unlikely to succeed for a stage 3 vault defect with this profile (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) with type 1 polypropylene mesh is the durability gold-standard for symptomatic vault prolapse where sexual function is to be preserved.
