Sacrospinous fixation is the principal native-tissue alternative. Colpocleisis is reserved for frail, non-sexually-active patients. Transvaginal mesh kits are not available in Australia since 2017.
key_points: TGA withdrew transvaginal mesh kits in 2017; sacrocolpopexy mesh is placed abdominally and remains available
Sacrocolpopexy preserves vaginal length and axis better than vaginal approaches
Sacrospinous fixation: native tissue, vaginal route, higher recurrence
Colpocleisis: obliterative, only for non-sexually-active
Conservative measures should be trialled first unless not feasible
explanation.pearls: The Australian Senate Inquiry into transvaginal mesh shaped the current TGA stance and is why the abdominal vs vaginal mesh distinction is essential to know.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

q.subtopic_detail: Vault prolapse after hysterectomy - surgical choice

q.stem: A 52-year-old G3P3 woman attends with 12 months of urinary leakage. On a 3-day bladder diary she records: 8 leakage episodes total - 6 preceded by sudden urgency (mostly on the way to the toilet or hearing running water), and 2 small-volume leaks on coughing or sneezing. Daytime void frequency is 9, nocturia 2, urgency score on Patient Perception of Bladder Condition is severe, and quality-of-life impact is dominated by the urge episodes (she has stopped attending her grandson's school events because of fear of leakage with running to a toilet). PVR is 40 mL, urinalysis is negative, and a cough stress test reproduces a small leak with full bladder. BMI 31, no smoking, no relevant medications, no neurological symptoms. She declines surgery at this stage and has not yet had any structured conservative therapy or pharmacotherapy. She is willing to commit to a 3-month structured conservative trial before considering any pharmacological or surgical option. There are no red flags such as visible haematuria, recurrent UTI or palpable mass and her cervical screening is up to date. She is open to commencing a structured 3-month conservative trial now.

data_table.Bladder diary: 8 leakage episodes; 6 urge, 2 stress. Frequency 9, nocturia 2.
data_table.Tests: PVR 40 mL, urinalysis negative, cough stress test +ve (small leak).

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management approach?

options.text: PFMT, bladder training, weight loss and caffeine reduction; review at 3 months
options.rationale: NICE and Continence Foundation Australia advise structured conservative therapy as initial management for mixed UI, with treatment then directed at the predominant symptom (urge here). PFMT helps both components, bladder training targets urge, and weight loss helps both; structured trial for at least 3 months before escalation (NICE NG123; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer immediately for a tension-free vaginal tape mid-urethral sling procedure
options.rationale: Surgery is not first-line for mixed UI, and stress-incontinence surgery may worsen pre-existing urge symptoms; she also declines surgery (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Commence an antimuscarinic plus duloxetine combination from the outset of management
options.rationale: Combined pharmacotherapy from outset is not recommended; conservative therapy is first-line, with single-agent pharmacotherapy directed at the predominant symptom if conservative therapy is inadequate (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer for invasive urodynamic studies before offering any conservative management
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not required before conservative management of uncomplicated mixed UI; they are reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, prior incontinence surgery, suspected voiding dysfunction or where surgery is being planned (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Insert a continence pessary and commence indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: Continence pessaries are useful adjuncts mainly for stress UI driven by hypermobility, and topical oestrogen is reserved for urogenital atrophy; neither addresses her predominant urge component (Continence Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Mixed UI is managed with a structured conservative bundle first (PFMT at least 3 months, bladder training, weight loss if BMI >30, caffeine and fluid optimisation, smoking cessation). When pharmacotherapy is needed, target the predominant symptom; urodynamics are reserved for selected indications.
key_points: Bladder diary identifies the predominant component
PFMT >=3 months helps both stress and urge
Weight loss of 5-10% halves leakage episodes
Pharmacotherapy targets predominant symptom (antimuscarinic/mirabegron for urge; duloxetine for stress)
Urodynamics not routine before conservative management
explanation.pearls: Treating the predominant component first often unmasks or relieves the secondary component without separate intervention.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

q.subtopic_detail: Mixed urinary incontinence - prioritising treatment by predominant symptom

q.stem: A 72-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%, on metformin + dapagliflozin) presents with 6 months of small-volume continuous urinary leakage, daytime urinary frequency 12, nocturia 4 with leakage on rising, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She has no urgency that precedes the leakage. Her GP has been treating her empirically as urge incontinence with oxybutynin for 8 weeks with worsening of her leakage. On examination she has a palpable bladder to the umbilicus, mildly impaired ankle reflexes with reduced light touch and pinprick over both feet bilaterally, and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Speculum reveals a stage 2 cystocele to the hymen with no other prolapse. Urinalysis: protein 1+, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside bladder scan post-void shows a PVR of 720 mL. Bloods: Hb 118 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 36 mL/min/1.73m2 (down from 68), K+ 5.3 mmol/L, HbA1c 8.4%. Renal tract ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis. She is on no other medications that obviously precipitate retention and is not currently constipated. She lives independently and has supportive family. The team is being asked to differentiate chronic high-pressure urinary retention with post-obstructive AKI from primary urge or stress incontinence, particularly given the clinical picture of bladder distension to the umbilicus, very high PVR, new renal impairment and bilateral hydronephrosis on imaging. The unit has 24-hour urology cover and capacity for immediate catheterisation and assessment for safe relief of retention.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Palpable bladder to umbilicus, peripheral neuropathy, stage 2 cystocele.
data_table.Investigations: PVR 720 mL on bladder scan. Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 36, K+ 5.3 mmol/L. Renal USS: bilateral hydronephrosis. Urinalysis: protein 1+, no infection.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease oxybutynin, insert indwelling catheter and refer urgently to urology
options.rationale: PVR 720 mL with new bilateral hydronephrosis and rising creatinine indicates high-pressure chronic retention with obstructive AKI - bladder decompression with an indwelling catheter and urgent urology review is the immediate priority; antimuscarinics worsen retention and must be stopped (NICE NG112; Continence Foundation Australia; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NICE NG112 Lower urinary tract symptoms in men (decompression principles applicable)

options.text: Add mirabegron 50 mg daily to the oxybutynin and review again in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Adding further pharmacotherapy for assumed OAB ignores high-volume retention with renal compromise; doing so risks worsening retention and renal injury (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Refer for urgent mid-urethral sling surgery for presumed stress urinary incontinence now
options.rationale: She has overflow incontinence from chronic retention, not stress incontinence; a sling in this context will precipitate complete retention and worsen renal injury (NICE).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg daily to manage her presumed nocturnal polyuria
options.rationale: Frusemide increases urine production into an already overloaded obstructed bladder and worsens both retention and AKI (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange outpatient urodynamics in 6 weeks and continue current therapy unchanged
options.rationale: Outpatient delay is unsafe when there is post-obstructive AKI with bilateral hydronephrosis; immediate bladder decompression is required (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

explanation.summary: Continuous small-volume leakage with high PVR, palpable bladder, and rising creatinine with hydronephrosis is overflow incontinence from high-pressure chronic retention with post-obstructive AKI. Immediate bladder decompression with an indwelling catheter and urgent urology review is mandatory. Monitor for post-obstructive diuresis and hyperkalaemia.
key_points: Overflow UI: continuous leakage, high PVR, no urgency, often diabetic or neurogenic
High-pressure retention with hydronephrosis + rising creatinine = obstructive AKI
Decompress immediately with IDC
Stop all antimuscarinics
Watch for post-obstructive diuresis (replace half losses cautiously)
explanation.pearls: Any patient on antimuscarinics whose leakage gets worse should have a PVR scan before any further dose change - the most common missed cause of overflow incontinence is iatrogenic.

sources.label: NICE NG112 Lower urinary tract symptoms in men (decompression principles applicable)
NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

q.subtopic_detail: Overflow incontinence with chronic urinary retention

q.stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with 10 months of vulval and vaginal soreness, burning dyspareunia and recurrent contact-bleeding from the vagina. On examination there is glazed erythema of the labia minora and vestibule with a serpiginous white reticular lacy pattern at the inner labia, painful erosions at the introitus, and on speculum examination there is friable erythematous mucosa of the upper vagina with synechiae causing partial vaginal narrowing and obliteration of the vaginal fornices. There is no labial fusion, no figure-of-eight pallor extending around the anus, and the clitoral hood is intact and not buried. She also has lacy white striae on the buccal mucosa and a desquamative gingivitis confirmed by her dentist 6 months ago. There are no skin lesions on the trunk or limbs. She has no urinary symptoms, BMI 24, on no medications, never smoked. Vulval biopsy is taken from the edge of an erosion and shows a band-like sub-epithelial lymphocytic infiltrate with basal cell vacuolar degeneration and Civatte bodies; there is no homogeneous dermal hyalinisation. She has no skin lesions on the trunk or limbs and no genital herpes history.

data_table.Examination: Wickham striae inner labia, vestibular erosions, vaginal synechiae with friable mucosa.
data_table.Extragenital: Oral lichen planus (buccal lacy striae, desquamative gingivitis).
data_table.Histology: Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate, basal vacuolar degeneration, Civatte bodies. No hyalinisation.

q.lead_in: Which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Erosive vulvovaginal lichen planus with classic mucocutaneous involvement
options.rationale: Wickham striae, painful erosions, vaginal involvement with synechiae and friability, concurrent oral lichen planus and the histology of band-like lymphocytic infiltrate with Civatte bodies all point to erosive lichen planus; vaginal involvement is a key feature that distinguishes LP from LS (BSSVD; RCH-Vulval Disorders guidance via Royal Women's Vic).

options.text: Lichen sclerosus with figure-of-eight pallor and architectural change
options.rationale: LS shows porcelain-white atrophic skin with figure-of-eight perianal involvement, labial resorption and clitoral hood phimosis; vaginal mucosa is NOT involved in LS, and histology shows dermal hyalinisation, which is absent here (BSSVD).

options.text: Vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (HSIL/uVIN) on the vulval mucosa
options.rationale: HSIL/uVIN shows discrete pigmented or whitish plaques with acetowhitening and HPV-related dysplastic epithelium on histology; the inflammatory band, Civatte bodies and oral involvement here are not VIN features (RANZCOG; BSSVD).

options.text: Plasma cell vulvitis (Zoon vulvitis) of the vulval vestibule
options.rationale: Zoon vulvitis presents with glistening orange-red patches without Wickham striae or oral lesions, and histology shows a dense plasma cell infiltrate, not band-like T-cell infiltrate with Civatte bodies (BSSVD).

options.text: Atrophic vaginitis from genitourinary syndrome of menopause
options.rationale: GSM produces diffuse pallor, loss of rugae and dryness without erosions, Wickham striae or oral involvement; histology is not erosive lymphocytic (RANZCOG: Menopause).

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen planus, particularly the erosive subtype, differs from lichen sclerosus in three key ways: vaginal mucosal involvement (synechiae, friability) which LS does NOT cause; Wickham striae and concurrent oral lichen planus; and histology with band-like lymphocytic infiltrate plus Civatte bodies without dermal hyalinisation.
key_points: LP affects vaginal mucosa; LS does not
Wickham striae (lacy reticular pattern) is pathognomonic for LP
Oral lichen planus is present in many erosive cases - examine the mouth
First-line management: high-potency topical clobetasol; vaginal hydrocortisone foam for vaginal involvement
Lichen planus also carries a small but real risk of vulvar SCC and requires long-term surveillance
explanation.pearls: Always examine the mouth in any woman with persistent vulval erosions - erosive LP is multi-mucosal in most cases.

q.subtopic_detail: Lichen planus vs lichen sclerosus discrimination

q.subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
q.stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a remote community, with known severe rheumatic mitral stenosis (MVA 1.0 cm2, mean gradient 14 mmHg, PASP 56 mmHg on third-trimester echo, NYHA II antenatally) presents to a regional referral centre at 39+2 weeks in spontaneous labour. She has been on monthly BPG, metoprolol 50 mg BD, and prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily, ceased 18 hours ago. She is 6 cm dilated, contracting 4-in-10, the foetus is cephalic with reassuring CTG, and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. Over the last 30 minutes she has become increasingly breathless, oxygen saturation has fallen from 98% to 91% on room air, HR has risen from 96 to 132 (sinus tachycardia on ECG with no shortened diastole arrhythmia and no fibrillatory waves), BP is 105/68, JVP is elevated, and she has bibasal crackles. Chest x-ray shows pulmonary venous congestion with no focal consolidation. Bedside echo confirms unchanged severe MS with no left atrial thrombus and preserved LV function. The cardiac-obstetric-anaesthetic team is gathering at the bedside, blood is cross-matched, and a high-dependency bed is available. Foetal heart rate remains 145 with normal variability and accelerations, and the obstetric assessment is that vaginal birth with assisted second stage remains feasible if she stabilises within minutes She has been counselled antenatally about the haemodynamic risks of tachycardia in severe mitral stenosis and the rationale for assisted second stage to avoid prolonged Valsalva.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 105/68, HR 132 sinus, RR 28, SpO2 91% RA -> 95% on 4 L NP, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: JVP +4 cm, bibasal crackles, loud P2, mid-diastolic rumble apex; gravid uterus contracting 4-in-10
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachy, no AF; CXR pulmonary congestion; ABG pH 7.40, pO2 78 mmHg on O2, lactate 1.8; troponin negative
data_table.CTG: Baseline 145, variability normal, accels present, no decels
data_table.Medications: Metoprolol 50 mg BD, BPG monthly, enoxaparin 40 mg ceased 18 h ago, epidural sited

q.lead_in: What is the single best next intervention to stabilise her acutely?

options.text: IV furosemide plus additional IV beta-blocker to lower heart rate and reduce filling pressures
options.rationale: In severe MS the haemodynamic enemy is tachycardia (shortened diastole, raised LA pressure, pulmonary oedema). Lowering rate with beta-blockade and offloading with a loop diuretic is the cornerstone of acute management while planning an assisted second stage to limit Valsalva effort. Emergency caesarean and balloon valvuloplasty are only required if she fails to stabilise (RANZCOG; ESC Pregnancy Cardiovascular Disease Guideline).

options.text: Tocolyse with IV salbutamol to halt labour and proceed to emergency percutaneous mitral balloon valvuloplasty
options.rationale: Beta-agonist tocolysis causes tachycardia and pulmonary oedema in severe MS and is contraindicated; valvuloplasty is considered antenatally in refractory disease, not in established labour at term (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthesia for acute pulmonary oedema
options.rationale: Foetus is reassuring and labour is progressing; GA carries high haemodynamic risk in severe MS (induction-related tachycardia, swings in afterload). Vaginal birth with assisted second stage under regional anaesthesia is preferred if feasible (RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018).

options.text: Give an IV crystalloid bolus of 500 mL Hartmann's to maintain preload before considering further intervention
options.rationale: Fluid bolus in pulmonary congestion from severe MS worsens pulmonary oedema; preload must be preserved but not increased (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Commence IV amiodarone infusion for presumed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
options.rationale: ECG shows sinus tachycardia, not AF; amiodarone is reserved for arrhythmia and carries foetal thyroid risk (RHDAustralia; AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe MS decompensates with tachycardia and Valsalva. Acute management is rate control with beta-blocker, diuresis, oxygen, and planned assisted second stage to deliver vaginally under regional analgesia (RHDAustralia; RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018 Pregnancy CVD).
key_points: Diastolic filling time is the critical variable in MS (ESC 2018)
Beta-blocker rate control is first-line acute therapy (RHDAustralia)
Avoid fluid bolus; manage pulmonary congestion with diuresis (ESC 2018)
Assisted second stage (vacuum/forceps) to limit Valsalva (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
GA caesarean carries higher haemodynamic risk than well-conducted epidural (ESC 2018)
Continue endocarditis-prophylaxis consideration per RHDAustralia for high-risk lesions
explanation.pearls: Anaesthetic plan should be written and reviewed by 32 weeks for any severe valvular lesion in pregnancy (RANZCOG W-WH 22).

q.subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander intrapartum - rheumatic mitral stenosis decompensation

q.stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman presents to ED 10 days after a medical TOP at 8 weeks using mifepristone-misoprostol from her GP. The procedure was reported as successful with passage of products on day 1 and tapering bleeding for 6 days. Over the last 36 hours she has had heavy bleeding soaking 1 large pad per hour, lower abdominal cramping, low-grade fever (37.8 deg C), foul-smelling discharge, and feels lightheaded on standing. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, blood group O Rh positive, and breastfed her two prior children. On arrival: BP 96/58, HR 116, T 37.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%. Abdomen tender suprapubically with guarding but no peritonism. Speculum: open external os with brisk bleeding and brown malodorous discharge, products visible at the os. Bedside USS: heterogeneous endometrial collection 38 mm with vascularity on colour Doppler and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Bloods: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6, group-and-hold sent. She has had no ectopic risk factors and the pre-TOP scan confirmed intrauterine pregnancy with normal adnexa.

data_table.Examination: Tender suprapubic with guarding; open cervical os with brisk bleed, products visible, foul discharge
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4, CRP 142, beta-hCG 1820 IU/L, lactate 1.6; USS endometrial collection 38 mm vascular

q.lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Outpatient repeat misoprostol 800 microg vaginal plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: She has haemodynamic compromise, signs of sepsis and active bleeding; outpatient management is unsafe and inadequate source control (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, group-and-hold, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, then proceed to urgent surgical evacuation of retained products
options.rationale: Septic incomplete abortion with haemorrhage requires resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam per eTG), and source control by urgent surgical evacuation. Delaying evacuation prolongs the source of infection and bleeding (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; RANZCOG PPH/Gynae emergencies).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion with close observation, deferring evacuation pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Oxytocin has limited effect on a non-puerperal uterus, antibiotics should not wait for culture results in sepsis (within 1 hour per ANZICS), and source control must not be delayed (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV with observation and serial Hb without antibiotic cover until culture results
options.rationale: TXA is adjunctive but does not address source; delaying antibiotics in sepsis breaches the 1-hour standard and risks septic shock (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 for presumed ongoing intrauterine pregnancy and discharge home to GP
options.rationale: Methotrexate addresses neither retained products nor sepsis, and is contraindicated in active infection (AMH; eTG).

explanation.summary: Septic incomplete abortion requires concurrent resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, and surgical source control by evacuation of retained products. This is a gynaecological emergency (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).
key_points: Antibiotics within 1 hour for sepsis (ANZICS Sepsis)
Source control: surgical evacuation of RPOC (RANZCOG TOP)
Antibiotic regimen: amp + gent + metronidazole or pip-taz (eTG Antibiotic)
Anti-D 250 IU if Rh negative and unsensitised (Australian Red Cross)
Major haemorrhage protocol if Hb continues to fall (RANZCOG PPH)
Inform original prescriber and arrange contraception/follow-up
explanation.pearls: Beware Clostridium sordellii / perfringens infection after medical TOP: profound shock without fever, leucocytosis and haemoconcentration (RANZCOG TOP).

q.subtopic_detail: Medical TOP - haemorrhage complication management

q.stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 30 weeks gestation discloses intimate partner violence during a private antenatal visit after the midwife has seen her alone. Her partner has been verbally and physically abusive for 3 years; the frequency and severity have escalated over the past 6 weeks since she told him she was pregnant. Last week he choked her with both hands until she briefly lost consciousness, threatened to kill her if she left, and removed her mobile phone so she could not contact her sister. He has access to firearms because he is a licensed recreational shooter. She has two children aged 4 and 6 at home, no immediate family support locally, and no current Apprehended Domestic Violence Order. She has not previously disclosed to a clinician. She is asking what to do; she is frightened, oriented, and engaged. There is a social worker on site this afternoon and a state-funded specialist DV service with crisis accommodation available. The midwife has documented the disclosure in a separate confidential section of the record that the partner cannot access The midwife now needs to triage the risk level before activating crisis response.

data_table.Examination: Tender 4 cm bruise left flank, faint petechiae over neck, no current acute injury; SFH appropriate, FH 144
data_table.Social: Two children at home (4 and 6), no AVO, partner has firearms access, escalating frequency
data_table.Risk factors: Strangulation, threats to kill, controlling behaviour (phone removed), pregnancy, firearms access

q.lead_in: Which combination of features in this disclosure most strongly indicates EXTREME risk of intimate partner femicide and mandates immediate crisis-response activation?

options.text: Frequency of arguments and verbal abuse over the past 3 years without any physical injury
options.rationale: Verbal abuse and chronic frequency are markers of abuse but are not the discriminating extreme-risk features; the specific factors below are more strongly associated with lethality (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).

options.text: A past episode of pushing without injury approximately 18 months ago and now resolved
options.rationale: Historical low-severity violence is concerning but not in the same risk class as the cluster in option C (ANROWS).

options.text: Recent non-fatal strangulation, threat to kill, escalation in pregnancy, and firearm access
options.rationale: Non-fatal strangulation, an explicit threat to kill, escalation during pregnancy, abuser access to firearms, and coercive control (such as phone removal) are the strongest single predictors of intimate partner femicide. Together they meet criteria for EXTREME risk on the Danger Assessment and DVSAT and mandate immediate crisis-response activation (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT; ANROWS; Danger Assessment).

options.text: Disclosure occurring during the antenatal visit itself, without any other contextual features
options.rationale: The act of disclosure is positive engagement, not a risk marker; risk is determined by the content of the disclosure (ANROWS).

options.text: Absence of any Apprehended Domestic Violence Order despite the long abusive relationship history
options.rationale: Lack of AVO is common; it does not by itself stratify extreme risk (1800RESPECT).

explanation.summary: Non-fatal strangulation, explicit threats to kill, escalation in pregnancy, firearm access, and coercive control are the highest-yield extreme-risk features. Pregnancy is itself a peak-risk period. Activate crisis pathway, safety plan, and structured risk assessment (MARAM in Vic, DVSAT in NSW) (ANROWS; 1800RESPECT; MARAM; NSW DVSAT).
key_points: Non-fatal strangulation: ~7x increased risk of subsequent homicide (ANROWS)
Pregnancy is a peak risk period (1800RESPECT)
Firearm access is a major lethality factor (ANROWS)
Coercive control (phone removal, isolation) is a marker of severe pattern (1800RESPECT)
Use a structured framework: MARAM (Vic) or DVSAT (NSW)
Mandatory reporting of child exposure varies by state - check local Act
explanation.pearls: Strangulation may leave minimal external signs - ask specifically and examine for petechiae, voice change, dysphagia (ANROWS).

q.subtopic_detail: Domestic violence in pregnancy - escalation risk assessment

q.stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to ED 5 days (120 hours) after non-consensual unprotected vaginal intercourse with an unknown male at a party. She did not present sooner because of fear of judgement. She is otherwise well, has no medical history, no allergies, takes no regular medications, has a regular 28-day menstrual cycle with last period 11 days ago (so currently around day 11 and within the fertile window), and has previously had her three-dose HPV vaccine and a full primary hepatitis B course as a child. She has had no further intercourse since. She is uncertain about reporting to police but wants medical care. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable, the on-call gynaecology registrar can insert a copper IUD today, and the assailant's HIV status is unknown. She is asking what can still be offered for emergency contraception and infection prevention given the 5-day window since exposure, and she is open to all options including a long-acting method She declines police involvement today and asks specifically about emergency contraception that will still work at the 120-hour mark and about whether STI screening can be done at the same visit.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury; healing 1 cm abrasion left knee; speculum: no acute trauma, no visible discharge
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative; baseline HIV Ag/Ab, HBsAg/HBsAb/anti-HBc, HCV Ab, syphilis EIA, NAATs for chlamydia/gonorrhoea/trichomonas sent
data_table.Cycle: Day 11 of regular 28-day cycle; previous LMP 11 days ago

q.lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate component of management at this time?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 72-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel EC efficacy is limited to within 96 hours of unprotected intercourse and is not first-line beyond that window (Family Planning Australia; eTG).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 120-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Ulipristal extends the window to 120 hours but evidence is for up to 120 hours post-intercourse, and at exactly 120 hours efficacy is borderline; the copper IUD is the more reliable option still available now (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Copper IUD insertion as emergency contraception, with empirical STI prophylaxis and sexual assault referral
options.rationale: Copper IUD inserted up to 5 days after the earliest predicted ovulation is the most effective emergency contraception (failure rate <1%) and is the only option licensed beyond 120 hours. At day 11 of a 28-day cycle she remains within the fertile window. Empirical STI prophylaxis (ceftriaxone, azithromycin or doxycycline, metronidazole) and warm referral to the sexual assault service complete management (RANZCOG; ASHM Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Commence HIV non-occupational post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir-emtricitabine-dolutegravir for a full 28-day course
options.rationale: HIV PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; at 120 hours the window has closed and PEP is not indicated (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Accelerated hepatitis B vaccination course because the patient is presumed unprotected after childhood priming
options.rationale: She has a documented completed primary hepatitis B course; check anti-HBs titre rather than re-vaccinate (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

explanation.summary: At 120 hours post-assault, the most effective EC option still available is the copper IUD (up to 5 days). Oral EC efficacy is borderline; HIV PEP is no longer indicated. Empirical STI prophylaxis and sexual assault service referral remain appropriate (Family Planning Australia; Australasian STI Management Guidelines; ASHM; RANZCOG).
key_points: Levonorgestrel EC: up to 96 hours (Family Planning Australia)
Ulipristal EC: up to 120 hours (Family Planning Australia)
Copper IUD EC: up to 5 days after earliest ovulation or 5 days after intercourse (Family Planning Australia)
HIV PEP: must start within 72 hours (ASHM)
Forensic kit: typically <72 h, some samples up to 7 days (RANZCOG)
Sexual assault psychological support remains available regardless of timing
explanation.pearls: Copper IUD is the only EC method that simultaneously provides ongoing contraception (Family Planning Australia).

q.subtopic_detail: Sexual assault - presentation beyond 72 hours

q.stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed at 5 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She has focal epilepsy controlled on carbamazepine 400 mg BD (level stable, no seizures for 3 years), is exclusively breastfeeding her baby (infant weight tracking the 50th centile, no concerns at the maternal-child health nurse review), and had a normal vaginal birth with no thromboembolic history, no migraine, normotensive, BMI 24, non-smoker, and a normal Hb of 122 g/L. She has read about long-acting reversible contraception and is aware that carbamazepine is an enzyme inducer. She wishes to space the next pregnancy by at least 3 years and prefers a method that does not require daily adherence or rely on her remembering to attend for 3-monthly injections. She is happy with all options being on the table, including LARCs and combined methods, and asks the GP to recommend the most reliable option that will not be undermined by her antiepileptic. Her neurologist is aware and supportive of any contraceptive choice She has no history of menorrhagia and no copper allergy, and lactational amenorrhoea alone is not acceptable to her given the 3-year spacing she wants.

data_table.Examination: Well, lactating; uterus involuted
data_table.Medications: Carbamazepine 400 mg BD; folic acid 5 mg daily (planned for future pregnancy)
data_table.Pregnancy plans: Spacing at least 3 years; LARC preferred

q.lead_in: Which contraceptive option is the most appropriate first-line recommendation?

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (including carbamazepine) significantly reduce etonogestrel plasma levels via CYP3A4 induction; the implant is NOT recommended (UKMEC 3) in women on enzyme inducers because contraceptive efficacy is reduced (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 microg with levonorgestrel) taken daily
options.rationale: Enzyme inducers reduce COCP efficacy and the COCP is generally avoided <6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding due to milk supply and VTE concerns; not first-line here (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken at the same time every day
options.rationale: POP efficacy is reduced by enzyme inducers; not recommended (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) or copper IUD inserted as a LARC
options.rationale: Intrauterine devices (LNG-IUD and copper IUD) deliver hormone locally or are non-hormonal and have efficacy that is NOT reduced by enzyme inducers; both are UKMEC 1 in women on enzyme-inducing AEDs and compatible with breastfeeding. They provide the long-acting reversible spacing this woman requests (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscularly every 12 weeks as her contraceptive method
options.rationale: DMPA contraceptive efficacy is not significantly reduced by enzyme inducers (UKMEC 1), so it is acceptable; however it is not the SINGLE BEST recommendation when an IUD is suitable and the woman wants long-term spacing without quarterly injections, and DMPA has bone density and weight effects to discuss (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Enzyme-inducing AEDs reduce efficacy of oestrogen, progestogen-only pill and the etonogestrel implant. IUDs (LNG and copper) and DMPA are not significantly affected. IUD is preferred as long-acting, non-daily, breastfeeding-compatible (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Drug Interactions).
key_points: Enzyme inducers: carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarb, primidone, topiramate >200 mg, rifampicin (FSRH)
Affected: COCP, POP, implant, transdermal patch, ring
Not affected: LNG-IUD, copper IUD, DMPA (FSRH)
If on enzyme inducer and COCP required: 50 microg EE or alternative method (FSRH)
Lamotrigine, levetiracetam, sodium valproate are NOT enzyme inducers
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine levels are themselves reduced by COCP - bidirectional interaction; this is opposite to the carbamazepine direction (FSRH Drug Interactions).

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - breastfeeding + enzyme-inducing AED

q.stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed 6 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She had a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 3 years ago with weight loss from 142 kg to 78 kg (current BMI 28, stable). She is bottle feeding by choice, normotensive, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and is on regular vitamin and iron supplementation post-bariatric. She is asking the GP for contraception and is happy with all options, including LARCs. Her last pregnancy was carefully monitored post-bariatric and was uncomplicated other than mild iron deficiency. She specifically asks whether she can simply take her sister's combined oral contraceptive pill, because she has heard that any oral hormonal method is reliable. She also asks whether a return of regular cycles is required before she starts a method, given she is not yet menstruating because she is using progesterone-containing analgesia Her dietician has confirmed she is taking her bariatric multivitamin daily and her ferritin has normalised, and the GP is happy to insert a LARC at this visit if appropriate She specifically asks whether oral pills will still work post-bypass.

data_table.Examination: Well, lactation suppressed; surgical scars from RYGB
data_table.Medications: Multivitamin, iron, vitamin B12 IM 3-monthly, calcium + vitamin D

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate consideration when selecting contraception for this woman?

options.text: Oral hormonal contraception is unaffected because Roux-en-Y bypass does not alter small-molecule absorption
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y bypass alters oral hormonal absorption and reduces COCP and POP efficacy; FSRH advises against reliance on oral methods after malabsorptive bariatric procedures (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity and Contraception).

options.text: Long-acting reversible methods are preferred because oral hormonal absorption is reduced post-bypass
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass bypasses the proximal small bowel and reduces absorption of oral hormonal contraceptives, which lowers their reliability. LARCs (LNG-IUD, copper IUD, etonogestrel implant, DMPA) do not depend on enteric absorption and are first-line after bariatric bypass surgery (Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare; UKMEC; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraception is preferred to ensure cycle regularity post-bariatric surgery in this patient
options.rationale: Oral COCP efficacy is reduced after RYGB; transdermal/vaginal CHC may also be considered but post-bariatric weight loss instability and weight rebound risk make LARC the preferred first-line, not CHC for cycle control (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Await return of regular menstrual cycles before starting any contraception in this patient
options.rationale: Fertility returns rapidly postpartum (and dramatically improves post-bariatric); contraception should be in place to avoid early conception (Family Planning Australia; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Permanent sterilisation should be recommended because pregnancy is unsafe after bariatric surgery
options.rationale: Pregnancy after bariatric is safe with planned spacing (12-24 months recommended) and adequate nutritional surveillance; recommending sterilisation is not appropriate without patient-led discussion (RANZCOG; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

explanation.summary: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and other malabsorptive bariatric procedures reduce oral hormonal contraceptive absorption. LARCs (LNG-IUD, copper IUD, implant) and DMPA are the preferred choices because they bypass intestinal absorption. Restrictive procedures alone do not have the same effect (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Malabsorptive procedures: Roux-en-Y, BPD, duodenal switch (FSRH)
Restrictive only: sleeve gastrectomy, gastric band - oral methods acceptable (FSRH)
LARC bypasses absorption issue (FSRH)
Plan pregnancy 12-24 months post-bariatric (RANZCOG)
Folate, B12, iron, calcium, vitamin D surveillance pre-pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Post-bariatric the patient may be hyperfertile compared to her pre-operative state; do not assume the previous infertility persists (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - post-bariatric surgery

q.stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes elective surgical TOP at 13 weeks by dilation and evacuation in a day-procedure unit. The procedure itself is uneventful but in recovery 30 minutes later she is found to be soaking the pad with bright red blood. She has no significant past medical history, is on no anticoagulation, has no known bleeding diathesis, BMI 26, and no prior caesarean section. On urgent review: BP 88/52, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 99%, T 36.7, alert but pale and clammy. Abdominal palpation reveals a soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis. Speculum: brisk red bleeding from a parous cervical os, no obvious cervical laceration, no visible retained products at the os. Estimated blood loss is already 800 mL. The major haemorrhage protocol is being activated, two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ, and a group-and-hold has been sent. The most likely cause is uterine atony given the soft, poorly contracted uterus The on-call gynaecologist is en route; pharmacy has misoprostol, ergometrine and carboprost available The anaesthetic team has secured the airway adequately for now.

data_table.Examination: Soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between umbilicus and symphysis; brisk bleeding from os, no cervical laceration
data_table.Investigations: Hb pre-op 124 g/L, EBL 800 mL and ongoing; group and hold sent
data_table.Procedure: Surgical TOP at 13 weeks; no obvious tissue retained at speculum

q.lead_in: What is the single best initial uterotonic intervention while ongoing resuscitation continues?

options.text: IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus followed by oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L saline
options.rationale: Postabortal uterine atony is managed identically to postpartum atony: oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic, given as a slow 5 IU IV bolus followed by an infusion. Second-line agents (ergot alkaloids, carboprost, misoprostol) and tranexamic acid are added if atony persists; mechanical and surgical measures escalate from there (RANZCOG PPH; WHO PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg per rectum as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a third-line uterotonic in Australian PPH/TOP haemorrhage protocols, used when first-line agents are unavailable or contraindicated; IV oxytocin is the first-line (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole haemorrhage agent, withholding any uterotonic for now
options.rationale: TXA is a useful adjunct (1 g IV within 3 h of the onset of haemorrhage) but does not address atony; uterotonics are the primary mechanism-targeted therapy (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial).

options.text: Carboprost 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Carboprost is second-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; it is contraindicated in asthma. It is not the first-line agent (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Methylergometrine 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Ergometrine/methylergometrine is the standard second-line after oxytocin and is contraindicated in hypertension and pre-eclampsia. First-line uterotonic is oxytocin (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Post-TOP atonic haemorrhage is managed in parallel with major-haemorrhage resuscitation: bimanual compression, IV oxytocin bolus then infusion as first-line uterotonic, then ergometrine and carboprost if needed, plus TXA within 3 hours. Re-examine for retained tissue and cervical/uterine trauma; escalate to balloon tamponade, embolisation or laparotomy if uncontrolled (RANZCOG TOP; RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
key_points: First-line: IV oxytocin 5 IU bolus then 40 IU/L infusion (RANZCOG PPH)
Second-line: ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (avoid in HTN, PET) (RANZCOG PPH)
Third-line: carboprost 250 microg IM q15min, max 8 doses (avoid asthma) (RANZCOG PPH)
Backup: misoprostol 800 microg PR (RANZCOG PPH)
Adjunct: TXA 1 g IV within 3 h of onset (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial)
Always re-examine for retained tissue, cervical laceration, uterine perforation (RANZCOG TOP)
explanation.pearls: After surgical TOP haemorrhage that does not respond promptly to uterotonics, suspect retained tissue or uterine perforation and arrange urgent USS and theatre re-examination (RANZCOG TOP).

q.subtopic_detail: Surgical TOP - haemorrhage complication

q.stem: A 64-year-old woman presents with 4 hours of intermittent retrosternal chest tightness, worse on exertion, partially relieved by rest. She has type 2 diabetes on metformin, hypertension on perindopril, and is a current smoker. She has had two similar episodes in the past week. On arrival she is pain-free. Her ECG is in sinus rhythm with non-specific T-wave flattening in the lateral leads, no ST elevation or depression, no Q-waves, and is unchanged from a tracing 6 months ago. Initial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (hs-cTnI) drawn 4 hours after pain onset is 14 ng/L (99th percentile cut-off 16 ng/L; algorithm low-rule-out less than 5, rule-in greater than 52). A 1-hour repeat is 18 ng/L (absolute change 4 ng/L; algorithm rule-in delta greater than 6). Her renal function is normal, and she has no prior cardiac history or stress test. The cardiology registrar asks whether the patient sits in the rule-out, rule-in or observe zone and what the 3-hour repeat threshold is for this assay. The cardiology consultant on call has been notified and is en route. The patient is currently pain-free, eating, drinking and comfortable in resus, with a normal mental state, no signs of heart failure and stable vital signs while she awaits the disposition decision.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pain-free, chest clear, heart sounds dual no murmur, calves soft.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: SR, non-specific lateral T-wave flattening, unchanged from 6 months ago. hs-cTnI 0h 14 ng/L, 1h 18 ng/L (delta 4). Creatinine 72 micromol/L, Hb 132 g/L, eGFR greater than 90.
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD, perindopril 5 mg daily.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with GP follow-up within 48 hours - the 0/1 hour algorithm rules out NSTEMI in this patient given the trivial 1-hour delta
options.rationale: She is in the observe zone (not low rule-out and not rule-in), so discharge without further workup is unsafe; a third troponin and provocative testing or CTCA are required (ESC 0/1h algorithm; Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

options.text: Diagnose NSTEMI now and proceed to inpatient invasive coronary angiography within 24 hours with adjunctive dual antiplatelet loading and fondaparinux
options.rationale: Diagnosis of NSTEMI on the 0/1 hour algorithm requires absolute delta above the rule-in cut-off (greater than 6 ng/L for hs-cTnI in this assay) or a value above the rule-in threshold; her delta is 4 ng/L and absolute value 18 ng/L is below the rule-in (ESC; Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Repeat troponin at 3 hours from presentation and admit to a cardiac observation unit pending the result and further risk stratification
options.rationale: Patients in the observe zone require a third hs-cTnI at 3 hours; if values plateau without rule-in delta, intermediate-risk pathways (CTCA or exercise/stress testing) apply, with admission for observation until a diagnosis is made (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS; ESC 0/1h algorithm).

options.text: Commence fibrinolysis with weight-adjusted tenecteplase given high cardiovascular risk profile and the early presentation to a non-PCI capable facility today
options.rationale: Fibrinolysis is contraindicated in NSTE-ACS - it increases bleeding without improving outcomes; reperfusion in NSTE-ACS is invasive (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for outpatient exercise stress echocardiogram in 2 weeks and discharge home tonight with safety-netting and short-term GTN spray as required
options.rationale: Deferring testing for 2 weeks while in the observe zone risks missed evolving infarction; she requires inpatient repeat troponin and provocative or anatomical testing before discharge (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

explanation.summary: The 0/1 hour high-sensitivity troponin algorithm rules out NSTEMI if the baseline value is very low (less than 5 ng/L for hs-cTnI) and chest pain onset was more than 3 hours ago, or if the 1-hour delta is below a defined threshold. Rule-in requires either a baseline above the rule-in cut-off (greater than 52 ng/L) or a 1-hour delta above the rule-in delta (greater than 6 ng/L). All others fall into the observe zone and need a 3-hour repeat plus further risk stratification.
key_points: 0/1h algorithm: rule-out, rule-in, or observe
Observe zone: repeat at 3h + CTCA or stress testing
Rule-in delta cut-offs are assay-specific - read your lab
Fibrinolysis is contraindicated in NSTE-ACS
Invasive angiography in NSTEMI: less than 2h if high-risk, less than 24h if intermediate
explanation.pearls: An 'observe zone' troponin pattern is not reassurance - it's a mandate for further workup.

q.subtopic_detail: NSTEMI - 0/1 hour high-sensitivity troponin algorithm

q.stem: A 76-year-old woman with newly diagnosed persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation attends a cardiology clinic. She has type 2 diabetes on metformin, hypertension on amlodipine, and a transient ischaemic attack 3 years ago with full recovery. She has CKD stage 3b with creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73 m2, and weighs 58 kg. Her haemoglobin is 122 g/L, platelets 230 x 10^9/L, and she has no history of GI bleeding or peptic ulcer. She is community-dwelling, manages her own medications, and has no plans for elective surgery. She takes no interacting medications (no rifampicin, no azoles, no anticonvulsants). She prefers fewer monitoring visits and is reliable with twice-daily dosing. The clinic registrar specifically asks whether apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily or the standard 5 mg twice daily is correct given the weight and creatinine, whether dabigatran 150 mg twice daily would be safer with her renal function, whether warfarin titrated to INR 2.0-3.0 should be preferred, and whether dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel would be a reasonable alternative for stroke prevention in this patient. She has no DOAC contraindication.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm irregular, RR 14.
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, chest clear, no neurological deficit, no peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73 m2, weight 58 kg, Hb 122 g/L, platelets 230 x 10^9/L, ALT/AST normal.
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD, amlodipine 5 mg daily.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anticoagulation?

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: CHA2DS2-VA = 6 (age greater than or equal to 75 = 2, diabetes = 1, hypertension = 1, prior TIA = 2). Apixaban dose-reduction criteria require any 2 of: age greater than or equal to 80, weight less than or equal to 60 kg, creatinine greater than or equal to 133 micromol/L. She meets weight and creatinine criteria, so 2.5 mg BD is correct (CSANZ AF Guideline; AMH).

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: She meets two apixaban dose-reduction criteria (weight 58 kg and creatinine 138 micromol/L), mandating the 2.5 mg BD dose; the 5 mg dose would lead to over-anticoagulation and increased bleeding risk (AMH; CSANZ AF).

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: Dabigatran is contraindicated in CrCl less than 30 mL/min; with eGFR 32 her CrCl is borderline and dabigatran is not preferred when other DOACs (apixaban) have a wider renal safety margin (AMH; CSANZ AF).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely
options.rationale: Warfarin is acceptable but not preferred for non-valvular AF in the absence of mechanical valve, moderate-severe mitral stenosis, or antiphospholipid syndrome; DOACs have lower intracranial bleeding rates and do not require INR monitoring (CSANZ AF).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus clopidogrel 75 mg daily
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy is inferior to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF (ACTIVE-W) and is not recommended for stroke prophylaxis (CSANZ AF).

explanation.summary: Australian practice uses CHA2DS2-VA (not VASc - sex modifier removed). Score greater than or equal to 2 in women or greater than or equal to 1 in men warrants anticoagulation. DOACs are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF. Apixaban dose-reduction criteria require any 2 of: age greater than or equal to 80, weight less than or equal to 60 kg, creatinine greater than or equal to 133 micromol/L.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA: CHF 1, HTN 1, Age 65-74 1 or greater than or equal to 75 2, DM 1, Stroke/TIA 2, Vasc 1
Apixaban dose-reduce if any 2 of age greater than or equal to 80, weight less than or equal to 60 kg, Cr greater than or equal to 133
Dabigatran contraindicated CrCl less than 30 mL/min
Rivaroxaban dose-reduce to 15 mg if CrCl 15-49
Warfarin remains required for mechanical valves and moderate-severe MS
explanation.pearls: Always check the apixaban triad - 80/60/133 - before defaulting to the standard 5 mg BD dose.

q.subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation - CHA2DS2-VA scoring and DOAC selection

q.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with exertional dyspnoea, one episode of syncope while climbing stairs last week, and a 2-month history of chest tightness on hills. He has hypertension on amlodipine, hyperlipidaemia on atorvastatin, and is otherwise well. On auscultation there is an ejection systolic murmur best heard at the right second intercostal space, radiating to both carotids, with a soft S2 and a slow-rising carotid pulse. The murmur is intensity grade 3/6, peaks late in systole, and is louder on squatting. There is no S3, no opening snap, no MR jet, and no pulmonary congestion clinically. His ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern. CXR shows a borderline-enlarged cardiac silhouette without pulmonary oedema. He has never had an echocardiogram. The team specifically asks about each candidate discriminator - the murmur location at the right upper sternal edge, ECG LVH alone, exertional syncope, the slow-rising carotid pulse with narrow pulse pressure, and the dynamic response of the murmur to squatting - and which one best separates severe AS from HOCM today at the bedside. The team specifically wants the one most reliable bedside test.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/72 mmHg (narrow pulse pressure), HR 76 bpm regular, RR 16, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: Slow-rising carotid pulse, soft S2, late-peaking ejection systolic murmur 3/6 RUSB radiating to carotids, louder on squatting. No S3. No mid-systolic click. JVP not elevated. No peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: LVH with strain. CXR: borderline cardiomegaly, clear lungs. Normal troponin and TSH.

q.lead_in: Which finding most strongly distinguishes severe aortic stenosis from hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy in this patient?

options.text: Murmur intensity increases on squatting and falls with Valsalva strain
options.rationale: In AS, increased venous return on squatting augments LV stroke volume and thus murmur intensity, whereas HOCM murmur diminishes on squatting (more LV filling reduces outflow tract obstruction). This dynamic response is the most reliable bedside discriminator (Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease; eTG Cardiovascular).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

options.text: Presence of an ejection systolic murmur at the right upper sternal edge today
options.rationale: Both AS and HOCM produce systolic murmurs in the precordial/parasternal area; location alone does not discriminate (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

options.text: ECG showing left ventricular hypertrophy with associated strain pattern in lateral leads
options.rationale: LVH occurs in both severe AS and HCM; ECG cannot reliably differentiate them (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

options.text: Exertional syncope as a presenting symptom in this elderly patient with murmur
options.rationale: Exertional syncope occurs in both severe AS and HOCM and is a high-risk feature in both (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

options.text: Presence of a slow-rising carotid pulse and a narrow pulse pressure today
options.rationale: Pulsus parvus et tardus does favour AS, but the question asks the strongest discriminator and is asking for a dynamic manoeuvre; squatting response is more specific and is reproducible at the bedside in the same exam (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

explanation.summary: Dynamic auscultation distinguishes fixed valvular outflow obstruction (AS) from dynamic LVOT obstruction (HOCM). Manoeuvres that decrease LV preload or afterload (Valsalva strain phase, standing) increase the HOCM murmur and decrease the AS murmur; manoeuvres that increase preload/afterload (squatting, handgrip) decrease the HOCM murmur and increase or maintain the AS murmur. Echocardiography confirms with peak velocity, mean gradient, AVA (severe AS: less than 1.0 cm2, peak velocity greater than or equal to 4 m/s, mean gradient greater than or equal to 40 mmHg).
key_points: AS murmur: louder with squatting, softer with Valsalva
HOCM murmur: softer with squatting, louder with Valsalva
Severe AS echo: AVA less than 1.0 cm2, peak velocity greater than or equal to 4 m/s, mean gradient greater than or equal to 40 mmHg
MR is holosystolic at apex radiating to axilla, with S3 if severe
Pulsus parvus et tardus favours AS but is insensitive in elderly with stiff vessels
explanation.pearls: Ask the patient to squat then stand during your exam - the murmur change tells you the lesion before the echo does.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease Statement

q.subtopic_detail: Valvular DDx - aortic stenosis vs HCM vs mitral regurgitation

q.stem: A 78-year-old woman presents with 2 days of progressively worsening dyspnoea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain. She had a right total knee replacement 4 weeks ago with delayed mobilisation. She has hypertension on amlodipine, osteoarthritis, and no prior VTE. She is on no anticoagulation since stopping enoxaparin 1 week post-op. She has had no haemoptysis but her right calf is mildly swollen with 2 cm difference compared with the left. On arrival she is haemodynamically stable, SpO2 92 percent RA improving to 96 percent on 2 L. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm with no S1Q3T3 and no right heart strain pattern. CXR is unremarkable. D-dimer is 720 microg/L (laboratory standard cut-off 500 microg/L; age-adjusted cut-off age x 10 microg/L for age greater than 50). The ED registrar specifically asks whether the age-adjusted D-dimer threshold of 780 microg/L safely excludes PE in this patient, whether CTPA with empirical anticoagulation is appropriate, whether V/Q scan should be first-line given the normal CXR, whether to repeat D-dimer in 24 hours, and whether bilateral lower-limb compression ultrasound should precede any chest imaging today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 92% RA improving to 96% on 2 L NP, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Right calf swollen with 2 cm circumference difference, chest clear, no JVP elevation, S1 S2 no S3.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 102 bpm, no S1Q3T3. CXR: unremarkable. D-dimer 720 microg/L. Troponin pending. Creatinine 84 micromol/L.

q.lead_in: Based on Wells score and D-dimer interpretation, which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home - D-dimer is below the age-adjusted threshold of 780 microg/L so PE is excluded
options.rationale: Age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10) is only applied in patients with LOW pretest probability (Wells less than or equal to 4, or two-tier 'PE unlikely'). Her Wells score is 7.5 (clinical DVT signs 3, PE likely as most probable diagnosis 3, HR greater than 100 1.5, recent surgery 1.5), so the D-dimer is not applicable and CTPA is required (eTG; Thrombosis & Haemostasis Society of Australia; BTS).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Order CT pulmonary angiogram and commence empirical therapeutic anticoagulation while awaiting imaging
options.rationale: Her Wells score is high (greater than 4, 'PE likely'); D-dimer should not be used as a rule-out and CTPA is the first-line imaging. Empirical anticoagulation (LMWH or DOAC) is recommended while awaiting imaging if it is delayed beyond a few hours and bleeding risk is acceptable (eTG; THANZ; BTS).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Order V/Q scan as first-line imaging given the normal CXR
options.rationale: V/Q is appropriate when CTPA is contraindicated (severe contrast allergy, severe renal impairment, pregnancy where it may be preferred), but CTPA is first-line when available (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Repeat D-dimer in 24 hours and arrange outpatient CTPA if it rises
options.rationale: Delaying imaging in a high-probability patient is unsafe; PE mortality is concentrated in the first 24 hours (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Arrange bilateral lower-limb compression ultrasound first and only proceed to CTPA if positive
options.rationale: Lower-limb US is useful when imaging the chest is contraindicated, or to add evidence of VTE in equivocal cases, but it is not the first-line test when PE is the principal clinical concern (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

explanation.summary: Wells score for PE risk-stratifies into 'PE unlikely' (less than or equal to 4) and 'PE likely' (greater than 4). In 'PE unlikely', age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 microg/L if greater than 50) safely excludes PE if below threshold. In 'PE likely', proceed directly to CTPA; D-dimer is not applicable. Empirical anticoagulation while awaiting imaging is recommended if delay is expected and bleeding risk is acceptable.
key_points: Wells PE score: DVT signs 3, PE most likely dx 3, HR greater than 100 1.5, immobilisation/surgery 1.5, prior VTE 1.5, haemoptysis 1, malignancy 1
Wells greater than 4: PE likely - go to CTPA
Wells less than or equal to 4: PE unlikely - use D-dimer (age-adjusted if greater than 50)
V/Q if CTPA contraindicated
Empirical anticoagulation while awaiting CTPA if delay and acceptable bleeding risk
explanation.pearls: Age-adjusted D-dimer is only valid in 'PE unlikely' - using it in a high-probability patient gives false reassurance.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

q.subtopic_detail: Pulmonary embolism - Wells score and age-adjusted D-dimer

q.stem: A 64-year-old man presents to ED with 3 days of fever, productive cough with rusty sputum, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and progressive dyspnoea. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history, hypertension on perindopril, and no known drug allergies. He has not travelled recently and has had no contact with unwell people. He has not had a pneumococcal or influenza vaccine in the past 12 months. On arrival he is alert but unwell-looking. His CXR shows dense right middle lobe consolidation with a small parapneumonic effusion. Bloods show WCC 18.4 x 10^9/L with neutrophilia, CRP 280 mg/L, albumin 28 g/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, urea 9.2 mmol/L, glucose 7.8 mmol/L. ABG on room air shows pH 7.42, pO2 58 mmHg, pCO2 36 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L. The ED registrar specifically asks whether to admit to a general ward with oral amoxicillin and doxycycline, to discharge home with oral moxifloxacin based on a CURB-65 of 1, to admit to a general ward on IV benzylpenicillin monotherapy, or to admit to ICU with empirical IV vancomycin and meropenem covering MRSA and Pseudomonas. HDU and ICU beds are available.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/64 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, RR 28, SpO2 90% RA, T 38.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Alert, dehydrated, dullness and bronchial breathing right lower zone, no neck stiffness, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: dense right middle lobe consolidation with small effusion. WCC 18.4, CRP 280, albumin 28 g/L, creatinine 96, urea 9.2, glucose 7.8. ABG: pH 7.42, pO2 58, pCO2 36, HCO3 24.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition and initial empirical antibiotic therapy?

options.text: Admit to general ward today with oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for atypical cover
options.rationale: His SMART-COP score is 6 (SBP less than 90 = 2, multilobar/effusion 1, RR greater than or equal to 25 1, tachycardia greater than or equal to 125 0, confusion 0, oxygenation pO2 less than 70 = 2, pH normal 0, albumin less than 35 = 1; SBP scores 2). With SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 (high risk of needing intensive respiratory or vasopressor support), oral therapy and general ward are inadequate (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to high-dependency or ICU; IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV azithromycin 500 mg daily, with empirical cover for atypicals
options.rationale: SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 indicates a high risk of needing intensive respiratory or vasopressor support and warrants HDU/ICU-level care. Empirical therapy for severe CAP in Australia is IV ceftriaxone plus IV azithromycin (Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory).

options.text: Discharge home with a 7-day course of oral moxifloxacin 400 mg once daily based on a CURB-65 of only 1 today
options.rationale: Even if CURB-65 underestimates severity in this case, his hypotension, hypoxia, and metabolic derangements meet inpatient criteria; SMART-COP is more sensitive for need for intensive support in younger patients (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to general ward today with intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g six-hourly monotherapy and review at 48 hours for response to therapy
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy does not cover Mycoplasma, Legionella, or atypicals and is inadequate empirical therapy for severe CAP requiring ICU-level support (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to ICU today with intravenous vancomycin 1.5 g twelve-hourly plus intravenous meropenem 1 g eight-hourly for empirical broad-spectrum coverage
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antipseudomonal and MRSA cover is reserved for patients with risk factors (structural lung disease, recent hospitalisation, recent broad-spectrum antibiotics, MRSA colonisation); he has none (eTG Respiratory).

explanation.summary: Australian severity scoring for CAP uses SMART-COP (intensive-support prediction) alongside CURB-65 (mortality prediction). SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 indicates high risk of needing inotropic or respiratory support, warranting HDU/ICU. Severe CAP empirical therapy in Australia is IV ceftriaxone plus IV azithromycin (or doxycycline) to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae plus atypicals.
key_points: SMART-COP: S BP less than 90 (2), M ultilobar (1), A lbumin less than 35 (1), R R greater than or equal to 25 (greater than or equal to 50 yo) (1), T achycardia greater than or equal to 125 (1), C onfusion (1), O xygen low (2), P H less than 7.35 (2)
SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 = high risk of intensive support
CURB-65: Confusion, Urea greater than 7, RR greater than or equal to 30, BP less than 90 sys or less than or equal to 60 dia, age greater than or equal to 65
Severe CAP: IV ceftriaxone + IV azithromycin (or doxycycline)
Step down to oral after 48 hours of clinical improvement
explanation.pearls: SMART-COP outperforms CURB-65 for predicting ICU need in younger patients - use both.

q.subtopic_detail: Community-acquired pneumonia - SMART-COP severity and antibiotic choice

q.stem: A 76-year-old woman presents at 3 am with 8 hours of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and a dry cough. She has HFrEF (LVEF 35 percent) on perindopril, bisoprolol, frusemide, and dapagliflozin; type 2 diabetes on metformin; and known paroxysmal AF on apixaban 5 mg twice daily. She was discharged 10 days ago after a 5-day admission for cellulitis. Since then she has been less mobile, sitting in her chair most of the day. She denies fever, chest pain, or haemoptysis. On arrival she is afebrile but distressed. JVP is elevated to the angle of the jaw at 60 degrees. There are fine bibasal crackles to the mid-zones and trace ankle oedema bilaterally. Her ECG shows AF at 118 bpm without acute ischaemic changes. CXR shows bilateral interstitial opacities with upper lobe blood diversion. Troponin I is 22 ng/L (cut-off less than 16, stable on repeat). NT-proBNP is 6800 ng/L. D-dimer is 1800 microg/L. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and small bilateral pleural effusions. Right calf circumference is 1 cm greater than left without erythema. Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 110). The ED registrar specifically asks whether to manage as ADHF with IV diuretic, nitrate and reserve CTPA for inadequate response, whether to treat as suspected PE with immediate CTPA and empirical LMWH despite therapeutic apixaban, whether to treat as severe community-acquired pneumonia with IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin, whether to activate the cath lab for primary PCI given the troponin elevation, or whether to defer imaging and shotgun all three pathways simultaneously.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/92 mmHg, HR 118 bpm AF, RR 28, SpO2 89% RA improving to 94% on 6 L Hudson, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, JVP elevated, bibasal crackles to mid-zones, trace ankle oedema bilaterally, right calf 1 cm greater than left.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: AF rate 118, no acute ischaemia. CXR: bilateral interstitial opacities with upper lobe diversion. Troponin I 22 ng/L (stable). NT-proBNP 6800 ng/L. D-dimer 1800 microg/L. POCUS: bilateral B-lines and small pleural effusions. Creatinine 142 (baseline 110).
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, dapagliflozin 10 mg, apixaban 5 mg BD, metformin 1 g BD.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic and management step?

options.text: Treat as acute decompensated heart failure with IV frusemide and GTN, and arrange CT pulmonary angiogram only if response is inadequate within 6 hours
options.rationale: Clinical, biochemical, and POCUS evidence (elevated JVP, NT-proBNP greater than 4000, B-lines, upper lobe diversion, congestion) points to ADHF as the primary problem; the elevated D-dimer is non-specific in HF and AF on apixaban makes PE unlikely. Initial management is decongestion with IV diuretic and nitrate, with CTPA reserved for inadequate response or evolving features of PE (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF; eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Treat as suspected pulmonary embolism with immediate CT pulmonary angiogram and empirical therapeutic-dose enoxaparin, withholding all diuretics until the imaging result is available for review
options.rationale: She is on therapeutic apixaban, has no haemodynamic instability, and her presentation is dominated by congestive features rather than pleuritic chest pain or focal hypoxia. D-dimer is elevated as expected in HF/inflammation and is not a rule-in test (THANZ; eTG).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Treat as severe community-acquired pneumonia with intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus intravenous azithromycin 500 mg daily and admit to a high-dependency monitored bed for observation
options.rationale: She is afebrile with no productive cough or focal consolidation; CXR pattern and BNP favour congestion not infection. Antibiotics are not first-line in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Activate the cardiac catheter lab for emergency primary percutaneous coronary intervention given the troponin elevation, AF with rapid ventricular response and bibasal pulmonary congestion on examination
options.rationale: The minor troponin elevation is stable and likely demand-related from the AF with rapid ventricular response and decompensated HF; there is no ECG evidence of STEMI and no NSTEMI dynamic rise. Primary PCI is not indicated (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

options.text: Defer all imaging and start broad empirical combination therapy targeting pulmonary embolism, community-acquired pneumonia and acute decompensated heart failure simultaneously without further bedside diagnostic disambiguation today
options.rationale: Shotgun therapy without diagnostic clarity exposes her to harm (broad antibiotics in non-infection, bleeding from triple-pathway anticoagulation on apixaban). The clinical picture is dominated by congestion and warrants targeted decongestion first (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF; eTG).

explanation.summary: Acute breathlessness in the elderly with multiple plausible aetiologies is best disambiguated by integrating bedside POCUS (B-lines, pleural effusion, RV size), biomarkers (NT-proBNP very high favours HF; troponin minor elevation often demand), CXR pattern (upper lobe diversion, Kerley B), and pretest probability for PE (already on therapeutic DOAC reduces probability). Treat the dominant diagnosis first; reassess at 4 to 6 hours and escalate imaging if response is inadequate.
key_points: NT-proBNP greater than 4000 ng/L strongly favours HF as the dominant problem
D-dimer is non-specific in HF, AF, recent infection - do not over-interpret
POCUS B-lines plus elevated JVP plus upper lobe diversion = cardiogenic congestion
Therapeutic DOAC compliance significantly reduces PE probability
Demand troponin in AF with RVR and HF is common and stable
explanation.pearls: Reach for the diuretic first, the CTPA second - reassess at 6 hours and pivot if her congestion improves but hypoxia persists.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

q.subtopic_detail: Acute breathlessness - integrative diagnosis between PE, ADHF, and CAP

q.stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 0200 brought by police after his housemate found him writing letters to family members and packing personal effects into labelled boxes. He recently separated from his fiancee and was dismissed from his job in mining 3 weeks ago. He has a 6-month history of worsening depression treated by his GP with escitalopram 20 mg daily. Tonight he tells you he obtained his late father's licensed firearm from his mother's garage on the weekend and stored it under his bed. He has researched suicide methods online for the past fortnight, has selected a remote bush location, and has chosen tomorrow as the day. He denies prior attempts, has no current intoxication (breath alcohol 0.00), and identifies his younger sister and his dog as the only things keeping him alive at present. He has no psychotic features, no medical comorbidities, and no allergies. Mental health triage rates him as Category 2 and the consultation-liaison registrar is on the way. His parents have been notified and his mother is en route from rural NSW.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 84, T 36.5 deg C, breath alcohol 0.00%, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: MSE: anxious, restricted affect, future plans focused only on death, no psychosis, insight partial, judgment impaired by hopelessness.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods unremarkable. Risk assessment summarised in stem.

q.lead_in: What is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: Discharge home with a written safety plan and beyondblue helpline contact details
options.rationale: Multiple high-risk features (specific plan, lethal means access, chosen date and location, recent stressors, hopelessness, final acts) make discharge unsafe; written safety planning alone is not sufficient when imminent risk is high (Black Dog Institute; LIFE in Mind).

options.text: Increase his escitalopram to 30 mg daily and reassess in clinic in one week
options.rationale: Dose escalation does not address acute risk in the next 24 hours; maximum recommended escitalopram dose is 20 mg (10 mg in elderly) and antidepressants can transiently worsen agitation in under-25s (Australian Medicines Handbook).

options.text: Remove the firearm via police and admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Means restriction (especially of firearms) is the single most evidence-based intervention to reduce suicide death; combined with admission under the state Mental Health Act when at imminent risk, this is the priority action (Black Dog Institute; RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; NSW/Vic Mental Health Acts).

options.text: Administer IM olanzapine 10 mg for chemical restraint and continued observation
options.rationale: He is not currently agitated, aggressive or psychotic; chemical restraint is reserved for behavioural emergencies and is inappropriate as primary suicide-prevention strategy (eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Have him sign a no-suicide contract and discharge to housemate supervision overnight
options.rationale: No-suicide contracts lack evidence of efficacy and may give false reassurance; lay supervision in the community does not equate to clinical safety with a loaded firearm at home (Black Dog Institute).

explanation.summary: Highest-evidence interventions to prevent suicide are restriction of access to lethal means (firearms, pesticides, large medication supplies, jumping points), assessment and management of underlying disorders, and structured follow-up. A safety plan is a useful adjunct but not a substitute for means restriction or admission when risk is imminent.
