key_points: Risk-magnifiers: specific plan, means access, chosen date/location, final acts, hopelessness, prior attempt, male, recent stressor, substance use, psychotic depression
Means restriction is the single most evidence-based intervention
Safety plan (Stanley/Brown): warning signs, internal coping, social distractions, contacts, professional/crisis services, means-restriction
Australian crisis: Lifeline 13 11 14, 13YARN 13 92 76, Suicide Call Back 1300 659 467, Kids Helpline 1800 55 1800
MHA admission criteria differ by state but include mental illness + risk to self/others + lack of less restrictive option
explanation.pearls: Always ask specifically about firearms in rural and ex-defence/police patients - they are the most lethal Australian suicide method per capita.

q.subtopic_detail: Suicide risk assessment and safety planning

q.stem: A 64-year-old woman maintained on lithium carbonate 500 mg nocte for bipolar I disorder for 12 years is brought to the emergency department by her daughter after 4 days of worsening coarse hand tremor, unsteady gait with two falls, slurred speech and confusion. Her usual psychiatrist documented her last lithium level 4 months ago as 0.7 mmol/L with normal renal function. Two weeks ago her GP commenced indapamide 2.5 mg daily for newly diagnosed hypertension and naproxen 500 mg BD for an osteoarthritic knee flare. She has been drinking less water than usual during a current heatwave (37 deg C ambient). She has no fever, no diarrhoea, no infective symptoms, and no chest pain. On arrival she is drowsy but rousable and has a coarse postural tremor, ataxic gait and mild dysarthria. There is no clonus, no rigidity, no diaphoresis and no mydriasis. ECG shows sinus rhythm with T-wave flattening. Her daughter brought all medication boxes and there has been no extra dosing or accidental ingestion Initial bedside lithium level returns at 3.8 mmol/L, creatinine has risen from baseline 70 to 168 micromol/L and sodium is 142 mmol/L with no anion-gap acidosis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 62, T 36.7 deg C, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, GCS 14 (E3 V5 M6).
data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor, mild dysarthria, broad-based ataxic gait, hyporeflexia bilaterally, no clonus, no rigidity.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 2.4 mmol/L. Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.6, urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 78), eGFR 38. TSH 2.1 mIU/L. Calcium 2.42 mmol/L. VBG: pH 7.39, lactate 1.8. ECG: sinus rhythm, T-wave flattening, QTc 420 ms.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally and reduce lithium to half dose tonight
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium and is not used in lithium toxicity; lithium must be withheld, not dose-reduced (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg and observation on the ward overnight
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation has minimal effect on lithium elimination; volume replacement is supportive but dialysis is indicated for severe toxicity (eTG Toxicology).

options.text: Continue lithium and recheck a 12-hour post-dose level in 6 hours
options.rationale: Continuing lithium in severe toxicity is dangerous; priority is removal of the agent and renal protection (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH).

options.text: Withhold lithium and naproxen only, continue indapamide and IV fluids
options.rationale: All three of indapamide, NSAID and dehydration must be addressed; thiazides increase proximal tubular lithium reabsorption and significantly raise levels (AMH; eTG).

options.text: Withhold all three drugs, IV 0.9% saline, urgent dialysis with nephrology
options.rationale: Severe lithium toxicity (level > 2.5 mmol/L OR > 2.0 mmol/L with neurological symptoms OR > 1.5 mmol/L with renal impairment) is an indication for haemodialysis per Therapeutic Guidelines; she has neurological compromise, AKI and a 2.4 mmol/L level (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH; Austin Health Poisons).

explanation.summary: Chronic lithium toxicity is precipitated by reduced clearance (NSAIDs, thiazides, ACE inhibitors, dehydration, AKI). Severity is judged by clinical features and level. Haemodialysis indications: level > 4.0 mmol/L regardless of symptoms; > 2.5 mmol/L with severe neurological features or renal impairment; > 1.5 mmol/L with severe features and inability to excrete.
key_points: Mild toxicity (1.5-2.5 mmol/L): tremor, GI upset, weakness - withhold drug, IV saline, monitor
Severe (> 2.5 mmol/L or symptomatic with renal failure): dialysis
Common precipitants: NSAIDs, thiazides, ACEi/ARB, dehydration, low-salt diet, AKI
Lithium also causes nephrogenic DI, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism with chronic use
Levels rebound post-dialysis: serial levels and often a second session required
explanation.pearls: In a heatwave, counsel lithium patients to maintain hydration and consider holding the dose if vomiting or diarrhoea; brief breaks are safer than waiting until they are ataxic.

q.subtopic_detail: Lithium toxicity precipitated by drug interaction

q.stem: A 31-year-old primipara is brought to the emergency department by her partner on day 9 postpartum following a normal vaginal birth at 39+5 weeks of a healthy girl. Over the past 36 hours her partner reports rapid escalation of symptoms: she has slept only 2 hours in 48 hours despite the baby sleeping, is convinced the midwives implanted a tracker in the baby's umbilical stump, hears her late mother's voice instructing her, and at times is bright and over-talkative, at other times tearful and withdrawn. This morning she said the baby was sent by spirits and should be returned to them. She has not been violent but is intermittently dishevelled. She has no past psychiatric history but her older sister has bipolar I. She had no antenatal mental health concerns and EPDS at 28 weeks was 5. She is breastfeeding intermittently. There are no infective symptoms, no headache, no neurological signs and recent obstetric review and bloods are unremarkable. Her partner is willing to stay with her but has expressed serious safety concerns about leaving her unsupervised with the baby.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 92, T 36.8 deg C, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: MSE: labile affect, pressured speech alternating with whispering, paranoid and bizarre delusions, second-person auditory hallucinations, partial insight.
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, CRP 4 mg/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, calcium normal, urine MCS no growth, urine drug screen negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Reassurance, daily community midwife visits and sertraline 50 mg daily with GP review weekly
options.rationale: This is postpartum psychosis, NOT postnatal depression; SSRI monotherapy and outpatient management do not address imminent risk to mother and baby (COPE Perinatal Guidelines).

options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV aciclovir for suspected postpartum HSV encephalitis
options.rationale: No fever, headache, neurological deficit; normal CRP and strong bipolar family history make postpartum psychosis most likely; LP not the first step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral diazepam 10 mg and discharge home overnight with the partner supervising
options.rationale: Discharge of a psychotic mother with an infant at home is unsafe; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychosis (RANZCP; COPE).

options.text: Admit to a Mother-Baby Unit, start antipsychotic, plan infant safety with partner
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with high suicide and infanticide risk; immediate admission (MBU preferred, general psychiatric ward if MBU unavailable) plus antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone), mood stabiliser if bipolar, and safety planning are standard (COPE; RANZCP; Black Dog Institute).

options.text: Repeat EPDS in 2 weeks and refer to perinatal psychologist for non-urgent assessment
options.rationale: EPDS screens for depression and is not the right tool for psychosis; non-urgent referral is unsafe given imminent risk (COPE Perinatal Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects 1-2 per 1000 births, typically presents in the first 2 weeks postpartum with rapid onset, fluctuating mood, confusion, paranoia and bizarre delusions often involving the baby. It is a psychiatric emergency with significant suicide and infanticide risk. Family history of bipolar disorder is a strong risk factor. Distinguish from baby blues (peaks day 3-5, self-limiting) and PND (gradual onset, mood-predominant).
key_points: Postpartum psychosis: onset typically days 3-14, abrupt, fluctuating, mixed mood/psychotic features
Bipolar family history is the strongest risk factor
Treatment: admit (MBU preferred), antipsychotic +/- mood stabiliser, ECT if severe/refractory
Postnatal depression: gradual onset, mood-predominant, screen with EPDS
Baby blues: peaks day 3-5, self-limiting <2 weeks, no specific treatment
explanation.pearls: Any new-onset psychosis in the first month postpartum is postpartum psychosis until proven otherwise - never discharge home overnight pending review.

q.subtopic_detail: Perinatal psychiatry - postpartum psychosis recognition

q.stem: A 28-year-old woman has had recurrent depression since adolescence. She describes that her mood lifts noticeably when good things happen (a friend visits, she gets praised at work) but plunges back within hours. She sleeps 11 hours daily including a 2-hour afternoon nap, has gained 12 kg in 9 months, experiences a profound leaden paralysis sensation in her limbs that makes physical activity feel impossible, and is acutely sensitive to perceived criticism or rejection (a tone of voice from her supervisor preoccupies her for days). She has had two prior trials of antidepressants: sertraline 100 mg for 10 weeks (PHQ-9 16 to 12) and escitalopram 20 mg for 12 weeks (no further improvement). She has no manic or hypomanic episodes, no psychotic features, no substance use, no eating disorder. She has well-controlled hypothyroidism on thyroxine 75 mcg with TSH 1.8. There are no other medical comorbidities, no allergies, and she does not drive. She is engaged in regular CBT with a psychologist for 4 months without major change. She and her psychiatrist have discussed the practicalities of dietary restriction and consider her able to comply.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 72, weight 84 kg, BMI 30.
data_table.Examination: MSE: depressed mood with episodic brightening, weight gain, hypersomnolence, leaden affect; no psychotic features. MMSE 30/30.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 18, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, FBC, UEC, LFTs unremarkable, fasting BSL 5.2, HbA1c 5.6%.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Add nortriptyline 50 mg orally nocte to her current escitalopram therapy
options.rationale: Adding a TCA to an SSRI risks elevated TCA levels (CYP2D6-inhibited SSRIs) and is not the recommended next step in atypical depression; evidence favours MAOI for atypical features (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

options.text: Increase escitalopram beyond licensed maximum and add bupropion adjunct
options.rationale: Exceeding licensed maximum dose risks QT prolongation (escitalopram) without proven benefit; bupropion is not registered for depression in Australia (only as Zyban for smoking cessation) (AMH; eTG).

options.text: Cross-taper to phenelzine MAOI with washout, dietary restriction and specialist care
options.rationale: Atypical depression features (mood reactivity, hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity) classically respond preferentially to MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) over SSRIs/TCAs; after two failed SSRI trials, escalation under specialist supervision with washout and dietary education is appropriate (eTG Psychotropic; AMH; RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Substitute mirtazapine 30 mg orally nocte for her current escitalopram dose
options.rationale: Mirtazapine causes further weight gain and sedation, worsening her existing weight gain and hypersomnia, and lacks specific evidence for atypical features (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Add levothyroxine 25 mcg to her current dose despite her normal TSH
options.rationale: Thyroid augmentation (T3) is sometimes used in TRD but adding T4 in a euthyroid patient does not have evidence for atypical features and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis (AMH; eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Atypical depression (DSM-5 specifier) = mood reactivity + 2 or more of significant weight gain or appetite increase, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, long-standing interpersonal rejection sensitivity. Historical RCTs (Liebowitz) demonstrated superior response to MAOIs over TCAs in atypical depression. After failure of two adequate SSRI trials, escalation to an MAOI under specialist supervision is a defensible next step, with strict attention to washout periods and dietary restriction.
key_points: Atypical features: mood reactivity + >=2 of weight gain/hyperphagia, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity
MAOIs: phenelzine, tranylcypromine, moclobemide (RIMA - less dietary restriction)
MAOI washouts: 2 weeks from most SSRIs, 5 weeks from fluoxetine, 1+ week from SNRI
Tyramine-rich foods to avoid with irreversible MAOIs: aged cheese, cured meats, fermented soy, broad bean pods, draught beer
Always check for occult bipolarity (atypical depression look-alike in young women)
explanation.pearls: If atypical features are accompanied by hypomanic episodes, the diagnosis is bipolar II and an MAOI is inappropriate without mood stabiliser cover - re-screen carefully before initiation.

q.subtopic_detail: Atypical depression - feature recognition and MAOI escalation

q.subtopic: Paediatric DKA cerebral oedema - risk recognition in young adult
q.stem: An 18-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes is admitted to a regional ED with severe DKA. Capillary glucose is 38 mmol/L, ketones 6.4 mmol/L, venous pH 6.98, HCO3 5 mmol/L, sodium 128 mmol/L, potassium 3.6 mmol/L, urea 11 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L. She received 2 L of 0.9 percent saline over the first hour for shock, followed by 0.9 percent saline at 500 mL/hour and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Five hours into treatment, blood glucose has fallen to 12 mmol/L and bicarbonate has risen to 11 mmol/L, but she develops a worsening frontal headache, increasing drowsiness with a GCS drop from 15 to 11, a single episode of vomiting and a slowing heart rate. Her blood pressure has risen from 102/64 to 142/86 mmHg. Capillary glucose is now 11 mmol/L and ketones 2.4 mmol/L. The receiving consultant is concerned about a deteriorating neurological picture. A bedside neurological exam reveals sluggish pupils and brisk lower limb reflexes. She has no haemoglobinopathy, no known intracranial pathology and no history of seizures. There was no head injury or fall before the deterioration. Retrieval to a tertiary paediatric ICU is being considered and the airway tray, mannitol, hypertonic saline and intubation drugs are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86 mmHg (up from 102/64), HR 58 bpm (down from 118), RR 18, SpO2 99 percent, T 36.6, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5).
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Sluggish pupillary response, no focal weakness. Brisk LL reflexes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 11 mmol/L (was 38); ketones 2.4; pH 7.18, HCO3 11; Na 130, K 3.8.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Increase the IV fluid infusion rate to correct the remaining estimated deficit much faster
options.rationale: Excessive fluid administration is a contributor to cerebral oedema in DKA; the appropriate response to suspected cerebral oedema is to halve or pause maintenance fluids, not increase them (BSPED / RCH CPG DKA).

options.text: Hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, halve fluids, head up, CT and retrieval
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline (or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg) is first-line for suspected cerebral oedema in DKA. Halving fluids, head-up positioning, securing airway and urgent imaging plus retrieval to a tertiary centre are concurrent priorities (RCH CPG: DKA; BSPED 2020).

options.text: Stop the IV insulin infusion entirely and simply observe her for any spontaneous improvement
options.rationale: Stopping insulin allows ketogenesis to resume and does not treat the rising intracranial pressure; insulin should be continued (often reduced) while neuroprotective measures are delivered (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Give IV 50 percent dextrose 50 mL because the cause of drowsiness is hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose 11 mmol/L is not hypoglycaemic; when DKA glucose falls below 14 mmol/L, the correct action is to add 5 or 10 percent dextrose to the maintenance fluid rather than push concentrated dextrose (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Administer IV ceftriaxone 2 g and dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: While headache and drowsiness raise meningitis as a differential, the pattern of Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia) and timing in DKA make cerebral oedema the most likely diagnosis; empirical antibiotics should not delay osmotherapy (RCH CPG: DKA).

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is a feared complication of DKA, especially in younger patients and the first 4 to 12 hours of treatment. Risk factors include severe acidosis, low partial pressure of CO2, raised urea, bicarbonate administration and aggressive fluid replacement. Treatment is osmotherapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol), reduced fluid rate, head-up positioning and urgent imaging plus retrieval (RCH CPG; BSPED 2020).
key_points: Triad of headache, decreased GCS and Cushing reflex in DKA = cerebral oedema until proven otherwise
Hypertonic saline 3 percent 5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg
Halve maintenance fluids, head up 30 degrees, secure airway if GCS drops further
Do NOT correct sodium too rapidly; corrected sodium should rise as glucose falls
explanation.pearls: Late teens with first presentation DKA, severe acidosis and hyponatraemia carry the highest risk - low threshold for retrieval to a paediatric ICU.

q.stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred by her GP for definitive management of Graves disease diagnosed 14 months ago. She was treated with carbimazole 20 mg daily for 12 months, achieving biochemical euthyroidism, but TSH-receptor antibody titres remain markedly elevated (TRAb 8 IU/L) and she relapsed within six weeks of stopping carbimazole. She has moderate active Graves orbitopathy with proptosis, mild diplopia on upgaze and intermittent retro-orbital pain; CAS (Clinical Activity Score) is 4/7. She has not yet completed her family and would like to conceive within the next 12 months. She is a non-smoker now (ex-smoker, ceased six months ago). Her thyroid is moderately enlarged without nodularity, and ultrasound shows no discrete nodules. She has no compressive symptoms. She has no contraindication to glucocorticoid prophylaxis. She lives with a partner and two pre-school children. She has no documented allergy to carbimazole or propylthiouracil, no prior neck surgery and no compressive symptoms. Her thyroid function tests are biochemically euthyroid on her current dose and her last full blood count and liver enzymes are normal. She has discussed all three options (long-term antithyroid drug, radioactive iodine and total thyroidectomy) with her endocrinologist. There is access to a high-volume thyroid surgeon at her local tertiary referral hospital, and pre-operative steroid prophylaxis is available if needed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88, weight 62 kg.
data_table.Examination: Diffuse smooth goitre, no nodules, no compressive symptoms. Proptosis with diplopia on upgaze, CAS 4/7.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.2 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L on carbimazole 20 mg; TRAb 8 IU/L (relapsed off treatment); thyroid US no nodules.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment option?

options.text: Radioactive iodine 131I ablation arranged within the next month as definitive therapy
options.rationale: RAI can worsen active Graves orbitopathy, especially with moderate-to-severe disease, smoking history and high TRAb; pregnancy must be avoided for 6 months after RAI, conflicting with her conception plans (ESA / ETA 2021).

options.text: Total thyroidectomy after pre-operative carbimazole and Lugol's iodine plus calcium and vitamin D loading
options.rationale: In Graves disease with moderate-to-severe active orbitopathy, large goitre, plans for pregnancy within 12 months, or patient preference, total thyroidectomy is preferred. It removes the antigenic stimulus, does not worsen orbitopathy and allows immediate trial of conception once levothyroxine is stable. Pre-operative preparation and a high-volume surgeon reduce complications (ETA 2021; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Indefinite low-dose carbimazole 5 mg once daily as long-term outpatient monotherapy
options.rationale: Long-term low-dose carbimazole is an emerging option for selected patients but is less effective at definitive cure; high TRAb, relapse history and active orbitopathy favour definitive treatment, and carbimazole carries teratogenicity risk in early pregnancy (eTG; AMH).

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil 100 mg three times daily continued indefinitely for ongoing control
options.rationale: PTU has a higher risk of severe hepatotoxicity than carbimazole and is reserved for first trimester of pregnancy or thyroid storm; it is not preferred for long-term outpatient management (AMH).

options.text: High-dose IV methylprednisolone 500 mg weekly for 6 weeks given alone as definitive treatment
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone treats active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy but does not treat the underlying hyperthyroidism; it complements but does not replace definitive thyroid management (ETA 2021).

explanation.summary: Choice between carbimazole, radioactive iodine and thyroidectomy in Graves disease is guided by relapse risk (TRAb titre, goitre size, age, gender), pregnancy plans and orbitopathy status. Active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy is a relative contraindication to RAI; pregnancy within 6 months precludes RAI; large goitre or compressive symptoms favour surgery (ETA 2021; eTG).
key_points: Carbimazole 12 to 18 months: ~50 percent relapse after stopping
RAI: definitive but worsens orbitopathy in ~15 percent unless steroid prophylaxis given; defer pregnancy 6 months
Surgery: rapid cure, preserves option of pregnancy soon, removes antigen in severe orbitopathy
PTU reserved for first trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm
explanation.pearls: Smoking strongly worsens Graves orbitopathy and RAI outcomes - cessation is mandatory.

q.subtopic: Hyponatraemia - SIADH vs cerebral salt wasting after SAH
q.stem: A 52-year-old man is on day 7 of his admission to neurosurgery after a Hunt-Hess 3 aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage clipped on day 1. On the morning ward round his nurse reports increased confusion and lethargy. His urine output over the last 24 hours has been 4.2 L. He has been on free water orally without IV fluids since day 4. Bloods today: sodium 122 mmol/L (was 134 on day 4), potassium 3.6, urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, serum osmolality 258 mosm/kg, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. Urine: sodium 95 mmol/L, osmolality 540 mosm/kg, specific gravity 1.022. He is on nimodipine. Clinically he has dry mucous membranes, BP 102/64 mmHg (was 128/74 yesterday) lying with a 14 mmHg systolic drop on standing, HR 96, normal JVP and no oedema. Bedside lung ultrasound shows A-lines bilaterally. Weight is down 2 kg from admission. He has no haematuria, no glycosuria and no recent contrast. He is being managed on the neurosurgery ward under a neurosurgery-led delayed cerebral ischaemia protocol that targets euvolaemia and avoids hypotonic fluids. His transcranial Doppler velocities yesterday were not elevated and he has had no new focal neurological deficit. His preceding bloods on day 4 had been normal apart from mild hypokalaemia. He has had no diuretic, no mannitol, and no recent IV contrast load. Cortisol stimulation is normal, thyroid function is normal, and he has not received DDAVP. The team are considering whether the underlying mechanism is SIADH or cerebral salt wasting, because the management of these two conditions is opposite and the wrong choice risks delayed cerebral ischaemia, rebleeding or osmotic demyelination.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 lying with 14 mmHg systolic postural drop, HR 96, T 36.7, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, JVP not visible, no oedema, weight down 2 kg.
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (was 134), K 3.6, urea 9.4, Cr 96, serum osm 258; urine Na 95, urine osm 540; UO 4.2 L/24h.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial fluid management?

options.text: Fluid restriction to 800 mL per day with daily sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is appropriate for SIADH (euvolaemic), but this patient is hypovolaemic with polyuria, weight loss and postural hypotension after SAH - restricting fluid would worsen cerebral hypoperfusion and the risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia (Australasian Stroke Foundation Guidelines; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 125 mL/hour with oral salt or 3 percent saline if sodium fails to rise
options.rationale: The clinical picture (polyuria, weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes after SAH) plus high urine sodium fits cerebral salt wasting (CSW), where treatment is volume and sodium replacement, not restriction. Maintaining euvolaemia and avoiding hyponatraemia reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia in SAH (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally given today with hourly sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Tolvaptan (vasopressin V2 antagonist) treats SIADH by increasing free water clearance; it would worsen hypovolaemic hyponatraemia and is not appropriate here (AMH).

options.text: IV 3 percent hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus over 10 minutes only, with no further fluid
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline bolus is appropriate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures or coma (target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L), but a single bolus without ongoing maintenance volume in CSW will under-treat the volume depletion and risk overshooting sodium correction (ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 5 percent dextrose at 100 mL/hour to reduce osmotic stress on the brain
options.rationale: Free water in 5 percent dextrose worsens hyponatraemia and is contraindicated; in any neurocritical patient it also increases intracranial water content (ENLS 2023).

explanation.summary: After SAH or other brain injury, distinguishing SIADH from CSW depends on volume status. Both have low serum osmolality and inappropriately concentrated urine with high urine sodium. CSW shows hypovolaemia (weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes, polyuria); SIADH shows euvolaemia. CSW is treated with isotonic saline plus sodium replacement; SIADH with fluid restriction (or tolvaptan if persistent). Limit sodium correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination (ENLS 2023; Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: Volume status is THE distinguishing feature between SIADH and CSW
After SAH, hypotonic fluids are avoided; isotonic or hypertonic preferred
Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: 3 percent saline 150 mL bolus, target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L
Limit correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 h (6 to 8 if high risk for ODS)
explanation.pearls: If sodium overcorrects, re-lower with DDAVP and 5 percent dextrose - cerebral pontine myelinolysis is devastating and preventable.

q.subtopic: Acute ischaemic stroke - thrombolysis and thrombectomy eligibility
q.stem: A 68-year-old right-handed man is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance. He was last seen well at 0815 by his wife; she returned at 1005 to find him slumped on the kitchen floor with right-sided weakness and aphasia. Time of arrival is 1110 (2 hours 55 minutes after last seen well). He has hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VA 4) on apixaban 5 mg BD with his last dose at 0700. He has no history of recent surgery, head injury, bleeding or stroke. On arrival his blood pressure is 198/112 mmHg, glucose 6.8 mmol/L, GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6) and NIHSS is 18 (gaze preference, right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left neglect). Non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage and an ASPECTS of 8 in the left MCA territory. CT angiography demonstrates a proximal left M1 occlusion with reasonable collaterals. There is a comprehensive stroke centre with thrombectomy capability 15 minutes away by road. There is no recent gastrointestinal bleeding, no recent intracranial surgery, no platelet count below 100 and no documented INR above 1.7. He has consented for thrombectomy through his next of kin and the neurointerventional team have been activated. He has no known iodinated contrast allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 198/112, HR 92 (AF), T 36.8, SpO2 97 percent on RA, GCS 12.
data_table.Examination: Right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left gaze preference. NIHSS 18.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 6.8 mmol/L; INR not on warfarin; apixaban 5 mg BD with last dose 0700; CT brain no haemorrhage, ASPECTS 8; CTA proximal M1 occlusion.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in acute reperfusion management?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase immediately and then transfer for mechanical thrombectomy at the comprehensive stroke centre
options.rationale: IV thrombolysis is contraindicated within 48 hours of a direct oral anticoagulant unless DOAC levels are confirmed sub-therapeutic; his last apixaban dose was at 0700. Transfer for mechanical thrombectomy is preferred without thrombolysis (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Lower BP to under 140/90 mmHg with IV labetalol before making any reperfusion decision
options.rationale: BP target prior to thrombolysis is <185/110 mmHg; before thrombectomy without thrombolysis higher BP is tolerated. Aggressive lowering risks penumbral hypoperfusion (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS).

options.text: Activate stroke team and arrange urgent transfer for thrombectomy without thrombolysis, BP <185/110
options.rationale: Within 6 hours of last seen well, with a proximal M1 occlusion, NIHSS >=6 and ASPECTS >=6, mechanical thrombectomy is indicated. IV thrombolysis is contraindicated due to recent apixaban; proceed directly to thrombectomy. BP target pre-procedure is <185/110 (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg orally and admit to the stroke unit for conservative management
options.rationale: Antiplatelet within 24 hours of thrombolysis is contraindicated and antiplatelet alone is not the reperfusion treatment for a proximal LVO; thrombectomy is the standard of care (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Administer IV idarucizumab to reverse apixaban first, then proceed to IV thrombolysis with alteplase
options.rationale: Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran, not apixaban; andexanet alfa partially reverses factor Xa inhibitors but is not generally used to enable thrombolysis. Direct transfer to thrombectomy is preferred (AMH; Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Acute ischaemic stroke reperfusion choices: IV thrombolysis within 4.5 hours of last seen well if no contraindication (BP <185/110, no recent DOAC dose within 48 h unless levels confirmed sub-therapeutic, no recent major surgery/bleeding/stroke). Mechanical thrombectomy within 6 hours for anterior circulation LVO (M1/ICA) with ASPECTS >=6 and disabling deficit; window extends to 24 hours if perfusion imaging shows salvageable penumbra. Thrombectomy is delivered with or without prior thrombolysis (Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: DOAC within 48 hours is a relative contraindication to thrombolysis unless levels measured
Thrombectomy window 0 to 6 h with CT/CTA; 6 to 24 h with perfusion imaging (DEFUSE-3, DAWN criteria)
Pre-thrombolysis BP target <185/110 mmHg
DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days then aspirin alone after minor stroke / high-risk TIA (CHANCE / POINT)
explanation.pearls: Door-to-needle target 60 minutes; door-to-groin target 90 minutes - process matters more than algorithm minutiae.

q.stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to her GP for review after a third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months. The first episode at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim with rapid recovery; the second at 2 years required a 48-hour admission with IV gentamicin for fever, vomiting and a CRP of 110 mg/L. The current episode resolved after 5 days of oral cefalexin. She is now well, afebrile, growing along the 50th centile and is fully toilet trained without daytime wetting. She has no constipation, no abnormal voiding pattern, no family history of vesicoureteric reflux and no antenatal renal abnormality on the 20-week scan. Examination is unremarkable with normal genitalia, no flank tenderness and a soft abdomen with no palpable bladder. Blood pressure is 96/58 mmHg (under the 90th centile for age and height). Most recent urea 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 32 micromol/L, eGFR within reference. A renal tract ultrasound performed two days ago showed normal kidney size with no hydronephrosis, normal corticomedullary differentiation and complete bladder emptying. The general practitioner asks which next-step imaging is most appropriate to guide longer-term follow up.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, afebrile
data_table.Examination: Normal genitalia, no flank tenderness, no palpable bladder
data_table.Investigations: Urea 4.2, creatinine 32, eGFR normal. Renal USS normal.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat renal tract ultrasound in 6 months and continue clinical follow up
options.rationale: Ultrasound is poor for detecting cortical scarring and low-grade vesicoureteric reflux, both of which change long-term management after recurrent or atypical UTI; further imaging is required (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

options.text: DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring
options.rationale: In a continent child over 3 years with recurrent or atypical UTI, DMSA is preferred over MCUG to detect renal cortical scarring and guide nephrology follow up; it is delayed 4-6 months after the acute infection to avoid false positives from acute pyelonephritis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram with fluoroscopy to grade vesicoureteric reflux severity
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive, requires catheterisation and contrast exposure, and is reserved for under 6 months, atypical or recurrent UTI with abnormal ultrasound, or family history of VUR; her ultrasound is normal (NICE NG224).

options.text: MAG3 diuretic renogram to assess differential function and obstructive drainage
options.rationale: MAG3 evaluates differential function and drainage in suspected obstruction; her ultrasound shows no hydronephrosis or dilatation, so obstructive uropathy is not the clinical question (KHA-CARI - Paediatric kidney disease).

options.text: Non-contrast CT urogram to evaluate the renal cortex and collecting system
options.rationale: CT delivers a high radiation dose and is not first-line for scarring assessment in children; DMSA gives functional cortical information with lower radiation (NICE NG224).

explanation.summary: After recurrent UTI in a child over 3 years with normal ultrasound, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection assesses cortical scarring; MCUG is reserved for selected high-risk groups, and CT is avoided due to radiation (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).
key_points: Ultrasound for all under 6 months and any atypical or recurrent UTI
DMSA detects cortical scarring; perform 4-6 months after acute infection
MCUG indicated under 6 months, atypical UTI, or abnormal USS/DMSA or family history of VUR
Recurrent = 2+ upper-tract or 3+ lower-tract UTIs
Antibiotic prophylaxis only after imaging suggests VUR or scarring
explanation.pearls: Avoid early DMSA - acute pyelonephritis causes reversible cold spots that look like scars.

q.subtopic: Renal and urology - Posterior urethral valves
q.stem: A 3-week-old boy is brought to a tertiary ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy and a weak urinary stream that his father has noticed only dribbles rather than arcs. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth. The 20-week morphology scan was reported as showing mild bilateral pelvicalyceal dilatation with a thickened bladder wall, but follow-up was delayed due to a family interstate move. He has not had a postnatal renal tract ultrasound. He is exclusively breastfed and has lost 12 percent of his birth weight since birth. On examination he is lethargic with mottled peripheries, cap refill 4 s, HR 168, RR 60, BP 64/40, T 36.4 degC, weight 2.8 kg (10th centile). The abdomen is distended with a palpable suprapubic mass that is dull to percussion and a palpable left kidney. Genitalia are normal male. Bloods: sodium 128, potassium 7.4, urea 28 mmol/L, creatinine 220 micromol/L, bicarbonate 12, pH 7.18, lactate 4.6 mmol/L. The renal registrar is en route. He has had 5 mL urine output in the last 6 hours and the bedside scanner shows a grossly distended bladder with bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and trabeculated bladder wall.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 60, BP 64/40, cap refill 4 s, T 36.4 degC
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, distended bladder, palpable kidney, normal male genitalia
data_table.Investigations: K 7.4, urea 28, Cr 220, HCO3 12, pH 7.18, lactate 4.6; bladder distended, bilateral hydronephrosis

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Transfer to theatre for primary cystoscopic posterior urethral valve ablation now
options.rationale: Definitive valve ablation is the eventual treatment but is performed only after the infant is resuscitated and bladder drained; immediate priority is decompression and resuscitation (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence furosemide infusion at 0.1 mg/kg/h to drive urine output
options.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in postrenal AKI with downstream obstruction; they worsen upper tract pressure and will not lower potassium until obstruction is relieved (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Insert an 8 Fr feeding tube urethrally to decompress the bladder
options.rationale: The immediate intervention in suspected PUV with obstructive AKI and electrolyte derangement is catheter drainage (small-calibre urethral catheter, not a Foley balloon which can be held in the prostatic urethra) to relieve the obstruction and allow biochemical correction before valve ablation (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange urgent percutaneous bilateral nephrostomy drainage under fluoroscopic guidance for upper-tract decompression
options.rationale: Nephrostomy is reserved for failed bladder drainage, ureteric obstruction or supravesical pathology; bladder catheterisation is the first-line decompression in PUV (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Give IV calcium gluconate plus salbutamol then arrange urgent haemodialysis transfer
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia stabilisation is appropriate adjunctively but does not address the obstructive cause; without bladder drainage the AKI and acidosis will not resolve (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).

explanation.summary: Suspected posterior urethral valves with obstructive AKI is managed by immediate small-calibre urethral catheterisation to decompress the bladder, followed by biochemical correction and definitive cystoscopic valve ablation once stable (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).
key_points: PUV is the commonest male infant obstructive uropathy
Antenatal clues: bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, thickened bladder, keyhole sign, oligohydramnios
First step: 8 Fr feeding tube as urethral catheter (avoid Foley)
Treat hyperkalaemia, acidosis, sepsis adjunctively
Definitive: cystoscopic valve ablation once stable
explanation.pearls: A Foley balloon can lodge in the prostatic urethra and worsen the obstruction; use a feeding tube or paediatric urinary catheter.

q.subtopic: Renal and urology - Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome
q.stem: A 6-year-old girl with biopsy-naive nephrotic syndrome diagnosed 5 weeks ago has been on oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 daily since presentation. Her initial presentation was typical: oedema, UPCR 520 mg/mmol, albumin 16 g/L, normal C3 and C4, no haematuria, normal blood pressure and normal renal function. She has remained compliant per home dosing diary and parental observation. At 4 weeks she remained heavily proteinuric (UPCR 460 mg/mmol) and albumin 19 g/L; methylprednisolone pulses 600 mg/m2 IV daily for 3 days were given according to local protocol with no remission. She is now Cushingoid, has BP 124/78 mmHg (over the 95th centile), urea 7.2 mmol/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L (baseline 30), albumin 18 g/L, UPCR 480 mg/mmol. Repeat C3 and C4 are normal. Hepatitis B and C serology, HIV, ANA and dsDNA are all negative. She has had no recent infections. Family history is negative for renal disease and there is no consanguinity. She is fully immunised including varicella and pneumococcal vaccines. Paediatric nephrology is reviewing her in clinic this afternoon, and the parents are asking about prognosis and alternatives. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized kidneys with normal corticomedullary differentiation and no thrombosis on Doppler imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg (>95th centile), afebrile
data_table.Examination: Cushingoid, persistent oedema
data_table.Investigations: UPCR 480, albumin 18, Cr 64 (baseline 30), C3/C4 normal, serology negative

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current prednisolone dose for a further 4 weeks and reassess
options.rationale: Failure to remit after 4-6 weeks of daily prednisolone (with or without IV methylprednisolone pulses) defines steroid resistance; prolonging steroid monotherapy exposes the child to toxicity without benefit (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Commence empirical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus) without further investigation
options.rationale: Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line therapy but should be initiated after biopsy and genetic results, since monogenic causes respond poorly and biopsy may reveal alternative pathology (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Arrange percutaneous renal biopsy with genetic testing for podocyte gene panel
options.rationale: Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome requires biopsy to characterise histology (commonly FSGS, less often MCD or membranous) and genetic testing for monogenic podocytopathies (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1) which predicts response to second-line immunosuppression (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome; KHA-CARI - Nephrotic syndrome in children).

options.text: Switch to rituximab induction and taper prednisolone over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Rituximab is used in frequently-relapsing or steroid-dependent disease and selected steroid-resistant cases, but only after biopsy and confirmation of histology and genetic status (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Add ACE inhibitor and cease steroids given Cushingoid features
options.rationale: ACEi is adjunctive antiproteinuric therapy in SRNS but immunosuppression cannot be ceased abruptly without a confirmed histological diagnosis and a second-line plan; abrupt cessation also risks adrenal insufficiency (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

explanation.summary: Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome (no remission after 4-6 weeks daily prednisolone with or without IV pulses) requires renal biopsy and genetic testing before tailoring second-line immunosuppression, which is typically a calcineurin inhibitor in the absence of monogenic cause (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).
key_points: Steroid resistance = no remission after 4-6 weeks high-dose prednisolone
Biopsy: FSGS most common; less often MCD, membranous, C3 glomerulopathy
Genetic panel: NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1, INF2, TRPC6
Monogenic SRNS poorly responsive to immunosuppression - kidney transplant has lower recurrence risk
Calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus or ciclosporin) first-line second agent in non-monogenic SRNS
explanation.pearls: Always confirm compliance and exclude secondary causes (HBV, HIV, lupus, malignancy) before labelling steroid resistant.

q.stem: A 3-year-old girl is admitted from a regional ED after 6 days of bloody diarrhoea attributed to a daycare gastro outbreak. Stool PCR has confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157. On day 7 of illness she becomes pale and oliguric with worsening lethargy and one episode of seizure-like jerks lasting 30 seconds. She had been managed with oral rehydration; she received no antibiotics or antimotility agents. On examination she is pale, has periorbital puffiness, scattered petechiae, T 36.8 degC, HR 132, BP 138/86 mmHg (above the 99th centile for age and height), cap refill 3 s. Bloods: Hb 72 g/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L, blood film shows schistocytes and burr cells, LDH 1620 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, direct Coombs negative, reticulocytes raised. Urea 24 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 35), sodium 132, potassium 5.4, bicarbonate 17, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L. Coagulation profile is normal. Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, granular casts present. She is alert between observation rounds. The paediatric registrar is weighing initial supportive measures with the on-call nephrologist. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized echogenic kidneys with no obstruction; chest X-ray demonstrates a small bilateral pleural effusion without consolidation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg (>99th centile), HR 132, cap refill 3 s
data_table.Examination: Pale, periorbital puffiness, scattered petechiae
data_table.Investigations: Hb 72, plt 38, schistocytes, LDH 1620, haptoglobin nil, Coombs neg, Cr 198 (baseline 35), normal coags

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management decision?

options.text: Transfuse one adult-dose platelet pool prophylactically to reduce bleeding risk from petechiae
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in STEC-HUS may exacerbate microvascular thrombosis and is avoided unless there is active major bleeding or a planned invasive procedure; thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption resolves with renal and haematological recovery (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Initiate urgent plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement therapy daily
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP and atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS, where it has not shown benefit and is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty pending ADAMTS13 results (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone to cover ongoing Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
options.rationale: Antibiotic treatment of STEC infection is associated with increased risk of HUS progression because of toxin release and is not recommended once HUS is established or in the acute STEC colitis phase (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Cautious isotonic fluids, transfuse red cells for symptomatic anaemia, avoid platelets
options.rationale: Management of STEC-HUS is supportive: cautious isotonic fluids to maintain euvolaemia (avoid overload in oliguric phase), red cell transfusion for symptomatic or severe anaemia (typically Hb <60-70 g/L), antihypertensive treatment, and avoidance of platelet transfusion unless active major bleeding because of microthrombus risk (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Give an antimotility agent such as loperamide to reduce diarrhoeal losses
options.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in STEC infection because they prolong toxin contact with the gut and increase HUS risk and severity (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

explanation.summary: STEC-HUS is treated supportively with cautious fluids, RBC transfusion for symptomatic anaemia, hypertension control, and avoidance of platelets, antibiotics and antimotility agents; dialysis is added if indications develop (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).
key_points: Triad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI
Avoid platelets unless major bleeding (microthrombus risk)
Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents in STEC phase
Supportive cautious fluids; avoid overload in oliguric phase
Eculizumab in atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS
explanation.pearls: Antibiotics and loperamide are the two interventions students reflexively give for bloody diarrhoea that worsen outcomes in suspected HUS.

q.stem: A 4-year-old boy with day 9 of confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli HUS is on the paediatric ward with worsening biochemistry over 24 hours despite cautious fluid restriction and one red cell transfusion. He has been anuric for 18 hours. He is alert but listless and complaining of nausea. On examination he has scattered petechiae, periorbital and pretibial oedema, mild bibasal crackles, T 36.8 degC, HR 124, RR 38, BP 142/92 mmHg, SpO2 96 percent on room air, weight 18.4 kg (up from admission weight of 17.8 kg). Bloods now: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 320 micromol/L, sodium 128, potassium 6.8 mmol/L, bicarbonate 12, pH 7.20, calcium 1.85 mmol/L corrected, phosphate 2.6, lactate 1.8. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2-V5 with QRS still 90 ms. He has been given IV calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose and salbutamol with only partial response (repeat K 6.4). Chest X-ray shows interstitial oedema. The on-call nephrologist is being phoned. He has no central venous access yet. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized echogenic kidneys with no obstruction and a small bladder. He has had no seizure activity and remains arousable but is increasingly tachypnoeic with subjective dyspnoea on minimal exertion.

data_table.Examination: Oedema, bibasal crackles, weight up 600 g
data_table.Investigations: K 6.8 -> 6.4 after stabilisation, HCO3 12 pH 7.20, Cr 320, anuric 18 h, CXR pulmonary oedema

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat IV calcium gluconate and continue medical therapy for hyperkalaemia
options.rationale: Medical management of hyperkalaemia provides only temporary shift and does not address fluid overload, acidosis or persistent anuria; failure to respond is itself a dialysis indication (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Commence furosemide infusion to drive a urine output of 1 mL/kg/hr
options.rationale: Diuretics are unlikely to be effective in established anuric AKI and are not a substitute for renal replacement when AEIOU dialysis indications are met (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Give 0.9 percent sodium chloride bolus 10 mL/kg to improve renal perfusion
options.rationale: Further fluid in established anuric HUS with rising weight, hypertension and pulmonary oedema worsens overload and does not restore perfusion when the cause is intrinsic glomerular and tubular injury (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Initiate acute renal replacement therapy for refractory hyperkalaemia, acidosis and fluid overload
options.rationale: Renal replacement is indicated when any AEIOU criterion is met: refractory acidosis (pH <7.2, bicarbonate <12), refractory electrolyte derangement (K >6.5 not responding to medical therapy), fluid overload causing pulmonary oedema, uraemic symptoms, or oligo/anuria with biochemical deterioration; this child meets multiple criteria (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Add oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate and review potassium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Resonium is slow and risks intestinal necrosis in unwell paediatric patients and does not address acidosis or fluid overload; the situation requires renal replacement (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

explanation.summary: Dialysis indications in paediatric AKI follow AEIOU (Acidosis, Electrolyte, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia); refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, pulmonary oedema and persistent anuria with rising urea collectively indicate immediate renal replacement (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).
key_points: Acidosis: pH <7.2 or bicarbonate <12 refractory to therapy
Electrolytes: K >6.5 not responding, severe hyper/hyponatraemia
Ingestion: dialysable toxins (salicylate, methanol, lithium)
Overload: pulmonary oedema unresponsive to diuretics
Uraemia: encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding, urea >40 in children
explanation.pearls: Half of children with STEC-HUS require dialysis; early planning of access avoids emergent line insertion in coagulopathic patients.

q.subtopic: Renal and urology - Bilateral cryptorchidism + DSD
q.stem: A term, phenotypically male newborn is reviewed in the postnatal ward at 18 hours of age. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week scan reported male genitalia. The neonatal examination raises concern. The infant is alert, has stable vitals for age (HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98 percent RA), capillary refill 2 s, normal cardiac auscultation, soft non-tender abdomen with no masses and no inguinal swellings. Genital examination shows a normal-sized phallus with a glanular meatus, no chordee, but the scrotum is small, mildly rugated and EMPTY bilaterally with NO palpable gonads in either scrotum or along the inguinal canals. There is no hyperpigmentation. The skin is normal and the infant has passed meconium and urine. Family history is unremarkable; there is no consanguinity and no relatives with infertility, salt-wasting illness or ambiguous genitalia. Birth weight is on the 50th centile. The newborn screening blood spot has not been collected yet. The midwife asks whether discharge can proceed in the morning as planned and the parents wish to register the birth as male. He has had no vomiting and feeding is established.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98%, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Normal phallus, empty bilaterally rugated scrotum, NO gonads palpable on either side
data_table.History: No family DSD or salt-wasting history; feeding established

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes and paediatric endocrine review
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male is a DSD red flag requiring urgent multidisciplinary workup to exclude congenital adrenal hyperplasia (a virilised 46,XX female), partial androgen insensitivity, gonadal dysgenesis or anorchia; investigations include karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP, electrolytes and androgen profile with sex assignment deferred (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development; RCH CPG - Undescended testes).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Discharge with outpatient paediatric surgical review at 6 weeks for bilateral cryptorchidism
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male newborn is a DSD until proven otherwise and risks missing salt-wasting CAH presenting in week 2; outpatient deferral is unsafe (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Arrange laparoscopic exploration in the first week to locate intra-abdominal gonads
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is the eventual investigation for confirmed non-palpable testes but only after DSD has been excluded; operating without a karyotype risks intervention on a virilised female (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical IM hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for presumed salt-wasting CAH
options.rationale: Empirical steroid and mineralocorticoid replacement is reserved for biochemically confirmed or strongly suspected adrenal crisis; empirical treatment without workup masks diagnosis and is not first-line in the stable infant (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Reassure parents this is constitutional and recheck at the 6-week well-child examination
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads is never a normal variant and reassurance delays critical workup; salt-wasting CAH can be lethal in week 2 if missed (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

explanation.summary: A phenotypic male newborn with bilateral non-palpable gonads must be worked up urgently as a DSD with karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP and electrolytes to exclude virilised 46,XX CAH, partial androgen insensitivity or gonadal dysgenesis; surgery and sex assignment are deferred (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
key_points: Bilateral non-palpable gonads = DSD until proven otherwise
Salt-wasting CAH presents day 7-14: vomiting, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, shock
Investigations: karyotype, pelvic USS, 17-OHP, electrolytes, LH/FSH/testosterone, HCG stimulation
Multidisciplinary input from endocrinology, urology, genetics, psychology
Defer birth registration if needed; ethics and family support essential
explanation.pearls: The cleanest way to flag this on the postnatal ward is to document the testes as palpable or not at every newborn check - the diagnosis is in the examination.

sources.label: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

q.subtopic: Renal and urology - Intermittent testicular torsion
q.stem: A 14-year-old boy attends a metropolitan ED at 02:00 with the third episode in 6 months of acute left testicular pain. Previous episodes lasted 30-90 minutes and resolved spontaneously before he could be assessed. Tonight the pain started 4 hours ago, was severe, associated with vomiting and lower abdominal radiation, and has now partially settled to a moderate ache. He has had no trauma, no urinary symptoms, no urethral discharge and is not sexually active. He is Tanner 4. He has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications and has no allergies. On arrival he is afebrile with HR 92, BP 118/68, RR 16, SpO2 99 percent. The left hemiscrotum is mildly swollen. The left testis is now in a normal vertical lie, mildly tender diffusely, the cremasteric reflex is present but sluggish on the left and brisk on the right, and there is no erythema. The right testis lies high in the scrotum on a horizontal axis (transverse lie) with a bell-clapper appearance. Urinalysis is negative. A formal scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler within 30 minutes reports normal symmetric arterial flow bilaterally with a heterogeneous left testis and a small reactive hydrocele. The on-call paediatric surgical fellow is reviewing the case. He is otherwise well and his pain has continued to settle since arrival. Past episodes have always resolved by morning. He has not started antibiotics and no analgesia beyond paracetamol has been given so far.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 118/68, SpO2 99%, afebrile
data_table.Examination: Left testis vertical, sluggish cremasteric reflex; right testis high/transverse - bell-clapper
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative. USS Doppler: symmetric flow, heterogeneous left testis, reactive hydrocele

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with analgesia and outpatient urology review given normal Doppler flow
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped episodes of acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution and a contralateral bell-clapper appearance are classic for intermittent torsion; normal Doppler does not exclude detorted torsion and discharge risks recurrent torsion with infarction (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Admit for observation with serial scrotal examinations and repeat Doppler later
options.rationale: Observation delays definitive treatment and exposes the testis to recurrent torsion-detorsion injury; the contralateral bell-clapper and recurrent history mandate operative exploration (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Arrange same-day MRI scrotum to characterise testicular perfusion and architecture
options.rationale: MRI is not the imaging modality of choice in suspected torsion and delays surgical exploration, which remains the safest and most informative investigation when clinical suspicion is high (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Proceed to urgent scrotal exploration with bilateral orchidopexy regardless of Doppler
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution is intermittent torsion; clinical diagnosis trumps imaging because Doppler is performed after detorsion and can be falsely reassuring. Bilateral fixation is required because the bell-clapper anomaly is typically bilateral, and the contralateral testis examination confirms it (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Negative urinalysis, no urethral discharge, no sexual activity and a contralateral bell-clapper testis make epididymo-orchitis unlikely; antibiotics delay definitive operative management of intermittent torsion (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).

explanation.summary: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain that resolves spontaneously, especially with a contralateral bell-clapper testis on examination, is intermittent torsion; normal Doppler after detorsion is falsely reassuring and the definitive treatment is bilateral scrotal exploration and orchidopexy (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).
key_points: Intermittent torsion is a clinical diagnosis; imaging supports but cannot exclude
Bell-clapper anomaly is typically bilateral - bilateral fixation required
Doppler performed post-detorsion can show normal or hyperaemic flow
Heterogeneous echogenicity and reactive hydrocele on USS support recent torsion
Risk of next torsion event becoming non-detorting and ischaemic - elective exploration is curative
explanation.pearls: If a teenager describes repeated identical episodes of severe scrotal pain with self-resolution, book the theatre even if today's Doppler is normal.

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl presents to a Victorian general practice with her 18-year-old sister. She has confirmed pregnancy at 7 weeks by dating ultrasound today and is requesting termination of pregnancy. The biological father is her 16-year-old boyfriend and the relationship is described by both as consensual and non-coercive. The patient does not want to tell either parent at present and asks specifically about confidentiality. She can describe both medical (mifepristone with misoprostol) and surgical termination options, explains the risks of each in age-appropriate language, and articulates that continuing the pregnancy is not what she wants. She has no medical contraindication to medical termination, is haemodynamically stable, and her social situation is stable with her sister supporting her. Her HEEADSSS screen reveals no self-harm, no substance use, and her mood is appropriately anxious but not depressed. She has no prior pregnancies, takes no medications, and is up to date with the Australian immunisation schedule. There is no Child Protection history. The general practitioner is comfortable with the procedure pathway and the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 framework but is unsure of the consent and confidentiality position for this patient given her age.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, no PV bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 22,000 IU/L, dating scan 7+0 weeks intrauterine, blood group O Rh positive, Hb 128 g/L
data_table.Social context: Year 10, stable home, sister supporting, no coercion
data_table.History: G1P0, no medical conditions, no medications

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer the case to the Victorian Children's Court for authorisation because termination decisions for under 16s require judicial sanction
options.rationale: Termination of pregnancy under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 does not require court authorisation for competent minors; Gillick competence is the relevant test, as per RANZCOG.

options.text: Mandatorily report the pregnancy to Victorian Child Protection today because the patient is under 16 and currently pregnant
options.rationale: Pregnancy in an under-16 is not by itself mandatorily reportable in Victoria; the threshold is significant harm and considers age difference, coercion, and exploitation, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

options.text: Require written parental consent from at least one parent before any termination referral because Gillick does not apply to termination decisions
options.rationale: Gillick competence applies to all medical decisions including termination of pregnancy; parental consent is encouraged but not required for a competent minor, as per RANZCOG guidance.

options.text: Document Gillick competence today, maintain confidentiality, encourage parental involvement, and refer for the patient's chosen termination pathway
options.rationale: A competent minor may consent to termination of pregnancy in Victoria; the clinician documents Gillick competence, encourages but does not require parental involvement, and refers for the patient's chosen pathway, as per RANZCOG and the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008.

options.text: Defer the decision by two weeks of additional counselling because the patient is too young to consent in the first trimester
options.rationale: Mandatory cooling off periods are not part of Victorian termination law and delay risks pushing the patient out of the medical termination window, as per RANZCOG.

explanation.summary: In Victoria, a Gillick competent minor may consent to termination of pregnancy without parental consent or court authorisation. The clinician documents the competence assessment, encourages parental involvement where safe and acceptable, and refers within the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 framework.
key_points: Gillick competence applies to termination decisions
Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 allows medical termination to 24 weeks
Parental involvement encouraged, not required
Pregnancy in under-16 is not automatically a Child Protection notification
Delay risks medical termination eligibility (typically <=9 weeks)
explanation.pearls: Court authorisation is reserved for non-therapeutic interventions; therapeutic terminations rely on Gillick.

sources.label: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

q.stem: A paediatric registrar at a metropolitan Victorian public hospital is asked to review an 11-year-old boy admitted after an episode of self-harm at boarding school. During the admission the boy discloses that a residential care worker employed by the boarding school, who is not a teacher, has been entering his bedroom at night for the past three weeks and tickling and wrestling with him in a way that has made him uncomfortable. There has been no disclosed penetrative contact and no injury, but the boy describes the contact as making him anxious and not wanting to return to the school. The boy's parents live overseas and the school is the legal guardian during term. The hospital social worker has been notified and an immediate safety plan has been put in place so the boy does not return to the school until further review. The registrar discusses the case with the consultant paediatrician on call. The hospital has both Child Protection and Police pathways available, and the consultant raises the additional question of whether the conduct falls under the Victorian Reportable Conduct Scheme administered by the Commission for Children and Young People. The school's head of welfare has been informed verbally. The hospital is a mandated entity under Victorian law and the registrar holds a current Working with Children Check.

data_table.Examination: Well, no acute injury, multiple superficial linear forearm abrasions, age-appropriate Tanner stage
data_table.Mental state: Anxious, congruent affect, no current SI, no psychotic symptoms
data_table.Social context: Boarding school in loco parentis, parents overseas, identified worker is residential care staff
data_table.History: First admission, no prior DHHS / Department of Families involvement, fully immunised

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate combination of statutory pathways for this disclosure?

options.text: Report to Victoria Police and Child Protection only and skip the Reportable Conduct Scheme today because it only covers schoolteachers and not residential care workers
options.rationale: The Reportable Conduct Scheme covers a wide range of organisations including schools and residential care providers, not only classroom teachers, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.
source_refs: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme

options.text: Make a Child Protection report alone because the boy is currently admitted to hospital and is safe under medical supervision
options.rationale: Hospital admission does not remove the duty to notify Police of possible criminal conduct or to trigger the Reportable Conduct Scheme for the alleged perpetrator, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.
source_refs: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme

options.text: Notify the Department of Families and Victoria Police of possible offending, and have the school notify the Commission under the Reportable Conduct Scheme
options.rationale: Three statutory pathways apply in parallel: Child Protection notification for the child's safety, Police for possible criminal conduct, and the Reportable Conduct Scheme via the head of the entity to the Commission for Children and Young People, as per Better Health Channel and the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting
Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme
Victoria Police Sexual Offences and Child Abuse Investigation

options.text: Notify the school principal alone and ask the principal to manage the staff member and any subsequent reports internally
options.rationale: Internal management does not satisfy mandatory reporting or the parallel Reportable Conduct Scheme obligations, both of which sit outside the school's internal HR process, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

options.text: Defer all statutory reporting until the boy has completed a forensic interview with the Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service later this week
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is not deferred for forensic interview; reports are made on reasonable belief and forensic assessment is arranged in parallel, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

explanation.summary: In Victoria, three pathways may run in parallel for institutional child abuse concerns: mandatory Child Protection notification, Police notification of suspected criminal conduct, and the Reportable Conduct Scheme administered by the Commission for Children and Young People when the alleged perpetrator works in a covered organisation.
key_points: Reportable Conduct Scheme covers schools, residential care, OOHC, religious bodies and more
Head of the entity must notify the Commission within 3 business days
Mandatory CP report is to the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing
Police notification covers suspected criminal offending
Pathways run in parallel, not sequentially
explanation.pearls: If the alleged perpetrator works for a covered organisation, the Reportable Conduct Scheme runs alongside the standard Child Protection pathway.

sources.label: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme
Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting
Victoria Police Sexual Offences and Child Abuse Investigation

q.stem: A 5-week-old girl is referred to a tertiary paediatric ED by her general practitioner with a 1 cm torn upper labial frenulum that the mother attributes to a recent bottle feed. The baby was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery, vitamin K was given, and she has been feeding adequately with appropriate weight gain. She has no other visible injuries and no current bleeding. The parents live together, both are employed, and there has been no previous contact with Child Protection. The infant is fully immunised to age and there is no family history of bleeding disorder, osteogenesis imperfecta, or sudden infant death. On examination there is the torn frenulum, an otherwise normal mouth, no posterior rib tenderness, normal anterior fontanelle, no head circumference change from birth percentile, and a normal neurological exam. Bedside coagulation screen and FBC are normal. The treating team is aware that a torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant is regarded as a sentinel injury and is planning the formal NAI workup according to RCH and Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service guidance. The on-call paediatric consultant and the hospital social worker have been notified, a mandatory report has been submitted to Child Protection, and the infant is now being admitted for full evaluation.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142 bpm, RR 42 /min, T 36.8 deg C, weight tracking 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Torn upper labial frenulum 1 cm, no other injury, normal fontanelle, normal eyes externally, no rib tenderness
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, INR 1.1, APTT normal, fibrinogen normal
data_table.History: Fully immunised, vitamin K given at birth, no FH bleeding

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate complete sentinel injury workup for this infant?

options.text: Skeletal survey now and at 11 to 14 days, neuroimaging, dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy, coagulation and bone studies, and forensic paediatric involvement
options.rationale: Standard NAI workup in an infant under 12 months with a sentinel injury includes initial and follow-up skeletal survey, neuroimaging, dilated indirect retinal exam by ophthalmology, bleeding and bone disorder screen, and forensic paediatric assessment, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: Skeletal survey performed once at admission only today, with no follow-up skeletal imaging and no dilated formal eye examination
options.rationale: Repeat skeletal survey at 11-14 days improves detection of healing rib and metaphyseal fractures; single survey alone is incomplete, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: MRI brain only today, with no skeletal imaging and no formal eye examination given the absence of bruising elsewhere on the body
options.rationale: Skeletal survey is required even without external evidence of fractures because clinically silent fractures are common in abused infants, as per RANZCR.
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: Bedside eye examination by the paediatric registrar without pupil dilation today, with no skeletal imaging and no brain imaging arranged
options.rationale: Retinal haemorrhage assessment requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by an ophthalmologist; bedside undilated fundoscopy is insufficient, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

options.text: Coagulation screen alone and a routine outpatient paediatric follow-up arranged because the injury appears small and isolated today
options.rationale: Sentinel injuries in non-mobile infants are predictive of further serious abuse; outpatient follow-up alone is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

explanation.summary: Sentinel injuries (torn frenulum, bruise in non-mobile infant, intra-oral injury) trigger formal NAI workup: skeletal survey now and at 11-14 days, neuroimaging (CT or MRI), dilated indirect retinal exam by ophthalmology, coagulation and bone studies, plus parallel Child Protection and forensic paediatric service involvement.
key_points: Sentinel injuries precede serious abuse in 25-30% of fatal cases
Two skeletal surveys (now and 11-14 days) increases yield
Retinal exam requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by ophthalmologist
Coagulation and bone screen rules out mimics, does not replace workup
Forensic paediatric service (VFPMS in Vic, NSW CASS equivalents) involved early
explanation.pearls: A torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant is the sentinel injury most commonly missed in primary care.

sources.label: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

q.stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric developmental clinic for assessment of behavioural and learning difficulties. He was placed in long-term foster care at 3 months of age and his birth mother is documented to have consumed alcohol at hazardous levels throughout the pregnancy. At school he has marked difficulty with executive function and working memory, with school reports describing impulsivity, poor adaptive functioning, and specific maths and language deficits despite intensive support. Cognitive testing performed by an educational psychologist shows a Full Scale IQ of 78 with significant scatter and specific deficits in three of the recognised neurodevelopmental domains (executive function, adaptive behaviour, language). He has met physical growth parameters along the 25th centile. On focused facial examination the paediatrician notes a smooth philtrum, a thin upper vermilion border, and short palpebral fissures (below the 3rd centile for age). Birth history is otherwise unremarkable with a normal newborn examination. There is no history of head injury, no seizure disorder, and no other syndromic features. The foster carer is engaged with the local Aboriginal community-controlled health service for ongoing care.

data_table.Growth: Height and weight 25th centile, OFC 25th centile
data_table.Facial features: Smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures (<3rd centile)
data_table.Cognitive: FSIQ 78, severe impairment in executive function, adaptive behaviour, and language domains
data_table.Prenatal: Confirmed hazardous prenatal alcohol exposure throughout pregnancy
data_table.History: Foster care from 3 months, fully immunised, no other syndromic features

q.lead_in: Which Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD diagnostic category best applies?

options.text: Foetal Alcohol Syndrome without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure because the facial features and growth pattern alone are diagnostic in this case
