options.rationale: Without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure the diagnosis is not made under the Australian Guide; this case has confirmed exposure so the exposure criterion is met, as per the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder combined presentation alone based on the executive function impairment and impulsivity findings reported today
options.rationale: ADHD may coexist but does not capture the facial dysmorphology and the breadth of severe impairment domains specific to FASD, as per the Australian Guide.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: FASD with less than three sentinel facial features documented today, despite this child being noted to have all three sentinel features on examination
options.rationale: This child meets criteria for three sentinel features and therefore falls into the 3-sentinel-features category, not the less-than-three category, as per the Australian Guide.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Static encephalopathy of unknown cause because measured IQ is above 70 and prenatal alcohol is not a recognised aetiology in this case
options.rationale: IQ above 70 does not exclude FASD; the diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ alone, as per the Australian Guide.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: FASD with three sentinel facial features, severe impairment in three or more neurodevelopmental domains, and confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure documented
options.rationale: The Australian Guide diagnoses FASD with 3 sentinel facial features when all three are present together with severe impairment in three or more of ten neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed or assumed prenatal alcohol exposure, as per the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

explanation.summary: The Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD recognises two diagnostic categories: FASD with 3 sentinel facial features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures), and FASD with less than 3. Both require severe impairment in at least 3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed (or assumed) prenatal alcohol exposure.
key_points: Three sentinel facial features: smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, short palpebral fissures
Severe impairment in >=3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains required
Prenatal alcohol exposure confirmed OR assumed
IQ alone does not make or exclude the diagnosis
Domains include cognition, executive function, language, adaptive, motor, memory, attention, affect, social, sensory
explanation.pearls: Sentinel facial features only develop with first trimester exposure during a narrow window; their absence does not exclude FASD.

sources.label: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to a tertiary ED by her mother for review after losing 12 kg over five months. She has been progressively restricting her intake, exercising compulsively, and has missed three menstrual periods. She is medically naive and on no medications. On arrival she is alert and oriented, denies suicidal ideation, but is cold to touch and describes near-syncope on standing this morning. Her current weight is 38 kg and her premorbid weight was 50 kg. Body mass index is 14.8 kg/m2 (well below the 3rd centile for age). Her supine heart rate is 38 beats per minute with a postural drop to systolic blood pressure of 78 mmHg standing. Core temperature is 35.4 deg C. Bedside ECG shows sinus bradycardia with a QTc of 470 ms. Bloods reveal potassium 3.0 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, magnesium 0.65 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, and liver enzymes mildly raised with ALT 78 U/L. She has no past psychiatric admission, sees a community psychologist weekly, and her family is supportive and engaged. The on-call paediatrician is applying the Australian and New Zealand Academy for Eating Disorders MARSIPAN-AU criteria to decide on admission.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 bpm supine, BP 96/60 supine to 78/52 standing, T 35.4 deg C, RR 14 /min
data_table.Anthropometrics: Weight 38 kg, BMI 14.8 kg/m2 (<3rd centile), 24% weight loss over 5 months
data_table.ECG: Sinus bradycardia, QTc 470 ms, no arrhythmia
data_table.Investigations: K 3.0 mmol/L, PO4 0.7 mmol/L, Mg 0.65 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, ALT 78 U/L
data_table.Mental state: Cognitively intact, no SI, mildly depressed mood, motivated to recover

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home today with twice-weekly community eating disorder team review and oral potassium and phosphate replacement at home
options.rationale: This patient meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria (bradycardia <40, postural BP drop, hypothermia, hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, QTc >450 ms) which mandate inpatient medical admission, as per the ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU Guide.

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward under medical care with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding starting at 20 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte replacement, and ED team involvement
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria mandate medical admission with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding to prevent refeeding syndrome, electrolyte and glucose management, and dual physical and eating disorder team care, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Admit directly to a child and adolescent mental health inpatient unit today with full caloric refeeding at 50 kcal/kg/day starting from day one
options.rationale: Medical stabilisation precedes mental health admission for MARSIPAN-AU high risk patients; aggressive caloric refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome and is contraindicated, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Transfer her today to a peripheral hospital for rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses and overnight monitoring
options.rationale: Aggressive crystalloid bolus risks cardiac failure in the malnourished heart; the management is medical admission with measured refeeding rather than resuscitation-style fluids, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Refer her today for outpatient review in an eating disorder day program with weekly bloods, ECG monitoring and a six week follow up plan
options.rationale: Day program review does not address the immediate medical risk; multiple high risk physical criteria require inpatient admission, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

explanation.summary: MARSIPAN-AU defines medical admission criteria for severe restrictive eating disorders. High risk features (bradycardia <40, postural BP drop >20 mmHg systolic, hypothermia <35.5, K <3.0, PO4 <0.5, glucose <3.0, QTc >450 ms, rapid weight loss >1 kg/week, BMI <14 in adolescents) mandate inpatient care with cardiac monitoring and cautious refeeding.
key_points: Bradycardia <40 bpm = high risk
Postural drop >20 mmHg systolic = high risk
Hypothermia <35.5 deg C = high risk
Refeeding starts low (20 kcal/kg/day) and titrates
Dual medical + eating disorder team is the standard
explanation.pearls: The malnourished heart can fail on aggressive crystalloid bolus; treat the metabolic risk first.

q.stem: A 15-year-old girl is reviewed by a child and adolescent psychiatrist after 8 weeks of structured cognitive behaviour therapy delivered weekly by a psychologist for a moderate to severe major depressive episode without psychotic features. She continues to have pervasive anhedonia, weight loss, early morning awakening, hopelessness, and passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. There is no substance use, no mania history, and she lives with both parents who are engaged in her care. She has no medical comorbidity, no QT prolonging medications, no known family history of medication intolerance, and her ECG is unremarkable with QTc 410 ms. Her clinician is now considering adding antidepressant pharmacotherapy. The family is concerned about the Therapeutic Goods Administration boxed warning regarding antidepressants and suicidal thinking in young people and wants to understand both the choice of medication and the monitoring required if treatment is started. The patient is open to medication trial with appropriate safety planning. She has regular sleep patterns interrupted by depressive insomnia, no eating disorder, and a supportive school counsellor in place. Her psychologist will continue weekly CBT alongside any medication.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, T 36.6 deg C
data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms, no arrhythmia
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all normal; urine pregnancy negative
data_table.Mental state: Pervasive anhedonia, early morning awakening, hopelessness, passive SI without plan
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no QT prolonging agents

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate pharmacological choice with associated monitoring plan?

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 10 mg daily with weekly clinical review in the first 4 weeks for emergent suicidality, alongside ongoing CBT
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is the first line antidepressant in paediatric MDD with the strongest evidence base; the TGA boxed warning mandates close clinical monitoring (weekly initially) for emergent suicidal thinking and behaviour, alongside continued psychological therapy, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines and beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines
beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines Depression Adolescents

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg extended release daily with monthly clinical review and no additional suicidal thinking monitoring planned
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is not first line in adolescents and has higher discontinuation symptoms; close weekly monitoring for emergent suicidality is required regardless of agent, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence paroxetine 20 mg daily with monthly clinical review and no formal safety planning arranged for the next four weeks
options.rationale: Paroxetine is not recommended in adolescents because of its discontinuation profile and a less favourable risk benefit, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg at night and titrate to therapeutic dose, with regular ECG monitoring planned over the coming months
options.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants are not effective for paediatric depression and carry overdose risk; they are not recommended, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Stop the current psychological therapy today and commence sertraline 50 mg daily as monotherapy without further structured psychological therapy
options.rationale: Continuing psychological therapy alongside antidepressants is the recommended combination treatment for moderate to severe adolescent depression, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: Fluoxetine is first line antidepressant pharmacotherapy in adolescent major depressive disorder, used in combination with continued psychological therapy. The TGA boxed warning requires close clinical monitoring (typically weekly) for emergent suicidal thinking and behaviour during the first 4 weeks of treatment.
key_points: Fluoxetine = first line SSRI in adolescent MDD
Sertraline and escitalopram are second line
Boxed warning: emergent suicidal ideation in age <25
Weekly review in first 4 weeks then fortnightly
Continue CBT alongside antidepressant
explanation.pearls: Paroxetine and venlafaxine are not first line in adolescents due to discontinuation and tolerability concerns.

sources.label: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines
beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines Depression Adolescents

q.stem: A 14-year-old presents to a tertiary gender service for assessment having identified as a transgender boy for the past four years. He was assigned female at birth, has been consistently insistent and persistent regarding gender identity, has socially transitioned two years ago with family support including legal name change at school, and reports persistent and increasing distress related to ongoing pubertal development. Menarche occurred at age 12 and he reports menstrual periods as a major and worsening source of dysphoria over the past 18 months. He is at Tanner stage 4 breast and pubic hair development with regular menstrual cycles every 28 days. He has been supported by an experienced child and adolescent psychiatrist for the past 18 months with no current major mental health condition; previous mild depressive symptoms have improved substantially with social transition and ongoing family acceptance. Both parents are actively supportive of the assessment process and engaged, and the multidisciplinary team includes paediatric endocrinology, child and adolescent psychiatry, gynaecology, and fertility counselling. The patient has cognitive capacity to engage in informed consent discussions and has been counselled about fertility, bone density, and the reversibility of different intervention types over multiple sessions. The team is now reviewing the next clinical decision in the Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines pathway from RCH Melbourne. He has had a normal bone age, normal baseline LH and FSH, and a normal pelvic ultrasound this month with no contraindication to GnRH analogue use.

data_table.Pubertal staging: Tanner stage 4 breast and pubic hair, menarche at 12, regular cycles
data_table.Mental health: 18 months psychiatric care, prior mild depression improved with social transition
data_table.Bone health: Bone age normal, no prior fractures, vitamin D 78 nmol/L, DEXA scheduled
data_table.Investigations: LH and FSH baseline normal, oestradiol mid follicular, normal pelvic ultrasound
data_table.Family + MDT: Both parents supportive, MDT includes endocrinology, psychiatry, gynaecology, fertility counselling

q.lead_in: According to the RCH Australian Standards of Care, what is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Commence gender affirming testosterone immediately because the patient is past Tanner stage 2 and has already menstruated for some years
options.rationale: Gender affirming hormones in adolescents under 16 are introduced through a structured pathway with MDT consensus and informed consent including fertility; immediate testosterone without prior consideration of puberty suppression is not the standard, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Commence GnRH analogue puberty suppression to halt further pubertal progression with informed consent, MDT review, and ongoing bone, mood, and fertility monitoring
options.rationale: GnRH analogue puberty suppression is the typical stage 1 intervention to halt further pubertal progression and dysphoria from menses in this Tanner 4 adolescent with consistent gender identity, MDT support, and family agreement; consent does not require court authorisation per Re Kelvin, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines
AusPATH Position Statement on Trans Youth

options.text: Refer to the Family Court for authorisation of stage 1 puberty suppression as the required pathway under current Australian law
options.rationale: Court authorisation is no longer required for stage 1 puberty suppression in Australia following the Re Jamie 2013 and Re Kelvin 2017 Family Court decisions where parents and clinicians agree, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Decline any medical intervention today until the patient reaches the age of 18 and continue with psychological support alone in the interim
options.rationale: The Australian Standards of Care explicitly do not require deferral to age 18 and recognise puberty suppression and gender affirming hormones may be appropriate earlier with MDT agreement, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Perform irreversible chest reconstruction surgery now to relieve dysphoria before any further pubertal progression continues to develop
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is not stage 1 in Australian adolescent gender affirming care; reversible medical interventions precede consideration of surgery later in adulthood under Australian guidelines, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

explanation.summary: Australian Standards of Care for trans and gender diverse children and adolescents (RCH Melbourne) describe a staged pathway: stage 1 GnRH analogue puberty suppression, stage 2 gender affirming hormones, stage 3 surgery in adulthood. Court authorisation is not required where parents, clinicians and the Gillick competent young person agree (Re Kelvin 2017).
key_points: Stage 1: GnRH analogue puberty suppression (reversible)
Stage 2: gender affirming hormones (partially reversible)
Stage 3: surgery, generally in adulthood
Re Kelvin 2017: no court authorisation needed where consensus exists
MDT includes endocrinology, psychiatry, fertility counselling
explanation.pearls: Fertility counselling before stage 2 hormones is mandatory under the Australian Standards of Care.

sources.label: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines
AusPATH Position Statement on Trans Youth

q.subtopic_detail: Down syndrome - atlantoaxial instability and sport clearance
q.stem: A 9-year-old boy with trisomy 21 attends his paediatrician for clearance to join a local junior trampolining club, having previously only participated in school swimming. His parents are keen because of his social benefit but want a clear answer on safety. He had a balanced AVSD repair at 5 months with no residual lesion and has been discharged from cardiology. He attends a mainstream school with aide support and his TSH and full blood count from 4 months ago were normal. He has had no neck pain, no torticollis, no changes in gait, no recent decline in fine motor skills, no urinary incontinence, and no clumsiness compared with his usual baseline. His neurological examination today shows the expected mild generalised hypotonia of trisomy 21, symmetric reflexes with no clonus or upgoing plantars, normal tone in all four limbs, and a gait that is age-typical for him with no ataxia. He has never had a cervical spine X-ray. His mother asks whether he requires imaging or specialist review before starting trampolining, swimming and football at his new school.

data_table.Examination: Typical T21 features, mild generalised hypotonia, no torticollis, symmetric reflexes, plantars downgoing, no clonus, age-appropriate gait

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step before he begins trampolining?

options.text: Order routine flexion-extension and lateral cervical spine radiographs before clearing him for any sport participation
options.rationale: Routine screening cervical spine radiographs in asymptomatic children with Down syndrome are no longer recommended because they have poor predictive value for clinically important atlantoaxial instability and do not change management in asymptomatic children (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Clinical screen with focused history and neurological examination, and counsel that trampolining is high-risk and best avoided
options.rationale: Current guidance is symptom and examination based: ask about neck pain, torticollis, gait change, weakness, sphincter changes; examine for myelopathic signs. Sports involving forced cervical flexion or risk of head-first impact (trampolining, gymnastics, diving, rugby contact, boxing) are specifically discouraged in T21 because of the elevated background risk of atlantoaxial instability (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Refer urgently for MRI of the cervical spine before clearing him for any sport participation
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for children with symptoms or signs suggestive of cervical cord compression (neck pain, torticollis, gait deterioration, new incontinence, hyperreflexia); it is not routinely indicated in asymptomatic children for sports screening (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

options.text: Clear him for trampolining and all contact sports given the entirely normal neurological examination today
options.rationale: A normal examination does not exclude atlantoaxial instability and does not negate the elevated risk inherent to T21; trampolining, gymnastics, diving and contact rugby remain discouraged regardless of a normal examination (AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Restrict him from all organised competitive sport until a paediatric orthopaedic spine specialist review
options.rationale: Blanket sport restriction is not recommended and would deprive him of significant developmental and social benefit; swimming, football of certain codes and athletics are encouraged with appropriate selection (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

explanation.summary: Routine cervical spine radiography for asymptomatic children with Down syndrome has been abandoned in current Australian and AAP guidance because of poor predictive value. Sports clearance relies on a focused symptom-and-examination screen plus counselling about avoiding activities with forced cervical flexion or head-first impact, such as trampolining and gymnastics (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).
key_points: Screen by history (neck pain, torticollis, gait change, new incontinence, weakness) and examination (tone, reflexes, clonus, plantars, gait)
Trampolining, gymnastics, diving, boxing and certain contact codes are discouraged regardless of imaging
Image only when symptoms or signs raise concern, then with MRI for cord assessment
explanation.pearls: Atlantoaxial instability in T21 presents with subtle changes: torticollis, decline in handwriting or gait, new toe-walking, or new incontinence - all warrant urgent neurosurgical and imaging review.

q.subtopic_detail: Williams syndrome - hypercalcaemia management
q.stem: A 16-month-old girl with genetically confirmed Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion on chromosomal microarray) is reviewed in a tertiary metabolic clinic after a routine annual calcium check showed an elevated corrected calcium. She has known supravalvular aortic stenosis with a peak gradient of 22 mmHg under cardiology follow-up, the characteristic elfin facies, and a sociable, indiscriminately friendly behavioural profile. Over the past 6 weeks her parents describe increased irritability, constipation despite adequate dietary fibre, polyuria with new night-time wetting after months of dry nappies, and reduced appetite. She has been receiving a standard infant multivitamin including 400 IU/day vitamin D and her formula has been a regular age-appropriate cows-milk-based formula. She has had no recent vitamin D loading, no thiazide diuretic exposure, no calcium supplements, and no excessive sun exposure unusual for Brisbane. She is otherwise well, has no fever, no rash, and no signs of dehydration on examination today. The team plans an outpatient management approach but is reviewing dietary, supplement and pharmacological options.

data_table.Examination: Elfin facies, no goitre, no peripheral oedema, abdomen soft, no palpable mass, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 3.10 mmol/L, ionised calcium 1.55 mmol/L, PTH suppressed 0.4 pmol/L, 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, phosphate 1.2 mmol/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, urinary calcium:creatinine ratio elevated
data_table.Imaging: Renal ultrasound: mild bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis
data_table.Medications: Multivitamin with 400 IU vitamin D daily

q.lead_in: Which single intervention is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase her oral fluid intake only and recheck the serum calcium in 6 weeks without other change
options.rationale: Hydration alone, without removing the offending vitamin D and limiting dietary calcium, will not address the underlying problem and risks progressive nephrocalcinosis (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day and continue her current diet and vitamin D supplements unchanged
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for hypercalcaemia refractory to dietary and supplement adjustment; first-line management is removal of vitamin D and calcium restriction with specialist input (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Commence a single intravenous infusion of pamidronate in the day-stay unit and continue current diet
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates are reserved for severe, symptomatic, or refractory hypercalcaemia and have long-lasting effects on bone in young children; they are not first-line in mild-to-moderate Williams syndrome-related hypercalcaemia (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision; RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Admit for intravenous fluid rehydration and intravenous frusemide infusion to promote urinary calcium excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are reserved for severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia with adequate hydration support; in mild outpatient-manageable hypercalcaemia, removing the exogenous calcium and vitamin D source is sufficient and avoids hypokalaemia and dehydration (RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Stop the vitamin D-containing multivitamin and start a low-calcium, vitamin D-free formula with endocrine input
options.rationale: Idiopathic infantile hypercalcaemia is a recognised feature of Williams syndrome and the first-line intervention is to remove exogenous vitamin D, switch to a low-calcium specialty formula, and limit dietary calcium under specialist endocrine and dietetic guidance; this approach typically normalises calcium without bisphosphonate or steroid escalation (Williams Syndrome Association clinical care recommendations; AAP Health care supervision for children with Williams syndrome).

explanation.summary: Children with Williams syndrome are predisposed to idiopathic infantile hypercalcaemia. The first-line approach for mild-to-moderate hypercalcaemia is dietary calcium restriction and vitamin D withdrawal with paediatric endocrine guidance; pharmacological escalation (steroids, bisphosphonates, loop diuretics) is reserved for refractory or severe cases (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).
key_points: Annual calcium and urinary calcium:creatinine screening is recommended in Williams syndrome
Renal ultrasound assesses for nephrocalcinosis when hypercalcaemia is detected
Use low-calcium specialty formula and avoid vitamin D supplementation as first-line management
explanation.pearls: Suppressed PTH plus hypercalcaemia + nephrocalcinosis + elfin facies + supravalvular AS is a syndrome-defining cluster - send microarray for 7q11.23 deletion.

q.subtopic_detail: Marfan syndrome - aortic protection and activity
q.stem: A 15-year-old boy with previously diagnosed Marfan syndrome on the basis of revised Ghent criteria and a confirmed FBN1 pathogenic variant attends his annual paediatric cardiology review at a tertiary centre. He plays competitive rowing five times per week, including ergometer sessions involving repetitive Valsalva. His most recent transthoracic echocardiogram shows an aortic root at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.0 cm with a Z-score of 3.2 indexed to body surface area, increased from a Z-score of 2.5 one year ago. There is mild aortic regurgitation. He has no history of dissection, no syncope, no chest pain, and no documented hypertension. He is currently not on any cardiovascular medications, having declined them previously because his aortic root was within range. He has no asthma, no diabetes, and no contraindications to beta-blockade or angiotensin receptor blockade. His mother died of a presumed type A dissection in her late 30s. He is keen to continue elite rowing if at all possible and is also asking whether he should continue resistance training for rugby.

data_table.Examination: Tall Marfanoid habitus, soft early diastolic murmur at LSE consistent with mild AR, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Imaging: Aortic root 4.0 cm, Z-score 3.2 (was 2.5 one year ago), mild aortic regurgitation, no dissection

q.lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue the current competitive rowing programme, defer medication, and review again in 12 months' time
options.rationale: An aortic root Z-score that has increased from 2.5 to 3.2 over 12 months warrants both pharmacological aortic protection and modification of high-intensity isometric and burst-Valsalva sports (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations; AHA / Bethesda Conference guidance on athletic participation in Marfan syndrome).

options.text: Commence beta-blocker (or losartan if intolerant), restrict competitive isometric and contact sport, repeat imaging every 6 months
options.rationale: An aortic root Z-score of 3.2 with rapid progression warrants commencement of beta-blockade (or angiotensin receptor blocker such as losartan if intolerant), avoidance of competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sports, and intensified imaging surveillance every 6 months. Moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations; AHA / Bethesda Conference guidance).

options.text: Refer immediately for prophylactic aortic root replacement surgery despite the current aortic root diameter measurement
options.rationale: Elective prophylactic aortic root replacement is generally offered at aortic root diameter approximately 5.0 cm in adults with Marfan syndrome (or smaller with rapid progression, family history of dissection, or planned pregnancy); 4.0 cm with mild AR does not meet threshold (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

options.text: Commence a beta-blocker but allow unrestricted rowing and rugby resistance work because medication is fully protective
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy does not substitute for activity modification; competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sport are restricted in Marfan syndrome irrespective of medication (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance).

options.text: Stop all exercise indefinitely and avoid any organised or recreational physical activity going forward
options.rationale: Complete activity cessation is not recommended; moderate aerobic, non-isometric, non-competitive exercise is actively encouraged because of overall cardiovascular and psychosocial benefit (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance Marfan).

explanation.summary: Aortic protection in Marfan syndrome combines pharmacotherapy (beta-blockade or angiotensin receptor blockade), activity modification (avoid competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sport, continue moderate aerobic activity), and serial imaging. Surgical thresholds are 5.0 cm aortic root, or smaller with rapid progression, family history of early dissection, planned pregnancy, or significant aortic regurgitation (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).
key_points: Beta-blockade and angiotensin receptor blockade slow aortic root growth in Marfan syndrome
Family history of early dissection lowers the surgical threshold
Restrict competitive isometric/contact sports; encourage moderate aerobic exercise
explanation.pearls: Indexed aortic Z-score and rate of change matter more than a single absolute diameter when deciding to start or escalate therapy in a growing adolescent.

q.subtopic_detail: Ehlers-Danlos - vascular vs hypermobile discrimination
q.stem: A 17-year-old woman is referred to a paediatric genetics service from a regional clinic for assessment of suspected Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Her sister, aged 21, was recently admitted to a metropolitan tertiary hospital after a spontaneous splenic artery rupture and underwent emergency embolisation; sister's subsequent connective tissue gene panel confirmed a pathogenic COL3A1 variant. The patient herself has generalised joint hypermobility with a Beighton score of 7/9, soft velvety skin, easy bruising in unusual locations (forearms, thighs) that her mother has long attributed to clumsiness, and translucent skin over the chest wall with prominent visible veins. She has had no major vascular events, but reports two episodes of mild epistaxis in the past year and a self-resolving spontaneous left-sided pneumothorax aged 14 (managed conservatively). She has thin lips, a thin nose, and prominent eyes. She has no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation on a recent echocardiogram (root 2.6 cm, Z-score 0.4), no ectopia lentis, no scoliosis, and a normal palate. Her current GP queried hypermobile EDS at age 14 based on the Beighton score and skin findings. She is asking about her diagnosis, her own pregnancy risk in future, and what surveillance she needs.

data_table.Examination: Beighton 7/9, soft velvety translucent skin, visible chest wall veins, easy bruising, thin lips, thin nose, prominent eyes; no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation
data_table.Family history: Sister with confirmed COL3A1 pathogenic variant, splenic artery rupture
data_table.History: Spontaneous pneumothorax age 14, recurrent epistaxis

q.lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely diagnosis and the best next step?

options.text: Hypermobile EDS - reassure and focus on physiotherapy and pain management, with no genetic testing arranged
options.rationale: Hypermobile EDS does not explain translucent skin, prominent chest-wall venous pattern, spontaneous pneumothorax, easy bruising, characteristic facial features, or a known familial COL3A1 variant; missing vascular EDS in this context risks a life-threatening vascular event (International EDS classification 2017).

options.text: Vascular EDS - cascade COL3A1 testing, baseline non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol if confirmed, plus pregnancy counselling
options.rationale: The combination of translucent skin, characteristic facies, easy bruising in unusual locations, spontaneous pneumothorax, and a first-degree relative with a confirmed pathogenic COL3A1 variant strongly suggests vascular EDS. Cascade targeted testing for her sister's variant is the next step; if confirmed, baseline non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol (where available - the BBEST trial showed reduction in arterial events), avoidance of routine arteriography, and detailed pregnancy counselling are recommended (International EDS classification 2017; EDS Society care recommendations).

options.text: Marfan syndrome - arrange FBN1 testing and commence losartan empirically while awaiting confirmation of the diagnosis
options.rationale: There is no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation, no ectopia lentis, no high-arched palate, and a known familial COL3A1 variant rather than FBN1; Marfan syndrome is not the working diagnosis (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).

options.text: Classical EDS - send COL5A1 and COL5A2 testing rather than the COL3A1 gene that her sister carries
options.rationale: Classical EDS features include atrophic scarring, marked skin hyperextensibility, and widely splayed scars rather than translucent skin, prominent venous pattern, and spontaneous arterial rupture; the family variant is in COL3A1, not COL5 (International EDS classification 2017).

options.text: Confirmed vascular EDS - proceed directly to catheter arteriography to comprehensively map the entire arterial tree
options.rationale: Catheter arteriography in vascular EDS carries a high risk of arterial dissection and is generally avoided; non-invasive cross-sectional vascular imaging (MRA or CTA) is preferred (EDS Society care recommendations).

explanation.summary: Vascular EDS (COL3A1) presents with translucent skin, characteristic facies, easy bruising, spontaneous pneumothorax or hollow viscus rupture, and arterial events that can occur in adolescence. With a known familial pathogenic variant, cascade testing followed by non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol (where available), and avoidance of invasive arteriography is the management strategy (International EDS classification 2017).
key_points: Major diagnostic features of vEDS: arterial/intestinal/uterine rupture, family history of vEDS, characteristic facies, easy bruising
Celiprolol reduced arterial events in the BBEST trial; not yet routinely PBS-listed but used internationally
Pregnancy in vEDS carries substantially elevated mortality risk and warrants pre-conception multidisciplinary counselling
explanation.pearls: Avoid arteriography, central venous access where possible, and elective surgery in vEDS - tissue fragility makes surgical complications common and severe.

q.subtopic_detail: Tuberous sclerosis - infantile spasms
q.stem: A 7-month-old infant girl is brought to a regional paediatric ED with a 5-day history of new clusters of stereotyped flexion movements occurring on waking and after feeds. The clusters consist of brief symmetrical flexion of the neck, trunk, and arms occurring every 5 to 10 seconds for up to 2 minutes, after which she appears distressed and then drowsy. Her mother has captured a video showing typical infantile spasms. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy, but a foetal MRI at 30 weeks performed because of a cardiac rhabdomyoma seen on antenatal scan showed multiple cortical tubers. A postnatal genetic panel confirmed a pathogenic TSC2 variant. On general examination today she has 3 hypopigmented ash-leaf macules on her trunk and a developmental regression noted by her parents - she had been babbling and reaching for objects 6 weeks ago but is now less responsive and no longer reaches. Her cardiac rhabdomyoma is being followed by cardiology and is currently asymptomatic. EEG performed today shows hypsarrhythmia.

data_table.Examination: 3 ash-leaf macules, regression of social and motor milestones, no focal neurology
data_table.Imaging: Multiple cortical tubers on prior MRI; cardiac rhabdomyoma stable

q.lead_in: Which single first-line therapy is most appropriate?

options.text: Levetiracetam monotherapy
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is not first-line for infantile spasms; randomised evidence supports vigabatrin (in TSC) and hormonal therapy (otherwise) as first-line (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

options.text: Oral prednisolone monotherapy
options.rationale: Hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) is first-line for infantile spasms without an identified cause, but in TSC-related spasms vigabatrin is preferred because randomised data show superior efficacy in this subgroup (International TSC Consensus 2021).

options.text: Sodium valproate monotherapy
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is not first-line for infantile spasms and is generally avoided in young female infants because of the long-term reproductive teratogenicity profile and lower efficacy than hormonal therapy or vigabatrin (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

options.text: Vigabatrin monotherapy
options.rationale: In infantile spasms occurring in the context of tuberous sclerosis complex, vigabatrin is the first-line agent and is preferred over ACTH or prednisolone because of superior efficacy in this specific subgroup; visual field monitoring is required because of the recognised risk of peripheral visual field loss (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms; International TSC Consensus Recommendations 2021).

options.text: Ketogenic diet (first-line)
options.rationale: The ketogenic diet is a recognised option for refractory infantile spasms but is not first-line; pharmacological control with vigabatrin should be attempted first in TSC (International TSC Consensus 2021).

explanation.summary: Infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia in TSC are treated first with vigabatrin, which has superior efficacy in this subgroup compared with hormonal therapy. Visual field monitoring is essential because of the recognised risk of peripheral visual field constriction (International TSC Consensus 2021).
key_points: Vigabatrin first-line for TSC-related infantile spasms
Hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) first-line in non-TSC infantile spasms
Early recognition and treatment improves developmental outcome
explanation.pearls: An antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is a strong predictor of tuberous sclerosis - early genetic confirmation enables proactive surveillance for spasms, autism, and TAND.

q.subtopic_detail: Epilepsy - valproate switch and neonatal vitamin K
q.stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with generalised tonic clonic epilepsy is reviewed by the obstetric medicine clinic at 34 weeks. She has been on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily since adolescence and has had no seizures since her late teens. Her previous pregnancy four years ago ended in a healthy term infant who is meeting all developmental milestones, although she was not taking folate at conception that time. She booked for this pregnancy at 9 weeks and her neurologist discussed switching to a less teratogenic antiepileptic but she elected to remain on valproate given her excellent control and the late presentation; she did commence folic acid 5 mg daily from that visit. Her morphology scan at 20 weeks was normal with no neural tube, cardiac or skeletal anomalies and growth has tracked along the 50th centile on three serial scans. She is also taking iron 105 mg daily and pregnancy multivitamins. She has no other comorbidities, no allergies and her partner is well with no family history of bleeding disorders. The team plans a vaginal birth at term and the obstetric registrar asks about additional prophylactic measures specifically aimed at reducing the risk of neonatal haemorrhagic disease related to maternal antiepileptic therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: SFH 34 cm, longitudinal cephalic, FHR 142 bpm, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 110 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, LFTs normal, anti-Ro negative.
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily, iron, pregnancy multivitamin.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate additional measure to reduce neonatal haemorrhagic disease in this pregnancy?

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg to the neonate at birth as routine prophylaxis
options.rationale: All neonates in Australia receive IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth (or oral schedule if parents decline IM). For infants of mothers on antiepileptic drugs the IM route is specifically recommended; this is the principal evidence-based measure to prevent vitamin K deficient bleeding in this group (RANZCOG; NHMRC Joint statement on vitamin K).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment
NHMRC: Joint statement on vitamin K administration to newborns

options.text: Maternal oral vitamin K 10 mg daily from 36 weeks until delivery
options.rationale: Routine maternal antenatal vitamin K is no longer recommended for women on antiepileptic drugs in Australia; evidence does not support a clinically meaningful reduction in neonatal haemorrhagic disease beyond standard neonatal IM vitamin K (RANZCOG; RCOG epilepsy in pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment

options.text: Switch valproate to lamotrigine now at 34 weeks gestation here
options.rationale: Switching antiepileptics in the third trimester carries a high risk of breakthrough seizures and offers no benefit for the foetus given organogenesis is complete; any change must occur preconception (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology - epilepsy and pregnancy

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce bleeding risk
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not alter neonatal vitamin K deficient bleeding risk; vaginal birth remains appropriate for women with controlled epilepsy on antiepileptics (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma transfusion to the neonate within 6 hours of birth
options.rationale: FFP is reserved for clinically bleeding neonates with documented coagulopathy; it is not a prophylactic measure (Royal Women's Hospital neonatal guidelines).
source_refs: Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Neonatal vitamin K

explanation.summary: Australian guidance for women on enzyme-inducing or other antiepileptic drugs is that the neonate receives standard IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth; routine maternal antenatal oral vitamin K is no longer recommended. Antiepileptic switches must occur preconceptionally, not in late pregnancy (RANZCOG; NHMRC).
key_points: Valproate has the highest teratogenic risk of common antiepileptics; counsel and switch preconception only
All Australian neonates are offered IM vitamin K 1 mg or 3-dose oral schedule
Breastfeeding is generally safe on valproate, lamotrigine and levetiracetam
Plan continuous CTG in labour for women with active epilepsy
explanation.pearls: Once she is in the third trimester, antiepileptic switching causes more harm than good. Focus on a safe birth plan and neonatal vitamin K.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment
NHMRC: Joint statement on vitamin K administration to newborns
Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology - epilepsy and pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Neonatal vitamin K

q.subtopic_detail: Acute severe asthma in pregnancy - escalation
q.stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 28 weeks gestation presents to the obstetric assessment unit with two days of worsening cough, breathlessness and wheeze on a background of moderate persistent asthma. She had a viral upper respiratory infection last week and her partner had similar symptoms. She is on budesonide/formoterol 200/6 micrograms twice daily and reports using salbutamol every 30 minutes overnight without sustained relief. She has had two previous emergency department presentations with asthma in the last 18 months but no ICU admissions and no history of intubation. She has no fevers, no chest pain, no calf swelling, no haemoptysis and no reduced foetal movements; she received corticosteroid coverage for lung maturity two days ago at booking with respiratory. On arrival 30 minutes ago she was given continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 500 micrograms, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg and high flow oxygen titrated to SpO2 95%, with minimal subjective improvement. She has no allergies, takes no other regular medication and her booking bloods including platelet count and renal function were normal. The current observations and bedside investigations are shown below. The obstetric and respiratory teams are at the bedside and asking about the next pharmacological step before considering anaesthetic and ICU input.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 32, HR 132 bpm, BP 134/86 mmHg, T 37.4 degC, SpO2 94% on 8 L/min mask, peak flow 38% of personal best.
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, audible wheeze, accessory muscle use, unable to complete full sentences. SFH 28 cm, CTG normal baseline 140 with variability and accelerations.
data_table.Investigations: ABG on 8 L: pH 7.36, PaCO2 36 mmHg, PaO2 78 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L. CXR (with shielding): hyperinflated, no consolidation.

q.lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step in management?

options.text: Oral leukotriene receptor antagonist loading dose of montelukast 20 mg
options.rationale: Montelukast has no role in acute severe asthma; it is a maintenance add-on agent for chronic control (National Asthma Council Australia).

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading dose then maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline has a narrow therapeutic window, significant maternal and foetal toxicity and is reserved for refractory cases after magnesium has been trialled; it is not the next step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 500 micrograms intramuscular every 5 minutes
options.rationale: IM adrenaline is the management of anaphylaxis, not acute asthma; uteroplacental vasoconstriction in pregnancy is a particular concern (ASCIA; National Asthma Council Australia).

options.text: Emergency caesarean delivery to improve maternal oxygenation now
options.rationale: Delivery is not the treatment for acute asthma and the foetus is currently reassured on CTG; pharmacological escalation and ICU support precede any consideration of delivery (RANZCOG; Royal Women's).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes once now
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma not responding to back-to-back bronchodilators and IV corticosteroid warrants IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes; this is recommended in Australian asthma-in-pregnancy guidance and a normalising PaCO2 with rising lactate signals impending respiratory failure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; National Asthma Council Australia).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

explanation.summary: Acute severe asthma in pregnancy is treated the same as in the non-pregnant adult: oxygen to keep SpO2 >=94%, back-to-back salbutamol with ipratropium, early systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes if not improving. Pregnancy is a strong reason to escalate early rather than late (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
key_points: A normalising PaCO2 in a tachypnoeic asthmatic is a red flag for tiring, not improvement
Position left lateral from 20 weeks to relieve aortocaval compression
Continuous CTG once viable; involve obstetrics and ICU early
Document discussion about steroids for foetal lung maturation if preterm delivery considered
explanation.pearls: Do not be falsely reassured by a 'normal' PaCO2 in a severe asthmatic - she is tiring.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

q.subtopic_detail: Graves disease - antithyroid choice by trimester
q.stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with Graves disease diagnosed 18 months ago is reviewed at her booking visit at 7 weeks gestation. Initial treatment was with propylthiouracil and propranolol, but she was switched to carbimazole 10 mg daily six months ago after biochemical control was achieved and has remained euthyroid since. Her TSH is 2.1 mIU/L and free T4 is within range on bloods drawn yesterday. She has no eye signs and no goitre on examination today. She has no other medical problems, no allergies and her only other medications are folic acid 500 micrograms daily and a pregnancy multivitamin. TSH receptor antibody titre four months ago was mildly elevated at 2.5 IU/L (cut-off 1.75 IU/L). She is asymptomatic, has no palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance or tremor and her resting heart rate is 78 bpm. She lives with her supportive partner who is well, and there is no family history of thyroid autoimmunity. She has read online that some thyroid tablets cause birth defects and asks whether she should keep taking carbimazole or change to something safer for the first part of the pregnancy. She has not yet had any further investigations for this pregnancy and her dating ultrasound is booked for next week.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, weight 68 kg.
data_table.Examination: No goitre, no exophthalmos, no tremor, no proximal weakness.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, free T4 within range, TRAb 2.5 IU/L 4 months ago (mildly raised).
data_table.Medications: Carbimazole 10 mg daily, folic acid 500 micrograms, pregnancy multivitamin.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate change to her antithyroid medication for this stage of pregnancy?

options.text: Continue carbimazole 10 mg daily throughout the pregnancy and recheck thyroid function tests every trimester
options.rationale: Carbimazole/methimazole is associated with embryopathy including aplasia cutis, choanal atresia and oesophageal atresia when used in the first trimester; alternative therapy is required during organogenesis (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Refer for definitive radioactive iodine ablation this week and commence levothyroxine replacement therapy immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy because of foetal thyroid ablation risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

options.text: Stop all antithyroid medication now and monitor maternal TSH and free T4 every two weeks until delivery
options.rationale: Stopping antithyroid therapy in a woman with biochemically active Graves disease risks maternal thyrotoxicosis and foetal goitre or tachycardia, particularly with raised TRAb (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil now and review at the start of the second trimester for return to carbimazole
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance is to use propylthiouracil during the first trimester to avoid carbimazole embryopathy, then switch back to carbimazole from around 16 weeks to reduce ongoing risk of PTU hepatotoxicity (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Add propranolol 40 mg three times daily to her current carbimazole and continue both throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Beta blockade is used for symptomatic adrenergic relief in active thyrotoxicosis, not as a strategy to mitigate carbimazole teratogenicity, and the patient is asymptomatic biochemically euthyroid (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: First-trimester carbimazole exposure causes a recognisable embryopathy. Australian practice is to use propylthiouracil through organogenesis (typically up to about 16 weeks) and then switch back to carbimazole because of cumulative PTU hepatotoxicity. TRAb should be measured in the third trimester to predict neonatal thyroid disease (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
key_points: Carbimazole embryopathy: aplasia cutis, choanal/oesophageal atresia, omphalocele
PTU rare but serious hepatotoxicity - hence switch back at ~16 weeks
Target free T4 at the upper limit of the trimester-specific reference range
Check TRAb in the third trimester; high titres warn of foetal/neonatal Graves
explanation.pearls: First trimester = PTU. Second trimester onward = carbimazole. Always raise TRAb in the third trimester.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: SLE - anti-Ro/La and neonatal lupus risk
q.stem: A 30-year-old G2P0 woman with SLE diagnosed seven years ago is reviewed in a combined obstetric medicine clinic at 11 weeks gestation. She has been in clinical remission for two years on hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and low dose prednisolone 5 mg daily. Her one previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage at 9 weeks with no known cause identified at the time. Her most recent rheumatology bloods three months ago confirmed normal complement, negative dsDNA, and positive anti-Ro at 120 U/mL (positive >10) and positive anti-La at 70 U/mL. She has no past history of lupus nephritis, no thromboembolism and her antiphospholipid antibody screen has been repeatedly negative on three occasions over the last two years. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily and aspirin 100 mg daily for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis at 11 weeks. She has no other medical problems, no allergies and no relevant family history. Her partner is well with no family history of congenital heart disease or arrhythmia. The obstetric medicine team plans regular antenatal review and is finalising the foetal monitoring plan with the paediatric cardiologists. She specifically asks about the relevance of her antibody pattern to the baby's heart and her own ongoing medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, weight 62 kg.
data_table.Examination: No active joint synovitis, no rash, no oral ulcers.
data_table.Investigations: Anti-Ro 120 U/mL positive, anti-La 70 U/mL positive, dsDNA negative, C3/C4 normal, APL screen repeatedly negative, urine PCR 3 mg/mmol.
data_table.Medications: Hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily, prednisolone 5 mg daily, aspirin 100 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily.

q.lead_in: Which surveillance plan is the most appropriate addition for this pregnancy?

options.text: Serial foetal echocardiograms from 16 to 28 weeks for congenital heart block
options.rationale: Anti-Ro and anti-La cross the placenta and can cause neonatal lupus, of which congenital complete heart block is the most serious feature. Australian and EULAR guidance is to perform serial foetal echocardiography (typically weekly to fortnightly) between 16 and 26-28 weeks in anti-Ro positive women (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic; EULAR SLE pregnancy guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: SLE in pregnancy
EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

options.text: Stop hydroxychloroquine at the start of the second trimester now
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is continued throughout pregnancy in SLE because it reduces flares, congenital heart block recurrence in subsequent pregnancies and possibly the risk of pre-eclampsia (EULAR; RANZCOG).
source_refs: EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE
RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

options.text: Empirical IVIG infusions from 16 weeks to prevent congenital heart block
options.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to reliably prevent first-occurrence congenital heart block and is not standard of care; surveillance with echo and prompt intervention if first-degree block is detected is preferred (EULAR).
source_refs: EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

options.text: Stop aspirin because anti-Ro positive SLE increases bleeding risk
options.rationale: Aspirin is recommended for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis in SLE from before 16 weeks; anti-Ro positivity does not change this (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

options.text: Routine empirical low molecular weight heparin from 12 weeks gestation
options.rationale: LMWH prophylaxis is indicated for antiphospholipid syndrome or significant VTE risk, neither of which apply here; SLE alone without these features does not mandate LMWH (RCOG; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies cross the placenta and may cause neonatal lupus syndromes, with congenital heart block as the most feared. Serial foetal echocardiography between 16 and 28 weeks is the surveillance standard. Hydroxychloroquine should be continued throughout pregnancy (RANZCOG; EULAR).
key_points: Risk of congenital heart block ~2% in anti-Ro positive first pregnancies, ~15-20% recurrence
Hydroxychloroquine reduces recurrence of neonatal lupus and SLE flares
Aspirin 100-150 mg daily from before 16 weeks for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis in SLE
Plan delivery and neonatology input if any conduction abnormality detected
explanation.pearls: Anti-Ro changes the foetal cardiology pathway, not the rheumatology drug list. Keep the hydroxychloroquine.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: SLE in pregnancy
EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

q.subtopic_detail: Hepatitis B - infant immunoprophylaxis and maternal tenofovir
q.stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman who emigrated to Australia from a high HBV prevalence country is reviewed at 26 weeks gestation in the obstetric medicine clinic. She was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis B 8 years ago during routine antenatal screening in her first pregnancy. She is HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, anti-HBe negative, and her HBV DNA at 24 weeks is 2.3 x 10^8 IU/mL. Her ALT is 28 U/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, INR 1.0 and her FibroScan was 5.6 kPa six months ago, consistent with minimal hepatic fibrosis. She has no co-infection with HIV, HCV or HDV on booking serology and no clinical features of chronic liver disease. She has no other medical problems, is on no regular medication and has no known drug allergies. Her first child, now four years old, was born in her country of origin and received hepatitis B immunoglobulin and vaccine within 12 hours of birth and is hepatitis B negative on subsequent serology with documented anti-HBs response. She asks specifically what the team will do to reduce the risk of her current baby getting hepatitis B given how high her viral level is, and is keen to breastfeed if possible. She has been counselled that vertical transmission is the dominant route of childhood acquisition.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.
data_table.Examination: No jaundice, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, SFH 26 cm.
data_table.Investigations: HBsAg+, HBeAg+, anti-HBe-, HBV DNA 2.3 x 10^8 IU/mL, ALT 28 U/L, platelets 220, INR 1.0, FibroScan 5.6 kPa (low fibrosis).

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate maternal intervention to reduce vertical transmission in this pregnancy?

options.text: Hepatitis B immunoglobulin to the mother at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Maternal HBIG does not have a role in reducing vertical transmission; HBIG is administered to the infant after birth and to non-immune individuals after high-risk exposure (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Entecavir 0.5 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Entecavir is not preferred in pregnancy because of more limited safety data; tenofovir disoproxil is the agent of choice for HBV in pregnancy (ASHM; GESA).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance recommends maternal tenofovir disoproxil from approximately 28 weeks gestation for women with HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL (or HBeAg positive with high VL) because this substantially reduces the residual vertical transmission risk that remains despite infant immunoprophylaxis (ASHM; Gastroenterological Society of Australia; AASLD/EASL).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations
EASL Clinical Practice Guideline: Hepatitis B

options.text: Routine elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce transmission risk
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not influence HBV vertical transmission rates when standard immunoprophylaxis is provided to the infant (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Advise no breastfeeding because of high maternal HBV DNA levels here
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is safe in HBV-positive mothers provided the infant has received standard HBIG and the birth dose vaccine; high maternal DNA is not a contraindication (Australian Immunisation Handbook; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Women with HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL should commence tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg daily from about 28 weeks to reduce residual vertical transmission. The infant additionally receives HBIG and the hepatitis B vaccine birth dose within 12 hours of birth, with completion of the vaccine schedule (ASHM; GESA; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
key_points: Treatment threshold for antiviral in pregnancy: HBV DNA > 200,000 IU/mL or > 5.3 log10
Tenofovir disoproxil is preferred over entecavir in pregnancy
Infant receives HBIG + first vaccine dose within 12 hours of birth in Australia
Breastfeeding is safe once infant has received immunoprophylaxis
explanation.pearls: High HBV DNA in pregnancy means tenofovir for the mother and HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine for the baby.

sources.label: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations
EASL Clinical Practice Guideline: Hepatitis B

q.subtopic_detail: Mechanical mitral valve - anticoagulation by trimester
q.stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 woman with a bileaflet mechanical mitral valve replacement for rheumatic heart disease attends preconception clinic with her cardiologist and an obstetric physician. Her rheumatic disease was acquired in childhood before she immigrated to Australia at age 12; surveillance now demonstrates only the prosthetic valve as a residual lesion. She has been on warfarin 4 mg daily with stable INR 2.5-3.5 since her surgery five years ago, monitored at a community anticoagulation clinic, with no thrombotic or bleeding events. She is currently in sinus rhythm, NYHA functional class I with no exertional symptoms during stair climbing, and her most recent transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal mechanical valve function with no regurgitation, no left atrial enlargement, no pulmonary hypertension and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%. She has no other medical problems, no history of thromboembolism, no allergies and is on no other regular medication. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily and would like to conceive in the next three to six months. She asks the obstetric medicine team how her anticoagulation will be managed in pregnancy and what trade-offs she should understand. She has no contraindication to either warfarin or low molecular weight heparin and her partner is well. The cardiology team will share care with obstetric medicine throughout the pregnancy and a multidisciplinary management plan is being prepared in advance of any conception attempts.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm regular.
data_table.Examination: Mechanical S2, no murmurs, no signs of failure, NYHA I.
data_table.Investigations: INR 2.8, LVEF 60%, normal valve function on TTE.
data_table.Medications: Warfarin 4 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily.

q.lead_in: Which anticoagulation strategy is most appropriate to plan from conception through to delivery?

options.text: Use therapeutic-dose enoxaparin with anti-Xa monitoring for the entire pregnancy from positive test until 36 weeks then switch to unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: LMWH throughout is associated with a higher rate of valve thrombosis than warfarin-containing strategies, particularly in mechanical mitral valves; ESC reserves this strategy for women who decline warfarin after counselling (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Continue warfarin at the same dose from conception through to elective induction at term to minimise mechanical valve thrombosis risk throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin throughout pregnancy minimises maternal thromboembolism but at the cost of warfarin embryopathy (6-12 weeks) and foetal bleeding at delivery; pure warfarin throughout is rarely chosen without a structured discussion of trade-offs (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to therapeutic-dose enoxaparin from positive pregnancy test, return to warfarin from 13-36 weeks, switch back to LMWH from 36 weeks to delivery
options.rationale: ESC and RANZCOG guidance recommend dose-adjusted LMWH (with anti-Xa monitoring) in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy when the daily warfarin dose is moderate to high, warfarin in the second and early third trimester for stable INR and lowest thrombotic risk on the valve, then LMWH again from 36 weeks to allow safe neuraxial anaesthesia and delivery (ESC 2018 Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy; RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily preconception and continue this direct oral anticoagulant throughout the entire pregnancy and puerperium
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants are contraindicated in pregnancy and ineffective in mechanical valves; they have no role here (ESC 2018; AMH).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy and reintroduce therapeutic LMWH at 14 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Mechanical mitral valves are at very high thrombotic risk and never go unanticoagulated; stopping in any trimester is contraindicated (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Anticoagulation in mechanical heart valves in pregnancy balances maternal valve thrombosis (best minimised with warfarin) and foetal embryopathy/bleeding (worse with warfarin). A common sequenced strategy is LMWH in the first trimester (especially if warfarin dose >5 mg), warfarin in mid-pregnancy, and LMWH again from 36 weeks for safe peridelivery management (ESC 2018; RANZCOG).
key_points: Daily warfarin dose <5 mg: lower embryopathy risk so warfarin throughout is more acceptable
Therapeutic LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring (peak 4 hours post dose)
Plan delivery on LMWH; convert to IV unfractionated heparin 36 hours before and stop 4-6 hours before neuraxial
Restart anticoagulation 6-12 hours after vaginal birth or 12-24 hours after caesarean if no bleeding
explanation.pearls: Mechanical valves are anticoagulation jigsaws: never stop entirely, plan the trimester swaps in advance.

sources.label: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: Postnatal VTE prophylaxis - risk-based dosing
q.stem: A 36-year-old G3P2 woman delivers a healthy 3.6 kg infant by emergency caesarean section at 39+1 weeks for failure to progress in the first stage of labour. Her BMI on booking was 33 kg/m2 and her current weight is 92 kg with proportionate gestational weight gain. She is otherwise well with no personal or family history of thrombosis, no known thrombophilia and no immobilising disease. The caesarean was technically straightforward with estimated blood loss 600 mL, no transfusion required and no postpartum haemorrhage. She is now on the postnatal ward six hours after delivery, observations stable, mobilising with assistance, no signs of bleeding, lochia normal and the wound dressing dry. Her two previous deliveries were both by vaginal birth at term without complications or postpartum thromboembolic events. Her booking bloods were normal including a normal platelet count and renal function, and she has no allergies. She does not smoke and is in stable accommodation with supportive family. The obstetric registrar is asked to write a postnatal VTE prophylaxis plan based on her current risk profile, including her BMI, age, emergency caesarean and parity. She is keen to breastfeed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, dressing dry, lochia normal, calves soft and non-tender, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L (booking 122), platelets 240 x10^9/L, U&E normal, INR 1.0.
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol PRN, ibuprofen PRN. No anticoagulant yet.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate VTE prophylaxis plan for this patient?

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally for 7 days post-discharge
options.rationale: DOACs are not recommended for postpartum thromboprophylaxis in Australia, particularly in women who are breastfeeding; LMWH is preferred (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Mechanical thromboprophylaxis with TED stockings alone for the duration of admission
options.rationale: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is insufficient for a patient with multiple risk factors plus emergency caesarean; pharmacological prophylaxis is required (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Therapeutic dose anticoagulation is reserved for treatment of confirmed VTE or for very high-risk prophylaxis (e.g. recent VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome with prior thrombosis); this patient meets prophylactic, not therapeutic, criteria (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for at least 10 days from 6-12 hours postoperatively
options.rationale: Emergency caesarean plus age >=35 plus BMI >=30 plus parity >=3 each adds points on Australian and RCOG postnatal VTE risk scoring; this combination indicates pharmacological prophylaxis for at least 10 days postpartum. Enoxaparin 40 mg daily is appropriate at her weight (RCOG Green-top 37a; RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium
RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium
KEMH: VTE prophylaxis in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks postpartum as VTE prophylaxis
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended as routine postnatal VTE prophylaxis in this risk profile; LMWH provides the most evidence-based reduction in VTE postpartum (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE risk assessment uses cumulative risk factors. Emergency caesarean alone earns prophylaxis for at least 10 days; combined with BMI >=30, age >=35 and parity >=3 the indication is robust. Standard agent is prophylactic-dose LMWH starting 6-12 hours after delivery if no bleeding (RCOG; RANZCOG; KEMH).
key_points: Score in late pregnancy and again postnatally; rescore if status changes
Standard prophylactic enoxaparin dose 40 mg daily for weight 50-90 kg; weight-band adjust outside this range
Continue 10 days postpartum at minimum; 6 weeks if previous VTE, persistent risk factors or thrombophilia
LMWH compatible with breastfeeding
explanation.pearls: Emergency caesarean plus any second risk factor almost always equals 10 days of enoxaparin.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium
RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium
KEMH: VTE prophylaxis in pregnancy and the puerperium

q.subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy versus HELLP
q.stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 36+4 weeks presents to the maternity assessment unit with 36 hours of nausea, vomiting and right upper quadrant pain. She is increasingly fatigued and her partner reports she has been progressively confused for the last few hours, with intermittent disorientation to time and place. There is no headache, no visual disturbance, no vaginal bleeding and she reports no reduced foetal movements but feels generally unwell. She has had a normal antenatal course in this pregnancy with two prior uncomplicated term vaginal births. She is not on any regular medication, has no allergies and has no personal or family history of liver disease. She does not smoke or drink alcohol and there is no recent travel or sick contacts. On arrival her BP is 138/88 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 98% on air and she appears jaundiced with mild asterixis. Her abdomen is tender in the right upper quadrant without rebound; CTG shows a normotypical baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability. Her bedside capillary glucose is 2.6 mmol/L. Urgent bloods reveal an ALT of 540 U/L, AST 520 U/L, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 95 x10^9/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, urate 0.46 mmol/L and creatinine 138 micromol/L. Lactate is 4.2 mmol/L and the blood film shows no schistocytes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/88 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 98%, CBG 2.6 mmol/L.
data_table.Examination: Jaundice, mild asterixis, RUQ tenderness, GCS 14, no peripheral oedema, CTG reduced variability.
data_table.Investigations: ALT 540 U/L, AST 520 U/L, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 95 x10^9/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, urate 0.46 mmol/L, creatinine 138 micromol/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, urine PCR 28 mg/mmol.

q.lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours acute fatty liver of pregnancy over HELLP syndrome?

options.text: Mild hypertension with a urine PCR above 30 mg/mmol
options.rationale: Hypertension and proteinuria are core features of pre-eclampsia/HELLP and are less characteristic of AFLP, where they may be absent or mild (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right upper quadrant pain with raised transaminases above 500 U/L
options.rationale: RUQ pain and transaminitis are shared between HELLP and AFLP; this feature alone does not discriminate (Swansea criteria).
source_refs: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy

options.text: Platelet count below 100 x10^9/L on this presentation
options.rationale: Thrombocytopenia <100 x10^9/L is part of HELLP criteria and can occur in AFLP from synthetic failure; it does not preferentially identify either (RANZCOG).

options.text: Maternal hypoglycaemia with raised ammonia and coagulopathy out of proportion to platelet drop
options.rationale: Hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia and a coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, raised INR) that is disproportionate to the relatively modest thrombocytopenia are highly suggestive of acute fatty liver of pregnancy, reflecting hepatic synthetic failure rather than microangiopathic haemolysis (Swansea criteria; RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic).
source_refs: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy

options.text: Serum urate above the upper limit of normal in the third trimester
options.rationale: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia/HELLP and not specific to AFLP; it does not discriminate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a hepatic synthetic failure syndrome of the third trimester. Distinguishing features versus HELLP include hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, encephalopathy and a coagulopathy that exceeds what platelet count alone would explain. Definitive treatment for both is prompt delivery with maternal stabilisation (Swansea criteria; RANZCOG).
key_points: Swansea criteria: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, urate, leucocytosis, ascites/bright liver, transaminitis, ammonia, AKI, coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis (>=6 of 14)
Maternal stabilisation focuses on glucose, coagulopathy reversal and timely delivery
Consider LCHAD deficiency screening in the infant after maternal AFLP
