Multidisciplinary management with hepatology and intensive care
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia and hyperammonaemia in a third-trimester woman = think AFLP, not HELLP.

sources.label: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy

q.subtopic_detail: Ovarian torsion - emergency recognition and detorsion
q.stem: A 24-year-old G0 woman presents to ED at 02:00 with sudden-onset severe right iliac fossa pain that woke her from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. The pain is colicky, radiates to the right groin and posterior thigh, and is currently 9/10 in intensity despite paracetamol 1 g PO taken 90 minutes ago. She had a recent pelvic ultrasound 2 weeks ago for cyclical pelvic discomfort showing a 6 cm right ovarian dermoid (mature cystic teratoma); she was awaiting an outpatient gynaecology appointment. Her last menstrual period was 10 days ago, regular 28-day cycles, and she has no intermenstrual bleeding or discharge. She is sexually active in a long-term relationship, uses condoms, and beta-hCG taken on arrival is negative. She has no fevers, no urinary symptoms and no diarrhoea or rectal bleeding. Her partner reports she has not eaten or drunk anything since the pain began. She has no relevant past medical history, no allergies, and takes only ibuprofen as required for cyclical pain. On examination she is in obvious distress, splinted on the right, with cool peripheries from pain. She has not previously had any abdominal or pelvic surgery and has never been pregnant.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 112 bpm, RR 22, T 37.1 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen: marked right iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no peritonism. Bowel sounds present. Bimanual: very tender right adnexal mass, palpable approximately 7 cm, with severe pain on cervical motion. Uterus normal size, anteverted.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. WCC 13.2 x10^9/L, CRP 12 mg/L. Urinalysis negative. Bedside TVUS: enlarged right ovary 9 cm with peripheral follicles and a 6 cm complex mass; minimal Doppler flow within the ovarian parenchyma compared with the left.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for serial observation, IV analgesia and a formal radiology TVUS at 08:00
options.rationale: Delaying definitive assessment risks complete vascular compromise and ovarian loss; torsion is a time-critical diagnosis and surgical exploration should not wait for elective imaging (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Emergency CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast before any surgical decision
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the imaging of choice; CT adds delay and radiation without changing surgical management when ultrasound already shows an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow and a known dermoid (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right salpingo-oophorectomy via laparotomy because the ovary is likely non-viable
options.rationale: Even ovaries appearing dusky or black at surgery should be detorted and reassessed because parenchyma often recovers; oophorectomy as initial action sacrifices fertility unnecessarily (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Urgent diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian-preserving cystectomy under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, an enlarged ovary with peripheral follicles and reduced Doppler flow on TVUS, and a known ovarian mass strongly suggest ovarian torsion; emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and conservative cystectomy preserves ovarian function. Doppler flow is unreliable for excluding torsion and clinical suspicion should drive surgery (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: IV opioid analgesia and discharge with outpatient gynaecology follow-up next week
options.rationale: Discharging a patient with suspected torsion is unsafe; emergency surgical management is the standard of care (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency. Suspicion is clinical and ultrasound supports the diagnosis (enlarged ovary, peripheral follicles, reduced/absent Doppler flow), but preserved Doppler does not exclude torsion. Management is emergency laparoscopy, detorsion and conservative cystectomy where possible to preserve fertility (RANZCOG; RCH CPG).
key_points: Risk factors: ovarian cyst >5 cm, dermoid, pregnancy, ovulation induction
Hallmarks: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting
TVUS: enlarged ovary, peripheral follicles, abnormal Doppler (but preserved flow doesn't exclude)
Treat with emergency laparoscopy + detorsion + cystectomy
Sacrifice ovary only if necrotic and not recoverable after detorsion period
explanation.pearls: Doppler is sensitive but not specific - up to 60% of confirmed torsions have preserved Doppler flow at the time of imaging. Clinical suspicion takes precedence (RANZCOG).

q.subtopic_detail: Lichen sclerosus - ultrapotent topical steroid + cancer surveillance
q.stem: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with a 12-month history of progressive vulval pruritus, soreness, and dyspareunia limiting intercourse with her long-term partner. She has noticed white, paper-thin skin around the labia minora and perineum, with occasional fissuring and contact bleeding when itching at night and during attempts at intercourse. Multiple courses of clotrimazole pessaries and over-the-counter hydrocortisone 1% over the past 6 months have not helped. She has had no postmenopausal bleeding from the cervix and her last cervical screening test 12 months ago was HPV not detected. She has Hashimoto's thyroiditis on stable thyroxine 75 mcg daily, well-controlled hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 28, and no immunosuppression. She has not used local or systemic oestrogen replacement. She has no personal or family history of vulval, cervical, breast or other gynaecological cancer. On examination there is well-demarcated 'figure-of-eight' white atrophic change involving the labia minora, clitoral hood and perianal skin, with partial resorption of the labia minora, narrowing of the introitus, and early posterior fourchette adhesion. There is one persistent indurated nodule 8 mm in diameter on the right labium majus that has been present for 6 weeks and has not responded to topical hydrocortisone. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic, paper-thin white change in classic figure-of-eight distribution. Loss of labia minora architecture. Discrete 8 mm indurated nodule on right labium majus, firm, non-tender. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening 12 months ago: HPV not detected.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Punch biopsy the indurated nodule and start clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic for lichen sclerosus; treatment is ultrapotent topical steroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) typically nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance. The 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC means any persistent indurated, ulcerated or pigmented lesion must be biopsied promptly (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Trial topical oestrogen cream for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: The findings (figure-of-eight white atrophic change, architectural loss) are classic for lichen sclerosus, not generalised atrophic vaginitis; topical oestrogen would not address the diagnosis or the suspicious nodule (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly and review again in 12 weeks
options.rationale: Although ultrapotent topical steroids are the mainstay treatment for lichen sclerosus, the new indurated nodule must be biopsied first because lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma, and an indurated persistent lesion is suspicious (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the entire affected vulval area
options.rationale: Vulvectomy or wide excision is not the standard treatment for lichen sclerosus; medical control with ultrapotent steroids is first-line and surgery is reserved for biopsy-proven malignancy or symptomatic architectural distortion (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer to dermatology for systemic methotrexate as steroid-sparing therapy now
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for refractory disease unresponsive to ultrapotent topical steroids; it is not first-line, and the immediate clinical priority is biopsy of the suspicious nodule (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

explanation.summary: Lichen sclerosus is treated with ultrapotent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) in a tapering regimen with maintenance. Vulval SCC develops in 4-5% lifetime, so any persistent indurated, ulcerated, pigmented or non-responding lesion must be biopsied. Lifelong surveillance is required (RANZCOG; BAD).
key_points: Classic appearance: figure-of-eight white atrophic vulval skin with architectural loss
First-line: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment - induction then taper to maintenance
Lifetime vulval SCC risk: approximately 4-5%
Biopsy any persistent indurated, ulcerated, pigmented, or steroid-unresponsive lesion
Lifelong surveillance review at least annually
explanation.pearls: Patient self-application is a key skill; demonstrate with a mirror, advise a fingertip-unit per application, and emphasise that maintenance therapy reduces both symptoms and the risk of malignant progression (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

q.subtopic_detail: Bartholin abscess - Word catheter as primary care management
q.stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 woman presents to her GP with a 3-day history of progressively painful right-sided labial swelling. The swelling is now egg-sized, tense, exquisitely tender and is interfering with sitting and walking. She has had a low-grade fever of 37.8 degC at home but has not had rigors. She has had no abnormal vaginal discharge for the past 3 months, no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, no urinary symptoms and no constitutional symptoms. She is in a monogamous relationship of 5 years and her most recent STI screen 4 months ago was negative for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis and HIV. She had a similar but smaller episode on the same side 2 years ago that resolved with incision and drainage in ED but recurred 3 months later as a painless cyst that has since fluctuated in size. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, is not pregnant (LMP 10 days ago, regular cycles), and has no other significant medical history. The GP clinic has Word catheters in stock, the GP is trained in their insertion under local anaesthetic, and the patient consents to a procedure today after written information.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Tense, fluctuant 4 cm swelling at the right posterior introitus (5 o'clock position), overlying erythema, exquisitely tender. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. No vulval ulceration. Speculum deferred due to pain.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of this Bartholin abscess?

options.text: Simple incision and drainage with packing, no catheter or marsupialisation
options.rationale: Simple I&D without a Word catheter or marsupialisation has a high recurrence rate (up to 40%); the duct often re-closes once the cavity collapses, particularly in a patient with prior recurrence (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Outpatient oral antibiotics alone (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BD) without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy without drainage rarely resolves a tense fluctuant abscess; drainage is the primary intervention, with antibiotics reserved for systemic features, cellulitis, immunosuppression or pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Incision and drainage with Word catheter insertion retained in situ 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: Word catheter placement after incision and drainage is the recommended primary-care option for an acute Bartholin abscess: it maintains an epithelialised tract while the cavity heals, with a recurrence rate substantially lower than simple I&D. The catheter is removed after 4-6 weeks (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Excision of the Bartholin gland under general anaesthetic as definitive management
options.rationale: Excision is reserved for recurrent abscesses unresponsive to drainage with Word catheter or marsupialisation, or for suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (especially in women over 40 with a solid mass); it carries higher morbidity (bleeding, dyspareunia) than less invasive options (RANZCOG).

options.text: Sitz baths and oral analgesia, with re-review in 1 week if not improving
options.rationale: Conservative measures are insufficient for a tense fluctuant abscess; while sitz baths help drainage of small early lesions, definitive drainage is required for an established abscess of this size (KEMH Bartholin abscess).

explanation.summary: An acute Bartholin abscess is best managed with incision and drainage plus Word catheter placement to maintain an epithelialised tract for 4-6 weeks. Marsupialisation is an effective alternative, particularly for recurrence. Simple I&D alone has a recurrence rate up to 40%. Antibiotics are reserved for systemic features, cellulitis, immunosuppression or pregnancy (RANZCOG; KEMH).
key_points: First-line: I&D + Word catheter, retained 4-6 weeks
Alternative: marsupialisation (suturing cyst wall to skin) - durable but requires more skill
Simple I&D alone -> high recurrence rate
Antibiotics indicated for cellulitis, sepsis, immunosuppression, pregnancy
Excision considered for refractory recurrence or suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (esp. age >40 with solid mass)
explanation.pearls: In women over 40 with a persistent solid Bartholin mass rather than cystic abscess, biopsy/excision is essential to exclude Bartholin gland carcinoma (RANZCOG).

q.subtopic_detail: Tamoxifen-related endometrial bleeding - investigate symptoms, do not screen asymptomatic
q.stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 woman has been taking tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 4 years as adjuvant endocrine therapy for ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative, node-negative left breast cancer treated 4 years ago with breast-conserving surgery and adjuvant radiotherapy. She was previously postmenopausal (natural menopause age 52, no bleeding for 12 years prior to commencing tamoxifen) and is now referred to gynaecology after presenting to her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks, each lasting 2-3 days and requiring two pads daily. She has had no postcoital bleeding, no discharge, no pelvic pain, no abdominal bloating and no urinary or bowel symptoms. Her last cervical screening 18 months ago was HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She has hypertension (perindopril 5 mg daily, BP 138/82 on review), a BMI of 31, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.9%, metformin 1 g BD). She has no personal or family history of Lynch-associated cancers and meets no Lynch testing criteria. She has never used hormone therapy or unopposed oestrogen pre-tamoxifen. She takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents and has no history of VTE. She is fully adherent to tamoxifen, has not missed doses, and is keen to complete her planned 10-year adjuvant course but is anxious that bleeding may signal cancer. Her breast oncologist's letter notes a low risk of breast cancer recurrence on validated risk calculators but does not advise on gynaecological management. She has had no abnormal surveillance mammograms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft. Speculum: atrophic mucosa, small amount of blood at the os, no cervical lesion. Bimanual: uterus normal size, mobile, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (115-165). TVUS performed by GP: endometrium 9 mm, heterogeneous, with several small cystic spaces; no focal mass identified; ovaries normal.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discontinue tamoxifen and substitute an aromatase inhibitor without further gynaecological investigation
options.rationale: Stopping tamoxifen unilaterally compromises breast cancer outcomes; substitution decisions belong to the breast MDT after gynaecological diagnosis is established. Tamoxifen change does not address the immediate need to exclude endometrial pathology (eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires structural and histological assessment. Tamoxifen causes subepithelial stromal change and cystic atrophy that thicken the endometrium on TVUS even in benign disease, making the standard >4 mm threshold less reliable; hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is the recommended investigation in any symptomatic tamoxifen user. Routine TVUS surveillance of asymptomatic tamoxifen users is NOT recommended in Australian guidance (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Repeat TVUS in 3 months to assess for any change in endometrial thickness
options.rationale: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without changing the indication for histology in a symptomatic tamoxifen user; TVUS findings in this population are also poorly specific for malignancy due to tamoxifen-related cystic stromal change (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

options.text: Reassure as endometrial thickness is acceptable for tamoxifen users; review if bleeding recurs
options.rationale: The standard >4 mm threshold cannot be applied to symptomatic tamoxifen users, and any postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires histological exclusion of cancer; reassurance is unsafe (eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Commence routine 6-monthly TVUS surveillance and biopsy only if thickness exceeds 8 mm
options.rationale: Asymptomatic surveillance of tamoxifen users with TVUS is NOT recommended in Australian guidance; surveillance does not improve outcomes and frequently triggers unnecessary investigations. Bleeding, not thickness, is the investigation trigger in tamoxifen users (eviQ Tamoxifen; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Tamoxifen approximately doubles endometrial cancer risk in postmenopausal users. Routine TVUS surveillance is NOT recommended for asymptomatic users because tamoxifen-related stromal change reduces the specificity of endometrial thickness measurements. Any abnormal bleeding requires hysteroscopy with directed biopsy. Treatment decisions about continuing tamoxifen are made by the breast MDT (eviQ Tamoxifen; eviQ Endometrial cancer; RANZCOG).
key_points: Tamoxifen has anti-oestrogenic effects on breast but pro-oestrogenic on endometrium
Approximately 2-fold increase in endometrial cancer risk in postmenopausal users
No asymptomatic TVUS surveillance - it doesn't improve outcomes
Any abnormal bleeding -> hysteroscopy + directed biopsy
TVUS thickness threshold of >4 mm is not reliable in tamoxifen users
Tamoxifen continuation decisions made by breast MDT after gynae diagnosis
explanation.pearls: The 'investigate symptoms, don't screen asymptomatic' principle is paramount for tamoxifen surveillance - it is one of the few oncology contexts where active surveillance is explicitly NOT recommended (eviQ Tamoxifen).

q.subtopic_detail: Discriminating sickle crisis types - sequestration vs aplastic
q.stem: A 3-year-old girl with known HbSS sickle cell disease, on daily penicillin V prophylaxis and folic acid, is brought to a regional ED by her mother. Over the last 24 hours she has become progressively lethargic, with poor oral intake but no vomiting or diarrhoea. Her older brother had a febrile illness with a malar rash one week ago. She has not had any cough, dyspnoea, chest or limb pain, no preceding bony pain and no haematuria. She is afebrile today, but two days ago had a low-grade temperature. Vaccinations are up to date and her last transfusion was 4 months ago. On examination she is pale and tired, alert but quiet, with no scleral icterus, no rash, clear chest and no neurology. Her abdomen is soft with a non-tender spleen palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, unchanged from her baseline. Observations: T 36.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/56, capillary refill 2 s. Bedside bloods: Hb 48 g/L (baseline 85), WCC 6.8 x10^9/L, platelets 256, reticulocytes 12 x10^9/L (baseline approximately 300), bilirubin 24 micromol/L, LDH mildly elevated. Mother brought her in because of pallor noticed at bath time.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/56, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Pale, alert, spleen 2 cm below costal margin (baseline), no jaundice, no haemodynamic compromise.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 48 g/L (baseline 85), reticulocytes 12 x10^9/L (baseline ~300), WCC 6.8, platelets 256, bilirubin 24 micromol/L.

q.lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
options.rationale: Splenic sequestration is characterised by a sudden enlargement of the spleen by 2 cm or more from baseline with a brisk reticulocyte response and signs of hypovolaemic shock; this child has an unchanged spleen and a suppressed reticulocyte count (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis from delayed transfusion reaction
options.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis presents with jaundice, dark urine, raised LDH and bilirubin and a brisk reticulocytosis, typically within 1-3 weeks of transfusion; her last transfusion was 4 months ago and the reticulocyte count is suppressed (BSH guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Transfusion in sickle cell disease

options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor dietary intake
options.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months and would not drop the haemoglobin by nearly half in 24 hours; reticulocytes in chronic iron deficiency are typically normal rather than profoundly suppressed (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection
options.rationale: A rapid fall in haemoglobin with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count (here 12 against a baseline of approximately 300 x10^9/L) in a child with chronic haemolysis is diagnostic of an aplastic crisis. Parvovirus B19, the cause of fifth disease (the brother's malar rash), suppresses erythroid precursors for 7-10 days and is the typical precipitant (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; eviQ).

options.text: Vaso-occlusive bone marrow infarction
options.rationale: Marrow infarction causes severe pain rather than asymptomatic anaemia, and is usually associated with leucocytosis and an appropriate reticulocyte response (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

explanation.summary: Aplastic crisis in sickle cell disease is a sudden drop in haemoglobin with a suppressed reticulocyte count (typically < 50 x10^9/L), most often triggered by parvovirus B19 which transiently halts erythropoiesis. It is distinguished from sequestration (sudden splenomegaly, hypovolaemia, reticulocytosis) by the reticulocyte count and spleen size (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: Aplastic: suppressed retics + unchanged spleen + parvovirus contact
Sequestration: spleen enlarged 2+ cm + shock + reticulocytosis
Hyperhaemolysis: post-transfusion + jaundice + brisk retics
Manage aplastic crisis with transfusion and parvovirus isolation
Isolate from pregnant staff/family until resolution
explanation.pearls: A fifth-disease rash in a sibling is the classic clue - the index child may never have been febrile.

sources.label: BSH guideline: Transfusion in sickle cell disease

q.subtopic_detail: SCID red flags - recognition and urgent investigation
q.stem: A 4-month-old boy is brought to a regional ED for his third admission, this time with 10 days of persistent oral thrush despite two courses of oral nystatin, an erythematous nappy-area candidiasis, and a 5-week history of chronic non-bloody diarrhoea with poor weight gain (current weight on the 3rd centile, having tracked along the 50th at birth). His past admissions in the last 6 weeks have been for pneumocystis-like interstitial pneumonia requiring nasal high-flow and one episode of CMV viraemia. He was born at term to non-consanguineous parents; his mother had a brother who died of an unexplained pneumonia at 7 months of age. He received BCG at birth in his country of birth, and rotavirus oral vaccine at 6 weeks in Australia. On examination he is small and pale, with oral plaques, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on a recent chest X-ray, no palpable lymph nodes or tonsillar tissue, and bilateral fine inspiratory crackles. T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA. FBC today: Hb 96, WCC 5.2 with absolute lymphocyte count 0.6 x10^9/L (well below the age-specific lower limit of around 3.0). Total IgG, IgA, IgM are all low. The paediatric team is concerned about a primary immunodeficiency.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA.
data_table.Examination: Failure to thrive, oral thrush, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on CXR, absent tonsils/lymph nodes, bilateral crackles.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96, WCC 5.2, ALC 0.6 x10^9/L, IgG / IgA / IgM all low.
data_table.Medications: Recent rotavirus oral vaccine, BCG at birth overseas.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next step?

options.text: Empirical oral fluconazole and clinic review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of a likely SCID; oral fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and the time-critical need for transplant pathway evaluation (ASCIA: SCID).

options.text: Same-day immunology referral, lymphocyte subsets, isolation, no live vaccines
options.rationale: The presentation is highly suspicious for severe combined immunodeficiency: failure to thrive, severe lymphopenia (ALC < 2.5-3.0), opportunistic infection (Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, persistent candidiasis), maternal family history of unexplained infant death, BCG exposure, and absent thymic shadow. Urgent immunology referral, T/B/NK lymphocyte subsets, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines and use of irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical (ASCIA: SCID; eviQ).

options.text: Reassure parents that recurrent infections are normal at 4 months
options.rationale: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age and warrant urgent specialist evaluation (RCH CPG: Recurrent infections; ASCIA).

options.text: Give routine 6-month MMR catch-up vaccination at this visit
options.rationale: Live vaccines including MMR, rotavirus, BCG and varicella are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection; the immediate priority is to stop further live vaccine exposure and confirm the diagnosis (ASCIA: SCID; NCIRS).

options.text: Send HIV antibody (not PCR) and clinic review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists in infants under 18 months irrespective of HIV status; HIV PCR (proviral DNA or RNA) is the test of choice and should be sent in parallel to immunology workup, not as a stand-alone next step (RCH CPG: HIV in children).

explanation.summary: SCID is a paediatric emergency. Red flags include severe lymphopenia (ALC under age-specific lower limit, often below 2.5 x10^9/L), failure to thrive, opportunistic infection (PCP, CMV, persistent candidiasis), absent thymic shadow, and family history of early unexplained infant death. Urgent immunology referral, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines, and irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical; haematopoietic stem cell transplant in the first 3.5 months of life carries a markedly better prognosis (ASCIA: SCID).
key_points: Severe lymphopenia at any age: think SCID and check subsets
Withhold live vaccines: rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella
Use irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products
Early HSCT before 3.5 months improves survival markedly
Family history of unexplained infant infectious death is a red flag
explanation.pearls: If a 4-month-old has had Pneumocystis pneumonia, the diagnosis is SCID until immunology proves otherwise.

q.subtopic_detail: Measles - public health management and contact prophylaxis
q.stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised child has measles confirmed on PCR after a maculopapular rash spread from the face caudally over 24 hours. He had three days of fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis before the rash, and Koplik spots were identified at the GP visit the day before rash onset. He attended a community playgroup on the day before his rash appeared and the morning of rash onset, while infectious. The public health unit has been notified and is contacting the playgroup register. Among the playgroup contacts identified within the 4 hours before to 4 days after his rash, several need urgent post-exposure management decisions. They include: a 5-month-old infant whose mother is rubella-immune but has no documented measles history or serology; a 13-month-old child who received a single dose of MMR five days ago; a 35-year-old pregnant playgroup helper at 22 weeks gestation with no documented measles immunity and a negative measles IgG on urgent serology done this morning; a 7-year-old child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukaemia in week 2 of induction chemotherapy and currently neutropenic; and a 30-year-old playgroup leader born in 1992 with two documented MMR doses on her workplace immunisation record.

data_table.Exposure window: 4 hours before prodrome to 4 days after rash onset (still infectious)
data_table.Contacts requiring decision: 5-month infant; 13-month with one MMR five days ago; pregnant 22-week non-immune helper; 7-year-old on induction chemo for ALL; 30-year-old with 2x MMR documented
data_table.Time since exposure: Within 72 hours for all listed contacts

q.lead_in: Which contact most appropriately receives normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) rather than MMR vaccine as post-exposure prophylaxis?

options.text: The 5-month-old infant, given MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is not given under 6 months of age - it is poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody; infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written (MMR) is wrong for this age (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The 13-month-old who received MMR five days ago, given a second MMR now
options.rationale: MMR five days before exposure is too recent to have generated protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 hours of exposure may still help; NHIG is not first-line here and a single early dose is recorded but the routine schedule (second dose at 18 months) should be followed (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The pregnant 22-week non-immune playgroup helper, given NHIG within 6 days of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is a live vaccine and CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. A non-immune pregnant contact within 6 days of exposure should receive NHIG to attenuate or prevent maternal measles and complications; serology has confirmed she is non-immune (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles; CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: The 7-year-old with ALL on induction chemotherapy, given MMR within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy; this child should receive NHIG, so the option as written is incorrect (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The 30-year-old playgroup leader with two documented MMR doses, given a third MMR booster
options.rationale: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention or booster is required for this contact (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

explanation.summary: For susceptible measles contacts, MMR within 72 hours is preferred in those eligible for live vaccine, but NHIG within 6 days is indicated in those for whom live vaccine is contraindicated: infants under 6 months, pregnant non-immune contacts, and severely immunocompromised patients (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).
key_points: MMR within 72h of exposure for eligible non-immune contacts (AIH: Measles).
NHIG within 6 days for pregnant non-immune, infants <6 months, severely immunocompromised (AIH: Measles).
Two documented MMR doses count as presumptive immunity in Australia (AIH: Measles).
Measles is notifiable - phone the public health unit on clinical suspicion (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
explanation.pearls: Pregnant non-immune contacts get NHIG, never MMR - live vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

q.subtopic_detail: Varicella - immunocompromised post-exposure management
q.stem: A 6-year-old boy is in week 3 of induction chemotherapy for high-risk B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia at a tertiary paediatric oncology centre. He shared a 1-hour indoor classroom session in the day hospital school room with another child who has since been confirmed by PCR to have early varicella, with a vesicular rash that had been present for less than 24 hours during the session. The exposure was 36 hours ago. The boy has had no past episode of clinical varicella, no varicella vaccination on his record (his older sibling refused MMRV at 18 months for the family on philosophical grounds and the patient was diagnosed before catch-up), and his pre-treatment baseline varicella IgG was negative on serology drawn before induction. He is currently profoundly neutropenic with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.4 x 10^9/L, has no rash, no fever, no oral lesions, and feels well. He has no drug allergies. The parents are anxious and want to know what should happen next, and the oncology team has already isolated him in a single room with contact and airborne precautions while the management decision is finalised. Zoster immunoglobulin and intravenous antivirals are both available on site.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 96, RR 20, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Well; no rash, no vesicles, no oral lesions
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 0.4 x 10^9/L, pre-chemo varicella IgG negative
data_table.History: ALL induction week 3; significant face-to-face exposure 36 hours ago to a child with early varicella

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer the varicella vaccine within 72 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and is CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Administer zoster immunoglobulin (ZIG/VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severely immunocompromised susceptible contacts (varicella IgG negative, no prior infection or vaccination) with a significant exposure should receive ZIG within 96 hours (preferably as soon as possible, but the AIH allows extension up to 10 days in some circumstances) to attenuate or prevent disseminated varicella (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Commence prophylactic oral valaciclovir from day 7 to day 21 post-exposure
options.rationale: Antiviral prophylaxis with aciclovir or valaciclovir from days 7 to 14 may be considered as an adjunct or alternative when ZIG is unavailable, but ZIG is the preferred first-line option in this scenario per the AIH (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Reassurance and observation, treating with intravenous aciclovir only if a rash develops
options.rationale: Waiting for the rash to appear before acting forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised children carries significant mortality (RCH CPG: Varicella; Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Send urgent varicella IgG and IgM and decide on prophylaxis based on the result
options.rationale: Repeat serology is unnecessary - the pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative, so he is documented susceptible; waiting for serology delays ZIG and reduces its effectiveness (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

explanation.summary: Severely immunocompromised susceptible children with significant varicella exposure should receive ZIG (zoster immunoglobulin) as soon as possible, ideally within 96 hours; live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in this group (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).
key_points: Significant exposure = household OR >=1 hour indoor face-to-face OR shared room OR substantial hospital contact (AIH: Varicella).
Susceptible = no past disease, no vaccination, and seronegative if tested (AIH: Varicella).
ZIG first-line in immunocompromised, neonates, and non-immune pregnant women (AIH: Varicella).
Antiviral prophylaxis day 7 to 14 is an adjunct/alternative when ZIG unavailable (AIH: Varicella).
explanation.pearls: Neonates born to mothers who develop varicella from 7 days before to 2 days after delivery also need ZIG - very high mortality without it.

q.subtopic_detail: Pregnant contact of parvovirus B19 - obstetric implications
q.stem: A 32-year-old early childhood teacher who is 18 weeks pregnant attends her GP after multiple confirmed cases of erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) were diagnosed in the kindergarten class she teaches over the last fortnight. She was in close prolonged indoor contact with two affected children one week ago, before they were excluded with their characteristic facial erythema and lacy peripheral rash. She is asymptomatic with no rash, arthralgia, or fever. This is her second pregnancy; her first was uncomplicated and the child is now in her class. Her morphology scan at 18 weeks last week was reported as structurally normal with appropriate growth and amniotic fluid volume, and she is up to date with routine antenatal care, including the first-trimester combined screen which was low risk. She has no past clinical history of slapped cheek and no documented parvovirus B19 serology on file. She has no allergies and is on pregnancy multivitamins only. She has not had any blood transfusion. Examination is normal with no rash, no joint swelling, and an unremarkable obstetric examination with fundal height consistent with dates. The GP is unsure whether she is immune or susceptible, what test to request, and how each possible result will change her antenatal pathway.

data_table.Examination: Normal, no rash, gravid uterus at fundal height consistent with dates
data_table.History: 18 weeks pregnant; significant occupational exposure to confirmed parvovirus B19 1 week ago; no documented prior serology

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Administer normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) intramuscularly within 7 days of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: NHIG is not indicated for parvovirus exposure in pregnancy; there is no proven prophylactic benefit and current Australian guidance recommends serological assessment and surveillance instead (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Arrange urgent amniocentesis for parvovirus B19 PCR to detect vertical foetal transmission
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is invasive and is reserved for confirmed maternal infection with evidence of foetal compromise; it is not a first-line investigation in an asymptomatic exposed contact (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Weekly foetal middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler velocimetry from now for 12 weeks
options.rationale: MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia is appropriate AFTER confirmed maternal infection (typically weekly for 8 to 12 weeks); routinely starting it before serology in an exposed but possibly already-immune woman is inappropriate use of resources (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Maternal parvovirus B19 IgG and IgM serology to determine immune status and acute infection
options.rationale: Serology distinguishes pre-existing immunity (IgG positive, IgM negative - no further action), susceptible non-infected (both negative - repeat in 4 weeks), and recent infection (IgM positive +/- IgG seroconversion - escalate to MFM for serial foetal ultrasound looking for hydrops) (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy; KEMH: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Reassure and discharge - parvovirus B19 has no documented effect on a pregnancy past 12 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal parvovirus B19 infection can cause foetal anaemia and hydrops fetalis with the highest risk in the second trimester (around 9 to 20 weeks); discharge without investigation is unsafe at 18 weeks (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

explanation.summary: Pregnant women exposed to parvovirus B19 need serology to determine immune status; confirmed acute maternal infection in early-to-mid pregnancy triggers MFM-led MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia and hydrops (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).
key_points: About 50% of women of reproductive age are already immune (RANZCOG).
IgG positive / IgM negative = immune, reassure (RANZCOG).
IgG and IgM both negative = susceptible, repeat in 4 weeks (RANZCOG).
IgM positive = confirmed recent infection, refer MFM for serial MCA Doppler and ultrasound for hydrops (RANZCOG).
explanation.pearls: Highest risk for foetal loss/hydrops is exposure before 20 weeks; after 20 weeks the foetus has greater haematopoietic reserve and the foetal risk drops sharply.

q.subtopic_detail: Erythema multiforme vs Stevens-Johnson syndrome - recognition and triage
q.stem: An 11-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department with a rash and oral pain. He started oral lamotrigine 3 weeks ago after a recent diagnosis of focal epilepsy with secondary generalisation, and the dose was rapidly titrated by his treating neurologist alongside ongoing sodium valproate, which he had been taking for 6 months. He has had no other new medications, no recent herpes simplex outbreak, and no recent Mycoplasma-like respiratory illness. Over the last 36 hours he has developed a flu-like prodrome with fever to 38.7 deg C, malaise, photophobia, and a sore mouth and eyes. Today the rash has spread rapidly. On examination he is unwell, mildly tachycardic, with temperature 38.6 deg C, heart rate 124, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98% room air, and BP 105/65. There are confluent dusky erythematous macules with central duskiness and bullae forming on the trunk and proximal limbs covering an estimated 8% body surface area, with sloughing on light lateral pressure on intact-appearing skin. He has haemorrhagic crusting of the lips, painful ulcers on the buccal mucosa and palate, bilateral red painful eyes with conjunctival injection and mucopurulent discharge, and dysuria with genital erosions visible on inspection. There are some flat lesions on the palms but no classic three-zone targets. He is alert, distressed, and able to drink only sips.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 105/65
data_table.Examination: Dusky macules with central blistering on 8% BSA, positive lateral skin shear (Nikolsky), mucous membrane involvement of mouth, eyes, and genitals
data_table.Medications: Lamotrigine started 3 weeks ago, rapidly titrated

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Switch lamotrigine to oral carbamazepine and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome affects younger children (typically <5 years), spares mucous membranes, and is not associated with target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine carries the same SJS risk as lamotrigine and is contraindicated (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Continue lamotrigine at half dose and start oral prednisolone for presumed erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: Erythema multiforme is characterised by classic three-zone target lesions, mostly acral distribution, and is usually post-HSV; the dusky macules with sloughing, positive Nikolsky, recent high-risk drug, and three mucosal sites here are SJS, not EM, and continuing the trigger drug is dangerous (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Commence empirical IV aciclovir and admit to a general paediatric ward for HSV-associated erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; the diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fit SJS and ward-level care is inappropriate intensity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Cease lamotrigine immediately, admit to a paediatric burns/HDU service, and consult ophthalmology and dermatology urgently
options.rationale: Bullae plus positive Nikolsky sign plus involvement of at least two mucosal sites plus recent high-risk drug (lamotrigine, particularly with rapid titration or with concurrent valproate) is Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN spectrum. Immediate cessation of the offending agent, admission to a paediatric burns or HDU equivalent for fluid and skin care, and urgent ophthalmology review (ocular sequelae are the leading long-term morbidity) are mandatory (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome; RCH CPG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol, and arrange neurology review in 2 weeks for lamotrigine dose review
options.rationale: SJS has 5 to 15% mortality with delayed care, and ocular sequelae are sight-threatening - outpatient management is unsafe (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

explanation.summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN is a drug-induced, life-threatening mucocutaneous reaction with epidermal necrosis. Lamotrigine (especially with rapid titration or concurrent valproate) is one of the highest-risk agents. Stop the drug immediately, admit to burns/HDU, and engage ophthalmology early to prevent permanent ocular morbidity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
key_points: SJS: <10% BSA detachment; TEN: >30%; SJS/TEN overlap 10 to 30% (eTG: SJS).
Highest-risk drugs in children: lamotrigine, allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin), NSAIDs (eTG: SJS).
Mucosal involvement >=2 sites is required for SJS - eye involvement is the leading long-term morbidity driver (eTG: SJS).
Add to ADR / Lifetime Drug Alert / Australian Vaccine and Medicine Allergy Service notification (TGA).
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine titration too fast (or alongside valproate, which doubles the lamotrigine level) is the classic exam trigger - in real life, titration must follow product information to halve the SJS risk.

q.subtopic_detail: Atypical exanthem with shock - differentiating toxin-mediated rashes
q.stem: A 6-year-old previously well fully immunised girl is brought to a tertiary paediatric emergency department by her parents. Five days ago she had varicella that was treated supportively at home, with the rash now beginning to crust over and no new lesions for 48 hours. Three days ago she developed pain in her left thigh which has been progressively worsening - she now refuses to bear weight on that leg and her parents describe the pain as seeming out of proportion to the modest external skin changes they can see. Over the last 12 hours she has become febrile to 39.8 deg C, vomited twice without diarrhoea, and developed a diffuse blanching macular erythematous rash particularly on the trunk and the affected thigh. On arrival she is grey, mottled, and confused, with temperature 39.6 deg C, heart rate 178, respiratory rate 48, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 70/35, and capillary refill 6 seconds in the limbs and 5 seconds centrally. Her left thigh has a 6 x 8 cm area of dusky erythema overlying a healing varicella lesion, with marked tenderness extending well beyond the visible erythema and a small area of skin anaesthesia centrally. There is no obvious crepitus on the limb. Blood cultures have been drawn. Bloods show WCC 24 x 10^9/L with marked left shift, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L (baseline normal), ALT 240 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, and platelets 92 x 10^9/L. The on-call paediatric surgeon and ICU registrar are on their way.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 178, RR 48, SpO2 95% RA, BP 70/35, CRT 6 sec
data_table.Examination: Diffuse blanching erythroderma, dusky tender 6 x 8 cm patch on thigh overlying healing varicella, pain extending beyond erythema, focal skin anaesthesia
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6, creatinine 110, ALT 240
data_table.History: Varicella 5 days ago, now crusting

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: MRI of the left thigh before intervention to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement
options.rationale: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis - the diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic; resuscitation and antibiotics begin in the resus bay (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Admit, commence IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin for atypical Kawasaki disease
options.rationale: Atypical or incomplete Kawasaki disease can mimic this picture but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, and the dusky soft tissue lesion overlying healing varicella are not Kawasaki; treating as Kawasaki misses life-threatening necrotising fasciitis (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir monotherapy for disseminated varicella with secondary haemodynamic compromise and septic shock
options.rationale: Disseminated varicella in an immunocompetent child is rare; the focal painful thigh lesion with anaesthesia and the systemic toxin-mediated picture point to invasive bacterial superinfection, not viral dissemination (RCH CPG: Varicella).

options.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and admit to a general paediatric ward for scarlet fever observation
options.rationale: Scarlet fever causes a sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and is not associated with shock, lactic acidosis, AKI, or pain disproportionate to skin findings - oral therapy and ward-level care are dangerously inadequate (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Resuscitate, give IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin and clindamycin, and call surgery for urgent debridement
options.rationale: Post-varicella, pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, shock and multi-organ derangement (acute kidney injury, transaminitis, lactic acidosis) is invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection plus streptococcal toxic shock syndrome. Broad-spectrum cover (e.g. ceftriaxone) PLUS anti-staphylococcal (vancomycin) PLUS clindamycin (toxin suppression and ribosomal action) are essential, with urgent surgical debridement the definitive intervention - antibiotics do not penetrate necrotic tissue (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).

explanation.summary: Post-varicella invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection and streptococcal toxic shock syndrome is a paediatric emergency. Recognition rests on pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, and rapid multi-organ failure. Resuscitate, broad antibiotics including clindamycin, and urgent surgical debridement (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).
key_points: Pain out of proportion + focal anaesthesia + dusky skin + shock = nec fasc until proven otherwise (eTG).
Clindamycin is critical - inhibits exotoxin production (eTG).
Definitive treatment is surgical - imaging must not delay theatre (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).
iGAS post-varicella is a recognised pattern in Australian and international cohorts (CDNA: iGAS SoNG).
explanation.pearls: If you are not sure whether to call the surgeons, you already are - operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic and there is no safer time than now.

q.subtopic_detail: Safety planning vs admission decision in BPD
q.stem: A 23-year-old woman with established BPD attends the emergency department after taking 8 paracetamol tablets following a fight with her current partner. This is her fourth low-lethality presentation in the past 6 months. Serum paracetamol is below the treatment line at 8 hours and LFTs are normal. On interview she is engaged, has agreed to handover of remaining medication to a relative, identifies her older sister as a strong protective factor able to stay with her tonight, and has a current outpatient DBT clinician who can see her tomorrow morning. She denies current plan or intent for further self-harm, denies psychotic features, and her K-10 is 28. She has no current major depressive episode by SIGECAPS criteria. She has previously had a 4-week voluntary admission that she describes as destabilising, with an increase in self-harm in the week after discharge. Her treating team has a documented care plan recommending brief, focused community management for low-lethality presentations.

data_table.Examination: Alert, reactive affect, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, multiple healed and recent linear forearm scars.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol below Rumack-Matthew treatment line, ALT 22 U/L, INR 1.0, K-10 28.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with a structured safety plan and next-day DBT review
options.rationale: For low-lethality chronic self-harm in BPD with adequate community supports, NHMRC and RANZCP recommend a structured safety plan (means restriction, identified supports, crisis numbers, early follow-up) and rapid return to the patient's usual psychological therapy; admission is not required when risk can be managed less restrictively (NHMRC BPD; LifeSpan/Black Dog safety planning).

options.text: Brief admission for 48 hours to monitor for further self-harm
options.rationale: Routine short admissions for chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD are not recommended; the NHMRC guideline favours community management with brief admissions only when safety cannot otherwise be managed, and her previous admission worsened her trajectory (NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission criteria
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires mental illness plus risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; she has engaged voluntarily, has a clear safety plan and a treating clinician next day, so the least-restrictive principle is not satisfied (state Mental Health Acts; NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and discharge
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not recommended for the core BPD presentation; starting an antipsychotic at discharge after a low-lethality overdose adds metabolic risk without core benefit (NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Refer for inpatient DBT programme as next-day transfer
options.rationale: Australian DBT is predominantly delivered as an outpatient or day-programme model; inpatient DBT is not standard and her existing outpatient DBT clinician should be the first point of escalation (NHMRC BPD).

explanation.summary: For chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD with adequate supports, the evidence-based response is a structured safety plan and rapid return to outpatient psychological therapy. Prolonged or routine admissions can worsen outcomes and erode autonomy; involuntary care requires least-restrictive justification.
key_points: Safety planning (Stanley-Brown structure): warning signs, internal coping, social distraction, people to ask for help, professionals/crisis lines, means restriction
Means restriction is a high-yield intervention - handover of medications, removal of access to lethal means
Admission reserved for: acute change in pattern, high-lethality attempt, intent without supports
Comorbidities (MDD, substance use) may shift the threshold for admission
Document risk reasoning explicitly, including least-restrictive justification
explanation.pearls: Chronic vs acute-on-chronic risk: the threshold for admission rises when the pattern is chronic and low-lethality and falls when intent, lethality or means change suddenly (NHMRC BPD).

q.subtopic_detail: Hypoactive delirium vs depression
q.stem: An 84-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with a urinary tract infection. On day 3 of intravenous ceftriaxone the nursing staff are concerned that she is withdrawn, eating poorly, sleeping during the day and unable to engage with the ward physiotherapist. Her usual GP letter from 2 weeks ago documents independent ADLs with walking aid, no prior mood disorder, and stable cognition with an MMSE of 27 at her last GP review. On examination she is drowsy but rousable, slow to respond, attention is markedly impaired on backwards-counting, and her thinking is disorganised when she does engage. She has no thoughts of suicide and her affect is flat rather than clearly sad. Sodium is 128 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, WCC 12.6 x10^9/L, CRP 84 mg/L. She is on paracetamol regularly and was started on oxycodone 5 mg PRN for hip pain two days ago. There is no prior antidepressant history.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, markedly impaired attention, disorganised thinking, flat affect, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, urea 12, Cr 102, WCC 12.6, CRP 84, MSU growing E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone
data_table.Medications: Ceftriaxone IV, paracetamol regular, oxycodone 5 mg PRN (commenced day 1)

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence sertraline 25 mg daily for late-life depression
options.rationale: This presentation has acute onset, fluctuating attention and disorganised thinking - consistent with hypoactive delirium rather than depression. Starting an antidepressant before treating delirium is premature and risks SIADH worsening hyponatraemia (ACSQHC Delirium CCS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD for behavioural disturbance
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are reserved for delirium with severe agitation or distress that risks harm; hypoactive presentations should not be routinely treated with antipsychotics (ACSQHC; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Withhold oxycodone, correct hyponatraemia and treat the UTI
options.rationale: The CAM features are met for hypoactive delirium. The single most appropriate step is to identify and reverse precipitants: stop the newly added opioid, correct hyponatraemia with judicious fluid management, ensure ongoing UTI treatment, optimise environment, hydration, mobilisation and orientation (ACSQHC Delirium CCS).

options.text: Arrange CT brain to exclude an acute infarct
options.rationale: CT brain is reserved for delirium with focal neurology, head injury, anticoagulation or unexplained drop in conscious level; routine imaging is not first-line in delirium with a clear precipitant (ACSQHC).

options.text: Refer to consultation-liaison psychiatry for ECT consideration
options.rationale: ECT is not first-line in this acute medical context; the diagnosis is not depression and ECT is reserved for severe treatment-resistant or psychotic depression in older adults (RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Hypoactive delirium is the most missed delirium subtype and is often mislabelled as depression. CAM features (acute onset, fluctuating attention, disorganised thinking or altered consciousness) point to delirium. Management is to identify and reverse precipitants - the PINCH-ME mnemonic (Pain, Infection, Nutrition, Constipation, Hydration, Medication, Environment) is useful.
key_points: Hypoactive delirium > hyperactive in prevalence and is associated with worse outcomes
Antidepressants do not treat delirium and may worsen confusion, hyponatraemia or anticholinergic burden
Common precipitants: opioids, anticholinergics, infection, electrolytes, urinary retention, constipation
Antipsychotics: lowest effective dose, only when distress or risk; never for hypoactive presentations routinely
Mortality and length of stay rise with delirium - prevention and early reversal are core geriatric care
explanation.pearls: Quiet withdrawn older inpatient is delirium until proven otherwise, not depression (ACSQHC Delirium Clinical Care Standard).

q.subtopic_detail: Capacity assessment - decision-specific
q.stem: A 79-year-old woman with mild Alzheimer dementia is admitted with a displaced intracapsular fractured neck of femur after a fall at home. The orthopaedic team plan a hemiarthroplasty within 48 hours. She lives independently with a son nearby, manages her own bills, has an advance care directive that does not specifically address fractures, and has no documented enduring guardian. On interview she is alert and conversant, oriented to person and place, knows the year but not the date. She knows she has broken her hip and that the doctors want to do an operation to put it back together. She can repeat back, in her own words, that the operation involves replacing part of the bone, that risks include bleeding, infection and clots, and that without surgery she would be bed-bound with significant pain. She says she wants to have the surgery so she can walk again. Her MoCA today is 22 of 30 with deficits in delayed recall and visuospatial domains.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented to person/place, MoCA 22/30, conversant, no acute confusion
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118, EUC unremarkable, INR 1.0, ECG NSR, AP pelvis: displaced intracapsular NOF fracture
data_table.Medications: Donepezil 10 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily, paracetamol 1 g QID

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate determination of capacity for this surgical consent?

options.text: She lacks capacity because her MoCA is below 26
options.rationale: Capacity is not determined by a cognitive test score; the MoCA cut-off is for screening cognitive impairment, not capacity. Decision-specific capacity assessment is required (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP capacity assessment).

options.text: She lacks capacity because of her dementia diagnosis
options.rationale: A diagnosis of dementia does not equal incapacity; many people with mild dementia retain capacity for specific decisions, and assumption of incapacity is contrary to Australian law (Australian Law Reform Commission).

options.text: Defer to her son to provide substitute consent
options.rationale: Substitute decision-makers are engaged only when the person lacks capacity for the decision; defaulting to family bypasses her autonomy (state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: She has capacity for this specific decision at this time
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. She can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the information relevant to this decision in her own words; she meets the functional test for capacity for this surgical consent despite mild dementia (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP; respective state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Request a Mental Health Act assessment for involuntary treatment
options.rationale: The Mental Health Act does not authorise consent to surgical procedures; for incapable patients without an advance directive or guardian, guardianship pathways or emergency consent provisions apply, not the Mental Health Act (state Mental Health Acts).

explanation.summary: Capacity is decision-specific, time-specific, and presumed unless disproven. The functional test asks whether the person can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the information for the specific decision. Cognitive screening informs but does not determine capacity.
key_points: Functional test: understand, retain, weigh, communicate
Decision-specific - capacity for surgery can co-exist with incapacity for finances
Time-specific - reassess if delirium, intoxication or medication effects change
Diagnosis alone (dementia, mental illness) does not establish incapacity
If incapable: enduring guardian > tribunal-appointed guardian > responsible person under state law > medical practitioner emergency provisions
explanation.pearls: Document capacity assessment in the patient's own words; that record is the strongest medico-legal protection (RACGP).

q.subtopic_detail: Complex elderly - delirium superimposed on dementia + capacity
q.stem: An 84-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia (premorbid MMSE 22 documented 6 weeks ago) is admitted from her home with a urinary tract infection and dehydration after her daughter found her drowsy. On day 4 of intravenous ceftriaxone the infection has clinically resolved with CRP falling from 156 to 24 mg/L, MSU now sterile, and sodium corrected from 122 to 137 mmol/L. Her attention this morning is back to baseline on backwards-counting, her CAM is negative, her behaviour has settled, and the family-nurse and OT report she is at the cognitive level documented before this admission. She lives alone with twice-weekly family visits and a recently increased home-care package. Today she tells the team she wants to go home and refuses to engage with proposed residential aged care assessment. Her daughter, who is the documented enduring guardian, disagrees and is pushing strongly for nursing home placement. The team is debating whether the patient can refuse the daughter's plan.

data_table.Examination: Alert, attention preserved on backwards-counting, MMSE 22 (back to documented baseline), CAM negative
data_table.Investigations: Na 137, urea 6.4, Cr 88, CRP 24, MSU sterile, FBC normal
data_table.Medications: Donepezil 10 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Override her refusal because the daughter is her enduring guardian
options.rationale: Enduring guardians make substitute decisions only when the person lacks capacity for the particular decision at the relevant time; if the patient currently has decision-specific capacity, the guardian cannot override her (Australian Law Reform Commission; state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission to keep her in hospital
options.rationale: The Mental Health Act addresses mental illness with risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; it does not authorise placement decisions for cognitive impairment, and there is no acute mental illness requiring detention here (state Mental Health Acts).

options.text: Discharge immediately because the delirium has resolved
options.rationale: Discharge requires assessment of safe destination, supports and (if capacity is in question) a documented capacity assessment; immediate discharge without that step is not in the patient's interest (RACGP; ACSQHC Delirium).

options.text: Continue antibiotics for another 7 days while debate continues
options.rationale: The infection has resolved; ongoing antibiotics expose her to harm without benefit and do not resolve the discharge question (eTG Antibiotic; AMS principles).

options.text: Reassess decision-specific capacity for the discharge destination
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. The delirium has now resolved (CAM negative, returned to baseline cognition); a fresh functional assessment for the specific decision of discharge destination is required before any substitute decision-maker is engaged or any application made to the Civil and Administrative Tribunal of the relevant state (ALRC; RACGP; state Guardianship Acts).

explanation.summary: Delirium-superimposed-on-dementia is the geriatric trap: once the delirium has resolved, capacity must be reassessed at the new baseline before any substitute decision-maker steps in. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific; an enduring guardian acts only when capacity is absent for the relevant decision. The least-restrictive principle applies throughout.
key_points: Delirium often unmasks underlying dementia but resolves to baseline once the precipitant is treated
Always reassess capacity at the new baseline before placement decisions
Enduring guardian acts only when capacity is absent for the decision in question
Risk of harm in the community is balanced against autonomy under the least-restrictive principle
If capacity is uncertain, the relevant state guardianship tribunal arbitrates
Document the capacity reasoning in the patient's own words
explanation.pearls: Family wishes do not override capable refusal - they are weighed and discussed, not enacted by default (Australian Law Reform Commission).

q.subtopic: Anaemia of chronic disease vs IDA discrimination
q.subtopic_detail: Ferritin interpretation in inflammation
q.stem: A 68-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 20 mg weekly and prednisolone 5 mg daily presents with worsening fatigue. She had a flare two months ago managed with a prednisolone pulse. She has no overt bleeding, no melaena, no menstruation since age 52 and a normal colonoscopy three years ago. She has no chronic kidney disease (eGFR 76) and no malignancy. Her appetite is preserved and BMI is 26 kg/m^2. She takes paracetamol regularly and avoids NSAIDs because of methotrexate. She has no overt source of blood loss. On examination she has active synovitis of MCPs and wrists with mild conjunctival pallor and no organomegaly. There is no telangiectasia and no hepatomegaly. She lives independently and is a retired nurse. She has had no recent transfusion. Her ESR is 78 mm/h and CRP 42 mg/L. The registrar wonders whether she has iron deficiency superimposed on chronic disease anaemia. She wants to know whether she should start oral iron.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 126/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Active synovitis MCPs/wrists, conjunctival pallor, no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 84 fL (normocytic), ferritin 142 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 12%, serum iron 6 micromol/L (RR 10-30), TIBC 38 micromol/L, CRP 42 mg/L, ESR 78, soluble transferrin receptor elevated, reticulocyte index low, B12 and folate normal.

q.lead_in: Which result pattern most strongly indicates that iron deficiency is contributing in addition to anaemia of chronic disease?

options.text: Normocytic indices with ferritin 142 micrograms/L in isolation
options.rationale: Ferritin 142 in isolation suggests adequate stores, but in active inflammation ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and can be falsely normal; this finding alone does NOT identify combined deficiency (BCSH IDA Guideline; eTG Haematology).

options.text: Low serum iron with normal TIBC and elevated ESR
options.rationale: Low serum iron with normal/reduced TIBC is the classic ACD pattern alone; this does not establish combined iron deficiency without additional markers (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Hb 98 g/L with active synovitis, suggesting any anaemia in this context must be iron deficiency
options.rationale: ACD alone can produce a normocytic anaemia at Hb 90-110 g/L; clinical inflammation is not specific for combined IDA without confirmatory iron studies (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Transferrin saturation 12% AND elevated soluble transferrin receptor
options.rationale: In active inflammation ferritin is unreliable; transferrin saturation under 20% with elevated soluble transferrin receptor (which is NOT acute-phase modified) reliably indicates true iron deficiency superimposed on ACD. This pattern justifies iron replacement, ideally parenteral given inflammation reduces oral absorption (BSG IDA 2021; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: MCV 84 fL and low reticulocyte index
options.rationale: Normocytic indices with hypoproliferative reticulocytes are characteristic of ACD itself; this pattern does not differentiate combined IDA from pure ACD (BCSH IDA Guideline 2021).

explanation.summary: In active inflammation, ferritin is acute-phase elevated and unreliable for diagnosing iron deficiency. Transferrin saturation under 20% with elevated soluble transferrin receptor (not affected by inflammation) is the most reliable combined-deficiency signature (BSG IDA 2021).
key_points: Ferritin <100 micrograms/L in active inflammation may still indicate iron deficiency
Transferrin saturation <20% supports iron deficiency
Soluble transferrin receptor is NOT an acute-phase reactant
Parenteral iron preferred when oral absorption is impaired by inflammation
explanation.pearls: In rheumatoid arthritis with CRP >10, use ferritin cut-off of 100 (not 30) before excluding iron deficiency.

q.subtopic_detail: 4Ts score and alternative anticoagulation
q.stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been receiving prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since post-op day 1 plus an unfractionated heparin infusion for 48 hours intraoperatively and post-op. He develops new right calf swelling, tenderness and a confirmed proximal DVT on ultrasound. His platelet count has fallen from 280 x10^9/L pre-op to 78 x10^9/L today, a nadir 72% drop. There is no bleeding. He has no skin necrosis at injection sites and no thrombocytopenia of other obvious cause - no sepsis, no DIC, no recent IV contrast. The blood film shows no schistocytes. He has not been septic. He has not received recent platelet transfusion. The cardiothoracic registrar calculates a 4Ts score of 7 (thrombocytopenia 2, timing 2, thrombosis 2, no other cause 1). The lab confirms PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is sent. He has no contraindication to alternative anticoagulants. He has CKD with eGFR 42. He is asking what should happen next.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.0 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Right calf swollen and tender, no skin necrosis, no bleeding, sternotomy wound clean.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 78 x10^9/L (pre-op 280), Hb 102 g/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, no schistocytes, PF4 antibody strongly positive ELISA, 4Ts 7 (high probability), eGFR 42.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Stop heparin and observe; restart heparin once platelets recover
options.rationale: Stopping alone leaves the patient in a prothrombotic state with high risk of new venous and arterial events; rechallenge with heparin is contraindicated (ASH 2018).

options.text: Continue enoxaparin and add aspirin
options.rationale: All heparins (including LMWH) must be stopped immediately when HIT is suspected at a high probability score; aspirin is inadequate (ASH 2018).

options.text: Start warfarin immediately to bridge while heparin is withdrawn
options.rationale: Warfarin must NOT be started in acute HIT until platelets recover above 150 because of risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis from protein C depletion (ASH 2018).

options.text: Transfuse platelets to correct thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: Prophylactic platelet transfusion is contraindicated in HIT unless major bleeding is occurring, because it can worsen thrombosis (ASH 2018; ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Stop all heparin AND start a non-heparin anticoagulant (e.g. fondaparinux or argatroban) at therapeutic dose
options.rationale: HIT is a prothrombotic, life-threatening condition. Stopping heparin alone is INSUFFICIENT - patients require therapeutic-dose non-heparin anticoagulation. Fondaparinux is acceptable off-label in stable patients; argatroban is preferred in renal/critically ill (ASH HIT Guideline 2018; eTG).

explanation.summary: HIT is a clinical-laboratory diagnosis with a high 4Ts score plus a positive PF4 antibody confirmed by functional assay. Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) AND substitute a non-heparin therapeutic anticoagulant; do not give warfarin or platelets in acute phase (ASH 2018).
key_points: 4Ts 0-3 low, 4-5 intermediate, 6-8 high probability
Stop all heparin AND substitute therapeutic non-heparin agent
Avoid warfarin in acute HIT until platelets > 150
Avoid prophylactic platelet transfusion
explanation.pearls: Timing nadir 5-10 days post heparin exposure is classic; rapid-onset HIT (<24 hours) occurs with recent prior exposure within 100 days.

sources.label: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

q.subtopic_detail: Mediastinal mass workup before steroids
q.stem: A 42-year-old previously well non-smoker presents to ED with one week of progressive facial swelling, neck swelling, dyspnoea and headache worse on bending forward. She has noticed dilated veins on her chest wall. She has had two episodes of pre-syncope in the last 24 hours. She has no haemoptysis or chest pain. On examination she has facial plethora, periorbital oedema, distended jugular veins to the angle of the jaw, dilated collateral veins on the anterior chest wall and a positive Pemberton sign. Stridor is absent at rest. There is no airway compromise currently but she is sitting forward in bed and saturating 95% on 2 L O2. CT chest with contrast shows a large anterior mediastinal mass compressing the SVC with extensive thrombus. She has not been previously diagnosed with malignancy. She has no fever or features of infection. She has no smoking history. Her FBC, U&Es, LFTs and LDH (elevated at 950) are pending tissue diagnosis. She has good IV access. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids. She is haemodynamically stable. She has no airway stridor at rest but the ED team are concerned about deterioration.

data_table.Examination: Facial plethora, periorbital oedema, distended JVPs, dilated chest wall collaterals, positive Pemberton sign, no stridor at rest.
data_table.Investigations: CT: large anterior mediastinal mass compressing SVC with thrombus; LDH 950; no tissue diagnosis yet.

q.lead_in: In the absence of airway compromise or critical neurological symptoms, which step is most important BEFORE starting empirical corticosteroids?

options.text: Start dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat regardless of biopsy timing
options.rationale: Empirical steroids before biopsy are appropriate only with airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms; otherwise they may obscure lymphoma histology (eviQ SVC).

options.text: Start mediastinal radiotherapy empirically before tissue diagnosis
options.rationale: Radiotherapy without histology obscures pathology, can render large areas uninterpretable, and the regimen depends on tumour type. Reserve for life-threatening compression with no other options (eviQ).

options.text: Obtain tissue diagnosis (core biopsy of accessible peripheral node or mediastinal biopsy)
options.rationale: In SVC obstruction without airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms, tissue diagnosis should be obtained BEFORE corticosteroids whenever possible because steroids can transiently shrink lymphoma and obscure histology, delaying definitive treatment by weeks.
