Anterior mediastinal mass + young non-smoker with high LDH = lymphoma until proven otherwise (eviQ SVC obstruction; BSH Lymphoma).

options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation alone for the SVC thrombus
options.rationale: Anticoagulation may be added for thrombus, but it does not address the underlying tumour; tissue diagnosis remains the priority (eviQ).

options.text: Endovascular SVC stenting as definitive management
options.rationale: SVC stenting provides rapid symptomatic relief and is appropriate for refractory or critical SVCO, but it should not delay tissue diagnosis when the patient is clinically stable; histology guides chemotherapy choice (eviQ).

explanation.summary: SVC obstruction in a previously undiagnosed patient: get tissue diagnosis FIRST unless airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms force empirical steroids. Anterior mediastinal mass in a young non-smoker is lymphoma until proven otherwise, and lymphoma is curable with histology-directed chemotherapy (eviQ).
key_points: Stable SVCO: tissue diagnosis first
Critical SVCO (airway, CNS): empirical steroids +/- stent
Anterior mediastinal mass DDx: lymphoma, thymoma, teratoma, thyroid
Stenting and anticoagulation for thrombus
explanation.pearls: Steroids before biopsy can obscure lymphoma histology for weeks - delay treatment, not diagnosis.

q.subtopic_detail: 1:1:1 ratio and metabolic complications
q.stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis is admitted to ED with massive haematemesis. He has bled approximately 2.5 L by the time he arrives and is in haemorrhagic shock. He has no known allergies. He is haemodynamically unstable - BP 78/42, HR 142, peripherally shut down, GCS 13. The ED activates the massive transfusion protocol. He has received 6 units of packed red cells, 4 units of FFP and 1 pool of platelets over the last 60 minutes via rapid infuser. He is being continued on the MTP. Repeat bloods show Hb 78 g/L (after transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10-1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62. ECG shows tall T waves in V2-V4. He has good central access. The blood bank is keeping pace. He has not received tranexamic acid yet within this episode. He has no known cardiac arrhythmia. He has no contraindication to calcium replacement. The bleeding source is being controlled endoscopically.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/42, HR 142, GCS 13, T 35.4 (mild hypothermia).
data_table.Examination: Cold peripheries, ongoing haematemesis, jaundice, ascites.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (post-transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1, K 6.2, ionised Ca 0.84, pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, BE -12, Mg 0.62; ECG tall T waves V2-V4.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most important next intervention to address an immediately life-threatening complication of massive transfusion?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL (or calcium chloride 10% 10 mL via central access)
options.rationale: Citrate in stored blood products chelates ionised calcium causing acute hypocalcaemia that depresses myocardial function and worsens coagulopathy. Ionised calcium 0.84 is severe and immediately life-threatening when combined with ongoing transfusion. Calcium replacement (gluconate peripherally or chloride centrally) is the single most important next intervention; it also helps stabilise myocardium against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP; NBA Patient Blood Management).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not correct the underlying lactic acidosis of hypoperfusion; the priority is restoring perfusion, replacing calcium and addressing hyperkalaemia. Bicarbonate also acutely lowers ionised calcium, worsening the hypocalcaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Insulin 10 units IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is reasonable for hyperkalaemia, but ionised calcium 0.84 with continuing massive transfusion is the more immediate life-threat because it impairs cardiac contractility and coagulation. Calcium also stabilises the membrane against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Stop the massive transfusion protocol pending haemodynamic improvement
options.rationale: He remains in haemorrhagic shock with ongoing bleeding; stopping MTP risks exsanguination. Address metabolic complications IN PARALLEL with continued resuscitation (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: TXA should be given (within 3 hours of bleeding onset) for major haemorrhage and is part of MTP, but it does not address the immediate cardiac risk of ionised calcium 0.84 plus hyperkalaemia; calcium correction is more urgent for this stem (ANZSBT MTP).

explanation.summary: Massive transfusion complications cluster as the 'lethal triad' (acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy) plus citrate-induced hypocalcaemia and hyperkalaemia from stored cells. Calcium replacement is the most overlooked but critical intervention; ratio 1:1:1 of RBC:FFP:platelets reduces coagulopathy (ANZSBT MTP; NBA PBM).
key_points: Ratio 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets
Replace calcium - citrate chelation is rapid and dangerous
Watch K+ from stored RBC; treat if symptomatic or >6.5
Add TXA (within 3 h) and target fibrinogen >1.5
explanation.pearls: Ionised calcium below 0.9 mmol/L is the silent killer of MTP - check and replace at every 4-unit interval.

q.subtopic: Infective endocarditis - Duke criteria and AV block
q.subtopic_detail: Empirical regimen and ECG monitoring for aortic root abscess
q.stem: A 62-year-old man with a bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement five years ago for severe aortic stenosis presents with three weeks of low-grade fever, malaise, anorexia and a 4 kg weight loss. He had a dental scaling six weeks ago without antibiotic prophylaxis. He has no penicillin allergy. On admission, T 38.2 degrees C, HR 92, BP 132/78. He has a soft early diastolic murmur not present at his last cardiology review. There are two small painful nodules on his left index finger pad and a single splinter haemorrhage. There is no embolic stroke on examination, no skin emboli to soles. Two of three blood cultures drawn 12 hours apart grow viridans group streptococci. A transthoracic echocardiogram suggests mild aortic regurgitation; transoesophageal echocardiography demonstrates a 10 mm mobile vegetation on the bioprosthetic valve and an early perivalvular abscess. ECG on admission shows PR 168 ms with normal QRS. He has commenced empirical IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly plus IV gentamicin per eTG. Renal function is normal (creatinine 78 micromol/L). He is haemodynamically stable. Cardiac surgery has reviewed and plans urgent valve replacement within 72 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2, HR 92, BP 132/78, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Early diastolic murmur (new), Osler nodes left index finger, splinter haemorrhage. No focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: TTE mild AR. TOE: 10 mm vegetation + perivalvular abscess. Cultures: viridans strep x 2 of 3. ECG PR 168 ms.
data_table.Medications: Benzylpenicillin 1.8 g IV 4-hourly + gentamicin per eTG. No allergies.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate additional monitoring step over the next 72 hours?

options.text: Weekly CT chest with contrast to screen for septic pulmonary or systemic emboli
options.rationale: Imaging for septic emboli is driven by symptoms, not weekly routine; this does not address the conduction-system risk in aortic root abscess (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Single daily 12-lead ECG on the ward round, no continuous telemetry monitoring required
options.rationale: Daily ECG can miss intermittent block evolving over hours. Continuous telemetry detects evolving heart block in aortic root abscess (BMJ Best Practice: Infective endocarditis).

options.text: Twice-weekly serum vancomycin trough levels for therapeutic drug monitoring
options.rationale: He is on penicillin and gentamicin, not vancomycin; gentamicin troughs are appropriate but do not screen for conduction-system extension (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Daily transthoracic echocardiography to track vegetation size and perivalvular extension
options.rationale: Serial TTE has lower sensitivity than TOE for perivalvular extension and is not the recommended monitoring strategy; surveillance is by clinical examination, telemetry and repeat TOE if features change (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Continuous ECG / telemetry to detect progressive PR prolongation or AV block
options.rationale: Aortic root abscess extends into the conduction system and progressive PR prolongation or new AV block predicts perivalvular extension and need for urgent surgery. Telemetry is mandatory until source is controlled (BMJ Best Practice: Infective endocarditis; eTG).

explanation.summary: Aortic-valve endocarditis with perivalvular abscess can extend into the AV node, producing progressive PR prolongation or complete heart block. Continuous telemetry detects this earlier than daily ECGs and informs urgent surgical timing (BMJ Best Practice; eTG).
key_points: Modified Duke: definite IE = 2 major OR 1 major + 3 minor
Perivalvular extension manifests as new AV block
Continuous telemetry until source control
Surgery indications include heart failure, abscess, persistent bacteraemia
explanation.pearls: Viridans strep prosthetic valve endocarditis needs penicillin + gentamicin per eTG and early cardiac surgery referral.

q.subtopic_detail: Sapporo criteria and warfarin target INR for venous thrombosis
q.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a first unprovoked left popliteal DVT confirmed on Doppler ultrasound. She has had two prior first-trimester miscarriages and one mid-trimester foetal death, all with no anatomical or chromosomal cause identified. Her sister has SLE. She is on no regular medications, smokes 5 cigarettes per day, drinks no alcohol, and uses copper IUD contraception. She has no other thrombotic risk factors and no recent travel or immobilisation. On examination she is well, BP 124/76, HR 78, with the tender swollen left calf, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods sent: lupus anticoagulant positive on dilute Russell viper venom time at presentation and again 12 weeks later, anti-cardiolipin IgG strongly positive at both timepoints, anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I IgG positive at both timepoints. FBC normal, UEC normal, LFTs normal, ANA positive at 1:160 with anti-dsDNA negative and complements normal. APTT mildly prolonged that does not correct on 1:1 mixing. She has been commenced on therapeutic LMWH and a long-term anticoagulation plan is being formulated, with a working diagnosis of primary antiphospholipid syndrome.

data_table.Examination: Left calf swollen, tender, warm. Otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: DVT on US. LA positive x2 (12 weeks apart), aCL IgG strong, anti-B2GPI IgG positive x2. APTT prolonged, does not correct on mix.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: DOACs (especially rivaroxaban; less robust evidence with apixaban) are associated with higher recurrence rates than warfarin in APS, particularly triple-positive APS, and are not recommended (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR target 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely
options.rationale: In triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome with venous thromboembolism, warfarin with target INR 2.0 to 3.0 is the recommended long-term anticoagulant. DOACs are inferior in APS, particularly triple-positive disease (EULAR APS 2019; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily monotherapy for life
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient for secondary prevention after VTE in APS; full-intensity anticoagulation with warfarin is required (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR target 3.0 to 4.0
options.rationale: Higher-intensity warfarin (INR 3-4) does not reduce thrombotic recurrence over standard intensity (INR 2-3) in venous APS, while increasing bleeding risk; standard intensity is preferred for venous events (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Three months of LMWH then cessation of anticoagulation
options.rationale: Unprovoked VTE with triple-positive APS carries a high recurrence rate; indefinite anticoagulation is recommended unless contraindicated (EULAR APS 2019).

explanation.summary: Triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome with venous thromboembolism is treated long-term with warfarin targeted to INR 2.0-3.0. DOACs are inferior, particularly in triple-positive APS. Sapporo criteria require persistent (12-week) positivity of LA, anti-cardiolipin or anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I antibodies plus a clinical thrombotic or obstetric event (EULAR APS 2019).
key_points: Sapporo: clinical event + persistent antibody at 12 weeks
Venous APS target INR 2.0-3.0 on warfarin
DOACs inferior, especially triple-positive
Arterial APS: warfarin +/- low-dose aspirin
explanation.pearls: Suspect APS in unprovoked VTE in a young person, recurrent miscarriage or stroke under 50.

q.subtopic: Gout vs septic arthritis - joint aspiration
q.subtopic_detail: Acute monoarthritis discrimination and intra-articular steroid timing
q.stem: A 64-year-old man presents with a 24 hour history of an exquisitely painful, hot, swollen right knee. He has had three prior episodes of acute right first metatarsophalangeal joint pain in the past 4 years, each lasting 5-7 days and self-managed with NSAIDs. He is on hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension, has chronic kidney disease (eGFR 38), and a history of duodenal ulcer 2 years ago on proton pump inhibitor maintenance. He has no fever today but reports brief rigors 12 hours ago. He has no recent skin breaks, no IV drug use, no overseas travel and no immunosuppression. He has no penicillin allergy. On examination, T 37.8, HR 92, BP 142/86. The right knee is held in slight flexion, with a tense effusion, marked warmth and erythema spreading 4 cm beyond the joint line. Active and passive movement is severely restricted by pain. There are no other joints involved. Bloods: WCC 12.4, CRP 142, urate 0.52 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L, eGFR 38. Joint aspiration is performed: cloudy yellow fluid, leukocyte count 28 000 cells/microL with 85 percent neutrophils, Gram stain negative, polarised microscopy reveals negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Cultures are pending.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8, HR 92, BP 142/86, SpO2 99 percent.
data_table.Examination: Right knee tense effusion, hot, erythema beyond joint, ROM severely limited.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 12.4, CRP 142, urate 0.52, creatinine 168, eGFR 38. Aspirate WCC 28 000, 85 percent neutrophils, negatively birefringent needle crystals, Gram stain negative.

q.lead_in: What is the single best acute pharmacological management while awaiting cultures?

options.text: Empirical IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly without diagnostic joint aspiration or microbiology samples
options.rationale: Joint aspirate already shows crystals with negative Gram stain and the clinical picture fits gout; empirical antibiotics await culture rather than treating without indication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral indomethacin 50 mg three times daily for 5 days with a proton pump inhibitor for gastroprotection
options.rationale: NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated with eGFR 38 and a prior duodenal ulcer; indomethacin in particular has high renal and GI risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Allopurinol 300 mg orally daily started immediately during the acute flare to lower the serum urate
options.rationale: Urate-lowering therapy is not started during an acute flare unless the patient is already established on it; initiating allopurinol acutely can prolong the flare. It is started after the flare settles, with concurrent prophylactic colchicine cover (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Intra-articular methylprednisolone 40 mg plus oral colchicine 1 mg then 500 micrograms 1 hour later
options.rationale: Negatively birefringent needle crystals plus prior podagra confirm gout. With eGFR 38 and recent peptic ulcer, NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated. eTG and BMJ Best Practice support intra-articular steroid in monoarticular gout with low-dose colchicine (cap 1.5 mg total in 24 hours per eTG, with dose reduction for renal impairment). The negative Gram stain supports gout, but await culture before fully excluding co-existing septic arthritis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology; BMJ Best Practice: Gout).

options.text: Oral colchicine 1 mg six times daily continued until profuse diarrhoea develops as a dosing endpoint
options.rationale: Old high-dose colchicine regimens are discarded because of toxicity; current dosing caps at 1.5 mg in the first 24 hours, with renal dose-adjustment. Provoking diarrhoea is not a dosing endpoint (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

explanation.summary: Crystal-confirmed acute monoarticular gout with NSAID and steroid trade-offs is best managed with intra-articular corticosteroid plus low-dose colchicine (1 mg then 500 micrograms an hour later, then 500 micrograms 12-hourly until settling, with renal dose-adjustment). Always send joint aspirate for culture - co-existing septic arthritis is rare but can be missed (eTG; BMJ Best Practice).
key_points: Gout = negatively birefringent needle crystals
Pseudogout = positively birefringent rhomboid crystals (CPPD)
Acute therapy: intra-articular steroid OR colchicine OR NSAID
Avoid NSAID if eGFR < 60, ulcer disease, heart failure, anticoagulated
explanation.pearls: If Gram stain or culture suggests sepsis, treat as septic arthritis regardless of crystals - they can coexist.

q.subtopic_detail: Sequencing phosphate binder vs activated vitamin D vs cinacalcet
q.stem: A 58-year-old man with CKD stage 5 not yet on dialysis (eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m^2 due to ADPKD) is reviewed in nephrology clinic. He is asymptomatic of bone pain or pruritus but has cramping. He follows a low-phosphate diet with the renal dietitian. Medications: amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, sodium bicarbonate 840 mg TDS, cholecalciferol 1000 IU. He is not yet anuric. Bloods at today's visit (consistent with the trend over the last 3 months): corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L, phosphate 1.95 mmol/L (rising from 1.5 three months ago), PTH 78 pmol/L (rising from 45), 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L. He is engaged in dialysis education and a fistula has been formed. He is being worked up for kidney transplantation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm.
data_table.Examination: No bone tenderness; mature radiocephalic AV fistula on left forearm with good thrill.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 2.18 mmol/L, phosphate 1.95 mmol/L (rising), PTH 78 pmol/L (rising), 25-OH vit D 78 nmol/L, ALP 142 U/L, HCO3 22, eGFR 12.
data_table.Medications: Amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, NaHCO3 840 mg TDS, cholecalciferol 1000 IU.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in managing his CKD-MBD?

options.text: Commence oral calcium carbonate 600 mg with each meal as a phosphate binder
options.rationale: Calcium-containing binders add to overall calcium load and are associated with greater progression of vascular calcification in CKD; KDIGO 2017 recommends restricting their use, particularly when other binders are available (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Start oral calcitriol 0.25 microgram daily to suppress the rising PTH
options.rationale: Activated vitamin D analogues raise both calcium and phosphate via gut absorption; starting calcitriol with phosphate 1.95 mmol/L will worsen hyperphosphataemia and accelerate vascular calcification. Phosphate control must come first (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Commence oral cinacalcet 30 mg daily as a calcimimetic
options.rationale: Cinacalcet (a calcimimetic) is PBS-restricted to dialysis patients in Australia and lowers calcium; with corrected calcium already 2.18 mmol/L hypocalcaemia is a risk. It is not first-line in non-dialysis CKD (Source: PBS - cinacalcet; AMH).

options.text: Add a non-calcium phosphate binder (sevelamer) and reinforce dietary phosphate restriction
options.rationale: KDIGO and KHA-CARI advise lowering phosphate towards the normal range as the first lever in CKD 5 with rising phosphate and PTH. A non-calcium binder is preferred over calcium-based binders because of vascular calcification risk, particularly with a calcium near the lower limit (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017 Update; KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

options.text: Refer urgently for subtotal parathyroidectomy given the rising PTH
options.rationale: Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe medically refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism, typically PTH persistently above approximately 88 pmol/L (800 pg/mL) despite optimal medical therapy with hypercalcaemia/hyperphosphataemia or symptomatic disease; medical therapy has not yet been escalated (Source: KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

explanation.summary: In CKD-MBD, control phosphate first using diet plus a non-calcium binder, then address PTH with activated vitamin D once phosphate is acceptable. Avoid loading calcium and avoid activated vitamin D while phosphate is high (Source: KDIGO 2017).
key_points: Phosphate first, PTH second in CKD-MBD
Non-calcium binders preferred (sevelamer, lanthanum)
Activated vitamin D raises phosphate - sequence after phosphate control
Cinacalcet restricted to dialysis in Australia (PBS)
explanation.pearls: Rising PTH with rising phosphate = give a binder before reaching for calcitriol.

q.subtopic_detail: Discriminating TTP, HUS, aHUS
q.stem: A 38-year-old woman is admitted with three days of fatigue, easy bruising, and confusion. She had a vague URTI two weeks ago, denies diarrhoea, and is not pregnant (LMP one week ago). Medications: nil regular. No new drugs or supplements. Examination: T 37.8, BP 146/90, HR 104, GCS 14 (oriented to person, intermittent disorientation to place), petechiae on shins, mild jaundice, no lymphadenopathy. Bloods: Hb 78 g/L with schistocytes on blood film, platelets 18 x10^9/L, LDH 1820 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, indirect bilirubin elevated, DCT negative, INR 1.0, APTT normal, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (was 70), urea 12, troponin mildly elevated, urinalysis blood 1+ protein 1+. Stool culture for Shiga toxin-producing E. coli has been sent but is not yet back. ADAMTS13 activity has been sent. She has no history of malignancy, no recent transplant or chemotherapy.

data_table.Examination: GCS 14, intermittent disorientation, petechiae, mild jaundice.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 + schistocytes, plt 18, LDH 1820, haptoglobin undetectable, DCT negative, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 3.2, Cr 142 (was 70), troponin mildly raised.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before ADAMTS13 results return?

options.text: Await stool Shiga toxin PCR and ADAMTS13 activity before any TMA-specific therapy
options.rationale: Withholding treatment for TMA pending results is unsafe; untreated TTP carries > 90% mortality. Empirical plasma exchange is the standard of care when ADAMTS13 deficiency is clinically likely (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of platelets to raise the platelet count above 50 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in suspected TTP can precipitate further microvascular thrombosis and is contraindicated unless there is life-threatening bleeding or a procedure is required (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Start eculizumab as the first-line therapy for presumed complement-mediated TMA
options.rationale: Eculizumab is the treatment of choice for atypical HUS (complement-mediated), suggested by predominant renal injury without severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. The clinical picture here is TTP-predominant; eculizumab is considered only if ADAMTS13 is preserved and aHUS is diagnosed (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Commence urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and high-dose corticosteroids for empirical TTP
options.rationale: MAHA + severe thrombocytopenia + neurological involvement + cardiac involvement + only modest renal dysfunction is the textbook TTP phenotype. ADAMTS13 results take time and TTP has > 90% untreated mortality, so empirical plasma exchange and steroids start immediately on clinical suspicion (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020; BSH 2023).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis-related disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.rationale: DIC is unlikely with a normal INR/APTT and normal fibrinogen; the picture is non-immune MAHA + thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation, which is the haematological signature of TMA, not DIC (Source: ISTH DIC vs TMA review).

explanation.summary: TMA = MAHA + thrombocytopenia + organ injury. TTP: neuro/cardiac dominant, mild renal, ADAMTS13 < 10%, plasma exchange empirically. Typical HUS: diarrhoeal Shiga toxin (children especially). aHUS: renal-predominant, complement-mediated, treat with eculizumab (Source: KDIGO 2021).
key_points: PLASMIC score helps stratify TTP probability while awaiting ADAMTS13
Avoid platelet transfusion in suspected TTP
DIC ruled out by normal INR/APTT and fibrinogen
Drug-induced TMA (quinine, calcineurin inhibitors, gemcitabine) - withdraw the drug
explanation.pearls: Neuro signs + cardiac troponin + relatively preserved kidneys = think TTP, start PLEX.

q.subtopic_detail: Rate of correction and osmotic demyelination prevention
q.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of harmful alcohol use is brought to ED after being found drowsy. She has eaten poorly for two weeks. Examination: GCS 11, BP 110/70, HR 96, RR 14, T 36.5, weight 58 kg, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology, no asterixis. Bloods on arrival: Na 108 mmol/L, K 3.0, urea 2.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.2, serum osmolality 232 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 460 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 38 mmol/L, TSH normal, random cortisol normal. She is not on diuretics; her only regular medication is thiamine (irregularly taken). She has had one self-limited generalised tonic-clonic seizure since arrival. No history of CCF, cirrhosis or recent surgery; no SSRI, no carbamazepine.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 11, BP 110/70, HR 96, T 36.5, weight 58 kg.
data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes moist, no oedema, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 108, K 3.0, urea 2.4, glucose 4.2, serum osm 232, urine osm 460, urine Na 38, TSH and cortisol normal.

q.lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV hypertonic saline (3%) 150 mL boluses, capping the 24-hour sodium rise at 8-10 mmol/L
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, GCS 11) warrants rapid initial correction with 100-150 mL hypertonic saline boluses to relieve cerebral oedema, but the 24-hour rise must be capped at 8-10 mmol/L (and 18 mmol/L over 48 h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. This is doubly important here given malnutrition and harmful alcohol use, which are additional ODS risk factors (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014; ANZICS / Australian intensivist statements).

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 250 mL/h with hourly sodium measurement
options.rationale: Isotonic saline is too slow to address acute cerebral oedema with seizures and may paradoxically worsen hyponatraemia in SIADH (de-saltation effect). Hypertonic saline is the agent of choice for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Free water restriction to 800 mL/day as the sole acute intervention
options.rationale: Water restriction is appropriate for asymptomatic chronic SIADH but is inadequate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure; rapid initial correction with hypertonic saline is needed first (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally to block V2 receptors and aquarese free water
options.rationale: Vaptans risk over-rapid correction and ODS, especially in alcoholic malnourished patients; they are not recommended in severe symptomatic hyponatraemia at presentation (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Allow spontaneous correction by treating the underlying cause aggressively over 24 hours
options.rationale: Uncontrolled correction is the precise mechanism that produces ODS; an explicit cap on the 24-hour rise (8-10 mmol/L) is mandatory in high-risk patients (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: hypertonic saline boluses to gain 4-6 mmol/L quickly for symptom relief, then cap the 24-h rise at 8-10 mmol/L (10-12 in low-risk, but lower in alcohol/malnutrition/liver disease) and 18 mmol/L over 48 h. If over-correction, re-lower with 5% dextrose and consider desmopressin (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).
key_points: Symptom severity (seizure, GCS drop) drives use of hypertonic saline, not absolute Na
ODS risk highest if Na < 105, alcohol, malnutrition, hypokalaemia, liver disease
Replace K (low here) carefully - K replacement raises Na as well
Reverse over-correction with 5% dextrose +/- desmopressin
explanation.pearls: Replace K - it counts as a corrected osmole and contributes to over-rapid Na rise.

q.subtopic_detail: Mixed disorder with delta-delta in DKA
q.stem: A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is brought to ED with vomiting for two days and reduced consciousness. She admits to skipping her basal insulin. Examination: GCS 13, BP 102/64, HR 124, RR 34 with deep Kussmaul breathing, mucous membranes very dry, ketotic breath, abdomen mildly tender. Bloods: Na 132 mmol/L, K 5.2 mmol/L, Cl 88 mmol/L, HCO3 6 mmol/L, urea 14, creatinine 124 micromol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 7.8 mmol/L. ABG: pH 7.06, pCO2 16 mmHg, pO2 110 mmHg on 2 L/min O2, HCO3 6 mmol/L, BE -22, lactate 1.8. Albumin 36 g/L. She has no history of salicylate use, no ingestion of methanol or ethylene glycol, and no toxidromic features.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 13, BP 102/64, HR 124, RR 34 (Kussmaul).
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, ketotic breath.
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 5.2, Cl 88, HCO3 6, glucose 32, beta-OHB 7.8, pH 7.06, pCO2 16, lactate 1.8, alb 36.

q.lead_in: Which is the most accurate description of her acid-base disorder?

options.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and concurrent metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 132 - (88 + 6) = 38. Delta anion gap = 38 - 12 = 26. Delta HCO3 = 24 - 6 = 18. Delta-delta = 26/18 = approximately 1.4 - this is just above 1, but in DKA delta ratios commonly drop towards 1 when sodium loss/dilution accompanies ketoacidosis. With sustained vomiting adding bicarbonate-rich gastric losses (proton loss), the bicarbonate is being pulled upward by a concurrent metabolic alkalosis. Winters' formula predicts expected pCO2 = (1.5 x 6) + 8 = 17 mmHg; measured 16 is within +/- 2 so respiratory compensation is appropriate (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base; UpToDate Acid-base).

options.text: Pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate Kussmaul respiratory compensation
options.rationale: If the disorder were pure HAGMA, the fall in bicarbonate should equal the rise in anion gap (delta ratio approximately 1). Two days of vomiting would predict a delta ratio > 1 because gastric H+ losses tend to elevate bicarbonate relative to the gap rise (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Mixed high anion gap and normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (delta ratio < 1)
options.rationale: A delta ratio below 1 implies an additional non-anion-gap acidosis (e.g. diarrhoea, RTA); the calculated delta ratio here is above 1, not below 1 (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Primary respiratory alkalosis with secondary metabolic compensation through bicarbonate loss
options.rationale: The primary disorder is metabolic with a pH of 7.06; the low pCO2 of 16 is compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul) for the acidosis, not a primary respiratory alkalosis (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with a primary respiratory alkalosis overlay
options.rationale: The anion gap is 38, well above the normal range (8-12), so this is a high anion gap acidosis. NAGMA with respiratory alkalosis is the wrong characterisation (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

explanation.summary: Stepwise acid-base: pH then primary disorder, then anion gap, then delta-delta to detect mixed disorders, then check Winters' for respiratory compensation. Albumin-correct the gap if low (add 2.5 per 10 g/L below 40) (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base).
key_points: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3); normal 8-12 in most labs
Delta ratio < 1: HAGMA + NAGMA; 1-2: pure HAGMA; > 2: HAGMA + metabolic alkalosis
Winters' formula: expected pCO2 = 1.5 x HCO3 + 8 (+/- 2)
Common DKA mixed pictures: vomiting (alkalosis), pre-existing CKD (NAGMA), sepsis (lactic)
explanation.pearls: Always do delta-delta in DKA - the bedside picture of 'just DKA' often hides another driver.

q.subtopic_detail: Threshold for switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons
q.stem: A 41-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia has been on olanzapine 20 mg nocte for 4 years with partial symptomatic response (residual negative symptoms, no positive symptoms). She lives independently with case-management support and attends a community mental health clinic three-monthly. Over the past 3 years her weight has risen from 76 kg to 102 kg (BMI 36.1 kg/m2), waist 112 cm. Today's review shows BP 142/92 mmHg (confirmed on repeat 5 minutes apart), fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L on two separate occasions, HbA1c 58 mmol/mol (7.5%), LDL 4.1 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.4 mmol/L. She has type 2 diabetes diagnosed 8 months ago (on metformin 1 g BD), and stage 1 hypertension (on perindopril 5 mg). She has had three trials of structured lifestyle intervention with no sustained weight loss across the past two years. She has never been on clozapine and has not had treatment-resistance formally assessed. Her psychiatrist is considering switching antipsychotic. Mental state today is stable with no current psychotic features. She prefers oral medication and has a documented history of akathisia on haloperidol but tolerated a brief aripiprazole augmentation trial 3 years ago without akathisia or activation.

data_table.Comorbidities: T2DM on metformin 1 g BD; HTN on perindopril 5 mg.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next antipsychotic management step?

options.text: Continue olanzapine 20 mg and add topiramate for weight loss
options.rationale: Topiramate has modest evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain and is third-line after lifestyle and metformin; with established type 2 diabetes, frank metabolic syndrome, and a high-burden antipsychotic still onboard, the priority is to switch to a lower-burden agent rather than add a third adjunct (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Halve the olanzapine dose to 10 mg nocte, continue current diabetes and blood pressure therapy unchanged, and review weight, fasting glucose, lipids, and BP again in 3 months with no other modifications
options.rationale: Dose reduction has only weak effect on weight in established olanzapine-induced metabolic syndrome, and risks symptomatic relapse without resolving the underlying metabolic liability of the agent (AMH; RANZCP).

options.text: Switch to chlorpromazine 200 mg BD
options.rationale: Chlorpromazine is also high-burden for weight gain, sedation, and anticholinergic load, and adds postural hypotension on top of her antihypertensive; it is not a metabolically favourable switch (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole (or lurasidone), maintain metformin, continue diabetes and BP management, monitor closely for relapse
options.rationale: She has overt metabolic syndrome with T2DM (HbA1c 7.5%) and >7% sustained weight gain on olanzapine. RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic recommend switching to a metabolically lower-burden agent (aripiprazole or lurasidone, both PBS-listed) via cross-taper, with continued antidiabetic and antihypertensive therapy and close monitoring for relapse; she previously tolerated aripiprazole.

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery and continue olanzapine 20 mg
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery may have a role in selected cases but is not first-line ahead of switching to a metabolically favourable antipsychotic; continuing the offending agent unchanged is not appropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

explanation.summary: The trigger for switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons is sustained weight gain >7%, new or worsening dysglycaemia or dyslipidaemia, or established metabolic syndrome despite lifestyle and pharmacological adjuncts (RANZCP).
key_points: Switch agents: aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone, brexpiprazole have lowest metabolic burden
Cross-taper rather than abrupt switch to limit relapse risk
Continue diabetes and BP management through the switch
Reassess metabolic profile 6-12 weeks after switch
explanation.pearls: Document the metabolic rationale clearly - it supports later prescribing decisions and audit (RANZCP).

q.subtopic_detail: Citalopram QT cap and discriminating serotonin syndrome from neuroleptic malignant syndrome
q.stem: A 69-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife found him agitated, sweaty, and shaking in the lounge room of their home. Over the past 24 hours he has become progressively tremulous, restless, and confused, with deteriorating mental state through the morning. His usual medications include citalopram (recently up-titrated 5 days ago from 20 mg to 40 mg daily for persistent depressive symptoms despite a previously adequate response), risperidone 2 mg nocte (commenced 6 weeks ago for new psychotic features overlaid on depression), perindopril 5 mg daily for hypertension, and tramadol 50 mg up to four times daily started 4 days ago by an after-hours general practitioner for an acute lower back strain. He had a small ischaemic stroke 3 years ago without residual neurological deficit and remains independent in all activities of daily living at baseline. On arrival he is restless and agitated, GCS 13, temperature 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 124 sinus, BP 158/96, RR 22, SpO2 97% on room air. Examination shows mydriasis, profuse diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, tremor more pronounced in the lower limbs than the upper limbs, spontaneous sustained inducible clonus in both ankles (right > left), ocular clonus on lateral gaze, and globally brisk reflexes. He is NOT rigid - tone is normal with no lead-pipe rigidity and no cogwheeling. ECG: sinus tachycardia, QTc 502 ms (baseline 6 months ago 432 ms). Bloods: Na 138, K 4.2, creatinine 86, CK 412 U/L, lactate 2.6, paracetamol level negative. He has no recent dose changes to risperidone and has not been febrile or unwell at home in the days leading up to this presentation.

data_table.Medications: Citalopram 40 mg (up from 20 mg, 5 days ago), risperidone 2 mg (stable 6 weeks), perindopril 5 mg, tramadol 50 mg QID (4 days).
data_table.Examination: Diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, lower-limb-predominant tremor, sustained inducible ankle clonus (R>L), ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, NO rigidity.
data_table.Bloods/ECG: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 86, CK 412, lactate 2.6, QTc 502 ms (baseline 432).

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis and immediate management combination?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from citalopram (above the over-65 dose cap of 20 mg) plus tramadol; stop citalopram and tramadol immediately, give benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus, supportive cooling and IV fluids, consult NSW Poisons Information Centre, monitor QT and CK, and consider cyproheptadine in refractory cases
options.rationale: His features (clonus, especially inducible and spontaneous lower-limb ankle clonus, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds, diaphoresis, fever, lower-limb predominance, NO rigidity) meet Hunter criteria for serotonin syndrome. The trigger is the 40 mg citalopram dose (which exceeds the TGA over-65 cap of 20 mg due to dose-dependent QT prolongation and is reflected here in QTc 502 ms) combined with newly added tramadol (serotonergic). Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic, the AMH, and the NSW Poisons Information Centre recommend stopping serotonergic agents, benzodiazepine sedation, cooling, IV fluids, ECG/CK monitoring, and cyproheptadine for refractory cases.
source_refs: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome triggered by risperidone (commenced 6 weeks ago) is the diagnosis; stop risperidone immediately, give intravenous dantrolene and oral bromocriptine as specific antidotes, provide aggressive supportive care with active cooling and IV fluids, and continue citalopram and tramadol since the toxidrome is dopamine-blockade-mediated rather than serotonergic in origin
options.rationale: NMS classically presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and very high CK (often >1000 U/L), with a slower onset; he has no rigidity, modest CK rise, lower-limb-predominant tremor with clonus, and the timing is locked to citalopram up-titration and tramadol introduction. The picture is serotonin syndrome (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome

options.text: Sepsis with delirium; broad-spectrum antibiotics and intravenous fluids only
options.rationale: Lactate is mildly raised and there is no clear source; the focal clonus and ocular clonus point strongly to serotonin syndrome rather than sepsis. Missing the serotonergic toxidrome risks ongoing exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from tramadol; give physostigmine
options.rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome features dry skin and dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, and absent bowel sounds; he is diaphoretic with hyperactive bowel sounds, which excludes anticholinergic syndrome (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

options.text: Acute ischaemic stroke recurrence; thrombolyse within the window
options.rationale: There are no focal neurological deficits; the symmetric clonus, fever, and autonomic features are not consistent with stroke. Thrombolysis would be wholly inappropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

explanation.summary: Two key Australian safety items combine here: the TGA citalopram dose cap of 20 mg in adults over 65 (driven by dose-dependent QT prolongation), and the serotonin syndrome triad of mental status change, neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), and autonomic hyperactivity (TGA; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).
key_points: Citalopram max in adults over 65: 20 mg daily (TGA QT advisory)
Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially ocular and inducible/lower-limb), hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds; NO rigidity
NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, very high CK; slower onset; antipsychotic trigger
Tramadol is a frequent co-trigger via serotonergic activity
Management: stop offenders, benzodiazepines, cooling, IV fluids, NSW Poisons Information Centre consult, cyproheptadine if refractory
explanation.pearls: Lower-limb-predominant clonus with normal tone is the textbook serotonin-syndrome physical sign; lead-pipe rigidity points the other way to NMS (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome

q.subtopic_detail: Refeeding syndrome electrolyte management
q.stem: A 19-year-old woman with a 3-year history of severe restrictive anorexia nervosa is admitted to a metropolitan tertiary hospital with BMI 13.4 after several weeks of minimal oral intake (estimated <500 kcal per day for at least 14 days). Admission electrolytes showed potassium 3.4 mmol/L, phosphate 0.81 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, and corrected calcium 2.32 mmol/L. The team commenced supervised oral refeeding at 1200 kcal/day with prophylactic oral thiamine 300 mg daily, oral phosphate supplements, and a multivitamin per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit refeeding pathway. After 72 hours of advancing intake to 1800 kcal/day, she develops new bilateral lower limb pitting oedema extending to the mid-calf, generalised fatigue, and mild dyspnoea on exertion. She has no chest pain and no orthopnoea. Repeat bloods show potassium 2.8 mmol/L, phosphate 0.38 mmol/L, magnesium 0.58 mmol/L, sodium 136 mmol/L, urea 2.1 mmol/L, creatinine 48 micromol/L, glucose 5.9 mmol/L, and corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L. Her observations are HR 96 regular, BP 108/68 mmHg, oxygen saturation 96 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, and a clear chest on auscultation. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia and flattened T waves without arrhythmia. She remains alert, oriented, and engaged with the treating team.

data_table.Bloods (day 3): K 2.8 mmol/L, PO4 0.38 mmol/L, Mg 0.58 mmol/L, Na 136, urea 2.1, creatinine 48, corrected Ca 2.18, glucose 5.9
data_table.Examination: Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-calf, mild exertional dyspnoea, clear chest

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Increase caloric intake to 2200 kcal/day to keep up with rising energy demands and continue oral phosphate supplementation alone without intravenous electrolyte replacement
options.rationale: Advancing calories during refeeding syndrome worsens the electrolyte shifts; the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit and MARSIPAN-AU recommend halting advancement (and often dropping back) until phosphate, potassium, and magnesium are corrected.

options.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg orally daily for the lower limb oedema and continue caloric advancement as planned
options.rationale: Refeeding oedema is driven by insulin-mediated sodium and water retention and capillary leak; loop diuretics worsen hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia and are contraindicated per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit, which recommends conservative management with leg elevation and time.

options.text: Hold caloric advancement, replace intravenous phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, and continue thiamine; treat refeeding oedema conservatively
options.rationale: This is refeeding syndrome (the classic triad of hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia after starting nutrition in a severely malnourished patient). The NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit and MARSIPAN-AU recommend holding caloric advancement, intravenous replacement of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, continuing thiamine 300 mg daily to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and managing refeeding oedema conservatively with leg elevation since diuretics worsen electrolyte loss.

options.text: Cease all oral intake for 48 hours to allow electrolytes to normalise, then restart at 1200 kcal/day
options.rationale: Ceasing nutrition prolongs starvation and worsens prognosis; MARSIPAN-AU advises continuing nutrition (often dropping calories modestly rather than ceasing) while aggressively replacing electrolytes.

options.text: Transfuse 4 units of 20 percent albumin to address the lower limb oedema
options.rationale: Refeeding oedema is not driven by hypoalbuminaemia and resolves with time and electrolyte correction; albumin transfusion is not indicated and carries volume overload risk per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit.

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome features hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia driven by insulin-mediated intracellular electrolyte shifts when carbohydrate is reintroduced. Management is to hold caloric advancement, replace electrolytes intravenously, and continue thiamine (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).
key_points: Highest risk: BMI <14, >10% weight loss in 3-6 months, minimal intake >5 days
Replace phosphate, potassium, magnesium IV; continue thiamine 300 mg/day
Refeeding oedema is conservative-management (no diuretics)
Wernicke encephalopathy prevention with thiamine is mandatory
explanation.pearls: Phosphate falls because intracellular ATP synthesis surges with carbohydrate refeeding (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).

q.subtopic_detail: Lithium and Ebstein anomaly - pregnancy planning in bipolar disorder
q.stem: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable for 4 years on lithium carbonate 900 mg orally nightly with steady-state 12-hour trough level 0.8 mmol/L, presents to her psychiatrist in metropolitan Adelaide because she and her male partner are planning a pregnancy within the next 6 months. She has had three previous severe manic episodes (two requiring involuntary inpatient admission with psychotic features) and one severe depressive episode with active suicidal ideation requiring electroconvulsive therapy. Trials of sodium valproate (discontinued due to substantial weight gain of 14 kg over 6 months), olanzapine (discontinued due to metabolic syndrome with HbA1c rising to 50 mmol/mol), and quetiapine (discontinued due to inadequate prophylaxis with breakthrough mania despite a dose of 600 mg daily) have all been documented in her psychiatric file. Lithium has been the only mood stabiliser to achieve sustained euthymia. She has a family history of bipolar disorder (her mother, who also required lithium maintenance) and a maternal aunt with completed suicide. Her renal function is normal (eGFR 95 mL/min/1.73 m squared, urinary albumin-creatinine ratio normal), thyroid function is normal on no replacement (TSH 1.6 mIU/L), and she has no cardiac, renal, or thyroid disease. She has no current substance use, takes a folate supplement 5 mg daily, and her partner is supportive and aware of the risks discussed. She asks specifically about the teratogenic risk of continuing lithium through pregnancy compared to switching to an alternative mood stabiliser, what foetal cardiac surveillance is recommended given the first-trimester exposure already planned, and what peripartum lithium dose adjustments are typically required. The TGA Pregnancy Database currently categorises lithium as D.

data_table.Current regimen: Lithium 900 mg nocte, level 0.8 mmol/L, euthymic 4 years
data_table.Prior trials: Valproate (weight gain), olanzapine (metabolic syndrome), quetiapine (breakthrough mania)
data_table.History: 3 severe manic episodes (2 inpatient), 1 severe depressive with ECT and SI

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discontinue lithium abruptly preconception and continue with no mood stabiliser through pregnancy given TGA category D rating
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium discontinuation precipitates relapse in around 50 percent within 6 months and is particularly dangerous in a woman with severe bipolar disorder and prior ECT-requiring depression; postpartum psychosis risk also rises sharply per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Switch from lithium to sodium valproate immediately as a safer alternative through pregnancy, citing the TGA category D rating of lithium and concerns about Ebstein anomaly
options.rationale: Sodium valproate has the HIGHEST teratogenicity of mood stabilisers (around 10 percent major malformations including neural tube defects, plus an IQ reduction of up to 9 points). The TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program prohibits valproate in pregnancy unless no alternative exists; switching to valproate during pregnancy planning is contraindicated.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine immediately as a safer alternative with a more favourable teratogenicity profile than both lithium and valproate, continue without further foetal surveillance, and avoid foetal echocardiography given the absence of lithium exposure during the planned conception
options.rationale: Carbamazepine carries around 3 percent neural tube defect risk and other malformations; it is not safer than lithium in this severe case, particularly with her treatment-resistant history per the COPE Guideline.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Stop lithium for the first trimester only, then restart in trimester 2
options.rationale: Episodic discontinuation increases relapse risk and provides no clear advantage for a patient with this severity; current Australian guidance (COPE) is to continue the lowest effective lithium dose with frequent level monitoring and detailed foetal cardiac surveillance, rather than stopping and restarting.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Continue lithium at the lowest effective dose with twice-monthly level monitoring (more frequent peripartum), arrange a foetal echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks for Ebstein anomaly screening, and plan delivery with peripartum lithium reduction and neonatal monitoring
options.rationale: For severe bipolar disorder with multiple failed alternatives, the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline and RANZCP perinatal psychiatry guidance recommend continuing lithium at the lowest effective dose. Lithium's first-trimester Ebstein anomaly risk is real but lower than historically quoted (around 1 in 1000 to 2 per 1000 absolute risk, relative risk around 10-20 fold above baseline ~1 in 20,000). Management requires twice-monthly lithium levels (more frequent peripartum due to changing volume of distribution), a detailed foetal cardiac echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks, peripartum dose reduction or brief withholding around delivery to prevent neonatal toxicity, and avoidance of breastfeeding on lithium if alternatives exist. Lamotrigine is a consideration for bipolar DEPRESSION prophylaxis but is less effective for mania prevention; in her predominantly manic history, lithium remains optimal.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

explanation.summary: For severe bipolar disorder with prior treatment failures, continuing lithium at the lowest effective dose with intensive monitoring and foetal cardiac surveillance is the safest plan per COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline. The benefit of relapse prevention outweighs the small absolute Ebstein anomaly risk.
key_points: Lithium first-trimester Ebstein risk: ~1-2 per 1000 absolute (relative risk ~10-20)
Valproate is the worst mood stabiliser in pregnancy (10% major malformations, IQ loss)
Lamotrigine is preferred for bipolar DEPRESSION but inferior for mania prophylaxis
Postpartum bipolar relapse rate >50% without prophylaxis
Lithium levels need more frequent monitoring in pregnancy (volume of distribution changes)
explanation.pearls: Lithium dose often falls in pregnancy (rising GFR), then must be reduced rapidly postpartum to avoid toxicity (COPE Guideline).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

q.subtopic_detail: DOAC selection in advanced CKD
q.stem: A 79-year-old man with persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation diagnosed 4 years ago has worsening chronic kidney disease with a current eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD-EPI), serum creatinine 218 micromol/L, and stable weight of 64 kg (BMI 22.4). His comorbidities include hypertension on perindopril, type 2 diabetes mellitus on basal insulin (HbA1c 7.1%), and a prior ischaemic stroke 2 years ago with mild residual right hemiparesis but functional independence. His calculated CHA2DS2-VASc score is 6 (age >=75 = 2, HTN = 1, DM = 1, prior CVA = 2) and HAS-BLED score is 3 (age >65, HTN, abnormal renal function). He has been on rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for 3 years, but his renal function has progressively declined over the past 6 months from an eGFR of 38 to the current 22. He is not yet on dialysis and his treating nephrologist estimates he is 12 to 18 months from requiring renal replacement therapy. He has no mechanical heart valve and no moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis on his last echocardiogram. He has had one prior epistaxis requiring nasal packing 18 months ago but no other bleeding. He lives independently with his wife in suburban Melbourne and is keen to remain on anticoagulation given his high stroke risk. He has no active malignancy and his platelet count, liver function tests and coagulation profile are normal.

data_table.Other: No mechanical valve, no significant MS, no active malignancy.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily requires CrCl >50 mL/min; for CrCl 15 to 49 the AMH dose is 15 mg daily, and the AMH advises caution for CrCl <30. Continuing 20 mg is inappropriate.

options.text: Switch to apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily with periodic renal function monitoring
options.rationale: Apixaban is the DOAC with the most renal-impairment evidence and has the lowest renal clearance (~27%). He meets the apixaban dose-reduction criteria: at least two of age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L - he has age 79 (not quite >=80) and creatinine 218 micromol/L (yes). However the AMH/PI also reduces dose to 2.5 mg BD when eGFR is 15 to 29 regardless of the standard criteria. The NHFA/CSANZ 2018 AF Guideline supports apixaban as the preferred DOAC in advanced CKD over warfarin given lower bleeding and similar efficacy.

options.text: Switch to dabigatran 110 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Dabigatran is ~80% renally cleared and is contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min in Australia (AMH). With eGFR 22, dabigatran is contraindicated.

options.text: Switch to warfarin with INR target 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin is acceptable in advanced CKD but DOACs (apixaban) are now preferred where eGFR is >=15 due to lower intracranial haemorrhage risk and comparable efficacy; warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves, significant mitral stenosis, dialysis or DOAC intolerance/contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation and refer for left atrial appendage occlusion
options.rationale: LAAO is reserved for patients with absolute contraindication to long-term anticoagulation, which he does not have; he tolerates anticoagulation and only needs dose/agent adjustment (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: DOAC selection in CKD is driven by renal clearance: dabigatran (~80%) > rivaroxaban (~35%) > edoxaban (~50%) > apixaban (~27%). Dabigatran is contraindicated below CrCl 30; rivaroxaban dose-reduces in CKD 3 to 4; apixaban is preferred in advanced CKD with the 2.5 mg BD dose criteria.
key_points: Dabigatran contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min
Rivaroxaban 15 mg daily for CrCl 15 to 49
Apixaban 2.5 mg BD if any 2 of: age >=80, wt <=60 kg, Cr >=133 micromol/L
Warfarin only for mechanical valves, significant MS, dialysis, or DOAC intolerance
explanation.pearls: Apixaban has the lowest renal clearance and best evidence in advanced CKD - the right DOAC for declining eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

q.subtopic_detail: Acute pulmonary oedema - early NIV and IV nitrate
q.stem: A 78-year-old woman with known HFrEF (LVEF 30% on her most recent echocardiogram 4 months ago) and a background of long-standing hypertension, permanent atrial fibrillation rate-controlled on bisoprolol and apixaban, and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline eGFR 48) is brought to a busy metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance with severe dyspnoea. She has been progressively breathless over the previous 24 hours after dietary indiscretion at a family birthday and woke with acute orthopnoea 2 hours ago. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is sitting upright bolt-upright, gasping, with marked accessory muscle use, tracheal tug, and visible pink frothy sputum at the lips. Vital signs at triage: HR 132 in atrial fibrillation, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on 15 L non-rebreather mask, afebrile at 36.6 degrees Celsius. Examination shows JVP elevated to the angle of the jaw, widespread bilateral fine inspiratory crackles to the mid-zones, gallop rhythm with audible S3, and cool peripheries with capillary refill of 3 seconds. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate 130 to 140 and no acute ischaemic changes. Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates with Kerley B lines and bilateral pleural effusions. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 38 ng/L (<14), creatinine 142 micromol/L (up from baseline 110), potassium 4.6, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. She is not opioid-naive at home (uses regular tramadol for chronic back pain) and has no fixed obstruction valve lesion. She has a current Resuscitation Plan documenting a wish for full active treatment short of invasive intubation if it can be avoided.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 AF, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on NRB 15 L, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, pink frothy sputum, JVP to jaw, widespread crackles, gallop, cool peripheries CRT 3 s.
data_table.ECG: AF rate 130 to 140, no ST changes.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 38 ng/L, Cr 142, K 4.6, lactate 2.4. CXR: bilateral alveolar oedema, Kerley B.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management combination?

options.text: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus, IV morphine 5 mg for anxiolysis and venodilation, with low-flow oxygen at 4 L via nasal prongs
options.rationale: Morphine in acute heart failure is associated with increased mortality and ICU admission; routine use is not recommended. Low-flow nasal prongs cannot deliver adequate FiO2 for SpO2 86% on NRB (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Intubate immediately for invasive mechanical ventilation
options.rationale: She has a Resuscitation Plan favouring avoidance of intubation if possible; non-invasive ventilation should be trialled first and very often averts intubation in acute pulmonary oedema (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: IV digoxin 500 micrograms loading and IV metoprolol 5 mg for rate control
options.rationale: IV beta-blocker in decompensated HF with cool peripheries and elevated lactate risks haemodynamic collapse; rate control is secondary to afterload reduction and ventilation in hypertensive APO (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence IV nesiritide and IV dobutamine infusion
options.rationale: Nesiritide is not available in Australia, and inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension; this patient is hypertensive (BP 192/108) and needs afterload reduction, not inotropy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence BiPAP (e.g., IPAP 12 to 14, EPAP 6 to 8 cmH2O), IV GTN infusion titrated to BP, and IV frusemide 40 mg bolus
options.rationale: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema is afterload-mediated; the priorities are oxygenation/ventilation and afterload reduction. Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP) reduces intubation rates and improves dyspnoea and oxygenation (3CPO trial; NHFA/CSANZ). IV nitrate is the afterload reducer of choice in hypertensive APO (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular). IV loop diuretic addresses congestion. The combination addresses oxygenation, preload, and afterload simultaneously. Avoid morphine.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

explanation.summary: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema management: sit up, high-flow oxygen, NIV (CPAP or BiPAP) early, IV nitrate for afterload reduction, IV loop diuretic for congestion. Avoid morphine. Inotropes only for cardiogenic shock. Identify and treat the precipitant (AF, ACS, infection, non-compliance).
key_points: NIV reduces intubation in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (3CPO)
GTN infusion is the afterload reducer in hypertensive APO
Loop diuretic addresses congestion; do not over-diurese into shock
Avoid morphine - higher mortality and ICU admission
explanation.pearls: BP 192/108 with APO = afterload problem; reduce afterload before chasing diuresis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

q.subtopic_detail: Brugada syndrome - type 1 ECG with syncope
q.stem: A 34-year-old male software engineer of South-East Asian background is brought to a tertiary hospital emergency department after a single witnessed episode of syncope at 0300 hours while sleeping next to his wife. His wife reports that he became briefly stiff for around 10 seconds with gasping agonal-type respirations and then recovered spontaneously over 1 minute with no confusion or post-event lethargy. He drinks approximately 4 standard drinks twice weekly, takes no regular medications, and has had a non-specific febrile illness over the previous 48 hours with paracetamol use for symptomatic relief of myalgia. He has had no previous episodes of syncope, pre-syncope, or palpitations. Family history is notable: a paternal first cousin died suddenly at age 28 during sleep with no clear cause identified at coronial autopsy; his father is well at age 62 with no cardiac history. Examination today is unremarkable; he is fully oriented with no neurological deficit. BP 124/72, HR 64 sinus, RR 14, T 37.8 degrees Celsius (febrile), SpO2 99% room air. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates a coved-type ST elevation of 2.5 mm in V1 and V2 with negative T waves and a J point >=2 mm, type 1 pattern present in two right precordial leads, persisting on repeat ECG with leads in standard and high (second intercostal space) positions. The QTc interval is 410 ms (normal). Cardiac troponin, electrolytes, magnesium and TSH are all within reference range. Echocardiogram is structurally normal with no regional wall motion abnormality. Urine toxicology screen is negative. There is no history of cocaine, tricyclic antidepressant, or sodium channel blocker exposure. He plans to return to work in 4 days but the cardiology team is reviewing him in the chest pain unit for definitive management.

data_table.ECG: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation >=2 mm with negative T in V1 and V2. QTc 410 ms.
data_table.Family Hx: Paternal cousin sudden death age 28 during sleep.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin, electrolytes, Mg, TSH normal. Echo structurally normal. Tox screen negative.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol for fever, and arrange outpatient cardiology review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: He has spontaneous type 1 Brugada pattern AND a syncopal episode of likely arrhythmic origin (sleep, brief, no prodrome) plus family history of sudden death; this is the highest-risk group for sudden cardiac death and requires definitive therapy, not outpatient deferral (CSANZ/HRS Brugada statement; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Refer for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) implantation under the symptomatic Brugada syndrome Class I indication
options.rationale: Symptomatic Brugada syndrome with spontaneous type 1 ECG and unexplained syncope likely arrhythmic in origin carries a Class I indication for ICD per the HRS/EHRA/APHRS expert consensus (endorsed by CSANZ) and is reflected in BMJ Best Practice. Family history of sudden death adds to risk. Fever (which unmasks/exacerbates Brugada) should be aggressively treated with paracetamol as adjunct, but does not obviate the indication for definitive therapy. Quinidine and EP-guided VT ablation are alternatives in some scenarios but ICD remains primary.
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence flecainide 100 mg twice daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Flecainide is a sodium channel blocker that UNMASKS and worsens Brugada (used as a provocative challenge test); it would precipitate ventricular arrhythmia. Class IC agents are contraindicated (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence amiodarone 200 mg daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Amiodarone has no proven benefit in Brugada and does not replace an ICD for proven symptomatic disease; quinidine is the antiarrhythmic with the strongest Brugada-specific evidence as ICD adjunct, but ICD remains primary (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Arrange genetic testing for SCN5A and other Brugada-associated variants, and await the result before deciding on further definitive management
options.rationale: Genetic testing informs cascade screening of relatives but a negative SCN5A does not exclude Brugada (only ~20-30% of probands have identified pathogenic variants) and does not change management of a clinically diagnosed symptomatic patient. Delaying ICD to await genetics is unsafe (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

explanation.summary: Brugada syndrome diagnosis requires spontaneous type 1 ECG (coved ST elevation >=2 mm in >=1 right precordial lead V1 or V2) plus a clinical feature (documented VF/polymorphic VT, syncope of probable arrhythmic origin, nocturnal agonal respiration, family history of sudden death <45, type 1 ECG in family member). Symptomatic Brugada has Class I ICD indication. Class IC drugs and fever provoke the syndrome.
key_points: Type 1 Brugada pattern + syncope = ICD (Class I)
Avoid sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone) and TCAs
Fever unmasks Brugada - treat aggressively with paracetamol
Quinidine is the antiarrhythmic of choice as ICD adjunct or bridge
explanation.pearls: Look for spontaneous (not just drug-induced) type 1 pattern in any young adult with nocturnal syncope or sudden death family history (CSANZ/HRS).

sources.label: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

q.subtopic_detail: Complete heart block with haemodynamic compromise - immediate management
q.stem: A 74-year-old man is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance after a witnessed syncopal episode at home. His wife reports that he stood from his armchair, became pale, and collapsed without warning, hitting his head lightly on the carpeted floor; he was unresponsive for approximately 90 seconds before recovering some awareness. On arrival to the resuscitation bay he is confused (GCS 13: E4 V3 M6), cool peripherally, pale and diaphoretic. Vital signs at triage: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, blood glucose 7.8 mmol/L on point-of-care testing. Capillary refill is prolonged at 4 seconds. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates complete atrioventricular dissociation with an atrial rate of 84 and a ventricular escape rhythm of 28 with broad QRS complexes (160 ms) and a left bundle branch block morphology consistent with an infra-nodal escape. There are no acute ischaemic ST changes and no Q waves. He has no known cardiac history. His regular medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and metformin 1 g twice daily. He has had no recent illness, no diarrhoea, no overdose (no recent prescription changes, no access to other medications), and no environmental exposures. Initial bloods drawn at arrival: troponin pending, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, glucose 7.8, urea and creatinine within normal limits, calcium and magnesium normal. The ANZCOR adult bradycardia algorithm is being followed by the ED senior. The on-call cardiology registrar has been telephoned and is 15 minutes away from the department. The patient remains in extremis with poor perfusion despite being supine and on high-flow oxygen.

data_table.Vitals: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Cool peripherally, CRT 4 s, pale, diaphoretic, confused.
data_table.ECG: Complete AV dissociation. Atrial rate 84, ventricular escape rate 28, broad QRS 160 ms (LBBB morphology). No acute ischaemic changes.

q.lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention while awaiting cardiology?

options.text: IV adrenaline 1 mg bolus stat and prepare for immediate rapid sequence intubation as the airway intervention takes priority over rate restoration
options.rationale: 1 mg adrenaline bolus is the cardiac arrest dose; for symptomatic bradycardia, ANZCOR recommends adrenaline INFUSION 2 to 10 micrograms/min titrated to response, or 20 to 50 microgram boluses. A 1 mg bolus risks hypertensive crisis and arrhythmia; intubation is not the immediate priority while perfusion can be restored with pacing.

options.text: Atropine 600 micrograms IV, repeated every 3 to 5 minutes to a maximum cumulative dose of 3 mg, as the sole pharmacological strategy while awaiting cardiology review
options.rationale: Atropine acts at the AV node and is generally INEFFECTIVE in complete heart block with a wide, infra-nodal escape rhythm; ANZCOR explicitly recommends moving directly to pacing or chronotropic infusion in such cases, although atropine can be tried briefly as a temporising step. Atropine alone is insufficient as the immediate intervention for this haemodynamic state.

options.text: Commence transcutaneous pacing with analgesia/sedation, set rate 70 to 80, increase output until electrical and mechanical capture confirmed, while arranging emergent transvenous pacing
options.rationale: ANZCOR Adult Bradycardia algorithm: in haemodynamically compromised bradycardia unresponsive to atropine or where atropine is unlikely to work (infra-nodal CHB with wide escape), transcutaneous pacing is first-line emergency intervention. Confirm BOTH electrical capture (pacing spike followed by QRS) AND mechanical capture (palpable pulse or arterial tracing). Provide adequate sedation/analgesia (TCP is painful). Arrange definitive transvenous pacing followed by permanent pacemaker. Atropine can be tried concurrently but TCP should not be delayed.
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: 1 L 0.9% saline bolus over 15 minutes to optimise preload and reassess perfusion and conscious state before considering rate-restoring measures
options.rationale: Fluid will not address the underlying conduction problem and may precipitate pulmonary oedema in an elderly patient with likely impaired LV reserve. Definitive treatment is restoring rate (ANZCOR).

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution given over 10 minutes empirically while awaiting biochemistry, with repeat ECG at 5 minutes
options.rationale: Calcium is for confirmed or suspected hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker toxicity; K is 4.4 mmol/L and amlodipine overdose is not suggested by history. Not the immediate intervention (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: ANZCOR adult bradycardia algorithm: if signs of life-threatening haemodynamic compromise, atropine 600 microgram IV (may repeat) as initial pharmacological step, but for wide-QRS infra-nodal block atropine often fails - escalate immediately to transcutaneous pacing (with sedation) or chronotropic infusion (adrenaline 2 to 10 micrograms/min, isoprenaline). Arrange transvenous pacing then permanent device.
key_points: Always confirm BOTH electrical and mechanical capture during TCP
Atropine often ineffective in wide-QRS infra-nodal block
Adrenaline infusion 2 to 10 mcg/min is an alternative when pacing unavailable
Identify and treat reversible causes (ischaemia, hyperkalaemia, drugs)
explanation.pearls: Mechanical capture (palpable pulse) is the goal, not just pacing spikes on the monitor (ANZCOR).

sources.label: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

q.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a tight, non-productive cough. He has a known history of atopy, including eczema and a peanut allergy, and has had two previous hospital admissions for viral-induced wheeze. His parents report he had a coryzal illness starting three days ago. He received six puffs of salbutamol via a spacer every three hours at home with minimal effect. On examination, he is alert but agitated, sitting forward and speaking in single words. He has a prominent tracheal tug, severe intercostal recessions, and widespread biphasic wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally. He is immediately commenced on high-flow oxygen, continuous nebulised salbutamol, nebulised ipratropium bromide, and intravenous hydrocortisone. One hour later, he remains severely distressed with poor air entry.

data_table.Vital signs: Initial: HR 165 bpm, RR 48/min, SpO2 90% (room air). Current (1hr): HR 175 bpm, RR 45/min, SpO2 92% (8 L/min O2 mask)
data_table.Examination: Agitated, single words, severe work of breathing, poor air entry.
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone given in ED.

q.lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intramuscular adrenaline.
options.rationale: Despite the peanut allergy history, there are no signs of anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension) to justify adrenaline over asthma-specific adjuncts. (Source: ASCIA: Acute Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Commence an intravenous continuous aminophylline infusion.
options.rationale: Aminophylline is a third-line agent in paediatric severe asthma and is only considered after magnesium sulfate and IV salbutamol have failed. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Intubate and commence mechanical ventilation immediately.
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is fraught with complications and reserved for absolute respiratory arrest or exhaustion; medical adjuncts must be trialled first. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Administer a single bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate.
options.rationale: In a child with severe acute asthma failing continuous nebulised therapy and systemic corticosteroids, a bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate is the next recommended step. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Administer a fluid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: While asthmatic children may be mildly dehydrated, fluid boluses do not relieve the acute bronchospasm and are not the priority over bronchodilation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

explanation.summary: This child has severe acute asthma refractory to first-line therapies (continuous bronchodilators and corticosteroids). The next appropriate step in the Australian step-wise management of paediatric status asthmaticus is an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)
