key_points: Severe asthma is characterised by agitation, inability to talk in sentences, severe work of breathing, and SpO2 < 92%.
Initial escalation involves bursts of inhaled bronchodilators transitioning to continuous nebulisers if no improvement.
When continuous nebulisers fail, the first-line intravenous adjunct is magnesium sulfate, followed by an IV salbutamol infusion if needed in an ICU setting.
explanation.pearls: A 'silent chest' or poor air entry indicates critically severe bronchospasm where wheeze may not be audible due to minimal airflow.

q.stem: A 4-week-old male infant is brought to the clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his persistent yellowish skin discolouration. He was born at 39 weeks gestation. His newborn screening test was reported as normal, and he received his birth dose of hepatitis B and vitamin K intramuscularly. His mother had an uncomplicated pregnancy but required a brief course of oral antibiotics for a presumed urinary tract infection at 36 weeks. The infant is exclusively breastfed, feeding every 3 hours, and producing 6 - 8 wet nappies per day. His stools have become noticeably pale and chalky over the past week, and his urine leaves a dark yellow stain on the nappy. On examination, the infant is alert and vigorous, with a weight of 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.4 kg). He has frank scleral icterus and widespread cutaneous jaundice extending to the umbilicus. Abdominal examination reveals a smooth, firm liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin. A formal venous blood panel is obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, RR 35/min, Temp 36.9 C
data_table.Investigations: Total Bilirubin 210 micromol/L, Conjugated Bilirubin 145 micromol/L, ALT 85 U/L, AST 90 U/L, GGT 410 U/L, ALP 550 U/L, Hb 145 g/L, DAT negative.

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the mother and continue exclusive breastfeeding.
options.rationale: While breast milk jaundice is a common cause of prolonged neonatal jaundice, it presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; this infant's conjugated fraction is severely elevated. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Commence intensive blue-light phototherapy immediately.
options.rationale: Phototherapy is only indicated for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. In cholestasis, it is ineffective and can cause 'bronze baby syndrome'. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Prescribe a trial of oral ursodeoxycholic acid.
options.rationale: Although used for cholestasis, empirical treatment without ruling out a time-critical surgical emergency (biliary atresia) is inappropriate and delays definitive care. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Arrange an outpatient hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan.
options.rationale: A HIDA scan is part of the workup, but an abdominal ultrasound must be performed first to assess structural anatomy, and the timeline must be urgent, not routine outpatient. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Admit for an urgent fasting abdominal ultrasound.
options.rationale: Pale stools, dark urine, and a conjugated bilirubin fraction >20% of total indicate pathological cholestasis. Biliary atresia must be urgently excluded via ultrasound as first-line imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

explanation.summary: This 4-week-old infant has prolonged jaundice that is conjugated (direct fraction > 20% of total), accompanied by pale stools and dark urine. This is a red flag for neonatal cholestasis, with biliary atresia being the most time-critical diagnosis. Immediate admission and an urgent fasting abdominal ultrasound are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
key_points: Any jaundice persisting beyond 14 days (or 21 days in preterm infants) requires fractionation of bilirubin.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia is always pathological and never physiological or due to breast milk.
Biliary atresia presents with conjugated jaundice, pale (acholic) stools, dark urine, and hepatomegaly, requiring a Kasai procedure ideally before 60 days of life.
explanation.pearls: Never dismiss prolonged jaundice in a breastfed infant without first checking a fractionated bilirubin level to rule out cholestatic disease.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

q.stem: A 3-year-old girl is rushed to the emergency department by ambulance. Her parents report a 12-hour history of high fever, extreme lethargy, and vomiting. She is fully immunised to schedule. On arrival, she is poorly responsive, groaning to pain. She is notably pale with mottled skin and a non-blanching purpuric rash spreading rapidly across her lower limbs and abdomen. Her extremities are cold to the touch with a capillary refill time of 5 seconds. Two wide-bore intravenous cannulae are immediately secured. Initial point-of-care testing shows a lactate of 5.0 mmol/L. She is immediately given a 20 mL/kg rapid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and intravenous ceftriaxone. Reassessment 15 minutes later shows no clinical improvement, and a second 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride is administered (total 40 mL/kg given). On repeat assessment, she remains obtunded, capillary refill is 4 seconds, and her blood pressure continues to fall.

data_table.Vital signs: Initial: HR 170 bpm, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR 40/min, Temp 39.5 C, SpO2 92% (room air). Post-fluid (40mL/kg): HR 165 bpm, BP 65/35 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Obtunded (GCS 9). Cold peripheries, mottled. Spreading purpuric rash.
data_table.Investigations: Venous gas: pH 7.20, pCO2 35 mmHg, Lactate 5.0 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L.

q.lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a third 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: In paediatric sepsis, fluid-refractory shock is defined as persisting shock after 40 mL/kg of crystalloid. Further boluses increase the risk of pulmonary oedema without correcting the vasoplegia. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Perform immediate rapid sequence endotracheal intubation.
options.rationale: While she is obtunded, intubating a profoundly shocked child before restoring vascular tone with inotropes is extremely high risk for peri-intubation cardiac arrest due to loss of sympathetic drive. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Commence a peripheral intravenous adrenaline infusion.
options.rationale: The child has fluid-refractory 'cold' septic shock (likely meningococcal). Australian guidelines mandate the early commencement of inotropes (adrenaline) peripherally while awaiting central access. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a 10 mL/kg bolus of 4% human albumin solution.
options.rationale: Colloids are not superior to crystalloids in initial sepsis resuscitation. Furthermore, her shock is fluid-refractory, meaning vasoactive agents, not more volume, are the physiological priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intravenous hydrocortisone sodium.
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are indicated for suspected adrenal insufficiency or catecholamine-resistant shock, but only AFTER an adrenaline/noradrenaline infusion has been commenced and optimised. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

explanation.summary: This critically ill child has classic features of meningococcal septicaemia with purpura fulminans, presenting in profound 'cold' shock (tachycardia, prolonged cap refill, hypotension, mottled skin). After receiving 40 mL/kg of crystalloid with no haemodynamic response, she is in fluid-refractory shock. The most critical next step is initiating vasoactive support with an adrenaline infusion via peripheral IV access to restore perfusion pressure. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)
key_points: Paediatric septic shock is often 'cold' (low cardiac output, high systemic vascular resistance) requiring adrenaline, compared to 'warm' shock requiring noradrenaline.
Fluid-refractory shock is defined as persisting signs of poor perfusion after 40 mL/kg of fluid boluses.
Do not delay inotropes to obtain central venous access; peripheral infusions are safe as a bridge in an emergency.
Intubating a severely shocked patient precipitates cardiovascular collapse; optimise haemodynamics with fluids and inotropes first if the airway is maintainable.
explanation.pearls: Hypotension is a late and ominous sign of decompensated shock in children; rely on tachycardia and prolonged capillary refill to guide early aggressive resuscitation.

q.stem: A 6-week-old term infant is brought to the emergency department in mid-winter with 3 days of coryza, cough, and increasing work of breathing. He was born at 39 weeks, has been feeding half his usual volume for 24 hours, and has had 2 wet nappies today. He is fully immunised for age, has no congenital heart disease, and takes no regular medicines. His older sibling has a runny nose, and he attends daycare. On examination he is alert but tired, with subcostal recessions, fine widespread crackles, and wheeze. He has no focal chest signs. He was born with a mild tongue tie that has not affected feeding. His mother asks whether he needs antibiotics because a cousin with pneumonia was admitted last month. This infant remains on room air but is becoming more tachypnoeic.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.8 deg C, HR 168 bpm, RR 62/min, SpO2 94% in room air
data_table.Examination: Subcostal recessions, nasal flaring, widespread crackles and wheeze, no focal crepitations, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no drug allergies

q.lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin and arrange outpatient review tomorrow
options.rationale: This is not a focal bacterial pneumonia picture, and routine antibiotics do not improve typical bronchiolitis. The key management issue here is supportive care with close observation, not community-acquired pneumonia treatment. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Provide suction, oxygen if needed, and support oral or nasogastric fluids
options.rationale: This infant has typical bronchiolitis with mild hypoxia, feeding reduction, and increased work of breathing, so supportive care is the appropriate next step. Oxygen and hydration are the mainstays, while antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated bronchiolitis. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Order a chest x-ray and commence intravenous ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Typical bronchiolitis does not need routine imaging or empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics. The absence of focal chest signs and the age-appropriate viral prodrome point away from bacterial consolidation. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol and reassess after 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis management because they do not meaningfully change outcomes in typical cases. Wheeze alone does not make this asthma. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Administer dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline
options.rationale: This is a lower-airway bronchiolitis presentation rather than croup, as there is no barking cough or stridor. Steroids and nebulised adrenaline are used for croup, not routine bronchiolitis. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; RCH CPG: Croup.

explanation.summary: This infant has typical bronchiolitis, which is managed with supportive care focused on oxygenation, hydration, and observation. Routine antibiotics, bronchodilators, steroids, and chest imaging are not part of standard care in an uncomplicated presentation. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.
key_points: Bronchiolitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis in young infants with viral prodrome, wheeze, crackles, and increased work of breathing. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.
Supportive care is the main treatment, including oxygen if needed and assistance with fluids. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.
Antibiotics, salbutamol, and steroids are not routine therapies for typical bronchiolitis. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.
explanation.pearls: Feeding difficulty and rising work of breathing are often the real reasons bronchiolitis infants need admission, not the chest sounds alone. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

q.stem: A 10-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes diagnosed 18 months ago is brought to the emergency department after 24 hours of vomiting and lethargy. His insulin pump tubing disconnected overnight and he has had progressive abdominal pain and thirst since morning. He received a correction bolus by pen 2 hours ago. On arrival he is drowsy but obeys commands. During initial treatment with isotonic fluids and intravenous insulin, he becomes increasingly irritable and complains of severe headache. His mother reports he is now asking repetitive questions. Capillary glucose has fallen from 28.6 mmol/L to 18.2 mmol/L over 90 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 30/min deep respirations, Temp 37.1 C, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, delayed capillary refill 3 seconds, new confusion and reduced orientation
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.08, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, ketones 5.9 mmol/L, Na 131 mmol/L, K 4.7 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Continuous insulin infusion commenced, isotonic fluids running

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate next step?**

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion rate and repeat venous blood gas in 30 minutes before further action
options.rationale: Biochemical reassessment is important, but new neurologic deterioration during DKA should prompt immediate treatment for cerebral oedema rather than observation. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently escalate for presumed cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Headache, irritability, altered mental status, and repetitive questioning during DKA treatment strongly suggest cerebral oedema. Immediate hyperosmolar treatment and senior escalation are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Increase isotonic fluid replacement because persistent dehydration is the likely explanation
options.rationale: Excessive fluid administration may worsen cerebral oedema risk. Neurologic decline in this context should not be treated as simple dehydration progression. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT brain before initiating further treatment to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay treatment when cerebral oedema is clinically suspected during DKA management. Treatment is time critical. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Cease insulin and commence dextrose-containing fluids as the glucose is falling rapidly
options.rationale: Dextrose may be added later to allow continued insulin, but stopping insulin risks worsening ketosis and does not address acute neurologic deterioration. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: The key discriminator is acute neurologic deterioration during DKA treatment. Cerebral oedema remains a clinical diagnosis and must be treated immediately with hyperosmolar therapy and escalation before confirmatory imaging. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
key_points: Headache and altered mental state during treatment are red flags for cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
CT imaging should not delay empiric treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A falling glucose does not exclude worsening intracellular cerebral swelling. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

q.stem: A 10-month-old boy is brought to the ED with 8 hours of intermittent screaming episodes, drawing up of the legs, and repeated non-bilious vomiting. Between episodes he is pale and settles briefly, but he has become increasingly lethargic. His mother says he had a mild viral illness last week. He was born at term, has no previous surgery, and his immunisations are up to date. On examination he is afebrile, mildly dehydrated, and has a soft but mildly distended abdomen. A small amount of red mucus is seen in the nappy after a pain episode. His father asks whether this could be gastroenteritis because several children at daycare were unwell. There is no bilious vomiting and no peritonism.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.2 deg C, HR 156 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 86/54 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Intermittent colicky pain, mild abdominal distension, no guarding, no palpable hernia
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate initial investigation?**

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound
options.rationale: This presentation is classic for intussusception, and ultrasound is the preferred initial investigation. The episodic pain, lethargy, vomiting, and red mucus all support that pathway. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Plain abdominal x-ray
options.rationale: A plain x-ray can show obstruction or perforation, but it is not the best first test when intussusception is suspected and the child is stable. Ultrasound is more accurate for diagnosis. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study
options.rationale: An upper GI study is used for malrotation or volvulus, especially with bilious vomiting. This child has intermittent colicky pain and red mucus instead, which points to intussusception. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
options.rationale: CT is not the usual initial investigation in children with suspected intussusception because it adds radiation and is not needed when ultrasound is available. Bedside ultrasound is the preferred test. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Stool PCR panel for infectious gastroenteritis
options.rationale: The colicky episodic pain and red mucus are not explained well by routine gastroenteritis. A stool test would delay diagnosis and treatment of a surgical emergency. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

explanation.summary: This infant has a classic presentation of intussusception, and ultrasound is the preferred initial investigation. Gastroenteritis is a distractor here because the episodic pain, lethargy, and red mucus are more concerning for bowel telescoping. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
key_points: Intussusception commonly presents with intermittent colicky pain, vomiting, lethargy, and red mucus or blood per rectum. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
Ultrasound is the initial test of choice in a stable child. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
Bilious vomiting and peritonism would raise concern for a different surgical emergency. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.
explanation.pearls: Between pain episodes, the child may look deceptively well, which is why the history is so important. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

q.stem: A 7-year-old girl receiving chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presents with fever and malaise. She had her last vincristine and dexamethasone cycle 6 days ago and now has worsening tiredness, reduced oral intake, and mild abdominal discomfort. Her central venous line is clean at the skin but has not been accessed in 2 days. She takes trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis and ondansetron as needed. Her parents report no cough, sore throat, or urinary symptoms, but she had one episode of loose stool this morning. On examination she looks unwell but is not in respiratory distress. She is pale, has cool peripheries, and appears tachycardic. Her oncology team advises that she may be neutropenic. There is no focal source of infection, and she has no drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.1 deg C, HR 148 bpm, RR 26/min, BP 94/58 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Tired, pale, cool peripheries, central line site clean, abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 0.7 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.1 x10^9/L, platelets 62 x10^9/L, Hb 88 g/L; CRP 96 mg/L; lactate 2.7 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis, ondansetron PRN, chemotherapy 6 days ago

q.lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Take blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
options.rationale: This child has febrile neutropenia with systemic features, so urgent empiric intravenous antibiotics after appropriate cultures is the correct next step. Treatment should not be delayed while searching for a source. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Observe for 4 hours and repeat FBC before treating
options.rationale: This is unsafe because fever with severe neutropenia is a medical emergency. Waiting for repeat blood counts delays time-critical antibiotics. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and discharge if stable
options.rationale: Oral therapy and discharge are not appropriate for a febrile child with profound neutropenia and raised inflammatory markers. She needs urgent intravenous treatment and admission. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Remove the central venous line before cultures are taken
options.rationale: Line removal is not the first step in an otherwise line-preserved febrile neutropenia presentation. Cultures should be obtained promptly, and antibiotics started immediately. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

options.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and await marrow recovery
options.rationale: G-CSF does not replace urgent empirical antibiotics in febrile neutropenia. The immediate priority is antimicrobial therapy and sepsis management. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
source_refs: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

explanation.summary: This child has febrile neutropenia with profound neutropenia and systemic features, which requires prompt cultures and immediate broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics. Delay to repeat counts, discharge, or line removal would be unsafe. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
key_points: Fever with severe neutropenia is an oncologic emergency. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
Empiric IV antibiotics should be given promptly after cultures are taken. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
A clear focus is not required before treatment starts. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.
explanation.pearls: Profound neutropenia can make a child appear less floridly septic than expected, so do not be reassured by the absence of a focal source. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

sources.label: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia

q.stem: A 13-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes is brought in with 18 hours of abdominal pain, vomiting, and increasing lethargy after a viral illness. She has missed two doses of insulin because she was not eating well and thought it would be safer to stop it. Her mother says she has been drinking large volumes of water and passing urine frequently for 2 days. She is drowsy but rousable, has deep breathing, and smells strongly of ketones. She has no history of asthma or renal disease. She takes basal-bolus insulin and uses a continuous glucose monitor, which has been reading high. There is no focal abdominal tenderness, and pregnancy testing is negative. Her parents ask whether she can have insulin first because her sugar is very high. The team needs the safest immediate plan.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.4 deg C, HR 132 bpm, RR 30/min with Kussmaul pattern, BP 94/60 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, tachypnoea, fruity breath, capillary refill 3 s, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: BGL 31.8 mmol/L, venous pH 7.14, HCO3 8 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, Na 130 mmol/L, K 5.5 mmol/L, creatinine 86 micromol/L

q.lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Start insulin infusion immediately without fluids
options.rationale: Insulin is essential in DKA, but fluids and careful resuscitation come first or alongside initial management. Starting insulin without addressing intravascular depletion is unsafe. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Give a normal saline bolus and begin the paediatric DKA protocol
options.rationale: This child has moderate to severe DKA with dehydration, acidosis, and ketosis, so the correct next step is fluid resuscitation and a standard DKA protocol. Insulin is then started in a controlled manner after initial fluids and assessment. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Administer subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin and discharge if ketones fall
options.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin is not appropriate for this degree of acidosis, dehydration, and altered mental state. She requires hospital-based DKA management. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Give sodium bicarbonate because the pH is below 7.20
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely used in paediatric DKA and is reserved for very specific circumstances. Standard therapy is fluids, insulin, and close biochemical monitoring. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Treat hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate before any fluids
options.rationale: Her potassium is elevated but typical DKA management focuses on controlled rehydration and insulin, with electrolyte monitoring and replacement as needed. There is no indication here for emergency calcium first. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

explanation.summary: This adolescent has diabetic ketoacidosis with dehydration, acidosis, ketosis, and altered conscious state. The safest next step is initial fluid resuscitation and institution of the paediatric DKA protocol, with controlled insulin therapy and close monitoring. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
key_points: DKA is a medical emergency requiring structured fluid and insulin management. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
Insulin should not be the first standalone step before resuscitation in a dehydrated child. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
Bicarbonate is not routine therapy in paediatric DKA. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.
explanation.pearls: Missing insulin during intercurrent illness is a classic trigger for DKA in adolescents with type 1 diabetes. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

q.subtopic: Bronchiolitis and escalation of respiratory support
q.stem: An 8-month-old ex-35-week infant is brought to the emergency department in winter with 4 days of cough, coryza, and poor feeding. He was reviewed by a GP yesterday and diagnosed with bronchiolitis. Overnight he took less than half his usual feeds and had two episodes of post-tussive vomiting. His mother reports increasing work of breathing and fewer wet nappies today. He has no history of congenital heart disease but required CPAP briefly after birth. In the emergency department he is started on low-flow oxygen via nasal prongs with transient improvement in saturations. Thirty minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with persistent subcostal and intercostal recession. Capillary blood gas shows mild hypercapnia without acidosis. A junior doctor suggests trialling salbutamol because there is occasional wheeze on auscultation and the father has asthma.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 62/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, Temp 37.8 C, SpO2 89% room air improving to 93% on 2 L/min oxygen
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, diffuse crackles with scattered wheeze, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: CBG pH 7.34, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medications, immunisations up to date

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal prong therapy and continue supportive management with nasogastric hydration
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and moderate work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen indicate need for escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy. Supportive care remains the cornerstone of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol and reassess respiratory rate before considering respiratory support escalation
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis because they do not reliably improve clinically important outcomes. Escalation of respiratory support is more appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because the hypercapnia suggests developing bacterial pneumonia
options.rationale: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not itself suggest bacterial pneumonia. There are no focal findings or septic features supporting empiric antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Arrange urgent intubation because preterm birth increases risk of sudden respiratory decompensation
options.rationale: Although prematurity increases bronchiolitis severity risk, this infant does not yet show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis, or exhaustion requiring immediate intubation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with frequent oral fluids because oxygen saturations improved on nasal prongs
options.rationale: Persistent tachypnoea, increased work of breathing, poor oral intake, and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Ongoing inpatient supportive care is indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: This infant has moderate-to-severe bronchiolitis with persistent respiratory distress despite low-flow oxygen. Australian bronchiolitis guidance recommends escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy with supportive hydration rather than bronchodilators or antibiotics in typical disease. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
key_points: Bronchiolitis management is primarily supportive. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
Poor feeding and persistent work of breathing are indications for admission and escalation of respiratory support. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)
explanation.pearls: Scattered wheeze alone does not make bronchodilator-responsive asthma more likely in typical infant bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

q.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department with fever, vomiting, and progressive lethargy over 12 hours. Three children at his childcare centre were recently absent with febrile illnesses. He returned from visiting extended family in rural Queensland 5 days ago. His parents report he complained of leg pain earlier today and became difficult to wake after an afternoon nap. He has no significant medical history and is fully immunised according to the National Immunisation Program. On arrival he is confused and intermittently agitated. A blanching maculopapular rash on his trunk has evolved over the past hour into scattered non-blanching purpuric lesions over the lower limbs. Peripheral IV access is difficult and blood cultures have not yet been obtained. During assessment he develops cool peripheries and prolonged capillary refill. A junior doctor asks whether ceftriaxone should be delayed until cultures are secured because lumbar puncture may also be needed urgently.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.7 C, HR 168 bpm, RR 36/min, BP 76/42 mmHg, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: Confused, mottled skin, purpuric rash over legs, neck stiffness difficult to assess, capillary refill 5 seconds
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.24, lactate 5.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.9 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medications, no known drug allergies

q.lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture before antibiotics because meningitis confirmation will guide therapy duration
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and should not delay treatment of suspected meningococcal sepsis. Immediate empiric therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Administer empiric intravenous ceftriaxone immediately while initiating aggressive septic shock resuscitation
options.rationale: This child has meningococcal septic shock with purpura, hypotension, and elevated lactate. Empiric antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation must not be delayed for cultures or lumbar puncture. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and CT brain before antimicrobial therapy because altered consciousness raises concern for raised ICP
options.rationale: Neuroimaging and cultures are secondary priorities in unstable septic shock. Delaying antibiotics increases mortality in invasive meningococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence aciclovir and transfer for MRI because encephalitis is more likely than bacterial sepsis
options.rationale: Purpura, shock physiology, prolonged capillary refill, and elevated lactate strongly support meningococcal sepsis rather than isolated viral encephalitis. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin and observe response before escalating to intravenous therapy
options.rationale: Pre-hospital IM benzylpenicillin may be considered in remote settings, but this critically unwell child requires immediate definitive intravenous therapy and full septic shock management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: The critical reasoning step is prioritising immediate empiric treatment over diagnostic completeness in suspected meningococcal septic shock. Purpura, hypotension, prolonged capillary refill, elevated lactate, and altered consciousness indicate invasive meningococcal disease requiring urgent antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)
key_points: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)
Antibiotics should not be delayed for imaging or cultures in septic shock. (Source: ANZCOR Paediatric Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: Leg pain and evolving purpura are early clues to meningococcal disease in children before classic meningism becomes obvious. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

q.stem: A 13-year-old boy with moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath over 8 hours after developing rhinorrhoea and cough 2 days earlier. He used salbutamol every 2 hours overnight with progressively shorter relief. He was admitted to hospital twice last year and required PICU admission at age 9 but has never been intubated. His preventer adherence is poor. On arrival he can speak only in short phrases and prefers to sit upright. He receives inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium with oral dexamethasone. Ninety minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with increasing fatigue. Air entry is now reduced bilaterally and wheeze is quieter than at presentation. A venous blood gas is repeated because nursing staff are concerned he appears more drowsy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148 bpm, RR 38/min, BP 118/70 mmHg, SpO2 91% on room air improving to 94% with oxygen
data_table.Examination: Marked accessory muscle use, reduced bilateral air entry, quieter wheeze, intermittent drowsiness
data_table.Investigations: Initial VBG pH 7.37 pCO2 39 mmHg, repeat VBG pH 7.29 pCO2 56 mmHg
data_table.Medications: Repeated inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium, oral dexamethasone

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Continue intermittent salbutamol only because the oxygen saturation has improved with supplemental oxygen
options.rationale: Improved oxygen saturation alone does not indicate clinical stability. Rising carbon dioxide and fatigue suggest impending respiratory failure requiring escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

options.text: Arrange discharge with prednisolone because reduced wheeze indicates treatment response
options.rationale: A quieter chest with worsening hypercapnia and fatigue is concerning for severe airflow limitation rather than improvement. Discharge would be unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Escalate to intensive care review for intravenous therapy and possible respiratory support
options.rationale: Rising pCO2, fatigue, altered mental state, and reduced air entry indicate life-threatening asthma with impending respiratory failure requiring PICU-level escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

options.text: Administer intravenous antibiotics because viral-triggered bacterial pneumonia is now most likely
options.rationale: There are no focal infective findings supporting bacterial pneumonia. The major issue is worsening ventilatory failure from severe asthma. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Cease bronchodilators and commence non-invasive ventilation on the ward because tachycardia suggests toxicity
options.rationale: Tachycardia is common after beta-agonists and severe respiratory distress. Bronchodilators should not be ceased in deteriorating acute severe asthma. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

explanation.summary: The key discriminator is recognising deterioration despite treatment. Rising carbon dioxide in acute asthma, especially with fatigue and reduced air entry, indicates impending respiratory failure and need for intensive care escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)
key_points: Normalising or rising pCO2 in severe asthma is a dangerous sign. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)
Reduced wheeze can reflect critically poor airflow rather than improvement. (Source: ANZCOR Acute Asthma)
explanation.pearls: In severe asthma, a quieter chest can be more concerning than loud wheeze. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

q.stem: A 7-week-old previously healthy girl presents with fever and reduced feeding for 10 hours. She was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery and required no neonatal admission. Her mother reports increased sleepiness and only two wet nappies since morning. There is no cough, rhinorrhoea, vomiting, or diarrhoea. An older sibling attends childcare and recently had a mild viral illness. On examination the infant briefly opens her eyes to stimulation but settles quickly back to sleep. Peripheral perfusion is mildly delayed. There is no rash and the fontanelle is soft. Urinalysis obtained by catheter specimen shows positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites. A junior doctor suggests discharging her after one dose of oral antibiotics because she appears comfortable when held by her mother.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 C, HR 178 bpm, RR 44/min, BP 82/48 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild lethargy, delayed capillary refill 3 seconds, soft fontanelle, no increased work of breathing
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.8 x10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L, urinalysis positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Discharge with oral cephalexin because urinary tract infection is the likely source of fever
options.rationale: Young febrile infants under 3 months require inpatient assessment and parenteral antibiotics because of high risk of invasive bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

options.text: Commence empiric intravenous antibiotics and admit for full septic evaluation and observation
options.rationale: This infant is under 3 months with fever, lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI. Full sepsis assessment and inpatient IV antibiotics are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department without antibiotics until blood culture results become available
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a potentially septic young infant is unsafe given age-related risk of rapid deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

options.text: Arrange renal ultrasound before treatment because structural urinary abnormalities are likely
options.rationale: Renal imaging may be considered later but should not delay urgent evaluation and treatment of febrile UTI in a young infant. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Repeat observations after paracetamol because fever alone may explain the tachycardia and lethargy
options.rationale: Although fever contributes to tachycardia, lethargy and poor perfusion in a young infant require immediate sepsis-focused management. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

explanation.summary: Febrile infants younger than 3 months require a low threshold for full septic workup and inpatient management. This infant has lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI, making IV antibiotics and admission essential. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)
key_points: Young infants may deteriorate rapidly despite initially subtle examination findings. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)
Positive nitrites on catheter urine strongly support bacterial UTI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: A well-appearing moment while being comforted does not reliably exclude serious illness in young infants. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

sources.label: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

q.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 14 hours of fever, headache, vomiting, and increasing irritability. His parents say he was well yesterday but became sleepy and complained of pain when the room was bright. He has not had a seizure. He is fully immunised, including Hib and pneumococcal vaccines, and has no medical history. On examination he is drowsy but rousable, with neck stiffness and photophobia. He has a blanching maculopapular viral exanthem over his trunk, but no purpuric rash. His capillary refill is 3 seconds, and he is more tachycardic than expected. The registrar is considering lumbar puncture, but the child looks increasingly unwell. Blood cultures have not yet been taken. His mother says his older sibling had migraine yesterday and wonders if this is the same.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.6 deg C, HR 154 bpm, RR 28/min, BP 94/58 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, neck stiffness, photophobia, no focal neurology, capillary refill 3 s
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately before any antibiotics
options.rationale: This child is systemically unwell with suspected meningitis and possible sepsis, so treatment should not be delayed for lumbar puncture. Stabilisation and early antibiotics take priority. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

options.text: Take blood cultures and start empirical intravenous antibiotics
options.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis with evolving sepsis requires prompt blood cultures and empirical intravenous antibiotics without waiting for LP. The photophobia, neck stiffness, and altered alertness make meningitis more likely than migraine or a simple viral illness. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

options.text: Observe for 2 hours and repeat neurological examination
options.rationale: Observation alone is unsafe in a child with fever, meningism, and reduced conscious level. Delay would increase the risk of missed bacterial meningitis and sepsis. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

options.text: Give oral ibuprofen and discharge with safety-netting
options.rationale: This is not a benign viral syndrome. Drowsiness, neck stiffness, photophobia, and haemodynamic abnormality require urgent hospital treatment, not discharge. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

options.text: Arrange CT brain before deciding on antibiotics
options.rationale: CT is not the first step in an unstable child with suspected meningitis unless there are specific signs of raised intracranial pressure or focal neurology. Antibiotics should not be delayed while seeking imaging. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.

explanation.summary: This child has suspected bacterial meningitis with possible sepsis, so blood cultures and immediate empirical intravenous antibiotics are the correct next step. Lumbar puncture and imaging must not delay treatment when the child is clinically worsening. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.
key_points: Neck stiffness, photophobia, fever, and altered alertness are high-risk features for meningitis. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.
Blood cultures and early IV antibiotics should precede LP if the child is unstable. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.
A viral rash does not exclude serious bacterial infection. Source: RCH Febrile child.
explanation.pearls: When the child is deteriorating, the question is not whether LP is eventually needed - it is whether treatment can safely wait. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.

q.stem: A 14-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department with episodic distress and vomiting for 9 hours. His parents report sudden periods of screaming and drawing his knees to his chest lasting 1 to 2 minutes, followed by lethargy and apparent exhaustion. Earlier today he tolerated small sips of fluid but has since vomited twice, most recently with bilious content. He had a mild viral upper respiratory illness 4 days ago. There is no history of trauma or previous abdominal surgery. On arrival he appears pale and intermittently drowsy between episodes of pain. During examination he becomes irritable and inconsolable before settling again. A junior doctor suggests acute gastroenteritis because there has been one loose stool. Shortly afterwards the child passes a small stool mixed with blood and mucus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, Temp 37.2 C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Intermittent abdominal guarding, mild abdominal distension, palpable sausage-shaped mass in right upper quadrant
data_table.Investigations: VBG lactate 2.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Commence oral rehydration therapy and observe because viral gastroenteritis remains the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: Intermittent severe pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, and red currant jelly stool strongly suggest intussusception rather than uncomplicated gastroenteritis. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Arrange urgent air enema reduction after surgical consultation and initial stabilisation
options.rationale: This child has classic intussusception features. Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic after appropriate resuscitation and surgical involvement. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Request urgent CT abdomen because the palpable abdominal mass suggests malignancy
options.rationale: Ultrasound and air enema are preferred first-line investigations and management tools for suspected intussusception in children. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy because bilious vomiting confirms bowel perforation
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting suggests bowel obstruction but does not alone confirm perforation. Non-operative reduction remains appropriate if no peritonitis or perforation exists. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Gastrointestinal)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone because occult bacteraemia commonly presents with episodic abdominal pain
options.rationale: The episodic nature of pain and red currant jelly stool are more consistent with intussusception than primary invasive bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

explanation.summary: This vignette integrates classic but evolving intussusception findings: episodic colicky pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, sausage-shaped mass, and red currant jelly stool. The appropriate next step is stabilisation followed by air enema reduction with surgical involvement. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)
key_points: Children with intussusception may appear lethargic between painful episodes. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)
Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic in stable patients without perforation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Gastrointestinal)
explanation.pearls: A single loose stool early in illness can mislead clinicians toward gastroenteritis before classic bowel obstruction features emerge. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

q.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with persistent fever for 6 days. He was initially diagnosed with viral infection by a local clinic after developing conjunctival injection and irritability. Over the following days he developed cracked lips, reduced oral intake, swelling of the hands and feet, and a polymorphous truncal rash. Yesterday he began refusing to walk because of discomfort in his feet. His parents returned from Japan 3 weeks ago and are concerned about measles because he received only one MMR vaccine dose. In the emergency department he is miserable and difficult to console but alert. There is no cough or coryza. Cervical lymphadenopathy is present on the left side. Blood cultures collected yesterday by the GP are negative to date. The admitting doctor debates whether to wait another 48 hours because the platelet count is not yet elevated.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.4 C, HR 148 bpm, RR 26/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cracked lips, oedematous hands and feet, diffuse rash
data_table.Investigations: CRP 132 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, platelets 390 x10^9/L

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Continue supportive management because thrombocytosis has not yet developed and diagnosis remains uncertain
options.rationale: Thrombocytosis often develops later in Kawasaki disease and treatment should not be delayed when clinical criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin with echocardiography assessment
options.rationale: This child meets criteria for Kawasaki disease with prolonged fever and classic mucocutaneous findings. Early IVIG reduces coronary artery complication risk. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Isolate and commence vitamin A because incomplete measles vaccination makes measles the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: Measles typically presents with cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, whereas this child has classic Kawasaki mucocutaneous and extremity findings. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because persistent fever with cervical lymphadenopathy suggests occult sepsis
options.rationale: The combination of conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity swelling, rash, and prolonged fever is far more consistent with Kawasaki disease than bacterial sepsis. (Source: American Heart Association Kawasaki Guideline)

options.text: Delay treatment until coronary artery abnormalities are confirmed on echocardiography
options.rationale: Treatment is guided by clinical diagnosis and should occur promptly to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk rather than waiting for echocardiographic changes. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: This child has classic Kawasaki disease with prolonged fever, conjunctivitis, mucosal changes, rash, extremity changes, and lymphadenopathy. The challenge is resisting delay due to misleading alternative explanations and absence of late thrombocytosis. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)
key_points: IVIG reduces risk of coronary artery aneurysm when given early. (Source: American Heart Association Kawasaki Guideline)
Platelet elevation often appears later in illness. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)
explanation.pearls: Extreme irritability is a common but under-recognised clue to Kawasaki disease in young children. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

q.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought in after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. He has had 4 days of cough, fever, and reduced appetite, and his parents have been encouraging large volumes of water because he 'did not want to drink milk'. He has been more sleepy since morning and vomited once before the seizure. He takes no regular medicines and has no chronic illness. On arrival he is postictal but arousable. He is not meningitic and has no focal neurology. There is no head trauma. His chest examination suggests a lower respiratory infection, but he has no wheeze. The registrar is concerned about an electrolyte cause of the seizure and wants the best immediate management step.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.5 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 98/62 mmHg, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Examination: Postictal, no focal deficit, no meningism, chest crackles at the right base
data_table.Investigations: Serum sodium 119 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, glucose 5.6 mmol/L, urea 3.2 mmol/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medicines; no known drug allergies

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate management?**

options.text: Restrict fluids and repeat sodium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Fluid restriction may be part of later management depending on the cause, but a child who has seized from severe hyponatraemia needs urgent active correction. Waiting risks further seizures or cerebral oedema. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Give 3% hypertonic saline for symptomatic hyponatraemia
options.rationale: This child has symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with a seizure, so urgent hypertonic saline is indicated. The concurrent respiratory infection and excess free-water intake support a dilutional mechanism, but seizure treatment comes first. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Start oral sodium chloride and observe on the ward
options.rationale: Oral salt is too slow for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizure. This is an emergency requiring controlled intravenous correction. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Treat as febrile seizure and discharge once alert
options.rationale: A febrile seizure does not explain a sodium of 119 mmol/L. The biochemical abnormality is the key driver of immediate management. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

options.text: Administer desmopressin to reduce urinary water loss
options.rationale: Desmopressin would worsen water retention in this context. It is not the treatment for symptomatic hyponatraemic seizure. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

explanation.summary: This child has symptomatic severe hyponatraemia presenting with a seizure, so urgent hypertonic saline is the correct immediate treatment. The respiratory infection and excess free-water intake provide a plausible cause, but they do not change the emergency response. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
key_points: Seizure is a symptom of dangerous hyponatraemia and requires immediate treatment. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
Hypertonic saline is used for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
A febrile illness may be the trigger, but the sodium level is what mandates urgent correction. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.
explanation.pearls: In a child with both fever and a low sodium, do not stop at the fever diagnosis if the sodium is severe enough to explain the seizure. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

q.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with worsening abdominal pain and a rash over 4 days. One week earlier he had a mild upper respiratory illness that resolved spontaneously. His parents initially noticed small red-purple spots over his ankles and buttocks, followed by swelling around both knees. Today he developed intermittent crampy abdominal pain severe enough to wake him overnight and has vomited twice. He has no diarrhoea. A junior doctor considers appendicitis because the pain localises intermittently to the right lower quadrant. On examination he is uncomfortable but alert. The rash is palpable and non-blanching. His knees and ankles are mildly swollen but not erythematous. Urinalysis performed at triage demonstrates new microscopic haematuria and proteinuria.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.1 C, HR 118 bpm, BP 126/82 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura over lower limbs and buttocks, mild periarticular swelling, diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis positive blood and protein, creatinine 74 micromol/L, platelets 338 x10^9/L

q.lead_in: **Which finding most strongly indicates need for closer inpatient monitoring in this child?**

options.text: Recent upper respiratory infection preceding the onset of rash and joint symptoms
options.rationale: A preceding viral illness is common in IgA vasculitis and does not itself indicate severe disease or need for admission. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Palpable purpura involving the buttocks and extensor surfaces of the lower limbs
options.rationale: This is characteristic of IgA vasculitis but is not by itself a marker of severe disease requiring closer monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Microscopic haematuria with elevated blood pressure despite preserved creatinine
options.rationale: Renal involvement with hypertension increases concern for significant nephritis and requires closer monitoring and follow-up even if creatinine is initially normal. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Mild periarticular swelling affecting both knees and ankles without erythema
options.rationale: Transient arthralgia and periarticular swelling are common and usually self-limited manifestations of IgA vasculitis. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Intermittent crampy abdominal pain associated with two episodes of vomiting
options.rationale: Abdominal pain is common in IgA vasculitis, although severe or persistent pain may require investigation for intussusception. The renal findings are more concerning here. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

explanation.summary: This child has IgA vasculitis with classic purpura, abdominal pain, and arthralgia. The discriminating feature is renal involvement with hypertension, which predicts greater risk of clinically significant nephritis and warrants closer inpatient monitoring and follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
key_points: Renal involvement determines long-term prognosis in IgA vasculitis. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guideline)
Hypertension in children with IgA vasculitis should not be dismissed even with normal creatinine. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
explanation.pearls: Abdominal pain in IgA vasculitis may precede or overshadow the rash, leading to mistaken surgical diagnoses. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

q.stem: A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 5 hours ago. He initially felt nauseated and vomited once. Earlier in the week he had mild groin discomfort after football training but remained otherwise well. He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, or recent sexual activity. On arrival he walks hunched over and appears distressed. Examination shows a swollen tender left hemiscrotum with the testis lying slightly higher than the right. Cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. Urinalysis is negative for leukocytes and nitrites. The emergency department is busy and ultrasound availability is delayed for several hours. A resident suggests treating presumed epididymitis because the patient is afebrile and there is no abdominal pain.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 C, HR 112 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 18/min
data_table.Examination: High-riding tender left testis, absent left cremasteric reflex, swollen hemiscrotum
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative for infection markers

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Commence oral antibiotics for epididymitis and arrange outpatient ultrasound review
options.rationale: Sudden severe pain with absent cremasteric reflex and high-riding testis strongly suggests torsion, which requires urgent surgical management rather than outpatient treatment. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Arrange urgent scrotal ultrasound before referral because imaging confirmation is required prior to surgery
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay surgical review when clinical suspicion for torsion is high because testicular viability declines with time. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Refer immediately for urgent surgical exploration of the scrotum
options.rationale: This presentation is highly concerning for testicular torsion. Immediate surgical exploration is indicated and should not be delayed for imaging. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion and discharge if the pain improves significantly afterwards
options.rationale: Manual detorsion may occasionally be attempted by experienced clinicians but does not replace urgent definitive surgical exploration and fixation. (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology Guideline)

options.text: Observe with serial examinations because the absence of fever reduces the likelihood of surgical pathology
options.rationale: Lack of fever does not reduce concern for torsion. Delayed treatment risks irreversible testicular ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

explanation.summary: The challenge is prioritising time-critical surgical management over diagnostic certainty. Sudden severe unilateral scrotal pain, absent cremasteric reflex, and high-riding testis strongly support torsion and warrant immediate exploration. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)
key_points: Ultrasound should not delay surgery in clinically likely torsion. (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology Guideline)
Intermittent prior discomfort may reflect intermittent torsion before complete torsion occurs. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)
explanation.pearls: A normal urinalysis helps argue against epididymitis but does not itself diagnose torsion. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

q.stem: An 11-year-old girl is transferred to a regional paediatric centre with newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis. She had 3 weeks of polyuria, nocturia, and weight loss followed by 24 hours of vomiting and abdominal pain. Initial management at a peripheral hospital included intravenous fluids and insulin infusion. Four hours after transfer she becomes increasingly irritable and complains of severe frontal headache. Her mother reports she seems slower to answer questions than earlier. The admitting resident notes intermittent confusion and asks whether the deterioration is likely due to persistent acidosis because the repeat glucose has improved substantially. Examination reveals reduced interaction and new urinary incontinence. There are no focal limb deficits. The insulin infusion remains running.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 64 bpm, BP 132/84 mmHg, RR 18/min with shallow respirations, Temp 36.7 C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, GCS 13, mild confusion, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: Initial pH 7.08, ketones 6.4 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L; repeat glucose 12 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: IV insulin infusion and isotonic fluids

q.lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?**

options.text: Increase insulin infusion rate because persistent ketosis is the most likely explanation for neurological deterioration
options.rationale: Neurological deterioration with headache, bradycardia, hypertension, and reduced consciousness during DKA treatment raises concern for cerebral oedema rather than inadequate insulin therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently involve intensive care for suspected cerebral oedema
options.rationale: This child has concerning features of cerebral oedema complicating DKA treatment. Immediate hyperosmolar therapy and escalation of care are required. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Continue current therapy and repeat venous blood gas in two hours before altering management
options.rationale: Delaying treatment risks progression of cerebral oedema and neurological injury. Clinical deterioration should prompt immediate action. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum antibiotics because occult meningitis commonly presents during DKA treatment
options.rationale: Although infection may precipitate DKA, the temporal deterioration during treatment with Cushing-type physiology strongly suggests cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture because headache and confusion indicate possible central nervous system infection
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is inappropriate in suspected raised intracranial pressure and would delay urgent treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: The key challenge is recognising cerebral oedema complicating DKA management. Headache, reduced consciousness, bradycardia, hypertension, and neurological decline during treatment are highly concerning and require immediate hyperosmolar therapy and ICU escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
key_points: Cerebral oedema is a feared complication of paediatric DKA treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)
Clinical deterioration should be treated immediately rather than waiting for imaging confirmation. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
explanation.pearls: Improving glucose levels do not exclude worsening intracranial pathology during DKA treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

q.stem: An 18-day-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by his parents who report that he has felt hot to touch for the past 6 hours and has been feeding less vigorously. He was born at 39+3 weeks gestation by normal vaginal delivery with no maternal Group B Streptococcus detected on screening at 36 weeks. The membranes ruptured 4 hours prior to delivery, and there were no maternal risk factors for sepsis. He has been well since birth and is exclusively breastfed. On examination, the infant is quiet but responsive with a weak cry. His axillary temperature is 38.1°C (confirmed with rectal temperature 38.3°C). His HR is 175 bpm, RR is 48, and SpO2 is 97% on room air. His capillary refill time is 3 seconds centrally, and his peripheries feel cool. The anterior fontanelle is flat. He has mild subcostal recession but clear breath sounds. He has no rash and no focus of infection on examination of the ears, throat, chest, and abdomen. His birth weight was 3.4 kg, and his weight today is 3.6 kg. The parents report that his 2-year-old sibling had an upper respiratory tract infection last week.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.3°C rectal, HR 175 bpm, RR 48, SpO2 97% RA, CRT 3 sec centrally, peripheries cool
data_table.Examination: Quiet, weak cry, flat fontanelle, mild subcostal recession, clear breath sounds, no rash, no focal infection identified
data_table.Investigations: Birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 3.6 kg; maternal GBS screen negative at 36 weeks; ROM 4 hrs prior to delivery

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Perform a full septic workup (FBE, CRP, blood culture, urine culture via catheter, and lumbar puncture for CSF), commence IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg 12-hourly and IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg 36-hourly, and admit to hospital
options.rationale: Febrile neonates ≤28 days of corrected age require a full septic workup including FBE, CRP, blood, urine and CSF cultures (lumbar puncture), and empiric IV antibiotics regardless of clinical appearance. The recommended empiric regimen is IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin, which covers Group B Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes. All febrile neonates should be admitted. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Perform a partial septic workup (FBE, CRP, blood culture, urine culture) without lumbar puncture as the infant appears relatively well and the fontanelle is flat, then commence IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg 12-hourly and admit
options.rationale: For febrile neonates ≤28 days, a FULL septic workup INCLUDING lumbar puncture is mandatory regardless of clinical appearance. A flat fontanelle does not exclude meningitis in this age group. Cefotaxime alone does not provide adequate Listeria cover, which is essential in neonates. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Observe the infant in the emergency department short stay unit for 4 hours with regular observations, and if the temperature normalises and feeding improves, discharge home with a follow-up appointment with the general practitioner in 24 hours
options.rationale: Febrile neonates ≤28 days cannot be managed with observation and discharge. The risk of serious bacterial infection (SBI) in this age group is 10-15%, including bacteraemia, meningitis, and UTI. All febrile neonates require a full septic workup, empiric IV antibiotics, and hospital admission. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Collect blood and urine cultures, administer a single dose of IM ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg, and admit the infant to the paediatric ward for 48 hours of observation; perform a lumbar puncture only if the infant deteriorates
options.rationale: A single dose of IM antibiotics is insufficient; IV antibiotics should be continued until cultures are negative at 48 hours. Ceftriaxone alone does not cover Listeria and is generally avoided in neonates due to bilirubin displacement and calcium precipitation risks. Lumbar puncture should be performed as part of the initial workup, not deferred. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture, commence IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily and IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg 8-hourly, and admit the infant under the paediatric team with contact precautions for possible viral meningitis
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is not the first-line antibiotic for neonatal sepsis (ampicillin plus gentamicin is preferred, covering Listeria). Acyclovir should be added if there is clinical suspicion of HSV infection (maternal history, vesicles, seizures, or CSF pleocytosis), but is not routinely indicated for all febrile neonates. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)

explanation.summary: This 18-day-old febrile neonate requires a full septic workup including lumbar puncture, and empiric IV antibiotics (ampicillin plus gentamicin) with hospital admission. Febrile neonates ≤28 days are at high risk (10-15%) of serious bacterial infection, and clinical examination cannot reliably exclude meningitis or bacteraemia. The recommended empiric antibiotic regimen in Australia is IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin, which covers GBS, E. coli, and Listeria. Ceftriaxone is generally avoided in neonates due to risks of bilirubin displacement, and acyclovir is reserved for suspected HSV.
key_points: All febrile neonates ≤28 days corrected age require: FBE, CRP, blood culture, urine culture, lumbar puncture (CSF), ± CXR, and empiric IV antibiotics. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

Recommended empiric antibiotic regimen: IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg 12-hourly PLUS IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg 36-hourly (dosing interval depends on gestational and postnatal age). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)
The appearance of the neonate does NOT alter the need for full septic workup; even well-appearing febrile neonates have a 10-15% risk of SBI. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)
Ceftriaxone is generally avoided in neonates due to bilirubin-albumin displacement (risk of kernicterus) and calcium-ceftriaxone precipitation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: In febrile neonates, a normal CRP, WCC, or chest X-ray does NOT exclude serious bacterial infection. CSF is mandatory. The most dangerous mistake is deferring the lumbar puncture because the infant 'looks well'.

q.stem: A 7-year-old boy with known moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation. He has had a coryzal illness for 3 days, and his asthma has been worsening despite increasing his fluticasone propionate preventer to 250 mcg BD and using salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI and spacer every 2-3 hours. On arrival, he is unable to complete sentences in one breath, speaking only 2-3 words at a time. He is sitting upright with marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, and audible expiratory wheeze. His RR is 42, HR is 135 bpm, and SpO2 is 90% on room air. His PEFR is unmeasurable due to poor technique. He has received salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI and spacer from his mother immediately prior to arrival. His usual medications are fluticasone propionate 125 mcg BD and salbutamol 2 puffs PRN. He has no known drug allergies and his immunisations are up to date including the annual influenza vaccine.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 42, HR 135 bpm, SpO2 90% RA, Temp 37.2°C, BP 105/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Unable to complete sentences (2-3 words), sitting upright, marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, audible expiratory wheeze, PEFR unmeasurable
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed at this stage
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone propionate 125 mcg BD (usual); Salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI + spacer given by mother prior to arrival

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the emergency department?

options.text: Administer salbutamol 12 puffs (100 mcg/puff) via MDI and spacer with tidal breathing, add ipratropium bromide 4 puffs (21 mcg/puff) via MDI and spacer, and give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately
options.rationale: While MDI and spacer is an acceptable delivery method, this child has severe/life-threatening asthma (SpO2 90%, unable to speak, marked recession). He requires OXYGEN-DRIVEN nebulised bronchodilators to ensure adequate drug delivery while correcting hypoxaemia. MDI and spacer may be insufficient in severe cases. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Commence oxygen via Hudson mask at 8 L/min to target SpO2 >94%, administer nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium bromide 250 mcg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, and repeat salbutamol nebulisers every 20 minutes for the first hour
options.rationale: This child has severe acute asthma (SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession). Management includes: oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol (5 mg for >5 years) plus ipratropium (250 mcg) for the first hour (3 doses at 20-minute intervals), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg). Continuous clinical assessment is essential. If there is inadequate response after the first hour, escalate to IV magnesium sulphate, IV salbutamol, or aminophylline, and involve ICU. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal prong oxygen at 2 L/kg/min, administer IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, and arrange an urgent chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia before commencing bronchodilator therapy
options.rationale: IV hydrocortisone is an alternative to oral prednisolone but is not superior. Bronchodilator therapy should NOT be delayed for investigations such as chest X-ray, which is not routinely indicated in acute asthma. HFNP oxygen may be used but conventional oxygen with nebulised bronchodilators is the first-line approach. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes as first-line therapy, followed by oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, and commence oxygen via nasal prongs at 2 L/min to maintain SpO2 >92%
options.rationale: IV magnesium sulphate is a SECOND-LINE therapy for severe asthma that has not responded to initial inhaled bronchodilator therapy. It should not be used as first-line. Inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium with oral corticosteroids are the first-line agents. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg alone via oxygen-driven nebuliser, give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, and observe for 1 hour; if no improvement, add ipratropium and consider IV therapy
options.rationale: The salbutamol dose of 2.5 mg is appropriate for children 2-5 years, not for a 7-year-old who requires 5 mg. Additionally, ipratropium should be ADDED from the start in severe exacerbations, not deferred until after an observation period. Combined salbutamol and ipratropium therapy in the first hour reduces hospitalisation rates in severe paediatric asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

explanation.summary: This 7-year-old has acute severe (bordering on life-threatening) asthma: SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession, and audible wheeze. The immediate management priorities are: oxygen to target SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg repeated every 20 minutes for the first hour (3 doses), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. Continuous assessment of response is essential. If inadequate response after the first hour, escalation to IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg, IV salbutamol infusion, or aminophylline infusion, and ICU consultation is warranted. Chest X-ray is not routinely indicated.
key_points: Classification of acute asthma severity: mild (SpO2 >92%, sentences), moderate (SpO2 >92%, phrases), severe (SpO2 <92%, words, recession), life-threatening (SpO2 <92% with silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion). (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

First-line for severe asthma: oxygen, nebulised salbutamol (5 mg if >5 yrs, 2.5 mg if 2-5 yrs) PLUS ipratropium 250 mcg, repeated every 20 min for first hour, and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Ipratropium added to salbutamol in the first hour significantly reduces hospitalisation in children with moderate-severe asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
