explanation.pearls: In severe paediatric asthma, a silent chest is more dangerous than a wheezy chest. A silent chest with SpO2 <90% is life-threatening asthma - call a code blue, prepare for intubation, and give IV salbutamol and magnesium.

q.stem: An 8-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss, and a 24-hour history of vomiting, abdominal pain, and drowsiness. On examination she is thin, dehydrated, and responding only to pain. Her GCS is 11 (E2 V3 M6). She has Kussmaul breathing with a respiratory rate of 36 breaths/minute. Her heart rate is 140 bpm, blood pressure 90/55 mmHg, capillary refill time 3 seconds, and mucous membranes are very dry. Her weight is 24 kg (estimated pre-illness weight 28 kg). Venous blood gas shows pH 7.05, pCO₂ 18 mmHg, bicarbonate 5 mmol/L. Blood glucose is 28 mmol/L, ketones >6 mmol/L, sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 5.2 mmol/L, urea 10.5 mmol/L, creatinine 90 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 36 (Kussmaul), GCS 11, CRT 3 sec, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Thin, severe dehydration (~14% weight loss), drowsy, Kussmaul breathing, very dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.05, pCO₂ 18, HCO₃⁻ 5; Glucose 28 mmol/L; Ketones >6 mmol/L; Na⁺ 130, K⁺ 5.2, Urea 10.5, Cr 90

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial intravenous fluid regimen?

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride over 15 minutes, repeat once if shock persists, then start rehydration at 10 mL/kg/hour with 0.45% sodium chloride and added potassium
options.rationale: A 0.45% sodium chloride solution is NOT used in the initial rehydration of paediatric DKA due to the risk of rapid osmolar shifts and cerebral oedema. The recommended fluid is 0.9% sodium chloride with added potassium after the initial bolus. The rehydration rate should be calculated to correct the deficit evenly over 48 hours, not rapidly. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD Guidelines)

options.text: Give a 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 minutes, then calculate the deficit (approximately 14% x 28 kg ≈ 4 L) and replace 50% of deficit plus maintenance over the first 24 hours, 50% over the next 24 hours, using 0.9% sodium chloride with 40 mmol/L potassium chloride once urine output is confirmed
options.rationale: Paediatric DKA management prioritises slow, controlled rehydration to minimise the risk of cerebral oedema. RCH and ISPAD recommend an initial 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 30-60 minutes (repeat once if shock). Total fluid deficit is replaced evenly over 48 hours: half the deficit plus maintenance in the first 24 hours, remainder over the next 24 hours. Fluids are 0.9% sodium chloride (NOT hypotonic) with added potassium once UOP confirmed. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD Guidelines 2022)

options.text: Administer a rapid 40 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride over 1 hour to restore circulating volume, then calculate deficit and replace over 24 hours with 0.9% sodium chloride
options.rationale: Rapid large-volume boluses increase the risk of cerebral oedema, the most feared complication of paediatric DKA. Boluses should be limited to 10-20 mL/kg, given slowly, and only for shock. Deficit replacement must be over 48 hours, not 24 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Commence an IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately before any fluid bolus, and give maintenance fluids with 0.9% sodium chloride and 5% dextrose
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation should begin BEFORE insulin. Insulin administration in a severely dehydrated and acidotic child without prior volume expansion can cause cardiovascular collapse. Additionally, insulin should not be started within the first hour of fluid resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Start intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate (40 mL/hour) plus a 10 mL/kg bolus, and commence IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to rapidly correct the severe acidosis
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in paediatric DKA. It does not improve outcomes and may paradoxically worsen intracellular acidosis and increase the risk of cerebral oedema. Bicarbonate administration is reserved for life-threatening hyperkalaemia or severe acidosis (pH <6.9) with cardiovascular compromise, and only in consultation with a specialist. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: This child has severe DKA (pH 7.05, bicarbonate 5, ketones >6, GCS 11) with ~14% dehydration. Paediatric DKA fluid management focuses on preventing cerebral oedema. The recommended regimen: initial 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus over 30-60 minutes (repeat once if shocked). The total deficit is calculated and replaced evenly over 48 hours (50% deficit + maintenance in first 24h, 50% in second 24h) using 0.9% NaCl with added potassium (40 mmol/L) once urine output is confirmed. Insulin is commenced 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation begins. Bicarbonate is not routinely used.
key_points: Paediatric DKA rehydration: replace deficit evenly over 48 hours using 0.9% NaCl, to minimise cerebral oedema risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD Guidelines)
Initial fluid bolus: 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl over 30-60 min, repeat once if shock. Avoid rapid large-volume boluses. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
Insulin infusion (0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr) is started 1-2 hours after commencing fluids. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
Sodium bicarbonate is NOT routinely recommended in DKA; it increases the risk of cerebral oedema. (Source: ISPAD Guidelines 2022)
Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of death in paediatric DKA. Warning signs: headache, bradycardia, hypertension, altered consciousness. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)
explanation.pearls: In paediatric DKA, 'slow and steady wins the race': cerebral oedema typically occurs 4-12 hours after treatment begins, not at presentation. The fluid calculation should be meticulous, and neurological observations (including GCS) should be performed hourly.

q.stem: A 2-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of high fever, irritability, and vomiting. Her parents report she has been difficult to rouse for the past hour and has developed a new rash on her legs that does not fade when pressed with a glass. On examination she is drowsy, responding only to painful stimuli (GCS 9). She is tachycardic at 170 bpm, hypotensive with BP 70/40 mmHg, capillary refill time 5 seconds, and peripheries are cold and mottled. There is a purpuric rash over her lower limbs and buttocks, with several lesions >2 mm. She has neck stiffness but no photophobia. Her temperature is 39.8°C. She is fully immunised (including meningococcal B and ACWY vaccines as per Australian schedule).

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.8°C, HR 170 bpm, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR 40, SpO₂ 94%, GCS 9, CRT 5 sec, cold mottled peripheries
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, purpuric rash lower limbs/buttocks (non-blanching, >2 mm), neck stiffness, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: None performed yet
data_table.Medications: Fully immunised including MenB and MenACWY

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture and blood cultures before antibiotics, then commence IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg 4-hourly
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamically unstable patients with suspected meningococcal disease. It can precipitate cerebral herniation and delays life-saving antibiotics. Antibiotics must be given immediately, not after LP. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

options.text: Administer IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 2 g) immediately, take blood cultures, and commence IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus repeated as needed
options.rationale: Suspected meningococcal septicaemia with shock is a medical emergency. Immediate actions: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg, max 2 g, or cefotaxime 50 mg/kg) within minutes of recognition, blood cultures (ideally before antibiotics but do not delay), and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus, repeat up to 60 mL/kg). LP is deferred. The patient must be isolated with droplet precautions. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

options.text: Obtain an urgent CT brain to exclude cerebral oedema before lumbar puncture, then perform LP and start IV antibiotics
options.rationale: CT brain before LP is indicated if there are focal neurological signs, papilloedema, or GCS <9 with concerns about raised ICP, but in this case the immediate priority is antibiotics and resuscitation, not neuroimaging. The child is in septic shock; antibiotics must not be delayed. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg 20 minutes before antibiotics, then give IV ceftriaxone and IV fluids, to reduce the risk of neurological sequelae
options.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone is indicated in bacterial meningitis (especially Hib and pneumococcal) to reduce hearing loss and neurological sequelae, but it must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics. However, in meningococcal septicaemia with shock, the priority is rapid antibiotics and fluids. Dexamethasone use is more established for Hib and pneumococcal meningitis, and its benefit in meningococcal meningitis is less certain. Moreover, in this scenario, the child is in extremis; antibiotics should not be delayed by waiting 20 minutes for dexamethasone. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

options.text: Notify the public health unit and await their advice before administering antibiotics, as meningococcal disease is a notifiable condition requiring public health intervention
options.rationale: While meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable, notification must NOT delay treatment. Antibiotics should be given immediately. Notification and contact tracing occur concurrently. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; CDNA National Guidelines)

explanation.summary: This child has meningococcal septicaemia with septic shock: purpuric rash, fever, shock (tachycardia, hypotension, prolonged CRT, mottled skin), and reduced consciousness. The priority is immediate IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg) and aggressive fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl boluses). Blood cultures should be taken but must not delay antibiotics. LP and neuroimaging are deferred in haemodynamically unstable patients. Dexamethasone may be considered for bacterial meningitis but is secondary to resuscitation. Notification occurs concurrently.
key_points: Meningococcal septicaemia with shock: give IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 2 g) immediately, within minutes of recognition. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)
Fluid resuscitation: 20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus, repeat up to 60 mL/kg, with early involvement of ICU. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)
LP is contraindicated in shock, raised ICP, coagulopathy, or respiratory compromise. Do not delay antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)
Meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable to the local public health unit. Prophylaxis (rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) is recommended for close contacts. (Source: CDNA Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: The glass test (tumbler test) is a useful bedside tool: press a clear glass against the rash; if it does not blanch, it is purpuric and meningococcal disease should be considered. However, a positive test is not required for action - treat on clinical suspicion.

q.subtopic: BRUE – Risk stratification and admission criteria
q.stem: A 2-week-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his parents witnessed him stop breathing, become pale, and go limp while sleeping. The episode lasted approximately 20 seconds and resolved spontaneously when his mother picked him up and rubbed his back. He had been feeding well prior to the event and has not had any similar episodes. He was born at 35+2 weeks gestation and spent 1 week in the special care nursery for feeding difficulties and mild respiratory distress, from which he recovered fully. He has been home for 1 week. On examination, he is alert, active, and feeding normally. His observations are: temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 140 bpm, respiratory rate 38, SpO₂ 98% on room air. There are no dysmorphic features, and the neurological examination is normal. His weight is 2.6 kg (birth weight 2.5 kg). The parents are understandably anxious and have another child at home. They have a video baby monitor and have read about SIDS. The infant is currently in the emergency department short stay unit and has been observed for 2 hours with no further events.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8°C, HR 140 bpm, RR 38, SpO₂ 98% RA, BP 70/40 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert, active, normal neurology, no dysmorphism, well hydrated, feeding normally
data_table.Investigations: None performed yet. Parents describe a 20-second apnoeic, pale, limp episode resolved spontaneously with tactile stimulation.

q.lead_in: According to Australian guidelines, what is the most appropriate disposition for this infant?

options.text: Discharge home with a home apnoea monitor, education on safe sleeping and CPR, and arrange a next-day follow-up with the GP and maternal child health nurse
options.rationale: This infant is HIGH RISK for a recurrent event or serious underlying condition: age <60 days, born preterm (35+2 weeks), and the event was a brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE). Home apnoea monitors are not recommended for BRUE as they do not reduce SIDS risk and can increase parental anxiety. High-risk infants should be admitted for observation. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent Life Threatening Event / BRUE; AAP BRUE Guideline)

options.text: Admit to hospital for cardiorespiratory monitoring for a minimum of 24 hours, with a 12-lead ECG, and consider pertussis PCR and other investigations only if clinically indicated
options.rationale: This infant meets high-risk BRUE criteria: age <60 days, born preterm (<37 weeks), and the event required tactile stimulation to terminate. Current guidelines recommend admission for observation and cardiorespiratory monitoring for at least 24 hours, a 12-lead ECG to exclude prolonged QT, and consideration of pertussis PCR (given age and potential for apnoeic presentation). Extensive investigations (EEG, neuroimaging, pH study) are not routinely indicated without specific clinical clues. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent Life Threatening Event; AAP BRUE Guideline 2016)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture, commence empiric IV antibiotics, and admit to the neonatal unit for a minimum 48 hours of observation
options.rationale: A full septic workup is indicated if there are clinical signs of sepsis (fever, hypothermia, poor feeding, lethargy) or maternal risk factors. This infant is well-appearing with normal vital signs and no signs of infection. Routine full sepsis evaluation is not indicated for all BRUE presentations, only when sepsis is suspected. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Arrange an urgent outpatient polysomnography (sleep study) and reflux study, and discharge home with strict return precautions and a nasal cannula oxygen setup
options.rationale: Polysomnography is not a first-line investigation for BRUE. It may be considered in recurrent or severe events, but this is a single event in a preterm infant. Discharging a high-risk BRUE with home oxygen is not standard and could be dangerous without an identified cause. Admission is required. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Refer to child protection services immediately as a BRUE in a preterm infant is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury, and admit for a skeletal survey and ophthalmology review
options.rationale: While child protection concerns should always be considered, a single BRUE in a preterm infant with a plausible history does not automatically require a child protection referral. The event occurred during sleep and was witnessed by both parents, with no concerning examination findings. A child protection consult would be considered if there were unexplained injuries, inconsistent history, or recurrent events. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

explanation.summary: This preterm (35+2 weeks), 2-week-old infant has experienced a BRUE (brief resolved unexplained event) characterised by apnoea, pallor, and limpness requiring tactile stimulation. He is classified as HIGH RISK because of age <60 days, prematurity, and the need for intervention. Current RCH and AAP guidelines recommend admission for cardiorespiratory monitoring (minimum 24 hours), a 12-lead ECG (to exclude prolonged QT syndrome), and consideration of pertussis PCR (apnoea is a known presentation in neonates). Extensive investigations are guided by clinical suspicion, not routine. Home monitoring is not recommended. Child protection referral is not automatic but should be considered if red flags are present.
key_points: BRUE definition: brief (<1 min) apnoea, colour change, altered tone, or choking, with full recovery to baseline. (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline 2016)
HIGH-risk features: age <60 days, preterm <37 weeks, multiple events, CPR required, abnormal examination. These warrant admission and monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE; AAP Guideline)

Recommended investigations for high-risk BRUE: 12-lead ECG (prolonged QT), pertussis PCR (if respiratory symptoms), and observation for at least 24 hours. EEG, neuroimaging, and reflux studies are not routine. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)
Home apnoea monitors are NOT recommended; they do not reduce SIDS risk and may increase parental anxiety. (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The term 'ALTE' has been replaced by 'BRUE' to better reflect the benign nature of most events when a cause is not identified. However, the 'U' stands for 'Unexplained' - always ensure the event is truly unexplained before applying the BRUE label.

q.subtopic: Neonatal sepsis – Early-onset, risk factors and timing
q.stem: A term infant is born at 39+2 weeks by vaginal delivery to a mother who had a temperature of 38.2°C during labour, with ruptured membranes for 20 hours. GBS screening at 36 weeks was negative. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (benzylpenicillin) was commenced only 2 hours before delivery. The infant appears well at birth, Apgars 9 and 9, and has remained asymptomatic for 6 hours in the postnatal ward. What is the most appropriate management plan?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, RR 45, Temp 36.9°C, SpO₂ 98%
data_table.Examination: Alert, normal tone, no respiratory distress, normal perfusion
data_table.Investigations: Maternal temp 38.2°C, ROM 20 hr, GBS negative, IAP given 2 hr before delivery

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan for this infant?

options.text: Observe in hospital for 24-48 hours with regular clinical assessments; no antibiotics or bloods required
options.rationale: The combination of maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) and prolonged ROM constitutes risk factors for early-onset sepsis, even if GBS is negative. A more conservative approach is indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Perform a full septic workup including blood culture, FBE, CRP, and lumbar puncture, and commence IV antibiotics
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is not routine for early-onset sepsis in well-appearing infants without meningeal signs. Blood culture and CRP are appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Perform blood culture, FBE, and CRP at 6-12 hours of age; if CRP is normal and cultures negative at 48 hours, discontinue antibiotics if started, with close observation
options.rationale: This is reasonable, but the presence of maternal fever ≥38°C and prolonged ROM even with negative GBS increases the risk of non-GBS organisms. Many guidelines recommend empiric antibiotics until cultures are negative. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin empirically after taking blood culture and CRP, monitor clinically, and review at 36-48 hours; discontinue antibiotics if cultures negative, CRP normal, and infant remains well
options.rationale: Maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) is a major risk factor for neonatal sepsis, independent of GBS status. The incomplete IAP duration (<4 hours) further increases risk. The RCH guideline recommends empiric antibiotics for infants with one or more major risk factors. Blood culture, CRP, and a septic screen (excluding LP if asymptomatic) are performed, and antibiotics continued until cultures are negative for 48 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

options.text: Observe for 12 hours; if the infant remains well, discharge home at 24 hours with an early follow-up by the maternal child health nurse
options.rationale: Early discharge with only observation is not recommended given the maternal fever; the infant is at moderate risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

explanation.summary: The key risk factor here is maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C), which is a major risk for neonatal sepsis, along with prolonged rupture of membranes. Even with negative GBS, the infant should be investigated with blood culture, FBE, CRP, and started on empiric antibiotics (benzylpenicillin + gentamicin) until cultures are negative at 48 hours. Observation alone is insufficient because the risk of non-GBS organisms (e.g., E. coli, other streptococci) is significant. The incomplete IAP (<4 hours prior to delivery) also means the infant did not receive full protection.
key_points: Maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) is an independent risk factor for early-onset neonatal sepsis, requiring investigation and empiric treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Empiric antibiotics: benzylpenicillin (60 mg/kg IV 12-hourly) + gentamicin (5 mg/kg IV 36-hourly) after blood culture. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Lumbar puncture is not routinely indicated in asymptomatic infants unless there is a positive blood culture or clinical suspicion of meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
Observation alone without antibiotics is not recommended when one or more major risk factors are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: Maternal chorioamnionitis (fever plus one other sign) often has polymicrobial origin; always cover with broad-spectrum antibiotics until cultures confirm the organism.

q.subtopic: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura) – Renal involvement management
q.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of a palpable purpuric rash on his lower limbs and buttocks, diffuse colicky abdominal pain, and swelling of both knees and ankles. His mother reports that he had a sore throat 10 days ago. On examination, he is afebrile, HR 90 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg (90th centile for age). The rash is non-blanching and palpable. His abdomen is tender but soft. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein and 2+ blood on dipstick. A spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (UPCR) is 250 mg/mmol (normal <20). Serum creatinine is 45 µmol/L, and albumin is 32 g/L. He has mild periorbital oedema. What is the most appropriate next step in management of his renal involvement?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 (90th centile), HR 90, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpuric rash lower limbs/buttocks, swollen knees and ankles, tender abdomen, mild periorbital oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urine: 3+ protein, 2+ blood; UPCR 250 mg/mmol; serum Cr 45, albumin 32 g/L

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy and possible corticosteroid or immunosuppressive therapy, as he has nephrotic-range proteinuria
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR >200 mg/mmol or >1 g/m²/day) in the setting of HSP indicates severe glomerulonephritis and warrants referral to paediatric nephrology. A renal biopsy is often performed to assess the degree of crescent formation, which guides immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids, cyclophosphamide, or mycophenolate). This is beyond primary care management. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks to treat the nephritis and prevent chronic kidney disease
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone alone has not been shown to prevent or treat established HSP nephritis. Corticosteroids may be used for severe abdominal pain or joint symptoms, but nephrotic-range proteinuria requires nephrology input and possible biopsy before initiating immunosuppression. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)

options.text: Discharge home with advice to monitor urine dipstick weekly for 6 weeks, as most cases of HSP nephritis resolve spontaneously without treatment
options.rationale: While mild urinary abnormalities (microscopic haematuria, mild proteinuria) may be monitored, nephrotic-range proteinuria requires specialist referral and investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)

options.text: Start an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (enalapril) to reduce proteinuria and control blood pressure
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are used for persistent proteinuria in chronic kidney disease, but in acute HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, they are an adjunct, not the primary therapy. Renal biopsy and disease-modifying therapy are the priorities. (Source: KDIGO Guidelines)

options.text: Admit for intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy 30 mg/kg/day for 3 days, as severe HSP nephritis requires immediate high-dose steroids
options.rationale: While IV methylprednisolone may be used after renal biopsy confirms crescentic nephritis, it is not the first step. The next step is nephrology referral and biopsy to guide therapy. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: This child has HSP (IgA vasculitis) with nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR 250 mg/mmol), which is classified as severe nephritis. The appropriate next step is urgent referral to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy. Biopsy guides immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids with cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate) if significant crescent formation is present. Mild cases without nephrotic-range proteinuria can be monitored, but severe cases require specialist care to prevent chronic kidney disease.
key_points: HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria (>200 mg/mmol UPCR) requires paediatric nephrology referral and renal biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura; KDIGO Guidelines)
Renal biopsy is indicated for persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria, renal impairment, or hypertension. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)
Corticosteroids alone do not prevent HSP nephritis; immunosuppression is reserved for biopsy-proven crescentic disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)
Urine dipstick and blood pressure monitoring are essential during and after HSP for early detection of renal involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)
explanation.pearls: In HSP, the severity of the rash does not predict the severity of renal involvement. A child with a few spots can have severe nephritis, while a child with widespread purpura may have normal urine.

q.subtopic: Cystic fibrosis – Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome
q.stem: A 12-year-old girl with known cystic fibrosis (ΔF508 homozygous) presents with a 2-day history of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and bilious vomiting. She has not opened her bowels for 3 days, whereas normally she passes 2-3 loose stools daily despite pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon 10,000 units/kg/day). She had an admission for distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) 2 years ago, which resolved with conservative management. On examination, she is afebrile, HR 100 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg. The abdomen is distended, tympanic to percussion, and tender in the right lower quadrant with a palpable faecal mass. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Erect abdominal X-ray shows faecal loading in the distal ileum and caecum with no free air and no air-fluid levels. A Gastrografin enema performed in the emergency department shows a meconium-like material obstructing the distal ileum without complete obstruction. The surgical team has reviewed and agrees that non-operative management is appropriate.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 22, Temp 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Distended, tender abdomen, palpable faecal mass RLQ, hyperactive bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: AXR: faecal loading distal ileum/caecum, no free air, no air-fluid levels; Gastrografin enema: impacted material in distal ileum without complete obstruction
data_table.Medications: Creon 10,000 U/kg/day, inhaled tobramycin, dornase alfa

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate non-operative therapy for this episode of DIOS?

options.text: Oral polyethylene glycol 3350 (Movicol) 2 sachets/kg/day with increased fluids and continue pancreatic enzymes
options.rationale: Oral PEG is used for chronic constipation in CF, but in acute DIOS, especially with bilious vomiting and palpable mass, more aggressive bowel clearance is required. PEG alone may not clear the impacted material. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal Complications)

options.text: Oral Gastrografin (diatrizoate meglumine) 50 mL diluted in 200 mL of water or juice, followed by intravenous fluids and nasogastric decompression if vomiting persists
options.rationale: Gastrografin is an osmotically active contrast agent that draws fluid into the bowel, hydrating and loosening the inspissated faecal material in DIOS. It is used both as a diagnostic enema and as an oral therapy for non-operative management. The standard dose is 50-100 mL of Gastrografin diluted in water, juice, or soft drink, with close monitoring of fluid balance. IV fluids are given to prevent dehydration from the osmotic shift. If vomiting occurs, an NG tube is placed. This approach avoids surgery in most cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal Complications; CF Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: Nasogastric N-acetylcysteine 10 mL of 20% solution four times daily to break down the mucus plugs
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is sometimes used as an adjunctive agent to thin the tenacious mucus in DIOS, but it is not the primary therapy. Oral Gastrografin is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

options.text: Lactulose 10 mL three times daily and a phosphate enema per rectum to stimulate colonic evacuation
options.rationale: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS, which involves inspissated material in the distal ileum and caecum, not simple constipation. Gastrografin is the specific therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

options.text: Intravenous metoclopramide 0.15 mg/kg 8-hourly and continuous nasogastric suction with nil by mouth for 48 hours
options.rationale: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic but does not resolve the impaction. NG suction may relieve vomiting but does not treat the underlying obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

explanation.summary: Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) in CF is caused by inspissated faecal material and mucus in the distal ileum, presenting with bowel obstruction. Non-operative management with oral Gastrografin (diluted, 50-100 mL) is the cornerstone, creating an osmotic gradient that hydrates the impacted material. This is combined with IV fluids to prevent dehydration, and NG decompression if vomiting. Most episodes resolve without surgery. Polyethylene glycol is used for maintenance but not for acute DIOS. N-acetylcysteine may be used as an adjunct.
key_points: DIOS: acute distal small-bowel obstruction in CF, distinct from chronic constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)
First-line non-operative therapy: oral Gastrografin 50-100 mL diluted, with IV hydration and NG decompression if vomiting. (Source: CF Foundation Guidelines; RCH CPG)
Surgical intervention is reserved for complete obstruction, failed medical management, or perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)
Maintenance therapy to prevent DIOS: adequate hydration, pancreatic enzymes, and regular PEG if needed. (Source: CF Foundation Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Gastrografin is both a diagnostic and therapeutic agent for DIOS. It is hyperosmolar and works by drawing fluid into the bowel lumen – ensure adequate IV fluid replacement.

q.subtopic: Pertussis – Diagnosis and management in a 4-week-old
q.stem: A 4-week-old male infant presents with a 10-day history of cough that began as mild coryza and has progressed to paroxysms of coughing followed by apnoeic pauses and post-tussive vomiting. The cough is non-productive, and he becomes cyanotic during the spells. He was born at term, is fully breastfed, and his mother received the diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis vaccine at 28 weeks gestation. His 2-year-old sibling has had a persistent cough for 3 weeks. On examination, the infant is alert but tachypnoeic with RR 50, SpO₂ 90% during a coughing paroxysm, HR 180 bpm. Between paroxysms, SpO₂ is 97%. Chest auscultation reveals transmitted upper airway sounds but no crackles. There is no fever. A nasopharyngeal aspirate for Bordetella pertussis PCR is positive. Full blood count shows WCC 45 ×10⁹/L with lymphocyte predominance (70%), haemoglobin 140 g/L, platelets 480 ×10⁹/L. The infant is admitted to the paediatric ward. What is the most important aspect of management?

data_table.Vital signs: RR 50, SpO₂ 90% during paroxysm, HR 180, Temp 36.9°C
data_table.Examination: Paroxysmal cough with apnoea and cyanosis, post-tussive vomiting, transmitted upper airway sounds, no crackles
data_table.Investigations: NP aspirate: B. pertussis PCR positive; WCC 45, lymphocytes 70%, Hb 140, Plt 480

q.lead_in: What is the most important aspect of management?

options.text: Commence oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days to reduce infectivity, and manage apnoeic episodes with careful monitoring in hospital
options.rationale: Azithromycin is indicated for pertussis to eliminate the organism and limit spread, but it does not alter the clinical course once the paroxysmal phase has started. The more critical aspect in a young infant with extreme leukocytosis is the risk of pulmonary hypertension and death. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Initiate exchange transfusion or leukapheresis to reduce the white cell count, as extreme leukocytosis (>50 ×10⁹/L) is associated with fatal pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Severe pertussis in infants can cause extreme leukocytosis (WBC >50-100 ×10⁹/L), which is an independent risk factor for pulmonary hypertension and death. Hyperviscosity from lymphocytosis leads to pulmonary vascular obstruction. Exchange transfusion or leukapheresis is recommended to rapidly reduce the WBC count and improve pulmonary blood flow. Supportive care for apnoeas and prevention of secondary infection are important, but the leukocytosis-directed therapy is the critical life-saving intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDC Pertussis Guidelines)

options.text: Commence IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to neutralise pertussis toxin, as the toxin mediates the severe disease
options.rationale: There is no evidence that IVIG alters the course of pertussis. Management is primarily supportive, with antibiotics to limit transmission. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to reduce airway inflammation and prevent apnoeic spells
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not recommended for pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression. They have no proven benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Provide continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) via nasal prongs to splint the airways and reduce apnoea frequency
options.rationale: CPAP may be used for apnoea, but the immediate life-threatening problem is the extreme leukocytosis causing pulmonary hypertension. CPAP does not address the underlying pathology. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

explanation.summary: This 4-week-old infant has confirmed pertussis with extreme leukocytosis (WCC 45, predominantly lymphocytes). In neonates and young infants, severe pertussis can lead to hyperleukocytosis (WCC >30-50 ×10⁹/L), which causes pulmonary vascular leukostasis, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiogenic shock. The most critical intervention is to reduce the white cell count via exchange transfusion or leukapheresis. Azithromycin is given to eliminate Bordetella pertussis and prevent spread but does not alter established disease. Supportive care for apnoeas and nutritional support are essential, but the leukocytosis-directed therapy is life-saving.
key_points: Extreme leukocytosis (WCC >30-50 ×10⁹/L) in infant pertussis is a major risk factor for pulmonary hypertension and mortality. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDC Guidelines)
Exchange transfusion or leukapheresis is recommended to rapidly reduce the WBC count. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is given to eliminate nasopharyngeal carriage and reduce transmission. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
Do not delay ICU referral for severe pertussis; early intubation and ventilation may be required. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
explanation.pearls: In a young infant with pertussis, the white cell count is a prognostic marker. A WCC >30 ×10⁹/L should trigger immediate ICU review and consideration of exchange transfusion.

q.subtopic: Severe atopic dermatitis – Wet dressings and infection
q.stem: A 3-year-old girl with severe atopic dermatitis presents with a 5-day flare that has not responded to her usual topical corticosteroid (mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment twice daily) and moisturisers. Her mother reports she has been scratching intensely and not sleeping. On examination, there is extensive erythema, lichenification, and weeping of the flexural areas of both arms and legs. There are also pustules on the cheeks and perioral area. She is afebrile. The lesions are not vesicular and do not look punched-out. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.1°C, HR 100, RR 22
data_table.Examination: Weeping erythematous flexural eczema, pustules on cheeks and perioral area, no vesicles, no systemic signs
data_table.Investigations: Not performed

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily for secondary bacterial infection, and apply wet dressings with diluted corticosteroids
options.rationale: Pustular lesions on a background of severe eczema suggest secondary Staphylococcus aureus infection (impetiginised eczema). Oral cephalexin or flucloxacillin is indicated. Wet dressings with diluted topical corticosteroid will cool the skin, reduce inflammation, and increase steroid penetration. Eczema herpeticum (viral) should be considered if vesicles, punched-out ulcers, or fever are present, but it is not typical here. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis; Therapeutic Guidelines: Atopic Dermatitis)

options.text: Start oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for suspected eczema herpeticum
options.rationale: Eczema herpeticum presents with monomorphic punched-out vesicles, often with systemic symptoms. This child has pustules, more suggestive of staphylococcal infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema Herpeticum)

options.text: Prescribe a stronger topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment) and add a calcineurin inhibitor
options.rationale: While a short course of stronger topical steroid may be used, the presence of pustules indicates infection requiring antibiotic therapy before escalating steroids. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)

options.text: Refer to dermatology for consideration of oral prednisolone or cyclosporine
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe refractory atopic dermatitis and would be inappropriate before treating an acute bacterial superinfection. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)

options.text: Perform a skin swab for culture and await results before prescribing antibiotics
options.rationale: Swabs can be taken but treatment should not be delayed. Empiric antibiotics should be started immediately. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Atopic Dermatitis)

explanation.summary: This child has a severe flare of atopic dermatitis with secondary bacterial infection (pustules, weeping, lichenification). Appropriate management includes oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (cephalexin or flucloxacillin) and wet dressings with diluted topical corticosteroids to rapidly control inflammation. Eczema herpeticum should be suspected if vesicles or punched-out ulcers, but pustules suggest bacterial infection.
key_points: Impetiginised eczema is treated with oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg TDS or flucloxacillin. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)
Wet dressings applied over topical steroids improve drug penetration and cooling. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)
Eczema herpeticum: look for monomorphic vesicles, fever, and systemic illness; treat with IV aciclovir. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema Herpeticum)
explanation.pearls: In a child with severe eczema and pustules, always consider Staphylococcus aureus first, but if vesicles appear monomorphic, think herpes and start aciclovir.

q.stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with a limp and a swollen right knee of 6 weeks' duration, worse in the morning with stiffness lasting 30 minutes. She is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no rash. The right knee is warm, swollen, and has a small effusion but is not erythematous. The left knee and other joints are normal. Investigations show: WCC 9.5 ×10⁹/L, CRP 15 mg/L, ESR 35 mm/hr, ANA positive (1:640). What is the most important associated investigation or examination?

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0°C, HR 95, RR 22
data_table.Examination: Swollen, warm right knee with effusion, no other joint involvement, no rash
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.5, CRP 15, ESR 35, ANA 1:640, X-ray: soft tissue swelling, no bony erosion

q.lead_in: What is the most important associated investigation or examination?

options.text: Slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist to screen for chronic anterior uveitis
options.rationale: Oligoarticular JIA, especially in young girls with positive ANA, carries a high risk (up to 30%) of asymptomatic chronic anterior uveitis, which can lead to blindness if untreated. Regular slit-lamp examinations are mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)

options.text: MRI of the right knee with contrast
options.rationale: MRI is not routinely required for oligoarticular JIA diagnosis; it is clinically based. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

options.text: Echocardiogram to assess for pericarditis
options.rationale: Pericarditis is associated with systemic JIA, not oligoarticular JIA. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

options.text: Anti-streptolysin O titre to exclude acute rheumatic fever
options.rationale: The prolonged duration (6 weeks) and lack of migratory arthritis or cardiac signs make ARF unlikely. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate to exclude leukaemia
options.rationale: The isolated joint involvement, normal WCC, and positive ANA are consistent with JIA; leukaemia would likely show systemic symptoms and haematological abnormalities. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

explanation.summary: Oligoarticular JIA in ANA-positive young girls is strongly associated with chronic asymptomatic anterior uveitis. Regular slit-lamp eye examinations are the standard of care to prevent irreversible visual loss. Arthritis management includes NSAIDs and intra-articular corticosteroids.
key_points: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA: highest risk for uveitis; slit-lamp screening every 3-4 months. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)
Uveitis is usually asymptomatic; blindness can occur before redness or pain. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)
explanation.pearls: A limp and swollen joint in a preschool girl with positive ANA = JIA until proven otherwise; eye screening is critical.

q.subtopic: Inflammatory bowel disease – Distinguishing Crohn's from ulcerative colitis
q.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with 3 months of bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and weight loss of 6 kg. She has also noticed a painful perianal lump that discharges pus. On examination, she is pale, BMI is below the 5th centile, and there is a perianal fistula with purulent discharge. Bloods show Hb 92 g/L, CRP 58 mg/L, albumin 28 g/L, and faecal calprotectin >2000 mcg/g. Colonoscopy reveals continuous inflammation from the rectum to the transverse colon, with skip lesions in the caecum, and deep serpiginous ulcers in the terminal ileum. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Pale, thin, perianal fistula, no mouth ulcers
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92, CRP 58, albumin 28, faecal calprotectin >2000; colonoscopy: continuous recto-sigmoid inflammation, skip lesions caecum, terminal ileal ulcers

q.lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Crohn's disease
options.rationale: The presence of perianal fistula, skip lesions, terminal ileal involvement, and deep ulcers are characteristic of Crohn's disease. Ulcerative colitis lacks perianal fistulae and skip lesions, and only involves the colon. (Source: RCH CPG: Inflammatory Bowel Disease)

options.text: Ulcerative colitis
options.rationale: UC is continuous colonic inflammation starting at the rectum, without perianal disease or small bowel involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

options.text: Infectious colitis
options.rationale: Chronicity, weight loss, perianal fistula, and endoscopic findings point to IBD, not acute infection. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

options.text: Coeliac disease
options.rationale: Coeliac disease causes malabsorption and weight loss but not bloody diarrhoea or perianal fistulae. (Source: RCH CPG: Coeliac Disease)

options.text: Appendicitis with abscess
options.rationale: The chronic history and colonoscopy findings exclude acute appendicitis. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

explanation.summary: Crohn's disease is suggested by perianal fistulae, skip lesions, terminal ileal involvement, and transmural inflammation. Ulcerative colitis is limited to the colon, continuous, and lacks perianal disease. Faecal calprotectin and colonoscopy with histology confirm the diagnosis.
key_points: Crohn's disease: skip lesions, terminal ileal involvement, perianal fistulae, transmural ulcers. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
Ulcerative colitis: continuous colonic inflammation from rectum, no small bowel or perianal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
Perianal disease is almost pathognomonic for Crohn's in children. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
explanation.pearls: A teenager with bloody diarrhoea, weight loss, and perianal fistula has Crohn's disease until proven otherwise.

q.subtopic: Neonatal lupus – Congenital complete heart block
q.stem: A term female infant is born to a 32-year-old mother with known systemic lupus erythematosus (anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibody positive). At 2 hours of age, an annular erythematous rash is noted on the infant's face and scalp. On examination, the heart rate is 70 bpm and regular; the infant is pink, well-perfused, and in no respiratory distress. An electrocardiogram shows complete atrioventricular block (third-degree heart block) with an atrial rate of 140 bpm and a ventricular rate of 70 bpm. The QRS complexes are narrow. Bedside echocardiography shows normal cardiac anatomy and good ventricular function. There is no hydrops. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 70 bpm (ventricular), RR 45, SpO₂ 96% RA, BP 65/35 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Erythematous annular rash face/scalp, bradycardia, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: ECG: complete AV block, atrial rate 140, ventricular rate 70, narrow QRS; Echo: normal anatomy, good function; maternal anti-Ro/SSA positive

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to clear maternal autoantibodies
options.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to reverse established complete heart block. It may be used in some protocols for incomplete block, but not for established third-degree block. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus; ACR Guidelines)

options.text: Monitor the infant closely for signs of low cardiac output or heart failure, and refer to paediatric cardiology for consideration of permanent pacemaker insertion if the bradycardia persists or becomes symptomatic
options.rationale: Complete congenital heart block due to maternal anti-Ro antibodies is permanent. In an asymptomatic infant with a ventricular rate of 70 bpm and normal cardiac function, initial management is close monitoring. If the infant becomes symptomatic or the ventricular rate is persistently low, a pacemaker is indicated. Temporary pacing is rarely needed in stable infants. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus; AAP Guidelines)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to suppress the autoimmune-mediated injury to the conduction system
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have not been shown to reverse established complete heart block. They may be considered for active inflammation but are not standard for an asymptomatic infant with third-degree block. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)

options.text: Observe without specific monitoring, as the heart block typically resolves spontaneously within the first 6 months
options.rationale: Complete heart block caused by maternal anti-Ro antibodies is usually permanent and does not resolve; it requires lifelong monitoring and often a pacemaker. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)

options.text: Insert a temporary transvenous pacemaker immediately to increase the ventricular rate to 100 bpm
options.rationale: Temporary pacing is reserved for infants with symptomatic bradycardia, haemodynamic compromise, or ventricular rates <50 bpm. This infant is stable. (Source: AAP Guidelines on Neonatal Lupus)

explanation.summary: Congenital complete heart block due to maternal anti-Ro antibodies is a permanent condition. In an asymptomatic infant with an adequate escape rhythm (ventricular rate 70 bpm), initial management is close monitoring with paediatric cardiology input. A permanent pacemaker is indicated if the infant becomes symptomatic or the ventricular rate is inadequate. The rash is self-limiting.
key_points: Maternal anti-Ro/SSA antibodies can cause congenital complete heart block in 1–5% of exposed foetuses. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
Established complete heart block is irreversible; management focuses on monitoring and pacemaker if needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
The neonatal lupus rash is transient and resolves without treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Lupus)
explanation.pearls: Mothers with anti-Ro antibodies should have foetal echocardiography starting at 16–18 weeks to detect emerging heart block, as early treatment of incomplete block may prevent progression.

q.subtopic: Kawasaki disease – Giant coronary aneurysms and thromboprophylaxis
q.stem: A 4-year-old boy was diagnosed with Kawasaki disease on day 8 of fever. He received intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion and high-dose aspirin 30 mg/kg/day. The fever defervesced within 48 hours, and he was transitioned to low-dose aspirin 3 mg/kg/day. A follow-up echocardiogram at 3 weeks shows a giant aneurysm of the left anterior descending artery measuring 8 mm in diameter (z-score +10) and a smaller aneurysm of the right coronary artery (4 mm, z-score +4). The child remains afebrile and well. Platelet count is normal at 350 ×10⁹/L. What is the most important additional medication to reduce the risk of thrombosis?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Well, no mucocutaneous signs, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Investigations: Echo: giant LAD aneurysm 8 mm (z +10), RCA aneurysm 4 mm (z +4); Plt 350; INR 1.0

q.lead_in: What is the most important additional medication?

options.text: Add clopidogrel 0.2 mg/kg/day to dual antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel) is an option for medium-sized aneurysms but for giant aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10), systemic anticoagulation with warfarin is recommended in addition to aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Add warfarin with a target INR of 2.0–3.0 in addition to low-dose aspirin
options.rationale: Giant coronary artery aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10) carry a high risk of thrombosis and myocardial infarction. The AHA and RCH guidelines recommend systemic anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) plus low-dose aspirin. If warfarin is contraindicated, low-molecular-weight heparin or clopidogrel may be considered. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Increase aspirin dose back to 30 mg/kg/day as high-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: High-dose aspirin is used during the acute inflammatory phase for its anti-inflammatory effect, not for thromboprophylaxis of giant aneurysms. Systemic anticoagulation is needed. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)

options.text: Administer a second dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to accelerate aneurysm resolution
options.rationale: A second dose of IVIG may be used for persistent fever or inflammation, but it does not reduce established aneurysm size or thrombosis risk. This child is afebrile. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: No additional medication; low-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for giant aneurysms
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient to prevent thrombosis in giant aneurysms; systemic anticoagulation is recommended. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)

explanation.summary: Giant coronary artery aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10) after Kawasaki disease require systemic anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) in addition to low-dose aspirin to prevent thrombosis. Dual antiplatelet therapy with clopidogrel is an alternative if warfarin is not possible. Close echocardiographic follow-up is essential.
key_points: Giant aneurysms (≥8 mm or z ≥10): anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0) plus aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG)
Medium aneurysms (5–7 mm): aspirin alone or aspirin + clopidogrel. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)
Small aneurysms (<5 mm): aspirin alone. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Giant aneurysms in Kawasaki disease have the highest risk of myocardial infarction – lifelong anticoagulation is the standard of care.

q.stem: A 9-month-old boy with colicky abdominal pain, currant jelly stool, and a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant has an ultrasound-confirmed ileocolic intussusception. He is haemodynamically stable, afebrile, and has a soft abdomen without peritonism. There is no evidence of pneumoperitoneum on abdominal X-ray. What is the most appropriate definitive management?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 150 bpm, BP 85/55 mmHg, RR 36, Temp 37.2°C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, palpable mass RUQ
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: ileocolic intussusception; AXR: no free air

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Non-surgical reduction via air enema under fluoroscopic guidance
options.rationale: In a stable child without signs of peritonitis or perforation, non-surgical reduction with air enema is the first-line treatment for intussusception. It is safe and effective. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Emergency laparotomy with manual reduction
options.rationale: Laparotomy is reserved for failed enema reduction, perforation, or peritonitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Observation with serial abdominal examinations
options.rationale: Intussusception will not resolve spontaneously; definitive reduction is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with reduction
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is an alternative surgical approach but not first-line; enema reduction is attempted first. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Intravenous antibiotics and bowel rest
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone will not reduce the intussusception; mechanical reduction is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: In a stable child with confirmed intussusception and no signs of perforation or peritonitis, air enema reduction is the standard first-line treatment. Surgery is reserved for failed reduction or contraindications.
key_points: Air enema is first-line for uncomplicated intussusception. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
Contraindications: peritonitis, perforation, shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)
explanation.pearls: Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic; success rates exceed 80% in experienced centres.

q.subtopic: Cystic fibrosis – Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome
q.stem: A 13-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous ΔF508) presents with a 2-day history of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and bilious vomiting. She has not opened her bowels for 3 days, whereas normally she passes 2–3 loose stools daily on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon 10,000 U/kg/day). She had an episode of distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) 2 years ago, treated with oral Gastrografin. On examination, the abdomen is distended, tympanic, and tender in the right lower quadrant with a palpable faecal mass. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Erect abdominal X-ray shows faecal loading in the distal ileum and caecum with no free air. A Gastrografin enema shows impacted material in the distal ileum without complete obstruction. Surgical review confirms non-operative management is appropriate. What is the most appropriate medical therapy?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 105 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 22, Temp 37.1°C
data_table.Examination: Distended, tender RLQ, palpable mass, hyperactive bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: AXR: faecal loading distal ileum/caecum, no free air; Gastrografin enema: impacted material without complete obstruction

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate medical therapy?

options.text: Oral polyethylene glycol 3350 (Movicol) 2 sachets/kg/day with increased fluids
options.rationale: PEG is used for chronic constipation, not acute DIOS, which requires an osmotic agent that hydrates the inspissated material. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal)

options.text: Oral Gastrografin (diatrizoate meglumine) 50–100 mL diluted in water or soft drink, with IV fluids and NG decompression if vomiting persists
options.rationale: Gastrografin is a hyperosmolar contrast agent that draws fluid into the bowel, hydrating and loosening the impacted material in DIOS. It is the first-line medical therapy. IV fluids prevent dehydration from the osmotic shift. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI; CF Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: N-acetylcysteine 10 mL of 20% solution via nasogastric tube four times daily as sole therapy
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is an adjunct to thin mucus but is not the primary therapy for DIOS. Gastrografin is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

options.text: Lactulose 10 mL three times daily and a phosphate enema
options.rationale: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS, which involves inspissated material in the distal ileum, not simple constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

options.text: Intravenous metoclopramide 0.15 mg/kg 8-hourly and nil by mouth for 48 hours
options.rationale: Prokinetics and bowel rest do not resolve the obstruction. Gastrografin is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

explanation.summary: DIOS is a common complication of CF caused by inspissated faecal material in the distal ileum. Oral Gastrografin is the first-line medical therapy, acting as an osmotic laxative. IV fluids and NG decompression are supportive. Surgery is reserved for complete obstruction or failed medical management.
key_points: DIOS: acute distal small-bowel obstruction in CF, managed medically with oral Gastrografin. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)
N-acetylcysteine may be used as an adjunct to thin mucus. (Source: CF Foundation Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Gastrografin is both diagnostic and therapeutic in DIOS – it confirms the location and hydrates the impacted stool.

q.subtopic: Pertussis – Severe disease and exchange transfusion
q.stem: A 3-week-old unimmunised infant is admitted with a 10-day history of paroxysmal cough, apnoeic spells, and post-tussive vomiting. He was born at term and has been exclusively breastfed. His 4-year-old sibling had a persistent cough. On examination, the infant is alert but tachypnoeic with a respiratory rate of 55, and becomes cyanotic during coughing paroxysms with an SpO₂ of 85%. The white cell count is 52 ×10⁹/L with 75% lymphocytes. A nasopharyngeal aspirate is positive for Bordetella pertussis PCR. He is started on azithromycin and admitted to the paediatric intensive care unit. What additional intervention is most likely to improve his outcome?

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 85% during paroxysm, HR 175 bpm, RR 55
data_table.Examination: Paroxysmal cough, apnoeas, cyanosis, transmitted airway sounds
data_table.Investigations: WCC 52, lymphocytes 75%; pertussis PCR positive

q.lead_in: What additional intervention is most likely to improve his outcome?

options.text: Exchange transfusion to reduce the white cell count
options.rationale: Extreme leukocytosis (WCC >50 ×10⁹/L) in infant pertussis is a major risk factor for pulmonary hypertension and death. Exchange transfusion rapidly reduces the WCC, improving pulmonary blood flow. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDC Guidelines)

options.text: Add intravenous salbutamol infusion to treat the bronchospasm
options.rationale: Pertussis does not cause bronchospasm; the cough is toxin-mediated. Bronchodilators are ineffective. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to neutralise pertussis toxin
options.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to alter the clinical course of pertussis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 5 days
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Commence continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) to reduce apnoeas
options.rationale: CPAP may support breathing but does not address the life-threatening pulmonary hypertension from hyperleukocytosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

explanation.summary: Severe pertussis in young infants can cause extreme leukocytosis, leading to pulmonary hypertension and death. Exchange transfusion or leukapheresis is recommended to rapidly reduce the WCC. Azithromycin reduces infectivity but does not alter the established disease course.
key_points: WCC >30–50 ×10⁹/L in infant pertussis: consider exchange transfusion. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)
Antibiotics limit transmission but do not change clinical outcomes once paroxysmal cough has started. (Source: CDC Pertussis Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: The white cell count in pertussis is a prognostic marker; a rising WCC should trigger consideration of exchange transfusion.

q.subtopic: Acute liver failure – Paracetamol poisoning in a child
q.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents after they discovered she ingested approximately 50 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (total 25 g) following an argument at school. The ingestion occurred approximately 10 hours ago. She complains of nausea and mild right upper quadrant discomfort. She weighs 45 kg. On examination, she is alert and oriented, with slight epigastric tenderness. Vital signs: heart rate 100 bpm, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 99%, temperature 36.9°C. A paracetamol level drawn at 9 hours post-ingestion returns at 1800 µmol/L (275 mg/L). Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is 45 U/L (normal <35), AST 50 U/L, total bilirubin 18 µmol/L, international normalised ratio (INR) 1.1, serum creatinine 60 µmol/L. She has no known medical history. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

data_table.Examination: Alert, mild RUQ tenderness, no jaundice, no encephalopathy
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level 1800 µmol/L at 9h; ALT 45, AST 50, bilirubin 18, INR 1.1, Cr 60

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Commence intravenous N-acetylcysteine (NAC) according to the three-bag protocol (150 mg/kg over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) and monitor liver function tests and INR
options.rationale: The paracetamol level at 9 hours is 1800 micromol/L, well above the Rumack-Matthew nomogram treatment line (approximately 500 micromol/L at 10 hours post-ingestion in Australian guidelines). NAC is indicated and must be started immediately to prevent hepatotoxicity. The standard IV NAC protocol in Australia is the three-bag regimen. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning; Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Activate oral charcoal 50 g as the ingestion was within 2 hours, then reassess the need for N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: The ingestion occurred 10 hours ago; activated charcoal is only effective within 1–2 hours post-ingestion and is not indicated at this time. The priority is NAC. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)

options.text: Observe without treatment, as the ALT and INR are currently normal, and repeat blood tests in 12 hours to detect any delayed hepatotoxicity
options.rationale: A normal ALT at presentation does not exclude impending hepatotoxicity; the paracetamol level is toxic and NAC is indicated to prevent liver injury. Waiting for abnormal LFTs is dangerous. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)

options.text: Refer for urgent liver transplantation evaluation, as the high paracetamol level and early symptoms indicate irreversible hepatic necrosis
options.rationale: Liver transplant is reserved for fulminant hepatic failure unresponsive to NAC, not for early presentation with normal liver function. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)

options.text: Administer intravenous pantoprazole 40 mg for gastric protection and arrange psychiatric review for the intentional overdose
options.rationale: While psychiatric assessment is important after stabilisation, the immediate medical priority is NAC to prevent liver damage. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)

explanation.summary: Paracetamol poisoning with a toxic blood level requires immediate N-acetylcysteine infusion to prevent hepatocyte necrosis. The Rumack-Matthew nomogram guides treatment; this level is above the treatment threshold. Normal transaminases do not exclude the need for NAC. Activated charcoal is ineffective after 2 hours. Liver transplant is only for fulminant failure.
key_points: Administer NAC if paracetamol level is above the treatment line on the Australian nomogram (e.g., approximately 1000 micromol/L at 4 hours, dropping to approximately 500 micromol/L at 10 hours). (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)
The standard IV NAC regimen in Australia is 300 mg/kg over 20 hours (three-bag protocol). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)
Normal ALT/AST at presentation does NOT rule out impending hepatotoxicity. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol Poisoning)
explanation.pearls: Time is liver: NAC is most effective within 8 hours of ingestion, but still beneficial later. Start NAC while awaiting levels if the history suggests a significant overdose.

q.subtopic: Severe acute asthma – Escalation to IV therapy
q.stem: A 9-year-old boy with known severe persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation that started 6 hours ago. Despite using his fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 two puffs twice daily and salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer over the past hour, he remains severely distressed. On arrival, he speaks in single words, has marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, and an audible expiratory wheeze. SpO₂ is 88% on a non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min. He has received three back-to-back nebulisers of salbutamol 5 mg and ipratropium 250 mcg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, and oral prednisolone 40 mg (1 mg/kg) 1 hour ago. His condition has not improved. Heart rate is 150 bpm, respiratory rate 45, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg. A chest X-ray shows hyperinflation without focal consolidation or pneumothorax. What is the most appropriate next pharmacotherapy?

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 88% on O₂, HR 150 bpm, RR 45, BP 100/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Single words, severe recessions, wheeze, reduced air entry
data_table.Investigations: CXR: hyperinflation, no consolidation/pneumothorax
data_table.Medications: Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
options.rationale: IV magnesium sulphate is the recommended second-line therapy for severe acute asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled bronchodilators and systemic corticosteroids. It acts as a smooth muscle relaxant and may prevent the need for intubation. If no response, IV salbutamol or aminophylline may be considered. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Commence intravenous salbutamol infusion at 1 mcg/kg/min
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is a third-line agent reserved for refractory cases after IV magnesium has been tried, or in life-threatening asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Start intravenous aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 mg/kg/hour infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is less commonly used and has a narrow therapeutic index; it is considered after magnesium and salbutamol infusion have failed. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Repeat the nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium every 20 minutes for another hour before considering IV therapy
options.rationale: After three nebulisers with no improvement, further inhaled therapy is unlikely to be sufficient, and escalation to IV magnesium is indicated. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Perform endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation, as the child is in life-threatening asthma refractory to medical therapy
options.rationale: Intubation is a last resort and carries significant risk; medical therapy with IV magnesium should be attempted first. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

explanation.summary: For acute severe asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled bronchodilators and steroids, IV magnesium sulphate is the next recommended step. It is a safe and effective bronchodilator that can prevent progression to respiratory failure. IV salbutamol or aminophylline are third-line.
key_points: IV magnesium 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
Escalate to IV salbutamol or aminophylline if no response to magnesium. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Intubation is reserved for refractory cases or impending respiratory arrest. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
explanation.pearls: A silent chest in severe asthma is a pre-terminal sign – if the wheeze disappears and air entry diminishes, call ICU immediately.

q.stem: A term infant is born by emergency caesarean section for suspected placental abruption. Apgars are 2 at 5 minutes and 4 at 10 minutes. Cord blood pH is 6.85, base deficit 18 mmol/L. At 2 hours of age, the infant is hypotonic, has intermittent apnoea, and a weak cry. Neurological examination shows reduced consciousness, decreased spontaneous movements, and an absent suck reflex. The infant meets criteria for moderate hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy (HIE) by clinical staging. Amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) shows moderately abnormal background activity. The infant's weight is 3.2 kg, and vital signs are stable on respiratory support. The neonatal team decides to commence therapeutic hypothermia. What is the most appropriate core temperature target and duration?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120 bpm, BP 55/30 mmHg, RR assisted, SpO₂ 95%
data_table.Examination: Hypotonic, reduced consciousness, absent suck, intermittent apnoea
data_table.Investigations: Cord pH 6.85, BD 18; aEEG moderately abnormal; no seizures on aEEG

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate core temperature target and duration?

options.text: Cool to 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by slow rewarming at 0.5°C per hour
options.rationale: Therapeutic hypothermia for moderate to severe HIE in term infants involves whole-body cooling to a rectal temperature of 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by controlled rewarming. This reduces death and disability. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline; TOBY Trial; ILCOR Guidelines)

options.text: Cool to 32°C for 24 hours, then actively rewarm over 6 hours
options.rationale: The standard protocol is 33.5°C for 72 hours, not 32°C for 24 hours. Deeper cooling has not shown benefit and increases adverse effects. (Source: ILCOR Guidelines)

options.text: Provide only supportive care with maintenance of normothermia (36.5–37.5°C), as cooling is not indicated for moderate HIE
options.rationale: Moderate HIE with abnormal aEEG is an indication for therapeutic hypothermia, which improves neurodevelopmental outcomes. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)

options.text: Cool to 34.5°C for 48 hours and reassess neurological status before continuing
options.rationale: The evidence supports 72 hours at 33.5°C; shorter durations or higher temperatures are not standard. (Source: TOBY Trial; ILCOR Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg and maintain normothermia, as seizures are the primary concern
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is used for clinical or electrographic seizures, but the primary neuroprotective intervention is therapeutic hypothermia. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Therapeutic hypothermia for HIE is provided at 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by controlled rewarming. It is indicated for term infants with moderate to severe HIE and abnormal aEEG. The intervention reduces death and major neurodevelopmental disability.
key_points: Cooling criteria: ≥36 weeks, evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, moderate to severe HIE, abnormal aEEG. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)
Temperature target: 33.5°C (33–34°C) for 72 hours, rewarm at 0.5°C/hr. (Source: ILCOR Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Cooling should be initiated within 6 hours of birth for maximum benefit. Every hour of delay reduces neuroprotection.

q.subtopic: Crohn's disease – Perianal fistula and biologic therapy
q.stem: A 12-year-old girl with known ileocolonic Crohn's disease on oral azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day and mesalazine presents with a 2-week history of painful perianal swelling and purulent discharge. She has no abdominal pain or diarrhoea. Examination reveals an indurated perianal fistula with expressible pus. A pelvic MRI confirms a transsphincteric fistula with an associated small abscess. She is afebrile and systemically well. Her paediatric gastroenterologist is consulted. What is the most appropriate definitive management in addition to antibiotics and surgical drainage?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85 bpm, BP 110/65 mmHg, Temp 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Perianal fistula with purulent discharge, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: MRI: transsphincteric fistula, small abscess; CRP 35 mg/L; Hb 105 g/L

q.lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management in addition to antibiotics and surgical drainage?

options.text: Commence a tumour necrosis factor (TNF) inhibitor, such as infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, and 6 weeks, then every 8 weeks, combined with an immunomodulator
options.rationale: Perianal fistulising Crohn's disease is an indication for anti-TNF therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) in combination with an immunomodulator (azathioprine or methotrexate). Antibiotics and surgical drainage treat the acute abscess, but anti-TNF therapy is needed for fistula closure. (Source: RCH CPG: Inflammatory Bowel Disease; ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)

options.text: Increase azathioprine dose to 3 mg/kg/day and continue mesalazine, as the fistula will heal with medical therapy alone
options.rationale: Perianal fistulae rarely close with azathioprine alone; biologics are far more effective. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)

options.text: Perform a fistulotomy and primary closure under general anaesthesia, and continue azathioprine
options.rationale: Fistulotomy alone in Crohn's disease may result in non-healing and risk of incontinence; combined medical therapy with biologics is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

options.text: Insert a Seton drain and treat with oral metronidazole 10 mg/kg three times daily for 6 months, without escalation of immunosuppression
options.rationale: Seton placement is often used to control drainage, but definitive closure requires biologic therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

options.text: Refer for a diverting colostomy, as perianal Crohn's disease is refractory to medical therapy
options.rationale: Colostomy is a last resort for severe refractory disease; medical therapy with biologics is effective in most. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

explanation.summary: Perianal fistulising Crohn's disease requires a combination of surgical drainage (if abscess), antibiotics, and escalation to anti-TNF therapy (infliximab) combined with an immunomodulator. Anti-TNF agents are the most effective medical therapy for fistula closure.
key_points: Anti-TNF therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) is indicated for fistulising Crohn's disease. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)
Continue azathioprine as combination therapy to reduce immunogenicity. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)
explanation.pearls: A perianal fistula in a child with Crohn's disease is a sign of aggressive disease phenotype and warrants early biologic therapy.

q.stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of vomiting, increasing lethargy, and now a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. His parents report that he had an upper respiratory tract infection 3 days ago and had been eating less. There is no family history of note. On arrival, the child is obtunded with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 7, responding only to pain. Vital signs: heart rate 160 bpm, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 40 and deep, temperature 37.8°C. He is intubated for airway protection. Initial venous blood gas shows pH 7.48, pCO₂ 24 mmHg, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. Ammonia level is 420 µmol/L (normal <60).
